###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Y2K: History, what's being done, how to prepare

- **Date:** 1998-09-01T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<35ec0e22.5389263@news.apk.net>`
- **References:** `<35e027ed.21890305@news.supernews.com> <789A42CD2F240033.AB510F024AC7DE8E.C7A20A200EF560E3@library-proxy.airnews.net> <35DBB0B7.1820@inficad.com> <35DCEE88.6D42A1B0@att.net> <6rj0ib$l3d@sjx-ixn3.ix.netcom.com> <35DF9869.84F51BD@att.net> <35e9c578.5191387@news-s01.ny.us.ibm.net> <35E9F88B.52DAD0CC@att.net> <7WBG1.114$eZ3.165673@news3.mia.bellsouth.net>`

```
On Mon, 31 Aug 1998 18:27:15 GMT, "Judson McClendon"
<judmc123@bellsouth.net> enlightened us:

>Bill Lynch wrote:
>>Paul Knudsen wrote:
…[40 more quoted lines elided]…
>800 number, so I can call it free from anywhere in the U.S.

Judson,

I have the same thing you do but it costs me only 4.95 a month.  I can
access the bank through the Internet or through a private network
using an 800 number as well.  My only complaint is that the transfer
speed of the pages is sometimes *very* slow.  But I like the service
very much.  My bank sends payments electronically to those companies
that can accept it (e.g. AMEX, VISA etc.) and by bank check (cheque
for all you non Americans) to those that can't.   In over a year I've
only had two payments go awry.  The bank handled repayment on their
own after I notified them with no further interference by me.

Regards,


          ////
         (o o)
-oOO--(_)--OOo-
It's not the pace of life that concerns me, it's the sudden stop at the end.
 Steve
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Y2K: History, what's being done, how to prepare

- **From:** paulr@bix.com (paulr)
- **Date:** 1998-09-01T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<6sfsak$7d3@lotho.delphi.com>`
- **References:** `<35e027ed.21890305@news.supernews.com> <VA.00000e95.000330e4@access.digex.net>`

```

Oh, and a "new" one too that just bit me. I was traveling last
week, adn so let my bank balance dip down to around $300
or so. Wrote a check for $150, two small $15 checks, and
three visits to the gas station with a direct debit card.
Each visit to the gas station incurred a 3 day $50 hold
on my account! For $5/$7/$9 dollars worth of gas! 

Needless to say, the bank merrily bounced the two $15 
checks, and paid the $150 one. At $30 each - plus they
"paid" the $5 gas charge - with another $30 bank charge.
Yepper, that is right, there was perfectly enough 
money in there to cover everything I spent, but when the 
bank was finsihed with $120 worth of charges, there 
sure as hell wasn't. 

Maybe it works different in G.B., but over here, the
system is crazy. We simply are NOT going to use a direct debit
card for anything except CASH withdrawals anymore. It is
interesting to note that these holds do NOT happen when
you use a normal credit card, only when you use a debit 
card. 

I'm burned, I'm annoyed, and I'm looking for the 
snail mail addresses of my congresscritters. They 
won't do anything of course, but at least I can 
complain loudly. 

-Paul


FrancisA.Ney wrote:
: > From: Bill Lynch <wblynch@att.net>
: > Date: 31 Aug 1998 01:12:13 GMT
: >  
: > > I can't belive you guys don't use online banking!
: >  
: > I'll use it when it's free! I work for a bank, so my checking account is
: > completely free (I pay a service charge if I bounce a check, of course).
: > Online banking costs substantially more, even with an employee discount. 

: AOHell, for example, charges an extra $5/mo to direct draft the monthly fee 
: out of a checking account as opposed to a credit card.

: When companies stop pulling this shit, I'll start using my direct debit more.


: Frank Ney  N4ZHG  WV/EMT-B  VA/EMT-A  LPWV  NRA(L)  GOA  CCRKBA  JPFO
: Are you ready for Y2K?  Read "Time Bomb 2000" by Ed Yourdon
: Abuses by the BATF  http://www.access.digex.net/~croaker/batfabus.html
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Y2K: History, what's being done, how to prepare

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc123@bellsouth.net>
- **Date:** 1998-09-01T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<rORG1.631$D45.680282@news2.mia.bellsouth.net>`
- **References:** `<35e027ed.21890305@news.supernews.com> <VA.00000e95.000330e4@access.digex.net> <6sfsak$7d3@lotho.delphi.com>`

```
I have never understood why anyone would want a bank draft card in the
first place.  You can get free credit cards which allow you to keep
your money for at least 30 days before paying, at absolutely no cost to
you.  If a direct draft gets screwed up, your money is *gone*, and you
must ask them to return it.  If your credit card bill is messed up, you
still have your money while you debate it with them.  Why in the world
would anyone want to let anybody take money directly from their bank
account like that?  Beats me.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Y2K: History, what's being done, how to prepare

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** Frank Swarbrick <infocat@sprynet.com>
- **Date:** 1998-09-01T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<35ECA203.3E29@sprynet.com>`
- **References:** `<35e027ed.21890305@news.supernews.com> <VA.00000e95.000330e4@access.digex.net> <6sfsak$7d3@lotho.delphi.com> <rORG1.631$D45.680282@news2.mia.bellsouth.net>`

```
Judson McClendon wrote:
> 
> I have never understood why anyone would want a bank draft card in the
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> account like that?  Beats me.

A lot of people simply don't have the willpower to pay off their credit
card.  With the debit card you have no choice!  I use the debit card simply
in place of checks.

Which isn't to say you're point isn't valid, because it is.

By the by, you can dispute a card debit and the bank *should* credit your
account back until the dispute gets resolved.  But you're right, you sometimes
have to argue well to get the money back.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Y2K: History, what's being done, how to prepare

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** Kitty Carr <kcarr1@bellsouth.net>
- **Date:** 1998-09-02T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<35ECD8B7.5305@bellsouth.net>`
- **References:** `<35e027ed.21890305@news.supernews.com> <VA.00000e95.000330e4@access.digex.net> <6sfsak$7d3@lotho.delphi.com> <rORG1.631$D45.680282@news2.mia.bellsouth.net>`

```
Judson McClendon wrote:
> 
> I have never understood why anyone would want a bank draft card in the
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> 


Are you kidding?  At absolutely no cost to you?  That's NOT the way I
remember it.  I remember thousands of dollars in interest; applying for
another card at a lower interest rate to pay this one off; and then
another one to pay these two off, and on and on.  Finally, I came to my
senses (I thought), and closed all the accounts, and tore up all the
cards but one, for "emergencies."  Puh-leeze!  "Virtual Pool" is an
emergency?  A Sig-Sauer?  A microscope? I finally gave up my "emergency"
card, and strangely enough, I rarely have emergencies.  

I realize this is a fatal character flaw, and I have tried, really
TRIED, to change.  I've made serious vows that tomorrow, the way I am
will not be the way I'll be.  But I always forget.

What's PeopleSoft?
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Y2K: History, what's being done, how to prepare

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc123@bellsouth.net>
- **Date:** 1998-09-02T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<RZbH1.1175$eZ3.1363955@news3.mia.bellsouth.net>`
- **References:** `<35e027ed.21890305@news.supernews.com> <VA.00000e95.000330e4@access.digex.net> <6sfsak$7d3@lotho.delphi.com> <rORG1.631$D45.680282@news2.mia.bellsouth.net> <35ECD8B7.5305@bellsouth.net>`

```
Kitty Carr wrote:
>Judson McClendon wrote:
>>
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
>card, and strangely enough, I rarely have emergencies.


Woah! Woah!  You are talking apples, I am talking oranges.  You *do not*
pay any interest on most credit cards if you pay off the balance before
the due date when you get a bill.  Don't confuse a lack of discipline
handling credit with the pros and cons of a particular service.

I mean no offense, Kitty, because many people are like you in this.  But
God help us if our culture has to face real adversity.  I am concerned
that if Y2K, or something else, comes along that requires anything like
the sacrifices demanded by the Great Depression or WWI, our civilization
might crumble.  If our citizens can't muster the willpower to not buy
some useless gee-gaw they can't afford and don't need, how would we
muster the grit to make the sacrifices needed to rebuild a severely
damaged economy?
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: OT Warning (was: Y2K: History, what's being done, how to prepare)

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** Bill Lynch <wblynch@att.net>
- **Date:** 1998-09-01T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<35EC299E.8240EC80@att.net>`
- **References:** `<35e027ed.21890305@news.supernews.com> <VA.00000e95.000330e4@access.digex.net> <6sfsak$7d3@lotho.delphi.com>`

```
paulr wrote:
> 
(snip)
> Maybe it works different in G.B., but over here, the
> system is crazy. We simply are NOT going to use a direct debit
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> card

Wishful thinking, Dude. Holds are placed on CR card accounts for a
variety of reasons (I bumped into that years ago, this is the first I've
heard of holds on a DB card - doesn't make sense to me?). I rent cars
from time to time - a hold is always placed on my CR card for more than
my rental should be - to cover them if I keep the car longer (which I
have done occasionally) or decide to play bumper cars (which I haven't).
I must be naive, or something, but I thought the big feature of DB cards
was that the merchant gets his/her funds immediately, no waiting. What
purpose does the hold serve - other than to freeze my money so the bank
can lend it out in overnight funds? This is considered extortion when a
loan shark does it.

I'm definitely going to check with my bank to see if they're placing
holds when I use my ATM card to buy groceries. I don't have an actual DB
card, yet.

Bill Lynch
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: OT Warning (was: Y2K: History, what's being done, how to prepare)

_(thread depth: 11)_

- **From:** "Donald Tees" <donald@willmack.com>
- **Date:** 1998-09-03T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<6sn36q$ch5$1@news.igs.net>`
- **References:** `<35e027ed.21890305@news.supernews.com> <6sklbq$iq5$1@news.igs.net> <6smah6$nlu$1@callisto.clark.net> <6smovr$55$1@news.igs.net> <6smprk$ohj$1@callisto.clark.net>`

```

>... and international banks are?  I cannot speak for other countries, of
>course, but here in the USA the supply of paper for currency-printing is
>rather... *tightly* controlled.


Really?  When I use a debit card, and the bank introduces
a 15 hour delay between taking it out on my account and putting
it in yours, then they have just increased the monetary supply for
the period.  You do not really think they place that money
in a trust fund, do you?

Secondly, If I use a debit card in your country, I am not using
USA funds.  The government, by allowing debit cards, have
made all currencies the same ... they are simply converted
back and forth in an invisible manner. I can stay in your country
for weeks, and never use a penny of USA money.  If your
government visibly made all currencies the same, would you
still say they had control?
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: OT Warning (was: Y2K: History, what's being done, how to prepare)

_(thread depth: 12)_

- **From:** docdwarf@clark.net ()
- **Date:** 1998-09-04T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<6soq8m$rnv$1@callisto.clark.net>`
- **References:** `<35e027ed.21890305@news.supernews.com> <6smovr$55$1@news.igs.net> <6smprk$ohj$1@callisto.clark.net> <6sn36q$ch5$1@news.igs.net>`

```
In article <6sn36q$ch5$1@news.igs.net>,
Donald Tees <donald@willmack.com> wrote:
>
>>... and international banks are?  I cannot speak for other countries, of
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
>in a trust fund, do you?

I cannot back-read in threads on this reader (trn) but I believe the
original assertion was that international banks can print money; if you
can show me where you've demonstrated that such printing takes place I'd
appreciate the refinement of my reading skills.

>
>Secondly, If I use a debit card in your country, I am not using
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>still say they had control?

Once again, Mr Tees... where are the presses which I recall references
having been made to?  I may go towards an object or have an object brought
towards me with the net effect of decreased proximity being the same...
if, however, I say, as the object is being carried towards me while I
remain stationary 'See?  I *am so* going towards the object, it should be
obvious since our proximity decreases' then I would say that my use of the
language and associated concepts is... curious, to say the least.

Where are the presses, Mr Tees... and the inks and the paper-stocks?  Such
things are required for what is usually referred to as 'printing'.

DD
```

Anarchist's cookbook, 1998.

1. Everybody on the internet gets one debit card,
with $5.00 in the account. We all trade, at random,
and do all banking with the card we receive.

2. Everybody stops filing income tax returns.

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: OT Warning (was: Y2K: History, what's being done, how to prepare)

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** Charles F Hankel <charles@hankel.mersinet.co.uk>
- **Date:** 1998-09-11T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<35F93D41.4B1E588F@hankel.mersinet.co.uk>`
- **References:** `<35e027ed.21890305@news.supernews.com> <6sfsak$7d3@lotho.delphi.com> <35EC299E.8240EC80@att.net> <6shk1f$o6g_001@p16-term3-and.netdirect.net> <6sjihm$j3t$1@callisto.clark.net>`

```
docdwarf@clark.net wrote:
> 
> Some folks see not having to carry sums of cash as a benefit.

I had this argument thrown at me only last week.  I went into a large
shop to buy a newspaper and the woman in front of me at the cash desk
offered up her credit card in payment for a newspaper and a cheap
ballpen - total 64 pence (that's about a dollar US).

In order to speed up proceedings, I offered to pay for them along with
my newspaper.  Bad move.  She ranted on about it being unsafe to carry
cash, I responded that this was a load of bunkum and that she was
flattering herself and was pretty rude in the way that she refused my
kind offer to someone who obviously needed the goods but didn't have
enough money.

Some folks probably don't the sense to be aware of the environment in
which they move.  Even where banditry is rife it isn't necessarily
unsafe to carry cash.

Mind you, when I used to sell things to the general public, there was a
nice polite sign (beautifully painted by my own fair hand) that
indicated to all that credit cards were not acceptable for purchases
under UKP10.00 (about USD16.00).  This was because most of the profit
would disappear in bank charges and that as I was not in the banking
business, the bank should not get the profits from mine.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: OT Warning (was: Y2K: History, what's being done, how to prepare)

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** "Dennis J. Minette" <dennis_minette@email.msn.com>
- **Date:** 1998-09-12T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<eApDdMi39GA.186@upnetnews03>`
- **References:** `<35e027ed.21890305@news.supernews.com> <6sfsak$7d3@lotho.delphi.com> <35EC299E.8240EC80@att.net> <6shk1f$o6g_001@p16-term3-and.netdirect.net> <6sjihm$j3t$1@callisto.clark.net> <35F93D41.4B1E588F@hankel.mersinet.co.uk>`

```

Charles F Hankel wrote in message
<35F93D41.4B1E588F@hankel.mersinet.co.uk>...
>docdwarf@clark.net wrote:
>>
…[24 more quoted lines elided]…
>


This reminds me of the sign behind the bar at the local bar:
We have made a deal with the local bank, we have agreed that this bar would
not cash checks if the bank will refrain from selling beer.

Another thought:  Customers of retail businesses do not realize that most
banks levy a separate charge to merchants for each deposit item (i.e.,
check) in excess of some modest number per month.  Many banks are even
charging a "handling" fee (e.g., $0.12 per $100) for cash deposits in excess
of certain stated amounts!  Banks are just getting more and more fee
oriented as time goes by.

Regarding an earlier branch of this thread - there is no justification that
I can discern for putting a $50 hold on funds in an account that is to be
debited $5 for a *completed* transaction.  Nominal, estimated holds on fund
amounts for incomplete transactions (e.g., car rental, starting a hotel
stay, etc.) are however a justifiable fact of life nowadays.  Could it be
that the $5 amounts mentioned previously were for payment (in advance?) for
video rentals, in which case the excessive hold amount placed on money in
the debit account might have been in anticipation of non-return of the
merchandise - in effect, a refundable deposit held against the eventual
return of the video cassettes??
```

On Fri, 11 Sep 1998 16:09:53 +0100, Charles F Hankel
<charles@hankel.mersinet.co.uk> wrote:
>Mind you, when I used to sell things to the general public, there was a
>nice polite sign (beautifully painted by my own fair hand) that
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>business, the bank should not get the profits from mine.

Do that nowadays(at least here in the U.S.), and you risk losing your
credit card merchant status.  Ditto for adding a 'credit card surcharge' to
the posted price when the customer pays by credit card(oddly enough,
however, it is acceptable to offer a 'cash discount' for those who *don't*
pay by credit card).

Ben

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: OT Warning (was: Y2K: History, what's being done, how to prepare)

_(thread depth: 9)_

- **From:** Charles F Hankel <charles@hankel.mersinet.co.uk>
- **Date:** 1998-09-20T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36055AE4.D6A332B9@hankel.mersinet.co.uk>`
- **References:** `<35e027ed.21890305@news.supernews.com> <6sfsak$7d3@lotho.delphi.com> <35EC299E.8240EC80@att.net> <6shk1f$o6g_001@p16-term3-and.netdirect.net> <6sjihm$j3t$1@callisto.clark.net> <35F93D41.4B1E588F@hankel.mersinet.co.uk> <36001d1e.417416152@news.mindspring.com>`

```
Ben Coleman wrote:
> 
> On Fri, 11 Sep 1998 16:09:53 +0100, Charles F Hankel
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
> pay by credit card).

If I were to get back into retail, it's something that I would do again
and risk the wrath of XYZ card company.  As for the question of
surcharges, it's not worth the effort because it immediately alienates
people.  However by stating the lower limit for card acceptance, I can
easily explain the reason and the customers generally understand.

I only ever had one argument with someone but then he was starting from
the viewpoint that said that plastic was the same as cash.  When I
pointed out that I could accept his card for a measly five quid, I
wouldn't be able to go across the road and spend his voucher on my
lunch, he demanded to see my boss.  I'd be about forty three years old
at the time... still, the entertainment was free of charge.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: OT Warning (was: Y2K: History, what's being done, how to prepare)

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@aiservices.com>
- **Date:** 1998-09-02T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<F5nH1.343$I%.532065@news1.atlantic.net>`
- **References:** `<35e027ed.21890305@news.supernews.com> <VA.00000e95.000330e4@access.digex.net> <6sfsak$7d3@lotho.delphi.com> <35EC299E.8240EC80@att.net>`

Bill Lynch wrote in message <35EC299E.8240EC80@att.net>...
>
>Wishful thinking, Dude. Holds are placed on CR card accounts for a
…[14 more quoted lines elided]…
>
Some debit cards are based upon credit cards, as such, they are
subject to holds!

In my case, I have a Visa credit card that is tied to an investment
account. The processing is no different than a credit card, except
for the fact the funds are debited from the investment account, upon
clearing of the transaction.

In other words, it looks like a credit card but it is actually a debit card.
-------------------------------
Rick Smith
e-mail: < ricksmith@aiservices.com >
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: OT Warning (was: Y2K: History, what's being done, how to prepare)

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** paulr@bix.com (paulr)
- **Date:** 1998-09-02T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<6si2m5$h68@lotho.delphi.com>`
- **References:** `<35e027ed.21890305@news.supernews.com> <VA.00000e95.000330e4@access.digex.net> <6sfsak$7d3@lotho.delphi.com> <35EC299E.8240EC80@att.net>`

```

Well, all I can say is I have never seen a $50 precharge
on my Visa or Mastercard, but they sure did on the debit card.
Debit cards are nice in a couple ways; first, there is never
a danger of a finance charge, and there is not penalty (i.e.
higher interest rate, immediate interest accural, etc.) for
taking out cash. But we won't use it again...

-Paul


Bill Lynch (wblynch@att.net) wrote:
: paulr wrote:
: > 
: (snip)
: > Maybe it works different in G.B., but over here, the
: > system is crazy. We simply are NOT going to use a direct debit
: > card for anything except CASH withdrawals anymore. It is
: > interesting to note that these holds do NOT happen when
: > you use a normal credit card, only when you use a debit
: > card

: Wishful thinking, Dude. Holds are placed on CR card accounts for a
: variety of reasons (I bumped into that years ago, this is the first I've
: heard of holds on a DB card - doesn't make sense to me?). I rent cars
: from time to time - a hold is always placed on my CR card for more than
: my rental should be - to cover them if I keep the car longer (which I
: have done occasionally) or decide to play bumper cars (which I haven't).
: I must be naive, or something, but I thought the big feature of DB cards
: was that the merchant gets his/her funds immediately, no waiting. What
: purpose does the hold serve - other than to freeze my money so the bank
: can lend it out in overnight funds? This is considered extortion when a
: loan shark does it.

: I'm definitely going to check with my bank to see if they're placing
: holds when I use my ATM card to buy groceries. I don't have an actual DB
: card, yet.

: Bill Lynch
```

- **From:** riplin@kcbbs.gen.nz (Richard Plinston)

```
In message <<6sfsak$7d3@lotho.delphi.com>> paulr@bix.com  writes:

> Oh, and a "new" one too that just bit me. I was traveling last
> week, adn so let my bank balance dip down to around $300
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> on my account! For $5/$7/$9 dollars worth of gas! 

I am confused by the '$50 hold'.  Either I am missing something
or the idea of 'direct debit' is different than it is in
New Zealand.  (I know it is different in UK, it seems useless
there).  In NZ: use a direct debit card and it is debited
in real time.  I can only imagine that your 'direct debit'
is not 'direct' but is batch updated at some later time
over the next few days.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: .l

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** scm@enterprise.net (Shaun C. Murray)
- **Date:** 1998-09-02T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<6sj9bq$3hk$3@news.enterprise.net>`
- **References:** `<6sfsak$7d3@lotho.delphi.com> <3298243.71557.28888@kcbbs.gen.nz>`

```
In article <3298243.71557.28888@kcbbs.gen.nz>, riplin@kcbbs.gen.nz says...
>
>I am confused by the '$50 hold'.  Either I am missing something
>or the idea of 'direct debit' is different than it is in
>New Zealand.  (I know it is different in UK, it seems useless
>there).

Which form of debit do you mean?

"Direct Debit" in the uk is you fill in a form giving permission to a 3rd party 
to directly debit your account without you having to sign a bill/cheque. eg. 
you tell the electricity company to automatically debit your account with what 
you owe them on the 1st of every month.

It's very useful (no effort involved on your part) and usually gets you a 
discount because the admin overhead for the elec. company is less.

"Debit Cards" are exactly like credit cards except the money comes out of your 
bank account. It comes out in real time and you sign a receipt. It's similar to 
the US but they use a PIN pad. ie. you swipe your card and enter your PIN. Must 
say I always thought that the US system is open to abuse as it doesn't rely on 
the customer signing anything so stolen cards with known PIN's is a problem.


>  In NZ: use a direct debit card and it is debited
>in real time.  I can only imagine that your 'direct debit'
>is not 'direct' but is batch updated at some later time
>over the next few days.

Some debit card systems don't work in real time 24/7. It's not uncommon for 
systems to automatically accept debits at weekends and bank holidays.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: .l

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** Frank Swarbrick <infocat@sprynet.com>
- **Date:** 1998-09-02T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<35EE1DFF.57DC@sprynet.com>`
- **References:** `<6sfsak$7d3@lotho.delphi.com> <3298243.71557.28888@kcbbs.gen.nz> <6sj9bq$3hk$3@news.enterprise.net>`

```
Shaun C. Murray wrote:
> "Debit Cards" are exactly like credit cards except the money comes out of your
> bank account. It comes out in real time and you sign a receipt. It's similar to
> the US but they use a PIN pad. ie. you swipe your card and enter your PIN. Must
> say I always thought that the US system is open to abuse as it doesn't rely on
> the customer signing anything so stolen cards with known PIN's is a problem.

Hmmm, I have the exact opposite thought.  It is much easier for someone to steal
your card and use it, even if they don't know the PIN, because all they have to
do is sign.  How often do you see a merchant actually verify the signature with
the back of your card?  Not very often!
 
> >  In NZ: use a direct debit card and it is debited
> >in real time.  I can only imagine that your 'direct debit'
> >is not 'direct' but is batch updated at some later time
> >over the next few days.

That is, in fact, actually true.  An immediate authorization is given, but
the actual transaction does not post for a few days.  This is mostly a holdover
from credit cards, and before they had total electronic processing.  You'd call
in for an authorization (or use a "floor limit" auth), send in all your credit
card slips to your processor, and the account wouldn't be debited for days.  I
think this is going to start going away.

Generally if you have a PIN based transaction the debit comes out that night
(or the next, if it's late in the day).  But if you only sign for it it takes
a few days.
```

o tell the difference in the cards. Obviously, they certainly

so greatly benefit the banks. My bank just collected $120 
off me, which is quite a bit more than I pay in finance
charges on my credit cards. For a year. :/

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: .l

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** Charles F Hankel <charles@hankel.mersinet.co.uk>
- **Date:** 1998-09-11T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<35F940F3.627FB4E3@hankel.mersinet.co.uk>`
- **References:** `<6sfsak$7d3@lotho.delphi.com> <3298243.71557.28888@kcbbs.gen.nz> <6si2r2$h68@lotho.delphi.com>`

```
paulr wrote:
> 
> Yes, debit transactions are processed through the VISA or
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> charges on my credit cards. For a year. :/

If you want to play with their toys, then you must accept the rules of
their game.  Of course, you could set your own agenda and minimise their
involvement by using cash.  Cash is way cool and talks more pointedly
than cards.  In all of my major purchases, cash gets me a discount that
cards won't get, and sometimes a very substantial discount.  And it
doesn't matter where in the world this is, even in the USA.

The only downside is that Hertz and Avis won't rent me a car; but then
this is something I can live without because the ones that do recognise
cash are invariably a lot less expensive.  For a three week trip
stateside, its cheaper to buy a car and sell it back to the same dealer
at the end of the trip, especially if you haven't banged it about.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: .l

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** paulr@bix.com (paulr)
- **Date:** 1998-09-12T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<6tctt7$due@lotho.delphi.com>`
- **References:** `<6sfsak$7d3@lotho.delphi.com> <3298243.71557.28888@kcbbs.gen.nz> <6si2r2$h68@lotho.delphi.com> <35F940F3.627FB4E3@hankel.mersinet.co.uk>`

```
Just a couple problems with that around here - a lot of places
are very leary about accepting cash. (Ever tried to pay a
mortgage or rent payment in cash? They turn green and
nasty... :) Second, carrying around a lot of cash means 
tracking a lot of cash. Credit cards or debit cards
do all that tracking for you. 

I use a particular Visa card for all my business purchases,
and I get back a nice itemized list of everything I spend. 
Sames me grief. 

Cash is fine in a lot of places but plastic is far more 
convienence almost every time. 
:) 

-Paul

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: .l

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** Charles F Hankel <charles@hankel.mersinet.co.uk>
- **Date:** 1998-09-12T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<35FA8C2A.5D7E500C@hankel.mersinet.co.uk>`
- **References:** `<6sfsak$7d3@lotho.delphi.com> <3298243.71557.28888@kcbbs.gen.nz> <6si2r2$h68@lotho.delphi.com> <35F940F3.627FB4E3@hankel.mersinet.co.uk> <6tctt7$due@lotho.delphi.com>`

```
paulr wrote:
> 
> Just a couple problems with that around here - a lot of places
> are very leary about accepting cash. (Ever tried to pay a
> mortgage or rent payment in cash? They turn green and
> nasty... :)

I don't have a mortgage but I used to pay rent years ago in cash, no
worries.  If anyone refuses to accept cash, I would insist that they do
so in writing since such refusal negates their right to take me to court
for non-payment, mainly because the basis of non-payment is the debtors
refusal to pay.  The refusal of the offer of legal tender is not grounds
for litigation, at least not in the UK it isn't.

>              Second, carrying around a lot of cash means
> tracking a lot of cash. Credit cards or debit cards
> do all that tracking for you.

I don't really see how credit cards track cash.  They only track those
payments that you have made with them.  OTOH, getting a receipr from
people is probably a better way of tracking expenditure since everything
that you buy can be tracked in this way.
 
> I use a particular Visa card for all my business purchases,
> and I get back a nice itemized list of everything I spend.
> Sames me grief.

I just slap the business expenses in a different column - it's up-to
date every night - no grief.
 
> Cash is fine in a lot of places but plastic is far more
> convienence almost every time.
> :)

Nah, can't agree.  I can get out of a taxi much quicker by waving money
at the driver rather than a piece of plastic.  I can clear any till
faster by using cash than anyone can with plastic, anywhere in the
world.

And then there's the conversation:
   "How much do you want for this?"
   "Sixty quid, mate."
   "How about fifty?" <holding out a fifty pound note>
   "Well... OK, it's a deal."
   (Goods and cash change hands all in less than 30 seconds)
or:
   "How much do you want for this?"
   "Sixty quid, mate."
   "How about fifty?"
   "Cash?"
   "Er, plastic."
   "No, I couldn't go below sixty on plastic."
   "Well... OK, it's a deal."
   (goods sold for sixty quid, plastic processed,
    signature checked - all in about two minutes)
or:
   "How much do you want for this?"
   "Sixty quid, mate."
   "How about fifty?"
   "Cash?"
   "Er, plastic."
   "No, I couldn't go below sixty on plastic."
   "You let him have one for fifty quid."
   "Well, he had cash, didn't he?"
   "Well a credit card is as good as cash."
   "OK, give me your credit card and we'll see what I can buy with it."
   (no deal and a waste of time)

Waving plastic at people is never as effective as waving cash under
their noses.  And what about when you're not sure if you have enough to
pay?  With cash you know, with plastic only the bank knows beforehand. 
If haven't got the money, you can't afford it.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: .l

_(thread depth: 9)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1998-09-12T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<6teg07$4gr@dfw-ixnews5.ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<6sfsak$7d3@lotho.delphi.com> <3298243.71557.28888@kcbbs.gen.nz> <6si2r2$h68@lotho.delphi.com> <35F940F3.627FB4E3@hankel.mersinet.co.uk> <6tctt7$due@lotho.delphi.com> <35FA8C2A.5D7E500C@hankel.mersinet.co.uk>`

```
(just to add to this highly OT thread)

The reality in the US is that many small and medium size businesses are now
strongly preferring credit cards to cash.  Often, they will give discounts
for credit card purchases.  (This is done because - apparently? - the
security involved is better.  I.e., the credit card companies will "stand
behind" a VERIFIED transaction with a credit card - while no one supports
counterfeit cash.)

I have also found that when I travel in a foreign (mostly European - I don't
know about Asian or South American) country, that I am charged LESS by
merchants in the way of surcharge if I use a credit card than if I use
travelers checks.  Using local cash might be cheaper, but somewhere I need
to change US "value" into local currency and this usually costs.  Although I
am aware that the credit card companies ALWAYS charge me the worst possible
conversion rate, it still (usually) comes out cheaper in the end.

Having said all that about CREDIT cards, I have not yet used a debit card
and (personally) have no strong desire to switch to one.  I do use
"automatic payment"plans but do not use online banking (except to transfer
from one account to another within the same institution).

Now, my question is:
    How did my "innocent" question about checks having pre-printed "19" in
the date field get to this really prolonged and off topic discussion (and
NO, please don't really answer this).
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: .l

_(thread depth: 10)_

- **From:** Charles F Hankel <charles@hankel.mersinet.co.uk>
- **Date:** 1998-09-20T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36055FE7.E5EEA00E@hankel.mersinet.co.uk>`
- **References:** `<6sfsak$7d3@lotho.delphi.com> <3298243.71557.28888@kcbbs.gen.nz> <6si2r2$h68@lotho.delphi.com> <35F940F3.627FB4E3@hankel.mersinet.co.uk> <6tctt7$due@lotho.delphi.com> <35FA8C2A.5D7E500C@hankel.mersinet.co.uk> <6teg07$4gr@dfw-ixnews5.ix.netcom.com>`

```
William M. Klein wrote:
> 
> (just to add to this highly OT thread)
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> counterfeit cash.)

That's not helped by your currency notes hardly ever being changed.  It
gives the counterfeiter too much time.

With most European currencies, the notes are changed from time to time
as a measure against the counterfeiters with some really weird printing
techniques designed to thwart, for instance, copier technology.  Some
currency notes in some countries are even embossed to help the blind. 
In the UK we have a metal strip that runs through the paper, partly
concealed partly exposed.  Nothing is proof against counterfeiters but
card fraud is probably a greater crime over here.

The US is the only place in my recent experience that has all currency
notes the same size and the same colour with the differences in
denominations being comparatively minor notations.  It's not easy to
tell a ten dollar bill from a hundred dollar one without reading it.  I
can put my hand in my trouser pocket [*] and count my cash without
taking it from my pocket because of the relative sizes of the notes,
something I can't do in the USA.

> Now, my question is:
>     How did my "innocent" question about checks having pre-printed "19" in
> the date field get to this really prolonged and off topic discussion (and
> NO, please don't really answer this).

Oh, OK.
```

about the metal strips; we have them in the us, also.  guess what?
(here's where i go WAY off topic) a special scanner used by police and
highway patrol (especially highway patrols!) can register how many of
those strips are in your wallet and the cop can inquire as to why you
have so much money [read: more money than the law lets you carry]
a friend of mine 3 years ago was stopped [and scanned] and 2000 dollars
was taken from him.  he was on his way with friends to spring break in
ft. lauderdale.  he had to drive 200 miles back home to get proof from
his bank that it was HIS money before he could get it back.
take the time and tear the strips out!

> William M. Klein wrote:
…[27 more quoted lines elided]…
> something I can't do in the USA.

[*] I don't carry a wallet (=US:billfold).  I bought one when I was
about sixteen and my dad told me that when you lose a wallet, you lose
verything that you keep in it.  That put an end to wallets for me.
```

  Just as an amusing sideline for those running IBM compilers on MVS.  If
you happen to have an "old" OS/VS COBOL compiler around somewhere, try using
USAGE DISPLAY-ST or using either PIC D or PIC d  (yes, that is a lower-case
"d").  It is particularly interesting if you are doing either arithmetic or
Report Writer "SUM" clauses.

For those who don't have an OS/VS COBOL compiler handy, I'll let you guess
what happens from the part of Charles' note that I did not "snip".  For
those of you who DO have the compiler,if you want to read the documentation
on this, it was last documented in the ANS COBOL V4 language reference
manual - under the topic "Sterling support"!

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Y2K: History, what's being done, how to prepare

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** Frank Swarbrick <infocat@sprynet.com>
- **Date:** 1998-09-01T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<35ECA133.7E8A@sprynet.com>`
- **References:** `<35e027ed.21890305@news.supernews.com> <VA.00000e95.000330e4@access.digex.net> <6sfsak$7d3@lotho.delphi.com>`

```
paulr wrote:
> 
> Oh, and a "new" one too that just bit me. I was traveling last
…[24 more quoted lines elided]…
> complain loudly.

Working for a bank myself I have to say that is a very strange situation.  Whenever
I go to the gas station they simply get a $1.00 authorization simply to make sure
the card is valid.  The actual charge, as with all card debits, doesn't post for a
few days.  It may, in fact, be the gas stations themselves causing the havoc.

Also at our bank we do not bounce checks based on the fact that there are card debit
authorizations against your account.  For instance, let's say you have $50.00 in your
account.  If you make a card debit on Monday it usually won't actually post against your
account until perhaps Wednesday.  So you can have a $50.00 check clear on Monday or
Tuesday, and you're still able to make a deposit on Wednesday to cover the debit.  Now
if you *don't* conver it by Wednesday you *will* get a handling charge.  The item will
post, still, as card debits cannot be "bounced."

I am also a little lost regarding them bouncing to $15.00 checks and paying the $150.00
one.  Were they all presented on the same day?  If the $150 one came in earlier than
the $15 one, and it cleared all the money out of your account, then there's nothing you
can complain about.  How would the bank know about the other checks?  On the other hand,
if they all came in on the same day then you have every reason to be upset.  At my bank
we pay the small checks first so as many get paid as possible (per day) before any
handling charges are accrued.  Now card debits, ATM withdrawals, and "at bank" withdrawals
do get first priority, paying before *any* checks no matter the amount, because we are
already out the money.  Electronic payments also have priority, something I do not agree
with because they can be returned.

You may or may not be interested to know that Visa is coming out with what's called
"Debit Card 2" sometime soon.  These items, instead of "floating" for a few days like
credit cards and current debit cards, will post the same day just like ATM cards.
Actually, this probably isn't good for people who like having the few days to
cover it, but it does make it easier to know how much money you actually have in your
account.

Sorry to go off on this tangent.  I'm sure that not all banks are as good as the one
I work for, but I must defend it if only for my own sake.  I worked at an actual office
of the bank for five years, and now programming for two years.  Ask me anything!  (Not
that I'll necessarily know, but...)
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Y2K: History, what's being done, how to prepare

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** paulr@bix.com (paulr)
- **Date:** 1998-09-02T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<6sjj1i$vir@lotho.delphi.com>`
- **References:** `<35e027ed.21890305@news.supernews.com> <VA.00000e95.000330e4@access.digex.net> <6sfsak$7d3@lotho.delphi.com> <35ECA133.7E8A@sprynet.com>`

```

So Frank, what bank do you work for? Three checks came in
the same day, two for $14.91, and one for $150. Because of the
$150 worth of holds on the account, there was a $149.xx available
in the account (actual balance was ~$200 or just a little bit over...)
- they paid the $150 check charging me a $30 handling charge
- they returned BOTH $14.91 checks, charging me $60 in handling charges

The next day, a debit card transaction came through, for $5.xx, which
they paid (claiming a negative balance!) charging me another
$30 handling fee.) And of course, I got the notices on Saturday.
I just found out today that the two $14.91 checks were represented
early MOnday mornining, and of course, returned, costing me *another*
$60 - despite the fact that I popped over to the bank early 
Monday am and deposited a couple checks - which they promptly 
held for a day. (Wouldn't have mattered anyway, they processed
the checks on Sunday night, so there was no way to get money 
in there *anyway*.) 

This is, of course, in the Philadelphia area, so scams are 
pretty prevalent. The bank did reverse $60 in charges, to their
credit, but I'm still out a hunk of money I can ill afford to 
loose. 

THis is quite a profitable sideline for someone...

-Paul


Of course, all this

and can withdraw cash immediately, at any time, day or night. As for
you being in a particular city, what has that got to do with it?  One
major advantage of the card system is that your bank can be anywhere,
in any currency, and it is totally invisible to the merchant you are dealing
with.

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Y2K: History, what's being done, how to prepare

_(thread depth: 9)_

- **From:** iscdavid@aol.com (ISCDavid)
- **Date:** 1998-09-04T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<1998090414334500.KAA10391@ladder03.news.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<35EE1F04.55A1@sprynet.com>`

```
How about a teeny bit of Banking DDA 101?<G>

Point #1, Debit Cards whether at an ATM or at a merchant work pretty much just
like a check. The big difference is between "Online Merchants", those that
actually process directly thru your bank, and the vast majority of smaller
merchants that process your debit card exactly as if it were a Visa or
Mastercard. However, once the transaction gets to your bank its usually
processed like it was a check. The big difference happens during the day. The
true online transactions are "memo posted" against your balance. The real DDA
(checking) systems are all batch. Not for the least of the reasons because of
those pesky pieces of paper called checks:)

As to what order checks are handled and what will bounce or not bounce, the
answer varies depending on the specific software the bank uses.

ALL DDA systems process by chronological order within trancode so that deposits
are posted first, followed by internal transactions (real withdrawals from
either the bank in person or ATMs or online debits) and finally checks. If a
large check is processed first, the balance is reduced as long as there are
funds to cover it. If the next check causes an overdraft, it is rejected and
the program continues trying each subsequent check.

Some (a very very few) DDA systems once tried to minimize the number of checks
bounced by sorting by value. But....with the fee income that can be generated I
doubt most do now. All that I know of DO keep trying to process the remaining
checks however.

Since I've been playing with banking systems for the better part of 30 years, I
think I do have a few insights:)

BTW...NONE of what I said above applies to anything the bank considers a
"critical" account. If you just happen to be Microsoft or GM, you'll probably
NEVER have a check bounce<G>

David Olson
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Y2K: History, what's being done, how to prepare

_(thread depth: 9)_

- **From:** "Jerry Peacock" <bismail-nospam@bisusa.com>
- **Date:** 1998-09-04T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<35ef759e.0@news.intnet.net>`
- **References:** `<35e027ed.21890305@news.supernews.com> <VA.00000e95.000330e4@access.digex.net> <6sfsak$7d3@lotho.delphi.com> <35ECA133.7E8A@sprynet.com> <6sjj1i$vir@lotho.delphi.com> <6sklk6$ir5$1@news.igs.net> <6sksht$top@lotho.delphi.com> <35EE1F04.55A1@sprynet.com>`

```
>>
>> Philadelphia has the highest bank charges of anywhere in the
>> nation, especially in the area of service charges - as I found
>> to my everlasting regret.


<< snip >>

Which illustrates that the next (first) time you leave the country, you
should open an expatriate account in a foreign bank - preferably one
that does not have an American branch.

Some interesting places:
Isle of Mann - The oldest, continuous democracy in the world, first
    organized in the tenth century. Never had a bank or insurance company
     failure.
Royal Bank of Austria (Now National Bank of Austria) - Has not missed a
    premium since its inception including, in this century, two world wars
    and the great depression.
Mexico - No American has ever lost a dime in a Mexican Bank (or so I am
    told).
Israel - Bank secrecy that makes the Swiss look like blabber-mouths.
    Banks there are prohibited by law from even telling their own government
    about depositors.
Scottish banks offer Visa Cards (an interesting way to get your money BACK
    into the country - via an ATM machine).
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Y2K: History, what's being done, how to prepare

_(thread depth: 10)_

- **From:** Charles F Hankel <charles@hankel.mersinet.co.uk>
- **Date:** 1998-09-11T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol

```
Jerry Peacock wrote:
> 
> Some interesting places:
> Isle of Mann - The oldest, continuous democracy in the world, first
>     organized in the tenth century. Never had a bank or insurance company
>      failure.

Isle of Man actually.  It's only a short sea trip, or flight, from
here.  It also has other interesting facets vis-ï¿½-vis taxation, great
food, decent nightlife and the most dangerous motorcycle races in the
world.  OK the races aren't dangerous, just the riders and spectators,
but they have fatalities on an almost regular basis.

Vanuatu would be an interesting place for this sort of thing.  There is
no personal taxation, there is state health care, education and
subsidised food.  Mind you, I'm told that the weather can be sunny and
that the pace of life is relaxed but then there's always a downside to
everything.

Belgium's not too shabby on the confidentiality front and nor is
Luxembourg but the UK is crap - they'd tell your next door neighbour as
a way of relieving the boredom.

> Scottish banks offer Visa Cards (an interesting way to get your money BACK
>     into the country - via an ATM machine).

As do other British banks.  It's not unusual here.  Maybe more people
use ATMs here than in the USA?  I can certainly use one of my ATM cards
on mainland Europe and it may be so with the others.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Y2K: History, what's being done, how to prepare

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** Charles F Hankel <charles@hankel.mersinet.co.uk>
- **Date:** 1998-09-11T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<35F93535.632DFAAD@hankel.mersinet.co.uk>`
- **References:** `<35e027ed.21890305@news.supernews.com> <VA.00000e95.000330e4@access.digex.net> <6sfsak$7d3@lotho.delphi.com>`

```
paulr wrote:
> 
<snipped a tale of minor fiscal disaster>
> Maybe it works different in G.B., but over here, the
> system is crazy. We simply are NOT going to use a direct debit
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> complain loudly.

It may seem paradoxical for a professional programmer, but I do not have
credit cards nor debit cards nor cheques.  I have three hole-in-the-wall
cards that will give me instand access to an aggregate UKP850.00
(USD~1350.oo) per day in cash and various electronic means at my
disposal to get money to where it needs to go.

The only problem area has been in dealing with companies in the US. 
Internic for instance insist on credit card or US bank cheques (I
suppose that in this context that should read checks) but even so, I am
able to obtain such a cheque.

Funnily enough, a former colleague mentioned to me that it might even be
difficult for me to get a credit card because I don't have any debts,
though I don't know if this is true, never having tried nor have I found
a reason to want to apply.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Y2K: History, what's being done, how to prepare

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** mark0young@aol.com (Mark0Young)
- **Date:** 1998-09-12T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<1998091221021900.RAA10188@ladder01.news.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<35F93535.632DFAAD@hankel.mersinet.co.uk>`

```
In article <35F93535.632DFAAD@hankel.mersinet.co.uk>, Charles F Hankel
<charles@hankel.mersinet.co.uk> writes:

>Funnily enough, a former colleague mentioned to me that it might even be
>difficult for me to get a credit card because I don't have any debts,
>though I don't know if this is true, never having tried nor have I found
>a reason to want to apply.

I have heard the same thing here. However, that is not the only criteria for
getting a credit card. For example, you may find it easy to get a credit card
where you have substantial savings or a checking account or otherwise regularly
do business. For example, I got my first credit card from my credit union,
where I had both a checking account and a savings account.

My principle credit card is at my current credit union, with a 02/00 (mm/dd)
expiration date. I have used that to drop tidbits about Y2k at the merchants
where I have used it.

Mark A. Young
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Y2K: History, what's being done, how to prepare

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** "WOB" <wobconsult@sprynet.com>
- **Date:** 1998-09-12T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<6teu6o$fkg$1@juliana.sprynet.com>`
- **References:** `<35F93535.632DFAAD@hankel.mersinet.co.uk> <1998091221021900.RAA10188@ladder01.news.aol.com>`

```
Mark,

    VisaNet and BankNet (MasterCard) have mandated Y2K compliance for ALL
Acquirers and Issuers. AFAIK, most have complied. It's when you travel
overseas you'll find a Merchant here and there that's using non-Y2K
compliant POS terminals. BTW, the Expiration Date in an Authorization
request is now a conditional field in the variable-length ISO-message. If
it's omitted, the Issuer (the owner of the card) should not decline the
request for that reason. If they do decline you, then it's time for a new
issuer.

    For the most part, you're pretty safe in Western civilization.

Cheers,

WOB

Mark0Young wrote in message
<snipped>

>My principle credit card is at my current credit union, with a 02/00
(mm/dd)
>expiration date. I have used that to drop tidbits about Y2k at the
merchants
>where I have used it.
>
>Mark A. Young
```

That doesn't surprise me. Nor would it stop me from mentioning to merchants
that their entire operations have to be Y2k-ready. Charge card processing is
just a small (but visible) part of it.

Mark A. Young

Charles F Hankel wrote:
> 
(snip)

> It may seem paradoxical for a professional programmer,

Nah.

> but I do not have credit cards nor debit cards nor cheques. I have three hole-in-the-wall cards that will give me instand > access to an aggregate UKP850.00 (USD~1350.oo) per day in cash and various electronic means at my disposal to get money to > where it needs to go.

Is a "hole-in-the-wall" card what I'd call an ATM card here in the USA?
If so, is it on a savings account? I'm having trouble envisioning no CR
cards, no DB cards, and no cheques.

Bill Lynch

PS: My spell checker, being a Yank, didn't like "cheques" and suggested

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Y2K: History, what's being done, how to prepare

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** Charles F Hankel <charles@hankel.mersinet.co.uk>
- **Date:** 1998-09-17T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3601794C.A99D1392@hankel.mersinet.co.uk>`
- **References:** `<35e027ed.21890305@news.supernews.com> <VA.00000e95.000330e4@access.digex.net> <6sfsak$7d3@lotho.delphi.com> <35F93535.632DFAAD@hankel.mersinet.co.uk> <35FA4C89.7174CC41@att.net>`

```
Bill Lynch wrote:
> 
> Charles F Hankel wrote:
…[15 more quoted lines elided]…
> cards, no DB cards, and no cheques.

Yes, hole-in-the-wall is an ATM.  I have one bank account that has no
chequebook, it pays interest to me all the time that the bank owes me
money which is, I hope, all of the time.  If I need to spend money when
shopping, I use cash that I have withdrawn from the hole in the ATM (I
thought I'd try to bridge the ocean).

If I need to pay my bills, I do this by telephone in one of two ways.

First is to have a two-way conversation with some poor soul who has
volunteered to work evenings, weekends and nights - mainly because I
seem to forget the bills until fairly odd hours.  They, after an
authorisation conversation in which I have to mention one of my keywords
and two numbers from an agreed string, will arrange for the money to
leave my account and arrive in the account of whoever I ened to pay.

Second is to dial an automatic response number.  I have ten possible
payment destination codes established with the details of the
recipient's bank account and by using the telephone keypad, I key in my
5-digit PIN, the number (0-9) of the account I want to make a payment to
and then the amount.

Both of these mechanisms make the transfer immediately.

With another bank account, I have software on my PC and something that
resembles a pocket calculator.  I dial in to the bank and use the
calculator thing as a security code calculator.  On this system, I can
look at all of my bank accounts, regardless of bank, in the UK and sit
here and play shuffle-my-money.  It's not a system that I am over fond
of because I see the access to multiple banks as a weakness, but then it
is a weakness regardless of whether you have the system at home (or
office) or not.

As a fallback, I can always get a cashier's cheque for any sum in any
currency in any country althought there is a charge for this when the
transaction is not a UK sterling transaction.

So, in answer to you question, I use cash quite frequently, I use
electronic means of various sorts and very occasionally a cashier's
cheque.  In truth, the latter has been exclusively for purchases from
the USA because quite a lot of European banking systems can be accessed
electronically.

> PS: My spell checker, being a Yank, didn't like "cheques" and suggested
> "Cleese" instead. Wasn't there a thread recently concerning programmers
> (good) & Monty Python?

LOL!  Over here, a check is something that one sees on golf trousers,
tweed jackets and gingham shirts amongst other things.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ OT: Re: Y2K: History, what's being done, how to prepare

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** cadams@cadams-ntw.acucorp.com (Chris Adams)
- **Date:** 1998-09-14T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<slrn6vqiee.c0.cadams@cadams-ntw.acucorp.com>`
- **References:** `<35e027ed.21890305@news.supernews.com> <VA.00000e95.000330e4@access.digex.net> <6sfsak$7d3@lotho.delphi.com> <35F93535.632DFAAD@hankel.mersinet.co.uk>`

```
On Fri, 11 Sep 1998, Charles F Hankel <charles@hankel.mersinet.co.uk> wrote:
>Funnily enough, a former colleague mentioned to me that it might even be
>difficult for me to get a credit card because I don't have any debts,
>though I don't know if this is true, never having tried nor have I found
>a reason to want to apply.

This is probably quite true. If you don't have any sort of credit history, 
you'll have to take a poorer rate to establish one. I have a relative who 
filed bankruptcy awhile back; he gets more attractive offers than I do just
because his rating exists and he's got about 30 years on me.  Bad credit 
apparently is better than no credit...

Youth is another excuse they use to gouge you. It can be semicomical for young
techies: when I bought my car, I had to get a parent to cosign it even though 
I had a [significantly] higher income and half the obligations. They seem to 
think that anyone under a certain age is irresponsible and working in the 
fast-food industry...



----
# Chris Adams <cadams@acucorp.com>
The opinions above are my own and may or may not match those of my employer.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: OT: Re: Y2K: History, what's being done, how to prepare

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** mark0young@aol.com (Mark0Young)
- **Date:** 1998-09-21T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<19980921040000.AAA08274@ladder01.news.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<slrn6vqiee.c0.cadams@cadams-ntw.acucorp.com>`

```
In article <slrn6vqiee.c0.cadams@cadams-ntw.acucorp.com>,
cadams@cadams-ntw.acucorp.com (Chris Adams) writes:

>If you don't have any sort of credit history, 
>you'll have to take a poorer rate to establish one. I have a relative who 
>filed bankruptcy awhile back; he gets more attractive offers than I do just
>because his rating exists and he's got about 30 years on me.

I don't think the bad credit history has to do with it, but rather the
inability for someone to file for bankruptsy more often than a certain number
of years (7? 14?), so the lenders know that the person's obligations cannot be
dismissed before that period of time.

However, it seems to help to have had experience buying something on time, even
if one didn't have to, and consider the interest as the admission price for
having a good credit history; or get a charge card, even with
less-than-stirling interest rate, and use it occasionally, even if one does pay
it off in full each time to minimize interest (but try to get one with a "grace
period" where no interest is charged if you do pay the balance off).

Mark A. Young
```

Jeff Farkas  <Jeffrey.Farkas@gte.net> wrote:

>>

>like Homework, its Homework.

Well, I got a reply to my reply... check this out!

--begin quote of my reply:

From docdwarf@clark.net Thu Sep 10 14:24:54 1998
Date: Thu, 10 Sep 1998 14:24:35 -0400 (EDT)
From: docdwarf@clark.net
To: Wei Lu <luw@me.udel.edu>
Subject: Re: ? binary

On Thu, 10 Sep 1998, Wei Lu wrote:

> > > Please do your own job,
> > 
…[20 more quoted lines elided]…

Well, let's see... I know it is your homework because I took the very same
classes and know what kind of questions they ask... and your .edu address
doesn't hide much.

> It's ridiculous.

Glad you agree... now do your own homework, please.

> Actually, I am posting
> the question for my friend who is working.

How interesting... you see, *I* do this kind of work and questions like
yours *never* come up on the job... just in the classes to prepare you for
the job.  I think you are telling a small lie here.

> Please do some free things to
> help the people in need and don't send a lot of junk mails to this newsgroup, 
> if you are really nice.

Please do your own homework.

DD

--end quote of reply

So, you see... it is not *his* homework... it is a *friend's* homework!

DD

Jeff Farkas  <Jeffrey.Farkas@gte.net> wrote:

[snippage of 'it is my friend's homework, not mine!']

>  Gee DD, I know that there is a desperate need for COBOL
>programmers but have we started to hit the bottom of 
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>  Wei Lu, Have your *friend* do their own homework!

Mr Farkas, it gets better... here's another!

--beg quoted reply
From docdwarf@clark.net Thu Sep 10 15:25:17 1998
Date: Thu, 10 Sep 1998 15:24:58 -0400 (EDT)
From: docdwarf@clark.net
To: Wei Lu <luw@me.udel.edu>
Subject: Re: ? binary

On Thu, 10 Sep 1998, Wei Lu wrote:

> > > How do you know it's my homework?
> > 
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> You are really ridiculous.

You are welcome to your opinion, of course.

> > 
> > > Actually, I am posting
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> cobol homework?

I have no reason why *I* do things, let alone you; I assume that you do
the COBOL homework because you take the COBOL class.

> Did you see "me" in my email address? Don't assume all the
> things , when you know very little. Kids always do that.

When it walks like a duck, quacks like a duck and makes duck-farts then I
usually assume it is a duck... and I am usually not wrong, either.

> 
> I am doing my job very well, and helped my friend.

Now I am confused... if your friend is on a job and has questions like
these to ask why is he asking a researcher in mechanics about COBOL?
Shouldn't he look in his textbook, or in a library, or check the notes
from the classes he took?  Maybe the best way for you to help him is to
let him get fired for incompetence so he has the time to really learn what
he is trying to do on his job.

> Also, I am discussing
> your personal problems with you personally, never send a lot of nonsense
> junk mails to affect other folks like what you always did. 

You are not me... then again, this is not news.

> In this sense, I am much nicer than you.

In just about *any* sense it is not difficult to be nicer than I am.

> 
> Being kind and modest is the very basic for you who want to be nice.
> Start from this, you will be nice. That's my sincere hope.

Start from doing your own homework, that is the way to learn COBOL.

DD

--end quoted reply

Just lovely, isn't it?  Maybe I'll get some lessons in niceness in
exchange for the homework in COBOL

DD
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: ? binary

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** Manzari <pmanzari@arrow.com>
- **Date:** 1998-09-11T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<35F94226.5223@arrow.com>`
- **References:** `<1998082500584300.UAA19553@ladder01.news.aol.com> <1tGZ16mMk7i4-pn2-qn9lMVoHd2T9@dsm4merlin.iix.com> <Pine.OSF.3.95q.980826165704.20334G-100000@octane.dasb.fhda.edu> <1tGZ16mMk7i4-pn2-ZLzSLHJxDBDZ@dsm4merlin.iix.com> <Pine.OSF.3.95q.980827160640.1682A-100000@octane.dasb.fhda.edu> <1tGZ16mMk7i4-pn2-kFdoRhnCseSU@dsm4merlin.iix.com> <Pine.OSF.3.95q.980828154030.14099A-100000@octane.dasb.fhda.edu> <Pine.SOL.3.95.980910112526.20908B-100000@me.udel.edu>`

```
It's amazing how many people will try so hard to prove themselves rude,
inconsiderate morons.  If anyone who worked for me responded in such a
manner to a polite question from a colleague, they would be out the door
so fast their head would spin.

Anyway, in answer to your question...

An S9(4) COMP field is a 2 byte binary, or halfword field.  To determine
if a CCCCMMDD date field will fit, you need to know the largest value
that will fit into a halfword field.  The largest value is x'FFFF' or
65,535.  Therefore, a date in the format you describe will not fit.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: ? binary

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** "COBOL Frog (Huib Klink)" <H.Klink@IMN.nl>
- **Date:** 1998-09-11T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<35F98914.DAB45D6F@IMN.nl>`
- **References:** `<1998082500584300.UAA19553@ladder01.news.aol.com> <1tGZ16mMk7i4-pn2-qn9lMVoHd2T9@dsm4merlin.iix.com> <Pine.OSF.3.95q.980826165704.20334G-100000@octane.dasb.fhda.edu> <1tGZ16mMk7i4-pn2-ZLzSLHJxDBDZ@dsm4merlin.iix.com> <Pine.OSF.3.95q.980827160640.1682A-100000@octane.dasb.fhda.edu> <1tGZ16mMk7i4-pn2-kFdoRhnCseSU@dsm4merlin.iix.com> <Pine.OSF.3.95q.980828154030.14099A-100000@octane.dasb.fhda.edu> <Pine.SOL.3.95.980910112526.20908B-100000@me.udel.edu> <35F94226.5223@arrow.com>`

```
Manzari wrote one or two apparently wrong things:

First:

> It's amazing how many people will try so hard to prove themselves rude,
> inconsiderate morons.  If anyone who worked for me responded in such a
> manner to a polite question from a colleague, they would be out the door
> so fast their head would spin.

I never met a boss that paid his people to help others, outside his
organization, being in classes, with homework. Except educational
institutions of course. So you are an exception, a boss of some school or
wrong.But, to stay polite, I think (mr/ms) Manzari that what most of the ng
members try to do is:
1 - learn school/student/learning people to do their homework THEMSELVES.
That's the intention of homework, 'cause the one who does it, learns from
it, not the one that returns the answers.
2 - help people to better understand or use COBOL, not to learn them to
program.
3 - React (answer) in a way that is conforming the question. Mind the part
"form" in "conforming". When you follow this ng a while, lots of homework
questions are characterized by:
--> short, atomic, nothing saying subjects, like ? binary
--> typo's or semi-typo's: ? i.s.o ", spaces before ? and .
--> yelling UPPERCASES.
--> incomplete questions of flaws like "DATE" and "DATE1"
--> lack of basic knowledge
Most members react in the style of the question.

Example:

> Hi, to ALL,

"all" would do

> If a DATE is defined as

"DATE" is a reserved word for a special register. Or is "DATE1" meant? Or
"date field" / "date variable"?

>      DATE1  PIC  S9(4) COMP .

Here's such a space

> Can its format be as   ?ccyymmdd? since it?s a BINARY ?

"?"?BINARY? Does he/she think COMP = BINARY?

> THANKS .

It is indeed odd how many times this little things are part of homework
questions. But it works for me: bells start ringing and normally I even
don't answer. In most cases others react with "do your own homework".

Second

> Anyway, in answer to your question...
>
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> 65,535.  Therefore, a date in the format you describe will not fit.

I assume that you assumed that COMP stands for binary, as did the original
questioner and would do most people. (An assumption that is
compiler-dependent). Further let us assume 8 bits in one byte and indeed 2
bytes in total, then we need at least one bit for the sign. Now the largest
value within the 15 remaining bits = 2^15 - 1 = 32,767. The largest negative
value is 32,768. Together with zero, these form 65,536 discrete (integer)
values.

You were right in that this range is too small to fit a ccyymmdd-formatted
date.

The Frog
```

COBOL Frog (Huib Klink) wrote:
> 
> Wei Lu wrote:
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
> since WHAT is a binary?

Okay, if we're going to be serious, a binary *what*? In my experience,
"binary" is usually an adjective, e.g., a binary field or a binary
value. When people refer to "a binary", I usually think they mean the
compiled program (a load module in MVS terms).

Bill Lynch
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: ? binary

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** vbandke@ibm.net (Volker Bandke)
- **Date:** 1998-09-15T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<35ff191d.2039472@news2.ibm.net>`
- **References:** `<1998082500584300.UAA19553@ladder01.news.aol.com> <1tGZ16mMk7i4-pn2-qn9lMVoHd2T9@dsm4merlin.iix.com> <Pine.OSF.3.95q.980826165704.20334G-100000@octane.dasb.fhda.edu> <1tGZ16mMk7i4-pn2-ZLzSLHJxDBDZ@dsm4merlin.iix.com> <Pine.OSF.3.95q.980827160640.1682A-100000@octane.dasb.fhda.edu> <1tGZ16mMk7i4-pn2-kFdoRhnCseSU@dsm4merlin.iix.com> <Pine.OSF.3.95q.980828154030.14099A-100000@octane.dasb.fhda.edu> <Pine.SOL.3.95.980910112526.20908B-100000@me.udel.edu>`

```
On Thu, 10 Sep 1998 11:25:50 -0400, Wei Lu <luw@me.udel.edu> wrote:

>Hi, to ALL,
> 
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>      


Sure you can - but in a different way than what you would expect:

We have 31 days a month - for this we would need 5 bits
We have 12 months a year - this would require 4 bits
We would need 7 bits for the yyears, and another 7 for the century
That makes 7+7+4+5=23 bits out of the available 32 bits - we have lots of room to spare
now, haven't we?

Todays date would be : 
cc=	19 	= B'0010011'
yy=	98	= B'1100010'
mm=	09	= B'1001'
dd=	15	= B'01111'

The full value would be B'0000 0000 0001 0011 1100 0101 0010  1111'                
or X'0013C52F'

HTH


with kind regards

Volker Bandke
(BSP GmbH)
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: ? binary

_(thread depth: 11)_

- **From:** "COBOL Frog (Huib Klink)" <H.Klink@IMN.nl>
- **Date:** 1998-09-16T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36001398.846888F2@IMN.nl>`
- **References:** `<35ff191d.2039472@news2.ibm.net> <3298257.70589.12749@kcbbs.gen.nz> <35FF2FFE.225F901A@att.net> <35ff89d1.631698@news3.ibm.net> <Jl0PnHJ5PvPd-pn2-0dmyDgxV4nRy@Dwight_Miller.iix.com>`

```
Thane Hubbell wrote:8<

> > Sure, using PKBitZip....  Is there an OS0390 verion of it as well?
> > with kind regards
> >
>
> There is, and it makes use of the half bit slack space.

This is simply NOT true. After dramatically enhancing the reliability of
memory chips, the IBM mainframes now-a-days use the nineth bit no longer
for parity checking but as extra information holders. For two byte
binary fields (usage comp or binary, pic 9(n) or s9(n) with n between 1
and 4) this enlarges the range from -32768/+32767 to -65536/+65535
(2^1=2 times better) and the possibility to record it as either a real
or an imaginary number. Four byte binaries (usage comp or binary, pic
9(n) or s9(n) with n between 5 and 9) go up from -2147483648/+
2147483647 to -17179869184/17179869183+ (2^3=8 times better). Eight byte
binary fields are similarly improved in capacity. IBM is still seeking
for other improvements in data storage. The charset EBCDIC will likely
be renamed EEBCDIC or E2BCDIC. Some serious thoughts are given to add
the option PARALLEL DUPLICATES to indexed files. The nineth bit in each
byte of the primary key would enable to add duplicate keys. While the
keys seems duplicate in EBCDIC, the eight bit view, in E2BCDIC they are
unique. The longer the key is, the more dups are allowed this way; the
maximum total being 2^n - 1 (n is the length of the key). Especially for
DB/2 this is considered a major new option in the next release with
which a new step ahead of other dbms vendors will be made.

More information will be provided by your local IBM dealer.

The Frog
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: ? binary

_(thread depth: 14)_

- **From:** "Warren G. Simmons" <warrens@inficad.com>
- **Date:** 1998-09-16T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<360053B8.BDA0FE82@inficad.com>`
- **References:** `<35ff191d.2039472@news2.ibm.net> <3298257.70589.12749@kcbbs.gen.nz> <35FF2FFE.225F901A@att.net> <35ff89d1.631698@news3.ibm.net> <Jl0PnHJ5PvPd-pn2-0dmyDgxV4nRy@Dwight_Miller.iix.com> <36001398.846888F2@IMN.nl> <36001836.0@news1.ibm.net> <360023DD.F1080B98@IMN.nl>`

```


COBOL Frog (Huib Klink) wrote:

> Yep! l'Histoire ce repï¿½te.
>
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
> >

I recall the IBM 7000 series of systems that preceded the 360, and the
explanation they
made for going to 32 bits.  First their 7000 series came in two groups.
Scientific, and Data Processing. Some could argue that there was a third all
encompassing group. Anyway,
the Scientific had 36 bit words, and the GE600 was patterned after that in their
effort to beat IBM to the market with a new machine.  IBM told users that via
extensive research they determined that double word 32 bit  could easily handle
the double precision needs of the world, and besides why make two different
systems when one family would do? So,
my take is that it was marketing, and accounting fighting over the methods to
use. Now, we've found new uses for bits. <G> Or perhaps half-bits.  Don't forget
the new IBM drive smaller than a 3.5 inch floppy disk will hold one hell of a lot
of data.

Warren Simmons
warrens@iname.com
```

You are behind the times, the latest technology simply gets rid of all
the zeros, and keeps the ones.  After all, it is the ones that store the
data, the zeros are wasted.

<a id="t-6ddb58b9d3"></a>
## Thread: Why COBOL is still there ? — 19 this month / 47 total

_Topics: [`COBOL's future, legacy, and obsolescence`](topics.md#future)_

_Thread started [1998-08](1998-08.md#t-6ddb58b9d3)._

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: morphed topic: Which Languages?(was:Re: Why COBOL is still there ?)

_(thread depth: 9)_

- **From:** Ibis redibis numquam peribis <rrg10300@dasb.fhda.edu>
- **Date:** 1998-09-01T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<Pine.OSF.3.95q.980901172349.31804H-100000@octane.dasb.fhda.edu>`
- **References:** `<3298240.73240.29992@kcbbs.gen.nz> <1998083000084000.UAA21208@ladder01.news.aol.com> <u4V7FK709GA.326@upnetnews03> <6sahj5$heg$1@news.igs.net> <Pine.OSF.3.95q.980829213851.27712J-100000@octane.dasb.fhda.edu> <35E94FAF.1821@erols.com> <Pine.OSF.3.95q.980830114241.880C-100000@octane.dasb.fhda.edu> <35e9c202.0@news1.ibm.net>`

```


On Sun, 30 Aug 1998, Leif Svalgaard wrote:

> Ibis redibis numquam peribis wrote in message ...
[excised]
=>>And these questions lead on to another set: how much should a
=  professional programmer know about the hardware organization of
=  computers and how much about the applications wherein his or her
=  programs are used?
=> 
=> 
=>Each computer language defines an 'abstract machine. Programs should be
=>written to that abstract or virtual machine without regard to the
=underlying 'real' machine. So, in that sense, knowing ANYTHING about the
=real machine is not good. All too often, we see statements like: "on my
=system you *just* do blah blah and it works". This is not good, albeit
=sometimes necessary.
=> 
=> 
Sorry for taking so long to respend...been BZ.

Given the sense you mean, I certainly agree: portability und normalen uber
alles. But let me fine tune my phraseology: ouside of the programmer's
role as a HLL programmer, have there been instances when knowing something
about the real vs the virtual machine has helped you to develop a solution
to a programming problem without your having necessarily incorporated that
knowledge in your programming?

To keep this from being way too vague, consider a shop that upgrades from
an Amalgamated Abacus Corp's model Abracadabra VI to an Abracadabra XXV
which included a memory upgrade from 128MB to 1GB. Suppose they go to
compile their treasue trove of programs and find that some of the larger
and more complex programs fail with an "insufficient virtual memory
error". Would it help if they knew that the VI was a CISC machine while
the XXV was a RISC machine?

rrg
==========================================
All business is show business; so, show me.
==========================================
```

   [recycled]

                             **********************
                                           /
=>                                        /
=> Bill Lynch <g>                        /
=>                                      /
=>                                     /   
                                        :-(  are ya  crackin' wise
                                             about my dEC????????????
                                             Are youse really Bill Lynch
                                             or are you that big bobwolfe?
                                             
                                              
Boy oh boy, why would they recompile on a hardware upgrade? 
Because they thought it would run faster on the RISC machine if the code
was recompiled rather than run through a translator. And they had dreams
of finishing the production work on 2nd shift so that 3rd shift could be
eliminated.

rRg
===========================================================
How come we could get to the moon with slide rules,
but we cannot get there now with Pentiums?

Where there is a political will, there is a political way.
==========================================================
```

Mr. Ibis:

Ibis redibis numquam peribis <rrg10300@dasb.fhda.edu> wrote:

[snip]

>                                        :-(  are ya  crackin' wise
>                                             about my dEC????????????
>                                             Are youse really Bill Lynch
>                                             or are you that big bobwolfe?

No, he really is Bill Lynch and I'm the big bad wolfe.  I would never
say anything bad about your Compaq...errrr...Digital Equipment
machine.  ;-)

I happen to love them, because it is a platform that we support!  We
are also an official member of the Digital Equipment Association of
Software and Application Partners (ASAP)...catchy, isn't it?  I have
even tattooed the ASAP program logo on my posterior!

I even proudly wear my Digital Baseball cap which was given to me at
the CA World 98 show in New Orleans!  It is quite fashionable!

We even have a version of our Thin Client software coming out soon
which supports VMS to PeeCee's running Windows via TCP/IP!!  ARRRR!
Keep yer COBOL on the VAX, put the GUI on the Winders box, I always
says!

Please note...this is absolutely a shameless promotion!  When someone
refers to me as the "big bad wolfe" then it is up to me to prove that
I am indeed a bad boy...just like COBOL is a bad boy!....and I am
indeed a bad boy!


Bob Wolfe, flexus
Check out The Flexus COBOL Page at http://www.flexus.com
```

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** redsky@ibm.net (Thane Hubbell)
- **Date:** 1998-09-01T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<Jl0PnHJ5PvPd-pn2-B4SrU80md2bn@Dwight_Miller.iix.com>`
- **References:** `<3298240.73240.29992@kcbbs.gen.nz> <1998083000084000.UAA21208@ladder01.news.aol.com> <u4V7FK709GA.326@upnetnews03> <6sahj5$heg$1@news.igs.net> <Pine.OSF.3.95q.980829213851.27712J-100000@octane.dasb.fhda.edu> <35E94FAF.1821@erols.com> <Pine.OSF.3.95q.980830114241.880C-100000@octane.dasb.fhda.edu>`

```
On Sun, 30 Aug 1998 19:29:33, Ibis redibis numquam peribis 
<rrg10300@dasb.fhda.edu> wrote:

> Putting aside my initial "wise guy" response, I believe that this has the
> makings of an interesting and useful topic: How many languages should a
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>

If it's a programming language, and it has a book - and even sometimes
if the manuals are not available - I can program with it.  I'm a 
COMPUTER programmer, not a COBOL programmer.

How many languages do I know?  What does that MEAN?  How many do I 
know INSIDE OUT or how many have I used to develop applications.  What
about Variations on a theme?  I know COBOL on the PC, RM, Fujitsu, 
MicroFocus, Realia.  I know it on the mainframe, OS/VS COBOL, ANS 
COBOL, COBOL II, COBOL for OS/390 and VM, etc.  All are slightly 
different.  As far as other languages go, I started to write them down
once but gave up as I kept thinking of ones I had used that I forogt 
to add to the list. I am a COBOL expert.  I dabble and do what is 
necessary to get the job done in other languages.

However, I know programmers who know only a single language.  Throw 
something else at them and they are lost.
```

examined the source program, found the place and made the changes before
realising that I didn't know PL/1 and had never before come into contact
with it.  It compiled, tested and went live OK, though.
