###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: morphed topic: Which Languages?(was:Re: Why COBOL is still there ?)

_(thread depth: 11)_

- **From:** theodp@aol.com (Theo DP)
- **Date:** 1998-09-12T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<1998091218065600.OAA16021@ladder01.news.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<35FA49F0.8A2DBE6E@att.net>`

```
><HTML><PRE>Subject: Re: morphed topic: Which Languages?(was:Re: Why COBOL is
>still there ?)
…[15 more quoted lines elided]…
></PRE></HTML>

Be careful before you leap into other programming languages without knowing
what you're doing...

A number of years back, I got a call from the the IS head of a company I left a
couple of months earlier.

He was complaining about some PL/I programs I had written that no longer
worked. 
When asked what modifications had been made since my departure, he firmly
stated that no code had been changed. 

When pressed on the subject, he noted that their head COBOL geek, apparently
unaware of the concept of scope of variables, had noted that the PL/I program
violated his anal retentive COBOL standards and "improved" things by
rearranging all the PROCEDUREs into alphabetical order. The COBOL geek
reassured the IS head that this could not possibly cause any problems.

Ooops...
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: morphed topic: Which Languages?(was:Re: Why COBOL is still there ?)

_(thread depth: 12)_

- **From:** Charles F Hankel <charles@hankel.mersinet.co.uk>
- **Date:** 1998-09-20T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3605591A.43E0A1CA@hankel.mersinet.co.uk>`
- **References:** `<35FA49F0.8A2DBE6E@att.net> <1998091218065600.OAA16021@ladder01.news.aol.com>`

```
Theo DP wrote:
> 
> When pressed on the subject, he noted that their head COBOL geek, apparently
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> reassured the IS head that this could not possibly cause any problems.

LOL!

In my defence, at the time of the change described earlier, I did have
somewhat more than a solid grounding in COBOL, Assembler, EXEC(II), CMS,
CLIST, REXX and various other programming languages, 4GLs and pseudo
languages.  Certainly I would never dream of shifting existing code
around - too much work and too little time - and restrict myself to
finding the source of the error (the most time-consuming part) and
correcting it.  But then, we're all sensible here on c.l.cobol aren't
we?
```

<a id="t-0c029c7e4c"></a>
## Thread: COBOL and Perl — 13 msgs

### Re: COBOL and Perl

- **From:** mjd@op.net (Mark-Jason Dominus)
- **Date:** 1998-09-01T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.perl.misc,comp.lang.cobol,alt.folklore.computers
- **Message-ID:** `<6si90n$aju$1@monet.op.net>`
- **References:** `<6r7fi0$9a7$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com> <6r7tk8$3ql$1@client3.news.psi.net> <6rr7l5$g8q$1@justus.ecc.lu>`

```
In article <6rr7l5$g8q$1@justus.ecc.lu>,
Stefaan A Eeckels <Stefaan.Eeckels@ecc.lu> wrote:
>> COBOL was together with FORTRAN one of the first high level languages.
>> It dates from the late 40's/early 50's. Perl was developped 40 years
>Late fifties, Abigail.

Right.  Let's take a moment to remember a brief chapter from the
history of computers.  

ENIAC, the world's first large-scale general-purpose electronic
digital computer, was commissioned by the U.S. Navy to solve
ballistics problems for the war that was in progress.  It was
delivered in 1946.  Whoops.

You programmed the ENIAC with patch cables, by hooking together
various units so that they would sends the numbers to the right other
units.  No programming language.

For some reason I find it hard to forget that ENIAC was programmed
with cables, and impossible to forget that it was delivered late for
the war, so I don't forget that there were no programming languages in
1946.

Stored-program computers, such as EDSAC and UNIVAC, were not even in
the planning stages until 1946, and none was completed before 1949.

Programming languages followed.  Fortran appeared first; design began
in 1954 and the Fortran I compiler was finished around 1957; Fortran
IV, the language later standardized as Fortran '66, was complete in
1961.  Lisp was slightly later; McCarthy began to think about Lisp in
1956, with the first compiler appearing around 1958 or 1959.

COBOL followed soon after, around 1959-1961.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL and Perl

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** waltman@netaxs.com (Walt Mankowski)
- **Date:** 1998-09-14T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.perl.misc,comp.lang.cobol,alt.folklore.computers
- **Message-ID:** `<6ti5kv$kl7@netaxs.com>`
- **References:** `<6r7fi0$9a7$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com> <35ED0C98.3512@iridia.ulb.antispam_remove.ac.be> <slrn6uq8uu.73n.rivie@rivie.daautah.com> <35EF1AC7.F1F9B3DC@plano.net> <6soqtc$smb$1@monet.op.net> <35F8FC5B.2B9544E1@hankel.mersinet.co.uk>`

```
As someone who's written code both in Cobol and in Perl, I feel
compelled to add my 2 cents.

It's possible to write quite readable code in Perl, just as it's
possible to write nearly incomprehensible programs in Cobol.
Cobol programs do, on the whole, tend to be more readable than
Perl scripts.  Cobol is a fine langauge for doing what it was
designed to do -- processing data, reading and writing records in
files, printing reports, etc.  At my company we do the vast
amount of our back-end processing, including online transaction
processing, in Cobol, and on the whole it works out quite well
for us.

There are two things I really dislike about Cobol.  First of all,
it's difficult to write modern, structured code in Cobol.  In
particular it's hard to break code down into small subroutines as
one normally does in C or Perl.  Cobol programs tend to be build
around paragraphs which are just glorified Basic gosubs.  They
also tend to have dozens, if not hundreds, of global variables.

Second, in Cobol I often feel like I'm programming with one hand
tied behind my back.  In Cobol all you can do is write simple,
straightforward code.  You can do that in Perl as well, but you
have a lot of options at your disposal if you need them.  If you
need to do something reasonably complicated it's usually far
easier to write it in Perl.

Once at work we had to convert city names from upper case to
proper case, e.g. "NEW YORK CITY" to "New York City".  This would
be a difficult routine to write in Cobol, but in Perl you can do
it in a one-line regular expression:

s/(\W*\w)(\w*)(\W*)/$1\L$2\E$3/g;

Yes, I do realize how ugly this is.  For the sake of future
maintainers I try to not include a lot of these, and comment like
hell when I do.  But it's good to be ABLE to have tools like
regular expressions at your disposal when you need them.  

Walt
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL and Perl

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** cberry@cinenet.net (Craig Berry)
- **Date:** 1998-09-14T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.perl.misc,comp.lang.cobol,alt.folklore.computers
- **Message-ID:** `<6ti78d$r6m$1@marina.cinenet.net>`
- **References:** `<6r7fi0$9a7$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com> <35ED0C98.3512@iridia.ulb.antispam_remove.ac.be> <slrn6uq8uu.73n.rivie@rivie.daautah.com> <35EF1AC7.F1F9B3DC@plano.net> <6soqtc$smb$1@monet.op.net> <35F8FC5B.2B9544E1@hankel.mersinet.co.uk> <6ti5kv$kl7@netaxs.com>`

```
Walt Mankowski (waltman@netaxs.com) wrote:
: Once at work we had to convert city names from upper case to
: proper case, e.g. "NEW YORK CITY" to "New York City".  This would
: be a difficult routine to write in Cobol, but in Perl you can do
: it in a one-line regular expression:
: 
: s/(\W*\w)(\w*)(\W*)/$1\L$2\E$3/g;

That's actually a lot uglier than it needs to be.

  s/(\w+)/\u\L$1/g;

does the same thing more readably (at least IMO).  (And actually, this
version will go from any mix of capitalization into initial-capital,
trailing-lowercase form, though of course that's an unneeded 'feature' for
your spec.) 

: Yes, I do realize how ugly this is.

As a general rule, when something easily expressible like "initial-cap,
trailing-lowercase all words" ends up looking ugly as a Perl expression,
it should indicate you've not found the best way to write the Perl
expression.  "Making easy things easy," as the saying goes.

: For the sake of future
: maintainers I try to not include a lot of these, and comment like
: hell when I do.  But it's good to be ABLE to have tools like
: regular expressions at your disposal when you need them.  

Amen to that!

---------------------------------------------------------------------
   |   Craig Berry - cberry@cinenet.net
 --*--    Home Page: http://www.cinenet.net/users/cberry/home.html
   |      "Ripple in still water, when there is no pebble tossed,
       nor wind to blow..."
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL and Perl

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** waltman@netaxs.com (Walt Mankowski)
- **Date:** 1998-09-15T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.perl.misc,comp.lang.cobol,alt.folklore.computers
- **Message-ID:** `<6tkmck$l9v@netaxs.com>`
- **References:** `<6r7fi0$9a7$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com> <35ED0C98.3512@iridia.ulb.antispam_remove.ac.be> <slrn6uq8uu.73n.rivie@rivie.daautah.com> <35EF1AC7.F1F9B3DC@plano.net> <6soqtc$smb$1@monet.op.net> <35F8FC5B.2B9544E1@hankel.mersinet.co.uk> <6ti5kv$kl7@netaxs.com> <6ti78d$r6m$1@marina.cinenet.net>`

```
Craig Berry (cberry@cinenet.net) wrote:
: That's actually a lot uglier than it needs to be.
: 
:   s/(\w+)/\u\L$1/g;
: 
: does the same thing more readably (at least IMO).
: 
: : Yes, I do realize how ugly this is.
: 
: As a general rule, when something easily expressible like "initial-cap,
: trailing-lowercase all words" ends up looking ugly as a Perl expression,
: it should indicate you've not found the best way to write the Perl
: expression.  "Making easy things easy," as the saying goes.

And as another saying goes, there's always more than one way to
do it!  (Not always true in Cobol.)

Walt
```

- **From:** NukeEmUp@ThePentagon.com (David Cantrell)
- **Date:** 1998-09-02T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.perl.misc,comp.lang.cobol,alt.folklore.computers

```
On 1 Sep 1998 22:03:03 -0400,
  mjd@op.net (Mark-Jason Dominus) enlightened us thusly:

>Stored-program computers, such as EDSAC and UNIVAC, were not even in
>the planning stages until 1946, and none was completed before 1949.

Oh of course, the Manchester Mark I wasn't merkan so can't have been
first, despite becoming operational in 1948.

This does, of course have bugger all to do with perl or COBOL.
Followups set accordingly.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Cobol Forever!

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** scm@enterprise.net (Shaun C. Murray)
- **Date:** 1998-09-03T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<6sm290$pj4$1@news.enterprise.net>`
- **References:** `<1998083106515800.CAA29316@ladder01.news.aol.com> <6sgtq0$g4j$7@news.enterprise.net> <35ECC32E.61946F2@att.net> <Pine.OSF.3.95q.980902151733.12544A-100000@octane.dasb.fhda.edu>`

```
In article <Pine.OSF.3.95q.980902151733.12544A-100000@octane.dasb.fhda.edu>, 
rrg10300@dasb.fhda.edu says...
>
>
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
>=> Bill Lynch


It's usually called a 'hash' symbol in english. Pound is ï¿½

I think the confusion comes about because it's above the number 3 on a UK 
keyboard where americans usually have a #. Old printers that only coped with 7 
bit character sets used to have settings to substitute # for a pound symbol in 
the UK so a file generated in the UK when printed on a US printer would give #.


>=> 
>>                              1 2
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>


A What?

>
>parms??? how about "sharp", "number", "lb"

Sharp and Number maybe but not 'lb'. I'll stick with the hash ;-)

Still, I wonder what fun where going to have with Euro symbols!
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Cobol Forever!

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** riplin@kcbbs.gen.nz (Richard Plinston)
- **Date:** 1998-09-03T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3298245.69598.1129@kcbbs.gen.nz>`
- **References:** `<6sm290$pj4$1@news.enterprise.net>`

```
In message <<6sm290$pj4$1@news.enterprise.net>> scm@enterprise.net  writes:
> 
> It's usually called a 'hash' symbol in english. Pound is  
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> the UK so a file generated in the UK when printed on a US printer would give #.

Actually the Americans calling this symbol (#) pound has nothing
to do with computers, or with english currency.  They have
used it as 'number' and 'pounds weight' for several decades.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Cobol Forever!

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** vbandke@ibm.net (Volker Bandke)

```
On 3 Sep 98 19:19:58 GMT, riplin@kcbbs.gen.nz (Richard Plinston)
wrote:


>
>Actually the Americans calling this symbol (#) pound has nothing
>to do with computers, or with english currency.  They have
>used it as 'number' and 'pounds weight' for several decades.


And dito for other countries.  In Germany we had a pound sign (for
weight, 500 g) like that, until the weight # was officially abolished
in the 1920ies


with kind regards

Volker Bandke
(BSP GmbH)
```

```
#   <--- hash

*   <--- splat
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Cobol Forever!

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** Rodger Whitlock <totototo+newsgroups@mail.pacificcoast.net>
- **Date:** 1998-09-03T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<1998Sep3.130858.10425@vmsmail.gov.bc.ca>`
- **References:** `<1998083106515800.CAA29316@ladder01.news.aol.com> <6sgtq0$g4j$7@news.enterprise.net> <35ECC32E.61946F2@att.net> <35ee0e13.1333633@news.pond.com>`

```
raygberg@pond.com (Ray Greenberg) wrote:
>On 2 Sep 1998 04:01:31 GMT, Bill Lynch <wblynch@att.net> wrote:
>
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
>(Either that, or a sharp--)


Bzzzzt! Wrong! The little criss-cross thingie is formally known to the typographers 
(at least some of them) as an "octothorpe" and it is *not* the same glyph as the 
musical sharp sign. Informal names include "hash mark" and "pound sign." 

The fine folks in comp.fonts will be happy to enlighten disbelievers.

----
Rodger Whitlock
```

```
Shaun Murray posted the following on 98/09/03:

  ---------------------------  begin quote  ---------------------

It's usually called a 'hash' symbol in english. Pound is ï¿½

I think the confusion comes about because it's above the number 3 on a
UK 
keyboard where americans usually have a #. Old printers that only coped
with 7 
bit character sets used to have settings to substitute # for a pound
symbol in 
the UK so a file generated in the UK when printed on a US printer would
give #.

  ------------------------  end quote  ------------------------
```

```
incorporate a date reference (or whose names are maintained in another file
keyed by date.)
```

#### ↳ Re: ok, heres one...

- **From:** Lookin'@You.2 (WDS)
- **Date:** 1998-09-11T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<35f872b9.6016704@news1.frb.gov>`
- **References:** `<35EA0537.9A5E33D5@mindspring.com>`

```
On Sun, 30 Aug 1998 19:06:47 -0700, Mike Ganas
<skidmike@mindspring.com> wrote:

>[...]how do
>you delete a file totally from cobol?  i can open output it and then
>close it, but that leaves the filename in the directory.
>well?

This is implementation specific.  I have seen such things as:
    CLOSE file-name WITH PURGE,
    CLOSE file-name WITH DELETE, and
    CLOSE file-name FOR REMOVAL,
all of which either violate the standard or are extensions.  Check
your COBOL manual to see what feature, if any, is implemented for your
compiler.  Also see if you can send a command to the operating system
(such as a DEL command in DOS) from your COBOL program.
```

---

<a id="t-e9ced33715"></a>
## Thread: Getting started in Cobol — 8 msgs

### Getting started in Cobol

- **From:** mjd@op.net (Mark-Jason Dominus)
- **Date:** 1998-09-01T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<6si9vt$amk$1@monet.op.net>`

```
I know next to nothing about Cobol, and I've always felt like it was
the most important language missing from my language bag.  I'd like to
fix that.

First: What's a good book to read to learn the language?  I've been
programming for about twenty years in many, many languages, so I don't
need a book about how to program; a language reference manual might be
the right thing if it were clear and well-organized.

Second: I was talking to a friend of mine about Cobol, and I asked him
to show me typical Cobol code to turn an input like this

	Columbus      Ohio
	Frankfort     Kentucky
        Cleveland     Ohio
        Trenton       New Jersey
        Louisville    Kentucky
        Cincinnati    Ohio

into an output like this:

	Ohio          Columbus, Cleveland, Cincinnati
	Kentucky      Frankfort, Louisville
        New Jersey    Trenton

But my friend didn't know enough to show me how to do it.

It seemed to me that seeing a Cobol program to perform this task might
give me a good sense of what the language was like.  (Maybe this is
wrong; I don't know.)  Perhaps someone here would oblige me by sending
me a program to do this, or some other simple, generic task.

Thanks.
```

#### ↳ Re: Getting started in Cobol

- **From:** carloss940@aol.com (CarlosS940)
- **Date:** 1998-09-02T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<1998090203384100.XAA09692@ladder01.news.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<6si9vt$amk$1@monet.op.net>`

```

```

#### ↳ Re: Getting started in Cobol

- **From:** "Kim Kj���rsulf" <kim.kjaersulf@cyberdude.com>
- **Date:** 1998-09-03T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<01bdd70f$526872a0$9c0110ac@PC0052.vejlehs.dk>`
- **References:** `<6si9vt$amk$1@monet.op.net>`

```
Hi Mark

I am the author of The COBOL-85 Tutorial, an e-book in HTML-format. Of
cause I think it is an excellent book. You will find the free part of the
e-book at this site: http://www.geocities.com/~e-academy/

 I would solve the task in your second question by using an index
sequential file in COBOL. Build a record structure with two fields: State
and city, make both fields part of the key and allow for multiple records.
This way the data will be sorted, then you can access the cities
alphabetically within each state.

Hope this will help you
Sincerely
Kim Kj�rsulf
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Getting started in Cobol

- **From:** riplin@kcbbs.gen.nz (Richard Plinston)
- **Date:** 1998-09-03T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3298245.69265.956@kcbbs.gen.nz>`
- **References:** `<01bdd70f$526872a0$9c0110ac@PC0052.vejlehs.dk>`

```
In message <<01bdd70f$526872a0$9c0110ac@PC0052.vejlehs.dk>> "Kim Kj rsulf" <kim.kjaersulf@cyberdude.com> writes:
> 
>  I would solve the task in your second question by using an index
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> alphabetically within each state.

Make the state an alternate key (perhaps this is what you meant)
but you may also have to cater for duplicate town names, for
example London.

While the problem as stated, with a handful of towns, is 
easy for Perl or AWK, or many other languages, where it
becomes a problem that Cobol would be used to solve is when
it is necessary to do the task with every town, city and
district in the US.  And then have a few hundred users
able to access this in whatever way is necessary.

While it may be possible for Perl to read every name into
and array and arrange it in a useful way it is then
within the memory of just that one task, every user
who wanted to know the state for a town would have to
repeat handling the whole file, this assuming that
the machine has enough memory and CPU time to do this.

In education environments all tasks are simplistic
because the projects are to be completed within a
reasonable time, and small data sets make the tasks
easy to grasp.  Unfortunately some tasks get much
more complex as the data size grows, and solutions
change.

I recall one discussion some years ago.  There was a
hypothetical requirement for dynamically building
input shortcuts for data entry.  The C programmer
designed a mechanism that relied on building
a binary tree in memory as the user entered, say,
customer numbers so that it could predict that
required.

Absolutely useless for any real world application
because it had not occurred to him that several
people (or hundreds) may be doing the same task
and each building their own completion table in
memory would make zero savings of keying.
```

```
I beleive that the record area for a file is static only while it is
open.  I did think about the green fellow's response and fundamentally
it is a good programming approach.  Do not rely on anything once a
datastream goes into an undefined situation EOF being one of them.

This is plain good practice for use multi language people :-)

Ken
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Help needed with COBOL program.

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** mark0young@aol.com (Mark0Young)
- **Date:** 1998-09-01T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<1998090105401300.BAA27248@ladder03.news.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<35EAADEF.E9F0185D@zip.com.au>`

```
In article <35EAADEF.E9F0185D@zip.com.au>, Ken Foskey <waratah@zip.com.au>
writes:

>I beleive that the record area for a file is static only while it is
>open.

On IBM mainframes without any additional specifications (viz, without the SAME
AREA clause), the record area may be a pointer into a buffer (buffer large
enough to hold a block of data), and there may be two buffers. The result is
that after each READ or WRITE the record area points to the next record in the
buffer or, if it was already pointing to the last record of a buffer and two or
more buffers were allocated, the record area pointer points to the first record
of the next block.

What happens at an AT END time is not defined, but the record area pointer may
end up being a "wild pointer".

For keyed files, the record pointer may also be a "wild pointer" after a random
operation that failed to locate a record (e.g., a START that fails, or a KEYed
READ with the record not found).

These are indeterminate record areas in addition to the "record area" being
indeterminate before OPEN or after CLOSE.

If one needs access to the same record area before OPEN, after CLOSE, at AT END
time, or after random I/O operations that fail to locate a record, the only
safe way is to use a WORKING-STORAGE area to hold the record image.

Now if one is dealing with spanned records (i.e., records that may span the
block or span a CI for VSAM files or FBA disks), a record area may be allocated
and remain static. However, I find it risky to rely on charactersistics
external to a program to guarantee proper operation of the program. (As a
Systems programmer, one of the changes I generally look at is  whether or not
it is feasable to redefine a VSAM cluster so that the records are NOT defined
as spanned. I have broken Easytrieve-Plus programs that way.)

Mark A. Young
```

#### ↳ Re: thane and mr. wolfe, you'll love this

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc123@bellsouth.net>
- **Date:** 1998-09-02T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<kXcH1.1188$eZ3.1392688@news3.mia.bellsouth.net>`
- **References:** `<35ECD25C.CF4E81D2@mindspring.com>`

```
Mike Ganas wrote:
>fujitsu cobol seems to have learned math from "outcome-based education"
>now am in the programs to print estimates/invoices.  (thane has seen the
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>this machine and i'm not afraid to shoot!

Mike, if you will simply structure your expressions so that the multiplies
are done before the divides, you will eliminate the vast majority of such
problems.  For example, when you are computing a percent, don't write it:

   COMPUTE Percent = Pct-Value / Tot-Value * 100.

but write it:

   COMPUTE Percent = Pct-Value * 100 / Tot-Value.

In other words, don't just write the expression dumb, *think* about what
the compiler is going to do with your expression, and how it is going to
evaluate it.  I learned this as a newbie programmer 30 years ago, and
have rarely a problem getting a COMPUTE to work as I intend.

This issue is a prime example of why programmers should know at least
one assembly language.  The compiler's task of evaluating an expression
using fixed point arithmetic, without overflow or undue truncation, is
not an easy one.  Having an understanding of how the expression will be
processed at assembly language level really helps you to articulate what
you want so the compiler will get it right.  I have always been glad to
have learned assembly language first.  It always pays to have a solid
understanding of what is really going on inside the box.
```

- **From:** Bill Lynch <wblynch@att.net>

- **References:** `<35ECD25C.CF4E81D2@mindspring.com> <kXcH1.1188$eZ3.1392688@news3.mia.bellsouth.net>`

```

>
(snip)
> Mike, if you will simply structure your expressions so that the multiplies
> are done before the divides, you will eliminate the vast majority of such
…[20 more quoted lines elided]…
> understanding of what is really going on inside the box.

My approach to this issue has been to use parentheses to make the
calculation clear, even when they are not required. Certainly agree with
Judson that it's important to learn and remember the compiler's rules
for evaluating expressions, arithmetic & otherwise.

Bill Lynch
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: thane and mr. wolfe, you'll love this

- **From:** "Donald Tees" <donald@willmack.com>
- **Date:** 1998-09-02T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<6skkvn$iop$1@news.igs.net>`
- **References:** `<35ECD25C.CF4E81D2@mindspring.com> <kXcH1.1188$eZ3.1392688@news3.mia.bellsouth.net> <35ED7031.C649FEAD@att.net>`

```

>Judson McClendon wrote:
>>
>(snip)
>> Mike, if you will simply structure your expressions so that the
multiplies
>> are done before the divides, you will eliminate the vast majority of such
>> problems.  For example, when you are computing a percent, don't write it:
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>>    COMPUTE Percent = Pct-Value * 100 / Tot-Value.
understanding of what is really going on inside the box.
>
>Bill Lynch
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>for evaluating expressions, arithmetic & otherwise.


I tend to agree with Bill.  I use redundant parentheses a lot, both in
compute
statements and in logical IF statements where there is more than one
condition.  While the order of evaluation is supposed to be set by a
standard,
I still find that it is an area that often causes problems not only within
particular compilers but also in conversions.  Specifying a specific order
of evaluation by using parentheses is a cheap (compile time only) and
simple surety that has the advantage of also documenting the order.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: thane and mr. wolfe, you'll love this

- **From:** "Leif Svalgaard" <leif@ibm.net>
- **Date:** 1998-09-02T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<35ed7a3c.0@news1.ibm.net>`
- **References:** `<35ECD25C.CF4E81D2@mindspring.com> <kXcH1.1188$eZ3.1392688@news3.mia.bellsouth.net> <35ED7031.C649FEAD@att.net>`

```

Bill Lynch wrote in message <35ED7031.C649FEAD@att.net>...
>Judson McClendon wrote:
>> Mike, if you will simply structure your expressions so that the
multiplies
>> are done before the divides, you will eliminate the vast majority of such
>> problems.  For example, when you are computing a percent, don't write it:
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
>for evaluating expressions, arithmetic & otherwise.


Bill, the issue here is not really parenthesis but the fact some compilers
will truncate the result of the division to an integer, parenthesis or not.
Multiplying first solves that problem. Knowing assembler or how the
machine REALLY works is not important; knowing what the
compiler does is.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: thane and mr. wolfe, you'll love this

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc123@bellsouth.net>
- **Date:** 1998-09-02T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<M4fH1.1354$eZ3.1473394@news3.mia.bellsouth.net>`
- **References:** `<35ECD25C.CF4E81D2@mindspring.com> <kXcH1.1188$eZ3.1392688@news3.mia.bellsouth.net> <35ED7031.C649FEAD@att.net> <35ed7a3c.0@news1.ibm.net>`

```
Leif Svalgaard wrote:
>Bill Lynch wrote:
>>Judson McClendon wrote:
…[19 more quoted lines elided]…
>compiler does is.


You are correct about the parentheses.  But what the compiler does is
generate machine instructions.  How can you understand that, if you
have no concept about how they work? :-)  The answer is that you must
depend on round-about descriptions which may or may not be correct or
actually address the issue at hand.  Without knowing English, I might
know, because somebody told me, that English had certain strengths or
weaknesses for expressing a given concept.  But that is a very pale
substitute for actually knowing English, and being able to evaluate
first-hand what the difficulties are.  I'll bet that very few COBOL
programmers with much assembly language experience would need to ask
why you would multiply before divide when handling fixed point numbers.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: thane and mr. wolfe, you'll love this

_(thread depth: 4)_

```
Leif Svalgaard wrote:
> 
(snip)
> 
> Bill, the issue here is not really parenthesis but the fact some compilers
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> compiler does is.

I didn't want to take this fork in the code, but I don't use the COMPUTE
verb - too many problems with truncated results, different
implementations, etc. My point in bringing up parentheses is that coding
them even when they aren't required is good documentation.

Bill Lynch

Who does not COMPUTE :-)
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Cobol sucks

- **From:** Arnold Sommerfeld <arnoldsommerfeld@earthlink.net>
- **Date:** 1998-09-01T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,alt.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<35EC0EB3.2AE98BF6@earthlink.net>`
- **References:** `<35EBDFD0.29E1A9F3@hotmail.com> <35ec1ae2.0@news3.uswest.net>`

```
Chris Osborne wrote:
> 
> Bad?  As opposed to what?
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> >

Not bad. Rather inferior.

As a general programming language in ANY area of data processing, business
included, infinitely inferior to PL/I. Just a couple of reasons (there are
many more):

1. Virtual absence of intrinsic functions. If one thinks COBOL has got some
in 370, take a peek at the array of functions available from PL/I.
2. Total absence of anything even reminding PL/I ability to work with the
operating system. 
3. Absence of a macro processor. Makes elementary operations like working
with files whose names are data driven a chore, if implementable at all.
4. Efficient string manipulation cannot be had from a language using
statements instead of functions to handle character variables.
    
In some specific areas, such as statistics, ad hoc reporting, and data
mining, COBOL is absolutely worthless compared to SAS. 

In the rest of the fields not mentioned above, it is a great programming
language with a hairy heritage.

Arnold
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Cobol sucks

- **From:** "Chris Osborne" <chris_n_osborne@yahoo.com>
- **Date:** 1998-09-03T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,alt.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<35eeaa77.0@news2.uswest.net>`
- **References:** `<35EBDFD0.29E1A9F3@hotmail.com> <35ec1ae2.0@news3.uswest.net> <35EC0EB3.2AE98BF6@earthlink.net>`

```
Arnold,

On a point by point basis of weaknesses versuses strengths, I have to agree
to most of what you say.

However, I've been working with SAS for a while now (6.0.8).  The
documentation from SAS is effectively worthless (to me, at least).  All the
manuals talk like the reader already knows the paradigm behind SAS data
manipulation, SAS dataset storage in SAS libraries, and just how SAS works
in general.  The companion book for my operating system reads like the
author(s) weren't sure what they were talking about (I'm sure they did, but
what they wrote down didn't communicate what I need to know)
Most commercial SAS books are about the same.
This is difficult, because SAS has weird naming conventions (an eight letter
variable name limit!), weird error and runtime reporting, and doesn't
operate on data like COBOL (obviously), or C, C++, or Visual Basic.  The way
I see it, SAS doesn't work like any other language, and this is bad when it
comes to matching what I already know against what SAS can do.

If COBOL is useless for Ad Hoc reporting (I wholeheartedly agree), then I
must point out that SAS is less than useless for batch processing of any
kind without jumping through hoops.  This because SAS must take data and
fully read it into a SAS dataset before the SAS software engine can operate
on the data.  Before anything else can operate on data processed by SAS, SAS
must write the data out of the SAS dataset into a normal OS (whichever OS is
being used) dataset.  This is a massive waste of time.  SAS is also an
enormous CPU hog (at least on MVS, it is).
I think, if SAS could get around the SAS dataset problem (I'm pretty sure
SAS personnel and other SAS devotees don't see it as a problem), SAS would
be more widely accepted that it already is.


As for Ad Hoc reporting in general, and the usefulness of 4GLs in getting at
data for such requests . . .
I've been doing some Ad Hoc reporting for about seven months now, and a lot
of this stuff is repeating itself, over and over.  Since most 4GLs use more
CPU power for the tradeoff of easier programibility, a program quickly run
and data quickly produced is often valuable.  But when you use the same 4GL
over and over for exactly the same question, that tradeoff starts to look
less attractive.  If the program had been written in a more efficient batch
language, all that extra CPU time wouldn't have been wasted.
Of course, a great deal of this has to do with business people knowing about
what they want.  They ask for something as Ad Hoc, decide they like it, and
continue asking for it the same way, generating no justication to assign
enough time to do the job right.  With one Ad Hoc report doing a good thing,
more do better, and so an avalanche of Ad Hoc report requests begin.
As for me personally, some of the requests require moderate programming
levels, and I don't know enough about SAS (and thus my gripe about their
documentation), so I use COBOL.
If the people who requested this sort of thing could only figure out either
a)that they'd need this sort of information more than once, or b)that it's
okay to ask for more development if something is going to be done
permanently.


Permanent Ad Hoc reporting.



Arnold Sommerfeld wrote in message <35EC0EB3.2AE98BF6@earthlink.net>...
>Chris Osborne wrote:
>>
…[27 more quoted lines elided]…
>Arnold
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL & SAS (was: Cobol sucks)

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "Paul M. Dorfman" <pdorfma@ucs.att.com>
- **Date:** 1998-09-03T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,alt.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<35EF2FF0.34851FE4@ucs.att.com>`
- **References:** `<35EBDFD0.29E1A9F3@hotmail.com> <35ec1ae2.0@news3.uswest.net> <35EC0EB3.2AE98BF6@earthlink.net> <35eeaa77.0@news2.uswest.net>`

```
Chris,

An interesting post! Let me stop at some points. But first, as it is a serious
professional talk now, let us change the name of the thread to 'COBOL & SAS'. 
> 
> However, I've been working with SAS for a while now (6.0.8).  The
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> Most commercial SAS books are about the same.

True, but not nearly as bad as IBM documentation. In reality, nobody begins to
learn COBOL using it; the same with SAS. There are several superb books written
by users. For instance "SAS: A Gentle Introduction" by Frank Dilorio and, at a
higher level, "Professional SAS Programming Secrets" by Rick Aster and Rhena
Seidman. 
   
> This is difficult, because SAS has weird naming conventions (an eight letter
> variable name limit!), weird error and runtime reporting, and doesn't
> operate on data like COBOL (obviously), or C, C++, or Visual Basic.  The way
> I see it, SAS doesn't work like any other language, and this is bad when it
> comes to matching what I already know against what SAS can do.

Eight letter limit is a serious shortcoming but it will become history at the
end of 1998 with the release of version 7. It is going to be 32. Also, the
maximum length of a character variable, currently 200, will be expanded to
32760. Error and runtime reporting is not 'weird', it is simply different from
COBOL. I find it really informative and easy to rely upon. As a general
programming language, SAS operates on external data (i.e. not SAS datasets or
views) the same way other languages do. For instance, when it reads or
writes/updates a flat file, VSAM file, DB2 table. Let us say, we need to read
16-digit account number (signed packed decimal, columns 1-9) and 50 bytes
address (columns 10-49) from a flat file whose DD name is FILEIN. We need to
squeeze all multiple blanks in ADDRESS to a single one, convert ACCOUNT into a
16-digit string, and swap the fields places as we write them into FILEOUT. Is
there anything a COBOL programmer cannot understand just by looking at the
program:

DATA _NULL_;                                    * do not create any SAS
datasets;
   INFILE FILEIN END=EOF;                       * identify input file, set eof
field;
   FILE FILEOUT;                                * identify output file;
   DO UNTIL(EOF);                               * loop until eof;
      INPUT ACCOUNT PD9. ADDRESS $CHAR50.;      * read record;
      ADDRESS = COMPBL (ADDRESS);               * process record; 
      PUT ADDRESS $CHAR50. ACCOUNT 16.;         * write record out;  
   END;                                         * pair DO with END;
RUN;                                            * close files, etc.;

Looks pretty much like a regular COBOL, PL/I, etc. program with understandable
syntax distinctions. Maybe there is anything convoluted or unclear in the
messages sent to the SAS log? Let us see:

NOTE: The infile FILEIN is:                              
      Dsname=USERIDZ.INPUT,                              
      Unit=3390,Volume=TST903,Disp=SHR,Blksize=27880,    
      Lrecl=136,Recfm=FB                                 
                                                         
NOTE: The file FILEOUT is:                               
      Dsname=USERIDZ.OUTPUT,                             
      Unit=3390,Volume=TST913,Disp=NEW,Blksize=27920,    
      Lrecl=80,Recfm=FB                                  
                                                         
NOTE: 10000 records were read from the infile FILEIN.        
NOTE: 10000 records were written to the file FILEOUT.        
NOTE: The DATA statement used 0.09 CPU seconds and 3718K.

I do not think so! And surely SAS is not much different from C/C++ in working
with data considering the fact that C/C++ is its underlying software.   
  
>................... SAS is less than useless for batch processing of any
> kind without jumping through hoops. This because SAS must take data and
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> enormous CPU hog (at least on MVS, it is).

Not SAS but programs written based on the concept you just described. That SAS
must create a SAS dataset before doing anything, is the most widely spread
illusion about this software. In the program above, did you see SAS create a
single bit of a SAS dataset? No: it just reads a file, processes it, writes it
out. In other situations, when SAS datasets may really come in very handy, they
can be replaced with SAS views; thus only a few bytes of a view definition goes
to a disk. 

SAS is a CPU hog? Granted, its i/o engines are not as fast as those of COBOL and
PL/I. However, it is not the main reason. The real reason is that many SAS users
are not programmers, and they simply do not know what the hell they are doing.
SAS is easy to use; but it is also immensely easy to abuse. Here in UCS, 90% of
SAS CPU time is consumed by 5% of the SAS programs. They are written and run by
SAS 'gurus' from marketing, credit policy, CFO, and so on. Imagine a COBOL
program reading a 60M-record file, creating a backup both on DASD and tape,
erasing the original, and ... that's all! Will it not waste resources? Yet this
nonsense is a vital part of those programs, and does it have anything to do with
SAS per se?  
              
> I think, if SAS could get around the SAS dataset problem (I'm pretty sure
> SAS personnel and other SAS devotees don't see it as a problem), SAS would
> be more widely accepted that it already is.

Now you know that SAS affiliation or even being a SAS bigot does not apply here,
since this problem simply does not exist. First, some manager decides that
someone who cannot ride a bicycle is good for driving a multifunctional powerful
bulldozer; inspired, the latter uses the bulldozer to pick up a six-pack; and
then the former, wondering why the fuel is gone along with the neighborhood,
blames the bulldozer for inefficiency and waste.    
 
> As for Ad Hoc reporting in general, and the usefulness of 4GLs in getting at
> data for such requests . . .
…[19 more quoted lines elided]…
> 
Agreed. However, more often than seldom, customers request minor changes to the
reports that run almost the same way all the time. A sophisticated
multidimensional report coded with PROC TABULATE may take maybe 50 lines of
code. Now, if another statistic is requested, or the customer wants to transpose
the report making its columns rows and vice versa and readjust the formatting
accordingly, it may take, grossly exaggerating, a couple of hours from pulling
it from production to moving it back in. On the other hand, completely rewriting
a 50,000 lines of COBOL... Lately, CPU time has been a lot cheaper than a COBOL
programmer's time. That is why, unless a report is absolutely static, we keep it
in production as SAS - in the long run, it is cheaper. Even though a well
written COBOL program runs faster that a well written SAS program,
anyway.                
Best regards, Paul.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL & SAS (was: Cobol sucks)

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "Chris Osborne" <chris_n_osborne@yahoo.com>
- **Date:** 1998-09-04T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,alt.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<35f00951.0@news3.uswest.net>`
- **References:** `<35EBDFD0.29E1A9F3@hotmail.com> <35ec1ae2.0@news3.uswest.net> <35EC0EB3.2AE98BF6@earthlink.net> <35eeaa77.0@news2.uswest.net> <35EF2FF0.34851FE4@ucs.att.com>`

```
Paul,

I had no idea that SAS dataset creation could be stopped.  The MVS companion
doesn't mentions this up front.  Neither does Mastering The SAS System, or
SAS Application Programming.  They all start out by explaining that SAS
_must_ create its own internally formatted datasets.  They might not use the
word "must", but it is presented in that context over and over again.  What
I've read so far insists that SAS datasets must be created.  Now that I know
I can bypass them, I will probably experience a change of heart about its
use.

I've saved you're examples out, and will be studying them.

I have never seen an example of SAS code done the way you have it, here.
Not in any book.

Typically, they have you doing the INFILE and INPUT as single lines, without
a loop, with iteration through the file records automatically provided by
the DATA step.  Honestly, I've never seen it done another way; and the
various books do not suggest otherwise.

As for IBM documentation, the latest manuals for OS/390 are superior to what
they once were, but could still use improvement.  In the old stuff, though,
the most valuable book I've found is MVS/XA Architecture Overview.  A clear
and readable explanation of how the MVS operating system works, even now the
book can still serve as an excellent introduction (it's short) to MVS in
general.
```

- **From:** Jeff Farkas <Jeffrey.Farkas@gte.net>

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,alt.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<6sp897$65b$1@news-1.news.gte.net>`
- **References:** `<35EBDFD0.29E1A9F3@hotmail.com> <35ec1ae2.0@news3.uswest.net> <35EC0EB3.2AE98BF6@earthlink.net> <35eeaa77.0@news2.uswest.net> <6smilu$21h$1@news-1.news.gte.net> <35eed432.0@news2.uswest.net> <6smofs$dal$1@news-1.news.gte.net> <35ef2928.0@news3.uswest.net>`

```
Chris,

           Thanks for the tip on the book stores. Here is some SAS code
you may like.

USE: My customers would use an application and
one of the byproducts was several data sets. The
end result would be a lot of data sets that
were no longer needed and needed to be cleaned
up. The following code did it very nicely.

DATA _NULL_;
  INFILE DSNLIST;
  FILE DSNOUT;
  LENGTH DSN $45.;
  INPUT @003 DSN_NAME $45.;
  IF INDEX(DSN_NAME,'SS5470.CG5470.P')
  THEN
      DO;
         DSN = "'"||TRIM(DSN_NAME)||"'";
         PUT @02 "DELETE "
             @09 DNS $45.;
      END;
RETURN;
RUN;

This is the first step the second is an IDCAMS
which executes the delete commands created by
the SAS step. Use this correctly and you can get
rid of hundreds of data sets in a few seconds.


Chris Osborne wrote:

> Jeff,
>
…[51 more quoted lines elided]…
> <<snip of many previous messages>>
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Cobol sucks

- **From:** redsky@ibm.net (Thane Hubbell)
- **Date:** 1998-09-03T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** alt.cobol,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<Jl0PnHJ5PvPd-pn2-jFkFKPfCYh4S@Dwight_Miller.iix.com>`
- **References:** `<35EBDFD0.29E1A9F3@hotmail.com> <35ec1ae2.0@news3.uswest.net> <35EC0EB3.2AE98BF6@earthlink.net>`

```
On Tue, 1 Sep 1998 15:11:47, Arnold Sommerfeld 
<arnoldsommerfeld@earthlink.net> wrote:

> 1. Virtual absence of intrinsic functions. If one thinks COBOL has got some
> in 370, take a peek at the array of functions available from PL/I.

Not true since 1989 - however only recently has the IBM mainframe 
world received these.

> 4. Efficient string manipulation cannot be had from a language using
> statements instead of functions to handle character variables.

This is simply ludicrous.  COBOL has SUPERIOR string handling.  I'm 
still waiting for someone to show me where it doesn't.  Even WITHOUT 
intrinsic functions to handle strings (and there are some cool ones 
now), string handling in COBOL is easy, simple and fast.

Give me specific examples of string handling where you feel COBOL 
falls down on the job and I'll attempt to educate you about how to 
perform these tasks simply and quickly using COBOL.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Cobol sucks

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** theodp@aol.com (Theo DP)
- **Date:** 1998-09-04T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<1998090404021200.AAA04361@ladder03.news.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<Jl0PnHJ5PvPd-pn2-jFkFKPfCYh4S@Dwight_Miller.iix.com>`

```
><HTML><PRE>Subject: Re: Cobol sucks
>From: redsky@ibm.net (Thane Hubbell)
…[24 more quoted lines elided]…
></PRE></HTML>

Don't have any PL/I references handy, but  but let's start with the following:

1. INDEX - Finds location of a substring within another string.

2. REPEAT - Repeats a character string.

3. PUT/GET STRING/FILE EDIT - Allows one to apply a PICture clause against any
field when retrieving/storing it into a character string or file.

4. VARying length strings - Automatically keep track of lengths of strings
(which can be returned by the LENGTH function).

5. Repeated INITialization to a desired value for strings, with replication
factors.

6. LIFO allocation of strings to desired lengths.

7. MACRO versions of string functions.

8. LOCAL/GLOBAL string variables.

9. Pointer addressing/Based Variables - Similar to DSECTs, what COBOL tried to
do with POINTER . 

10. BIT strings - Allows one to test bits instead of calling convolted
expand/compress routines.

11. VERIFY function - Returns location of a character in a string that doesn't
match specified values.

12. Concatenation Operator 

13. Other (BIT, BOOL, STRING, UNSPEC, etc.)

14. GET/PUT LIST/DATA provide additonal input and output of character strings
from/to file or character string (like DISPLAY/EXHIBIT NAMED on steroids).

15. User defined internal FUNCTIONs and PROCEDUREs with call by value or
reference.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Cobol sucks

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** redsky@ibm.net (Thane Hubbell)
- **Date:** 1998-09-04T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<Jl0PnHJ5PvPd-pn2-5r61Q1dmbiNo@Dwight_Miller.iix.com>`
- **References:** `<Jl0PnHJ5PvPd-pn2-jFkFKPfCYh4S@Dwight_Miller.iix.com> <1998090404021200.AAA04361@ladder03.news.aol.com>`

```
On Fri, 4 Sep 1998 04:02:11, theodp@aol.com (Theo DP) wrote:

> >On Tue, 1 Sep 1998 15:11:47, Arnold Sommerfeld 
> ><arnoldsommerfeld@earthlink.net> wrote:
…[18 more quoted lines elided]…
> >

> 1. INDEX - Finds location of a substring within another string.

You can find this using unstring.

> 
> 2. REPEAT - Repeats a character string.
>

Move All does this...

> 3. PUT/GET STRING/FILE EDIT - Allows one to apply a PICture clause
> against any field when retrieving/storing it into a character string
> or file.

Move with edit patterns. 

> 
> 4. VARying length strings - Automatically keep track of lengths of
> strings (which can be returned by the LENGTH function).
> 

Again String with pointer..  

> 5. Repeated INITialization to a desired value for strings, with
> replication factors.

What's a replicatin factor?  Move handles this.

> 
> 6. LIFO allocation of strings to desired lengths.
>

This one does take an intrinsic function - Function Reverse

> 7. MACRO versions of string functions.
>

Whassat?

> 8. LOCAL/GLOBAL string variables.
>

Supported using oddly enough - Global!

> 9. Pointer addressing/Based Variables - Similar to DSECTs, what
> COBOL tried to do with POINTER . 
>

Reference modification.


> 10. BIT strings - Allows one to test bits instead of calling
> convolted expand/compress routines.
>

Granted not there - but is this really string manipulation?

> 11. VERIFY function - Returns location of a character in a string
> that doesn't match specified values.
> 

Inspect handles this.


> 12. Concatenation Operator 
>

String handles this.

> 13. Other (BIT, BOOL, STRING, UNSPEC, etc.)
> 

Dunno what Unspec is, the FUNCTION of the others is easily simulated
as needed.

> 14. GET/PUT LIST/DATA provide additonal input and output of
> character strings from/to file or character string (like
> DISPLAY/EXHIBIT NAMED on steroids

Again - Move - but is this really a string manipulation function?

> 
> 15. User defined internal FUNCTIONs and PROCEDUREs with call by
> value or reference.
> 

COBOL can do this too.  Perform, Call etc.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Cobol sucks

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc123@bellsouth.net>
- **Date:** 1998-09-04T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<nWUH1.3766$gM3.2766800@news4.mia.bellsouth.net>`
- **References:** `<Jl0PnHJ5PvPd-pn2-jFkFKPfCYh4S@Dwight_Miller.iix.com> <1998090404021200.AAA04361@ladder03.news.aol.com>`

```
Theo DP wrote:
>
>Don't have any PL/I references handy, but  but let's start with the following:
>
>1. INDEX - Finds location of a substring within another string.


INSPECT ... TALLYING ... FOR CHARACTERS BEFORE INITIAL ...

>2. REPEAT - Repeats a character string.


Narrow application, not very important.

>3. PUT/GET STRING/FILE EDIT - Allows one to apply a PICture clause against any
>field when retrieving/storing it into a character string or file.


You only define the PIC once on the field; you may move to it many times.
Applying the PIC at the move is inefficient.

>4. VARying length strings - Automatically keep track of lengths of strings
>(which can be returned by the LENGTH function).


Can be expensive CPU wise (e.g. BASIC), and not generally useful for business
applications.

>5. Repeated INITialization to a desired value for strings, with replication
>factors.


INITIALIZE

>6. LIFO allocation of strings to desired lengths.


See 4 above.

>7. MACRO versions of string functions.


See 4 above.

>8. LOCAL/GLOBAL string variables.


See LOCAL-STORAGE, GLOBAL clause.  Available in COBOL for called subprograms.
It would be nice for subroutines, however.  This is the only feature you
mention which COBOL lacks, that is important.

>9. Pointer addressing/Based Variables - Similar to DSECTs, what COBOL tried to
>do with POINTER .


You call that an 'advantage'?  Wow!  How many man years have been shot chasing
pointer bugs in C/C++?  I grieve that they are adding pointers to COBOL, because
it will have an unavoidable negative effect on overall system reliability, and
is rarely useful in a business environment.  You know what causes all those
Windows GP errors?  Errant pointers.  Wow, I can't wait. :-(

>10. BIT strings - Allows one to test bits instead of calling convolted
>expand/compress routines.


Good feature, very seldom needed in business programming.

>11. VERIFY function - Returns location of a character in a string that doesn't
>match specified values.


Very narrow application.

>12. Concatenation Operator


STRING

>13. Other (BIT, BOOL, STRING, UNSPEC, etc.)


See 4 and 10 above.

>14. GET/PUT LIST/DATA provide additonal input and output of character strings
>from/to file or character string (like DISPLAY/EXHIBIT NAMED on steroids).


Possibly fractionally as useful and powerful as SCREEN SECTION, which is being
added to the next standard.  (Many compilers have supported SCREEN SECTION for
years already, and more already support the new standard. e.g. Micro Focus,
many others.)

>15. User defined internal FUNCTIONs and PROCEDUREs with call by value or
>reference.

CALL ... USING {BY REFERENCE/BY CONTENT} ...
provides equivalent procedures in COBOL right now, and the new standard
provides functions as well.  (Many compilers support the new standard now,
or already provide functions. e.g. Micro Focus.)
```

```
How about INSPECT ... TALLYING using reference modification moving from
the back end of the target string to the front? You can precede this with

rrg
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Cobol editor

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc123@bellsouth.net>
- **Date:** 1998-09-04T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<bfUH1.3668$gM3.2737217@news4.mia.bellsouth.net>`
- **References:** `<35EACCC8.14B5@popd.ix.netcom.com> <6sf2m2$12m$1@news02.btx.dtag.de>`

```
Andreas Strzoda wrote:
>Don Tarshes schrieb:
>
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
>I think, it is gone or do s.o. know anything more ?

I've used Brief for over 10 years, since well before Borland acquired it.
You are correct, it is an outstanding programmer's editor, often the
standard by which other editors are compared.  Any decent editor today
will provide Brief emulation of some sort.  As far as I know, it is still
available, but Borland has not improved it since acquiring it.  The only
problem with Brief is that it is still a DOS editor, and the world is
moving to Windows, whether we like it or not.  I have already bought
Multi-Edit, and am in the process of learning it.  It's traumatic, like
getting divorced and remarried! :-)
```

- **From:** areymond@csi.com (Alain Reymond)

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<35eba55f.4521750@news.skynet.be>`
- **References:** `<35EACCC8.14B5@popd.ix.netcom.com>`

```
I personnaly use Visual SlickEdit. Works under Windows 3.x, Win95, NT.
There are versions for DOS and Unix. I also use one under SCO. It has
all the fiunctionalities you may want from a good editor.
http://www.slickedit.com

You may also try "Zeus", a shareware available on the net. But I don't
think it recognizes Cobol structures.

Alain Reymond

>Does anyone know of a good cobol editor, PD or otherwise?  One sensitive
>to reserved words, correct column positions, etc.?  Thanks for the help.
>
>Don
```

- **References:** `<6r7fi0$9a7$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com> <6rldhj$na5@lotho.delphi.com> <6rr8ge$5a9$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com> <35E16365.CF280FEC@hankel.mersinet.co.uk> <6rs4dd$3ft$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com> <35E1ABCE.363550E1@cks.ssd.k12.wa.us> <6ru2dm$639$3@news.enterprise.net> <35E5CC6B.1467@min.net> <6sgrck$g4j$2@news.enterprise.net>`

```
On 1 Sep 1998 13:04:20 GMT, Shaun C. Murray <scm@enterprise.net> wrote:
>In article <35E5CC6B.1467@min.net>, jdporter@min.net says...
>>
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
>languages. Excess punctuation too. 

But his explicit goal was to minimize lines, based on your
previous challenge:

>Post your perl code and I'm sure the group can do it in less lines in COBOL.
>Then give it to your PCS and tell him to take a refresher course.  

Most Perl programmers would not write code like that for production use,
since it is difficult to understand.

#!/usr/bin/perl -Tw

@field_widths = qw( 8 12 15 20 10 );
use strict;
use vars qw( @field_widths );

while(defined(my $line = <STDIN>)) {
  chomp($line); 
  my @fields  = split(/\t/, $line); 
  my @formats = map { '%.' . $_ . 's' } @field_widths;
  my $format  = join(' ', @formats);
  printf("$format\n", @fields);
}                                

That's basically the same program, but may be easier to follow.
Some of us trendy folks like the excess punctuation so that
it's simple to differentiate data types.  
```

```
(I haven't understood any of the (really _very_ few) lines of COBOL
I've seen; does that mean COBOL is bad, or just that I should learn
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL and Perl

- **From:** scm@enterprise.net (Shaun C. Murray)
- **Date:** 1998-09-04T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.perl.misc,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<6sp3vj$ld4$1@news.enterprise.net>`
- **References:** `<6r7fi0$9a7$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com> <6rldhj$na5@lotho.delphi.com> <tgu32qv43y.fsf@noise.bsd.uchicago.edu>`

```
In article <tgu32qv43y.fsf@noise.bsd.uchicago.edu>, 
d-edwards@nospam.uchicago.edu says...
>
>Obviously if one doesn't know the syntax of a language, getting
>semantics out of a particular utterance in the language is going
>to be tricky; that doesn't seem to me to be peculiar to perl though.

Sure but designing a language that involves cryptic symbols which have to be 
learned rather than english words seems a bit silly to me. COBOL isn't 
prefectly natural but parsers weren't up to much back in the 1960's.

I'd hope that any modern language would have a better parser so you could 
express yourself in a more naturalistic way. 

>(I haven't understood any of the (really _very_ few) lines of COBOL
>I've seen; does that mean COBOL is bad, or just that I should learn
>COBOL syntax before trying to parse COBOL (for semantics)?)

I find that difficult to believe. I came to COBOL straight from BASIC and it 
didn't take me very long to undertand how to lay out a program in the correct 
sections. The actual syntax is simple. Usually you can just type what your 
thinking and compile it straight away.

eg. 

   perform test-data until data-rec is true

   move 10 to percentage

   display "hello"

   open input data-file
   read data-record
      at end 
        display "end of file"
      not at end 
        add 1 to rec-no
   end-read
   close data-file
   stop run

It's verbose but then so is English. The punctuation makes sense. No curley 
braces, semi-colons or colons in sight. I don't want to learn shorthand as 
only people who know shorthand can read it.
```

```
Yes, this is not what I thought.

Now I tried to use Arrow Up and Down keys in ACCEPT
and, you are right, they do not go up and down,
instead CR-UP simulates CR-LEFT and CR-DOWN simulates CR-RIGHT.

I guess, this is another item that should be on fixme list at FSC.
Or maybe there is a magical way to overcome this that I do not know.

Vadim Maslov
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Fujitsu COBOL problems

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** "Fujitsu Software" <cobol@adtools.com>
- **Date:** 1998-09-04T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<6sp5g3$mag$1@nntp2.ba.best.com>`
- **References:** `<35e63d5f.0@139.134.5.33> <Jl0PnHJ5PvPd-pn2-3deiP3XiTlQt@Dwight_Miller.iix.com> <35E8B83B.6D9183C2@siber.com> <Jl0PnHJ5PvPd-pn2-tjevzCou75OG@Dwight_Miller.iix.com> <35EB9314.AE782AF7@siber.com> <Jl0PnHJ5PvPd-pn2-dspgZqLvKmkC@Dwight_Miller.iix.com> <6smp6d$448$1@news.igs.net>`

```
The Fujitsu COBOL V4 CBL_Routines are documented in the sample programs.

The Samples\CBL_Routines directory contains sample programs, which
demonstrate the use and
functionality of the Fujitsu COBOL CBL_ routines.

There are 76 routines, and 35 sample programs.  Each routine is demonstrated
and well documented.

Although a given routine may be used to implement functionality in various
programs, each routine has one program in which it is primarily
demonstrated.

A list of each routine, and its sample program follows:


Byte-Stream File routines:
 CBL_CLOSE_FILE         ByteStream\ByteS.cob
 CBL_CREATE_FILE        ByteStream\Bytes.cob
 CBL_FLUSH_FILE         ByteStream\ByteS.cob
 CBL_OPEN_FILE          ByteStream\ByteS.cob
 CBL_READ_FILE          ByteStream\ByteS.cob
 CBL_WRITE_FILE         ByteStream\ByteS.cob


Filename Routines:
 CBL_JOIN_FILENAME      SplitJoinFilename\SJ.cob
 CBL_SPLIT_FILENAME     SplitJoinFilename\SJ.cob


File Routines:
 CBL_CHANGE_DIR         FileRoutines\ChangeAndReadDir\CDRead.cob
 CBL_CHECK_FILE_EXIST   FileRoutines\CheckFileExist\ChkFil.cob
 CBL_COPY_FILE          FileRoutines\CopyFile\Copyfile.cob
 CBL_CREATE_DIR         FileRoutines\MakeDir\CBLMD.cob
 CBL_DELETE_DIR         FileRoutines\RemoveDir\CBLRD.cob
 CBL_DELETE_FILE        FileRoutines\DeleteFile\CBLDF.cob

CBL_LOCATE_FILE
 Mode 0                 FileRoutines\LocateFile\Locate.cob
 Mode 1 & 2             FileRoutines\LocateFile\Environment\Environ.cob

 CBL_READ_DIR           FileRoutines\ChangeAndReadDir\CDRead.cob
 CBL_RENAME_FILE        FileRoutines\RenameFile\CBLRen.cob

 PC_FIND_DRIVES         FileRoutines\Drives\Drives.cob
 PC_READ_DRIVE          FileRoutines\Drives\Drives.cob
 PC_SET_DRIVE           FileRoutines\Drives\Drives.cob


Memory Allocation Routines:
 CBL_ALLOC_MEM          MemoryAllocationRoutines\Mem.cob
 CBL_FREE_MEM2          MemoryAllocationroutines\Mem.cob


Virtual Heap Routines:
 CBL_OPEN_VFILE         VirtualHeaps\VFile.cob
 CBL_CLOSE_VFILE        VirtualHeaps\VFile.cob
 CBL_WRITE_VFILE        VirtualHeaps\VFile.cob
 CBL_READ_VFILE         VirtualHeaps\VFile.cob


Operating System Information Routines:
 CBL_GET_OS_INFO        OperatingSystemInfo\OS.cob


Text Routines:
 CBL_TOUPPER            TextRoutines\Case.cob
 CBL_TOLOWER            TextRoutines\Case.cob


Logic Operator Routines:
 CBL_AND                LogicOperatorRoutines\Logic.cob
 CBL_EQ                 LogicOperatorRoutines\Logic.cob
 CBL_IMP                LogicOperatorRoutines\Logic.cob
 CBL_NOT                LogicOperatorRoutines\Logic.cob
 CBL_OR                 LogicOperatorRoutines\Logic.cob
 CBL_XOR                LogicOperatorRoutines\Logic.cob


Screen Routines:
 CBL_GET_CSR_POS        ScreenRoutines\Cursor\Cursor.cob
 CBL_SET_CSR_POS        ScreenRoutines\Cursor\Cursor.cob
 CBL_SET_CSR_SHAPE      ScreenRoutines\Cursor\Cursor.cob
 CBL_CREATE_SCR         ScreenRoutines\CreateScreen\CreatScr.cob
 CBL_WRITE_SCR_TTY_CHAR ScreenRoutines\TTY\Character\TTYChar.cob
 CBL_WRITE_SCR_TTY      ScreenRoutines\TTY\String\TTYStr.cob
 CBL_CLEAR_SCR          ScreenRoutines\ClearScreen\CBLCLS.cob
 CBL_GET_SCR_SIZE       ScreenRoutines\ScreenSize\ScrSiz.cob
 CBL_GET_SCR_GRAPHICS   ScreenRoutines\Graphics\Graphics.cob
 CBL_GET_SCR_LINE_DRAW  ScreenRoutines\LineDraw\LineDraw.cob
 CBL_ALARM_SOUND        ScreenRoutines\Alarm\Alarm.cob
 CBL_BELL_SOUND         ScreenRoutines\Bell\Bell.cob
 CBL_GET_VGA_MODE       ScreenRoutines\GetVGAMode\GetVGA.cob
 CBL_WRITE_SCR_N_ATTR   ScreenRoutines\N_CALLs\N_CALLs.cob
 CBL_WRITE_SCR_N_CHAR   ScreenRoutines\N_CALLs\N_CALLs.cob
 CBL_WRITE_SCR_N_CHATTR ScreenRoutines\N_CALLs\N_CALLs.cob
 CBL_WRITE_SCR_ATTRS    ScreenRoutines\ReadWrite\RW.cob
 CBL_WRITE_SCR_CHARS    ScreenRoutines\ReadWrite\RW.cob
 CBL_WRITE_SCR_CHARS_ATTR     ScreenRoutines\ReadWrite\RW.cob
 CBL_WRITE_SCR_CHATTRS  ScreenRoutines\ReadWrite\RW.cob
 CBL_READ_SCR_ATTRS     ScreenRoutines\ReadWrite\RW.cob
 CBL_READ_SCR_CHARS     ScreenRoutines\ReadWrite\RW.cob
 CBL_READ_SCR_CHATTRS   ScreenRoutines\ReadWrite\RW.cob
 CBL_SWAP_SCR_CHATTRS   ScreenRoutines\ReadWrite\RW.cob
 CBL_SET_SCR_TERMKEY    ScreenRoutines\FunctionKeys\FKey.cob
 CBL_SET_SCR_KEYFILE    ScreenRoutines\FunctionKeys\FKey.cob
 CBL_READ_SCR_KEY       ScreenRoutines\FunctionKeys\FKey.cob


Mouse Routines:
 CBL_GET_MOUSE_MASK     Mouse\Events\Events.cob
 CBL_GET_MOUSE_STATUS   Mouse\Events\Events.cob
 CBL_INIT_MOUSE         Mouse\Events\Events.cob
 CBL_READ_MOUSE_EVENT   Mouse\Events\Events.cob
 CBL_SET_MOUSE_MASK     Mouse\Events\Events.cob
 CBL_TERM_MOUSE         Mouse\Events\Events.cob

 CBL_GET_MOUSE_POSITION Mouse\SetMouse\SetMouse.cob
 CBL_SET_MOUSE_POSITION Mouse\SetMouse\SetMouse.cob
 WIN_GET_MOUSE_SHAPE    Mouse\SetMouse\SetMouse.cob
 WIN_SET_MOUSE_SHAPE    Mouse\SetMouse\SetMouse.cob

 CBL_HIDE_MOUSE         Mouse\ShowHide\ShowHide.cob
 CBL_SHOW_MOUSE         Mouse\ShowHide\ShowHide.cob


Keyboard Routines:
 CBL_GET_KBD_STATUS     ScreenRoutines\Keyboard\KB.cob
 CBL_READ_KBD_CHAR      ScreenRoutines\Keyboard\KB.cob
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Fujitsu COBOL problems

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** riplin@kcbbs.gen.nz (Richard Plinston)
- **Date:** 1998-09-01T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3298243.72797.1566@kcbbs.gen.nz>`
- **References:** `<35EB9314.AE782AF7@siber.com>`

```
In message <<35EB9314.AE782AF7@siber.com>> Vadim Maslov <vadik@siber.com> writes:
> 
> But clients who are starting with MF/MS application 
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> This is how ENTER treatment in ACCEPT becomes a problem.

I have generally used accepts of individual fields inside
a performed paragraph so that I can process each field
in turn rather than leave validation to the end of screen.

          MOVE 1      TO Field-Number
          PERFORM Get-Fields
              UNTIL Field-Number > Field-Count
          ....

       Get-Fields.

          IF Field-Number < 1
               MOVE 1    TO Field-Number
          END-IF

           IF Field-Number = 1
               ACCEPT First-Field   AT Field-Pos(1)
                PERFORM Check-Function-Key
               IF Key-Special NOT = "Y"
                   validate field
               .


Withing Check-Function-Key the 'Exit' key will set Field-Number
to, say, 99, the 'Back' key will subtract 1, a valid field
will add 1.  If 'Help' or 'List values' key is pressed this
is processed and Field-Number is left at the same value.

I have done forms processing systems in which there is a 
full screen accept but specific trigger points where
fields may be entered and validated.  For example a 
name and address may just be entered on the form or, at
the name field start a customer number may be keyed and
a function will then convert this into a name and
address.  This allows for forms for customers or non-customers.

In this case the CUSOR IS Cursor-Position is used to find
where the function key was used and to set the next position.

In the case of just a screen accept a simple table of fields
(built by processing the SCREEN SECTION entries to generate
a copy book perhaps) could be used to set next-previous
position.

As a sophistication for, say, file mantenance, it can be
useful to allow the user to set a 'start position'.  Often
a lot of records need to be changed in one simple way, with
just one field actually changed.  When the first is done
the user presses a function key to mark that field, then
on subsequent records the program puts the cursor there
for the user.

Easy to do using Field-Number technique above or using
Cursor-Position.

Also helped if there is an 'end' key without having to
tab to the end of all fields.
```

- **References:** `<#8ezVnT19GA.235@upnetnews05> <6skna2$5ii$1@nntp2.ba.best.com> <35EE22BD.A30F7F51@mindspring.com> <35edfaad.0@news1.ibm.net> <Jl0PnHJ5PvPd-pn2-QK6xnpRj2Z84@Dwight_Miller.iix.com> <35eec5d9.0@news1.ibm.net> <Jl0PnHJ5PvPd-pn2-oNsSUdFoeXZZ@Dwight_Miller.iix.com> <35eee4ab.0@news1.ibm.net>`

```
On Thu, 3 Sep 1998 18:49:12, "Leif Svalgaard" <leif@ibm.net> wrote:

> The runtime system I use is 0.5 meg for the installer
> and 1 meg installED. Am I missing something?.
> 

EVERY module used ANYWHERE is installed by the Fujitusu Rintime 
installer.  They have to do it that way.

But they do now provide excellent docs on what DLL does what.  For 
example, the runtime installer loads the btrieve dll's, but if you 
don't use btrieve you don't need them.
```

<a id="t-f070706502"></a>
## Thread: Fujitsu and ASM — 6 this month / 10 total

_Topics: [`Compilers and vendors`](topics.md#compilers)_

_Thread started [1998-08](1998-08.md#t-f070706502)._

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Fujitsu and ASM

- **From:** sff5ky@aol.com (Sff5ky)
- **Date:** 1998-09-01T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<1998090102373500.WAA03606@ladder03.news.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<35eb3374.0@news1.ibm.net>`

```
In article <35eb3374.0@news1.ibm.net>, "Leif Svalgaard" <leif@ibm.net> writes:

>why would you like to use ASM? and not C or C++ or even Cobol?
>what is the REAL reason for this question?
>
>

In my case, it would help standardize code between the PC and Mainframe.
We already have an Assembly language utility on the mainframe that we can 
pass parameters to identify the external DD name to read, where to find the 
record key and what data from the record to return.  The assembler routine
loads
the entire file into memory (Dynamically allocated to use only the amount
required to store the file), returns a status to indicate success or failure of
load, receives requests for data and returns matched data or 'ZZZZZZ'.
We have yet to develop an equivalent on the Pc and opted to use implementation
specific COBOL/ISAM file processes created as .DLLs for a short term solution.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Fujitsu and ASM

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** sff5ky@aol.com (Sff5ky)
- **Date:** 1998-09-03T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<1998090302235600.WAA05164@ladder03.news.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<6sfsl2$mdn@dfw-ixnews7.ix.netcom.com>`

```
In article <6sfsl2$mdn@dfw-ixnews7.ix.netcom.com>, "William M. Klein"
<wmklein@ix.netcom.com> writes:

>>
>
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Unfortunately, I am not interrogating the information of the file to be
searched.
I am passing the DD name, key field location, and return field location to the
external Assembler routine so that it can get access to the file.  The file is
never actually defined in the calling COBOL program.
The mainframe stuff is done in COBOL for MVS and Assembler.
Our PC workaround is done in FujiCOBOL for the primary Calling program and
the implementation specific DLL using an ISAM file access to reduce memory
requirements that would be imposed using the Assembler routine loading the
entire file intot memory.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Fujitsu and ASM

- **From:** "Leif Svalgaard" <leif@ibm.net>
- **Date:** 1998-09-01T00:00:00

```
writes:

>>why would you like to use ASM? and not C or C++ or even Cobol?

>required to store the file), returns a status to indicate success or
failure of
>load, receives requests for data and returns matched data or 'ZZZZZZ'.
>We have yet to develop an equivalent on the Pc and opted to use
implementation
>specific COBOL/ISAM file processes created as .DLLs for a short term
solution.
>

You can easily write (in Cobol) a program that emulates the behavior of your
assembler routine. Hiding the file access in a separate program is very good
policy in itself. As you point out it helps to 'standardize code' between
platforms.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Fujitsu and ASM

- **From:** AS-DATA@t-online.de (Andreas Strzoda)
- **Date:** 1998-09-01T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<6sh4g8$hd9$2@news02.btx.dtag.de>`
- **References:** `<6scbbk$kij$1@news02.btx.dtag.de> <35eb3374.0@news1.ibm.net>`

```
Leif Svalgaard schrieb:

> why would you like to use ASM? and not C or C++ or even Cobol?
> what is the REAL reason for this question?
…[14 more quoted lines elided]…
> >

   Fujitsu report me, that they have a bug in the CBL_CRATE_SCR
routine, they could not use color's it should be. I'll try to
fix it by my own and to create own input-routines.

I'm translating a trading-program (about 200 sub-programs) from
MF-COBOL 2.0 to FJC. In MF-COBOL I've used many
own written procedures in assembler to set colors and check
function-key's. But the normal ACCEPT in FC will not return
my function-key's as I expectl (example using ALT-E for ending a
Program or the TAB-key to open a sub-dialog, not SAA-conform
but *very* fast...) and even not interprete the description
of my variable.

I can explain it with a short sample (pls don't laught, it was written
in 1987 for RM-COBOL 74 and then MF-Cobol 2.0,
today will know better way's for doing that)

01 S-NR PIC S9(6)V99 VALUE ZERO.
01 W-PIC PIC X(10).
...
MOVE "S9(6)V99" TO W-PIC.
CALL "_ACC" USING S-NR
   C-LI C-POS W-PIC C-ATR C-FKT.

so get in C-FKT the code of the function-key entering
the field and control the color with the attribute C-ATR.

This method made it very easy for making a generator
for whole masks.

But in FJC I can't use this routine, and can't replace it
by a COBOL routine (what I could do with
the WINDOW-routines for making a pop-up window)

I need a way to use this call's,so I've not to replace
the whole mask-coding with PCobol or other
GUI-masks-programms.

Thanks in advanced for your good ideas

AS
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Fujitsu and ASM

- **From:** "Leif Svalgaard" <leif@ibm.net>
- **Date:** 1998-09-03T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<35eeb7d9.0@news1.ibm.net>`
- **References:** `<6scbbk$kij$1@news02.btx.dtag.de> <35eb3374.0@news1.ibm.net> <6sh4g8$hd9$2@news02.btx.dtag.de>`

```
Andreas,

There is a way of doing VERY low-level reading of ANY key.
The program below shows how. It calls a DLL, ETK2SYST.DLL
which you can download from http://www.etk.com

It may be *too* low-level, but if that is what you need.
I'll e-mail an executable and ETK2SYST so you
can play with it.

000100 IDENTIFICATION DIVISION.
000200 PROGRAM-ID.    TKEY.
000300
000400 AUTHOR.        LEIF SVALGAARD.
000500 DATE-WRITTEN.  98/08/08
000600     -REVISED:  98/08/31.
000700
000800 ENVIRONMENT DIVISION.
000900
001000 CONFIGURATION SECTION.
001100 SOURCE-COMPUTER. PC-WIN32.
001200 OBJECT-COMPUTER. PC-WIN32.
001300
001400 DATA DIVISION.
001500
001600 WORKING-STORAGE SECTION.
001700
001800 01  BEGIN-DYNAMIC-STORAGE       PIC X(4)   VALUE "<ETK".
001900
002000 01  KEY-HANDLING.
002100     02  13-BITS                 PIC S9(4)  COMP  VALUE ZEROES.
002200     02  KEY-NBR                 PIC S9(3)  COMP.
002300     02  PREV-KEY-NBR            PIC S9(3)  COMP  VALUE ZERO.
002400     02  KEY-VALUE               PIC S9(3)  COMP.
002500
002600     02  KEY-BITS                PIC S9(3)  COMP-5 VALUE ZEROES.
002700     02  FILLER                  REDEFINES  KEY-BITS.
002800         03  KEY-BITS-LO         PIC X(1).
002900         03  FILLER              PIC X(1).
003000
003100     02  A-KEY                   PIC X      VALUE "X".
003200     02  KEY-ARRAY.
003300         03  THE-KEY             PIC X      OCCURS 256 TIMES.
003400
003500 01  ETK2SYST-CONTROL.
003600     02  ETK2SYST-OPERATION      PIC X.
003700     02  ETK2SYST-FEEDBACK       PIC X      VALUE SPACE.
003800     02  ETK2SYST-ERROR-CODE     PIC S9(9)  COMP-5.
003900     02  ETK2SYST-NUMBER         PIC S9(9)  COMP-5.
004000     02  ETK2SYST-TEXT.
004100         03  ETK2SYST-TEXT-CHAR  PIC X      OCCURS 256 TIMES.
004200
004300 01  ETK2SYST-NOT-USED-FOR-THIS  PIC X.
004400
004500 PROCEDURE DIVISION.
004600 BEGIN-PROGRAM.
004700     PERFORM ENSURE-CALLBACK-INSTALLED
004800     PERFORM TRAP-KEYS
004900       UNTIL ETK2SYST-FEEDBACK > SPACE
005000     .
005100
005200 RETURN-TO-CALLER.
005300     EXIT PROGRAM
005400     .
005500 STOP-THE-RUN.
005600     STOP RUN
005700     .
005800
005900 ENSURE-CALLBACK-INSTALLED.
006000     IF A-KEY = SPACE
006100         ACCEPT A-KEY
006200     .
006300
006400 TRAP-KEYS.
006500     MOVE   10    TO ETK2SYST-NUMBER
006600     MOVE "SLEEP" TO ETK2SYST-OPERATION
006700     PERFORM CALL-ETK2SYST
006800
006900     MOVE "KEYS" TO ETK2SYST-OPERATION
007000     PERFORM CALL-ETK2SYST
007100     MOVE ETK2SYST-TEXT TO KEY-ARRAY
007200
007300     PERFORM SHOW-KEY-IF-ANY
007400       VARYING KEY-NBR FROM 1 BY 1
007500         UNTIL KEY-NBR > 256
007600     .
007700
007800 CALL-ETK2SYST.
007900     CALL "ETK2SYST" WITH STDCALL LINKAGE
008000         USING ETK2SYST-CONTROL
008100               ETK2SYST-NOT-USED-FOR-THIS
008200     .
008300
008400 SHOW-KEY-IF-ANY.
008500     COMPUTE KEY-VALUE = KEY-NBR - 1
008600     MOVE THE-KEY (KEY-NBR) TO KEY-BITS-LO
008700
008800     IF KEY-VALUE = 13
008900         PERFORM DEAL-WITH-13
009000     ELSE
009100     IF KEY-BITS  = 1  OR 0
009200         DISPLAY X"00" NO ADVANCING
009300     ELSE
009400         PERFORM SHOW-THE-KEY
009500         IF KEY-VALUE = 27
009600             MOVE "NO MORE" TO ETK2SYST-FEEDBACK
009700     .
009800
009900*WINDOWS SOMEHOW TREATS ENTER KEY DIFFERENTLY:
010000 DEAL-WITH-13.
010100     IF KEY-BITS NOT = 13-BITS
010200         MOVE KEY-BITS TO 13-BITS
010300         PERFORM SHOW-THE-KEY
010400     ELSE
010500         DISPLAY X"00" NO ADVANCING
010600     .
010700
010800 SHOW-THE-KEY.
010900     IF KEY-NBR NOT = PREV-KEY-NBR
011000         MOVE KEY-NBR TO PREV-KEY-NBR
011100         DISPLAY " "
011200         DISPLAY KEY-VALUE ":" NO ADVANCING
011300     .
011400     DISPLAY KEY-BITS " " NO ADVANCING
011500     .

…[61 more quoted lines elided]…
```

### Re: Cobol to C Converter, anyone?

- **From:** "Darius Cooper" <Darius_cooper@Bigfoot.com>
- **Date:** 1998-09-01T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<6sidt2$6ai$1@news.megsinet.net>`
- **References:** `<35eac54f.21262862@netnews.worldnet.att.net>`

```
Why would anyone have a standard whereby one wrote in one language and then
ran it thru a code-converter. Why not go straight to a compile? Perhaps if
the reason for such a standard were made clear, one could choose a tool that
would fulfill the desired objective.

If the standard has not basis in reason, then... I know what I'd do.


Ramone wrote in message <35eac54f.21262862@netnews.worldnet.att.net>...
>Anyone know of a good Cobol to C, or C++ converter?  I have a PC
>application to be developed.  I prefer to develop in Cobol, but the
>application, in accordance with my client's standards, must run as
>C++.  Any leads appreciated.
```

```
working with a run time error 114.    Uninstalling Visual Studio 6.0
corrects this problem.  I will post a resolution when I get it from
Microfocus.
```

```


On Tue, 1 Sep 1998, Gene Webb wrote:

=> 
=> Listen to the experts they will tell you what can't be done, and why.
=  Then do it.
=> 
=> 
If I recall correctly, 
**********************
Herman Goldstine told the story of spending an
afternoon with John von Neuman and Alan Turing. Turing spent the time
arguing that a certain design Goldstine and von Neuman were testing would
fail because of signal to noise considerations. Goldstine and von Neuman
were convinced and when Julian Bigelow, the chief engineer on the project,
came into the room, they told him that the design would have to be
changed. Bigelow wished that they had told him sooner since he just got it
working.


rrg
================================================
To do the impossible you must see the invisible. 
================================================
```
