## Thread: Identifying MF COBOL — 8 msgs

_Topics: [`Compilers and vendors`](topics.md#compilers)_

### Identifying MF COBOL

- **From:** "Raymond A. Biondi" <biondi@ncweb.com>
- **Date:** 1998-09-01T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<6sh8re$oi7$1@supernews.com>`

```
I have a client with data in ".dat" files with a "coblib.dle" library (?)
file.  A hex dump of the coblib.dle indicates:

Copyright (C) 1985, 1991 Micro Focus Ltd.

Any clues?

TIA.
```

#### ↳ Re: Identifying MF COBOL

- **From:** "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@aiservices.com>
- **Date:** 1998-09-01T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<fnWG1.185$I%.111260@news1.atlantic.net>`
- **References:** `<6sh8re$oi7$1@supernews.com>`

```
Look at any .LBR, .GNT, or .INT that was generated by the compiler.
A hex dump should show the version. My guess is late version 2.
My 2.5 Language Reference is dated April 91. My 3.2 has a 92 date
in the .DLE.
-------------------------------
Rick Smith
e-mail: < ricksmith@aiservices.com >

Raymond A. Biondi wrote in message <6sh8re$oi7$1@supernews.com>...
>I have a client with data in ".dat" files with a "coblib.dle" library (?)
>file.  A hex dump of the coblib.dle indicates:
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Identifying MF COBOL

- **From:** scm@enterprise.net (Shaun C. Murray)
- **Date:** 1998-09-01T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<6shder$klf$1@news.enterprise.net>`
- **References:** `<6sh8re$oi7$1@supernews.com> <fnWG1.185$I%.111260@news1.atlantic.net>`

```
In article <fnWG1.185$I%.111260@news1.atlantic.net>, ricksmith@aiservices.com 
says...
>
>Look at any .LBR, .GNT, or .INT that was generated by the compiler.
…[16 more quoted lines elided]…
>>

You have to be careful with this. The version in an INT/GNT or LBR is the 
version of the compiler that compiled it. A compiler vendor doesn't change 
the compiler that builds the product unless there is a very good reason as it 
introduces a variable into the final product. ie. is any bug in a particular 
release a problem with the original program or the compiler that compiled it.

COBLIB.DLE/DLL isn't a bad place to look for the real version though.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Identifying MF COBOL

- **From:** "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@aiservices.com>
- **Date:** 1998-09-01T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<Pc_G1.210$I%.219446@news1.atlantic.net>`
- **References:** `<6sh8re$oi7$1@supernews.com> <fnWG1.185$I%.111260@news1.atlantic.net> <6shder$klf$1@news.enterprise.net>`

```

Shaun C. Murray wrote in message <6shder$klf$1@news.enterprise.net>...
>
>You have to be careful with this. The version in an INT/GNT or LBR is the
>version of the compiler that compiled it.

Exactly, Mr. Biondi wanted to know the version of the compiler that produced
the application. So you look in the INTs, GNTs, and LBRs that were shipped
as part of the application.

> A compiler vendor doesn't change
>the compiler that builds the product unless there is a very good reason as
it
>introduces a variable into the final product. ie. is any bug in a
particular
>release a problem with the original program or the compiler that compiled
it.

With Micro Focus, the version (of the development system) used to produce
the "current" release (development system) is always a few builds behind.
For example, Micro Focus used 3.2.16 to build the 3.2.24 that I use. The
"very good reason" is that each release contains "bug" fixes and other
improvements.

>
>COBLIB.DLE/DLL isn't a bad place to look for the real version though.


But COBLIB.DLE doesn't give its version unless you run it, as in
RUN COBLIB.DLE. It is not likely that the client would have the RUN
command. Therefore, the next best place is the INTs and GNTs.
-------------------------------
Rick Smith
e-mail: < ricksmith@aiservices.com >
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Identifying MF COBOL

- **From:** "Raymond A. Biondi" <biondi@ncweb.com>
- **Date:** 1998-09-02T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<6sjg0i$pi5$1@supernews.com>`
- **References:** `<6sh8re$oi7$1@supernews.com> <fnWG1.185$I%.111260@news1.atlantic.net>`

```
Rick Smith wrote in message ...
>Look at any .LBR, .GNT, or .INT that was generated by the compiler.
>A hex dump should show the version. My guess is late version 2.
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>e-mail: < ricksmith@aiservices.com >

The did not distribute intermediate or other post-source files.  They have
.EXE.  That is why I used the COBLIB to try to make the identification since
the EXE files are quite large.

On another note, is the specification for the .DAT files published anywhere
(like the DBF for XBASE)???

TIA.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Identifying MF COBOL

- **From:** "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@aiservices.com>
- **Date:** 1998-09-02T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<J1dH1.230$I%.445015@news1.atlantic.net>`
- **References:** `<6sh8re$oi7$1@supernews.com> <fnWG1.185$I%.111260@news1.atlantic.net> <6sjg0i$pi5$1@supernews.com>`

```

Raymond A. Biondi wrote in message <6sjg0i$pi5$1@supernews.com>...
>
>The did not distribute intermediate or other post-source files.  They have
>.EXE.  That is why I used the COBLIB to try to make the identification
since
>the EXE files are quite large.
>
>On another note, is the specification for the .DAT files published anywhere
>(like the DBF for XBASE)???
>
The formats for the data files are published in the Micro Focus manuals.
I am not aware of any web address containing the formats.
-------------------------------
Rick Smith
e-mail: < ricksmith@aiservices.com >
```

### Re: Cobol to C Converter, anyone?

- **From:** riplin@kcbbs.gen.nz (Richard Plinston)
- **Date:** 1998-09-01T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3298243.24969.11630@kcbbs.gen.nz>`

```
In message <<35eac54f.21262862@netnews.worldnet.att.net>> Ramone@worldnet.att.net  writes:

>Anyone know of a good Cobol to C, or C++ converter?  I have a PC

I understand that there are some Cobol to C that do work in
that they produce code that will go through a C compiler
without generating compiler errors.

The resulting C source is not 'C programs' in that it is
still the Cobol program in C syntax.

Were you wanting a conversion to C++ that included a redesign
of the system to follow OO methodology ?

But the major problem would seem to be getting any resulting
executable to produce the same results as the original 
Cobol.  Either the C is going to attempt to use floating
point and lose precision, or long and lose significant
digits, or some arithmetic package that emulates Cobol
and lose readability and any point at all.


>application to be developed.  I prefer to develop in Cobol, but the
>application, in accordance with my client's standards, must run as
>C++.  Any leads appreciated.

I would have thought it would be useful to have it 'run as'
machine code.

Are you doing the design or working to an existing design
that actually is suitable for C++ ?
```

<a id="t-35aca9a30b"></a>
## Thread: Defensive programming — 2 this month / 18 total

_Thread started [1998-08](1998-08.md#t-35aca9a30b)._

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: COMP-3, S9(8)

_(thread depth: 9)_

- **From:** riplin@kcbbs.gen.nz (Richard Plinston)
- **Date:** 1998-09-01T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3298243.25745.12538@kcbbs.gen.nz>`
- **References:** `<35EB0D31.B651C570@IMN.nl>`

```
In message <<35EB0D31.B651C570@IMN.nl>> "COBOL Frog  writes:
> 
> There is no bit needed at all to distinguish between short or lon, fixed or float, signed or unsigned or whatever
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
> conversion instructions.

Silly boy, of course there is a need for 'a bit needed to
distinquish between short/long, ...'  The bits that are
needed, and do exist, are embedded in the instruction set
of the processor.  When the compiler outputs the object
code it is choosing the instructions appropriate for
the types: these instructions are appropriate because
they have the bit settings for the type and size of the
data.

If you analyse most CPU instruct sets you may notice
that many arithmetic instructions are just sets of
bits to work one way or another, single or double
word, signed or unsigned.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: COMP-3, S9(8)

_(thread depth: 10)_

- **From:** "COBOL Frog (Huib Klink)" <H.Klink@IMN.nl>
- **Date:** 1998-09-01T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<35EBB083.312211CE@IMN.nl>`
- **References:** `<35EB0D31.B651C570@IMN.nl> <3298243.25745.12538@kcbbs.gen.nz>`

```
Richard Plinston wrote:

> In message <<35EB0D31.B651C570@IMN.nl>> "COBOL Frog  writes:
> >
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> > The compiler knows the type from its definition.

8<

> Silly boy, of course there is a need for 'a bit needed to
> distinquish between short/long, ...'  The bits that are
> needed, and do exist, are embedded in the instruction set
> of the processor.

I just thought you were talking about extra bits in the data fields / operands. Now I understand you meant the bits in the
operation code.

>  When the compiler outputs the object
> code it is choosing the instructions appropriate for
> the types: these instructions are appropriate because
> they have the bit settings for the type and size of the
> data.

They need not to contain the size of the data. This size could be in the opcode and would need just one bit for simple
choices like short/long and signed/unsigned. But there are many m/i sets in the real world where the length is an operand
and not in the opcode. especially for string handling m/i's

>  If you analyse most CPU instruct sets you may notice
> that many arithmetic instructions are just sets of
> bits to work one way or another, single or double
> word, signed or unsigned.

May be so. I believe you. This is about the op.code of course

And BTW, I am not entirely silly.

The COBOL Frog
```

<a id="t-ca0b196576"></a>
## Thread: Bull DPS7000 & Y2K — 1 msgs

_Topics: [`Y2K and Year-2000 remediation`](topics.md#y2k)_

### Bull DPS7000 & Y2K

- **From:** "G. van Vlimmeren" <gvlimmer@hoogenbosch.nl>
- **Date:** 1998-09-01T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<01bdd57b$52f00fc0$2c050380@s70>`

```
Hi,

For all you Cobolprogrammers working on Bull DPS7000 systems:
We've tested a complete administrative environment on a DPS with critical
dates in the near future.
(like 1999, 29 feb 2000, etc...). The system itself worked like a dream.
Only problems we found were some date-related conversions in our own
printing programs, that were fixed within 1 - 2 hours max.

The Mainframe itself, working with a 'non-2000-proof' version of GCOS7
performed outstandingly. The only glitch we encountered involved changing
the systemdate back to the past, while rebooting. This problem was solved
easily and will be removed from the current '2000-proof' version of GCOS7. 

We understood from the testing department at Bull that not many mainframes
had undertaken this physical step into the future yet,

So, rest assured. It'll get the job done.
```

---

<a id="t-359c79c696"></a>
## Thread: execute subroutine — 2 this month / 5 total

_Thread started [1998-08](1998-08.md#t-359c79c696)._

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: execute subroutine

- **From:** "COBOL Frog (Huib Klink)" <H.Klink@IMN.nl>
- **Date:** 1998-09-01T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<35EBABCD.8822871C@IMN.nl>`
- **References:** `<1998082500584300.UAA19553@ladder01.news.aol.com> <1tGZ16mMk7i4-pn2-qn9lMVoHd2T9@dsm4merlin.iix.com> <Jl0PnHJ5PvPd-pn2-lL65fiPKTCmL@Dwight_Miller.iix.com> <6scrgb$7p4$1@news.igs.net> <35ea0e7d.0@news1.ibm.net> <35EB1514.A7B5204E@IMN.nl> <35eb1c53.0@news1.ibm.net>`

```
Leif Svalgaard wrote:

> In Win32 there is no WinExec (AFAIK).
>
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> >the ability to start the subprogram separate or as real sub-program?

Leif,

You're wrong and right.
Wrong in that it not exists. Below I copied the description for WinExec from
the Win32 SDK help.
Right in that it should not be used. The remarks says that CreateProcess is
preferred. That remark as at the bottom op my copy below.

How about the START.EXE?
To get a description just type START in a DOS box.

The COBOL Frog
------SDK copy follows---------------------------------------
The WinExec function runs the specified application.
This function is provided for compatibility with earlier versions of Windows.
For Win32-based applications, use the CreateProcess function.
UINT WinExec(
    LPCSTR lpCmdLine, // address of command line
    UINT uCmdShow  // window style for new application
   );

Parameters
lpCmdLine
Points to a null-terminated character string that contains the command line
(filename plus optional parameters) for the application to be executed. If
the name of the executable file in the lpCmdLine parameter does not contain a
directory path, Windows searches for the executable file in this sequence:
 1. The directory from which the application loaded.
 2. The current directory.
 3. The Windows system directory. The GetSystemDirectory function retrieves
the path of this directory.
 4. The Windows directory. The GetWindowsDirectory function retrieves the
path of this directory.
 5. The directories listed in the PATH environment variable.

uCmdShow
Specifies how a Windows-based application window is to be shown and is used
to supply the wShowWindow member of the STARTUPINFO parameter to the
CreateProcess function. For a list of the acceptable values, see the
description of the nCmdShow parameter of the ShowWindow function. For a
non-Windows - based application, the PIF file, if any, for the application
determines the window state.

Return Values
If the function succeeds, the return value is greater than 31.
If the function fails, the return value is one of the following error values:

Value Meaning
0 The system is out of memory or resources.
ERROR_BAD_FORMAT The .EXE file is invalid (non-Win32 .EXE or error in .EXE
image).
ERROR_FILE_NOT_FOUND The specified file was not found.
ERROR_PATH_NOT_FOUND The specified path was not found.

Remarks
Win32-based applications should use the CreateProcess function rather than
this function. The WinExec function exists in Win32 to provide compatibility
with earlier versions of Windows. For more information about how the WinExec
function is implemented, see the Remarks section of the LoadModule function.
In Win32, the WinExec function returns when the started process calls the
GetMessage function or a time-out limit is reached. To avoid waiting for the
time out delay, call the GetMessage function as soon as possible in any
process started by a call to WinExec.
```

<a id="t-7f57e58317"></a>
## Thread: Help needed with COBOL program. — 1 this month / 4 total

_Topics: [`Help requests and how-to`](topics.md#help)_

_Thread started [1998-08](1998-08.md#t-7f57e58317)._

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Help needed with COBOL program.

- **From:** riplin@kcbbs.gen.nz (Richard Plinston)
- **Date:** 1998-09-01T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3298243.27712.13976@kcbbs.gen.nz>`
- **References:** `<6sehc2$otl$1@news02.btx.dtag.de>`

```
In message <<6sehc2$otl$1@news02.btx.dtag.de>> AS-DATA@t-online.de  writes:
> 
> > With any Cobol I have ever used, this is not true. After an open
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> with the START command.

BTrieve ain't Cobol.  Some Cobol implementations can use
BTrive as the file handler when using normal Cobol verbs.
In these cases the BTrieve files behave as other Cobol
files and the run time adds the appropriate START where
required (as long as appropriate compile options are used).

It may be possible to make the compiler work in a non-standard,
non-Cobol way, but that does justify claims about what
<<Cobol>> does.

> 
> You have to set the key also if it is not the primary-key.

Of course, but that just evades to issue.  It is always
valid to read through to end of file after doing an
OPEN without an explicit START when using Cobol.
```

---

- **From:** scm@enterprise.net (Shaun C. Murray)
- **Date:** 1998-09-01T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<6sgsck$g4j$4@news.enterprise.net>`
- **References:** `<6s1je8$pcq$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com> <3298238.73522.9762@kcbbs.gen.nz> <6sgjdu$3rl$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>`

```
In article <6sgjdu$3rl$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>, osvaldo.lucchini@l-soft.it 
says...
>My problem is actually that of understand what a
>CALL "77" USING [CALL-PARAMETERS]
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>documented. At the current version they seem to be also unsupported.

I don't think so. The call statement above will call a program called 77 in INT 
or GNT code. It's not a runtime call that I've ever come across.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: MF ver 4.0: where I can find numeric

- **From:** riplin@kcbbs.gen.nz (Richard Plinston)
- **Date:** 1998-09-01T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3298243.73676.2323@kcbbs.gen.nz>`
- **References:** `<6sgjdu$3rl$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>`

```
In message <<6sgjdu$3rl$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>> osvaldo.lucchini@l-soft.it writes:

> > > I'm trying to port an application written originally with a RM cobol, and
> > > actually working with MF (rel 3.1) on HP-UX (ver 9.05), on an HP-UX (rel
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
> documented. At the current version they seem to be also unsupported.

But why are you assuming that they must be called routines that
are/were provided by MicroFocus ?  They look like normal
program calls, in MF there can be a file 77.INT that would
respond to that CALL, but it is more likely to be a user
written assembler routine, look for an RTS.BIN, or similar
that would have a jump table in it.  I used assembler
in that form many years ago.
```

<a id="t-ee3c485fda"></a>
## Thread: IBM mainframe COBOL - how to close an output file and reopen it with a different DSN — 1 this month / 3 total

_Topics: [`Mainframe, z/OS, JCL, CICS`](topics.md#mainframe) · [`Help requests and how-to`](topics.md#help)_

### Re: IBM mainframe COBOL - how to close an output file and reopen it with a different DSN

- **From:** Ken Foskey <waratah@zip.com.au>
- **Date:** 1998-09-01T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,alt.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<35EAB5A5.8C8D3914@zip.com.au>`
- **References:** `<6s0p5r$4v0$1@holly.prod.itd.earthlink.net> <6s7dfq$31b$1@ash.prod.itd.earthlink.net> <6s9f25$cin@dfw-ixnews10.ix.netcom.com> <35ea94fc.773118@news.pnc.com.au>`

```
This is from the proposed standard and not in the currect MVS release.

Here is a generic description on how to do it without the standard
changes.

In MVS a DDName is a generic name that is associated with a real
filename (dataset name).  It is indeed static and for writing can only
really be one dataset.  In order to get the Dataset name to apparently
change to the cobol program there is an assembler trick that calls
dynalloc (look up your assembler modules book).

Your cobol program would look something like this (assuming you have two
assembler routines allocate and dealloc)

perform until all files processed

   call allocate using ddname, dataset
   open output ddname
      do your thing...
   close ddname
   call dealloc using ddname
end-perform

You do not actually need the dealloc if you use free=close (but it is
all done in assembler).

Has anyone got a copy of alloc and dealloc that they are prepared to
share. Mine are truly awful versions that only just work!

Hope this helps
Ken

Engelbrecht Computer Services Pty Ltd wrote:
> 
> Do you have an example of this dynamic name change.  How does this
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

## Thread: zero totals — 1 this month / 2 total

_Thread started [1998-08](1998-08.md#t-94be108f6d)._

#### ↳ Re: zero totals

- **From:** "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@aiservices.com>
- **Date:** 1998-09-01T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** alt.cobol,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<O0%G1.211$I%.219869@news1.atlantic.net>`
- **References:** `<35EB4B2C.3257@swbell.net> <6sfmv6$1oa@sjx-ixn5.ix.netcom.com>`

```

William M. Klein wrote in message <6sfmv6$1oa@sjx-ixn5.ix.netcom.com>...
>I haven't studied the attached code yet - but this person clearly
identified
>that they are doing homework AND they showed their work.  Can someone help?
>
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>
[...]
>
>Nick Woroniak wrote in message <35EB4B2C.3257@swbell.net>...
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>>prevent the lines from printing under those conditions.

Insert a conditional. Put IF whatever-TOTAL NOT EQUAL ZERO
before the print statements and END-IF after. Be sure to remove
periods from the end of the included lines.

It appears the changes, for this program, should go in
2300-END-DEPARTMENT and 3000-END-DIVISION.

It also appears that 3000-END-DIVISION is not in a "good" sequence.
IMO, the structure, of the program, would be clearer if you had used
something like 1x00 for all DIVISION processing, 2x00 for all
DEPARTMENT processing, and 3x00 for all SSN processing.
This would allow the processing for each of the control levels to be
near each other. Just a comment for the future!

[code snipped ...]

HTH, without helping TOO much!
-------------------------------
Rick Smith
e-mail: < ricksmith@aiservices.com >
```

>"Oh, it detected an incompetent operator. Say, what was your username?"
><clickety-click>


That is the current method.  Unfortunately *I* am the BOFH (whatever
that is).  I hit them with a $120 charge each time, but it is still a real
pain in the ass.  (I say "run the power failure recovery from the main
menu".  Then I hang up and mail the bill.)
```

Donald,

BOFH = Bastard Operator From Hell.  A series of humous--essays,
almost--rapidly proceeding to legendary status, about a nasty, nasty,
computer operator who can't tolerater losers, management, or pretty much
anyone but himself.  See Datamation Magazine's Net Humor page for a complete
compilation.

BTW, your response should have included looking up their userid and deleting
them from your system, followed by a quick modification of FBI files to
change their status to one of America's Most Wanted (itself followed rapidly
by the arrival of a SWAT team to eliminate the offender).


Donald Tees wrote in message <6sn3lg$dks$1@news.igs.net>...

##### ↳ ↳ Micro Focus NetExpress Status Codes (was Re: Microsoft NetExpress and File Status codes

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1998-09-02T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<6sk399$mq7@dfw-ixnews8.ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<35e4f286.0@139.134.5.33> <Jl0PnHJ5PvPd-pn2-WClQg8tjdcVU@Dwight_Miller.iix.com>`

```
I have received several replies from Micro Focus (not Microsoft!) about the
availability of getting MS compatible or non-ANS85 file status codes with
the NetExpress (32-bit) product.  The following is a reply that I have been
told that I could attribute to "Product Management at Micro Focus".

"Looks like we can tell the customer that it's a go for NetExpress V3.0.
GA in early December."

(but that it is impossible to retrofit back into the V2.0 product - even
thought the documentation says that it is there - and it is there in the
older 16-bit products)


Thane Hubbell wrote in message ...
>On Thu, 27 Aug 1998 05:47:48, "Robert Kovacic" <rjk@sbsoft.com.au>
>wrote:
>
>> We are evaluating NetExpress version 2.0 and have come across the
following
>> problem. Since we would be converting from Microsoft COBOL we want the
>> Microsoft COBOL V2 file status codes returned rather than the ANS85
codes.
>> The NetExpress documentation (the File Handler book) confirms that this
can
>> be done by setting two program directives viz. MS"2" and NOANS85. (BTW
these
>> are the same directives we use in our current Microsoft COBOL 4.5
compiler).
>>
>> We have done this but the ANS85 file status codes are still returned. We
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Micro Focus NetExpress Status Codes (was Re: Microsoft NetExpress and File Status codes

- **From:** Vadim Maslov <vadik@siber.com>
- **Date:** 1998-09-03T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<35EF160E.5AE9228@siber.com>`
- **References:** `<35e4f286.0@139.134.5.33> <Jl0PnHJ5PvPd-pn2-WClQg8tjdcVU@Dwight_Miller.iix.com> <6sk399$mq7@dfw-ixnews8.ix.netcom.com>`

```
William M. Klein wrote:
> 
> I have received several replies from Micro Focus (not Microsoft!) about the
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
> older 16-bit products)

We have a tool (written at request of a customer in 
just 2 days using CobolTransformer) that 
    adds calls to a Cobol Program that converts 
    MS IO status codes to MF IO status codes.
The calls are added immediately after each IO statement
that works on a file with FILE STATUS clause.

Example of this automatic conversion:

< 	   OPEN LOCKING IS EXCLUSIVE I-O MASTER-FILE.
< 	   OPEN LOCKING IS EXCLUSIVE I-O TRANS-FILE.
---
> 	   OPEN LOCKING IS EXCLUSIVE I-O MASTER-FILE
>            CALL "CBL_TRANSL_IO_STATUS" USING WS77-MASTER-STATUS
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> 	                                           .

Here SELECT for MASTER-FILE has FILE STATUS IS WS77-MASTER-STATUS clause.

So if you use our tool to convert your MS programs to MF,
you do not have to wait for the next version of NexExpress
that has the fix and/or pay for upgrade.

Only the lines that need to be converted are changed.
All other lines in a program are left intact -- so
there is no "lots of generated code" to deal with.

Conversion also happens in the copybooks (if they were 
included from Procedure Division and have IO statements). 

For more information please contact info@siber.com.

Regards,
Vadim Maslov
```

<a id="t-ea13d3b010"></a>
## Thread: simple file-opening problem — 9 msgs

### simple file-opening problem

- **From:** Theo van Brederode <see@reply.to>
- **Date:** 1998-09-02T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<35EDAD6E.6A6148E@reply.to>`

```
Hi all,

I'm having a problem opening a plain ascii sequential file in this
program:

000010 Environment Division.
000011 Input-output Section.
000012 File-control.
000013	  Select mut
000014		  assign to "mut4".
000017	  Select overz
000018		  assign to "mut5".

When I run the program (no compiling error), MS-COBOL  says:
ERROR accessing file: mut4
HLB030 Segment RT : ERROR 013 at COBOL 01B8

The mut-4 file is in the same directory as the hlb030.exe.

What's wrong with it?

Kind regards, Theo
```

#### ↳ Re: simple file-opening problem

- **From:** "Donald Tees" <donald@willmack.com>
- **Date:** 1998-09-02T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<6skv8j$mj4$1@news.igs.net>`
- **References:** `<35EDAD6E.6A6148E@reply.to>`

```
MS-Cobol uses the file identification to link to
data names. Try the following change:

Theo van Brederode wrote in message <35EDAD6E.6A6148E@reply.to>...
>Hi all,
>
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
>000018   assign to DISK.

FD MUT
                VALUE OF FILE-ID IS "mut4"
                DATA RECORD IS ...
```

#### ↳ Re: simple file-opening problem

- **From:** jcj0347@aol.com (JCJ0347)
- **Date:** 1998-09-03T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<1998090302183700.WAA00721@ladder01.news.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<35EDAD6E.6A6148E@reply.to>`

```
>000010 Environment Division.
>000011 Input-output Section.
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
>What's wrong with it?

It would really help to know what run-time error 013 is for
your system.  

Is the file OPEN command at offset 01B8?  Or does the error
occur when you try to READ the file?

Wild-ass-guess:  Try adding ORGANIZATION IS LINE SEQUENTIAL to the select.

Is mut5 similar and if so does it open OK?
James
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: simple file-opening problem

- **From:** Theo van Brederode <see@reply.to>
- **Date:** 1998-09-03T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<35EEF439.3B91DBF9@reply.to>`
- **References:** `<35EDAD6E.6A6148E@reply.to> <1998090302183700.WAA00721@ladder01.news.aol.com>`

```
JCJ0347 wrote:

> >ERROR accessing file: mut4
> Wild-ass-guess:  Try adding ORGANIZATION IS LINE SEQUENTIAL to the select.

Hey James, it seems you hit the jackpot!

 > Is mut5 similar and if so does it open OK?

Sorry for being incomplete, mut5 is an output-file. 
OK, the mut4 file can be accessed now with the ORGANIZATION statement,
and some output does occur in mut5.

Thank you all for giving this a tought!

Kind regards, Theo
```

…[20 more quoted lines elided]…

current directory.
```

## Thread: Fujitsu COBOL is Waperware, and there reps are toads! — 3 msgs

_Topics: [`Compilers and vendors`](topics.md#compilers)_

### Fujitsu COBOL is Waperware, and there reps are toads!

- **From:** "Garry Dell" <Garry_dell@hotmail.com>
- **Date:** 1998-09-02T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<uDhfKdu19GA.169@upnetnews03>`

```
I just got back from Dev Days where one of the sponsers was of all
companies:  (FUJITSU)  they proadly displayed the GOT COBOL banner and
lititure all around.  there was only one person at the information booth.
When I went there to inquire as to the current status of several aspects of
COBOL this person didn' even know what COBOL was!!!


HELLO!!!  If you plan to have someone giving verbal information, at least
make sure thewy KNOW what it is they're supposed to be doing.


Another case of Fujitsu's Vaporware and the complete disreguard for the
consumer.  (hey we ultimately pay your bills)




any comments would be welcome.




I openly invite anyone from the Fujitsu Corperation to defend their
practices.  I am of the opinoin that I will not probably purchase anything
friom a company that doesn't even stand behind their own products.  EVEN at
a show they help to sponser!!!!!
```

#### ↳ Re: Fujitsu COBOL is Waperware, and there re

- **From:** riplin@kcbbs.gen.nz (Richard Plinston)
- **Date:** 1998-09-03T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3298245.67982.237@kcbbs.gen.nz>`
- **References:** `<uDhfKdu19GA.169@upnetnews03>`

```
In message <<uDhfKdu19GA.169@upnetnews03>> "Garry Dell" <Garry_dell@hotmail.com> writes:
> companies:  (FUJITSU)  they proadly displayed the GOT COBOL banner and
> lititure all around.  there was only one person at the information booth.
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> consumer.  (hey we ultimately pay your bills)

Fujitsu Cobol is _not_ vaporware.  I have CDs of versions 1,
2, 3 and 4.  The first dated 1996.  Vaporware is a product
that is not available.

Fujitsu are a large company that sells far more than Cobol,
one person would not know all the products, or even all
the hardware systems.

I don't know why you have a chip on your shoulder, but why
do you think that getting their free starter set will
'ultimately pay their bills' ?

they are very good and very responsive.

#### ↳ Re: Question.

- **From:** abennett@primenet.com (Allen J. Bennett)
- **Date:** 1998-09-02T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<35edd7c6.117657333@news.primenet.com>`

```
On Wed, 02 Sep 1998 18:07:01 -0500, Angela Morrison
<armorri@midkan.com> wrote:

>I just want to know what exactly COBOL stands for.  It's for a homework
>assignment.  If you can help me, I'd really appreciate it.
>
>Thanks

COmmon Business Oriented Language

Allen J. Bennett

#### ↳ Re: Question.

- **From:** "WOB" <wobconsult@sprynet.com>
- **Date:** 1998-09-02T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<6skn5v$mdk$1@juliana.sprynet.com>`
- **References:** `<35EDCF95.6AE1@midkan.com>`

```
COmmon Business Oriented Language. Often called the mother of COBOL is
Admiral Grace Hopper (deceased) of the US Navy. A truly remarkable woman.
Knowing her background would also benefit your assignment and show
initiative to your instructor (which can't hurt). Try a search at ===>
http://www.yahoo.com/.

Cheers,

WOB

## Thread: Environment Variables... — 2 msgs

### Environment Variables...

- **From:** paulr@bix.com (paulr)
- **Date:** 1998-09-02T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<6sifqd$klf@lotho.delphi.com>`

```

Anyone have a pointer on how to get IBM Visualage COBOL
to go out and retrieve an environment variable? 
I'm definately spoiled. 

   accept variable-name from environment "env-var-name"

Works fine in AcuCobol, but that is of course, a 
proprietary extension. 

-Paul
```

#### ↳ Re: Environment Variables...

- **From:** AS-DATA@t-online.de (Andreas Strzoda)
- **Date:** 1998-09-02T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<6sit89$grd$2@news01.btx.dtag.de>`
- **References:** `<6sifqd$klf@lotho.delphi.com>`

```
paulr schrieb:

> Anyone have a pointer on how to get IBM Visualage COBOL
> to go out and retrieve an environment variable?
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> -Paul

In fujitsu it goes like this:

 SPECIAL-NAMES.
    ENVIRONMENT-NAME  IS ENV-NAME
    ENVIRONMENT-VALUE IS ENV-VALUE.
...
     DISPLAY "comspec" UPON ENV-NAME.
     ACCEPT  VAR-VALUE        FROM ENV-VALUE
             ON EXCEPTION MOVE SPACE TO VAR-VALUE.

so VAR-VALUE is empty or contents the COMSPEC-environment
Greatings

AS
```

<a id="t-9290085ef1"></a>
## Thread: DCLGEN Utility for SQL Server 6.5 or NetExpress — 2 msgs

_Topics: [`Databases and SQL`](topics.md#databases)_

### DCLGEN Utility for SQL Server 6.5 or NetExpress

- **From:** "Gene Webb" <gene.webb@newsguy.com>
- **Date:** 1998-09-02T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<6skcj9$nrp@enews3.newsguy.com>`

```
Does anyone know of a dclgen utility for SQL Server?  I have one that was
generate I thought from NetExpress but if it was I can't figure out how it
was generated?
```

#### ↳ Re: DCLGEN Utility for SQL Server 6.5 or NetExpress

- **From:** "David Sands" <davs@mfltd.co.uk>
- **Date:** 1998-09-03T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<6sm0h6$ikp@hyperion.mfltd.co.uk>`
- **References:** `<6skcj9$nrp@enews3.newsguy.com>`

```
Hi Gene,

The OpenESQL Assistant will generate your Host Variable declarations
(DCLGENs).

You can do it as follows:-

- Start up the Assistant from the View menu option.
- Connect to the relevant data source and then build the required Query
- Go to the Auxiliary Code tab and select the Host Variable check box
- Go to your source code working storage
- Press the Insert Source code button on the assistant. This will create a
CPY with the DCLGENs and also put a EXEC INCLUDE in the source code.

You can also use the assistant to insert the SQL , CONNECTS code into your
program as well.

Hope this helps
David.


Gene Webb wrote in message <6skcj9$nrp@enews3.newsguy.com>...
>Does anyone know of a dclgen utility for SQL Server?  I have one that was
>generate I thought from NetExpress but if it was I can't figure out how it
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

_Topics: [`Compilers and vendors`](topics.md#compilers) · [`Databases and SQL`](topics.md#databases)_

### Microfocus Cobol v4.0.26 and Embedded SQL ver2.1

- **From:** shaun.t@usa.net (shaun thompson)
- **Date:** 1998-09-02T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<35ed6280.29398482@news.iafrica.com>`

```
We are having problems with cursors being closed by SQL server after
they have been opened and an initial fetch has been executed.
The problem only arises during animation, if the application is
compiled and linked to an EXE, it executes without any problems.

These applications have been animated before. We are unable to
pinpoint the cause of this problem.

Please post any suggestions.

Many thanks
Shaun
```

#### ↳ Re: Microfocus Cobol v4.0.26 and Embedded SQL ver2.1

- **From:** "Gene Webb" <gene.webb@newsguy.com>
- **Date:** 1998-09-02T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<6sk5ci$mha@enews1.newsguy.com>`
- **References:** `<35ed6280.29398482@news.iafrica.com>`

```
Is it possible that you are doing a commit after the initial fetch? This
will cause the cursor to be closed.
```

## Thread: small brain boost please-not necessarily cobol — 2 msgs

### small brain boost please-not necessarily cobol

- **From:** suzie
- **Date:** 1998-09-02T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<6sjouk$mkl@pdrn.zippo.com>`

```
i have a file (several, actually) that i need to extract 11 fields from. i wrote
the fileaid to extract them and that works fine. But (isn't there always one of
these?), i need to convert one of the fields from 6 byte julian (998245=sep 02,
1998) to, well, what's in my equals in my example. i also need to look at two
other fields that i am extracting, and see if they are empty. if they are empty,
i need to look at two non-extracted fields and do some computations.
so, my question is this:
what's the most efficient way to go about this?? we are an mvs shop, i can use
sas or utilities or........ and, for that matter, what order do i want to go in?
these files are 1/2 million records and up on a flat file.
thanks for any input.
suzie
higgish@dhfs.state.wi.us
```

#### ↳ Re: small brain boost please-not necessarily cobol

- **From:** "Leif Svalgaard" <leif@ibm.net>
- **Date:** 1998-09-02T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<35ed6d9e.0@news1.ibm.net>`
- **References:** `<6sjouk$mkl@pdrn.zippo.com>`

```

suzie wrote in message <6sjouk$mkl@pdrn.zippo.com>...
>i have a file (several, actually) that i need to extract 11 fields from. i
wrote
>the fileaid to extract them and that works fine. But (isn't there always
one of
>these?), i need to convert one of the fields from 6 byte julian (998245=sep
02,
>1998) to, well, what's in my equals in my example. i also need to look at
two
>other fields that i am extracting, and see if they are empty. if they are
empty,
>i need to look at two non-extracted fields and do some computations.

to simplify matters, you should extract these two fields as well.
You can always get rid of them in a later pass.


>so, my question is this:
>what's the most efficient way to go about this?? we are an mvs shop, i can
use
>sas or utilities or........ and, for that matter, what order do i want to
go in?
>these files are 1/2 million records and up on a flat file.
>thanks for any input.
>suzie
>higgish@dhfs.state.wi.us

Title: VMS/Cobol Application Developer
Posting Reference Number: DSWK-3QHRNE

Responsibilities: Assist in the ongoing enhancement of the ACH payment processing system. 
 Perform development on the system to meet the business needs.  This would 
include reviewing modifications, executing applications, assessing output 
results based on business requirements.  Any new development will go through 
already established development methodologies.  Maintain system documentation 
and develop new documentation where needed.  Be available for operational 
support of the ACH process support.  This would include, but is not limited 
to, operator training, documentation, and production parallel support.

Required Skills: Minimum of two years experience with OpenVMS, Cobol, and the Dec Command 
Language in a corporate setting.  Structured development methodology experience 
is important.

Preferred Skills:

Thane,

Ah yes, I would be interesting if you could even get most programmers to go
through a real operations environment for a week.  But it will never happen.
The majority of programmers I have run into are utterly clueless about

older programmers.

This tends to cause all sorts of needless problems.  But as it is not one of

Aye, laddie, *them* was some days, a-puttin' in yer shift shovelin' coal
inta th' Steam Computer... th' air was blood-red from the flames, fulla
dust an' th' excitement a bein' on th' frontier a Somethin' Big... th'
hiss, th' *roar*, th' look on th' face a th' Chief Operator as he turned
that valve, openin' th' channels... hearin' them zeroes and ones clankin'
inta each other as they slid on thru...

... times ta make yer blood run *vital*, they were... times ta make ya
*proud*!

Depends on how old....
My previous supervisor was such a old fogey on programming...
that he couldnt even embrace the concept that pc's could do more than
mainframe (at least in our case at our site)...
Shoot, he couldnt even accept the fact that my hard drive in 1990 was 5
times bigger
in capacity than our whole site combined....

Or that a pc could operate our printers better & faster than what they had
before...
Or that he could save 10000 bu's a year by doing his forms a newer way than
iebgener/iebptpch   .... which are ok for most things...
But for straight text printing ....is overkill.. (especially in Jes2/Jes3
sites)...
I proved to him that he could do all his stuff at 100 bu's per year.... and
said my method was unproven despite the fact he was the only person in the
department
still doing things the same way he did in the early 60's ...

Good thing is that he was forced to retire 2 months after he laid me off...



However, some old fogies are great.... My current supervisor embraces new
concepts so fast
that his staff sometimes has trouble keeping up..
Unlike the previous supervisor, this one isnt just protecting his potential
retirement or job,
but trying new improvements & cutting costs when appropriate..

Paul Creamer wrote:
> 
> Depends on how old....
> My previous supervisor was such a old fogey on programming...
> that he couldnt even embrace the concept that pc's could do more than
> mainframe (at least in our case at our site)...

But they can't, can they?

PCs are OK as system components although I have met quite a number of
users who prefer the "old terminals" to work with.

Comments such as:
"Why do I have to hang around for ages before I can get a chance of
logging in?"
"How can I get rid of this mouse, it just gets in the way of
everything?"
"Oh well, the colours are pretty but it doesn't ..."
"The old terminal was a lot faster but I suppose someone knows what he's
doing."
are the sort of thing that I hear not infrequently.  I've been tempted
to answer the last one with "no he doesn't" on occasion.
```

I agreee somewhat....  but most of the people who preferred old terminals
usually due to lack of knowledge of what the pc based terminal could do ...
if only used as a mainframe terminal... Often faster...Especially when doing
multiple sessions..
What I was mainly speaking of ...was total rejection of pc's being able to
do anything
better than a mainframe....or even embracing new ways to do mainframe only
stuff...

Just before I left.... I actually trained half of all the people who were
insistant on staying with
mainframe terminals .... they didnt relize what they could do...
that they could literally do on a pc ..what they have been giving the
'graphics' department
to do for them....  The following year ...budget for that group was 1/10 of
previous year ...
and that was due to a total switchover to pc's ....

Graphics Department was slashed from 25 people to 3..

Actually..  Logging is only slower at my place...if using lan stuff... when
one bypasses the lan..
it often is just as fast as going mainframe.....(That assumes one doesnt
turn off either) ...
Funny... I actually had a mouse & lightpen on my mainframe terminal in the
early 80's ..
(Was more lightpen than mouse though)..

When we finally overrode him and the vp had new pc's put in ..over his
objection..
we found speed improvement of 600% .... and productivity improvement of us
by 300% ...
The pc's were finally going at near 100% of the speed ....

This shop was run by a guy who always had to be forced into new
technologies...
Even when just mainframe only.... But it was pc technologies that forced
retirement on him..

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Learning Cobol is a very enjoyable experience.

_(thread depth: 9)_

- **From:** "Chris Osborne" <chris_n_osborne@yahoo.com>
- **Date:** 1998-09-12T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<35faf532.0@news2.uswest.net>`
- **References:** `<35E98E6B.BF4B2C49@hankel.mersinet.co.uk> <1998083018345000.OAA27578@ladder01.news.aol.com> <Jl0PnHJ5PvPd-pn2-qOopfw0gCcSo@Dwight_Miller.iix.com> <35ef246c.0@news3.uswest.net> <1tGZ16mMk7i4-pn2-rjWuoZk89w65@dsm4merlin.iix.com> <6t28f9$22t@mercury.galstar.com> <35F92DD6.81625063@hankel.mersinet.co.uk> <6te7rt$ael@mercury.galstar.com>`

```
Paul

Paul Creamer wrote in message <6te7rt$ael@mercury.galstar.com>...
>I agreee somewhat....  but most of the people who preferred old terminals
>usually due to lack of knowledge of what the pc based terminal could do ...
>if only used as a mainframe terminal... Often faster...Especially when
doing
>multiple sessions..
>What I was mainly speaking of ...was total rejection of pc's being able to
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>

I have a PC on my desk, and I'm using it now.  But for mainframe work, my
little lovely 3278 IBM Infowindow terminal sits, and I'll use it over the PC
any day.  24 PF keys mean no shifting to get my customized keys (and I've
customized them all).  I can use it's layout for what I need faster. Plus 24
play-keys (I know the emulator can do hot-keys, but I couldn't remember more
than 24, anyway).  I always use 80col by 43 row screen, and my PC emulator
display's this format with the characters oddly stretched out.  I have yet
to figure out how to make the crosshairs appear on my PC, but it's only one
button away on my dumb (read: smart) terminal.

When the PC server goes down, I can still work on the mainframe.  When the
gateways are jammed or down, I can still work on the mainframe.  The
reliability of that simple and archaic SNA connection is astonishing.  Our
PC server has gone down mayber ten times in eight months, and the gateways,
two or three.  Several times, that was the whole day blown for people, but
not me.  How many times has my SNA connection gone down during that period?
Not once.

My dumb terminal is configured for two logical sessions at the controller,
and since I'm the one of the few smart terminal holdouts, I'm sure I could
convince network to configure it for more if I really wanted them.  We
already have TPX, so in one session, I can have TSO, CA7, and two or three
CICS regions, and whatever else I want (and that on both logical sessions,
because I have two accounts).  I always use my ISPF split screens--I can't
tell you how often I see people stumble through them or not use them at
all--and use TSO HOLD frequently.

My PC emulator has but one main and one secondary advantage for me.  Easy
file transfer, and easy screen prints.  Oh, easy screen copy and paste are
good, too.

Note, though, that out of about 70 programmers here, I'm about the only one
to regularly use a smart terminal.  Am I an old maniac, holding out for
yesteryear?  Nope.  I first did computers on an Apple IIe, and then moved to
the Mac, which blew my Apple IIe away, so I'm an old GUI interface advocate.
I did not come to mainframes until much later.

I just think existing emulators . . .
Nah, I won't be nasty


<snip>
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Learning Cobol is a very enjoyable experience.

_(thread depth: 9)_

- **From:** "Paul Creamer" <creamer@galstar.com>
- **Date:** 1998-09-12T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<6teb0a$aff@mercury.galstar.com>`
- **References:** `<35E98E6B.BF4B2C49@hankel.mersinet.co.uk> <1998083018345000.OAA27578@ladder01.news.aol.com> <Jl0PnHJ5PvPd-pn2-qOopfw0gCcSo@Dwight_Miller.iix.com> <35ef246c.0@news3.uswest.net> <1tGZ16mMk7i4-pn2-rjWuoZk89w65@dsm4merlin.iix.com> <6t28f9$22t@mercury.galstar.com> <35F92DD6.81625063@hankel.mersinet.co.uk> <6te7rt$ael@mercury.galstar.com>`

```

It depends...Just like I said originally....
In the case of our site....it definately was faster to go pc....
That supervisor even rejected the idea that my Amiga & PC (either one)..
had a bigger hd than our whole site combined (mainframe storage there...
minus tapes)..
Of course the site had bigger potential capacity...i was speaking of the
physical existing storage.
Not potential... which never was fully relized even when 100% mainframe or
even 100% main center..

Of course this was just a site that was a former main site and became a
satalite site...
But even as a satallite we were still bigger than when the location was the
main center...
(till late 80's)....

As far as pc versus mainframe ...it depends on what u are talking about...
For what I have used  mainframes for since 1978,..... Pc's are better....
But for system stuff .... it depends on how u use it...
Mainframe isnt always better solution ...even in the computer room ....

As a former Computer Operator ..and  VM Admin....   I have found PC's at a
minimum..
to give me more productivity capability...  and more options .....
Mainframe terminals...while neat in some ways (especially 3290's and
similar)...
were still too slow for me.... I can move so much faster (and accurately)
with a
multiple session environment on pc...than I ever could on mainframe
terminals...


For terminals.... Ive met very few people (other than those close to
retirement) ...that
would prefer mainframe terminal over a pc terminal .....
At my current location ...only 3 ....  out of 100's .... and only one
actually fought it...
So he became the last one to upgrade.... since he was the only one that
would actually require
pc training ... despite being our best mainframe programmer....(and oldest)
...

While all others had pentium's ...he was still on 286' ...because of his not
wanting to learn msexchange..
(we replaced PROFS w/ that) ... ... But he was forced to.... because we were
not going to put a
mainframe termainal in his house ...it became neccessary for him to give us
overnight support from home..

After getting new system ...he was actually doing much better ...and more
productive...


>
>Charles F Hankel wrote in message
…[30 more quoted lines elided]…
>

extra screen space!

I just purchased a "starview" switch, which allows me to connect my
keyboard/mouse/screen to four different computers.  Hooked to them
are a 233 & a 133 pentium, a 486DX, and a 486SX.  Each runs at
a different screen resolution and colour scheme, so that I can look
at my code in various modes.  The are networked, of course, so that
each can debug the same code at the same time.(great for record
lock type of thing).  Now that I have some desk space (one monitor
instead of four), I am going to shop about for a 19", and give my tired
old eyes a break.  I should even be able to read that at the low res
without my glasses.
>

I always recommend that you spend at least as much on the video card and
monitor as you did on the rest of the computer. It'll save you at
prescription increase on your glasses. The lowered eyestrain is also a relief
and probably helps productivity as much as the extra space does.



----
Civilization won't *die* from Y2k. It'll be more like Civilization goes 
out drinking and the next morning discovers the importance of drinking 
gin out of smaller containers.

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: PC's & terminal emulators (was Learning Cobol is a very enjoyable experience.)

_(thread depth: 11)_

- **From:** cadams@cadams-ntw.acucorp.com (Chris Adams)
- **Date:** 1998-09-18T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<slrn705q4o.bu.cadams@cadams-ntw.acucorp.com>`
- **References:** `<35E98E6B.BF4B2C49@hankel.mersinet.co.uk> <1998083018345000.OAA27578@ladder01.news.aol.com> <Jl0PnHJ5PvPd-pn2-qOopfw0gCcSo@Dwight_Miller.iix.com> <35ef246c.0@news3.uswest.net> <1tGZ16mMk7i4-pn2-rjWuoZk89w65@dsm4merlin.iix.com> <6t28f9$22t@mercury.galstar.com> <35F92DD6.81625063@hankel.mersinet.co.uk> <6te7rt$ael@mercury.galstar.com> <Jl0PnHJ5PvPd-pn2-sIXddYnBnbD1@Dwight_Miller.iix.com> <B2289F769668EEF654@0.0.0.0>`

```
On Fri, 18 Sep 1998 23:38:30 +0100, John Whistler <jaw@mail.indigo.ie> wrote:
>mode with multiple sessions running in overlapping windows. I can see
>a part of every session. I get extremely annoyed at my colleagues with
>Pentiums and17" monitors, running in 800x600 mode with a single
>window expanded to fill the entire screen. What I could do with that
>extra screen space!

Personally, unless I have a huge monitor I prefer to have multiple maximized
windows that I ALT+TAB through. I do leave things like email clients running in
the corner if they support changing the icon when you get mail.  The most
productive setup I've found so far is similar - a linux console with 12 virtual
terms. ALT+F1-12 cycle through and because it's text mode (albeit 132x50)
switches are closer to instaneous than I can measure.

This allows me to take advantage of the screen real estate (I *like* being able
to see 50-100 lines of my program at a time (or multiple source windows))
without dropping back to single-tasking. When I start a make, a keystroke takes
me over my email client or a file manager. Virtual desktops are also handy for
the same reason: if an email causes me to start a web search, I can put both
windows on one desktop while waiting for the search to respond. Meanwhile, I
can go back to the reading the rest.


----
"Civilization won't *die* from Y2k. It'll be more like Civilization goes out
drinking and the next morning discovers the benefits of drinking gin from
smaller containers."
The opinions above are my own and may or may not match those of my employer.
```

On Sat, 19 Sep 1998 00:00:24 -0400, Donald Tees wrote:

>>Personally, unless I have a huge monitor I prefer to have multiple
>maximized

>May I ask what you are using and how you like it?  I am
>shopping.

Actually, all I own at the moment is a old ViewSonic 17PS, which is only 17
inches[1]. One nice feature it has is two hookups on the back that can be
switched back and forth through the control panel.  I'm also looking for a
larger monitor at some point in the indefinite future. Of course, I recently
noticed that (IIRC) IBM is planning on selling LCD with something like 400
dpi. It's kind of tempting to stick with the more than adequate monitor I
have now until those are available, as the gains in DPI, color quality and
the zero dot pitch is really attractive.

The largest monitor I've ever seen was part of Arco's control center in Los
Angeles. As I recall, it was something like 50" inches and a touch screen.
With that you could control every major system at their refineries and
pipelines all over Southern California. Just tap on the map...

[1] I'm also impressed that the $300 no-name 17" monitor I bought for a
family member about 4 months ago seems as good as this monitor that cost $750
two years ago...

----
Civilization won't *die* from Y2k. It'll be more like Civilization goes 
out drinking and the next morning discovers the importance of drinking 
gin out of smaller containers.

It then went into major project cleanup of code....to improve effiency..
That kind of knowledge isnt gained by a programmer without some operations
knowledge...
Though possible..... its rare in my town.

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Learning Cobol is a very enjoyable experience.

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "Chris Osborne" <chris_n_osborne@yahoo.com>
- **Date:** 1998-09-04T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<35effa8d.0@news3.uswest.net>`
- **References:** `<Jl0PnHJ5PvPd-pn2-qOopfw0gCcSo@Dwight_Miller.iix.com> <1998090401153000.VAA09826@ladder03.news.aol.com>`

```
Carlos,

You need the following characteristics for the following different areas an
Operator can work in.

1-Print Room: Lift up to 100 pounds.  Learn the use of, commands for,
cleaning off, and jam clearing methods of all the printers you will be
required to operate.  Learn to rapidly break down print and distribute it
with 100% accuracy to any one of possibly thousands of destinations.
Whatever you do, don't let your work pile up, dump work onto the next shift
by putting it off a while, or blow off what you're doing as unimportant.
If you are called to personally deliver reports (I'm thinking on a regular
basis, here), then do your best to convince some of these recipients that
you actually care about what you're doing, and what they're doing too.

2-Computer Room (Tape): Start studying the relatively incomprehensible IBM
manuals.  The JES2 and JCL manuals, and the MVS console commands manual.
Learn and memorize as much of the MVS & JES2 Display commands as you can;
you won't be called on to execute very many of the others (a few, like Swap
and Mount), and the Display command is mostly harmless and knowing it makes
you look good (whatever you do, don't use JES2's Display command to show all
elements on the spool taking up 0% or more of the spool; if your spool has
even a few thousand elements, this will take a long time to scroll past the
system console).
If you are responsible for doing backups/DRA, double and triple check the
volser of every tape you send out; sending out the wrong tape is
embarrassing.
Don't put tapes away in the wrong slot in the racks.  Nothing will drive the
next person who needs to go after that tape more mad that having to search
through the entire library for it (and some libraries are huge).


3-Computer Room (Console): Know those JES2 and MVS Display commands by
heart.  Know how to use the majority of the others (some are useful only to
system programmers) with a cheat sheet.  Know JCL backwards and forwards,
including all the technical points of restarts, you will undoubtedly know
JCL better than the programmers you deal with (spotting an obscure JCL error
the programmer cannot will get you noticed (one way, or another)).  Know
your job scheduling package, and if you have one, your job restart package.
Get a good grasp of every support application in your shop, or at the least
know what they do.

If you're in a UNIX environment, then you need to know the appropriate UNIX
system commands, and how to read and write shell scripts.

Showing some basic network proficiency in all this can't hurt.

It may be obvious to some, but I'll say it anyway: be professional.

If you can do all this, and at the same time, show you can write programs of
business value in the language of your shop, you'll have an excellent
chance.





CarlosS940 wrote in message
<1998090401153000.VAA09826@ladder03.news.aol.com>...
>Do you mean that I should work as a computer operator before moving into
>programming position?  What are the usual qualification of being a computer
>operator?  Please advise.  Thanks.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Learning Cobol is a very enjoyable experience.

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** redsky@ibm.net (Thane Hubbell)
- **Date:** 1998-09-04T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol

Most shops operate differently.  If you are a fast learner, your DP 
background should be enough to land you an operations job.  Depending

fun job too.

<a id="t-8c723bec6c"></a>
## Thread: MF-COBOL vs ACUCOBOL — 5 msgs

_Topics: [`Compilers and vendors`](topics.md#compilers)_

### MF-COBOL vs ACUCOBOL

- **From:** dmisamore@discountlabels.com
- **Date:** 1998-09-03T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<6smrtt$84r$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>`

```
My company is evaluating a move from ACUCOBOL to MF-COBOL. I am inquiry if
there is much syntax difference between the two and if ACUCOBOL vision files
would be easily portable to the MF-COBOL file system.

We are trying to collect information other than provided by a salesman.

Thanks

-----== Posted via Deja News, The Leader in Internet Discussion ==-----
http://www.dejanews.com/rg_mkgrp.xp   Create Your Own Free Member Forum
```

#### ↳ Re: MF-COBOL vs ACUCOBOL

- **From:** "Sal Cambareri" <sal@lasermax.com>
- **Date:** 1998-09-07T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<KqUI1.9767$f01.7374046@news.teleport.com>`
- **References:** `<6smrtt$84r$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>`

```
If your company is committed to using the Vision file system you could
license the Vision file system package from Acucobol and link it into your
Microfocus build files.  One caveat: I don't know for sure, but it seems
possible that MF COBOL might not support some of the features of the Vision
file system.  Features such as multiple gigabyte record sizes aren't really
"syntax" issues, and so you should make sure that you verify that the limits
on file structures allowed in MF COBOL at least match what you are using in
your Acucobol programs.

Regards,
Sal

#### ↳ Re: MF-COBOL vs ACUCOBOL

- **From:** "Mike Rochford" <miker@easirun.co.za>
- **Date:** 1998-09-09T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<6t5u54$46j$1@news01.iafrica.com>`
- **References:** `<6smrtt$84r$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>`

```

dmisamore@discountlabels.com wrote in message
<6smrtt$84r$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>...
>My company is evaluating a move from ACUCOBOL to MF-COBOL. I am inquiry if
>there is much syntax difference between the two and if ACUCOBOL vision
files
>would be easily portable to the MF-COBOL file system.

Firstly my question would be WHY ??????
Secondly - If really depends upon what features you have used within
AcuCobol. If you have used their extensions to the cobol language,
particularly within the SCREEN SECTION, then you are going to find it
exceptionally difficult to move. To get the same kind of GUI screens out of
MicroFocus you are going to have to replce the AcuCobol screens with either
MicroFocus's Dialog system or something like SPII from Flexus or COBOL-WOW
from England Technical Services.

The only way I can think of to move the data files would be to write an
AcuCobol program to strip them into sequential format and then a MicroFocus
program to rebuild them.

Whatever you decide to do, I would very carefully assess the reasons before
embarking on this exercise.

We distribute both AcuCobol and MicroFocus (As well as RM) so if you have
any specific questions, you are welcome to e-mail me directly.

Mike.

>
>We are trying to collect information other than provided by a salesman.
>

Are you implying that Salesmen don't always tell the truth ????

That's fighting talk !!!!!

Mike.
```

## Thread: Ethical Question — 39 msgs

### Ethical Question

- **From:** "Garry Dell" <Garry_dell@hotmail.com>
- **Date:** 1998-09-03T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<#CD7Tf819GA.172@upnetnews05>`

```
I am doing some y2k conversions and I am comming across some extremely
poorly written code.


I have a question to ask of this newsgroup.


I have seen quite a few "GOTO"s, in several programs,  should I remove them
or leave them intact?


Any feedback would be greatly appriciated.


I personally believe that I should remove them.
```

#### ↳ Re: Ethical Question

- **From:** docdwarf@clark.net ()
- **Date:** 1998-09-04T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<6sor42$rpl$1@callisto.clark.net>`
- **References:** `<#CD7Tf819GA.172@upnetnews05>`

```
In article <#CD7Tf819GA.172@upnetnews05>,
Garry Dell <Garry_dell@hotmail.com> wrote:
>I am doing some y2k conversions and I am comming across some extremely
>poorly written code.
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
>I personally believe that I should remove them.

I can understand your desire... and i would suggest that you do your best
to restrain yourself.

You were, by your description, hired/assigned to make the code
Y2K-compliant, not to insure that the code adheres to a particular school
of program structure.

The fewer your modifications, the fewer the bugs that will be introduced
by your modifications.  Y2K is a fixed deadline; code which unstructured
but Y2K-compliant *will* run.

In other words...

IF PROGRAM-RUNS
    PERFORM NEXT-ASSIGNMENT
ELSE
   PERFORM CODE-LIKE-HELL UNTIL DAMNED-THING WORKS.

... and do what you were hired/assigned to do.

DD
```

#### ↳ Re: Ethical Question

- **From:** redsky@ibm.net (Thane Hubbell)
- **Date:** 1998-09-04T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<Jl0PnHJ5PvPd-pn2-mNuEdL4J7dKK@Dwight_Miller.iix.com>`
- **References:** `<#CD7Tf819GA.172@upnetnews05>`

```
On Fri, 4 Sep 1998 05:52:40, "Garry Dell" <Garry_dell@hotmail.com> 
wrote:

> I am doing some y2k conversions and I am comming across some extremely
> poorly written code.
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
> 

I don't write new code with GO TO.   HOWEVER, you stand a LARGE chance
of breaking the code if you mess with it.  Do your job, fix the Y2K, 
and program in the STYLE that the programmer before you used.  Even if
you don' t like it.
```

#### ↳ Re: Ethical Question

- **From:** "G. van Vlimmeren" <gvlimmer@hoogenbosch.nl>
- **Date:** 1998-09-04T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<01bdd7d3$e984aea0$2c050380@s70>`
- **References:** `<#CD7Tf819GA.172@upnetnews05>`

```

> I have seen quite a few "GOTO"s, in several programs,  should I remove
them
> or leave them intact?

Hi.

If you can get someone to pay for it, change them :)

Otherwise, don't. The most important task for a program is 'to work'.
Everything else, like structure or readablility, is a bonus. :) And hey,
would you repaint a Rembrandt, because the clothes on his paintings look
out of date?

Oh, you can always change the 'GO TO' into 'GO'. Is shorter, looks nicer,
doesn't look as much like a 'GOTO'.

Gerben 'GO' van Vlimmeren
```

#### ↳ Re: Ethical Question

- **From:** "Alan Macro" <AlanM@hpdi.no-luncheon-meat.demon.co.uk>
- **Date:** 1998-09-04T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<904898026.9805.0.nnrp-01.9e98b131@news.demon.co.uk>`
- **References:** `<#CD7Tf819GA.172@upnetnews05>`

```
Conform to the old adage "if it works, don't fix it"! Removing GOTOs from a
working program (I assume its working!) is going to involve some
re-structuring. This is likely to create new bugs and increase the amount of
de-bugging and testing required.

Alan Macro
Hill Price Davison, London
AlanM@no-luncheon-meat.hpdi.demon.co.uk
"no-luncheon-meat."(i.e. no spam)  should be removed from email address

#### ↳ Re: Ethical Question

- **From:** "Leif Svalgaard" <leif@ibm.net>
- **Date:** 1998-09-04T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<35efdd51.0@news1.ibm.net>`
- **References:** `<#CD7Tf819GA.172@upnetnews05>`

```

Garry Dell wrote in message <#CD7Tf819GA.172@upnetnews05>...
>I am doing some y2k conversions and I am comming across some extremely
>poorly written code.
>I have a question to ask of this newsgroup.
>I have seen quite a few "GOTO"s, in several programs,  should I remove them
>or leave them intact?

The golden rule of maintenance is to do only ONE thing at a time.
So if you are fixing Y2K don't mess with anything else.
Leave the GO TO structure the way it is.


Also, do NOT try to change the coding style of the original program.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Ethical Question

- **From:** scm@enterprise.net (Shaun C. Murray)

```
In article <35efdd51.0@news1.ibm.net>, leif@ibm.net says...

>The golden rule of maintenance is to do only ONE thing at a time.

Oh yes indeed and book it back into your source control system after each step 
too.

>So if you are fixing Y2K don't mess with anything else.
>Leave the GO TO structure the way it is.

As others have said - if it ain't broke, don't touch it.

>
>
>Also, do NOT try to change the coding style of the original program.
>

Of the original sure but if you're adding code, I don't think you should 
restrict your coding style to an outdated style guide. Eg. I don't think you 
should use GO TO's instead of EVALUATE just because the original style guide 
didn't support ANSI85 syntax.
```

#### ↳ Re: Ethical Question

- **From:** tim.oxler@NOSPAMteo-computer.com (Tim Oxler)
- **Date:** 1998-09-08T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<35f52a8f.2624900@news.teo-computer.com>`
- **References:** `<#CD7Tf819GA.172@upnetnews05>`

```
"Garry Dell" <Garry_dell@hotmail.com> wrote:

>I am doing some y2k conversions and I am comming across some extremely
>poorly written code.
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Depends on a lot of factors.

But first, is there time to do this additional work, and do you think
it's necessary.

Any program that is enhanced and maintained quite a bit, can be
justified for trying to make the code more maintainable.  Otherwise,
if it works, don't fixit.

Multiple GOTOs can lead to "spaghetti" code.   GOTOs should be,
generally speaking, avoided, but IMO, I think it's a mistake to
totally avoid GOTOs, on grounds of strict structured doctrine.

Something else you also need to keep in mind, is that all programmers
have different styles.  Some good, some very different, but still
good, and some not so good.  Whenever enhancing a program, try and
keep the logic flow consistant.  Radically different styles in the
same program will make things very difficult for the next programmer
to work on the program in question.

Tim Oxler
TEO Computer Technologies Inc.
http://www.teo-computer.com
Take the Y2k Quiz
http://www.teo-computer.com/dev/quiz.html
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Ethical Question

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Ethical Question

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc123@bellsouth.net>
- **Date:** 1998-09-09T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<ITtJ1.828$p61.1325388@news4.mia.bellsouth.net>`
- **References:** `<#CD7Tf819GA.172@upnetnews05> <35f52a8f.2624900@news.teo-computer.com> <35F606ED.B26FBDE1@mindspring.com>`

```
skidmike wrote:
>I'm not a professional, but in some pgms of mine, I use go to's.  For
>instance, 1st part of the pgm is screen i-o say 13-14 lines.  Since i am
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>the code is neat and well-documented, why not?

For very good reasons, why not.  From around 1960, there was a significant
amount of reaearch done on what makes 'good' code.  One of the surprising
things found (very, very, mucho good evidence) is that excessive use of
GO TO results in poor code.  This prompted Edsger W. Dijkstra's now famous
article in the March 1968 "Communications of the ACM", "Goto Statement
Considered Harmful".  The resulting uproar resulted in the formulation of
what we call "Structured Programming" and "Structured Design".  One of the
tenets of structured programming is that GO TO, if used at all, must be
confined to a very narrow pattern of use.

No one who is knowledgable about the issue would argue that this is not a
good idea.  The argument is about just how far we should go (not GO TO)
in eliminating GO TO. :)  One camp believes GO TO is like a disease, never
good, and should be eliminated entirely, no exceptions.  The other camp
believes that, while GO TO can be dangerous, there are certain very
limited uses for which GO TO is useful, and not harmful.  In general, you
will find that everyone agrees on at least this: that every code block
should have only one entry and only one exit point, and that the code block
should be called (or PERFORMED), and that there should *NEVER, EVER* be a
GO TO out of the code block.  That this is a good idea can be mathematically
proven.

In COBOL, this means that every paragraph is PERFORMED, and the only GO TO
ever allowed (if even that) is a GO TO the paragraph exit.  Understand, the
only real argument is whether we use

       PERFORM paragraph
          THRU paragraph-exit

    paragraph.
       ...
       GO TO paragraph-exit.
       ...
    paragraph-exit.
       EXIT.

and permit the GO TO exit, or

       PERFORM paragraph

    paragraph.
       ...

with no GO TO ever allowed.  Note that the only reason for using the
former instead of the latter is that most languages support constructs
to permit an early exit from a code block (here, a paragraph), but the
current COBOL 85 standard does not.  The new standard in progress does
support such constructs, and then the argument will be moot.  Without
an EXIT PARAGRAPH statement, the code will inevitably be more
complicated at times.  The disagreement is over whether it is better
to use GO TO to simplify the code, or to avoid GO TO altogether, even
at the cost of more complex code.

Having originally tried eliminating GO TO altogether, and seeing the
added costs in extra and more complex code, I personally use PERFORM
... THRU exit and GO TO exit.  But I will drop it like a hot potato
when EXIT PARAGRAPH is available.  Hopefully before I retire. :-)
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Ethical Question

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** scm@enterprise.net (Shaun C. Murray)
- **Date:** 1998-09-09T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<6t5tcf$o3q$1@news.enterprise.net>`
- **References:** `<#CD7Tf819GA.172@upnetnews05> <35f52a8f.2624900@news.teo-computer.com> <35F606ED.B26FBDE1@mindspring.com> <ITtJ1.828$p61.1325388@news4.mia.bellsouth.net>`

```
In article <ITtJ1.828$p61.1325388@news4.mia.bellsouth.net>, 
judmc123@bellsouth.net says...
>
>
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>when EXIT PARAGRAPH is available.  Hopefully before I retire. :-)

Why not just use sections all the time instead of paragraphs. Then use EXIT 
SECTION. You also avoid completely the PERFORM ... THROUGH trickle problem 
where another programmer inserts an unconnected paragraph in the middle of your 
PERFORM ... THROUGH 'structure'. 

If you use sections, you can avoid GO TO completely and not be forced into 
adding EXIT paragraphs.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Ethical Question

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "Chris Osborne" <chris_n_osborne@yahoo.com>
- **Date:** 1998-09-09T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<35f6a01f.0@news2.uswest.net>`
- **References:** `<#CD7Tf819GA.172@upnetnews05> <35f52a8f.2624900@news.teo-computer.com> <35F606ED.B26FBDE1@mindspring.com> <ITtJ1.828$p61.1325388@news4.mia.bellsouth.net> <6t5tcf$o3q$1@news.enterprise.net>`

```
Shaun C. Murray wrote in message <6t5tcf$o3q$1@news.enterprise.net>...
>In article <ITtJ1.828$p61.1325388@news4.mia.bellsouth.net>,
>judmc123@bellsouth.net says...
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
>where another programmer inserts an unconnected paragraph in the middle of
your
>PERFORM ... THROUGH 'structure'.
>
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>


My COBOL Reference manual from IBM says that SECTION is an organizational
division, and doesn't go any farther.  Our local shop's standard is to avoid
SECTION or PEFROM-THRU (and no GO TOs under any circumstances, it's that
camp of thought).

However, SECTION is obviously more than just this, because when sections and
paragraphs are mixed, as IBM puts it, "Your results may be unpredictable."

Does anyone know what SECTIONS are really for?  Beyond the organizational
feature.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Ethical Question

_(thread depth: 9)_

- **From:** Bob Berman <bberman@netbox.com>
- **Date:** 1998-09-10T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<35F8B106.3F47@netbox.com>`
- **References:** `<#CD7Tf819GA.172@upnetnews05> <ITtJ1.828$p61.1325388@news4.mia.bellsouth.net> <6t5tcf$o3q$1@news.enterprise.net> <35f6a01f.0@news2.uswest.net> <6t6afi$hg5$1@callisto.clark.net> <35F75ADD.1E59@netbox.com> <35f80dff.0@news2.uswest.net>`

```
Chris Osborne wrote:
> 
> I've read that it used to be done, but didn't know it was related to
…[15 more quoted lines elided]…
> >Bob

Sections generate CSECTS - if you aligned your CSECTS on a page
boundary, it was more likely that the all of the code relating to a
function, within a section, would be in main memory, and not be split
over multiple pages (4K), some of which might be paged out to virtual
storage

Bob.
```

rrg
=====================================================
Scattered sciences, remnants of a shattered Science

moles and molecules...her and hercules

and now for something compleatly different:

UNIFIED FIELD THEORY		
by Tim Joseph (amended by rrg aka ibis)

          I
In the beginning there was Aristotle.
And objects at rest tended to remain at rest,
And objects in motion tended to come to rest,
And soon everything was at rest.
And God saw that it was boring.

          II
Then God created Newton.
And objects at rest tended to remain at rest,
And objects in motion tended to remain in motion,
And energy was conserved, and momentum was conserved, 
          and matter was conserved.
And God saw that it was conservative.

          III
Then God created Einstein.
And everything was relative.
And fast things became short,
And straight things became curved,
And the universe was filled with inertial frames.
And God saw that it was relatively general, but some of it
                    was especially relative.

          IV
Then God created [Planck]
And there was the principle,
And the principle was quantum,
And all things were quantified,
But some things were still relative.
And God saw that it was confusing.

                                   V
                         Wherefore God created Pauli.
                         And Pauli begat Heisenberg.
                         From Pauli there was limited vacancy,
                         And Heisenberg found it to be uncertain.
                         And God saw that the confusion was indeterminate.

                                   VI
                         Wherefore God created Schroedinger.
                         And Schroedinger begat De Broglie.
                         And waves were probable,
                         And matter was wavy,
                         And waves were material.
                         And God saw that the confusion was dualistic.

         VII
Then God was going to create Furgeson.
And Furgeson would have unified,
And he would have fielded a theory,
And all would have been one.
But it was the seventh day,
And God rested,
And objects at rest tend to remain at rest.

=====================================================
