###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Ethical Question

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** "Donald Tees" <donald@willmack.com>
- **Date:** 1998-09-09T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<6t6jdd$94h$1@news.igs.net>`
- **References:** `<#CD7Tf819GA.172@upnetnews05> <35f52a8f.2624900@news.teo-computer.com> <35F606ED.B26FBDE1@mindspring.com> <ITtJ1.828$p61.1325388@news4.mia.bellsouth.net> <6t5tcf$o3q$1@news.enterprise.net> <35f6a01f.0@news2.uswest.net>`

```
>Does anyone know what SECTIONS are really for?  Beyond the organizational
>feature.
>

Most Cobol compilers use quite different programs for each section.
While that was probably not the original purpose, it does make the
compiler writer's job easier ... keeping in mind that most original Cobol
compilers were written for extremely small computers.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Ethical Question

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** "Leif Svalgaard" <leif@ibm.net>
- **Date:** 1998-09-09T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<35f6d3eb.0@news1.ibm.net>`
- **References:** `<#CD7Tf819GA.172@upnetnews05> <35f52a8f.2624900@news.teo-computer.com> <35F606ED.B26FBDE1@mindspring.com> <ITtJ1.828$p61.1325388@news4.mia.bellsouth.net> <6t5tcf$o3q$1@news.enterprise.net> <35f6a01f.0@news2.uswest.net> <6t6jdd$94h$1@news.igs.net>`

```

Donald Tees wrote in message <6t6jdd$94h$1@news.igs.net>...
>>Does anyone know what SECTIONS are really for?  Beyond the organizational
>>feature.
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>compilers were written for extremely small computers.


SECTIONS were a means of making and managing "overlays" of
code, so that the program would fit in the small memory of earlier
computers. I still have a Cobol program that in the environment
section specifies that it needs 400 words of core to run.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Ethical Question

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** john@watkins.cix.[nospam]co.uk (John Watkins)
- **Date:** 1998-09-11T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<memo.19980911002258.161R@john.watkins.cix.co.uk>`
- **References:** `<35f6a01f.0@news2.uswest.net>`

```
> Does anyone know what SECTIONS are really for?  Beyond the organizational
> feature.

We are a 'SECTION shop'. This means you get code style like this:

A000-MAIN SECTION.

    PERFORM B000-DO-SOMETHING.
    
    GOBACK.

B000-DO-SOMETHING SECTION.

    blah
    blah
    IF ERROR GOTO B000-MAIN-EXIT.
    blah
    blah
    and now fall though to EXIT paragraph...
    
B000-MAIN-EXIT.
    EXIT.    
    
In pre-ANSI'85 COBOL this meant that you could avoid excessive flag testing 
and/or nested IF statements by jumping out to the EXIT paragraph. Of course 
any section longer than 40 lines (just under two terminal screens) should be 
re-structured.

+--------------+------------------------------------+
| John Watkins | mailto:john@watkins_dot_cix.co.uk  |
|              | http://www_dot_cix.co.uk/~watkins/ |
+--------------+------------------------------------+
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Ethical Question

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** "Warren G. Simmons" <warrens@inficad.com>
- **Date:** 1998-09-10T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<35F88B21.E3916358@inficad.com>`
- **References:** `<35f6a01f.0@news2.uswest.net> <memo.19980911002258.161R@john.watkins.cix.co.uk>`

```
SECTIONS were added to COBOL to allow the complilation of program OVERLAYS.
PERIOD, PARAGRAPH.
Any other use has been added or became a feature that was unintended at the
time.
If your compiler SUPPORTS sections as designed, then a program that might
require
several megs to run (pick your own size), might run in a few, or only one meg of
RAM.

SECTIONS were important when RAM was very restricted.  There are situations
today where one might wish to page the code rather than pass the data.

Warren Simmons


John Watkins wrote:

> > Does anyone know what SECTIONS are really for?  Beyond the organizational
> > feature.
>
> We are a 'SECTION shop'. This means you get code style like this:
>
```

wrote:

> Why not just use sections all the time instead of paragraphs. Then use EXIT 
> SECTION. You also avoid completely the PERFORM ... THROUGH trickle problem 
> where another programmer inserts an unconnected paragraph in the middle of your

- **From:** beta1@bigfoot.com

Go to paragraph-exit.

Exit paragraph.

When the latter is available, it will generate the same code as the former now does
-- a branch to the end of the block of code.

A rose by any other name is still ... a go to.

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Ethical Question

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** riplin@kcbbs.gen.nz (Richard Plinston)
- **Date:** 1998-09-09T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3298251.82430.28056@kcbbs.gen.nz>`
- **References:** `<6t5tcf$o3q$1@news.enterprise.net>`

```
In message <<6t5tcf$o3q$1@news.enterprise.net>> scm@enterprise.net  writes:
> 
> Why not just use sections all the time instead of paragraphs. Then use EXIT 
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> adding EXIT paragraphs.

I think that you are confused.

The only reason to choose perform section or perform thru over
perform paragraph is that it is possible to do a GO TO in the
first two but cannot in the last.

If you do not use sections but only ever do a perform paragraph
then it eliminates the possibility of GO TO.
```

…[3 more quoted lines elided]…

READ file

will try and keep the logic style, and try to avoid adding GO TOs.

- **From:** Bill Lynch <wblynch@att.net>

Tim Oxler wrote:
> 
(snip)
> Agreed.
> 
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>         GO TO paragraph exit

No longer necessary to do this (assuming IBM and COBOL II, or higher):

READ file
     AT END
         SET END-OF-INPUT-FILE TO TRUE  (or)  MOVE 'Y'  TO
INPUT-FILE-SWITCH
     NOT AT END
         PERFORM record_processing_logic
END-READ

> But I have to say, if I'm enhancing a program that is "GO TO less", I
> will try and keep the logic style, and try to avoid adding GO TOs.

I'm with you.

Bill Lynch
```

Any reason not to use declarative and the FILE-STATUS variable? Then:

02 w-file-status PIC X(2).
    88  status-ok VALUE "00".
    88  end-file VALUE "10".
    88  others...

READ file
IF status-ok
    PERFORM record_processing_logic

I [ab]use the file status variable for a lot of things. It's practical
because you can invent your own "exceptions".

Regards
Albert Ratzlaff

I've only been professionally programming for a year, but so far I just
haven't found a reason to use GO TOs on my own.

To go hand in hand with this, I'm porting a COBOL application off an old
Prime computer to an IBM mainframe.  The code is classic GO TO filled
spaghetti.  One of my assistant programmers is often heard to say, "I'm
Italian, and even I can't follow it."

The programmer who wrote the original code felt no hesitation to read or
write to the five files used by the program from anywhere in the program.
He felt no hesitation to write the screen from anywhere (I just checked, and
he has over 240 separate lines of code writing to the screen).  He,
apparently, felt an unrelenting compulsion to use GO TOs everywhere, and
this was written back in about 1987.  This is compounded by instances of
puzzling code, where the author did crazy things: like clearing an array by
moving spaces to each element in a loop instead of just moving spaces to the
group-item above the loop; individually initializing every variable in the
program to zero or spaces (hundreds of them) instead of just using
Initialize; and other, less easily explainable insanities.

Although, overall, I can just attribute the problems of the program to the
author's lack of skill (or whatever), the use of GO TOs, and file and screen
calls all over the place, definitely contribute to horror this code has
become.
```

<a id="t-6b105b3c80"></a>
## Thread: Help: COBOL/CICS Re-useable Working-Storage Problem — 12 msgs

_Topics: [`Mainframe, z/OS, JCL, CICS`](topics.md#mainframe) · [`Help requests and how-to`](topics.md#help)_

### Help: COBOL/CICS Re-useable Working-Storage Problem

- **From:** Phil Davey <davey@powernet.co.uk>
- **Date:** 1998-09-03T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,alt.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<35EE41E5.AE90123B@powernet.co.uk>`

```
Help Please,

I have a Cobol program dynamically called in CICS  by many users/tasks.

The Program contains a 50k Table (of static parameters)  in
Working-Storage.

It appears to me (& I am not a cics programmer)  that each task/user is
getting a fresh copy of the Working-Storage for this Sub-program on each

call.

The table is clearly in the LOADLIB(loadmodule) and CICS is only doing
one program-fetch for the whole day.
But each/every task is using a lot more memory now.

OBJECTIVE:  What I want is a single central copy of the Sub-program AND
it's working Storage,
i.e. someway to bind the working storage to the program.
So that each/every user isn't using more storage,  all would go thru the

single/central/constant copy.

Q1) I THINK that COBOL may separate the Procedure-Divn  from the
Working-Storage to allow:
one central copy of the Procedure-instructions but
each user to have their own copy of Working-Storage.
Perhaps someone can confirm this is probably whats happening.

Q2) What should I do to acheive the "objective" above.

I suspect the answer may lie in Compile & link options  such as
RENT    REUSE    RES   NOADATA   DYNAM   TERM    etc but this a little
beyond my normal knowledge (& all others I have asked so-far).

Functionally the program is working 100% as wanted (except each/every
user uses more storage!).

Can anybody help please.
Regards
Phil Davey

====================
Other Supporting Information
====================

Current COMPILE Options:  NOADATA,  ADV, APOST, DATA(24), NOOPTIMIZE,
RENT, RMODE(AUTO) NOTERM,  NODYNAM, FLAG(I,W)
(there is no mention of RES,  and NoDynam is apparently not now needed
for dynamicly called routines).

Current LINK Options: LIST, XREF, SIZE=(2048K,512K), RMODE=ANY, AMODE=31

We are using IBM  COBOL-for-MVS.

The Sub-Program is not really a CICS program (we don't do a cics
precompile) - it makes no CICS calls and no other CALLS or I/O.

All our programs are running "below-the-line" - we are looking
separately at this with the hope that we can operate above the line -
but I don't know if this is going to change things a lot ???

---o0o---
```

#### ↳ Re: Help: COBOL/CICS Re-useable Working-Storage Problem

- **From:** "WOB" <wobconsult@sprynet.com>
- **Date:** 1998-09-03T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,alt.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<6sn3ud$m18$1@juliana.sprynet.com>`
- **References:** `<35EE41E5.AE90123B@powernet.co.uk>`

```
Phil,

    You can't do much about the Working-Storage. That's just the way it
works. What you're attempting to gain is 100 percent re-entrant code. COBOL
uses the terminology serially-reusable which just about does the same thing.
I think you're OK with that.

    Have you considered a Static-Assembler Table that gets addressed by your
COBOL/CICS Program via the LOAD command. Basically, you'd be addressing
storage that is EXTERNAL to your program. Whenever a change occurs to the
Table, just re-assemble it and off you go. It would certainly reduce task
storage significantly.

    You'll need to define a PPT Entry for your table. You may also want to
discuss with your Sys Prog about the possibility of defining it as
Core-Resident. This will ensure that it won't get paged-out, as it will be
placed in a non-pageable area of DSA or EDSA, depending on your AMODE.
However, if you feel that it's usage will be significant enough that CICS
won't page it out during his periodic compressions, then Core-Residency may
not be required.

    You'll need to define your DSECT (COBOL copybook) in the LINKAGE SECTION
of your program after DFHCOMMAREA. You can then establish addressability to
it via the LOAD command. Discuss with your Sys Prog about the Core-Resident
definition and possibility of loading the address of the table into a 4-byte
fullword in the CWA. By doing this, your program issues an ADDRESS CWA
command and you obtain the table address from the CWA. This method would
require a 3rd-Stage PLT/PI Program that would establish table addressability
and populate the CWA fullword at CICS Startup.

    Now, after having said all this, you may have to re-consider this design
if you are planning on migrating to a SYSPLEX (minimum CICS Version/Release
is 4.1.0 for MVS) environment, where CWA's and Assembler Tables are NOT in
the cards, in which case, a CICS User-Data Table (a VSAM File) may be your
answer. I won't go in to it too much, but let me say that SYSPLEX has
serious ramifications for users of TWA, CWA, Core-Resident Programs, GETMAIN
SHARED, the passing of addresses and several other CICS areas and commands
used by thousands of current systems. IBM has espoused COMMAREAS as the
means to pass data around and DPL (Distributed Program Link) to centralize
high-usage modules for all regions. Also, please remember that IBM sells
HARDWARE, something that you will definitely need to increase in the
CICSPLEX/SYSPLEX environment.

    BTW, when you say DYNAMIC, it really isn't. DYNAMIC issues an MVS FETCH
(such as a dynamically called batch program). But, in CICS, NODYNAM is
required and CICS locates the PPT address and establishes addressability to
the load module, much like an ASSEMBLER BALR 14,15. So much for particulars.

    Also, there are a number of PTF's (IBM fixes) that may need to be
applied to your COBOL/MVS environment that could be causing SOS
(Short-on-Storage) conditions. Verify with your Sys Prog that they have been
applied.

    The remaining issues you outline, such as below and above the line,
should be reviewed and discussed with your Sys Prog as certain parameters at
the PCT and PPT level must be considered.

    I know that this is a mountain of information, but hopefully it will
assist you. You may reply to me privately at wobconsult@sprynet.com if you
wish further assitance off-line....

Cheers,

WOB,
Atlanta

BTW. Try posting your CICS-related questions to ===> bit.listserv.cics-l

Phil Davey wrote in message <35EE41E5.AE90123B@powernet.co.uk>...
>Help Please,
>
…[63 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Help: COBOL/CICS Re-useable Working-Storage Problem

- **From:** Bill Lynch <wblynch@att.net>
- **Date:** 1998-09-04T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,alt.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<35EF7F40.B08D32BE@att.net>`
- **References:** `<35EE41E5.AE90123B@powernet.co.uk> <6sn3ud$m18$1@juliana.sprynet.com>`

```
WOB wrote:
> 
(snip)
> 
>     BTW, when you say DYNAMIC, it really isn't. DYNAMIC issues an MVS FETCH
> (such as a dynamically called batch program). But, in CICS, NODYNAM is
> required and CICS locates the PPT address and establishes addressability to
> the load module, much like an ASSEMBLER BALR 14,15. So much for particulars.

Bill, I'm curious about the above comment. When the original post said
it was being called dynamically I took that to mean the COBOL program
calls a data-name (not a literal) and that the called program has a PPT
entry. This is IBM's definition of dynamic calls under CICS (they made
the run time environment (currently LE/370) intelligent as to whether
it's batch or CICS). Conceptually a dynamic call in a CICS environment
is the same as one in batch.

Bill Lynch
```

#### ↳ Re: Help: COBOL/CICS Re-useable Working-Storage Problem

- **From:** rkrayhawk@aol.com (RKRayhawk)
- **Date:** 1998-09-03T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<1998090315582300.LAA00544@ladder01.news.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<35EE41E5.AE90123B@powernet.co.uk>`

```

On   Thu, 03 Sep 1998 08:14:45 +0100
Phil Davey <davey@powernet.co.uk>
asked

>>
Q1) I THINK that COBOL may separate the Procedure-Divn  from the
Working-Storage to allow:
one central copy of the Procedure-instructions but
each user to have their own copy of Working-Storage.
Perhaps someone can confirm this is probably whats happening.
<<

That is correct and will occur either if you invoke the  program as a
subprogram via COBOL CALL verb as well as if you invoke it with an EXEC CICS
LINK.

Both techniques make the table subprogarm part of the drivers overall
structure.  The COBOL and CICS facilities are designed to keep that structure
distinct from any other program structure within the system. 

The use of a common procedure division is simply an efficiency step.  The data,
constant or varible, must be kept distinct (according to the basic philosophy
of the tools you are using). 

This is a matter of structure, brought on by the manner of expression, a CALL
statement ( or also a LINK command).

What you may be after is a matter of scheduling.

Your ask, 
>>
Q2) What should I do to acheive the "objective" above.

... (single/central/constant copy.). <<

You may wish to schedule your program as a transaction oriented task.  This
will allow you to restrict the proliferation of the large table image (although
schedulable transactions are by no means restricted to just one copy, the
number of copies can be  controlled by CICS systems programmers).
Then a single copy (or relativley few copies) can be scheduled to handle the
talble activities in sequence.

That would require some redesign work.  The various invokers would have to be
prepared to go away and then come back when the table processor is scheduled
and then ends. The DFHCOMMAREA is intended to preseve the necessary information
while this round-robin sequence proceeds. Such a redesign may be too large in
scope.

Another approach is to place the table on a Temporary Storage Queue, and read
it into programs only when needed.
Potentially you could break the table into slightly smaller pieces and assign
them different ITEM numbers.

Yet another way is to reduce the table to some external file  (VSAM, of course)
and read only the records you need, .. and by  key.  VSAM can provide
surprisingly good performance if the table-like-file is basically locked into
memory because of constant hits.

I bet that you knew of most of these ideas already.  The Constant Table
Function is a fairly classic challenge in CICS.  Structuring the function as
part of your executable does proliferate the image into all tasks that invoke
the function.
The advantage is speed.  To trade out the huge storage penalty will introduce
complexity and cost you execution time.

The only way to avoid the penalties is to examine the table usage pattern.   If
you could divide the table into functional areas, then you could stick with the
lighting fast solution you have, get samller tables proliferated, and pay only
a slight penalty in program component management.



















Robert Rayhawk
RKRayhawk@aol.com
```

#### ↳ Re: Help: COBOL/CICS Re-useable Working-Storage Problem

- **From:** redsky@ibm.net (Thane Hubbell)
- **Date:** 1998-09-03T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** alt.cobol,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<Jl0PnHJ5PvPd-pn2-8vdvlqBW8yod@Dwight_Miller.iix.com>`
- **References:** `<35EE41E5.AE90123B@powernet.co.uk>`

```
On Thu, 3 Sep 1998 07:14:45, Phil Davey <davey@powernet.co.uk> wrote:

> Help Please,
> 
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> Working-Storage.
CICS loads a separate copy of working storage for each task instance. 
Period.

Move the table to a temp storage queue or an indexed file.  That will 
free up memory.
```

#### ↳ Re: Help: COBOL/CICS Re-useable Working-Storage Problem

- **From:** vbandke@ibm.net (Volker Bandke)
- **Date:** 1998-09-03T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,alt.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<35eed7f9.50960506@news2.ibm.net>`
- **References:** `<35EE41E5.AE90123B@powernet.co.uk>`

```
On Thu, 03 Sep 1998 08:14:45 +0100, Phil Davey <davey@powernet.co.uk>
wrote:

>Help Please,
>
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>Working-Storage.

The internal design of CICS requires that evfery CICS task gets a
separate copy of the programs working storage.  If you want a single
copy to be available to many programs, you have several possibilities:

1)  Load the data into a KSDS VSAM file and load it as a Data Table,
or a shared data table.  That makes the data available throgh EXEC
CICS commands to all applications in this CICS (or the CICSPLEX)

2) Use Temporary storage instead of VSAM

3) use DB2 instead of VSAM (ever heard of the guy that took a 38 ton
truck to buy a pack of cigarettes?)

4)  A little bit more involved, but doesn't need VSAM calls.  COBOL
programs can still you  indexing and/or subscripting to access the
table:

Create an Assembler program containing the static data:  This would
look similar to this:

MYTABLE  CSECT
	DC	CLxx'Data for first entry'
	DC	CLxx'Data for second entry'
	DC	CLxx'you get the idea...'
	...
	END

Assemble that program and link it into a module, MYTABLE, or any name
you choose.

The COBOL program needs the following to access the table


	WORKING-STORAGE SECTION.
	LINKAGE SECTION.
	01	MYTABLE.
		05  TABENTRY	 PIC X(xx) OCCURS nnn TIMES.
	PROCEDURE DIVISION.
		...
		EXEC CICS LOAD 
		    PROGRAM('MYTABLE') 
		    SET(ADDRESS OF MYTABLE
		    NOHANDLE
		END-EXEC	                	
		IF EIBRESP = DFHRESP(NORMAL)
		    now you can process TABENTRY(subscript)
		    ...


HTH
with kind regards

Volker Bandke
(BSP GmbH)
```

>  Does CICS support "linear data sets"?  If so,that too might be a possible

No, CICS doesn't (for application programming, that is)  The API is
restricted to ESDS, RRDS, and KSDS



with kind regards

Volker Bandke
(BSP GmbH)

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Help: COBOL/CICS Re-useable Working-Storage Problem

- **From:** Bill Lynch <wblynch@att.net>
- **Date:** 1998-09-04T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,alt.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<35EF7D6A.D989445@att.net>`
- **References:** `<35EE41E5.AE90123B@powernet.co.uk> <35eed7f9.50960506@news2.ibm.net> <35EF0A4E.42C0@ibm.net>`

```
Bal Oberoi wrote:
> 
(snip)
> 
> A neat solution! Any way a *batch* COBOL program could get
> addressability to MYTABLE?

Sure, if you're willing to CALL an ASM module (our batch system uses
this a lot). Post if you want a sample.

Bill Lynch

PS: The ASM's pretty basic (no pun intended), e.g.:

LOADER	CSECT
LOADER	AMODE 31
LOADER	RMODE ANY
	SAVE  (14,12)
	LR    BASER,R15
	USING LOADER,BASER
	LM    R2,R3,0(R1)          <<=== caller's parms
*                                I'm assuming R2 = A(table name)
*	                                      R3 = target area for table
(presumably in caller's working storage)
	LOAD  EP=(R2) 
	LTR   R15,R15
	BNZ   load_error

	STM   R0,R1,TABLE_ADDR     <<=== R0 = A(table)
*	                                 R1 = length(table) in doublewords
	SLL   R1,3                 <<=== multiply by 8 (to get the byte count)
	LR    R15,R1
	LR    R14,R3
	MVCL  R14,R0               <<=== move table to caller's area 
*	                      a programming palindrome, MVCL R14,R0 assembles
as x'0EE0' (fun for the feeble minded) <g>

	RETURN (14,12)



TABLE_ADDR  DC   2F'0'

This is right off the top - it's not reentrant and does not
provide/chain an RSA (which I would do in the real world). I'm assuming
you have register equates and that BASER is neither R2 nor R3. My
apologies if I remembered the parm for LOAD wrong. I'm sure there'll be
some suggestions/corrections, I never get these "off the top" coding
examples right. If you don't need to save the address of the table (so
you don't load it twice), then the code's reentrant. I've patterned this
after our main table loader, which is used for multiple tables.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Help: COBOL/CICS Re-useable Working-Storage Problem

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** Bal Oberoi <oberoi@ibm.net>
- **Date:** 1998-09-04T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,alt.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<35EFF4DC.4870@ibm.net>`
- **References:** `<35EE41E5.AE90123B@powernet.co.uk> <35eed7f9.50960506@news2.ibm.net> <35EF0A4E.42C0@ibm.net> <35EF7D6A.D989445@att.net>`

```
Bill Lynch wrote:

> > A neat solution! Any way a *batch* COBOL program could get
> > addressability to MYTABLE?
 

> 
> PS: The ASM's pretty basic (no pun intended), e.g.:
…[35 more quoted lines elided]…
> this after our main table loader, which is used for multiple tables.

Sounds like a good setup! Actually if one adopted the approach of using, 
say MYLOADR, to get access to MYTABLE1, MYTABLE2, etc. then this could  
be used as the common mechanism by *all* programs (batch or CICS, COBOL 
or Assembler) for the lookup of tables? 

If yes, then the coding sample of Volker for the COBOL CICS program 
would change from EXEC CICS LOAD PROGRAM('MYTABLE') ... to a plain CALL.
Since MYLOADR would have no changeable data itself, there would be no 
drawback in the CALL to MYLOADR being static. Any holes in this 
reasoning?

Nice weekend everyone!
Bal
----- 
oberoi@ibm.net
http://www.geocities.com/~oberoi/mainfrme.html
-----
"The universe is full of magical things, 
patiently waiting for our wits to grow sharper." - Eden Phillpotts

## Thread: Need an Inventory Program — 7 msgs

### Need an Inventory Program

- **From:** acecobra69@aol.com (AceCobra69)
- **Date:** 1998-09-04T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<1998090400334300.UAA00593@ladder01.news.aol.com>`

```
Hello.  A friend of mine gave me a COBOL compiler to help me with my business. 
I need to make an inventory report for my small business.  I know nothing of
COBOL at this time, but I'm trying to learn by some books he gave me.  If
someone could come up with a relatively simple Inventory report program, I'd
appreciate it.  Here's some of the guidelines I have worked out.

INPUT

Columns        Data
   9-13            Item Number
  34-48           Item Description
  49-50           Quantity on Hand
   51-52          Quantity Sold
   53-54          Store Number

OUTPUT
   The output will need to be a listing of input fields along with a generated
internal colde.  The internal code is a combination of the store number, the
last three digits of the item number, and the first two characters of the item
description.  Like I need store number 89 and item number 12345 with a
description of HAND SAWS would have an internal code of 89345HA.

Output as follows

Print Positions     Information
 6-7                     Store Number
13-17                   Item Number
23-27                   Item Description
43-44                   Quantity on Hand
51-52                   Quantity Sold
59-65                    Internal Code


I appreciate any help!  Feel free to email me.
AceCobra69
```

Here's the basic rule of invenrtory I learned back in the 60's:

New balance = (Old balance + receipts + transfers in) - (transfers out +
deliveries + spoilage)

Still works after 30 years,

HTH,
Bill Lynch

## Thread: Using VARCHAR with SQL Server 6.5 ODBC Driver (NetExpress 2.0 on NT 4.0) — 3 msgs

_Topics: [`Databases and SQL`](topics.md#databases)_

### Using VARCHAR with SQL Server 6.5 ODBC Driver (NetExpress 2.0 on NT 4.0)

- **From:** "Gene Webb" <gene.webb@newsguy.com>
- **Date:** 1998-09-03T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,alt.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<6sm8s9$1bo@enews2.newsguy.com>`

```
I have a table with a column defined as varchar (255). I have defined the
following data structure in my program.

01 sqlTestPath.
49 sqlTestPath-Len pic 9(4) comp-5.
49 sqlTestPath-Dat pic x(255).

I move a value to the sqlTestPath-Dat of say all 'x'.
I then move 255 to the sqlTestPath-Len.
I then try and do an insert on this row and receive the following error:

Current Operation: (Insert )
SQLCODE: (-000000260)
([Microsoft][ODBC SQL Server Driver][SQL Server]Disallowed implicit
conversion from datatype 'text' to datatype 'varchar' Table:
'Storage.dbo.DayOfWork', Column: 'DataPath' Use the CONVERT function to run
this query.

If I generate a data form with the NetExpress wizard it uses a pic x(255)
field. I have also tried that with the same results.

Here is the DDL for the table

/* Microsoft SQL Server - Scripting */
/* Server: WEBGEN0U */
/* Database: Storage */
/* Creation Date 9/3/98 8:38:51 AM */

/****** Object: Table dbo.DayOfWork Script Date: 9/3/98 8:38:51 AM ******/
if exists (select * from sysobjects where id = object_id('dbo.DayOfWork')
and sysstat & 0xf = 3)
drop table dbo.DayOfWork
GO

/****** Object: Table dbo.DayOfWork Script Date: 9/3/98 8:38:51 AM ******/
CREATE TABLE dbo.DayOfWork (
SiteId int NOT NULL ,
ProcessCenter int NOT NULL ,
DateOfWork datetime NOT NULL ,
JulianDate int NOT NULL ,
DataLocation int NOT NULL ,
RecordCount numeric(18, 0) NOT NULL ,
SegmentCount int NOT NULL ,
DataPath varchar (255) NOT NULL ,
CreateUserid char (12) NOT NULL ,
CreateDate datetime NOT NULL ,
UpdateUserid char (12) NOT NULL ,
UpdateDate datetime NOT NULL
)
GO

Here is the insert statement

*
EXEC SQL
INSERT into dbo.DayOfWork
(
SiteId,
ProcessCenter,
DateOfWork,
JulianDate,
DataLocation,
RecordCount,
SegmentCount,
DataPath,
CreateUserid,
CreateDate,
UpdateUserid,
UpdateDate
)
VALUES
(
:sqlSiteId,
:sqlProcessCenter,
:sqlFullDateOfWork,
:sqlJulianDate,
:sqlDataLocation,
:sqlRecordCount,
:sqlSegmentCount,
:atestPath,
:sqlCreateUserid,
:sqlCreateDate,
:sqlUpdateUserid,
:sqlUpdateDate
)
END-EXEC.

Here are the other host variables
*
01 sqlHostVariables.
05 sqlSiteId pic s9(9) comp-5.
05 sqlProcessCenter pic s9(9) comp-5.
05 sqlDOW.
10 sqlDateOfWork pic x(10).
10 filler pic x(09).
88 initSqlDateOfWork value ' 12:00:00'.
05 sqlFullDateOfWork pic x(19).
05 sqlJulianDate pic s9(9) comp-5.
05 sqlDataLocation pic s9(9) comp-5.
05 sqlRecordCount pic s9(18) comp-3.
05 sqlSegmentCount pic s9(9) comp-3.
05 sqlDataPath pic x(255).
05 sqlCreateUserid pic x(12).
05 sqlCreateDate pic x(19).
05 sqlUpdateUserid pic x(12).
05 sqlUpdateDate pic x(19).
```

You indicate that you have a drive c which is fat and drive d which is NTFS
... if you are saving files to the c drive you will get an 8.3 (fat) name
when you do a directory but a long file name when you use explore as NT
keeps track of the extended attributes.  If you are saving to drive d, do
you receive an error when you open the file, etc.  Where do you see the 8.3
name in dos or from explorer?  If you think it is the compiler make a call
to the winapi CreateFileA and see if the name gets saved correctly.

#### ↳ Re: AN IDEA?

- **From:** redsky@ibm.net (Thane Hubbell)
- **Date:** 1998-09-03T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<Jl0PnHJ5PvPd-pn2-qP3qtPBszCOD@Dwight_Miller.iix.com>`
- **References:** `<35E8D882.1B6C579A@mindspring.com>`

```
On Sun, 30 Aug 1998 04:43:46, Mike Ganas <skidmike@mindspring.com> 
wrote:

> and i want f12 to back up to the previous field.  hows about a table to
> tell the program to go to depending on f12=back up and f4=done with

this procedure was used by the original microfocus product 
"CO-Writer".

It's a valid approach, but hard to maintain.  Everything is fine until
you insert a field.  Then the whole table has to shift and you have to
change ALL of the numbers after the inserted field.

However, if you added a third field - Field ID say - and used that to 
point to the next and prior field ID - and then looked up the fields 
position, maintenance would be much easier.
```

- **From:** Mike Ganas <skidmike@mindspring.com>

that occurred to me on paper.  right now i'm still considering it, but
that will come later (way later) in the project.  it's gonna be hard for
our shop to quit hitting <esc> when we dump "pipedream 3.x" and start
using <f4> instead of <f2>
btw, tech hint for newbies in the group.  when you have a dll that does
math on a regular basis, clear all computational fields as soon as the
proc div starts.  i learned that the hard way, 2 nights scratching my
head and cursing and cleaning my gun while staring malevolently at this
pc.

In message <<Jl0PnHJ5PvPd-pn2-qP3qtPBszCOD@Dwight_Miller.iix.com>> redsky@ibm.net  writes:
> 
> It's a valid approach, but hard to maintain.  Everything is fine until
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> position, maintenance would be much easier.

Of course it is relatively to have these tables generated
automatically.  It is not too difficult to write a program
that can process a text file of the screen layout and 
generate some Cobol code that can be COPYed into your
program.

I find that it is more interesting, too.  Rather than 
manually edit tables of positions and sequencing, I change
the text screen (much easier to visualise too) and, if
necessary, add a new feature into the code generator.
```

<a id="t-015b50c791"></a>
## Thread: COMP-2 precision loss in MF Cobol — 4 msgs

_Topics: [`Compilers and vendors`](topics.md#compilers) · [`Language features and syntax`](topics.md#syntax)_

### COMP-2 precision loss in MF Cobol

- **From:** Lynne Kamckey <nibor@rogerswave.ca>
- **Date:** 1998-09-03T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<35EEE524.1230A62D@rogerswave.ca>`

```
Any suggestions?

I have two data types in COBOL

01  n1                    pic S9(8)V9(6).
01  n2                    usage comp-2.

The problem that I'm seeing happen is when I move data from n1 to n2 the
value is physically changed and yet COBOL does not recognize it.  I am
then passing n2 to a C program - an ODBC interface - which then inserts
the value into a table and truncates it.

Example:

move -8.36 to n1
(n1 now contains -8.36)
move n1 to n2
(n2 now contains -8.3599999....)
 n1 = n2  is true

[note: even if I assign -8.36 directly to n2, it becomes
-8.35999999.....]

n2 is passed as a parameter to a double in C which then inserts it into
an Access table
defined at 2 decimals of precision, -8.35.

Is there a MF COBOL data type comparable to C's float?  Will I have the
same problem with this type too?

Does anybody have any suggestions on how I might improve this
precision?  Or shall I just chalk this one up to the quirkiness of COBOL
and move on?

TIA,
Lynne Kamckey
nibor@rogerswave.ca
```

#### ↳ Re: COMP-2 precision loss in MF Cobol

- **From:** "Leif Svalgaard" <leif@ibm.net>
- **Date:** 1998-09-03T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<35eee8b7.0@news1.ibm.net>`
- **References:** `<35EEE524.1230A62D@rogerswave.ca>`

```
it is not a Cobol quirk.
It is the nature of floating point that you cannot represent
some (most) numbers exactly.
You may want to add to n2 a little bit, e.g. 0.0000004
to get the first six digits after the point to match Cobols,
but the bottom line is that YOU CANNOT REPRESENT
VALUES EXACTLY WITH FLOATING POINT NUMBERS.

Lynne Kamckey wrote in message <35EEE524.1230A62D@rogerswave.ca>...
>Any suggestions?
>
…[35 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: COMP-2 precision loss in MF Cobol

- **From:** "Donald Tees" <donald@willmack.com>
- **Date:** 1998-09-03T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<6sna6p$p8n$1@news.igs.net>`
- **References:** `<35EEE524.1230A62D@rogerswave.ca> <35eee8b7.0@news1.ibm.net>`

```

>>Is there a MF COBOL data type comparable to C's float?  Will I have the
>>same problem with this type too?
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>>and move on?


What you have to realize is that the fractions that are repeating
decimals are base dependant.  1/5, for example, is a repeating
decimal in binary.  As soon as you go floating point, you are going
to have to program for that.  In fact, Cobol is one of the *ONLY*
languages that tends to stick to base ten, just to avoid such
problems.  Your problems started when you started to think in C.

If you are going to do money stuff, then get rid of this idea that
floating point is useful. It creates untold problems.  Work with
either integers or stick to Cobol standard data.
```

#### ↳ Re: COMP-2 precision loss in MF Cobol

- **From:** riplin@kcbbs.gen.nz (Richard Plinston)
- **Date:** 1998-09-04T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3298246.3722.24810@kcbbs.gen.nz>`
- **References:** `<35EEE524.1230A62D@rogerswave.ca>`

```
In message <<35EEE524.1230A62D@rogerswave.ca>> Lynne Kamckey <nibor@rogerswave.ca> writes:
> 
> 01  n1                    pic S9(8)V9(6).
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
> -8.35999999.....]

This is perfectly 'normal' for floating-point and C.  It seems
that it is _impossible_ to represent -8.36 in a double, the
closest it can get is -8.35999999999.  Given 64 bits it is
only possible to represent 2^64 different values.  However,
the dynamic range of a floating point number may be 2^-256
to 2^256 (or so) with is hugely larger.  This means that
only certain discrete points on the range can be represented
and -8.36 is not one of the points.

> 
> n2 is passed as a parameter to a double in C which then inserts it into
> an Access table
> defined at 2 decimals of precision, -8.35.

It seems that it is truncating and not rounding.  Perhaps
you can use this information to cause your own rounding
to apply.  Add or subtract 0.005 to the number before
passing it and then the truncation will give the value
you want (probably).

> 
> Is there a MF COBOL data type comparable to C's float?  Will I have the
> same problem with this type too?

COMP-2 _is_ floating point, double in fact.  COMP-1 is a C 'float'
and has the same problems.

> 
> Does anybody have any suggestions on how I might improve this
> precision?  Or shall I just chalk this one up to the quirkiness of COBOL
> and move on?

It has nothing to do with 'Cobol quirkiness' and everything to
do with the quirkiness of floating-point numbers.  This is
why Cobol does not normally use floating point and why C is
a poor choice for a business language.
```

---

<a id="t-d379dba2df"></a>
## Thread: OT Warning (was: Y2K: History, what's being done, how to prepare) — 1 msgs

_Topics: [`Y2K and Year-2000 remediation`](topics.md#y2k) · [`Help requests and how-to`](topics.md#help) · [`Off-topic and spam`](topics.md#spam)_

### Re: OT Warning (was: Y2K: History, what's being done, how to prepare)

- **From:** paulr@bix.com (paulr)
- **Date:** 1998-09-03T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<6sksq0$top@lotho.delphi.com>`
- **References:** `<35e027ed.21890305@news.supernews.com> <VA.00000e95.000330e4@access.digex.net> <6sfsak$7d3@lotho.delphi.com> <35EC299E.8240EC80@att.net> <35ed8675.25596436@netnews.worldnet.att.net>`

```
You missed the point- they didn't place a hold on the 
committed funds - they placed a $50 hold on a $5
transaction. In fact, they did it on *every* trasnsaction.

I think most people would expect a hold on the actual 
charge amount, but 10X the amount is a little bit 
excessive, wouldn't you say? 
 
Bet it was written in PROGRESS or something, and 
goes TU in 1999. (*sigh*)

-Paul



Ramone (Ramone@worldnet.att.net) wrote:
: On 1 Sep 1998 17:06:18 GMT, Bill Lynch <wblynch@att.net> wrote:

: >paulr wrote:
: >> 
: >(snip)
: >> Maybe it works different in G.B., but over here, the
: >> system is crazy. We simply are NOT going to use a direct debit
: >> card for anything except CASH withdrawals anymore. It is
: >> interesting to note that these holds do NOT happen when
: >> you use a normal credit card, only when you use a debit
: >> card
: >
: >Wishful thinking, Dude. Holds are placed on CR card accounts for a
: >variety of reasons (I bumped into that years ago, this is the first I've
: >heard of holds on a DB card - doesn't make sense to me?). I rent cars
: >from time to time - a hold is always placed on my CR card for more than
: >my rental should be - to cover them if I keep the car longer (which I
: >have done occasionally) or decide to play bumper cars (which I haven't).
: >I must be naive, or something, but I thought the big feature of DB cards
: >was that the merchant gets his/her funds immediately, no waiting.

: That's the way the system appears externally.  How it works internally
: is a little different.  Banks to not post money to transaction
: accounts real-time.  If the merchant has an account at your bank, the
: debit clears to his account when nightly posting is run.  A hold is
: placed on your account to commit the funds until the debit is posted.
: If the merchant has no account at your bank, the debit must clear
: through the fed, and a hold is placed on your account to commit the
: funds until the debit arrives from the fed and is posted.  Banks used
: to require the merchant have an account at the bank to speed
: processing, but merchants simply refused to handle that bank's debit
: cards, so the requirement was dropped.  An item can take several days
: to clear through the fed.    

: > What
: >purpose does the hold serve - other than to freeze my money so the bank
: >can lend it out in overnight funds?
: >
: It serves the purpose of preventing you from going to another merchant
: and using the same funds on a duplicate transaction.  It's SOP.  Get
: used to it.
: >
: >This is considered extortion when a
: >loan shark does it.
: >
: Wrong.
: >
: >I'm definitely going to check with my bank to see if they're placing
: >holds when I use my ATM card to buy groceries.
: >
: I fail to see what difference it makes.  Once you commit the funds
: with a debit card they are no longer yours.  Why do you care how the
: transaction is handled?  Besides, the procedure is governed by strict
: federal regulations (which I make my living implementing), so you can
: bitch about it all you want but you won't change anything.    
: >
: >I don't have an actual DB
: >card, yet.
: >
: I've never had one, and I'll never get one until the banks require it.
: It's coming, but hopefully not in our lifetime.  We are simply
: reaching the limits of the technology in handing the tremendous
: volumes of paper transactions.  Debit cards are the eventual answer.

: >
: >Bill Lynch
```

Ramone wrote:
> 
(snip)
> >I must be naive, or something, but I thought the big feature of DB cards
> >was that the merchant gets his/her funds immediately, no waiting.

So I guess the answer here is "Yes".

> That's the way the system appears externally.  How it works internally
> is a little different.  Banks to not post money to transaction
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
> to clear through the fed.

Ramone, thanks for a very informative post. Your description makes
sense. OTOH, I'm sure the descriptions I've seen regarding DB cards
describe funds flitting about instantaneously as people do transactions.
Always a gap, I guess, between the sales brochures and the actual
implementation:-)

I understand the need for a hold until one's purchase clears, but why
$50 for $10 worth of gas?

Bill Lynch

<a id="t-7488bdbd38"></a>
## Thread: OT Warning (was: Y2K: History, what's being done, how to prepare) — 1 msgs

_Topics: [`Y2K and Year-2000 remediation`](topics.md#y2k) · [`Help requests and how-to`](topics.md#help) · [`Off-topic and spam`](topics.md#spam)_

### Re: OT Warning (was: Y2K: History, what's being done, how to prepare)

- **From:** Bill Lynch <wblynch@att.net>
- **Date:** 1998-09-03T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<35EE1A2F.B5E460E8@att.net>`
- **References:** `<35e027ed.21890305@news.supernews.com> <VA.00000e95.000330e4@access.digex.net> <6sfsak$7d3@lotho.delphi.com> <35EC299E.8240EC80@att.net> <35ed8675.25596436@netnews.worldnet.att.net>`

```
Ramone wrote:
> 
(snip)
> >I'm definitely going to check with my bank to see if they're placing
> >holds when I use my ATM card to buy groceries.
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> bitch about it all you want but you won't change anything.

(Sorry to all for duplicate responses to the same post)

Of course I don't expect to use the same $50 I spent on groceries to buy
new sneakers, anymore than I expect to use the same $50 bill on two
different purchases. The issue here is the difference between the hold
and purchase amounts, and the time for which the difference is tied up
and unavailable to me (or Paul, etc.).

What I *can* change, if my bank is putting a hold of, say, $250 when I
buy $50 worth of groceries is to pay for the groceries with cash & to
withdraw more cash if I'm low - no hold in excess of the actual amount
withdrawn on *that*, is there? I do not expect to change the way the Fed
or the banks do business.

Bill Lynch
```

#### ↳ Re: Should I learn Cobol before VB

- **From:** carloss940@aol.com (CarlosS940)
- **Date:** 1998-09-04T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<1998090402530600.WAA24718@ladder03.news.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<6snjao$q54$3@news-1.news.gte.net>`

```
I already have basic programming knowledge, but not BASIC that is not a good

```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Should I learn Cobol before VB

- **From:** Jeff Farkas <Jeffrey.Farkas@gte.net>
- **Date:** 1998-09-03T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<6snmn2$227$1@news-1.news.gte.net>`
- **References:** `<6snjao$q54$3@news-1.news.gte.net> <1998090402530600.WAA24718@ladder03.news.aol.com>`

```


CarlosS940 wrote:

> I already have basic programming knowledge, but not BASIC that is not a good
> programming language.

BASIC is a good language to start programming with. Assuming that you
are past that point I would go to COBOL if you want to make some money.
COBOL will pay the bills while VB is for thrills. First learn Cobol and become
good at that. Then take on VB if your interested in it. Keeping in mind that:
1) VB is proprietary. So you live and die with Bill Gates. 2) VB versions
come out fast enough to keep your wheels spinning trying to keep up, IMO.
3) The use of VB is as much a political decision as it is a logical one. I have
seen VB projects rewritten to PowerBuileder just because the new
bosses liked PowerBuilder.

IMO, Learn Cobol then try out C/Pascal or better yet both. Then you could go
to other languages a lot easier since quite a few of the new ones are
built upon C such as  Perl or JavaScript.
```

## Thread: 'Cutesie-Poo' Programmer's Tricks — 5 this month / 11 total

_Thread started [1998-08](1998-08.md#t-e06aa7444e)._

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Robotic Rights

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "Narga User" <narga@maties.sun.ac.za>
- **Date:** 1998-09-04T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,alt.books.isaac-asimov,comp.robotics.misc
- **Message-ID:** `<6softm$bfn$1@news.adamastor.ac.za>`
- **References:** `<6phc0c$eli@bgtnsc02.worldnet.att.net> <1998081818402000.OAA27687@ladder01.news.aol.com> <6rcjbk$3di$1@callisto.clark.net> <35DBA6B1.710E@netbox.com> <6rhjk8$bj7$1@callisto.clark.net> <35DEA4B0.6D2521D4@hankel.mersinet.co.uk> <bvUD1.2680$fw3.3852744@news2.mia.bellsouth.net> <6rp6t5$aob@lotho.delphi.com> <35E148DF.1A16C1E9@hankel.mersinet.co.uk> <35E22BEB.6CC176E2@att.net> <35E5661A.4756E53@hankel.mersinet.co.uk> <35E5AB24.EC452C2E@att.net> <35E96688.B8E343A0@hankel.mersinet.co.uk> <35EAFE68.48D1B163@IMN.nl>`

```
In as much as Robot rights are concerned (they are , after all, theoretical
constructs) , Robots should heve been equipped with some basic undelying
fundamentals , in a set of sophistication that Asimov gave to the Solarian
and Spacer Robots in books such as 'The Naked Sun' and 'The Robots of Dawn'.
Here are some examples :
1. A Robot's design should contain assembly parts, that , upon removal , are
so designed as to cause injury to a Human being, thus preventing the Robot
from self disassembly from a Human, as the First Law takes precedence in
such cases.
2. Robot self repair should require tools that no Robot has immediate access
to. This prevents the Robot from undergoing due processes of assuming harm
done to a human being as a result of inability to perform disassembly , and
also as a due consequence of the First Prerogative of dissasembly.
3. A Robot should not have the knowledge required for self dissasembly, as
this will prevent any conflict with the First and Second Prerogatives.

This set of short ' Laws' would have prevented the incident that nearly
caused severe problems with Andrew Martin on his first trip to the library.

Another set of 'Laws' could govern Robotic adherence to the Second Law :
1. Robots should not have to obey orders where irrepairable harm done to
themselves would harm their employer/owner in direct disobedience to the
First Law.
2. Robots should not harm other Robots as a direct form of harm to a Human
owner/employer of the said Robots, which would be a direct violation of the
First Law.

This is fairly comprehensive : Robots should not be able to harm themselves
or fellow Robots, or be able to Irrepairably damage themselves by humans
giving orders of malicious intent.

Comments, anyone ?

Martin La Grange
<9772790@narga.sun.ac.za>
```

```
One thing about killing 2 million robots.

Robots cost a lot of money and surley that money could be put to use
that would save many many more people. Would you be prepared to allow
the destruction of every single car (rubish example but so what) to save
one person's life?

As a car could save lots of peoples life so can a robot. There must be a
point where a robot can assess that its continued existance will
actually save more lives - hence the zeroth law!
```

### Anyone handled AIX IBM Cobol 2GB file size limit

- **From:** "Todd A. Wood" <tawood_at_ibm_dot_net@NO.SPAM>
- **Date:** 1998-09-04T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<35EFC413.DB1608DC@NO.SPAM>`

```
Has anyone using IBM Cobol for AIX (AIX v4.2 or higher) been able
to get around the 2 GB file size limit imposed by the Cobol runtime?

i.e.
Open an input file which is stored on AIX v4.2+ of size 4 GB and
process the entire file inside COBOL open/read/write/close routines?

I am assuming that some external linkage to C routines would work,
which I have done in the past.

These are generated Cobol programs, so if some sort of linking to
C routines "transparently" is possible would be nice... like
a custom cobol runtime library.

Just fishing for solutions....  hopefully someone has tackled
or seen this problem

Sincerely,
Todd A. Wood

(for email replies:
 substitue _at_ with @ and _dot_ with . and remove @NO.SPAM)
```

#### ↳ Re: Anyone handled AIX IBM Cobol 2GB file size limit

- **From:** "Leif Svalgaard" <leif@ibm.net>
- **Date:** 1998-09-04T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<35efdc0e.0@news1.ibm.net>`
- **References:** `<35EFC413.DB1608DC@NO.SPAM>`

```

Todd A. Wood wrote in message <35EFC413.DB1608DC@NO.SPAM>...
>Has anyone using IBM Cobol for AIX (AIX v4.2 or higher) been able
>to get around the 2 GB file size limit imposed by the Cobol runtime?

I thought this was an AIX (i.e. Unix) restriction.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Anyone handled AIX IBM Cobol 2GB file size limit

- **From:** "Todd A. Wood" <tawood_at_ibm_dot_net@NO.SPAM>
- **Date:** 1998-09-04T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<35EFEC4A.77054AC5@NO.SPAM>`
- **References:** `<35EFC413.DB1608DC@NO.SPAM> <35efdc0e.0@news1.ibm.net>`

```
Leif Svalgaard wrote:
> 
> Todd A. Wood wrote in message <35EFC413.DB1608DC@NO.SPAM>...
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> I thought this was an AIX (i.e. Unix) restriction.

There is no 2 GB restriction with AIX 4.2+ when the file system is
created with large file support.  However... the Cobol file
handler must be able to handle larger than 2 GB.

Todd.
```

## Thread: Developing with graphic and character screens with MF — 2 msgs

### Developing with graphic and character screens with MF

- **From:** "Euromercante" <EUROMERCANTE@MAIL.TELEPAC.PT>
- **Date:** 1998-09-04T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<01bdd7e6$ec584660$LocalHost@p200>`

```

Our development with Netexpress and Dialog System (V2),  under Windows98/NT
has graphic and character screens.

The main screen is graphic, and when we call a second screen witch is
character, the screen appears,
but with no "Focus". We need to click with mouse to get it active. When
second screen is graphic, it
works ok.

Other problem is how can i control the caracter windows, because until  the
moment i call a character screen
program, and when i exit, this character window continue maximized.

Can i have controle to minimize/maximize  or close this kind of window?


Many Thanks
EP
```

#### ↳ Re: Developing with graphic and character screens with MF

- **From:** "Gael Wilson" <gw@mfltd.co.uk>
- **Date:** 1998-09-04T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<01bdd817$2ca266d0$905481c1@gw-nt>`
- **References:** `<01bdd7e6$ec584660$LocalHost@p200>`

```
You can obtain the window handle for the text window by calling
PC_WIN_HANDLE. Once it has been obtained you can call the ShowWindow API to
set focus or maximize/minimize. However, you should not attempt to close
the window because that will cause the run-time to execute a STOP RUN.

Euromercante <EUROMERCANTE@MAIL.TELEPAC.PT> wrote in article
<01bdd7e6$ec584660$LocalHost@p200>...
> 
> Our development with Netexpress and Dialog System (V2),  under
Windows98/NT
> has graphic and character screens.
> 
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…

the
> moment i call a character screen
> program, and when i exit, this character window continue maximized.
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…

<a id="t-aa4583ca6a"></a>
## Thread: Right Justify Routine — 1 msgs

### Right Justify Routine

- **From:** redsky@ibm.net (Thane Hubbell)
- **Date:** 1998-09-04T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<Jl0PnHJ5PvPd-pn2-XiTbHJiS5z0r@Dwight_Miller.iix.com>`

```
Here's a little ditty some of you might be able to use.  Also, I am 
sure someone can post an improvement!

 Identification Division.
 Program-Id.  Test3.
* right justify
 Environment Division.
 Configuration Section.
 Source-Computer.  IBM-PC.
 Object-Computer.  IBM-PC.
 Data Division.
 Working-Storage Section.
 01  input-field    pic x(30)        value spaces.
 01  output-field   pic x(30)        value spaces.
 01  counter        pic 9(9) comp    value zeros.
 Procedure Division.
 test3-start.
     display "Enter field: "
     accept input-field
     if input-field > spaces
        perform varying counter from 1 by 1
                until function reverse (input-field) (counter:1) > 
space
          continue
        end-perform
        move function reverse (input-field) (counter:) to output-field
        move function reverse (output-field) to input-field
     end-if
     display "*" input-field "*"
     Stop Run
     .
```

## Thread: Fujitsu V4 Dll's — 23 msgs

_Topics: [`Compilers and vendors`](topics.md#compilers)_

### Re: Fujitsu V4 Dll's

- **From:** "Donald Tees" <donald@willmack.com>
- **Date:** 1998-09-07T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<6t1m1s$343$1@news.igs.net>`
- **References:** `<6t0ok5$5um$1@news.igs.net> <35f3ee49.0@news1.ibm.net>`

```

Leif Svalgaard wrote in message <35f3ee49.0@news1.ibm.net>...
>
>My ultimate goal in one big EXE, or next best:
>one small EXE and one big DLL.
>This is my midweek (and everyday!) whine.


Got it!
First, you set up a DLL in the project.  Do all the standard
stuff and build it.  Now, what happens is that the build creates
TWO files, XXX.DLL, and XXX.OBJ.  The xxx.obj references
the xxx.dll, so you have to add XXX.LIB where XXX is the DLL
name to the library folder of the program that uses it.
example:
main.cbl is going to call suba.cbl and subb.cbl from mainsub.dll.
1. build mainsub.dll as if it was an exe file. mainsub.lib will be
created in the same area. mainsub's source file folder should contain
suba.cbl and subb.cbl.
2. add main.exe to the program, an put main.cbl in the source
file, then put mainsub.LIB in the library file.  Mainsub.DLL never
gets mentioned in any MAIN folder, only MAINSUB.LIB.
3. Build the whole lot.

That will work as long as every CALL is of the form CALL "SUBA" etc.
If you are going to dynamically call as in:
    MOVE "SUBA" TO SUBROUTINE-NAME.
    CALL SUBROUTINE-NAME.
then it will not work.  There should be a way to force *that* as
well, but I have not figured it out yet.
```

#### ↳ Re: Fujitsu V4 Dll's

- **From:** "Gene Webb" <gene.webb@newsguy.com>
- **Date:** 1998-09-07T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol

- **References:** `<6t0ok5$5um$1@news.igs.net> <35f3ee49.0@news1.ibm.net> <6t1m1s$343$1@news.igs.net>`

```

following set theVariable to entry "NameOfDLL.DLL"

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Fujitsu V4 Dll's

- **From:** "Gene Webb" <gene.webb@newsguy.com>
- **Date:** 1998-09-07T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<6t2aml$qvs@enews1.newsguy.com>`
- **References:** `<6t0ok5$5um$1@news.igs.net> <35f3ee49.0@news1.ibm.net> <6t1m1s$343$1@news.igs.net> <6t1rif$mog@enews2.newsguy.com> <6t21jk$g7b$1@news.igs.net>`

```
working-storage.
01 pgmToCall pic x(8).
01 aProcedure procedure-pointer.
Procedure Division.

    move "SUBNAME" to pgmToCall.
    set aProcedure to entry pgmToCall.

Try something like that.  It should force that DLL to be loaded.

- **From:** "Leif Svalgaard" <leif@ibm.net>

Richard Plinston wrote in message <3298251.79765.25728@kcbbs.gen.nz>...

writes:

From a developer's point of view that is indeed to be preferred.
Form a deployment point of view one would prefer to have
ONE self-contained package.

You can replace a module with an enhancement, but you can
also replace it with one with a bug and all the sudden the
application doesn't work anymore.

Anyway, what I want is the CHOICE. If I or my customer think
that many small DLLs is the way to go, let us have it.
But if I or my customer want one big EXE, let us have that
too. Let the choice be ours.

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Fujitsu V4 Dll's

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "Fujitsu Software" <cobol@adtools.com>
- **Date:** 1998-09-10T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<6t953k$kp$1@nntp2.ba.best.com>`
- **References:** `<35f576bf.0@news1.ibm.net> <3298251.79765.25728@kcbbs.gen.nz>`

```
We understand your requirement for a single EXE which contains the necessary
Fujitsu COBOL Runtime components.

Producing one single native code EXE is a very rudimentary native code
generator.  The Fujitsu COBOL native code generator is very complex and has
significant optimizations for producing reentrant native object code which
uses global data structures and reusable Runtime System DLLs.

This is not a simple fix, this is a major architectural change and requires
a massive about of work to the Fujitsu COBOL compiler and native code
generator which will have limited appeal especially in multi-user high
volume transaction oriented COBOL applications.

We constantly reevaluating product feature priorities and continue to look
at the viability for generating a single EXE with all the necessary Runtime
components.  Unfortunately at this time, it is not high on our list of
priorities.  That said, we are customer driven and need to hear from our
customers as to where we invest our resources.

Fujitsu Software Corporation
Developer Tools Group
Phone:  (408) 428-0500
FAX:     (408) 428-0600
Email:   cobol@adtools.com
Web:    http://www.adtools.com


Richard Plinston wrote in message <3298251.79765.25728@kcbbs.gen.nz>...
>In message <<35f576bf.0@news1.ibm.net>> "Leif Svalgaard" <leif@ibm.net>
writes:
>>
>> I think you are missing the point here.
…[14 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Please, no double speak -- it is double plus Ungood!

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "Kevin G. Rhoads" <T_Rhoads@NoSpam.CLASSIC.MSN.COM>
- **Date:** 1998-09-11T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<01bdddf2$f0c66580$LocalHost@stupidwin95>`
- **References:** `<35f576bf.0@news1.ibm.net> <3298251.79765.25728@kcbbs.gen.nz> <6t953k$kp$1@nntp2.ba.best.com>`

```
>Producing one single native code EXE is a very rudimentary native code
>generator.  
Huh?  That is not a code-generator issue, that is a LINKER issue!

>The Fujitsu COBOL native code generator is very complex and has
>significant optimizations for producing reentrant native object code which
>uses global data structures and reusable Runtime System DLLs.
Great!  Glad to hear it!

Now just recompile and reassemble the source for the "Runtime System DLLs"
and then DON'T LINK IT into DLLs  instead LIB it into LIBRARIES!  Give
us real honest-to God libraries instead of import libs that insert windows systems
calls to dyna-link to the DLLs.  With real libraries we can STATICALLY link
(not a hardwired name in a dyna-link) the routines.  For Win32 code there is
no 65,536 byte segmentation issues now a DOS 640k limit, so the size of 
your (no doubt also very complex and highly optimized) run-time code
will not be an issue.

Once you provide libraries we can use the object files (you know, those .OBJ thingies your very
compex native code generator produces) and a LINKER and make
EXEs containing all the run-time code without any need for DLLs.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Please, no double speak -- it is double

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** riplin@kcbbs.gen.nz (Richard Plinston)
- **Date:** 1998-09-12T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3298254.72398.13653@kcbbs.gen.nz>`
- **References:** `<01bdddf2$f0c66580$LocalHost@stupidwin95>`

```
In message <<01bdddf2$f0c66580$LocalHost@stupidwin95>> "Kevin G. Rhoads" <T_Rhoads@NoSpam.CLASSIC.MSN.COM> writes:
> >generator.  
> Huh?  That is not a code-generator issue, that is a LINKER issue!

What was being asked for was _dynamic_ linking to within a
single EXE or DLL.  That is the program wants to do
CALL Program-Name and _not_ CALL "Program" to allow for
variations in which routine is actually called and then
have the run-time search the .EXE and any .DLLs looking
for a match on the text name.

This is _not_ a linker issue.

> 
> Now just recompile and reassemble the source for the "Runtime System DLLs"
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> (not a hardwired name in a dyna-link) the routines.  For Win32 code there is

Why do you think that the code can be 'just' statically linked
into the .EXEs ?  You are taking a very simplistic view of
how the code may have been developed, this is not DOS. Now
it _may_ be possible to link it all together, or it may not
for very many reasons, such as threading.

> no 65,536 byte segmentation issues now a DOS 640k limit, so the size of 
> your (no doubt also very complex and highly optimized) run-time code
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> EXEs containing all the run-time code without any need for DLLs.

But _why_ do you want this ?  I want shared dynamic libraries
because I may run several copies of the system (so that one
is doing customer enquiry, one order entry, one ...) and I
don't see the necessity for several copies of the support
routines to be memory resident.

You seem to be taking a very DOS-centric view.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Challenge: I'll do it for Fujitsu

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** "Kevin G. Rhoads" <T_Rhoads@NoSpam.CLASSIC.MSN.COM>
- **Date:** 1998-09-12T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<01bddea2$572bd300$LocalHost@stupidwin95>`
- **References:** `<01bdddf2$f0c66580$LocalHost@stupidwin95> <3298254.72398.13653@kcbbs.gen.nz>`

```
I, and others, would like the option of STATIC linking
as well as dynamic linking.  If I build an EXE with the
run-time routines STATICally linked to it, it cannot
possibly be subject to the "DLL hell" problems
that have become so endemic under Win3.x, 9x and NT.

As far as what was being asked for being not STATIC 
linking, well perhaps I missed the thrust of the thread,
but I suspect that more likely you did.  The original
issue was (as best as I could determine) how to 
get real libraries from Fujitsu, and not just import 
libs and dlls.  The confusions of dll calls using 
(the "static" dyna-link) or not using import libraries 
(the dynamic, dyna-link) was introduced by several
people along the thread -- but neither of those is
static linking with a linker and (real, honest to God,
code containing) libraries -- which is, to the best of
my determination, what was being asked for.

As for being DOS centric, static linking is also OS/360 centric,
RT-11 centric, CP/M-80 centric, Multics-centric, 
Unix-centric, QNX-centric, GECOS-centric &c &c
In fact of major OSes (past and present) with which I 
have some familiarity  only OS/2 and the Windows variations 
use dyna-linking rather  than static linking on a regular 
basis (or at all).  

So perhaps is it that you are Windows-centric?

I don't want to deny you (or others) the option of using
DLLs.  There are situations for which dyna-links make sense.
BUT THERE ARE ALSO SITUATIONS FOR WHICH
STATIC LINKING MAKES SENSE.

But you can't statically link using Fujitsu Cobol, 'cause
Fujitsu does not supply real libraries.  And when you
ask them about it, you get BULL SHIT, DOUBLE
SPEAK and RUN AROUNDS.  

If they don't want to supply real libraries, let them come out
and say so!  But I am much less pleased to hear lots of
pseudo-techno-bullshit about "how complex the code generator
is and how that makes static linking infeasible without too many
gazillions of man-hours of work..."  WHICH IS PURE CRAP.
_____________________________________________________
I repeat, the only work needed is to take the RTL source to .OBJs
and LIB it together.  

I HEREBY VOLUNTEER TO DO JUST THAT:  

I WILL SIGN A NON-DISCLOSURE FOR THE FUJITSU
RTL SOURCE AND WITHOUT CHARGE TO FUJITSU
I WILL RECOMPILE/REASSEMBLE AND BUILD 
REAL HONEST TO GOD LIBRARIES WHICH I WILL
RETURN TO FUJITSU FOR DISTRIBUTION.

There, Fujitsu, you can satisfy the requests (few though they 
may be) for real libraries AT NO COST AND NO INVESTMENT
OF MAN HOURS.

This is a sincere offer.  I am willing to sign any reasonable non-disclosure
agreement and keep your code confidential.
```

<T_Rhoads@NoSpam.CLASSIC.MSN.COM> wrote:

> I, and others, would like the option of STATIC linking
> as well as dynamic linking.  If I build an EXE with the
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…

You missed the boat somewhere along the line.

You CAN static link with Fujitsu, call programs and end up with a 
single EXE from YOUR source code.  This is a no brainer and easy to do
within the compiler/project.  You have a choice to STATIC link or 
DYNAMIC link.

WHERE there is a PROBLEM is that unlike, say "Realia" COBOL - where 
you could take your .EXE and the .EXE ONLY and run it on another 
computer, you cannot take your .EXE from Fujitsu and run it.  You need
all of the "supporting" DLL files.

I PERSONALLY don't see this as a big problem, but I would LIKE to see 
a version that statically links in the RUNTIME MODULES USED by the 
static linked EXE.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Challenge: I'll do it for Fujitsu

_(thread depth: 9)_

- **From:** "Kevin G. Rhoads" <T_Rhoads@NoSpam.CLASSIC.MSN.COM>
- **Date:** 1998-09-16T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<01bde180$7c0f8fa0$a8a6fed0@stupidwin95>`
- **References:** `<01bdddf2$f0c66580$LocalHost@stupidwin95> <3298254.72398.13653@kcbbs.gen.nz> <01bddea2$572bd300$LocalHost@stupidwin95> <Jl0PnHJ5PvPd-pn2-utPsEdJ4sBdc@Dwight_Miller.iix.com>`

```
I am sorry that I did not supply all relevant info.

I have the "quote prior post" automatic stuff off, and sometimes
don't quote enough manually to keep context.

The sub-thread in which I was responding was discussing the
issue of static linking to the Fujitsu run-time libraries.  I was
offering to rebuild the RTLs as libraries instead of  DLLs so that
static linking of RTL code was possible.  This would allow building
a single self-contain EXE -- requiring no RTL dlls.

Fujitsu responded in e-mail, as I've posted elsewhere,
with the additional information that the object code emitted
by their code generator has dyna-links (by way of Win32 API calls)
embedded into it.  <sigh>  

So all those dyna-links would need to be changed to external references -
either by rewriting the code generator, or by <shudder> post-processing the
code.  Only then could they be satisfied by the linker.

Oh well.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Fujitsu responds

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** "Kevin G. Rhoads" <T_Rhoads@NoSpam.CLASSIC.MSN.COM>
- **Date:** 1998-09-16T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<01bde17f$28f6c8c0$a8a6fed0@stupidwin95>`
- **References:** `<01bdddf2$f0c66580$LocalHost@stupidwin95> <3298254.72398.13653@kcbbs.gen.nz> <01bddea2$572bd300$LocalHost@stupidwin95>`

```
I received a direct e-mail response from Fujitsu upon this issue
and have a better understanding of the issue of attempting to 
implement static linking (not static dyna-links) with Fujitsu
Cobol.  After reviewing the explanations from Fujitsu I
reluctantly conclude that it is not easily possible to achieve 
a FUNCTIONING static link.  

What was not made clear before (to me, at least) was that
the .OBJs emitted by the Cobol compiler's back end contain
(according to Fujitsu) which already has DLL calls (implemented
via the appropriate Win32 API functions).  So although the external
references could be link satisfied using true libraries (rather than 
import libraries for DLLs) that would not remove nor change the
dyna-links embedded into the object code by the code generator.

So even were true libraries to made avaliable, the EXE linked against
them would still require the presence of all DLLs for which dyna-links
were generated by the code generator.  <sigh>

I was not made privy to which DLLs the code generator embeds 
references to, but I was given the impression that the list is extensive
enough, that any attempt to eliminate the need for DLLs  by true static
linking without making changes to the code generator is, in Fujitsu's
opinion, fruitless.

I wish to thank Fujitsu for taking the time to explain this more fully.
I would like the opportunity to use static linking and avoid DLLs
but given what Fujitsu has further explained, that seems less feasible.
```

<a id="t-42218dc590"></a>
## Thread: Fujitsu V4 Dll's — 5 msgs

_Topics: [`Compilers and vendors`](topics.md#compilers)_

### Re: Fujitsu V4 Dll's

- **From:** "Donald Tees" <donald@willmack.com>
- **Date:** 1998-09-07T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<6t1a4v$b7s$1@news.igs.net>`
- **References:** `<6t0ok5$5um$1@news.igs.net> <35f3ee49.0@news1.ibm.net>`

```
Well, I was able to get it all into one big EXE, but that is
not suitable, in this case.  To get it into one big exe, you
can never call a routine without naming it explicitly.

IE- convert "call program-name" to
        if program-name equals "sub1"
            call "sub1"
        else
        if program-name equals "sub2"
            call "sub2"
        etc.

When you create a DLL, it seems that Fujitsu ALSO creates a .LIB
as a side effect.  That can be put into the library folder for the project,
and will link provided every subroutine needed is referenced by
a direct call.  If you have a complete list, the above code could be
put into a subroutine like so:

    call program-name
is converted to:
    call "caller" using program-name.

Caller would just list the names in an if statement ... then you get
one big EXE.  My problem is that each customer uses a different
program to print invoices, and that program is named in a setup file.
That means my subroutine would have to have an IF for every
single customer.

>Don,
>I couldn't get this to work either.
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
>This is my midweek (and everyday!) whine.
```

#### ↳ Re: Fujitsu V4 Dll's

- **From:** riplin@kcbbs.gen.nz (Richard Plinston)
- **Date:** 1998-09-08T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3298250.75002.13193@kcbbs.gen.nz>`
- **References:** `<6t1a4v$b7s$1@news.igs.net>`

```
In message <<6t1a4v$b7s$1@news.igs.net>> "Donald Tees" <donald@willmack.com> writes:
> not suitable, in this case.  To get it into one big exe, you
> can never call a routine without naming it explicitly.
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>             call "sub1"

This makes them static calls rather than dynamic.  While you
can force a CALL "name" to be dynamic with a DLOAD option
you cannot make a CALL variable static, for obvious 
reasons.

With MF a dynamic call would seach the curent .EXE for
the name, so simply linking all the dynamically called
routines into the .EXE wouldn't make them statically
linked, just availble for the search.

I don't think that Fujitsu does any form of resolution
in this way, it is either a static linked in or a
dymanic with the .LIB linked into the main routine.
ie it is not really dynamic, just dynamic loading.
It allows modules to be replaced but not added.

With MF I can type a program name into the menu
program and have it run, Fujitsu doesn't seem to have
a mechanism to allow this.

> Caller would just list the names in an if statement ... then you get
> one big EXE.  My problem is that each customer uses a different
> program to print invoices, and that program is named in a setup file.
> That means my subroutine would have to have an IF for every
> single customer.

But why do you want one huge .EXE ?   I like dynamic loading
because it allows changes in the system to be isolated
into the called routines that are affected.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Fujitsu V4 Dll's

- **From:** "Leif Svalgaard" <leif@ibm.net>
- **Date:** 1998-09-08T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<35f5ad6f.0@news1.ibm.net>`
- **References:** `<6t1a4v$b7s$1@news.igs.net> <3298250.75002.13193@kcbbs.gen.nz>`

```
Richard Plinston wrote in message <3298250.75002.13193@kcbbs.gen.nz>...
>In message <<6t1a4v$b7s$1@news.igs.net>> "Donald Tees"
<donald@willmack.com> writes:
>> not suitable, in this case.  To get it into one big exe, you
>> can never call a routine without naming it explicitly.
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
>a mechanism to allow this.


you certainly can. This is how I run all my programs.
I too have a menu program that knows nothing of
what programs I might want to call.

>But why do you want one huge .EXE ?   I like dynamic loading
>because it allows changes in the system to be isolated
>into the called routines that are affected.


For a developer dynamic loading is far the best,
but when you deploy the system having a SINGLE
EXE is preferable, because the users tend to mess
up things, or download system DLLs, or in other ways
make management of the application difficult.

Of course, if what you ship is a beta with lots of
bugs you might want to ship DLLs...
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Fujitsu V4 Dll's

- **From:** "Donald Tees" <donald@willmack.com>

```
Speaking of which, I have another related problem.  I have
been attempting for the best part of a day now to call a screen
oriented (Cobol97 subroutine) from a PowerCobol menu bar.

I get a link error, saying that it cannot find the subroutine. The
interesting thing is that I can call it from a DLL via another Cobol97
mainline.  There does appear to be a difference between the two
regarding names ... power Cobol adds the underscore to the filename:
IE call "xxx" in power Cobol produces 'cannot find "_xxx".'

However, if I change the program id to "_xxx" and call "_xxx" from
Cobol97, it links correctly.  I can do this by creating a DLL, or by
linking in the .OBJ file.  I still get the same message from power Cobol.

I have tried several naming conventions, calling methods, and
project organizations, but still get the same message.  The *REALLY*
interesting part is that even though it says it could not find "_xxx",
it lists XXX's subroutines as unreconciled, but then links THEM
correctly as OBJs or as DLL's if I add them to the project. So if it
could not find "xxx", why does it need xxx's subroutines?

Has anybody managed to actually do this?
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Fujitsu V4 Dll's

- **From:** "Donald Tees" <donald@willmack.com>
- **Date:** 1998-09-08T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<6t4i51$4l0$1@news.igs.net>`
- **References:** `<6t1a4v$b7s$1@news.igs.net> <3298250.75002.13193@kcbbs.gen.nz>`

```
>> Caller would just list the names in an if statement ... then you get
>> one big EXE.  My problem is that each customer uses a different
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>

No, I want one big DLL.  The problem is that Fujitsu seems to
have a problem finding the right DLL, unless one references
the sub as a static.  Then the linker sets it up as dllname:subname.
I have found a work-around, and am on to something else now.

BTW-there is a reason ... it is heritage code, written as menu reading
the setup, then running all routines as subroutines.  I have converted
an absolute look-alike(keystroke only) as version 1. Today, I wrote a
GUI toolbar that replaces the menu, and it executes the same programs,
but each executed from a separate stub.  That means that each of my old
subroutines becomes a separate window. The subroutines will sit
in DLL, and I will end up with 50 or so stub.exe's that are executed from
the toolbar.

That gives me a non-gui system that can co-exist while I convert, and
makes training easy.  I plan to do the rest of the conversions on a module
by module basis, with the old versions and the new being able to co-exist.
The only things that need be common is the isam files on the server.
```

- **From:** "alisa" <alisa@amaze.net.au>
- **Date:** 1998-09-09T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<01bddbaf$2eb33c60$59d81fcb@alisa.amaze.net.au>`
- **References:** `<35f4355e.26944294@netnews.worldnet.att.net>`

```



Vern Bradner <Vern_Bradner@newcourt.com> wrote in article
<35f4355e.26944294@netnews.worldnet.att.net>...
> Hi,
> 
…[14 more quoted lines elided]…
>

 May be it's not the best variant, but it's better than nothing.

Good luck!

Alisa.

 IDENTIFICATION DIVISION.
 PROGRAM-ID. StringManipulation.
 DATA DIVISION.
 WORKING-STORAGE SECTION.
 01 LongName PIC X(15) VALUE "Hello world!  ?".
 01 ShortName PIC X(12).
 01 JustRightLongName PIC X(15) JUSTIFIED RIGHT. 
 PROCEDURE DIVISION.
		DISPLAY LongName.
		MOVE LongName TO ShortName.
		DISPLAY ShortName.
          MOVE ShortName TO JustRightLongName.
          DISPLAY JustRightLongName.
		STOP RUN.
```
