<a id="t-b44fd2172f"></a>
## Thread: Right Justify Routine — 25 msgs

### Re: Right Justify Routine

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc123@bellsouth.net>
- **Date:** 1998-09-07T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<RoTI1.48$g04.113436@news3.mia.bellsouth.net>`
- **References:** `<Jl0PnHJ5PvPd-pn2-XiTbHJiS5z0r@Dwight_Miller.iix.com> <o%VH1.3786$gM3.2803875@news4.mia.bellsouth.net> <trYH1.464$I%.1378959@news1.atlantic.net> <HtbI1.5187$eZ3.3317864@news3.mia.bellsouth.net> <BseI1.481$I%.1861130@news1.atlantic.net> <uCwI1.5340$gM3.3883152@news4.mia.bellsouth.net> <KfAI1.610$I%.2387649@news1.atlantic.net>`

```
Rick Smith wrote:
>
>Despite having programmed in assembly for over 20 years, mostly
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
>agrees with what you have been saying.

Yes, I agree with that.  I am not advocating that all code be optimized,
of course. :-)  I am just saying that to be a truly good programmer, one
should know what the tradeoffs are, and when to make them.  When I am
writing code in a HLL, I am constantly visualizing approximately what
kind of code the compiler will generate.  This allows me to always have
a feel for how efficiently I am coding.  I cannot imagine how one could
do this without having, at the minimum, a working knowledge of at least
one assembly language, and understanding of basic hardware architecture.
```

#### ↳ Re: Right Justify Routine

- **From:** "Leif Svalgaard" <leif@ibm.net>
- **Date:** 1998-09-07T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<35f405b9.0@news1.ibm.net>`
- **References:** `<Jl0PnHJ5PvPd-pn2-XiTbHJiS5z0r@Dwight_Miller.iix.com> <o%VH1.3786$gM3.2803875@news4.mia.bellsouth.net> <trYH1.464$I%.1378959@news1.atlantic.net> <HtbI1.5187$eZ3.3317864@news3.mia.bellsouth.net> <BseI1.481$I%.1861130@news1.atlantic.net> <uCwI1.5340$gM3.3883152@news4.mia.bellsouth.net> <KfAI1.610$I%.2387649@news1.atlantic.net> <RoTI1.48$g04.113436@news3.mia.bellsouth.net>`

```
Judson McClendon wrote in message ...
> I cannot imagine how one could
>do this without having, at the minimum, a working knowledge of at least
>one assembly language, and understanding of basic hardware architecture.


Donald Knuth ("The Art of Computer Programming") seems to agree with this
(as do I).
But the point about efficiency is that it is more important to get a simple,
clean
design than to worry about efficiency. With a simple, clean design,
efficiency
comes almost by itself. It is usually the tricky stuff that end up being
inefficient.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Right Justify Routine

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc123@bellsouth.net>
- **Date:** 1998-09-07T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<_AVI1.165$g04.197344@news3.mia.bellsouth.net>`
- **References:** `<Jl0PnHJ5PvPd-pn2-XiTbHJiS5z0r@Dwight_Miller.iix.com> <o%VH1.3786$gM3.2803875@news4.mia.bellsouth.net> <trYH1.464$I%.1378959@news1.atlantic.net> <HtbI1.5187$eZ3.3317864@news3.mia.bellsouth.net> <BseI1.481$I%.1861130@news1.atlantic.net> <uCwI1.5340$gM3.3883152@news4.mia.bellsouth.net> <KfAI1.610$I%.2387649@news1.atlantic.net> <RoTI1.48$g04.113436@news3.mia.bellsouth.net> <35f405b9.0@news1.ibm.net>`

```
Leif Svalgaard wrote in message <35f405b9.0@news1.ibm.net>...
>Judson McClendon wrote in message ...
>> I cannot imagine how one could
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
>stuff that end up being inefficient.


The results we have seen in this thread do not support the statement that
"efficiency almost comes by itself."  Algorithmic efficiency may, but code
efficiency does not.  I thought my first three statement solution

  move function reverse(input-field) to output-field
  inspect output-field tallying counter for leading spaces
  string input-field delimited by size into output-field with pointer counter

was more 'clean', but was far from being the most efficient, by a factor of
100.  Is the above code 'efficient enough'?  Perhaps so, but the nature of
the application would determine that.
```

#### ↳ Re: Right Justify Routine

- **From:** scm@enterprise.net (Shaun C. Murray)
- **Date:** 1998-09-07T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<6t11cs$86g$1@news.enterprise.net>`
- **References:** `<Jl0PnHJ5PvPd-pn2-XiTbHJiS5z0r@Dwight_Miller.iix.com> <o%VH1.3786$gM3.2803875@news4.mia.bellsouth.net> <trYH1.464$I%.1378959@news1.atlantic.net> <HtbI1.5187$eZ3.3317864@news3.mia.bellsouth.net> <BseI1.481$I%.1861130@news1.atlantic.net> <uCwI1.5340$gM3.3883152@news4.mia.bellsouth.net> <KfAI1.610$I%.2387649@news1.atlantic.net> <RoTI1.48$g04.113436@news3.mia.bellsouth.net>`

```
In article <RoTI1.48$g04.113436@news3.mia.bellsouth.net>, 
judmc123@bellsouth.net says...
>
>Yes, I agree with that.  I am not advocating that all code be optimized,
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>one assembly language, and understanding of basic hardware architecture.

I don't think that's really needed. You have to understand how basic compiler 
technology works but usually keeping your instructions simple, using only two 
operands where possible and using computational items rather than display get's 
you pretty much optimum performance in most environments.

Further than that invloves coding to a specific assembler, hardware level and 
compiler whereas there is merit in doing that when needed, it's not portable 
as an optimal solution.

In the case of the MF reverse intrinsic function, it's probably written in 
COBOL rather than assembler or C so rewriting it yourself may be more efficient 
as you can tune the parameters yourself rather than having to cope with a 
generalised function.

I hope also that when you did your timing tests you based it on speed of the 
actual function and not the speed of the program as extra time will be taken in 
dynamically loading the intrinsic function module and in the case of some 
versions of MF v2.5 (eg. 2.5.25), erroneously loading the floating point 
function module even though it is not required in the program.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Right Justify Routine

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc123@bellsouth.net>
- **Date:** 1998-09-07T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<RIVI1.166$g04.201513@news3.mia.bellsouth.net>`
- **References:** `<Jl0PnHJ5PvPd-pn2-XiTbHJiS5z0r@Dwight_Miller.iix.com> <o%VH1.3786$gM3.2803875@news4.mia.bellsouth.net> <trYH1.464$I%.1378959@news1.atlantic.net> <HtbI1.5187$eZ3.3317864@news3.mia.bellsouth.net> <BseI1.481$I%.1861130@news1.atlantic.net> <uCwI1.5340$gM3.3883152@news4.mia.bellsouth.net> <KfAI1.610$I%.2387649@news1.atlantic.net> <RoTI1.48$g04.113436@news3.mia.bellsouth.net> <6t11cs$86g$1@news.enterprise.net>`

```
Shaun C. Murray wrote:
>
>Judson McClendon wrote:
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
>you pretty much optimum performance in most environments.


You consider a 100:1 performance differential 'optimum performance'?

>Further than that invloves coding to a specific assembler, hardware level and
>compiler whereas there is merit in doing that when needed, it's not portable
>as an optimal solution.


Of course, that is not what I am suggesting.  The 100:1 improvement in speed
was made without once looking at the assembly output of the compiler, or by
any single consideration for what platform the program was targeted.  Other
people have gotten similar results with other compilers.

Nowhere will you find me advocating targeting a specific architecture.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Right Justify Routine

- **From:** riplin@kcbbs.gen.nz (Richard Plinston)
- **Date:** 1998-09-08T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3298250.73331.11763@kcbbs.gen.nz>`
- **References:** `<RIVI1.166$g04.201513@news3.mia.bellsouth.net>`

```
In message <<RIVI1.166$g04.201513@news3.mia.bellsouth.net>> "Judson McClendon" <judmc123@bellsouth.net> writes:

> >technology works but usually keeping your instructions simple, using only two
> >operands where possible and using computational items rather than display get's
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> You consider a 100:1 performance differential 'optimum performance'?


In that particular example program it accepted a string from
the user, manipulated it, then displayed it.  Given that
it may be severla _seconds_ in the accept, and some milliseconds
in the display, the performance of the reversal hardly
seems relevant.

However, the performance of the _programmer_ is relevant.  A
working right justify using different methods may take
anything from a few minutes to an hour, depending on the
programmer and the approach taken.  I'll take the one that:
 
      a)   works  (provably)
      b)   takes least programmer time

In that order.  If it does take an inordinate amount of
time in production then I'll worry about it.

But, I believe, the 100 x does not represent actual usage.
Very small test sets, such as the code shown in this thread,
may be 'optimised away' by the compiler or the CPU cache
giving apparent speeds never obtained in real programs.

I do understand _why_ functions use large amount of time
compared to hand coded routines, and I avoid them in
general.  STRING and INSPECT may also show, with _some_
compilers, similar performance problems because they may
be implemented by called general routines.

But then if C programmers really cared about performance
they would never use printf().
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Right Justify Routine

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** scm@enterprise.net (Shaun C. Murray)
- **Date:** 1998-09-09T00:00:00

In article <3298250.73331.11763@kcbbs.gen.nz>, riplin@kcbbs.gen.nz says...
>
>But, I believe, the 100 x does not represent actual usage.
>Very small test sets, such as the code shown in this thread,
>may be 'optimised away' by the compiler or the CPU cache
>giving apparent speeds never obtained in real programs.

Spoilsport!

As a real world example I have 18 Mb files incoming every day that need EBCDIC 
spaces stripped off the front and CRLF's bunged on the end after stripping the 
trailing spaces. This kind of optimization helps a lot. Also buffering file I/O 
helps a lot too. So, it *can* be important. I'm doing it in Delphi however so I 
have a different kettle of fish to contend with. One day, I'll write a COBOL 
version just to see if it's faster.


>
>I do understand _why_ functions use large amount of time
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>be implemented by called general routines.

That's the point, Know your Enemy!

>
>But then if C programmers really cared about performance
>they would never use printf().

And Delphi programmers would never use, er, Delphi ;-)

ps. My newserver is only dishing out the occasional article to me so if I miss 
responding to something, it's the servers fault.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Right Justify Routine

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** redsky@ibm.net (Thane Hubbell)
- **Date:** 1998-09-09T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<Jl0PnHJ5PvPd-pn2-41X3blkjMdPF@Dwight_Miller.iix.com>`
- **References:** `<RIVI1.166$g04.201513@news3.mia.bellsouth.net> <3298250.73331.11763@kcbbs.gen.nz>`

```
On Tue, 8 Sep 1998 20:22:11, riplin@kcbbs.gen.nz (Richard Plinston) 
wrote:

> I do understand _why_ functions use large amount of time
> compared to hand coded routines, and I avoid them in
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> 

I'm sorry, but I *Don't* see why the functions should not be the 
FASTEST way to accomplish the task (machine performance, not 
programmer).  If we as a group were able to make a routine that 
handled right justification (the main portion being that function 
performed by function reverse), that will handle any size field, WHY 
don't the compiler vendors use the SAME method to created the Fastest 
routine.  Ideally, the fastest one should have been Rick's original 
with 2 function reverses.  But it was the slowest.  That does not make
sense to me.  We should NOT be able to hand code a routine that is 
FASTER in execution than the intrinsic functions.  They should always 
be superior.  If they are not, the compiler vendors are not doing 
their job.
```

>On Tue, 8 Sep 1998 20:22:11, riplin@kcbbs.gen.nz (Richard Plinston)
>wrote:
…[20 more quoted lines elided]…

their (weak) excuse will probably be that the functions use a "virtual"
field. Since this cannot (easily) be allocated statically at compile time
as ordinary Cobol data-items can, every function call may have to do a
C-type malloc/dealloc at run-time and that is expensive.
But, I generally agree with you: they are letting us down by not doing
their job. I've been in the compiler business in an earlier life and
it ain't hard to do better.


BTW, my spellchecker choked on 'malloc' and suggested 'malice'
instead.
```

- **From:** vbandke@ibm.net (Volker Bandke)

On 9 Sep 98 19:36:11 GMT, redsky@ibm.net (Thane Hubbell) wrote:


>I'm sorry, but I *Don't* see why the functions should not be the 
>FASTEST way to accomplish the task (machine performance, not 
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
>their job.


Thane,

from the pratical point of view (practical from the compiler builders point) I heartily
disagree.  The compiler builder is creating general purpose routines, which have to do all
kind of TOR (type of request) Analysis, Error handling, etc.  If I were to tackle a
specific subset of the general purpose routine, I can of course eliminate a lot of the
overhead involved and can come "right to the point" .  I could code the "RIGHTJUSTIFIED"
Routine in just a few Assembler instructions, and incorporate them into my COBOL program
with a CALL (and would probably outperform quite a few COBOL programs...,of course, we
would have to benchmark that on an MVS machine - wanna buy a P0390 for me <g>)

With kind regards

Volker Bandke
(BSP GmbH)
with kind regards

Volker Bandke
(BSP GmbH)

Thane Hubbell wrote in message ...
>On Wed, 9 Sep 1998 20:12:22, vbandke@ibm.net (Volker Bandke) wrote:
>
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>> from the pratical point of view (practical from the compiler builders
point) I heartily
>> disagree.  The compiler builder is creating general purpose routines,
which have to do all
>
>I'm talking a native function, ie: Function Reverse - we can hand code
>it and it is 100 times faster than the compiler vendors provide.  If
>it's that easy, they should have done it that way in the first place.
>
I must make a minor adjustment to the figures. The fast right justify
routines did not emulate Function Reverse.

The differential for Function Reverse in MF 3.2 comes to about 70:1
when following the requirement for the use of a "temporary elementary
data item." When treating it "as if" it used a "temporary elementary
data item," the ratio is about 106:1 when the argument and receiving
operands are different and 125:1 when the argument and receiving
operands are the same.

This assumes the code is written in-line, in COBOL, and without
further optimization.
-------------------------------
Rick Smith
e-mail: < ricksmith@aiservices.com >
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Right Justify Routine

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** mark0young@aol.com (Mark0Young)
- **Date:** 1998-09-10T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<1998091005562600.BAA23086@ladder03.news.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<Jl0PnHJ5PvPd-pn2-41X3blkjMdPF@Dwight_Miller.iix.com>`

```
In article <Jl0PnHJ5PvPd-pn2-41X3blkjMdPF@Dwight_Miller.iix.com>,
redsky@ibm.net (Thane Hubbell) writes:

>I *Don't* see why the functions should not be the 
>FASTEST way to accomplish the task (machine performance, not 
>programmer).

Functions tend to be "general purpose" routines.

Generally speaking, a special-purpose routine will usually be faster than
general routines. For example, special-purpose routines don't have to be
concerned about wildly divergent argument sizes, pathological conditions,
double-byte characters, etc., but can often take shortcuts based on the
programmer's knowledge of what will and will not get passed to the routine.

Mark A. Young
```

#### ↳ Re: Right Justify Routine

- **From:** mark0young@aol.com (Mark0Young)
- **Date:** 1998-09-07T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<1998090717402500.NAA13081@ladder03.news.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<RoTI1.48$g04.113436@news3.mia.bellsouth.net>`

```
For some reasons, my bosses over the years would prefer a poorly optimized
program that works over a highly optimized program that doesn't work.

I try to encourage the programmers I work with to use numeric data types where
practical, especially for subscripts and accumulators (i.e., fields for
accumulating various totals), no matter what the language. Just changing from
USAGE IS DISPLAY to USAGE IS COMP (for subscripts) or COMP-3 (for accumulators)
can have a big impact on speeding up program execution with minimal to no
impact on code maintainability.

As far as unrolling loops go, I figure that is the responsibility of the
compiler, not the programmer--let the compiler unroll PERFORMs and loops if it
wants to, but keep the source easily maintainable!

In a business shop (as opposed to scientific/statistic), one has to balance out
the amount of effort and time ($) to tune a program against the anticipated
savings for the life of the program, including maintainability of the code.

In some cases, I have gotten better performance by tuning buffers (VSAM KSDS:
BUFNI= and BUFND=; Datacom/DB VSAM/T: tuning VITs; SAM and VSAM ESDS: adjusting
blocksizes or CISIZEs) than I would ever be able to achieve by adjusting the
code.

In a scientific/statistics setting where programs could run for hours doing
number crunching (as opposed to being I/O intensive), adjusting code may have
big pay-backs.

Mark A. Young
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Right Justify Routine

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc123@bellsouth.net>
- **Date:** 1998-09-07T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<5RVI1.168$g04.206667@news3.mia.bellsouth.net>`
- **References:** `<RoTI1.48$g04.113436@news3.mia.bellsouth.net> <1998090717402500.NAA13081@ladder03.news.aol.com>`

```
Mark0Young wrote:
>For some reasons, my bosses over the years would prefer a poorly optimized
>program that works over a highly optimized program that doesn't work.


Of course!  But why should he have to choose between efficient and works? :-)
I am not advocating 'highly optimized'.  I am only suggesting that we should
always be mindful of efficiency in everything we write.  There are shades of
efficiency between outright disregard and 'optimum'.

>I try to encourage the programmers I work with to use numeric data types where
>practical, especially for subscripts and accumulators (i.e., fields for
…[20 more quoted lines elided]…
>big pay-backs.


Mark, I agree with the things you suggest above.  But when a programmer is
knowledgeable and mindful of what kind of code is being generated by the code
constructs being used (in a general way, mind you), the programmer is in a
better position to write the code more efficiently out of the block.  No code
rewriting, just do it better as you go.  What's wrong with that? :-)
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Right Justify Routine

- **From:** mark0young@aol.com (Mark0Young)
- **Date:** 1998-09-07T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<1998090722423800.SAA22036@ladder03.news.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<5RVI1.168$g04.206667@news3.mia.bellsouth.net>`

```
In article <5RVI1.168$g04.206667@news3.mia.bellsouth.net>, "Judson McClendon"
<judmc123@bellsouth.net> writes:

>What's wrong with that? :-)

In theory, nothing, absolutely nothing! 8)

In practice, our programmers seem to have a poor idea of what happens behind
the scenes. The programmer I find the most frustrating is the one who wants to
drag 10,000 records in a CICS transaction and then doesn't understand why CICS
is so slow when users start using that transaction. He says that the fix is to
get a faster computer. Grrr....

Mark A. Young
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Right Justify Routine

- **From:** riplin@kcbbs.gen.nz (Richard Plinston)
- **Date:** 1998-09-08T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3298250.74311.12545@kcbbs.gen.nz>`
- **References:** `<5RVI1.168$g04.206667@news3.mia.bellsouth.net>`

```
In message <<5RVI1.168$g04.206667@news3.mia.bellsouth.net>> "Judson McClendon" <judmc123@bellsouth.net> writes:
> 
> Mark, I agree with the things you suggest above.  But when a programmer is
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> rewriting, just do it better as you go.  What's wrong with that? :-)

I always consider efficient coding and will eliminate costly
code wherever possible.  For example in searching a table
terminate the search when it is possible to determine 
whether the item exists of not,  only search to highest
item instaed of every occurance.  If it is likely that
data comes in 'batches', hold the last table item so that
if it matches it saves a search.

These are optimisations that can be driven by knowledge of
the _business_ and knowledge of the _data_ and some
common sense.

What you are advocating is knowledge of the underlying
machine and the compiler in what code is generated.  Most
Cobol programmers neither know nor care where the program
runs, let alone what the bit patterns look like.

Knowledge of the data flows can improve performance 
several fold in a real system, knowledge of compiler
output bit patterns will only help on the machine you
are targetting, until the next CPU upgrade, then you
start over (or just let the compiler do it for you).

You like visualising the flows of bits through the CPU
as you write your code, I concentrate on the user
interface and the data flows through files.  That's 
fine, the world needs all these skills, but I am 
already overloaded, and it only costs the user an
upgrade to a Pentium 400 if I get it really wrong, and
that is _much_ cheaper than rewriting to highly
efficient generated code.

In fact many of my systems run _multiUser_ on 486 and
Pentium 120s.  Take one client:  Pentium 120 with
DRI Multiuser-DOS, 12 terminals some with multi-tasks,
Interpreted .INT Cobol programs.

It still spends about 40% ot the time waiting for
keystrokes.
```

Take a look at local shared resources.  I tuned M&D products with this
and made an instant 50% execution time change on one program by JCL

Ken

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Right Justify Routine

- **From:** mark0young@aol.com (Mark0Young)
- **Date:** 1998-09-09T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<1998090904432700.AAA22733@ladder01.news.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<35F5314A.D70F620E@zip.com.au>`

```
In article <35F5314A.D70F620E@zip.com.au>, Ken Foskey <waratah@zip.com.au>
writes:

>Take a look at local shared resources.  I tuned M&D products with this
>and made an instant 50% execution time change on one program by JCL

In VSE/ESA, I am unaware of any product that automatically causes existing code
to use LSR in batch.

As far as tuning software goes, that generally involves looking at the ACB and,
from that, making assumptions on the type of access. Unfortunately,
Easytrieve-Plus (which most of our in-house batch is written in) always sets up
an ACB that causes such tuning software to assume that access will be
sequential, which just kills random processing. So then to use such products,
we end up having to look at what each program is really doing to give overrides
to the tuning software. If we go through that effort, we might as well code the
BUFNI= and BUFND= parameters on the DLBLs and save the cost of the tuning
software. Indeed, that is exactly what we did.

Mark A. Young
```

---

## Thread: Right Justify Routine — 13 msgs

### Re: Right Justify Routine

- **From:** AS-DATA@t-online.de (Andreas Strzoda)
- **Date:** 1998-09-07T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<6t147m$8i2$1@news01.btx.dtag.de>`
- **References:** `<Jl0PnHJ5PvPd-pn2-XiTbHJiS5z0r@Dwight_Miller.iix.com> <o%VH1.3786$gM3.2803875@news4.mia.bellsouth.net> <trYH1.464$I%.1378959@news1.atlantic.net> <HtbI1.5187$eZ3.3317864@news3.mia.bellsouth.net> <BseI1.481$I%.1861130@news1.atlantic.net> <uCwI1.5340$gM3.3883152@news4.mia.bellsouth.net>`

```
Judson McClendon schrieb:

> Since we're starting to really get into the optimization thing, let's
> take a look at another tweak.  I took Rick's program and tweaked it
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
>    0.55 hand-coded

..cut
I've two more versions, up to 100% faster then the
jud-hand-coded one!

the time of running is depending of the
input field, so i testet my routines with
different ones, the first is round 20% faster than the
hand-coded (every time), the second (AS1) is
sometimes 100% faster and can be slower.

try it in your environment:

 test3-start-AS.
     if input-field not = spaces
      compute i = function length (input-field)
      perform varying counter from i by -1 until
       input-field(counter:1) not = space
      end-perform
      compute k = function length(output-field) - counter *>assuming
that k > counter!
      move spaces to output-field (1:k)
      move input-field (1:counter) to output-field (k + 1:counter)
     end-if
     .
 test3-start-AS1. >*very fast binary method
     if input-field not = spaces
       compute l = function length (input-field)
       move l to j
       move 0 to flag p
       perform until j = 1
         compute j rounded = (j / 2 )
         if ad add      j   to p
          else subtract j from p
         end-if
         if input-field(p:l - p) = space
              move 1 to flag
*>subtract
              if input-field(p - 1:) not = space move 1 to j end-if
*>early end
         else move 0 to flag
*>add
         end-if
       end-perform
       if not ad subtract 1 from p end-if *>p points to the first
trailing space
       compute k = function length(output-field) - p *>assuming that k >
p!
       move spaces to output-field (1:k)
       move input-field (1:p) to output-field (k + 1:p)
     end-if
     .
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Right Justify Routine

- **From:** AS-DATA@t-online.de (Andreas Strzoda)
- **Date:** 1998-09-08T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<6t2mh3$aqm$1@news02.btx.dtag.de>`
- **References:** `<Jl0PnHJ5PvPd-pn2-XiTbHJiS5z0r@Dwight_Miller.iix.com> <o%VH1.3786$gM3.2803875@news4.mia.bellsouth.net> <trYH1.464$I%.1378959@news1.atlantic.net> <HtbI1.5187$eZ3.3317864@news3.mia.bellsouth.net> <BseI1.481$I%.1861130@news1.atlantic.net> <uCwI1.5340$gM3.3883152@news4.mia.bellsouth.net> <6t147m$8i2$1@news01.btx.dtag.de> <yA%I1.759$I%.3124690@news1.atlantic.net>`

```
Rick Smith schrieb:

> Andreas Strzoda wrote in message <6t147m$8i2$1@news01.btx.dtag.de>...
> [... all snipped]
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> e-mail: < ricksmith@aiservices.com >

   sorry, i moved it at the beginning:

 Identification Division.
 Program-Id.  Test3.
* right justify
 Environment Division.
 Configuration Section.
 Source-Computer.  IBM-PC.
 Object-Computer.  IBM-PC.
 Data Division.
 Working-Storage Section.
 01  input-field     pic x(30) value spaces.
 01  output-field    pic x(30) value spaces.
 01  teststrings-def.
     05 t1           pic x(30) value "ab".
     05 t2           pic x(30) value "abcdef".
     05 t3           pic x(30) value "abcdefghijklmn".
     05 t4           pic x(30) value "abcdefghijklmno".
     05 t5           pic x(30) value "abcdefghijklmnopqrstuvw".
 01  teststrings redefines teststrings-def.
     05 ti           pic x(30) occurs 5 times.
 01  tc              pic s9(4) comp.
 01  counter         pic s9(4) comp  value 1.
 01  char pic x.
 01  i               pic s9(4) comp.
 01  j               pic s9(4) comp.
 01  k               pic s9(4) comp.
 01  l               pic s9(4) comp.
 01  p               pic s9(4) comp.
 01  time-start      pic 9(4).
 01  time-end        pic 9(4).
 01  time-loop       pic 9(4).
 01  loop            pic s9(9) comp-5 value 500000.
 01  flag      pic 9 value 0.
     88 ad           value 0.
     88 sub          value 1.

 Procedure Division.
 test-3-start.
     perform varying tc from 1 by 1 until tc > 5
      move ti(tc) to input-field
      move space to output-field
      move function current-date (13:4) to time-start
      perform test3-start-AS loop  times
      move function current-date (13:4) to time-end
      compute time-loop = time-end - time-start
      display "AS :" time-loop output-field

      move ti(tc) to input-field
      move function current-date (13:4) to time-start
      perform test3-start-AS1 loop  times
      move function current-date (13:4) to time-end
      compute time-loop = time-end - time-start
      display "AS1:" time-loop output-field

      move ti(tc) to input-field
      move function current-date (13:4) to time-start
      perform test3-start-jud-coded loop times
      move function current-date (13:4) to time-end
      compute time-loop = time-end - time-start
      display "JUD:" time-loop output-field

     end-perform

     display "press enter"
     accept char
     stop run
     .

 for-later-use.
     move function current-date (13:4) to time-start
     perform test3-start-Rick loop times
     move function current-date (13:4) to time-end
     compute time-loop = time-end - time-start
     display time-loop

     move function current-date (13:4) to time-start
     perform test3-start-Judson loop times
     move function current-date (13:4) to time-end
     compute time-loop = time-end - time-start
     display time-loop

     move function current-date (13:4) to time-start
     perform test3-start-hand-coded loop times
     move function current-date (13:4) to time-end
     compute time-loop = time-end - time-start
     display time-loop

     move function current-date (13:4) to time-start
     move "abcdefghijklm" to input-field
     perform test3-start-jud-coded loop times
     move function current-date (13:4) to time-end
     compute time-loop = time-end - time-start
     display time-loop
     display "press enter"
     accept char
     stop run
     .

 test3-start-Rick.
     move 1 to counter
     if input-field > spaces
         move function reverse (input-field) to input-field
         inspect input-field tallying counter for leading spaces
         move function reverse (input-field (counter:))
             to input-field (counter:)
     end-if
     .
 test3-start-AS.
     if input-field not = spaces
      compute i = function length (input-field)
      perform varying counter from i by -1 until
       input-field(counter:1) not = space
      end-perform
      compute k = function length(output-field) - counter *>assuming
that k > counter!
      move spaces to output-field (1:k)
      move input-field (1:counter) to output-field (k + 1:counter)
     end-if
     .
 test3-start-AS1.
     if input-field not = spaces
       compute l = function length (input-field)
       move l to j
       move 0 to flag p
       perform until j = 1
         compute j rounded = (j / 2 )
         if ad add      j   to p
          else subtract j from p
         end-if
         if input-field(p:l - p) = space
              move 1 to flag
*>subtract
              if input-field(p - 1:) not = space move 1 to j end-if
*>early end
         else move 0 to flag
*>add
         end-if
       end-perform
       if not ad subtract 1 from p end-if *>p points to the first
trailing space
       compute k = function length(output-field) - p *>assuming that k >
p!
       move spaces to output-field (1:k)
       move input-field (1:p) to output-field (k + 1:p)
     end-if
     .

 test3-start-Judson.
     move 1 to counter
     if input-field not = spaces
         move function reverse(input-field) to output-field
         inspect output-field tallying counter for leading spaces
         string input-field delimited by size
             into output-field with pointer counter
     end-if
     .

 test3-start-hand-coded.
     if input-field not = spaces
         compute i = function length (input-field)
         Move i to j
         perform until input-field (i:1) not = space
             subtract 1 from i
         end-perform
         perform until i = 0
             move input-field (i:1) to input-field (j:1)
             subtract 1 from i
             subtract 1 from j
         end-perform
         if j > 0
             move spaces to input-field (1:j)
         end-if
     end-if
     .

 test3-start-jud-coded.
     if input-field not = spaces
         compute i = function length (input-field)
*         Move i to j
         perform until input-field (i:1) not = space
             subtract 1 from i
         end-perform
         compute j = function length(output-field)
         subtract i from j
         add 1 j giving k
         move input-field (1:i) to output-field (k:i)
         if j > 0
             move spaces to output-field (1:j)
         end-if
     end-if
     .
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Right Justify Routine

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc123@bellsouth.net>
- **Date:** 1998-09-08T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<qkbJ1.503$cE5.712476@news2.mia.bellsouth.net>`
- **References:** `<Jl0PnHJ5PvPd-pn2-XiTbHJiS5z0r@Dwight_Miller.iix.com> <o%VH1.3786$gM3.2803875@news4.mia.bellsouth.net> <trYH1.464$I%.1378959@news1.atlantic.net> <HtbI1.5187$eZ3.3317864@news3.mia.bellsouth.net> <BseI1.481$I%.1861130@news1.atlantic.net> <uCwI1.5340$gM3.3883152@news4.mia.bellsouth.net> <6t147m$8i2$1@news01.btx.dtag.de> <yA%I1.759$I%.3124690@news1.atlantic.net> <6t2mh3$aqm$1@news02.btx.dtag.de>`

```
Sorry, but I don't see any speed improvement in your routine, using
MF COBOL 3.2 (DOS), Pentium II 400:

  AS :0121                            ab
  AS1:0149                            ab
  JUD:0115                            ab

  AS :0104                        abcdef
  AS1:0105                        abcdef
  JUD:0098                        abcdef

  AS :0083                abcdefghijklmn
  AS1:0060                abcdefghijklmn
  JUD:0072                abcdefghijklmn

  AS :0077               abcdefghijklmno
  AS1:0181               abcdefghijklmno
  JUD:0071               abcdefghijklmno

  AS :0050       abcdefghijklmnopqrstuvw
  AS1:0159       abcdefghijklmnopqrstuvw
  JUD:0044       abcdefghijklmnopqrstuvw

Your code as you posted it would not compile.  I had to shift it over
six columns, remove your '*>' comments, and split a few statements
onto two lines.  I did not change the logic.  Here is the source as
I compiled and ran it:

       Identification Division.
       Program-Id.  Test3.
      * right justify
       Environment Division.
       Configuration Section.
       Source-Computer.  IBM-PC.
       Object-Computer.  IBM-PC.
       Data Division.
       Working-Storage Section.
       01  input-field     pic x(30) value spaces.
       01  output-field    pic x(30) value spaces.
       01  teststrings-def.
           05 t1           pic x(30) value "ab".
           05 t2           pic x(30) value "abcdef".
           05 t3           pic x(30) value "abcdefghijklmn".
           05 t4           pic x(30) value "abcdefghijklmno".
           05 t5           pic x(30) value "abcdefghijklmnopqrstuvw".
       01  teststrings redefines teststrings-def.
           05 ti           pic x(30) occurs 5 times.
       01  tc              pic s9(4) comp.
       01  counter         pic s9(4) comp  value 1.
       01  char pic x.
       01  i               pic s9(4) comp.
       01  j               pic s9(4) comp.
       01  k               pic s9(4) comp.
       01  l               pic s9(4) comp.
       01  p               pic s9(4) comp.
       01  time-start      pic 9(4).
       01  time-end        pic 9(4).
       01  time-loop       pic 9(4).
       01  loop            pic s9(9) comp-5 value 500000.
       01  flag      pic 9 value 0.
           88 ad           value 0.
           88 sub          value 1.

       Procedure Division.
       test-3-start.
           perform varying tc from 1 by 1 until tc > 5
            move ti(tc) to input-field
            move space to output-field
            move function current-date (13:4) to time-start
            perform test3-start-AS loop  times
            move function current-date (13:4) to time-end
            compute time-loop = time-end - time-start
            display "AS :" time-loop output-field

            move ti(tc) to input-field
            move function current-date (13:4) to time-start
            perform test3-start-AS1 loop  times
            move function current-date (13:4) to time-end
            compute time-loop = time-end - time-start
            display "AS1:" time-loop output-field

            move ti(tc) to input-field
            move function current-date (13:4) to time-start
            perform test3-start-jud-coded loop times
            move function current-date (13:4) to time-end
            compute time-loop = time-end - time-start
            display "JUD:" time-loop output-field

           end-perform

           display "press enter"
           accept char
           stop run
           .

       for-later-use.
           move function current-date (13:4) to time-start
           perform test3-start-Rick loop times
           move function current-date (13:4) to time-end
           compute time-loop = time-end - time-start
           display time-loop

           move function current-date (13:4) to time-start
           perform test3-start-Judson loop times
           move function current-date (13:4) to time-end
           compute time-loop = time-end - time-start
           display time-loop

           move function current-date (13:4) to time-start
           perform test3-start-hand-coded loop times
           move function current-date (13:4) to time-end
           compute time-loop = time-end - time-start
           display time-loop

           move function current-date (13:4) to time-start
           move "abcdefghijklm" to input-field
           perform test3-start-jud-coded loop times
           move function current-date (13:4) to time-end
           compute time-loop = time-end - time-start
           display time-loop
           display "press enter"
           accept char
           stop run
           .

       test3-start-Rick.
           move 1 to counter
           if input-field > spaces
               move function reverse (input-field) to input-field
               inspect input-field tallying counter for leading spaces
               move function reverse (input-field (counter:))
                   to input-field (counter:)
           end-if
           .
       test3-start-AS.
           if input-field not = spaces
               compute i = function length (input-field)
               perform varying counter from i by -1 until
                   input-field(counter:1) not = space
               end-perform
               compute k = function length(output-field) - counter
               move spaces to output-field (1:k)
               move input-field (1:counter)
                       to output-field (k + 1:counter)
           end-if
           .

       test3-start-AS1.
           if input-field not = spaces
             compute l = function length (input-field)
             move l to j
             move 0 to flag p
             perform until j = 1
               compute j rounded = (j / 2 )
               if ad add      j   to p
                else subtract j from p
               end-if
               if input-field(p:l - p) = space
                    move 1 to flag
                    if input-field(p - 1:) not = space
                       move 1 to j
                    end-if
               else move 0 to flag
               end-if
             end-perform
             if not ad subtract 1 from p end-if
             compute k = function length(output-field) - p
             move spaces to output-field (1:k)
             move input-field (1:p) to output-field (k + 1:p)
           end-if
           .

       test3-start-Judson.
           move 1 to counter
           if input-field not = spaces
               move function reverse(input-field) to output-field
               inspect output-field tallying counter for leading spaces
               string input-field delimited by size
                   into output-field with pointer counter
           end-if
           .

       test3-start-hand-coded.
           if input-field not = spaces
               compute i = function length (input-field)
               Move i to j
               perform until input-field (i:1) not = space
                   subtract 1 from i
               end-perform
               perform until i = 0
                   move input-field (i:1) to input-field (j:1)
                   subtract 1 from i
                   subtract 1 from j
               end-perform
               if j > 0
                   move spaces to input-field (1:j)
               end-if
           end-if
           .

       test3-start-jud-coded.
           if input-field not = spaces
               compute i = function length (input-field)
      *         Move i to j
               perform until input-field (i:1) not = space
                   subtract 1 from i
               end-perform
               compute j = function length(output-field)
               subtract i from j
               add 1 j giving k
               move input-field (1:i) to output-field (k:i)
               if j > 0
                   move spaces to output-field (1:j)
               end-if
           end-if
           .
```

I noticed the problem with Andreas' timer code, but did not want to
modify his code any more than necessary to run.  I was careful to run
the program so that it finished without rolling over a minute.

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Right Justify Routine

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** scm@enterprise.net (Shaun C. Murray)
- **Date:** 1998-09-09T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<6t5vhk$o3q$4@news.enterprise.net>`
- **References:** `<Jl0PnHJ5PvPd-pn2-XiTbHJiS5z0r@Dwight_Miller.iix.com> <o%VH1.3786$gM3.2803875@news4.mia.bellsouth.net> <trYH1.464$I%.1378959@news1.atlantic.net> <HtbI1.5187$eZ3.3317864@news3.mia.bellsouth.net> <BseI1.481$I%.1861130@news1.atlantic.net> <uCwI1.5340$gM3.3883152@news4.mia.bellsouth.net> <6t147m$8i2$1@news01.btx.dtag.de> <yA%I1.759$I%.3124690@news1.atlantic.net> <6t2mh3$aqm$1@news02.btx.dtag.de> <qkbJ1.503$cE5.712476@news2.mia.bellsouth.net>`

Since we're all doing it, I thought I'd add my opening gambit...

I've concentrated on using tight loops, native types and simple atomic 
instructions with few operands where possible which is how I do most of my 
programming anyway so it's a natural for me.

I've probably overlooked something but that was as I say, a first stab. I can 
probably get it quicker with a few more moves rather than using ref-mod.

Results from MF COBOL 4.0.32 on a PentiumII 300 running NT Server 4.0. 'SCM' 
results being mine. As you can see, the results are significantly quicker and 
there isn't much time difference between short and long strings unlike say 
Judson's or Andreas's first atempt.


AS :0161                            ab
AS1:0204                            ab
JUD:0165                            ab
SCM:0052                            ab
AS :0142                        abcdef
AS1:0142                        abcdef
JUD:0147                        abcdef
SCM:0048                        abcdef
AS :0113                abcdefghijklmn
AS1:0084                abcdefghijklmn
JUD:0113                abcdefghijklmn
SCM:0043                abcdefghijklmn
AS :0108               abcdefghijklmno
AS1:0256               abcdefghijklmno
JUD:0109               abcdefghijklmno
SCM:0043               abcdefghijklmno
AS :0077       abcdefghijklmnopqrstuvw
AS1:0210       abcdefghijklmnopqrstuvw
JUD:0076       abcdefghijklmnopqrstuvw
SCM:0036       abcdefghijklmnopqrstuvw


Program is as follows...

       Identification Division.
       Program-Id.  Test3.
      * right justify
       Environment Division.
       Configuration Section.
       Source-Computer.  IBM-PC.
       Object-Computer.  IBM-PC.
       Data Division.
       Working-Storage Section.
       01  input-field     pic x(30) value spaces.
       01  output-field    pic x(30) value spaces.
       01  teststrings-def.
           05 t1           pic x(30) value "ab".
           05 t2           pic x(30) value "abcdef".
           05 t3           pic x(30) value "abcdefghijklmn".
           05 t4           pic x(30) value "abcdefghijklmno".
           05 t5           pic x(30) value "abcdefghijklmnopqrstuvw".
       01  teststrings redefines teststrings-def.
           05 ti           pic x(30) occurs 5 times.
       01  tc              pic s9(4) comp.
       01  counter         pic s9(4) comp  value 1.
       01  char pic x.
       01  i               pic s9(4) comp.
       01  j               pic s9(4) comp.
       01  k               pic s9(4) comp.
       01  l               pic s9(4) comp.
       01  p               pic s9(4) comp.
       01  time-start      pic 9(4).
       01  time-end        pic 9(4).
       01  time-loop       pic 9(4).
       01  loop            pic s9(9) comp-5 value 500000.
       01  flag      pic 9 value 0.
           88 ad           value 0.
           88 sub          value 1.
       01  .
           03 scm-i        pic 9(4) comp-5.
           03 scm-j        pic 9(4) comp-5.

       Procedure Division.
       test-3-start.
           perform varying tc from 1 by 1 until tc > 5
            move ti(tc) to input-field
            move space to output-field
            move function current-date (13:4) to time-start
            perform test3-start-AS loop  times
            move function current-date (13:4) to time-end
            compute time-loop = time-end - time-start
            display "AS :" time-loop output-field

            move ti(tc) to input-field
            move function current-date (13:4) to time-start
            perform test3-start-AS1 loop  times
            move function current-date (13:4) to time-end
            compute time-loop = time-end - time-start
            display "AS1:" time-loop output-field

            move ti(tc) to input-field
            move function current-date (13:4) to time-start
            perform test3-start-jud-coded loop times
            move function current-date (13:4) to time-end
            compute time-loop = time-end - time-start
            display "JUD:" time-loop output-field

            move ti(tc) to input-field
            move function current-date (13:4) to time-start
            perform test3-start-scm loop times
            move function current-date (13:4) to time-end
            compute time-loop = time-end - time-start
            display "SCM:" time-loop output-field

           end-perform

           display "press enter"
           accept char
           stop run
           .

       for-later-use.
           move function current-date (13:4) to time-start
           perform test3-start-Rick loop times
           move function current-date (13:4) to time-end
           compute time-loop = time-end - time-start
           display time-loop

           move function current-date (13:4) to time-start
           perform test3-start-Judson loop times
           move function current-date (13:4) to time-end
           compute time-loop = time-end - time-start
           display time-loop

           move function current-date (13:4) to time-start
           perform test3-start-hand-coded loop times
           move function current-date (13:4) to time-end
           compute time-loop = time-end - time-start
           display time-loop

           move function current-date (13:4) to time-start
           move "abcdefghijklm" to input-field
           perform test3-start-jud-coded loop times
           move function current-date (13:4) to time-end
           compute time-loop = time-end - time-start
           display time-loop
           display "press enter"
           accept char
           stop run
           .

       test3-start-Rick.
           move 1 to counter
           if input-field > spaces
               move function reverse (input-field) to input-field
               inspect input-field tallying counter for leading spaces
               move function reverse (input-field (counter:))
                   to input-field (counter:)
           end-if
           .
       test3-start-AS.
           if input-field not = spaces
               compute i = function length (input-field)
               perform varying counter from i by -1 until
                   input-field(counter:1) not = space
               end-perform
               compute k = function length(output-field) - counter
               move spaces to output-field (1:k)
               move input-field (1:counter)
                       to output-field (k + 1:counter)
           end-if
           .

       test3-start-AS1.
           if input-field not = spaces
             compute l = function length (input-field)
             move l to j
             move 0 to flag p
             perform until j = 1
               compute j rounded = (j / 2 )
               if ad add      j   to p
                else subtract j from p
               end-if
               if input-field(p:l - p) = space
                    move 1 to flag
                    if input-field(p - 1:) not = space
                       move 1 to j
                    end-if
               else move 0 to flag
               end-if
             end-perform
             if not ad subtract 1 from p end-if
             compute k = function length(output-field) - p
             move spaces to output-field (1:k)
             move input-field (1:p) to output-field (k + 1:p)
           end-if
           .

       test3-start-Judson.
           move 1 to counter
           if input-field not = spaces
               move function reverse(input-field) to output-field
               inspect output-field tallying counter for leading spaces
               string input-field delimited by size
                   into output-field with pointer counter
           end-if
           .

       test3-start-scm.
           move length of input-field to scm-i
           move length of output-field to scm-j
           if input-field not = spaces
               perform until input-field(scm-i:1) not = space
                   subtract 1 from scm-i
               end-perform
               subtract scm-i from scm-j
               add 1 to scm-j
               move input-field(1:scm-i) to output-field(scm-j:)
           end-if
           .


       test3-start-hand-coded.
           if input-field not = spaces
               compute i = function length (input-field)
               Move i to j
               perform until input-field (i:1) not = space
                   subtract 1 from i
               end-perform
               perform until i = 0
                   move input-field (i:1) to input-field (j:1)
                   subtract 1 from i
                   subtract 1 from j
               end-perform
               if j > 0
                   move spaces to input-field (1:j)
               end-if
           end-if
           .

       test3-start-jud-coded.
           if input-field not = spaces
               compute i = function length (input-field)
      *         Move i to j
               perform until input-field (i:1) not = space
                   subtract 1 from i
               end-perform
               compute j = function length(output-field)
               subtract i from j
               add 1 j giving k
               move input-field (1:i) to output-field (k:i)
               if j > 0
                   move spaces to output-field (1:j)
               end-if
           end-if
           .
```

SCM:0071                            ab
SCM2:0051                            ab
SCM:0065                        abcdef
SCM2:0048                        abcdef
SCM:0053                abcdefghijklmn
SCM2:0043                abcdefghijklmn

Andreas Strzoda schrieb:
additional 20% speed up:

 test3-start-AS2.
     if input-field not = spaces
       compute l = function length (input-field)
       move l to j
       move 0 to flag p
       perform until j = 1
         compute j rounded = (j / 2 )
         if ad add      j   to p
          else subtract j from p
         end-if
->         if input-field(p:) = space
              move 1 to flag
*>subtract
->              if input-field(p - 1:1) not = space move 1 to j end-if
*>early end
         else move 0 to flag
*>add
         end-if
       end-perform
       if not ad subtract 1 from p end-if *>p points to the first
trailing space
       compute k = function length(output-field) - p *>assuming that k >
p!
       move spaces to output-field (1:k)
       move input-field (1:p) to output-field (k + 1:p)
     end-if
     .
```

```
indeed couldn't we all.
The problem is to discover and accept that
your compiler vendor has let you down.

New programmers invariably blame the compiler,
the system, the computer, the Easter Bunny, ... when
their programs don't work. Then later, you get to the
point where you finally accept that it is you making the
mistake and that the compiler is never at fault.
(well, hardly ever). That's why it's a slow process to accept
that the compiler actually did do you in. (sigh)

Donald Tees wrote in message <6t1ab1$b90$1@news.igs.net>...
>
>Leif Svalgaard wrote in message <35f3e7e2.0@news1.ibm.net>...
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Right Justify Routine

- **From:** paulr@bix.com (paulr)
- **Date:** 1998-09-10T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<6t8cil$1iq@lotho.delphi.com>`
- **References:** `<Jl0PnHJ5PvPd-pn2-XiTbHJiS5z0r@Dwight_Miller.iix.com> <o%VH1.3786$gM3.2803875@news4.mia.bellsouth.net> <trYH1.464$I%.1378959@news1.atlantic.net> <HtbI1.5187$eZ3.3317864@news3.mia.bellsouth.net> <BseI1.481$I%.1861130@news1.atlantic.net> <uCwI1.5340$gM3.3883152@news4.mia.bellsouth.net> <35f2ef66.0@news1.ibm.net> <35f32510.0@news1.ibm.net> <zkRI1.696$I%.2804567@news1.atlantic.net> <35f3e7e2.0@news1.ibm.net> <6t1ab1$b90$1@news.igs.net> <35f44246.0@news1.ibm.net>`

```
You know, just as an aside here, *why* are PC COBOL vendors,
with the notable exceptions of Fujitsu and IBM, so darned
persnickety - as well as expensive? You talk to them, and 
they act wierd. If you are talking less than $100K, they
don't even want to bother with you. Exceptions of course,
because all of the companies have some good people, but
I've had a real go around with RM (Liant), and a minor
tussel with AcuCOBOL. Microfocus is a great company, but
they are tunnel vision set on the Mainframe market, or
at least they were last year. 

For $300 I can pick up a very decent C compiler with tons
of documentation that has no runtime issues. Same with 
Pascal (Delphi) and Basic. The lowest cost COBOL compiler
is Fujitsu, which runs around $500 I think. VA COBOL is 
expensive, but carries no run time usage (to the best of
my knowledge that is...) and is a purely wonderful 
compiler. Fast too. (List is $2K I think, but you can pick 
it up very inexpensively though several IBM programs...) 

So why is it $3 to $4K for AcuCOBOL? Plus runtimes? 
(*sigh*) They act like it is a scarcity market or
something...

-Paul

## Thread: How to call Btrieve Funktion in FSC V3 — 7 msgs

_Topics: [`Help requests and how-to`](topics.md#help)_

### How to call Btrieve Funktion in FSC V3

- **From:** TKS-Schmitteck@t-online.de (Paul Schmitteck)
- **Date:** 1998-09-07T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<6t07jq$ekg$2@news02.btx.dtag.de>`

```
Hello Everybody,
       I have a question.. I have an application which uses brieve
functions via "_BTRV" requester supplied by MicroFocus cobol. Now we are
converting the program from MicroFocus Cobol to Fujitsu Cobol 3.0. 

Can anybody help me know how to call btrieve functions for accessing the
files in btrieve from the Windows environment ? Please include a sample
program if it is possible. It is very Urgent.

Thanks.
PAUL SCHMITTECK.
```

#### ↳ Re: How to call Btrieve Funktion in FSC V3

- **From:** AS-DATA@t-online.de (Andreas Strzoda)
- **Date:** 1998-09-07T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<6t13v0$8g0$1@news01.btx.dtag.de>`
- **References:** `<6t07jq$ekg$2@news02.btx.dtag.de>`

```
Paul Schmitteck schrieb:

> Hello Everybody,
>        I have a question.. I have an application which uses brieve
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
> PAUL SCHMITTECK.

   You don't have to...

just write in your SELECT-clause the file-name (like ART.BTR) a comma
and the word BTRV (...ART.BTR,BTRV) and the file is a BTRIEVE-file,
nice, uhh (if you whant the same in MF, try my BTRV.EXE, a file-handler
for MF-Cobol using BTRIEVE)
```

in our program there isï¿½nt a SELECT Clause.

get position
get direct

using a Select clause?

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: How to call Btrieve Funktion in FSC V3

- **From:** AS-DATA@t-online.de (Andreas Strzoda)
- **Date:** 1998-09-08T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<6t2mh5$aqm$3@news02.btx.dtag.de>`
- **References:** `<6t07jq$ekg$2@news02.btx.dtag.de> <6t13v0$8g0$1@news01.btx.dtag.de> <6t2iqg$dmn$1@news00.btx.dtag.de>`

```
Paul Schmitteck schrieb:

> Sorry
> in our program there isï¿½nt a SELECT Clause.
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
> TIA Paul

   Ohh, that doesn't work, you have to code an DLL-File
that will call by your routine but...

why do you need "GET DIRECT" or "GET POSITION"
in a cobol-environment??????

I think you only need it to reorganize the file!
(or, at a special reason an create supplemental,
you can do it by calling BUTIL -SINDEX ...
via the EXECute shell)

regards

Andreas Strzoda
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: How to call Btrieve Funktion in FSC V3

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** TKS-Schmitteck@t-online.de (Paul Schmitteck)
- **Date:** 1998-09-08T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<6t30lb$fq4$1@news00.btx.dtag.de>`
- **References:** `<6t07jq$ekg$2@news02.btx.dtag.de> <6t13v0$8g0$1@news01.btx.dtag.de> <6t2iqg$dmn$1@news00.btx.dtag.de> <6t2mh5$aqm$3@news02.btx.dtag.de>`

```
with get direkt you need only a 4 byte pointer to read a record again. We
store this 4 byte pointer into an array.

Andreas Strzoda schrieb in Nachricht <6t2mh5$aqm$3@news02.btx.dtag.de>...
>Paul Schmitteck schrieb:
>
…[29 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: How to call Btrieve Funktion in FSC V3

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc123@bellsouth.net>
- **Date:** 1998-09-08T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<mvbJ1.511$cE5.718189@news2.mia.bellsouth.net>`
- **References:** `<6t07jq$ekg$2@news02.btx.dtag.de> <6t13v0$8g0$1@news01.btx.dtag.de> <6t2iqg$dmn$1@news00.btx.dtag.de> <6t2mh5$aqm$3@news02.btx.dtag.de>`

```
Andreas Strzoda wrote:
>Paul Schmitteck schrieb:
>
…[18 more quoted lines elided]…
>in a cobol-environment??????


You need it to avoid excess index searching when accessing a record,
accessing other records, then re-accessing the first record.  In some
contexts, saving off the direct key to get back to a record can make
a very large difference in speed of execution.
```

001640*-----------------------------------------------------------------
001650 DATA DIVISION.
001660
001670 WORKING-STORAGE SECTION.
001680
001690 01  BTRIEVE-OPERATION-CODE           PIC 9(4) COMP-5 VALUE ZERO.
001700 01  BTRIEVE-POSITION-BLOCK           PIC X(128) VALUE SPACES.
001710 01  BTRIEVE-DATA-BUFFER.
           05  FILLER                       PIC X(1) VALUE SPACES
                                            OCCURS 1 TO 33455 TIMES
                                            DEPENDING ON
                                            BTRIEVE-DATA-BUFFER-LENGTH.
001720 01  BTRIEVE-DATA-BUFFER-LENGTH       PIC 9(4) COMP-5 VALUE ZERO.
001730 01  BTRIEVE-KEY-BUFFER               PIC X(256) VALUE SPACES.
001740 01  BTRIEVE-KEY-NUMBER               PIC 9(4) COMP-5 VALUE ZERO.
001750 01  BTRIEVE-STATUS-CODE              PIC 9(4) COMP-5 VALUE ZERO.
001760     88  BTRIEVE-IO-SUCCESSFUL           VALUE 0.
001770     88  BTRIEVE-INVALID-OPERATION       VALUE 1.
001780     88  BTRIEVE-IO-ERROR                VALUE 2.
001790     88  BTRIEVE-FILE-NOT-OPEN           VALUE 3.
001800     88  BTRIEVE-CANNOT-FIND-KEY-VALUE   VALUE 4.
001810     88  BTRIEVE-DUPLICATE-KEY-VALUE     VALUE 5.
001820     88  BTRIEVE-INVALID-KEY-PARAMETER   VALUE 6.
001830     88  BTRIEVE-KEY-NUMBER-CHANGED      VALUE 7.
001840     88  BTRIEVE-INVALID-POSITIONING     VALUE 8.
001850     88  BTRIEVE-END-OF-FILE             VALUE 9.
001860     88  BTRIEVE-KEY-NOT-MODIFIABLE      VALUE 10.
001870     88  BTRIEVE-FILENAME-INVALID        VALUE 11.
001880     88  BTRIEVE-CANNOT-FIND-FILE        VALUE 12.
001890     88  BTRIEVE-MICROKERNEL-INACTIVE    VALUE 20.
001900     88  BTRIEVE-FILE-ACCESS-DENIED      VALUE 46.
001910     88  BTRIEVE-RECORD-PAGE-LOCKED      VALUE 84.
001920     88  BTRIEVE-FILE-TABLE-FULL         VALUE 86.
001930     88  BTRIEVE-HANDLE-TABLE-FULL       VALUE 87.
001940     88  BTRIEVE-INCOMPATIBLE-MODE       VALUE 88.
001950     88  BTRIEVE-NO-CACHE-BUFFERS        VALUE 100.
001960
001970 01  BTRIEVE-FILE-NAME                PIC X(260) VALUE SPACES.
001980
001990 01  BTRIEVE-CLIENT-ID.
002000     05  FILLER                       PIC X(12) VALUE ZERO.
002010     05  BTRIEVE-SERVICE-AGENT-ID     PIC X(2) VALUE SPACES.
002020     05  BTRIEVE-CLIENT-IDENTIFIER    PIC 9(4) COMP-5 VALUE ZERO.
002030
002040 01  BTRIEVE-CREATE-DATA-BUFFER.
002050     05  BTRIEVE-FILE-SPECIFICATION.
002060         10  BTRIEVE-LOGICAL-RECORD-LENGTH PIC 9(4) COMP-5 VALUE ZERO.
002070         10  BTRIEVE-PAGE-SIZE             PIC 9(4) COMP-5 VALUE ZERO.
002080             88  BTRIEVE-512-PAGE-SIZE     VALUE 512.
002090             88  BTRIEVE-1024-PAGE-SIZE    VALUE 1024.
002100             88  BTRIEVE-1536-PAGE-SIZE    VALUE 1536.
002110             88  BTRIEVE-2048-PAGE-SIZE    VALUE 2048.
002120             88  BTRIEVE-2560-PAGE-SIZE    VALUE 2560.
002130             88  BTRIEVE-3072-PAGE-SIZE    VALUE 3072.
002140             88  BTRIEVE-3584-PAGE-SIZE    VALUE 3584.
002150             88  BTRIEVE-4096-PAGE-SIZE    VALUE 4096.
002160         10  BTRIEVE-NUMBER-OF-INDEXES     PIC 9(4) COMP-5 VALUE ZERO.
002170         10  FILLER                        PIC X(4) VALUE SPACES.
002180         10  BTRIEVE-FILE-FLAGS            PIC 9(4) COMP-5 VALUE ZERO.
002190         10  BTRIEVE-NUMBER-OF-DUPLICATE   PIC X VALUE X"00".
002200         10  FILLER                        PIC X VALUE SPACE.
002210         10  BTRIEVE-ALLOCATION            PIC 9(4) COMP-5 VALUE ZERO.
002220     05  BTRIEVE-KEY-SPECIFICATION.
002230         10  BTRIEVE-KEY-POSITION          PIC 9(4) COMP-5 VALUE ZERO.
002240         10  BTRIEVE-KEY-LENGTH            PIC 9(4) COMP-5 VALUE ZERO.
002250         10  BTRIEVE-KEY-FLAGS             PIC 9(4) COMP-5 VALUE ZERO.
002260         10  FILLER                        PIC X(4) VALUE SPACE.
002270         10  BTRIEVE-EXTENDED-DATA-TYPE    PIC X VALUE X"00".
002280             88  BTRIEVE-STRING            VALUE X"00".
002290             88  BTRIEVE-INTEGER           VALUE X"01".
002300             88  BTRIEVE-FLOAT             VALUE X"02".
002310             88  BTRIEVE-DATE              VALUE X"03".
002320             88  BTRIEVE-TIME              VALUE X"04".
002330             88  BTRIEVE-DECIMAL           VALUE X"05".
002340             88  BTRIEVE-MONEY             VALUE X"06".
002350             88  BTRIEVE-LOGICAL           VALUE X"07".
002360             88  BTRIEVE-NUMERIC           VALUE X"08".
002370             88  BTRIEVE-BFLOAT            VALUE X"09".
002380             88  BTRIEVE-LSTRING           VALUE X"0A".
002390             88  BTRIEVE-ZSTRING           VALUE X"0B".
002400             88  BTRIEVE-UNSIGNED-BINARY   VALUE X"0E".
002410             88  BTRIEVE-AUTOINCREMENT     VALUE X"0F".
002420             88  BTRIEVE-NUMERICSTS        VALUE X"11".
002430             88  BTRIEVE-NUMERICSA         VALUE X"12".
002440             88  BTRIEVE-CURRENCY          VALUE X"13".
002450             88  BTRIEVE-TIMESTAMP         VALUE X"14".
002460         10  BTRIEVE-NULL-VALUE            PIC X VALUE X"00".
002470         10  FILLER                        PIC X(2) VALUE SPACES.
002480         10  BTRIEVE-ASSIGNED-KEY-NUMBER   PIC X VALUE X"00".
002490         10  BTRIEVE-ACS-NUMBER            PIC X VALUE X"00".
002500     05  BTRIEVE-ACS-NUMBER-0              PIC X(256) VALUE SPACES.
002510
002520
002530
002540*-----------------------------------------------------------------
002550 PROCEDURE DIVISION.
002560
002570     DISPLAY "Fujitsu COBOL -- Btrieve Interface Sample"
002580     DISPLAY SPACES
002590
002600     *> Open Btrieve File
002610     MOVE ZERO TO BTRIEVE-DATA-BUFFER-LENGTH
002620     MOVE ZERO TO BTRIEVE-KEY-NUMBER
002630     MOVE "TEST.BTR" TO BTRIEVE-FILE-NAME
002640     MOVE BTRIEVE-OPEN TO BTRIEVE-OPERATION-CODE
002650     PERFORM CALL-BTRIEVE
002660     IF BTRIEVE-STATUS-CODE = ZERO
002670        DISPLAY "Btrieve Open Successful"
002680     ELSE
002690        DISPLAY "Error Opening file. Status=" BTRIEVE-STATUS-CODE
002700     END-IF
002710
002720     *> Get First Record
002730     MOVE 40 TO BTRIEVE-DATA-BUFFER-LENGTH
002740     MOVE ZERO TO BTRIEVE-KEY-NUMBER
002750     MOVE BTRIEVE-GET-FIRST TO BTRIEVE-OPERATION-CODE
002760     PERFORM CALL-BTRIEVE
002770     IF BTRIEVE-STATUS-CODE = ZERO
002780        DISPLAY "Btrieve Record: " BTRIEVE-DATA-BUFFER( 1 : 40 )
002790     ELSE
002800        DISPLAY "Error Getting First Record. Status="
BTRIEVE-STATUS-CODE
002810     END-IF
002820
002830     *> Close Btrieve File
002840     MOVE ZERO TO BTRIEVE-DATA-BUFFER-LENGTH
002850     MOVE ZERO TO BTRIEVE-KEY-NUMBER
002860     MOVE BTRIEVE-CLOSE TO BTRIEVE-OPERATION-CODE
002870     PERFORM CALL-BTRIEVE
002880     IF BTRIEVE-STATUS-CODE = ZERO
002890        DISPLAY "Btrieve Close File Successful"
002900     ELSE
002910        DISPLAY "Error Closing Btrieve File. Status="
BTRIEVE-STATUS-CODE
002920     END-IF
002930
002940     EXIT PROGRAM.
002950
002960
002970*-----------------------------------------------------------------
002980 CALL-BTRIEVE.
002990
003000     CALL "BTRCALL" WITH STDCALL USING BTRIEVE-OPERATION-CODE
003010                                       BTRIEVE-STATUS-CODE
003020                                       BTRIEVE-POSITION-BLOCK
003030                                       BTRIEVE-DATA-BUFFER
003040                                       BTRIEVE-DATA-BUFFER-LENGTH
003050                                       BTRIEVE-FILE-NAME
003060                                       BTRIEVE-KEY-NUMBER
003070     END-CALL.
003080
003090
003100*-----------------------------------------------------------------
003110*CALL-BTRIEVE-CLIENT.
003120
003130
003140*-----------------------------------------------------------------
003150 END PROGRAM FJ2BTRV.
003160*-----------------------------------------------------------------

Fujitsu Software Corporation
Developer Tools Group

FAX:    (408) 428-0600
Email:  cobol@adtools.com
Web:   http://www.adtools.com

…[9 more quoted lines elided]…

### Re: Right Justify Routine

- **From:** "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@aiservices.com>
- **Date:** 1998-09-07T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<cV%I1.762$I%.3132656@news1.atlantic.net>`

```
For anyone who is working with code from my earlier post,
please make the following changes.

Rick Smith wrote in message ...
>
>       test3-start-Rick.
>           move "abcdefghijklm" to input-field
>           move 1 to counter
>           if input-field > spaces
==> change the above to read
==>      if input-field not = spaces
>               move function reverse (input-field) to input-field
>               inspect input-field tallying counter for leading spaces
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
>               move output-field to input-field
==> add these lines
==>      else
==>          move spaces to output-field
>           end-if
>           .

Though the changes do not affect performance, it is still
better to have correct code.
-------------------------------
Rick Smith
e-mail: < ricksmith@aiservices.com >
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Right Justify Routine

- **From:** AS-DATA@t-online.de (Andreas Strzoda)
- **Date:** 1998-09-08T00:00:00

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Right Justify Routine

- **From:** "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@aiservices.com>
- **Date:** 1998-09-08T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<bL9J1.767$I%.3377741@news1.atlantic.net>`
- **References:** `<Jl0PnHJ5PvPd-pn2-XiTbHJiS5z0r@Dwight_Miller.iix.com> <o%VH1.3786$gM3.2803875@news4.mia.bellsouth.net> <trYH1.464$I%.1378959@news1.atlantic.net> <HtbI1.5187$eZ3.3317864@news3.mia.bellsouth.net> <BseI1.481$I%.1861130@news1.atlantic.net> <cV%I1.762$I%.3132656@news1.atlantic.net> <620J1.763$I%.3137036@news1.atlantic.net> <6t2mh4$aqm$2@news02.btx.dtag.de>`

```

Andreas Strzoda wrote in message <6t2mh4$aqm$2@news02.btx.dtag.de>...
>Rick Smith schrieb:
>
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
>
Actually, it is a requirement that the two fields be the same size.
Therefore, the length must be verified before the first execution
of the routines. Consider the following:

       procedure division.
       start-of-program.
           if function length ( input-field )
                   not equal function length ( output-field )
               display "Right justify field lengths are different"
               stop run
           end-if
      * Now get on with the reset of the program

Or make certain they are the same size when defined.
-------------------------------
Rick Smith
e-mail: < ricksmith@aiservices.com >
```

## Thread: Command Line Compiling Fujitsu — 2 msgs

_Topics: [`Compilers and vendors`](topics.md#compilers)_

### Command Line Compiling Fujitsu

- **From:** "Gene Webb" <gene.webb@newsguy.com>
- **Date:** 1998-09-07T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<6t1rma$mqa@enews2.newsguy.com>`

```
If anyone has a sample of;

The environment/path settings for Fujitsu
How to compile and link from the command line this will help me on my
Fujitsu tutorial for OO COBOL

I need one for creating a exe and one for a dll

Thanks.
```

#### ↳ Re: Command Line Compiling Fujitsu

- **From:** "Leif Svalgaard" <leif@ibm.net>
- **Date:** 1998-09-07T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<35f493df.0@news1.ibm.net>`
- **References:** `<6t1rma$mqa@enews2.newsguy.com>`

```

Gene Webb wrote in message <6t1rma$mqa@enews2.newsguy.com>...
>If anyone has a sample of;
>
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>I need one for creating a exe and one for a dll


Gene, here is what I use.



First cobol85.cbi    <====================================

[Compile Options]
ALPHAL=No
DLOAD=Yes
OPTIMIZE=Yes
TEST=No
LIB=.;%ETK32DIR%
MAIN=No
RSV=ALL
SRF=FIX,FIX
TAB=8
[COBOL85]
Language=E


then cobol.bat  for making DLL  <=================================:

@echo off
::   COBOL - compilation using FUJITSU COBOL
::   Date-Written:  97/03/09    Leif Svalgaard
::       -Revised:  97/09/22    Leif Svalgaard

if exist quiet.q$q   del quiet.q$q   > nul
if exist cobol85.reg del cobol85.reg > nul

if '%1' == '/Q' goto quiet
if '%1' == '/q' goto quiet
goto continue

:quiet
echo quiet>quiet.q$q
shift

:continue
if '%1' == '' goto help

:repeat
if exist %1.err del %1.err > nul
if exist %1.COB goto exists

:error
%lib%\cobol32 -i %etkdir%\cobol85.cbi %1.cob > %1.err

:stop
type %1.err | more
pause
goto end

:show
type %1.err | more
pause
goto loop

:try_again
:exists
if exist %1.obj del %1.obj > nul
if exist quiet.q$q goto compile

echo Compiling  %1...
:compile
%lib%\cobol32 -i %etkdir%\cobol85.cbi %1.cob > nul

:test
if errorlevel 1     goto error
if not exist %1.obj goto error
call linkup %1

:loop
shift
if not '%1' == '' goto repeat
goto end

:help
echo ������������������������������������������������������ͻ
echo � COBOL [/Q] program [program]...                      �
echo ������������������������������������������������������͹
echo � Compiles a COBOL program using FUJITSU Cobol. If the �
echo � program contains errors, these are displayed as they �
echo � are detected.  /Quiet switch minimizes chat.         �
echo � If errors were found, the file with extension .err   �
echo � is left as evidence.                                 �
echo ������������������������������������������������������ͼ
:end
if exist quiet.q$q   del quiet.q$q   > nul
if exist cobol85.reg del cobol85.reg > nul



it uses linkup.bat <=====================================:

@echo off
::   LINKUP - link a program to DLL
::
::   Date-Written:  97/03/09    Leif Svalgaard
::       -Revised:  97/09/22    Leif Svalgaard
::
if '%1' == '' goto help

:loop
if '%1' == '' goto end

if exist %1.obj goto link
echo %1.obj not found
goto end

:link
rem echo making DLL %1...
echo LIBRARY %1 >  %1.exp
echo EXPORTS %1 >> %1.exp
upper<%1.exp>%1.def

%lib%\lib/nologo /def:%1.def /out:%1.lib /machine:ix86 %1.obj  > nul
%lib%\link/nologo %1.obj %1.exp /dll /out:%1.dll @%etkdir%\f3libs

:done
if exist %1.def  del  %1.def > nul
if exist %1.exp  del  %1.exp > nul
if exist %1.lib  del  %1.lib > nul
if exist %1.obj  del  %1.obj > nul
shift
goto loop

:help
echo ����������������������������������������������������ͻ
echo � LINKUP {subprogram}...                             �
echo ����������������������������������������������������͹
echo � Create .DLL modules from .OBJ files.               �
echo � Example - LINKUP myprog                            �
echo ����������������������������������������������������ͼ

:end


then cobolm.bat to make EXE <====================================:
@echo off
::   COBOLM - compilation using FUJITSU COBOL
::   Date-Written:  97/03/09    Leif Svalgaard
::       -Revised:  97/09/22    Leif Svalgaard

if '%1' == '' goto help

:repeat
if exist %1.err del %1.err
if exist %1.COB goto exists

:error
%lib%\cobol32 -M -i %etkdir%\cobol85.cbi %1.cob > %1.err
type %1.err|more

:testdesk
if '%desk%' == 'on' pause
goto loop

:exists
if exist %1.obj del %1.obj > nul
echo Compiling  %1...
%lib%\cobol32 -M -i %etkdir%\cobol85.cbi %1.cob > nul

:test
if errorlevel 1 goto error
if not exist %1.obj goto error
call linkupm  %1

:loop
shift
if not '%1' == '' goto repeat
goto end

:help
echo ������������������������������������������������������ͻ
echo � COBOLM program [program]...                          �
echo ������������������������������������������������������͹
echo � Compiles a Main program using FUJITSU Cobol.  If the �
echo � program contains errors, these are displayed as they �
echo � are detected.                                        �
echo � If errors were found, the file with extension .err   �
echo � is left as evidence.                                 �
echo ������������������������������������������������������ͼ
if '%desk%' == 'on' pause
:end


it uses linkupm.bat  <====================================================


@echo off
::   LINKUPM  - link a root program to EXE
::
::   Date-Written:  97/03/09    Leif Svalgaard
::       -Revised:  97/09/22    Leif Svalgaard
::
if '%1' == '' goto help

:loop
if '%1' == '' goto end

if exist %1.obj goto link
echo %1.obj not found
goto end

:link
echo making EXE %1...
%lib%\link/nologo %1.obj @%etkdir%\f3libs /out:%1.exe
if exist %1.obj del %1.obj > nul

shift
goto loop

:help
echo ����������������������������������������������������ͻ
echo � LINKUPM {program}...                               �
echo ����������������������������������������������������͹
echo � Create .EXE modules from one or more .OBJ files.   �
echo � This procedure is normally only used to link the   �
echo � ETK main program.                                  �
echo � Example - LINKUPM etk                              �
echo ����������������������������������������������������ͼ

:end
```
