### Re: Right Justify Routine

- **From:** "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@aiservices.com>
- **Date:** 1998-09-07T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<vaTI1.700$I%.2860000@news1.atlantic.net>`
- **References:** `<Jl0PnHJ5PvPd-pn2-XiTbHJiS5z0r@Dwight_Miller.iix.com> <o%VH1.3786$gM3.2803875@news4.mia.bellsouth.net> <trYH1.464$I%.1378959@news1.atlantic.net> <HtbI1.5187$eZ3.3317864@news3.mia.bellsouth.net> <BseI1.481$I%.1861130@news1.atlantic.net> <uCwI1.5340$gM3.3883152@news4.mia.bellsouth.net> <35f2ef66.0@news1.ibm.net> <35f32510.0@news1.ibm.net> <zkRI1.696$I%.2804567@news1.atlantic.net> <35f3e7e2.0@news1.ibm.net>`

```

Leif Svalgaard wrote in message <35f3e7e2.0@news1.ibm.net>...
>With MF v2.5.116 your testprogram just shows spaces.
>In fact, none of the functions have any effect.
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>not expect too much...

Version 2.5 was the first Micro Focus version with intrinsic
functions. Since these features were part of the final release
(not early release), they must work, or the compiler is broken.
To me, this is more surprising than the 100 to 1 speed up of
the optimized right justify solutions

You should expect that all features of a compiler work even
if you do not use those features!

>Anyway, that was really not my point as I don't use
>functions in the programs compiled with that compiler
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>
I understood your original point; but had no explanation.

Thank You,
-------------------------------
Rick Smith
e-mail: < ricksmith@aiservices.com >
```

#### ↳ Re: Right Justify Routine

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1998-09-07T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<6t1fk6$kr8@dfw-ixnews7.ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<Jl0PnHJ5PvPd-pn2-XiTbHJiS5z0r@Dwight_Miller.iix.com> <o%VH1.3786$gM3.2803875@news4.mia.bellsouth.net> <trYH1.464$I%.1378959@news1.atlantic.net> <HtbI1.5187$eZ3.3317864@news3.mia.bellsouth.net> <BseI1.481$I%.1861130@news1.atlantic.net> <uCwI1.5340$gM3.3883152@news4.mia.bellsouth.net> <35f2ef66.0@news1.ibm.net> <35f32510.0@news1.ibm.net> <zkRI1.696$I%.2804567@news1.atlantic.net> <35f3e7e2.0@news1.ibm.net> <vaTI1.700$I%.2860000@news1.atlantic.net>`

```

Rick Smith wrote in message ...
>
>Leif Svalgaard wrote in message <35f3e7e2.0@news1.ibm.net>...
…[33 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Having been "intimately involved" with the initial Intrinsic Function
support, I can GUARANTEE that there was no "generally available" release of
Micro Focus COBOL for which the functions were accepted as valid syntax -
but did not work.  (We ran them thru the FIPS tests and they passed - before
we released the product.)  There were some early release products with
problems - and the 1st GA products required that you link some function
modules (exe? DLL?) into the system, but they did work.  I am quite certain
that those releases are so old that no one in MF would still support them,
but I really REALLY am certain that the functions did work in the operating
systems for which they were designed (documented) and with all the MF
support modules that were documented available.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Right Justify Routine

- **From:** "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@aiservices.com>
- **Date:** 1998-09-07T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<vA%I1.757$I%.3124690@news1.atlantic.net>`
- **References:** `<Jl0PnHJ5PvPd-pn2-XiTbHJiS5z0r@Dwight_Miller.iix.com> <o%VH1.3786$gM3.2803875@news4.mia.bellsouth.net> <trYH1.464$I%.1378959@news1.atlantic.net> <HtbI1.5187$eZ3.3317864@news3.mia.bellsouth.net> <BseI1.481$I%.1861130@news1.atlantic.net> <uCwI1.5340$gM3.3883152@news4.mia.bellsouth.net> <35f2ef66.0@news1.ibm.net> <35f32510.0@news1.ibm.net> <zkRI1.696$I%.2804567@news1.atlantic.net> <35f3e7e2.0@news1.ibm.net> <vaTI1.700$I%.2860000@news1.atlantic.net> <6t1fk6$kr8@dfw-ixnews7.ix.netcom.com>`

```

William M. Klein wrote in message <6t1fk6$kr8@dfw-ixnews7.ix.netcom.com>...
>
>Rick Smith wrote in message ...
…[16 more quoted lines elided]…
>but did not work.  (We ran them thru the FIPS tests and they passed -
before
>we released the product.)  There were some early release products with
>problems - and the 1st GA products required that you link some function
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>

In fact it has been my experience that the core Micro Focus products,
Professional COBOL and COBOL/2, have always worked very well,
which is why a problem with one of the features of the language is
very surprising.

Shaun C. Murrary confirmed that FUNCTION REVERSE works in
2.5.25; but Leif Svalgaard was unable to use it in 2.5.116. Obviously,
I cannot confirm whether COBINTFN.EXE (.OBJ, or .DLL) is available
on his system; but its absence should have been reported as an error.

In any case, there appears to be no need to pursue this any further.
Leif has stated that none of his existing programs require intrinsic
functions and the fact that it worked at one time is reassuring.
-------------------------------
Rick Smith
e-mail: < ricksmith@aiservices.com >
```

<a id="t-d7025d96da"></a>
## Thread: COBOL and Perl — 1 msgs

### Re: COBOL and Perl

- **From:** John Porter <jdporter@min.net>
- **Date:** 1998-09-07T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.perl.misc,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<35F435AC.50710098@min.net>`
- **References:** `<6r7fi0$9a7$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com> <6rldhj$na5@lotho.delphi.com> <tglnnz8roi.fsf@noise.bsd.uchicago.edu> <6t0dk0$ep5$4@news.enterprise.net>`

```
Shaun C. Murray wrote:
> 
> In article <tglnnz8roi.fsf@noise.bsd.uchicago.edu>,
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
> that that's evolved over 100's of years.

That was the whole idea behind Algol.
The fact that Perl is an Algol language is nothing against Perl, IMHO.


> >I mean yes, I have a vague idea of what's going on... the loop is
> >counting the records in the data-file, yes?  But if it's doing more
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> prove my point?

Only if you can honestly say that you don't have even the vaguest
notion of what the following code does:

    do { &test_data } until $data_rec;

    $percentage = 10;

    print "hello";

    open $data_file;
    read $data_file, $data_record;
    if ( eof )
    {
        print "end of file";
    }
    else
    {
        $rec_no = $rec_no + 1;
    }
    close $data_file;
    exit;


> >Obviously I could learn all of these things about COBOL, just
> >as you could learn enough about perl to understand the tab-splitting
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> fields yet the Perl example had the fields locations specified.

No, it was supposed to show in how few lines the algorithm could be
coded
in Perl, as an answer to the challenge that it could be done in fewer
lines
of COBOL.  It did it in 3 lines of Perl (plus two comments), but I could
do it in one.

As has already been discussed, COBOL is quite capable of reading in 
tab-separated variable-length fields; that was not the point.

Btw, what's wrong with specifying the widths of the output fields?
You would have to do as much (although via a very different syntax)
in COBOL.

Those who prefer "pictures" of the output might be more comfortable
with the following, which is an equivalent solution:

	format =
	@<<<<<<<@####.##@>>>>>>>
	@_
	.

	do { split /\t/; write; } while <>;

Here, the output fields are specified in the format; this format
contains pictures for three fields -- the first is left-justified,
the third is right-justfied, and the second is right-justified
with fixed-point numeric.


> Where you would use {}, begin/end etc in other languages,
> you have END- scope delimiters eg.
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>      end-if

Just like Unix shell scripting languages (Bourne shell and C shell).

John Porter
```

<a id="t-c2c7842606"></a>
## Thread: Unisis MAPPER Needed — 2 msgs

### Re: Unisis MAPPER Needed

- **From:** sff5ky@aol.com (Sff5ky)
- **Date:** 1998-09-07T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<1998090714315500.KAA19545@ladder03.news.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<35f370e4.1914876@netnews.worldnet.att.net>`

```
In article <35f370e4.1914876@netnews.worldnet.att.net>,
Current_Occupant@aol.com (Current Occupant) writes:

>
>>Unisys-MAPPER.     You
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>


I beg to differ.  I seem to recall that there was a MAPPER utility that would
read COBOL source code, merge in all copybooks, and produce an analysis summary
of the program detailing every paragraph, its entry points, exit points,
perform stack depth, and highlight dead code blocks.  This was one of the most
useful tools I have ever run across for getting up to speed on an existing
program you've been assigned to learn/maintenance.  Unfortunately, I have not
seen this utility around since '83.  If you find it or a PC equivalent, post

Thank you for the information, but could you send us one exemple?

Many Thanks
EP

*---------------------------------------------------------------------------
----------------------------------------------------*
*---------------------------------------------------------------------------
----------------------------------------------------*

You can obtain the window handle for the text window by calling
PC_WIN_HANDLE. Once it has been obtained you can call the ShowWindow API to
set focus or maximize/minimize. However, you should not attempt to close
the window because that will cause the run-time to execute a STOP RUN.

## Thread: COBOL and Perl — 1 msgs

### Re: COBOL and Perl

- **From:** Darrin Edwards <d-edwards@nospam.uchicago.edu>
- **Date:** 1998-09-07T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.perl.misc,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<tgr9xnx1dc.fsf@noise.bsd.uchicago.edu>`
- **References:** `<6r7fi0$9a7$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com> <6rldhj$na5@lotho.delphi.com> <tglnnz8roi.fsf@noise.bsd.uchicago.edu> <6t0dk0$ep5$4@news.enterprise.net>`

```
scm@enterprise.net (Shaun C. Murray) writes:

> >Well, I think that depends on the problem domain the language is
> >designed to handle.  Again, I don't think this criticism is particular
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
> notation for that that's evolved over 100's of years.

So one might infer from the age and popularity of such notational
conveniences that notational conveniences are sometimes considered
useful.
[More on shorthand at end.]

> >If I say that I really don't understand whether "test-data" and
> >"data-rec" here are builtin operators, user-supplied functions, or
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> from other languages. Try just reading it as English.
[snip my guesses]
> Nope that's all it was doing and you had more than a vague idea. From not 
> seeing any COBOL before, you know what the program segment was doing.
> Does that prove my point?

I said I didn't understand some of the constructs.  You said "Try reading
it as English," and I still don't understand what some of the constructs are
or what they are meant to do.  If your point was that the semantics
should be trivially extractable from the syntax, even for someone who
(like me) does not know the syntax, then I would have to say no.  (Does
this prove my point, that even a "relatively simple" syntax does not
guarantee transparency of semantics?)

> >  Is data-file the actual filename, or
> >is it a variable for a filename supplied elsewhere?
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> you understood it was a file.

Just as one might reasonably guess that a line of perl code like

$next_line = <DATA_FILE>;

was reading in another line of input from a data file.

> COBOL punctuation makes sense if you know English.

No; I know English, and didn't thoroughly understand your
example.  COBOL punctuation makes sense if you know COBOL.

> >Seriously, let's
> >take a simple example: line-termination chars vs. line-continuation
> >chars.

> COBOL doesn't have line termination of line continuation characters except in
	^^^^^^^^^^^^
> the case where you want to continue a long string. You stick a hyphen at the 
> beginning of the next line.

[snip COBOL e.g.'s]

> >  Since I don't see any of the former in your example, I'm gonna
> >guess that COBOL uses some punctuation to denote that a line is
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> See above. That's a wrong assumption. If I write a long sentance than
		      ^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^
> continues onto another line I can work out that it continues by parsing
> it. Why can't a computer?
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> You're wrong.
	^^^^^^^

So my _guess_ as to the semantics based only on a _limited knowledge_
of COBOL syntax from several lines of code was _wrong_.  Good, that's
pretty much the point I was trying to make! :)

> >To sum up an already long post: I don't think this is a criticism
> >of perl per se (these features are shared by many languages).
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> Simplification makes it much easier to understand other peoples code.

And simplification means different things in different problem
domains.  Do you disagree that a notational convenience ("shorthand")
can be a simplification?  Are your criticisms particular to perl?

> >> I don't want to learn shorthand as 
> >> only people who know shorthand can read it.
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> written it.

[Back to shorthand]
I wasn't calling you a secretary.  I was trying to point out examples
of problem domains in which shorthand could be useful.  One person's
"shorthand" ("line noise" was where I jumped in this thread :))
is another's mission-critical tool.

Darrin
```

<a id="t-aae4eb2dfe"></a>
## Thread: Right Justify Routine — 1 msgs

### Re: Right Justify Routine

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc123@bellsouth.net>
- **Date:** 1998-09-07T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<brVI1.113$g04.191952@news3.mia.bellsouth.net>`
- **References:** `<Jl0PnHJ5PvPd-pn2-XiTbHJiS5z0r@Dwight_Miller.iix.com> <o%VH1.3786$gM3.2803875@news4.mia.bellsouth.net> <trYH1.464$I%.1378959@news1.atlantic.net> <HtbI1.5187$eZ3.3317864@news3.mia.bellsouth.net> <BseI1.481$I%.1861130@news1.atlantic.net> <uCwI1.5340$gM3.3883152@news4.mia.bellsouth.net> <xkRI1.695$I%.2804567@news1.atlantic.net>`

```
Rick Smith wrote in message ...
>
>Judson McClendon wrote in message ...
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>working with an index speed it up or slow it down?


Very observant! :-)  Yes, I originally wrote a jud1-coded and jud2-coded.
Jud2-coded used indexing instead of reference modification in the loops.
It was somewhat faster then jud1-coded, until I changed the working data
fields to COMP-5, when it was about the same speed.  Below is the program.
The times on my PII400 using MF COBOL 3.2 (DOS) were:

  DISP  COMP-5 Work variable (i,j,k) usage
  100k   500k  Nbr of loops

 11.53  57.28  Rick
  6.21  31.20  Judson
  1.97   0.55  hand-coded
  0.88   0.38  jud1
  0.22   0.38  jud2

Please note that the timer resolution in PCs is only to 0.055 seconds, so
you will see some variations on consecutive runs of the same program.
Surprisingly, jud2 is about 9 times faster than hand-coded, and 4 times
faster than jud1, using display variables, but about the same speed using
comp-5 comp-5 variables.  The lines changed between the two above trials
are marked with '->' in the program below.

       Identification Division.
       Program-Id.  Test3.
      * right justify
       Environment Division.
       Configuration Section.
       Source-Computer.  IBM-PC.
       Object-Computer.  IBM-PC.
       Data Division.
       Working-Storage Section.
       01  input-field               value spaces.
           03  input-char  pic x     occurs 30 times
                                     indexed by icx.
       01  output-field    pic x(30) value spaces.
       01  counter         pic s9(4) comp-5  value 1.
       01 char pic x.

Trial 1:

->     01 i pic s9(4).
->     01 j pic s9(4).
->     01 k pic s9(4).
->     01 loop pic s9(9) comp-5 value 100000.

Trial 2:

->     01 i pic s9(4)    comp-5.
->     01 j pic s9(4)    comp-5.
->     01 k pic s9(4)    comp-5.
->     01 loop pic s9(9) comp-5 value 500000.

       Procedure Division.
       test-3-start.
           display function current-date (13:4)
           perform test3-start-Rick loop times
           display function current-date (13:4)
           perform test3-start-Judson loop times
           display function current-date (13:4)
           perform test3-start-hand-coded loop times
           display function current-date (13:4)
           perform test3-start-jud1-coded loop times
           display function current-date (13:4)
           perform test3-start-jud2-coded loop times
           display function current-date (13:4)
           accept char
           stop run
           .

       test3-start-Rick.
           move "abcdefghijklm" to input-field
           move 1 to counter
           if input-field > spaces
               move function reverse (input-field) to input-field
               inspect input-field tallying counter for leading spaces
               move function reverse (input-field (counter:))
                   to input-field (counter:)
           end-if
           .

       test3-start-Judson.
           move "abcdefghijklm" to input-field
           move 1 to counter
           if input-field not = spaces
               move function reverse(input-field) to output-field
               inspect output-field tallying counter for leading spaces
               string input-field delimited by size
                   into output-field with pointer counter
           end-if
           .

       test3-start-hand-coded.
           move "abcdefghijklm" to input-field
           if input-field not = spaces
               move function length (input-field) to i j
               perform until input-field (i:1) not = space
                   subtract 1 from i
               end-perform
               perform until i = 0
                   move input-field (i:1) to input-field (j:1)
                   subtract 1 from i
                   subtract 1 from j
               end-perform
               if j > 0
                   move spaces to input-field (1:j)
               end-if
           end-if
           .

       test3-start-jud1-coded.
           move "abcdefghijklm" to input-field
           if input-field not = spaces
               move function length (input-field) to i j
               perform until input-field (i:1) not = space
                   subtract 1 from i
               end-perform
               subtract i from j
               add 1 j giving k
               move input-field (1:i) to output-field (k:i)
               if j > 0
                   move spaces to output-field (1:j)
               end-if
           end-if
           .

       test3-start-jud2-coded.
           move "abcdefghijklm" to input-field
           if input-field not = spaces
               move function length (input-field) to j
               perform varying icx
                       from j by -1
                       until input-char (icx) not = space
               end-perform
               set i to icx
               subtract i from j
               add 1 j giving k
               move spaces to output-field
               move input-field (1:i) to output-field (k:i)
           end-if
           .
```

<a id="t-dac5de0294"></a>
## Thread: Should I learn Cobol before VB — 2 msgs

_Topics: [`Tutorials, books, learning`](topics.md#learning)_

### Re: Should I learn Cobol before VB

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc123@bellsouth.net>
- **Date:** 1998-09-07T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<68WI1.174$g04.217887@news3.mia.bellsouth.net>`
- **References:** `<5YwI1.5344$gM3.3891836@news4.mia.bellsouth.net> <3298248.80014.18949@kcbbs.gen.nz>`

```
Richard Plinston wrote:
>Judson McClendon writes:
>
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
>the issue.


This is the 'conventional wisdom', or the PC line, but it is simply not
valid.  The cost difference between two mainframes, one with 4 times the
CPU power of the other, is substantial.  Inefficient coding practices can
easily make 5 or 10 times the difference in CPU overhead.  And 'a few
guidelines' is simply not enough.  You cannot drive an automobile well
with only 'a few simple guidelines', and programming a computer requires
far more sophisticated judgement than driving an automobile.

>In any case there are vastly more important issues than
>individual instruction timeings:

Could you please point out where I have mentioned 'individual
instruction timings'?  Please do not create a 'straw man' here.

>A Quicksort in the most inefficient interpreted BASIC will
>usually outperform the best hand optimised assembler
>bubble sort (given a particualr dataset).


True, and a well coded one will still be 10 times faster than a poorly
coded one, perhaps even more.

>With Cobol systems the CPU time, especially on modern machines,
>is negligible compared to disk I-O time.  Most Cobol based
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>file than to bother about whether to use index or subscripts.


It is more important not to kill people than not to abuse them.  Should
we ignore the latter, hust because it is not *the* most important? ;-)

>This is why I would prefer a programmer who knew how the
>_business_ worked, because that is when you find the way
>the data is to be processed and how real savings may be
>made.


And once again, why should you not have both?  You think learning one
would 'push out' the knowledge of the other? :-)

>After all the next machine may have completely different
>optimisations that nullify all you ever knew.  As has
>been pointed out already, running short samples of code
>may get it stuck in the instruction cache and give
>completely the wrong idea about what is efficient.


Not so!  As I have stated several times, it is not about targeting a
specific machine.  It is about understanding general principles.
```

#### ↳ Re: Should I learn Cobol before VB

- **From:** Jeff Farkas <Jeffrey.Farkas@gte.net>
- **Date:** 1998-09-07T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<6t1dcv$bu1$1@news-1.news.gte.net>`
- **References:** `<5YwI1.5344$gM3.3891836@news4.mia.bellsouth.net> <3298248.80014.18949@kcbbs.gen.nz> <68WI1.174$g04.217887@news3.mia.bellsouth.net>`

```
Judson McClendon wrote:
> 
> Richard Plinston wrote:
> >Judson McClendon writes:
> >
<HUGE SNIPAGE>
Richard I have to agree with Judson's point to a greater degree than
your for the following reasons:

    1) Business knowledge is very important to develop a good usable
system. If the system is designed from a poor understanding of the 
users needs no one will use it. Regardless who the system was designed
for.

    2) Technical knowledge is very important as well. One can not
relay on the compiler or any other tool to design good code. The 
programmer could have a wonderful understanding of the business 
but with out a solid technical background the programs may be either
CPU/io hogs or even worse un-maintainable. Just imagine a VERY large 
company that has to process millions of transaction every night. Now
imagine that the "Batch Window" is only a few hours, due to the
need to have real time access. In this case, which can and does 
occur, every little bit matters between having the batch cycle
processed and the real time come up when the workers step in
the door and having people sit around and do nothing while the
program(s) waste valuable recourses.

     That is why it is crucial to have a balance between a solid
knowledge and a solid technical background.

> Not so!  As I have stated several times, it is not about targeting a
> specific machine.  It is about understanding general principles.
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> whoever believes in Him should not perish but have everlasting life."


Jeff Farkas
Jeffrey.Farkas@gte.net
```

> > What does (counter:1) mean?  (I mean specifically the colon.)  And

> Hello Kitty,

…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> then DISPLAY STR(2:2) will show "AL" and DISPLAY (1:) will show "H"

No, no! A little differnt! Display str(1:) will show "HALLO". leaving
the
number of chars out means "all the rest". F.e. display str (3:) is the
same as display str (3:4) and both show "LLO".

> Function is new too, that are the INSTRINSIC-Functions,
> getting arguments and returning something:
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> for the functions supported by your compiler.

All functions were once new, being added to the '85 standard in 1989.
Some
shops still use COBOL '85 without this '89 extension. But functions are
yet more than 8 years long in existance.New is relative.

<a id="t-6b03d3b486"></a>
## Thread: Unysis A COBOL : Info please — 12 msgs

### Re: Unysis A COBOL : Info please

- **From:** "Martin Nabbs" <mnabbs@hotmail.com>
- **Date:** 1998-09-08T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<6t2qkp$89h$1@reef.news.technocom.net>`
- **References:** `<6t0h3r$b2e$1@reader1.reader.news.ozemail.net>`

```
David Mowat wrote in message
<6t0h3r$b2e$1@reader1.reader.news.ozemail.net>...

>Can you give me some info on Unisys A COBOL please.
>Is the COBOL for Unisys A  ANS COBOL 74, or 85?

The COBOL compilers on the Unisys A Series normally have the ANSI version
number in its filename.  The exception being that the ANSI 68 version was
just called COBOL (Filename: SYSTEM/COBOL).

i.e.  COBOL74  (SYSTEM/COBOL74) - ANSI 74, COBOL85 (SYSTEM/COBOL85) - ANSI
85

If you have access to the A Series you can use the command PD <filename>
which will tell you what type of object code it is.

Hope this helps
Martin NABBS
```

#### ↳ Re: Unysis A COBOL : Info please

- **From:** "Charles Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com>
- **Date:** 1998-09-09T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<01bddc2e$402959a0$cef63bc0@mv-stevencc.mv.unisys.com>`
- **References:** `<6t0h3r$b2e$1@reader1.reader.news.ozemail.net> <6t2qkp$89h$1@reef.news.technocom.net>`

```
Martin Nabbs <mnabbs@hotmail.com> wrote in article
<6t2qkp$89h$1@reef.news.technocom.net>... 

> If you have access to the A Series you can use the command PD <filename>
> which will tell you what type of object code it is.
 
This isn't a fail-safe approach, though.  Those people who use WFL to do
all their compiles, or use the "WITH" modifier in their CANDE "C" commands,
might well have saved their files as type COB instead of C74 (or, for that
matter, C85).   

Some commonly-used items that are clues indicating COBOL (68) rather than
COBOL74:  
	$ ANSI74, $B2500, $USASI, $S360, $B5700
	USAGEs: 
		COMP-1, COMP-2, COMP-4, COMP-5, INDEX FILE
	`SELECT ... 
		ASSIGN TO integer-1 [* integer-2] 
		[INTERCHANGE] 
		[DIRECT] 
		[OR] 
		[SINGLE [BY AREA/CYLINDER]] 
		[SAVE]
	CONSTANT SECTION. 
	
COBOL74 and COBOL85 may largely be distinguished one from the other by the
ABSENCE of COBOL85-specific constructs like EVALUATE, in-line PERFORM, and
END-IF.  Moreover, COBOL74's USAGE BINARY is equivalent to A Series
COBOL85;s BINARY EXTENDED; COBOL74's BINARY TRUNCATED is equivalent to
COBOL85's BINARY.   Thus, if you see BINARY (COMP-n is a dead giveaway for
COBOL(68) on A Series) items, and no sign of END-IF, END-PERFORM, EVALUATE,
nested programs and the like, it's probably COBOL74.  

And it's probably COBOL74 anyway. Though we do have a growing customer base
for A Series COBOL74, the vast majority of existing COBOL programs on
Unisys A Series systems are in COBOL74, and the vast majority of A Series
users continue to develop their programs using this compiler.  And there is
a sizeable group of unconverted COBOL (68) programs floating around.  

Note that if indeed the programs are COBOL(68), many users attempting
conversion to another compiler run into a data alignment problem.  It's not
clear whether this was a standards issue or not, but GROUP items in Unisys
A Series COBOL (68) actually take on the USAGE declared for them.  I
suspect the relevant standard was silent on that issue.  This causes
problems for COMP-2 (packed decimal) groups which are permitted in
COBOL(68) to begin on an even-digit boundary.  For example, given the
COBOL(68) declaration 
	01  THE-RECORD. 
		03  FIELD-1 PIC 9(3) COMP-2. 
		03  GROUP-1 COMP-2. 
			05  FIELD-2 PIC 9(3) COMP-2.
			05  FIELD-3 PIC 9(3) COMP-2. 
		03  GROUP-2 COMP-2. 
			05  FIELD-4 PIC 9(3) COMP-2. 
			05  FIELD-5 PIC 9(3) COMP-2. 
		03  FIELD-6 PIC 999 COMP.  
		03  FIELD-7 PIC X.  
the record will occupy a total of 20 digits, or ten characters.  

COBOL74 (and COBOL85) standards require that GROUP items be treated as
ALPHANUMERIC, and thus the equivalent declaration in COBOL74 (COMP in
COBOL74 is equivalent to COMP-2 in COBOL(68)) will cause the compiler to
insert a one-digit filler before GROUP-1 as well as another before FIELD-7
because neither is permitted to begin on a "non-byte" boundary.  The length
of this record will thus be 22 digits, or eleven characters.  Clearly this
will present migration headaches for those attempting to migrate multiple
record declarations under an FD or REDEFINES records in WORKING-STORAGE.  

Mechanisms exist in A Series COBOL(68) to assist with identification and
conversion of such records and the files that contain them in a form
"palatable" to A Series COBOL74 and COBOL85. 
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Unysis A COBOL : Info please

- **From:** sff5ky@aol.com (Sff5ky)
- **Date:** 1998-09-09T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<1998090923301900.TAA23795@ladder01.news.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<01bddc2e$402959a0$cef63bc0@mv-stevencc.mv.unisys.com>`

```
In article <01bddc2e$402959a0$cef63bc0@mv-stevencc.mv.unisys.com>, "Charles
Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com> writes:

>or A Series COBOL74, the vast majority of existing COBOL programs on
>Unisys A Series systems are in COBOL74, and the vast majority of A Series
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>

I am appalled that COBOL68 programs were supported at all on the A-Series.  At
the time that the A was introduced, COBOL68 was supposedly placed on the
'non-supported' list and Unisys was strongly pushing people to move to COBOL74.
 (This was back around '84-87 and COBOL80 had been introduced.)
I don't doubt that COBOL68 code still exists in the V-Series environment, as
this environment was around well prior to COBOL74 and many functions that were
available in COBOL68 went away with COBOL74 and have yet to return with COBOL80
or COBOL85.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Unysis A COBOL : Info please

- **From:** "Charles Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com>
- **Date:** 1998-09-10T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<01bddce9$34f05be0$cef63bc0@mv-stevencc.mv.unisys.com>`
- **References:** `<01bddc2e$402959a0$cef63bc0@mv-stevencc.mv.unisys.com> <1998090923301900.TAA23795@ladder01.news.aol.com>`

```
Technically speaking, A Series COBOL (68) is no longer upported in the
usual sense; trouble reports from "casual" customers have been rejected out
of hand since 1997.  Enough demand for this product was generated, however,
for Unisys to allow individual customers to negotiate for continued support
for a limited period of time to allow them to migrate their programs to
COBOL85, but such users are on a "short list" and they pay (dearly, I
suspect) for that privilege.   

And while the impending demise of COBOL was indeed widely rumored through
much of the latter half of the 1980's, negotiations between the software
development managers and the various marketing arms of the company did not
reach enough of a consensus to allow the formal process to begin until
early 1992; the patch to the compiler that began to notify users of this
impending support posture change was applied to the compiler in mid-1992.  

Probably the major reason Unisys has continued to support the product for
so long is that there was sufficient demand and sufficient pressure from
enough large customers to warrant keeping it going on financial grounds. 
From the customer's standpoint, there are a few features -- features that
some of the current architects of both the system and the compiler feel
were perhaps ill-advised to begin with -- for which the later compilers do
not provide universally-satisfactory solutions.   Most current COBOL(68)
users are, I believe, reluctant to change for that reason.  We've provided
solutions to some of them, over time (the SYNCHRONIZED attribute as a
substitute for direct I/O, for example), but some stumbling-blocks remain. 
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Unysis A COBOL : Info please

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc123@bellsouth.net>
- **Date:** 1998-09-10T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<lBNJ1.35$te.42036@news2.mia.bellsouth.net>`
- **References:** `<01bddc2e$402959a0$cef63bc0@mv-stevencc.mv.unisys.com> <1998090923301900.TAA23795@ladder01.news.aol.com>`

```
Sff5ky wrote:
>
>I am appalled that COBOL68 programs were supported at all on the A-Series.  At
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>or COBOL85.


Perhaps you are not aware that the 'A' was not introduced as a totally new
line, but was just a relabeling of the old B5000/B6000/B7000 series, which
started around 1960 (same instruction set), and has been incrementally
enhanced and expanded over the years.  Just like the latest IBM mainframes
are direct descendents of System 360.  The COBOL 68 compiler existed well
before the A Series was introduced, the A just inherited it.  My guess is
that most currently running A Series programs are still COBOL74, simply
because there has been no compelling reason to convert them to the slower
COBOL85 compiler.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Unysis A COBOL : Info please

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "Charles Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com>
- **Date:** 1998-09-10T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<01bddcfa$cfcd12a0$cef63bc0@mv-stevencc.mv.unisys.com>`
- **References:** `<01bddc2e$402959a0$cef63bc0@mv-stevencc.mv.unisys.com> <1998090923301900.TAA23795@ladder01.news.aol.com> <lBNJ1.35$te.42036@news2.mia.bellsouth.net>`

```
Judson McClendon <judmc123@bellsouth.net> wrote in article
<lBNJ1.35$te.42036@news2.mia.bellsouth.net>...

<snip> 

> Perhaps you are not aware that the 'A' was not introduced as a totally
new
> line, but was just a relabeling of the old B5000/B6000/B7000 series,
which
> started around 1960 (same instruction set), and has been incrementally
> enhanced and expanded over the years.  

True, sort of; the date throws a monkey wrench into the argument.  What was
at various times called the B5000, the B5500 and the B5700 system was
indeed introduced (as the B5000) around 1960, but it was not compatible
with later, larger and faster machines, and should probably be considered
an ancestor of them (and, I believe, of the HP3000 as  well).   

A machine called the B6500 (designed beginning 1966; hit the market say,
late 1960's) was originally in its earliest designs supposed to be
upward-compatible with the B5500 but the designers quickly recognized the
limitations of the B5000 design and opted to build a whole new machine. 
This machine had such significant early hardware reliability problems that,
after the  major design problems were ironed out, it was reintroduced as
the B6700 in the very early 1970's.  

As the hardware was not compatible with B5000 software, all the software
was new, including a COBOL compiler written to adhere to the 1968
standards.  The compiler had some extensions to provide for compatibility
with the (one) COBOL compiler (I presume it was "COBOL(60)" or "DoD COBOL")
on the  B5000/B5500/B5700 systems.  No COBOL(68) compiler was written for,
or available on, the earlier systems, according to those I consulted who
worked on those systems. 

Thus, the B6500, B6700, B6800, B6900, B7700, B7800, B7900 and B5900
comprised a group of systems that became known at one point as the
B5/6/7000 series.  But the machine introduced in 1960 or thereabouts was
not a compatible member of that family, even though it was marketed (as the
B5700) alongside that series as a cheaper alternative well into the 1970's.
  

<snip>
 
> The COBOL 68 compiler existed well
> before the A Series was introduced, the A just inherited it.  

True.   But it also inherited the COBOL74 compiler, written for the B6700. 
Both had been widely distributed before the A Series proper was introduced.
 Optimizations for A Series in COBOL and COBOL74 date from the early
1980's; a copy was made of the symbol file of COBOL(68) in the late 1970's,
and development of an ANSI-74-compliant compiler begun based on that
symbol, in response to DoD requirements for an ANSI-compliant COBOL
compiler.  

COBOL85, on the other hand, was written ab initio without a backward look
at the predecessor B5900/B6500/B6700/B6800/B6900/B7700/B7800/B7900 systems,
and without incorporating any part of the symbol files that make up
COBOL(68) or COBOL74.  It's a whole new compiler.  

> My guess is
> that most currently running A Series programs are still COBOL74, simply
> because there has been no compelling reason to convert them to the slower
> COBOL85 compiler.

A Series COBOL85 compilation is very often slower than COBOL74, but
execution-time performance is more often faster than not (depending, of
course, on the construct being measured).  This was not always true, but
over the years COBOL85 has undergone significant improvements in this area.
 There are some areas that are extremely difficult in COBOL74,  and the
developers of COBOL85 have done a really good job of avoiding a number of
those pitfalls.  There are also some architectural limits to the COBOL74
compiler that the COBOL85 compiler does not have (maximum record sizes, for
example).  

While Unisys fully supports the A Series COBOL74 compiler, they also
provide a migration tool for free to assist our customers in migrating to
COBOL85 should they desire to do so.  This tool also assists in COBOL(68)
migration.   And the migration tools from V Series COBOL (of any flavor) to
A Series all target COBOL85 on the destination system.  Don't sell A Series
COBOL85 short!  Unisys has some large A Series customers who have migrated
or are migrating from COBOL74 to COBOL85 and have been pleased with the
execution-time performance.    
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Unysis A COBOL : Info please

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "Charles Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com>
- **Date:** 1998-09-10T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<01bddcfc$5e4213e0$cef63bc0@mv-stevencc.mv.unisys.com>`
- **References:** `<01bddc2e$402959a0$cef63bc0@mv-stevencc.mv.unisys.com> <1998090923301900.TAA23795@ladder01.news.aol.com> <lBNJ1.35$te.42036@news2.mia.bellsouth.net>`

```


Judson McClendon <judmc123@bellsouth.net> wrote in article
<lBNJ1.35$te.42036@news2.mia.bellsouth.net>...

<snip> 

> Perhaps you are not aware that the 'A' was not introduced as a totally
new
> line, but was just a relabeling of the old B5000/B6000/B7000 series,
which
> started around 1960 (same instruction set), and has been incrementally
> enhanced and expanded over the years.  

Actually, that's NOT quite true.  The A9 and A10 were wholly new machines,
though upward compatible with their predecessors.  Though the A3 I believe
shared some architectural features with the  B5900 that preceded it at the
low end of the price scale, and the A15 shared some characterstics with the
B7900 at the high end, none of the A Series lineup is appropriately
characterized as a "relabeling" of  any of their predecessors.  Unisys has
done that in the past (the B5500 was a slightly-modified B5000, the B5700 a
rebadged B5500; the B6700 was a reliability-enhanced and rebadged B6500,
and so on; there are more recent examples) on some individual machines, but
the A Series represented a new architecture upward compatible with the old,
and sporting new instructions in its repertoire.  The A Series was an
evolution from, not a rebadge of, its predecessors.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Unysis A COBOL : Info please

- **From:** "Charles Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com>
- **Date:** 1998-09-11T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<01bdddab$771a4b80$cef63bc0@mv-stevencc.mv.unisys.com>`
- **References:** `<01bddc2e$402959a0$cef63bc0@mv-stevencc.mv.unisys.com> <1998090923301900.TAA23795@ladder01.news.aol.com>`

```
Sff5ky <sff5ky@aol.com> wrote in article
<1998090923301900.TAA23795@ladder01.news.aol.com>...
> In article <01bddc2e$402959a0$cef63bc0@mv-stevencc.mv.unisys.com>, 

> I am appalled that COBOL68 programs were supported at all on the
A-Series.  At
> the time that the A was introduced, COBOL68 was supposedly placed on the
> 'non-supported' list and Unisys was strongly pushing people to move to
COBOL74.
>  (This was back around '84-87 and COBOL80 had been introduced.)
 
As I mentioned in another post, COBOL(68) [hereinafter C68, and COBOL74
will be C74] was placed on the "no further significant enhancements" list
around the time the A Series system was introduced, but it was not placed
on the "non-supported" list until last year, after several years of
warnings (both printed and generated by the system software).   

And interestingly enough, *very early* in C74's career (we're talking
around 1980 or before), the fundamental premise was that C68 was the Large
Systems (as it was known at the time)  "flagship" COBOL compiler; C74 was
available initially for the *primary* purpose of passing ANSI validation
tests to allow (then) Burroughs to sell systems to the federal government. 


By the time frame you cite, starting around 1984, we were indeed beginning
to encourage our C68 users to migrate to C74, and tools were made available
to accomplish that migration.  However, there were certain features
available in C68 that we did not wish to perpetuate into C74, for a variety
of reasons, and some of our users were unwilling to migrate until or unless
analogous features were made available in later compilers.  

Also, the changes necessary to make Large Systems C68 compatible with A
Series were fundamentally the same as those that were required to make A
Series C74 compatible, as the symbol files for these compilers had not
diverged significantly, so providing an A-series-compatible C68 compiler
was not all that onerous a task.  

As to being appalled that C68 was supported on A Series:  At the time the A
Series was introduced, there was CONSIDERABLE demand for this compiler.  We
could not get away with refusing to support it while simultaneously
guaranteeing upward compatibility of all existing object code from prior
systems.  

> I don't doubt that COBOL68 code still exists in the V-Series environment,
as
> this environment was around well prior to COBOL74 and many functions that
were
> available in COBOL68 went away with COBOL74 and have yet to return with
COBOL80
> or COBOL85.

The codefile support policy on V series is quite different from that on A
Series.   On V Series systems, every V Series (or any predecessor, as far
back as the B2500) code file is, as I understand it, guaranteed to be
capable of execution on any V Series system.  There is no mechanism on V
Series to inform users programmatically that a feature is going away and
that their programs must be changed at the source level, precisely because
existing features don't go away on that system.  A Series C68 had access to
features on the A Series system that still exist.  What we did not do was
provide access to those features in C74 (or C85 for that matter) precisely
because we had found them to be too dangerous in the hands of our users, or
too trouble-prone in the system software.  And insofar as possible we
endeavored to provide safer alternatives to those features.

I am at rather a loss to identify the compiler you are referring to as
"COBOL80" above, at least as it pertains to the Unisys A Series (or, for
that matter, any of the other "ex-Burroughs" system architectures).  A
Series has only had three basic COBOL compilers:  SYSTEM/COBOL,
SYSTEM/COBOL74 and SYSTEM/COBOL85.   And I am unaware of any standard for
COBOL being promulgated between ANSI X3.23-1974 and ANSI X3.23-1985.  What
compiler (or what system) are you referring to here?  
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Unysis A COBOL : Info please

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** sff5ky@aol.com (Sff5ky)
- **Date:** 1998-09-11T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<1998091123520200.TAA17324@ladder01.news.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<01bdddab$771a4b80$cef63bc0@mv-stevencc.mv.unisys.com>`

```
In article <01bdddab$771a4b80$cef63bc0@mv-stevencc.mv.unisys.com>, "Charles
Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com> writes:

>
>> I am appalled that COBOL68 programs were supported at all on the
…[17 more quoted lines elided]…
>

   Okay, 'appalled' may have been too strong a statement.  I was in the Small
systems (B1900-series) migration testing.  My shop had both Small and Medium 
systems onsite for development of financial software.  We initially got the
push to move from C68 to C74 as a facilitator towards expanding our range of
platforms supportted.  As far as I know, we did not have the option of testing
our C68 code on the A-Series.   We went thru a significant amount of
development to manually migrate our code from C68 to C74 prior to attempting
any performance and data integrity benchmarks.  The automated facilities
provided made only minor changes and the resultant code needed significant
follow-up analysis.  Unisys/Burroughs offered to handle the migration for us at
a fee, but required freezing our code for 6-18 months.


>
>I am at rather a loss to identify the compiler you are referring to as
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>-- 

I may have mis-stated the COBOL80.  I am reasonably certain though that there
was a publication and training provided for changes from C74 to C80.  I do not
recall relational modifiers or in-line performs as items being available in
C80.
It did indicate scope terminators for IF/END-IF and EVALUATE as new features.
It seems that the Intrinsic Functions for arithmetic operations, date
conversion, case conversion were missing as well.


I didn't mean to slam the A-Seires product. In fact, I wish that there were
more jobs available in my area on the Unisys platform in general.  I had to
switch to the IBM environment simply because of the number of opportunities out
there.  I have had approximately 15+ years experience in the Unisys/Burroughs
world from the B-500 thru V-580 and A4.  Even had an opportunity to work with
the COBOL Compiler developers when the single-pass C74 compiler was getting its
shake-down.   Corresponded a bit with a Mory Dent(?sp) on problem submissions.


Glad to get a reply from a reporsentative of Unisys.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Unysis A COBOL : Info please

- **From:** "Charles Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com>
- **Date:** 1998-09-18T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<01bde354$8f91d8a0$cef63bc0@mv-stevencc.mv.unisys.com>`
- **References:** `<01bddc2e$402959a0$cef63bc0@mv-stevencc.mv.unisys.com> <1998090923301900.TAA23795@ladder01.news.aol.com>`

```
Two updates for previous responses to this comment: 

<<(This was back around '84-87 and COBOL80 had been introduced.)>>

I finally ran across somebody who thought they might know what this was. 
We had a "COBOL8X" compiler on our BTOS/CTOS machines that had some of the
features included in earlier drafts of what became the COBOL85 standard. 
Best guess is that you had some B36 systems (or their predecessors, or
their CT equivalents) floating around the shop or among your users.   

And a further comment on migration from B1000 or V Series COBOL(68) to A
Series COBOL(68):  The first two products were very similar to one another,
and both were VERY different from the third.   

The V Series (and the Medium Systems that preceded it) were
"string-oriented" machines specifically designed for the express purpose of
running COBOL applications.   COMP was packed decimal, and the systems
handed decimal operations on such fields of virtually arbitrary length.  

The B1000, being a "soft" machine with dynamic language interpreters, to
large degree "emulated" the Medium System in its COBOL mode.  

The A Series, by contrast, was originally designed to run ALGOL (and
similar word-oriented, block-structured languages like its philosophical
descendant Pascal).  Thus, A Series COBOL was built around word-oriented
arithmetic, without a second glance at compatibility (with smaller
Burroughs machines, or with other manufacturers' machines either, for that
matter), and the defaults (like the meaning of COMP) were "skewed"  toward
that word orientation.  

Medium Systems COBOL (68) users could migrate their application systems
without all that much difficulty to B1000.  Going the other way presented
some problems because of the file naming convention differences between the
two machines, but was otherwise just as straightforward in most cases.

But migrating COBOL(68) programs/application systems from either B1000 or
B2/3/4000 systems to A Series presented significant challenges.   

These very architectural differences were the primary reason that when
COBOL74 loomed on the horizon, corporate management mandated a *single*
approach to salient characteristics of that language on *all* systems, to
ensure compatibility, not just of the programs, but of the files
produced/accessed by them.   To large degree, that architecture was more
closely allied with the existing B1/2/3/4000 systems approach than it was
the B5/6/7/A Series approach.  

Further, that common approach is why the conversion aids focused on COBOL74
(the latest and greatest at the time) as a target as well; migration to
COBOL74 increased the chances that the resulting source file would be
compatible with minimal changes across the entire Burroughs product line. 
  

And a further comment about the B1000 COBOL74B compiler discussed in some
of these threads:  COBOL74B was developed primarly to provide support for
TADS (Test And Debug System).  COBOL74B was out for only a short time
before new  development on B1000 system software products was discontinued
and did not enjoy widespread use..  

Sff5ky <sff5ky@aol.com> wrote in article
<1998090923301900.TAA23795@ladder01.news.aol.com>...
> In article <01bddc2e$402959a0$cef63bc0@mv-stevencc.mv.unisys.com>,
"Charles
> Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com> writes:
>

> >Unisys A Series systems are in COBOL74, and the vast majority of A
Series
> >users continue to develop their programs using this compiler.  And there
is
> >a sizeable group of unconverted COBOL (68) programs floating around.  
> >
> >
> 
> I am appalled that COBOL68 programs were supported at all on the
A-Series.  At
> the time that the A was introduced, COBOL68 was supposedly placed on the
> 'non-supported' list and Unisys was strongly pushing people to move to
COBOL74.
>  (This was back around '84-87 and COBOL80 had been introduced.)
> I don't doubt that COBOL68 code still exists in the V-Series environment,
as

were
> available in COBOL68 went away with COBOL74 and have yet to return with
COBOL80
> or COBOL85.
>

## Thread: EXEC=..,PARM=.. It works but I don't understand why — 19 msgs

### EXEC=..,PARM=.. It works but I don't understand why

- **From:** "Simon Ward" <tubby@toasts.force9.co.uk>
- **Date:** 1998-09-08T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<6t3lg3$jau@news3.force9.net>`

```
    I would be gratefull if someone could explain the the difference between
the 2 situations below. I am passing information using the PARM part of the
EXEC statement in JCL to pass information from the JCL into a COBOL
subroutine.

    If I pass a string, the first 4 bytes of the passed data contains the
length of the string in oacked decimal.

    If I pass a hex value (eg comp-3) the first 4 bytes also contain the
length but the data starts at the 6 byte! not the 5th! I don't understand
why this is so. Is the 5th byte used to store something?
```

#### ↳ Re: EXEC=..,PARM=.. It works but I don't understand why

- **From:** "Jeff" <a@a.com>
- **Date:** 1998-09-12T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<R1zK1.122$4E6.1569153@storm.twcol.com>`
- **References:** `<6t3lg3$jau@news3.force9.net>`

```
>length but the data starts at the 6 byte! not the 5th! I don't understand
>why this is so. Is the 5th byte used to store something?


One possible reason is that COBOL cannot use numbers of length 1. I do mean
numbers, not pic X. I assume the first byte you are looking at is '00'x
which simply means the hex value you passed was converted from 1 byte to 2
bytes.

It would be nice to see the exact hex value which is being passed to your
COBOL program.
```

- **From:** "Donald Tees" <donald@willmack.com>
- **Date:** 1998-09-12T00:00:00

- **Message-ID:** `<6tetl9$8c8$1@news.igs.net>`
- **References:** `<6t3lg3$jau@news3.force9.net> <R1zK1.122$4E6.1569153@storm.twcol.com>`

```
Huh?  what about PICTURE 9?

>One possible reason is that COBOL cannot use numbers of length 1. I do mean
>numbers, not pic X. I assume the first byte you are looking at is '00'x
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…

```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: EXEC=..,PARM=.. It works but I don't understand why

- **From:** "Jeff" <a@a.com>
- **Date:** 1998-09-12T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<nvFK1.142$4E6.1829838@storm.twcol.com>`
- **References:** `<6t3lg3$jau@news3.force9.net> <R1zK1.122$4E6.1569153@storm.twcol.com> <6tetl9$8c8$1@news.igs.net>`

```
>Huh?  what about PICTURE 9?


Look at a compile listing. You can make the variable any length you want.
COBOL has a minimum requirement of 2 bytes. You have to do some finagelling
to get at a one byte number when passed to a COBOL program. With maybe the
exception of a PIC X field. All numerics are a minimum of 2 bytes in COBOL.
The PIC 9 you refer to only affects the run of the program in so far as the
range of the number is checked. As in compiler directive SSRANGE and
NOSSRANGE. The same kind of thing happens with a PIC 9999 USAGE
PACKED-DECIMAL. Where the actual storage area of the variable is 3 bytes,
normally defining a 5 digit packed-decimal number.

I know it looks incorrect, but look at the cross reference information in a
COBOL listing with a PIC 9 field. There will be 2 bytes assigned to that
number. The cross reference information I am referring to is to the right of
the code. On a normal 80 column screen you have to scroll to the right to
see this information in the WORKING-STORAGE section.

In the end this may have been fixed with the newer versions of COBOL.
```

```

Jeff wrote in message ...
>>Huh?  what about PICTURE 9?
>
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>exception of a PIC X field. All numerics are a minimum of 2 bytes in COBOL.


Nonsense.  You probably have your compiler set to use Japanese
characters (16 bit) or something.  I can assure you that Cobol is quite
capable of byte level manipulation. What computer/compiler are you
using?
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: EXEC=..,PARM=.. It works but I don't understand why

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1998-09-12T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<6tfel0$hvp@dfw-ixnews4.ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<6t3lg3$jau@news3.force9.net> <R1zK1.122$4E6.1569153@storm.twcol.com> <6tetl9$8c8$1@news.igs.net> <nvFK1.142$4E6.1829838@storm.twcol.com>`

```
There is not now, nor has there ever been ANY IBM mainframe COBOL compiler
that allocated 2 bytes for a
    PIC 9  (USAGE DISPLAY - implied or stated)
field.  The only time you MIGHT have gotten two bytes for this - is if the
NEXT field required a "slack" byte because it needed to be on a half-word
(or whole- or double- word) boundary.  Please "snip" and show us YOUR XREF
if you compile a data division with.

    01  GROUP-ITEM.
           05   ONE-NUM   PIC 9.
           05   ANOTHER-NUM   PIC 9.
           05   MORE-STUFF  PIC X(10).

(All caps used so it will compile with the OLDEST IBM mainframe compilers.)
If you show an XREF with more than one byte for each numeric field, I will
be very VERY surprised.

(The rules for COMP, COMP-4, BINARY, fields are different - but even a
     PIC S9 COMP-3
item will fit in one byte.)


Jeff wrote in message ...
>>Huh?  what about PICTURE 9?
>
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
>number. The cross reference information I am referring to is to the right
of
>the code. On a normal 80 column screen you have to scroll to the right to
>see this information in the WORKING-STORAGE section.
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** riplin@kcbbs.gen.nz (Richard Plinston)

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol

>

…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> exception of a PIC X field. All numerics are a minimum of 2 bytes in COBOL.

specific implementation on the machine you are using.  Different
implementations may have different criteria in this area.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: EXEC=..,PARM=.. It works but I don't understand why

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1998-09-12T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<6tehjd$5l1@dfw-ixnews5.ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<6t3lg3$jau@news3.force9.net> <R1zK1.122$4E6.1569153@storm.twcol.com>`

```
Please see the Application Programming Guide for information on how to get a
"PARM" value into the Linkage Section of an IBM (MVS - OS/390) program.  The
brief summary is that it does NOT matter what type of data is passed
(numeric or alphanumeric) but that your linkage section should look like the
following:


         LINKAGE SECTION.
        01  PARMDATA.
             05  STRINGLEN             PIC 999  USAGE COMP.
             05  STRINGPARM          PIC X(6).

(This example is taken directly from the VS COBOL II application programming
guide - but the same technique can be used with any MVS-type COBOL)

Note: You can also use USAGE BINARY instead of COMP.  It would also be
possible to change the PIC 999 to PIC S9(04) (and I think most people do
this) HOWEVER, there is an MVS restriction of only 255 (I think) characters
in a PARM, so you will never need more than 3 "valid" digits.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: EXEC=..,PARM=.. It works but I don't understand why

- **From:** "Jeff" <a@a.com>
- **Date:** 1998-09-12T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<KIFK1.144$4E6.1838670@storm.twcol.com>`
- **References:** `<6t3lg3$jau@news3.force9.net> <R1zK1.122$4E6.1569153@storm.twcol.com> <6tehjd$5l1@dfw-ixnews5.ix.netcom.com>`

```
>brief summary is that it does NOT matter what type of data is passed
>(numeric or alphanumeric) but that your linkage section should look like
the
>following:
>         LINKAGE SECTION.
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>in a PARM, so you will never need more than 3 "valid" digits.



Not sure exactly what you mean here, but my comment didn't refer to the
STRINGLEN size or value. I was referring to the size of the actual field
being passed. And to that I say the smallest numeric variable available to
COBOL is 2 bytes in length. It may be PIC 9, but COBOL uses 2 bytes for the
storage of said number. Thus if you passed a HEX value 1 byte in length to
COBOL from a PARM field in JCL, I assumed the passed value would be
converted to a 2 byte numeric field for COBOL to use.

As to the STRINGLEN field length all binary fields have a storage area with
an even number of bytes, regardless of how they are defined. Such as 2,4, or
8. Look at the compile listing and check the byte size of the field usually
listed off to the right of the field in the listing, beyond column 80
somewhere. I have never seen a parm defined as PIC 999. I have always seen
it defined as PIC S9(04) USAGE COMP [or more acurately USAGE BINARY]. Never
sure why, but I assumed it was because binary fields are defined on an even
word boundary in COBOL.

This all may have been fixed with the newer versions of COBOL.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: EXEC=..,PARM=.. It works but I don't understand why

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1998-09-12T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<6tfel1$hvp@dfw-ixnews4.ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<6t3lg3$jau@news3.force9.net> <R1zK1.122$4E6.1569153@storm.twcol.com> <6tehjd$5l1@dfw-ixnews5.ix.netcom.com> <KIFK1.144$4E6.1838670@storm.twcol.com>`

```

Jeff wrote in message ...
>>brief summary is that it does NOT matter what type of data is passed
>>(numeric or alphanumeric) but that your linkage section should look like
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
>>this) HOWEVER, there is an MVS restriction of only 255 (I think)
characters
>>in a PARM, so you will never need more than 3 "valid" digits.
>
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>being passed.

The technique listed above WILL get you the exact length of the data passed
in the PARM= statement of your JCL - honest!  It will ALWAYS come in as a
two-byte binary field which can be defined - in COBOL - as

      Pic 9 Comp.
      Pic S9 Comp.
      pic  99 Comp.  Pic 999 COMP, Pic 9999 Comp
      Pic S99 Comp. Pic S99 Comp.  Pic S9999 Comp.
        or any of the above with "BINARY" instead of COMP.

That is the way it will come into your linkage section and that is what you
should use.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: EXEC=..,PARM=.. It works but I don't understand why

- **From:** sff5ky@aol.com (Sff5ky)
- **Date:** 1998-09-12T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<1998091220075900.QAA04772@ladder03.news.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<R1zK1.122$4E6.1569153@storm.twcol.com>`

```
In article <R1zK1.122$4E6.1569153@storm.twcol.com>, "Jeff" <a@a.com> writes:

>
>One possible reason is that COBOL cannot use numbers of length 1. I do mean
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Making a major assumption here.  In passing a hex value that you have
identified as representing only 1-byte, the system has expanded this to fit
within the smallest possible binary/hex field of 2-bytes.  Most binary fields
are 2,4,or 8-bytes in size.
```

#### ↳ Re: EXEC=..,PARM=.. It works but I don't understand why

- **From:** "COBOL Frog (Huib Klink)" <H.Klink@IMN.nl>
- **Date:** 1998-09-14T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<35FD90B3.B2FDFF99@IMN.nl>`
- **References:** `<6t3lg3$jau@news3.force9.net>`

```


Simon,

When passing data from JCL to COBOL, the following convention is used:

1) Only ONE parameter is passed to the COBOL program
2) That ONE parameter consists of two portions: length and content.
3) The length will be binary encoded in TWO bytes
4) 255 is the maximum amount of data you'll get

So code the following in your
LINKAGE SECTION.
01  ParmFromJCL.
    03  LengthOfParm  pic S9999 usage binary.
    03  Contents  pic X(255).

Of course 255 may be lessened.
Defining Contents as a group is also allowed.

No manual at hand, one thing I do not recall: is the length INCLUDING itself or
EXCLUDING?
So if JCL sais EXEC PGM=YourProg,PARM='ABACADABRA'
I don't know if length is 10 or 12.

The Frog
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: EXEC=..,PARM=.. It works but I don't understand why

- **From:** vbandke@ibm.net (Volker Bandke)
- **Date:** 1998-09-15T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<35fe14dc.951017@news2.ibm.net>`
- **References:** `<6t3lg3$jau@news3.force9.net> <35FD90B3.B2FDFF99@IMN.nl>`

```
On Mon, 14 Sep 1998 23:54:59 +0200, "COBOL Frog (Huib Klink)" <H.Klink@IMN.nl> wrote:

>When passing data from JCL to COBOL, the following convention is used:
>
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>4) 255 is the maximum amount of data you'll get
Not quite.  According to my JCL Reference, the maximum length of the parameter is 100, not
255

>
>So code the following in your
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>    03  Contents  pic X(255).

>No manual at hand, one thing I do not recall: is the length INCLUDING itself or
>EXCLUDING?
>So if JCL sais EXEC PGM=YourProg,PARM='ABACADABRA'
>I don't know if length is 10 or 12.

The length is the actual length of the parameter passed, excluding the enclosing
apostropies or brackets

HTH

with kind regards

Volker Bandke
(BSP GmbH)
```

Huib,

REFERENCE MODIFICATION to extract the parameter in a single MVC. MOVE CONTENTS
(1:LENGTHOFPARM) to WS-PARAMETER.

```

WOB@my-dejanews.com wrote in message <6tkgm4$fem$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>...
>In article <35FD90B3.B2FDFF99@IMN.nl>,
>  "COBOL Frog (Huib Klink)" <H.Klink@IMN.nl> wrote:
  <snips>
>>
>> So code the following in your
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>>

>
>Huib,
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>REFERENCE MODIFICATION to extract the parameter in a single MVC. MOVE
CONTENTS
>(1:LENGTHOFPARM) to WS-PARAMETER.
>
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>
I don't know WHY (maybe I am one of the "old-thinkers" who still numbers
paragraphs), but I would prefer the following to using reference
modification.

   LINKAGE SECTION.
   01  ParmFromJCL.
       03  LengthOfParm  pic S9(03) usage binary.
       03  Contents.
              05  All-Chars occurs 0 to 255 times
                        depending on LengthOFParm.
                    10  Each-Char    Pic X.

P.S. I haven't checked the manual either, but I am 90% certain that the
value of the length field does NOT count the half-word length field itself.
(and as a reminder the data that comes into "Contents" is everything on the
"correct" side of a "/" - things on the "wrong" side are passed to the
IBM/COBOL run-time system itself, e.g. UPSI, SSRANGE, etc)

P.P.S.  Anyone using this technique with LE, Please look up (and understand
the following)
     CBLOPTS - run-time option
     CEE3PRM - callable routine
```

items" came into the Standard.  If you want a detailed explanation of the
problems that caused, let me know.

Bill, good day!

You wrote:

> I don't have my '85 Standard with me, but I *know* that it allows 0 (as a
> change from '74).

I based myself on my (sometimes correct) own memory and on the (mostly correct)
Micro Focus Docs/help, which state for the format
   OCCURS [ integer-1 TO ] integer-2 TIMES DEPENDING ON data-name-1
the following:
   Where both integer-1 and integer-2 are used, integer-1 must be greater than
zero and integer-2 must be greater than integer-1.

Are you sure zero is allowed? Perhaps in the next standard?

>  This is how the entire problem with "zero-length group
> items" came into the Standard.  If you want a detailed explanation of the
> problems that caused, let me know.

I'm interested! If you don't need to type the story but have it at hand, please
email me!

CU
Huib

to make up a total of 15  characters.

#### ↳ Re: New to COBOL

- **From:** fujitsu_cobol@my-dejanews.com
- **Date:** 1998-09-10T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<6t9fv6$32e$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>`
- **References:** `<35f56079.8843178@n3.idirect.com>`

```
Fujitsu COBOL fully conforms to the ANSI and X/Open COBOL standards with
regards to ACCEPT statements.

Fujitsu COBOL supports the following five types of Screen Accepts:
1.  ACCEPT data-item
2.  ACCEPT data-item FROM CONSOLE
3.  ACCEPT data-item AT LINE number COLUMN number
4.  ACCEPT screen-section

If you do not want to complete fill a field with blanks, then you will want to
use ACCEPT data-item FROM CONSOLE

Fujitsu Software Corporation
Developer Tools Group
Phone: (408) 428-0500
FAX:   (408) 428-0600
Email: cobol@adtools.com
Web:   http://www.adtools.com

To Fujitsu:
   May I suggest that you run this by Artur Reimann - the Fujitsu
representative to J4?

If your product behaves differently for
    ACCEPT data-name
        versus
    ACCEPT data-name FROM CONSOLE

*and*

you document "from console" as your default input device, then YOU HAVE A
BUG ....
   This most certainly is NOT ANSI conforming.
      (and the X/Open definition for this type of ACCEPT is identical to the
ANSI one)

##### ↳ ↳ Re: New to COBOL

- **From:** riplin@kcbbs.gen.nz (Richard Plinston)
- **Date:** 1998-09-11T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3298253.70008.23121@kcbbs.gen.nz>`
- **References:** `<6t9fv6$32e$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>`

```
In message <<6t9fv6$32e$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>> fujitsu_cobol@my-dejanews.com writes:
> regards to ACCEPT statements.
> 
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> use ACCEPT data-item FROM CONSOLE

Items 3 and 4 are implicitly FROM CRT.  In MF the CONSOLE and the
CRT are the same screen, so these can be mixed, though it is
not recommended.  In Fujitsu (AFAIK) CONSOLE and CRT are
different windows, so cannot be mixed.

The real difference between ACCEPT FROM CONSOLE and FROM CRT
is that in the former it is accepting keystrokes while the
latter accepts the content of fields.

The 'data-item' can be preset to have any value and then
keystrokes will change the value character by character
and when the accept is completed it will cntain the 
data as changed (ie the combination of initial value and
keystrokes determined by the rules given to each key).

It is best to have the field displayed before the accept
or by it, so that the user can see the current field
content.
```

## Thread: Panvalet — 8 msgs

### Panvalet

- **From:** redsky@ibm.net (Thane Hubbell)
- **Date:** 1998-09-08T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<Jl0PnHJ5PvPd-pn2-rJJwtDTd4vZW@Dwight_Miller.iix.com>`

```

Yeah, I've heard it before - but seriously - NO MANUALS to be found.  
I'm working on a remote site that use Panvalet, and I need to export 
an ENTIRE Panvalet library to a PDS - anyone know the util? Pan#1? or 
the control statements?

Thanks.
```

#### ↳ Re: Panvalet

- **From:** "Jeff" <a@a.com>
- **Date:** 1998-09-08T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<zOeJ1.329$Lk3.2665348@storm.twcol.com>`
- **References:** `<Jl0PnHJ5PvPd-pn2-rJJwtDTd4vZW@Dwight_Miller.iix.com>`

```
Here is one:

<<JOBCARD HERE>>

//STEP01 EXEC  PGM=PAN#1
//PANDD1   DD  DSN=<<source panvalet library>>,DISP=SHR
//PANDD2   DD  SYSOUT=*
//SYSPRINT DD  SYSOUT=*
//SYSPUNCH DD  SYSOUT=*
//* WRITE TO WORK WITH NO OTHER CONTROL STATEMENTS EXPANDS INCLUDES
//* WRITES TO DDNAME PANDD2
//SYSIN    DD  *
++WRITE WORK,<<panvalet member name>>
//

where <<JOBCARD HERE>> is your jobcard.
where <<source panvalet library>> is the panvalet library you need to copy
from
where <<panvalet member name>> is the panvalet member name you need to copy
Set PANDD2 to the fully qualified PDS and member name you wish to write to.
I may have others, I will post them when I find them. If you know REXX I can
give you some nice examples which can be run from batch or foreground.


Thane Hubbell wrote in message ...
>
>Yeah, I've heard it before - but seriously - NO MANUALS to be found.
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Panvalet

- **From:** tmj <spam@spam.com>
- **Date:** 1998-09-10T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<35F858ED.63E9@spam.com>`
- **References:** `<Jl0PnHJ5PvPd-pn2-rJJwtDTd4vZW@Dwight_Miller.iix.com> <zOeJ1.329$Lk3.2665348@storm.twcol.com> <Jl0PnHJ5PvPd-pn2-Xg8GZxwWEUyd@Dwight_Miller.iix.com>`

```
If you have version 14.3 of PANVALET you can use wildcards (*) as
follows:

//PAN    EXEC  PGM=PAN#1                              
//STEPLIB  DD  DSN=yadayada.PANVALET.LINKLIB,DISP=SHR 
//PANDD1   DD  DSN=yadayada.PANVALET.TESTLIB,DISP=SHR 
//PANDD4   DD  DSN=yadayada.PANVALET.PRODLIB,DISP=SHR  
//PANDD2   DD DUMMY,DCB=BLKSIZE=3600                  
//SYSPRINT DD SYSOUT=*                                
//SYSPUNCH DD DSN=yadayada.THANE.COBOL,  
//           etc
//SYSIN    DD *                                                          
++WRITE PUNCH,PREFIX=THANE*                                            N 
//*                                     PUT "N" HERE------------------>* 

The "N" says don't expand ++INCLUDE, put an "E" there if you want them.

It put all the THANE programs into the one file.  I haven't figured out
how to put each PANVALET member into a seperate PDS member yet.  I think
that's what you wanted to do originally.


Thane Hubbell wrote:
> 
> On Tue, 8 Sep 1998 18:34:12, "Jeff" <a@a.com> wrote:
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
> Have something like that?
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Panvalet

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "Jeff" <a@a.com>
- **Date:** 1998-09-10T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<%b%J1.32$4E6.304947@storm.twcol.com>`
- **References:** `<Jl0PnHJ5PvPd-pn2-rJJwtDTd4vZW@Dwight_Miller.iix.com> <zOeJ1.329$Lk3.2665348@storm.twcol.com> <Jl0PnHJ5PvPd-pn2-Xg8GZxwWEUyd@Dwight_Miller.iix.com> <35F858ED.63E9@spam.com>`

```
good answer. wish I'd figured that one out a few months ago when I needed
it. writing to PUNCH I believe expands the ++INCLUDE. Writing to WORK does
not expand the ++INCLUDE statements. There are several ways to do it, but it
looks like Thane figured it out.

>++WRITE PUNCH,PREFIX=THANE*                                            N


Is there a way to get a list of the members?
```

## Thread: COBOL 9X — 6 msgs

### COBOL 9X

- **From:** skyraven@home.com (Sue Leonhard)
- **Date:** 1998-09-08T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<35f5a756.1671099@news>`

```
Has anyone heard anything about this? Has there been approval for it
to be released yet or is it still in the works? Just heard about it
the first day of COBOL I class so I'm kind of curious to hear everyone
else's thoughts on it....
```

#### ↳ Re: COBOL 9X

- **From:** "Leif Svalgaard" <leif@ibm.net>
- **Date:** 1998-09-08T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<35f5adbe.0@news1.ibm.net>`
- **References:** `<35f5a756.1671099@news>`

```
looks like it's going to be COBOL 00 or 01
:-(

Sue Leonhard wrote in message <35f5a756.1671099@news>...
>Has anyone heard anything about this? Has there been approval for it
>to be released yet or is it still in the works? Just heard about it
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

#### ↳ Re: COBOL 9X

- **From:** "Chris Osborne" <chris_n_osborne@yahoo.com>
- **Date:** 1998-09-08T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<35f5d146.0@news3.uswest.net>`
- **References:** `<35f5a756.1671099@news>`

```
Sue Leonhard wrote in message <35f5a756.1671099@news>...
>Has anyone heard anything about this? Has there been approval for it
>to be released yet or is it still in the works? Just heard about it
>the first day of COBOL I class so I'm kind of curious to hear everyone
>else's thoughts on it....
>

Sue,

    COBOL 9x is the working name for the next COBOL Standard.  If you go to
the various associated sites, you will find that the new standard won't be
formally published until sometime in the middle of 2000 (if all goes well),
So the name of the standard may change (each has been based on the year of
publication so far, but COBOL 00 would look too much like COBOL OO (zeros
versus ohs).  Maybe the standards committee will get with the Y2K swing and
call the new standard COBOL 2000, instead of that obsolete two digit date
format.

    You can, however, download the current draft of the standard (runs to
more than 400+ pages in PDF, so be prepared to wait).
http://www.microfocus.com/Standards/  You can download the draft from this
site, if you really want to.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL 9X

- **From:** skyraven@home.com (Sue Leonhard)
- **Date:** 1998-09-09T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<35f5eafe.18992830@news>`
- **References:** `<35f5a756.1671099@news> <35f5d146.0@news3.uswest.net>`

```

>more than 400+ pages in PDF, so be prepared to wait).

I think I'll pass on the 400+ pages deal even though I'm running a

(chuckle) I knew that there had been a lot of talk about the Y2K
compliancy with the applications and how COBOL was going to fit into
all of it though. Sometimes it just seems as though they're trying to
make a mountain out of a mole hill. Thanks for the web site though.
```

<a id="t-8927f38e2f"></a>
## Thread: Ethical Question — 7 msgs

### Re: Ethical Question

- **From:** Albert Ratzlaff <albert@infonet.com.py>
- **Date:** 1998-09-08T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<35F59275.C4CE0051@infonet.com.py>`
- **References:** `<6spk4b$ome$1@news-2.news.gte.net> <3298247.81807.423@kcbbs.gen.nz>`

```


Richard Plinston wrote:

> However, my real view is that there is nothing dangerous
> about a GO TO.  It is a very clear statement, has no
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> <snip>

I was'nt going to jump into this thread, as I sincerly suspected that the original poster was
just baiting. Ethical question? About GOTO?
But as this may be my last chance to put in my two bits on the GOTO thing (these threads have to
stop soon...), I must say that I agree with Richard.
However, I don't like and don't user PERFORM-THRU. It's only justification is to allow GOTOS to
the exit part. That means we are implementing a RETURN statement which COBOL lacks. I find that
only a small percentage of routines actually need a RETURN statement, and that does not justifty
using THRU-EXIT on *all* routines. For those cases that need it, I use a status flag to bypass
the rest of the code in the paragraph, and control jumps off the end.
What I do like, is a CONTINUE statement, which GOTOs the top of the paragraph. I use it whenever
the "loop" control does not belong in the calling routine. For example, a READ routine that
displays a "Record locked" message and tries again.

Regards
Albert Ratzlaff
```

#### ↳ Re: Ethical Question

- **From:** "Donald Tees" <donald@willmack.com>
- **Date:** 1998-09-08T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol

#### ↳ Re: Ethical Question

- **From:** riplin@kcbbs.gen.nz (Richard Plinston)
- **Date:** 1998-09-09T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3298251.80691.26495@kcbbs.gen.nz>`
- **References:** `<35F59275.C4CE0051@infonet.com.py>`

```
In message <<35F59275.C4CE0051@infonet.com.py>> Albert Ratzlaff <albert@infonet.com.py> writes:

> What I do like, is a CONTINUE statement, which GOTOs the top of the paragraph. I use it whenever

Actually, and this is a common misunderstanding, a CONTINUE
does absolutely nothing at all (except stop the compiler
complaining about an empty block).

> the "loop" control does not belong in the calling routine. For example, a READ routine that
> displays a "Record locked" message and tries again.

I sincerely hope that you put a blocking delay into that loop,
or at least a 'despatch' to the operating system.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Ethical Question

- **From:** Albert Ratzlaff <albert@infonet.com.py>
- **Date:** 1998-09-10T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<35F7B6CD.88CEA08F@infonet.com.py>`
- **References:** `<35F59275.C4CE0051@infonet.com.py> <3298251.80691.26495@kcbbs.gen.nz>`

```


Richard Plinston wrote:

> In message <<35F59275.C4CE0051@infonet.com.py>> Albert Ratzlaff <albert@infonet.com.py> writes:
>
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> complaining about an empty block).

Sorry about that. I meant the 'C' equivalent of CONTINUE, not the COBOL statement.

>
>
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> or at least a 'despatch' to the operating system.

Yep. I accept a one caracter variable so the operator has to hit ENTER.

Regards
Albert Ratzlaff
```

- **References:** `<01bdc87a$7a31eee0$135ebfa8@dagreatest>`

In article <01bdc87a$7a31eee0$135ebfa8@dagreatest>, chrisbougher@usa.net 
says...
>
>Can anyone tell me how to clear the screen in ms-dos with cobol?

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Clearing the screen

- **From:** jcj0347@aol.com (JCJ0347)
- **Date:** 1998-09-09T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<1998090923180800.TAA21878@ladder01.news.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<6t5teh$o3q$2@news.enterprise.net>`

```
>Can anyone tell me how to clear the screen >in ms-dos with cobol?

Never use any product containing amonia.

Alcohol wipes (available from most office supply and computer stores) work
best.

Clearing the screen with cobol can leave messy procedure division build-up,
which
has to be cleaned with rpg-based solvents.

Hope this helps.
JC Jones.
```
