002550
002560
002570
002580*-----------------------------------------------------------------
002590 PROCEDURE DIVISION.
002600
002610     DISPLAY "Fujitsu COBOL -- Btrieve Interface Sample"
002620     DISPLAY SPACES
002630
002640     *> Open Btrieve File
002650     MOVE ZERO TO BTRIEVE-DATA-BUFFER-LENGTH
002660     MOVE ZERO TO BTRIEVE-KEY-NUMBER
002670     MOVE "TEST.BTR" TO BTRIEVE-FILE-NAME
002680     MOVE BTRIEVE-OPEN TO BTRIEVE-OPERATION-CODE
002690     PERFORM CALL-BTRIEVE
002700     IF BTRIEVE-STATUS-CODE = ZERO
002710        DISPLAY "Btrieve Open Successful"
002720     ELSE
002730        DISPLAY "Error Opening file. Status=" BTRIEVE-STATUS-CODE
002740     END-IF
002750
002760     *> Get First Record
002770     MOVE 40 TO BTRIEVE-DATA-BUFFER-LENGTH
002780     MOVE ZERO TO BTRIEVE-KEY-NUMBER
002790     MOVE BTRIEVE-GET-FIRST TO BTRIEVE-OPERATION-CODE
002800     PERFORM CALL-BTRIEVE
002810     IF BTRIEVE-STATUS-CODE = ZERO
002820        DISPLAY "Btrieve Record: " BTRIEVE-DATA-BUFFER( 1 : 40 )
002830     ELSE
002840        DISPLAY "Error Getting First Record. Status="
BTRIEVE-STATUS-CODE
002850     END-IF
002860
002870     *> Close Btrieve File
002880     MOVE ZERO TO BTRIEVE-DATA-BUFFER-LENGTH
002890     MOVE ZERO TO BTRIEVE-KEY-NUMBER
002900     MOVE BTRIEVE-CLOSE TO BTRIEVE-OPERATION-CODE
002910     PERFORM CALL-BTRIEVE
002920     IF BTRIEVE-STATUS-CODE = ZERO
002930        DISPLAY "Btrieve Close File Successful"
002940     ELSE
002950        DISPLAY "Error Closing Btrieve File. Status="
BTRIEVE-STATUS-CODE
002960     END-IF
002970
002980     EXIT PROGRAM.
002990
003000
003010*-----------------------------------------------------------------
003020 CALL-BTRIEVE.
003030
003040     CALL "BTRCALL" WITH STDCALL USING BTRIEVE-OPERATION-CODE
003050                                       BTRIEVE-STATUS-CODE
003060                                       BTRIEVE-POSITION-BLOCK
003070                                       BTRIEVE-DATA-BUFFER
003080                                       BTRIEVE-DATA-BUFFER-LENGTH
003090                                       BTRIEVE-FILE-NAME
003100                                       BTRIEVE-KEY-NUMBER
003110     END-CALL.
003120
003130
003140*-----------------------------------------------------------------
003150*CALL-BTRIEVE-CLIENT.
003160
003170
003180*-----------------------------------------------------------------
003190 END PROGRAM FJ2BTRV.
003200*-----------------------------------------------------------------

- **From:** riplin@kcbbs.gen.nz (Richard Plinston)
- **Date:** 1998-09-09T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3298251.83045.28661@kcbbs.gen.nz>`
- **References:** `<Jl0PnHJ5PvPd-pn2-CFt1dztdOno3@Dwight_Miller.iix.com>`

```
In message <<Jl0PnHJ5PvPd-pn2-CFt1dztdOno3@Dwight_Miller.iix.com>> "Chris Osborne" <chris_n_osborne@yahoo.com> writes:
> 
> My COBOL Reference manual from IBM says that SECTION is an organizational
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> feature.

SECTIONs may be PERFORMed, and even PERFORM Section1 THRU Section2,
but the major problem is that the end of the section is only
indicated by a new SECTION header.  It is best to avoid this.

In some implementations, especially older ones, SECTIONS were
used for overlaying, I still have some programs that are
overlayed on DOS.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Finding Variable-Length Record Lengt

- **From:** "COBOL Frog (Huib Klink)" <H.Klink@IMN.nl>
- **Date:** 1998-09-11T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<35F972C2.8BC7DB07@IMN.nl>`
- **References:** `<Jl0PnHJ5PvPd-pn2-CFt1dztdOno3@Dwight_Miller.iix.com> <3298251.83045.28661@kcbbs.gen.nz>`

```
I wonder why this shows up in this thread i.s.o. the "Ethical Question" thread, but anyway ...

Richard Plinston wrote:

> In message <<Jl0PnHJ5PvPd-pn2-CFt1dztdOno3@Dwight_Miller.iix.com>> "Chris Osborne" <chris_n_osborne@yahoo.com> writes:

8<

> > However, SECTION is obviously more than just this, because when sections and
> > paragraphs are mixed, as IBM puts it, "Your results may be unpredictable."

This is to Chris:In IBM's LRM it was also exactly explained under which circumstances a mix was unpredeictable. One for
example is the PERFORM SECTIOBNAME THRU PARAGRAPHNAME or the vice versa variation.

> > Does anyone know what SECTIONS are really for?  Beyond the organizational
> > feature.
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> indicated by a new SECTION header.  It is best to avoid this.

This is to Richard:I don't see why this is a problem, let alone a major one. What's the difference between sections and
paragraphs here?. At most the compiler has some more problems in COBOL than in other languages: in COBOL the end of a
section is denoted by the beginning of another one or the end of a higher construct (the end of the linkage section is
denoted by the start of the procedure division f.e.) The same goes for paragraphs: they end where the next one starts or
where a section or other higher type of construct starts.
So two things:
* 1 * The end of a section is as much or as little a problem as the end of a section.
* 2 * As soon as the compiler knows the end, it has to do some "end processing", eventually resulting in some
return-to-caller-code, perhaps conditionally executed etc. My point is that this "end processing" is of the same kind as
in any compiler (any language) when an end of some unit is reached. (Like the } in C/C++, END in pl/1, pascal etc.) Only
the syntax parsing part of the compiler is a bit complexer in COBOL than it other languages. (Personally I suspect that a
lot of the simplicitiy in languages other than COBOL  is not built in for convenience of the programmer, but for the
compiler builders).

> In some implementations, especially older ones, SECTIONS were
> used for overlaying, I still have some programs that are
> overlayed on DOS.

Of course, in COBOL, sections were introduced as an extra divisional layer for the procedure division, to be used in
memory management. The syntactical possibility to add segmenting numbers etc. shows that. Even without explicit segmenting
it gave the compiler builders a way to segment the resulting code anyway. Lots of compilers used that, f.e. IBM
(DSECT/CSECT). I am almost sure that the developers of the COBOL standard had all this in mind when they choosed for
sections and paragraphs, besides their goal to mimic plain english and book-like structures. I would not have surprised
when they had invented CHAPTERs also :)

Grt.
The COBOL Frog
```

---

<a id="t-89ca20415e"></a>
## Thread: Fujitso Compiler Support for Object COBOL Bug? — 7 msgs

_Topics: [`Compilers and vendors`](topics.md#compilers)_

### Fujitso Compiler Support for Object COBOL Bug?

- **From:** "Gene Webb" <gene.webb@newsguy.com>
- **Date:** 1998-09-09T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,alt.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<6t78a4$au9@enews2.newsguy.com>`

```
I think I have discovered a fundamental bug with the Object Oriented COBOL
support for the Fujitsu Compiler version 3.0.  The same code works fine in a
normal strucuted COBOL program (Thanks Thane) but doesn't work in an Object
COBOL class library.  The error that is returned is VERY strange indeed.

Here is a sample of how to cause the error (notice a simple display
statement).

      *
       Identification Division.
       Class-Id. SampleClass
                 inherits FJBASE.
      *
       Environment Division.
       Configuration Section.
      *
       Repository.
           Class FJBASE.
      *
       Factory.
       Procedure Division.
       End Factory.
      *
       Object.
       Data Division.
       Working-Storage Section.
       Procedure Division.
      *
       Method-Id. sampleMethod.
       Data Division.
       Working-Storage Section.
      *
       01 sampleText.
          05 filler   pic x(06) value 'Hello'.
          05 filler   pic x(07) value 'World'.
      *
       Linkage Section.
      *
       Procedure Division.
      *
           display sampleText at line 01 column 01.
      *
       End Method sampleMethod.
      *
       End Object.
       End Class SampleClass.

This is the error message

Build Start.
E:\FUJITSU\COBOL97\COBOL32.EXE -iE:\FUJITSU\COBOL97\COBOL.CBI
E:\PROJECT\WORK\hellow.cob
** DIAGNOSTIC MESSAGE ** (SAMPLECLASS)
E:\PROJECT\WORK\hellow.cob 33: JMN5568I-S  DISPLAY STATEMENT FOR SCREEN
HANDLING CAN BE USED IN PROGRAM DEFINITION.
STATISTICS: HIGHEST SEVERITY CODE=S, PROGRAM UNIT=1
Build End.

Maybe I don't have my compiler options set correctky? I just run wincob at a
command prompt select the source file (hellow.cob) and compiler and it
produces this error.  Let me know if anyone see a really stupid error on my
part.  Again this works in a normal strucuted COBOL program.
```

#### ↳ Re: Fujitso Compiler Support for Object COBOL Bug?

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1998-09-09T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,alt.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<6t7dv8$bs@dfw-ixnews9.ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<6t78a4$au9@enews2.newsguy.com>`

```
What the message is saying (as I read it) is that you may not use an
"extension" display statement in a method (but can use it in a program).
If you remove

   "at line 01 column 01"

(so you have an ANSI conforming DISPLAY statement, I'll bet your program
will compile and run OK).

FYI, there was quite a bit of discussion in the ANSI and ISO committees
about whether methods should be allowed to have

    - screen section
    - report writer
    - communication section

I don't remember exactly where it ended up in the draft Standard, but it
looks like Fujitsu is (currently) not allowing the enhanced Accept/Display
in methods.


Gene Webb wrote in message <6t78a4$au9@enews2.newsguy.com>...
>I think I have discovered a fundamental bug with the Object Oriented COBOL
>support for the Fujitsu Compiler version 3.0.  The same code works fine in
a
>normal strucuted COBOL program (Thanks Thane) but doesn't work in an Object
>COBOL class library.  The error that is returned is VERY strange indeed.
…[54 more quoted lines elided]…
>Maybe I don't have my compiler options set correctky? I just run wincob at
a
>command prompt select the source file (hellow.cob) and compiler and it
>produces this error.  Let me know if anyone see a really stupid error on my
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Fujitsu Compiler Support for Object COBOL Bug?

- **From:** "Gene Webb" <gene.webb@newsguy.com>
- **Date:** 1998-09-10T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,alt.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<6t9nq9$db@enews1.newsguy.com>`
- **References:** `<6t78a4$au9@enews2.newsguy.com> <6t7dv8$bs@dfw-ixnews9.ix.netcom.com>`

```
I don't think you can do a display statement in the object cobol support
other than one without a line or column specification.  You can not even
define a screen section without getting the following error.  (I tried it
with and without a line and column number).

I have not received a respone from Fujitsu.  I am becoming very discouraged
with the object cobol support included with Fujitsu.  I can not speak to the
standard cobol support or even the GUI tools. Here are some VERY big holes I
see in the Fujitsu support:

Limited classes supplied with the compiler
No base support for collection classes
No support for call backs
You can not even define procedure pointers so you could write your own call
backs

If you are going to be doing OO COBOL I would strongly suggest the
NetExpress product or looking at the IBM product.


Build Start.
E:\FUJITSU\COBOL97\COBOL32.EXE -iE:\FUJITSU\COBOL97\COBOL.CBI
E:\PROJECT\WORK\hellow.cob
** DIAGNOSTIC MESSAGE ** (SAMPLECLASS)
E:\PROJECT\WORK\hellow.cob 20: JMN1686I-S  PROGRAM DEFINITION CAN BE
SPECIFIED IN SCREEN SECTION.
STATISTICS: HIGHEST SEVERITY CODE=S, PROGRAM UNIT=1
Build End.

This was produced by this code
      *
       Identification Division.
       Class-Id. SampleClass
                 inherits FJBASE.
      *
       Environment Division.
       Configuration Section.
      *
       Repository.
           Class FJBASE.
      *
       Factory.
       Procedure Division.
       End Factory.
      *
       Object.
       Data Division.
       Working-Storage Section.
      *
       Screen Section.
      *
       01 sampleText.
          03 sample1 line 01 column 01 value 'Hello'.
          03 sample2 line 02 column 01 value 'World'.
      *
       Procedure Division.
      *
       Method-Id. sampleMethod.
       Data Division.
       Working-Storage Section.
      *
       Linkage Section.
      *
       Procedure Division.
      *
      *    display sampleText.
      *
       End Method sampleMethod.
      *
       End Object.
       End Class SampleClass.

It does indeed compile if you remove the at line 01 column 01. I looked at
the standard this morning and unless I am reading it incorrectly it allows
for the line and column position.  I am trying to convert the userinterface
class for the object oriented cobol tutorial from NetExpress to Fujitsu.
This same code works fine in NetExpress.   I wouldn't say it is useless to
not be able to put a line and column position but it does but a large
constrant on what you can do. In my example I need to do the display at a
specific location or else it just comes out line after line or worse with no
advancing on top of each other.

I am going to put one semi-plug related to the draft new Standard.

The Fujitsu representative to J4 - when wearing ANOTHER HAT - (member of the
German delegation to WG4) is the most adamant opponent of adding and/or
requiring the entire enhanced Screen facility into the next Standard.

If you find the current Fujitsu restriction abhorrent (especially when
compared to the Micro Focus implementation), I would STRONGLY recommend that
you contact Fujitsu and request that they actively support the next Standard
allowing syntax like the example which is currently being rejected.

<a id="t-90a63232a5"></a>
## Thread: MF COBOL file reads are slowwww.... — 8 msgs

_Topics: [`Compilers and vendors`](topics.md#compilers)_

### MF COBOL file reads are slowwww....

- **From:** markd@bellsouth.net (MarkD)
- **Date:** 1998-09-09T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<CZAJ1.1996$g04.1581619@news3.mia.bellsouth.net>`

```
My company is using MF COBOL under AIX and I found that reading files
is exceptionally slow.  A brain-dead program that simply opens and
reads a sequential file with 1.8 million 184-byte records takes 15
minutes using MF COBOL (open input/read next) and just 25 *seconds*
using C (fopen/fread).  I ran the MF COBOL in both .gnt and natively
compiled (-x) formats.  The performance difference is the same on
various files - both sequential and indexed.

Is this inherent in MF COBOL's file handler, or do we have a real
problem?  If it's a problem, where should we start to look?

Thanks in advance.
markd@bellsouth.net
```

#### ↳ Re: MF COBOL file reads are slowwww....

- **From:** redsky@ibm.net (Thane Hubbell)
- **Date:** 1998-09-09T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<Jl0PnHJ5PvPd-pn2-imWCUpoGHPbb@Dwight_Miller.iix.com>`
- **References:** `<CZAJ1.1996$g04.1581619@news3.mia.bellsouth.net>`

```
On Wed, 9 Sep 1998 19:49:54, markd@bellsouth.net (MarkD) wrote:

> Is this inherent in MF COBOL's file handler, or do we have a real
> problem?  If it's a problem, where should we start to look?


MicroFocus does have some runtine switches that will allow you to 
increase the read buffering.  Beyond that, you are a slave to their 
slow I-O. Having a be-all do-all file handler does that.  Microfocus 
has always had slow I-O.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: MF COBOL file reads are slowwww....

- **From:** "Leif Svalgaard" <leif@ibm.net>
- **Date:** 1998-09-09T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<35f6e532.0@news1.ibm.net>`
- **References:** `<CZAJ1.1996$g04.1581619@news3.mia.bellsouth.net> <Jl0PnHJ5PvPd-pn2-imWCUpoGHPbb@Dwight_Miller.iix.com>`

```
Thane Hubbell wrote in message ...
>On Wed, 9 Sep 1998 19:49:54, markd@bellsouth.net (MarkD) wrote:
>
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>has always had slow I-O.


If the file is RECORD SEQUENTIAL I/O is faster because there
is no scanning for CR/tabs/etc.




>
```

#### ↳ Re: MF COBOL file reads are slowwww....

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc123@bellsouth.net>
- **Date:** 1998-09-09T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<QODJ1.1098$p61.1687841@news4.mia.bellsouth.net>`
- **References:** `<CZAJ1.1996$g04.1581619@news3.mia.bellsouth.net>`

```
MarkD wrote in message ...
>My company is using MF COBOL under AIX and I found that reading files
>is exceptionally slow.  A brain-dead program that simply opens and
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>problem?  If it's a problem, where should we start to look?


It is not inherent in the MF COBOL 3.2 DOS version.  I have written
a number of little utility programs in QuickBasic, COBOL and MS C
6.0a.  The COBOL ones *blow away* the QuickBasic and C programs on
sequential I/O.  I sometimes rewrite programs in COBOL, if I use them
much, just for the I/O speed.  I'm not kidding, the sequential I/O
from that compiler is *dramatically* faster than QuickBasic or MSC.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: MF COBOL file reads are slowwww....

- **From:** theodp@aol.com (Theo DP)
- **Date:** 1998-09-10T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<1998091001325100.VAA16956@ladder03.news.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<QODJ1.1098$p61.1687841@news4.mia.bellsouth.net>`

```
><HTML><PRE>Subject: Re: MF COBOL file reads are slowwww....
>From: "Judson McClendon" <judmc123@bellsouth.net>
…[30 more quoted lines elided]…
></PRE></HTML>

Reality check, please...13.84 megabytes per second - could this be right?
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: MF COBOL file reads are slowwww....

- **From:** markd@bellsouth.net (MarkD)
- **Date:** 1998-09-10T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<MKQJ1.1259$p61.2088477@news4.mia.bellsouth.net>`
- **References:** `<QODJ1.1098$p61.1687841@news4.mia.bellsouth.net> <1998091001325100.VAA16956@ladder03.news.aol.com>`

```
>><HTML><PRE>Subject: Re: MF COBOL file reads are slowwww....
>>From: "Judson McClendon" <judmc123@bellsouth.net>
…[14 more quoted lines elided]…
>>


>Reality check, please...13.84 megabytes per second - could this be right?

Yes, it's right.  The tests were done on a high end multi-processor
RS6000 under AIX with simple single record reads (no blocking/caching
methods implemented). That's why the MF COBOLs I/O rates seem
absolutely appalling, at best.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: MF COBOL file reads are slowwww....

- **From:** scm@enterprise.net (Shaun C. Murray)
- **Date:** 1998-09-10T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<6t8nqo$3qu$1@news.enterprise.net>`
- **References:** `<QODJ1.1098$p61.1687841@news4.mia.bellsouth.net> <1998091001325100.VAA16956@ladder03.news.aol.com>`

```
In article <1998091001325100.VAA16956@ladder03.news.aol.com>, theodp@aol.com 
says...
>
>Reality check, please...13.84 megabytes per second - could this be right?

What's unreal about that? My Dual Pentium II/300 with UW SCSI Raid array 
under NT get's 30+ Mb per second easy. 13.8 per second is chump change. Most 
reasonable SCSI controllers and newer IDE ones cope with that fine.
```

## Thread: Free COBOL compiler wanted - FAST — 4 msgs

_Topics: [`Compilers and vendors`](topics.md#compilers)_

### Free COBOL compiler wanted - FAST

- **From:** Paul@bdol.com (Paul)
- **Date:** 1998-09-09T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<35f6c1fc.8968165@199.190.65.4>`

```
Hi.

My college just dumped our dialup system. I need to work from
home. Does anyone know of any free compilers / interpreters
that I can use?

I need to know ASAP. E-mail may be the fastest way.

#### ↳ Re: Free COBOL compiler wanted - FAST

- **From:** rtwolfe@flexus.com (Bob Wolfe)
- **Date:** 1998-09-10T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<35f8243e.17705639@news.enter.net>`
- **References:** `<35f6c1fc.8968165@199.190.65.4>`

```
You have several alternatives...........

You should take a look at the COBOL FAQ which contains a variety of
choices for a free compiler.

The COBOL FAQ is located at:

http://www.cis.ohio-state.edu/hypertext/faq/usenet/computer-lang/cobol-faq/faq.html

Micro Focus has a couple of cheap student compilers.  One for Dos and
one for Windows.  The Windows version is actually a 16 bit compiler
but it will run in 16 or 32 bit Windows environments.  They're called
"Personal COBOL"  These compilers are for educational purposes which
means that you cannot create a stand alone executable.
http://www.mfltd.co.uk

Fujitsu has a free compiler for Windows.  I think that they have both
16 and 32 bit versions.
http://www.adtools.com

You can also buy a book called COBOL for Dummies by Arthur Griffith.
The ISBN number is : 0-7645-0298-0
It contains the Fujitsu compiler as well as demo versions of the
Acucobol and Micro Focus compilers.  Contact IDG Books at:

http://www.dummies.com/

and search for the word COBOL.

You can get a compiler in a book called, "The Revolutionary Guide to
COBOL."  I believe that it's a DOS compiler.
http://www.wrox.com.

You can also get a copy of the Realia COBOL compiler in a book called,
"COBOL: from Micro to Mainframe" by Robert T. Grauer & Carol Vazquez.
The ISBN number is 0-13-140179-3 and it's available from Prentice
Hall.  http://www.prenhall.com/divisions/ecs/cscat.html    The
toll-free number in the United States is 1-800-223-1360.

As a "public service" we keep a couple of copies of the shareware
COBOL compiler (COB650 and COBOL650) on our ftp site.  Please keep in
mind that we just keep it there for downloads.  We didn't write it.
We don't support it.
http://www.flexus.com

The book, entitled, Modern COBOL Programming by Wilson Price & Jack
Olson includes a copy of the Ryan McFarland compiler.  I suspect that
the compiler is limited in some fashion.  The Publisher is Mitchell
McGraw-Hill and the ISBN number is 0-07-837527-4.

Acucobol suggests that you download their eval version which
eventually times out.  http//www.acucobol.com.  I also believe that
Acucobol has provided a limited version of their compiler with the
Teach Yourself COBOL in 21 Days book.  Acucobol operates a special
program for bona-fide educational sites. Contact your local Acucobol
office for details. In the USA call 1-800-COBOL-85, or (619)-689-7220






Paul@bdol.com (Paul) wrote:

>Hi.
>
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Bob Wolfe, flexus
Check out The Flexus COBOL Page at http://www.flexus.com
```

## Thread: Making a file. — 6 msgs

### Making a file.

- **From:** Tim & Kim <peach@midwest.net>
- **Date:** 1998-09-09T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<35F6F05C.FDC7D19C@midwest.net>`

```
Hello.
     My name is Tim and I have just started in COBOL.  I am Schooling at
Western Il. Uni. and they have me working on their main-frame.  I drive
an hour to and from school so I must do work at home.  I have a question
that is probably stupid but can not find an answer to it.
     I am using the Fujitsu V3 compiler at home so I know things will
have to change from home to school.  The question is,  to test my
programs at home,  how do I make a test file?
Let's say I just want a file with   Name   Employee Number   Hours
Worked   &   Wage
Where would I enter this information for my COBOL program to read it?
Do I make it in a regular text file (Wordpad,  NotePad) or do I have to
make it in a database such as Access or DBase?
    Please e-mail if you have an answer or any help
peach@midwest.net
              Thanks a million!
                      Tim
```

<a id="t-8391795f28"></a>
## Thread: This Is Your Brain On SAS...This Is Your Brain On COBOL...Any Questions? — 12 msgs

### This Is Your Brain On SAS...This Is Your Brain On COBOL...Any Questions?

- **From:** theodp@aol.com (Theo DP)
- **Date:** 1998-09-09T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<1998090903121300.XAA14005@ladder03.news.aol.com>`

```
After more than 100 posts, the debate's still going strong about the following
COBOL right justification algorithms (see below). Just for grins, thought I'd
post how this would be accomplished with SAS...

*** SAS ***

OUTFIELD=RIGHT(INFIELD);

*** COBOL ***

Subject: Re: Right Justify Routine

       Identification Division.
       Program-Id.  Test3.
      * right justify
       Environment Division.
       Configuration Section.
       Source-Computer.  IBM-PC.
       Object-Computer.  IBM-PC.
       Data Division.
       Working-Storage Section.
       01  input-field     pic x(30) value spaces.
       01  output-field    pic x(30) value spaces.
       01  teststrings-def.
           05 t1           pic x(30) value "ab".
           05 t2           pic x(30) value "abcdef".
           05 t3           pic x(30) value "abcdefghijklmn".
           05 t4           pic x(30) value "abcdefghijklmno".
           05 t5           pic x(30) value "abcdefghijklmnopqrstuvw".
       01  teststrings redefines teststrings-def.
           05 ti           pic x(30) occurs 5 times.
       01  tc              pic s9(4) comp.
       01  counter         pic s9(4) comp  value 1.
       01  char pic x.
       01  i               pic s9(4) comp.
       01  j               pic s9(4) comp.
       01  k               pic s9(4) comp.
       01  l               pic s9(4) comp.
       01  p               pic s9(4) comp.
       01  time-start      pic 9(4).
       01  time-end        pic 9(4).
       01  time-loop       pic 9(4).
       01  loop            pic s9(9) comp-5 value 500000.
       01  flag      pic 9 value 0.
           88 ad           value 0.
           88 sub          value 1.

       Procedure Division.
       test-3-start.
           perform varying tc from 1 by 1 until tc > 5
            move ti(tc) to input-field
            move space to output-field
            move function current-date (13:4) to time-start
            perform test3-start-AS loop  times
            move function current-date (13:4) to time-end
            compute time-loop = time-end - time-start
            display "AS :" time-loop output-field

            move ti(tc) to input-field
            move function current-date (13:4) to time-start
            perform test3-start-AS1 loop  times
            move function current-date (13:4) to time-end
            compute time-loop = time-end - time-start
            display "AS1:" time-loop output-field

            move ti(tc) to input-field
            move function current-date (13:4) to time-start
            perform test3-start-jud-coded loop times
            move function current-date (13:4) to time-end
            compute time-loop = time-end - time-start
            display "JUD:" time-loop output-field

           end-perform

           display "press enter"
           accept char
           stop run
           .

       for-later-use.
           move function current-date (13:4) to time-start
           perform test3-start-Rick loop times
           move function current-date (13:4) to time-end
           compute time-loop = time-end - time-start
           display time-loop

           move function current-date (13:4) to time-start
           perform test3-start-Judson loop times
           move function current-date (13:4) to time-end
           compute time-loop = time-end - time-start
           display time-loop

           move function current-date (13:4) to time-start
           perform test3-start-hand-coded loop times
           move function current-date (13:4) to time-end
           compute time-loop = time-end - time-start
           display time-loop

           move function current-date (13:4) to time-start
           move "abcdefghijklm" to input-field
           perform test3-start-jud-coded loop times
           move function current-date (13:4) to time-end
           compute time-loop = time-end - time-start
           display time-loop
           display "press enter"
           accept char
           stop run
           .

       test3-start-Rick.
           move 1 to counter
           if input-field > spaces
               move function reverse (input-field) to input-field
               inspect input-field tallying counter for leading spaces
               move function reverse (input-field (counter:))
                   to input-field (counter:)
           end-if
           .
       test3-start-AS.
           if input-field not = spaces
               compute i = function length (input-field)
               perform varying counter from i by -1 until
                   input-field(counter:1) not = space
               end-perform
               compute k = function length(output-field) - counter
               move spaces to output-field (1:k)
               move input-field (1:counter)
                       to output-field (k + 1:counter)
           end-if
           .

       test3-start-AS1.
           if input-field not = spaces
             compute l = function length (input-field)
             move l to j
             move 0 to flag p
             perform until j = 1
               compute j rounded = (j / 2 )
               if ad add      j   to p
                else subtract j from p
               end-if
               if input-field(p:l - p) = space
                    move 1 to flag
                    if input-field(p - 1:) not = space
                       move 1 to j
                    end-if
               else move 0 to flag
               end-if
             end-perform
             if not ad subtract 1 from p end-if
             compute k = function length(output-field) - p
             move spaces to output-field (1:k)
             move input-field (1:p) to output-field (k + 1:p)
           end-if
           .

       test3-start-Judson.
           move 1 to counter
           if input-field not = spaces
               move function reverse(input-field) to output-field
               inspect output-field tallying counter for leading spaces
               string input-field delimited by size
                   into output-field with pointer counter
           end-if
           .

       test3-start-hand-coded.
           if input-field not = spaces
               compute i = function length (input-field)
               Move i to j
               perform until input-field (i:1) not = space
                   subtract 1 from i
               end-perform
               perform until i = 0
                   move input-field (i:1) to input-field (j:1)
                   subtract 1 from i
                   subtract 1 from j
               end-perform
               if j > 0
                   move spaces to input-field (1:j)
               end-if
           end-if
           .

       test3-start-jud-coded.
           if input-field not = spaces
               compute i = function length (input-field)
      *         Move i to j
               perform until input-field (i:1) not = space
                   subtract 1 from i
               end-perform
               compute j = function length(output-field)
               subtract i from j
               add 1 j giving k
               move input-field (1:i) to output-field (k:i)
               if j > 0
                   move spaces to output-field (1:j)
               end-if
           end-if
           .
```

<1998090903121300.XAA14005@ladder03.news.aol.com>...

following

I'd

##### ↳ ↳ Re: This Is Your Brain On SAS...This Is Your Brain On COBOL...Any Questions?

- **From:** "G. van Vlimmeren" <gvlimmer@hoogenbosch.nl>
- **Date:** 1998-09-09T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<01bddbd2$fe1efa20$2c050380@s70>`
- **References:** `<1998090903121300.XAA14005@ladder03.news.aol.com> <01bddbcb$d1fffae0$2c050380@s70>`

```
OOPS,

I was a bit sleepy...
This trick only works when the inputfield is smaller than the output field.

> > *** COBOL ***
> > 
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> Move input-field to output-field.

There is a shortcut compared to the previous listing, however:
This should be more like it:

 IDENTIFICATION DIVISION.                                             
 ENVIRONMENT DIVISION.                                                
 CONFIGURATION SECTION.                                               
 SOURCE-COMPUTER.             DPS-7.                                  
 OBJECT-COMPUTER.              DPS-7.                                  
 DATA DIVISION.                                                       
 WORKING-STORAGE SECTION.                                             
 01  INPUT-FIELD                        PIC X(30) VALUE SPACES.         
 01  OUTPUT-FIELD                     PIC X(30) VALUE SPACES.         
                                                                      
 01  INPUT-VALUES.                                                    
   03  INPUT-VALUE OCCURS 5           PIC X(30).                      

 01  INPUT-VULLEN.                                                    
   03  INPUT-1 PIC X(30) VALUE "AB".                                  
   03  INPUT-2 PIC X(30) VALUE "ABCDEF".                              
   03  INPUT-3 PIC X(30) VALUE "ABCDEFGHIJKLMN".                      
   03  INPUT-4 PIC X(30) VALUE "ABCDEFGHIJKLMNO".                     
   03  INPUT-5 PIC X(30) VALUE "ABCDEFGHIJKLMNOPQSRSTUVW".            
                                                                      
 01 X1 PIC 9(02) VALUE ZERO.                                          
 01 X2 PIC 9(02) VALUE ZERO.                                          
                                                                      
 PROCEDURE DIVISION.                                                  

 P000-MAIN.                                                           
      MOVE INPUT-VULLEN TO INPUT-VALUES.                              
      PERFORM VARYING X1 FROM 1 BY 1 UNTIL X1 > 5                     
          MOVE INPUT-VALUE (X1) TO INPUT-FIELD                        
          MOVE 0 TO X2                                                
          MOVE SPACES TO OUTPUT-FIELD                                 
          INSPECT INPUT-FIELD TALLYING X2 FOR CHARACTERS BEFORE       
                  INITIAL SPACE                                       
          COMPUTE X2 = (31 - X2)                                      
          STRING INPUT-FIELD DELIMITED BY SIZE INTO                   
                 OUTPUT-FIELD WITH POINTER X2                         
          DISPLAY "IN  : " INPUT-FIELD                                
          DISPLAY "OUT : " OUTPUT-FIELD                               
      END-PERFORM.                                                    
      STOP RUN.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: This Is Your Brain On SAS...This Is Your Brain On COBOL...Any Questions?

- **From:** scm@enterprise.net (Shaun C. Murray)
- **Date:** 1998-09-09T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<6t5ugl$o3q$3@news.enterprise.net>`
- **References:** `<1998090903121300.XAA14005@ladder03.news.aol.com> <01bddbcb$d1fffae0$2c050380@s70> <01bddbd2$fe1efa20$2c050380@s70>`

```
In article <01bddbd2$fe1efa20$2c050380@s70>, gvlimmer@hoogenbosch.nl says...
>          INSPECT INPUT-FIELD TALLYING X2 FOR CHARACTERS BEFORE       
>                  INITIAL SPACE                                       

That presumes the input-field doesn't contain spaces.

>          COMPUTE X2 = (31 - X2)                                      

That presumes it's 30 characters long. 

>          STRING INPUT-FIELD DELIMITED BY SIZE INTO                   
>                 OUTPUT-FIELD WITH POINTER X2                         

Why not just use a MOVE.eg.
 
           move input-field to output-field(x2:)
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: This Is Your Brain On SAS...This Is Your Brain On COBOL...Any Questions?

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@aiservices.com>
- **Date:** 1998-09-09T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<eKwJ1.875$I%.4060582@news1.atlantic.net>`
- **References:** `<1998090903121300.XAA14005@ladder03.news.aol.com> <01bddbcb$d1fffae0$2c050380@s70> <01bddbd2$fe1efa20$2c050380@s70> <6t5ugl$o3q$3@news.enterprise.net> <01bddbfa$0bcaf800$2c050380@s70>`

```

G. van Vlimmeren wrote in message <01bddbfa$0bcaf800$2c050380@s70>...
>
>Shaun C. Murray <scm@enterprise.net> schreef in artikel
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
>
The content could contain significant spaces. Consider the
following:

            move "Right Justify Me" to input-field

rather than a string with no significant spaces. That is why all
the previous offers of solutions look at trailing spaces.
-------------------------------
Rick Smith
e-mail: < ricksmith@aiservices.com >

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: This Is Your Brain On SAS...This Is Your Brain On COBOL...Any Questions?

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** "G. van Vlimmeren" <gvlimmer@hoogenbosch.nl>
- **Date:** 1998-09-10T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<01bddc8a$7aae9020$2c050380@s70>`
- **References:** `<1998090903121300.XAA14005@ladder03.news.aol.com> <01bddbcb$d1fffae0$2c050380@s70> <01bddbd2$fe1efa20$2c050380@s70> <6t5ugl$o3q$3@news.enterprise.net> <01bddbfa$0bcaf800$2c050380@s70> <eKwJ1.875$I%.4060582@news1.atlantic.net>`

```
Good point,

The solution I showed you was developed originally to scan the
'navigation-fields' of an application.
These navigation fields could consist of a number of choices, separated by
a dot (.).

So, someone could fill in '03.123456798.123' in a character-field about 40
positions long. A special routine would destill the 03, the 123456789 and
the 123 from that and deduce that the user wanted to go to client 03,
customer-id 123456789 and perform action 123. This routine didn't have to
take spaces inbetween texts into account. 

We used a special routine, kinda like the given example a few steps back in
the thread, to handle numeric conversion. Users wanted to be able to enter
an asterisk in a numeric field, to identify the field needed no changes (a
left-over from the old punchcard days). This meant we had to accept numeric
data in alphanumeric fields and had to scan these letter by letter to
identify the contents and convert them to proper numeric data when needed. 

> >> That presumes the input-field doesn't contain spaces.
> >That's why I FILL IT WITH SPACES in the first place :)
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>             move "Right Justify Me" to input-field
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: This Is Your Brain On SAS...This Is Your Brain On COBOL...Any Questions?

- **From:** "Leif Svalgaard" <leif@ibm.net>
- **Date:** 1998-09-09T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<35f6a080.0@news1.ibm.net>`
- **References:** `<1998090903121300.XAA14005@ladder03.news.aol.com> <01bddbcb$d1fffae0$2c050380@s70>`

```
We went over this several times.
JUSTIFIED RIGHT when the two fields have the same
length does nothing. If the sending field is shorter,
then the sending field (with spaces and all) are right
justified to the end of the receiving field, but the text
in the field is not justified.
Simple example:
aaa pic x(3).
bbb pic x(6) justified right.
...
move "sX" to aaa      <=== where the s is a space
move aaa to bbb      <==== will give:  bbb=ssssXs

G. van Vlimmeren wrote in message <01bddbcb$d1fffae0$2c050380@s70>...
>
>Theo DP <theodp@aol.com> schreef in artikel
…[22 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: This Is Your Brain On SAS...This Is Your Brain On COBOL...Any Questions?

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc123@bellsouth.net>
- **Date:** 1998-09-09T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<xkvJ1.907$p61.1366021@news4.mia.bellsouth.net>`
- **References:** `<27uJ1.833$p61.1330476@news4.mia.bellsouth.net> <1998090912184600.IAA14195@ladder01.news.aol.com>`

```
Theo DP wrote:
>
>Judson McClendon wrote:
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
>count!) and put it against the SAS solution and let us know which one you >honestly prefer...objective enough?

Sure, so long as you understand that one should not choose a language
based on a single function.  I do not even think such inter-language
comparisons are really useful.  You choose a language based on a whole
multitude of things, not the least of which is how well its features
fit the task.  For quick little utilities, or function prototyping, I
generally use BASIC, because it is unexcelled in such things, of the
languages I am familiar with (quite a few).  But I have only written
a few applications in BASIC, because it is not well suited for larger
projects.  I only used BASIC for those because it was for applications
on the PC before other languages became readily available.  There is
no 'best language', only languages more or less suitable for particular
kinds of tasks.  And COBOL happens to be the very best language there
is, for many types of business programming.

I love COBOL for what it is good for, I love BASIC for what it is good
for, and I love assembly for what it is good for.  I'm not sure I could
say that I 'love' C, but I appreciate C for what it is good for. :) (My
first code in publication was in C.)  I could say the same for a whole
bunch of languages I have used over the years, and my job would have
been more difficult without most of them.  All of them could have been
improved, at least for some tasks, and all of them had strengths. (Well,
maybe not RPG. :)
```

#### ↳ Re: vax cobol

- **From:** rtwolfe@flexus.com (Bob Wolfe)
- **Date:** 1998-09-09T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<35f67569.9459288@news.enter.net>`
- **References:** `<35f5d9da.1849324@nntp.ix.netcom.com>`

```
gvwmoore@ix.netcom.com (G Moore) wrote:

>does any1 know any links for rpc products for vax machines, as400
>machines, pcs, and cobol?
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>gvwmoore@ix.spam.netcom.com to reply remove the spam

Mr. Moore:

We have a close working relationship with England Technical Services.
I do know that Bob England just completed a prelimiary VAX VMS port of
the COBOL RPC product embedded in our COBOL sp2 Thin Client.

You should probably contact Bob England directly to determine when he
plans to release a production version of COBOL RPC for the VAX. The PC
support has been available for both 16 bit and 32 bit Windows
platforms for a long time, so that should be no problem at all.  I
don't believe that he supports OS/400 yet, but perhaps COBOL RPC can
also be ported to that operating system as well.

COBOL RPC essentially allows a COBOL program on machine A call a COBOL
program on machine B and pass the associated paramter block.  This is
accomplished through the TCP/IP communications protocol, so it can
easily work across the Internet.

Bob's product is actually quite easy to use because the programmer
doesn't really have to know a lot about TCP/IP and all programming is
done in good ol' COBOL.  Both machines do have to support a TCP/IP
stack.

You can contact Bob England at:

http://www.netins.net/showcase/etsinc/ or send him e-mail at "Bob
England" <ets@netins.net>

Good luck!





Bob Wolfe, flexus
Check out The Flexus COBOL Page at http://www.flexus.com
```

<a id="t-d14a979c89"></a>
## Thread: Should I learn Cobol before VB — 1 msgs

_Topics: [`Tutorials, books, learning`](topics.md#learning)_

### Re: Should I learn Cobol before VB

- **From:** "William R. Fink" <bfink@spam.apci.net>
- **Date:** 1998-09-09T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<6t6ahd$k3l@mhstl1.monsanto.com>`
- **References:** `<RrWH1.3805$gM3.2819757@news4.mia.bellsouth.net> <3298247.80109.29596@kcbbs.gen.nz>`

```
>
>I would prefer to work with a Cobol programmer who knew how the
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>

I would almost agree, however, programmers cost money.  The time it take a
programmer to develop/maintain code requires a very good understanding on
the technical end.  A programmer with a strong understanding of the computer
will debug his/her programs much faster than a person who just knows the
language.

When I went to college, Assembler was a required part of the MIS degree.
While I have not written an Assembler program in quite some time, the
knowledge it brought me makes me a better programmer.

Now, many colleges no longer require assembler.  This is a great disservice.
Anyone can write COBOL, but it takes understanding to efficiently
maintain/debug code.

I wonder how much time will/would be wasted because a programmer didn't
understand the internals well enough; and took 3 times as long to solve the
bug in the program.  (Person-hours add up).


Reminds me of a time when a new programmer could not figure how to read a
packed number.


In the end, a person who can learn COBOL is probably smart enough to learn
accounting (I find the reverse not the same).

--Bill
```

## Thread: COBOL GUI front end — 96 msgs

### COBOL GUI front end

- **From:** "Gordon & Gotch Publishing Limited" <technical@gordon-gotch.co.uk>
- **Date:** 1998-09-10T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** alt.cobol,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<6t84he$fsf$1@eros.clara.net>`

```
We are looking to put a GUI front end on our main application packages.
They are written in RM/COBOL and consist of a large number of programs.

We are currently looking at two main options:

    using COBOL-WOW
    or moving to ACUCOBOL

Our programs do not use screen sections, are fairly well structured using
sections.

Your comments most welcome!

Thanks

Graham

#### ↳ Re: COBOL GUI front end

- **From:** cadams@acucorp.com
- **Date:** 1998-09-11T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** alt.cobol,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<6tc5mb$rn4$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>`
- **References:** `<6t84he$fsf$1@eros.clara.net>`

```
In article <6t84he$fsf$1@eros.clara.net>,
  "Gordon & Gotch Publishing Limited" <technical@gordon-gotch.co.uk> wrote:
> We are looking to put a GUI front end on our main application packages.
> They are written in RM/COBOL and consist of a large number of programs.
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>     or moving to ACUCOBOL

Disclaimer: I'm an Acucorp employee.

Why Acucobol?

The Acucobol extensions feel considerably more "natural" than usage of
library routines. The normal DISPLAY and ACCEPT verbs are used instead of
repeated CALLs, which helps the code feel more like "classic" COBOL.  Also,
the same object file that displays a GUI on a Windows box will display a
similar character mode interface on non-GUI machines. This can be completely
transparent to the programmer - I have a fairly large system that is
single-source for GUI and CUI.

Because we've extended the COBOL syntax, a great deal of behaviour is
provided automatically by the runtime. If you have an entire screen of data,
you can display and accept it normally. The runtime will handle the details
for things such as menus, user navigation and cutting and pasting. If you
want to override the default behaviour, you can easily change the control(s)
in question. Additionally, because you're using COBOL for everything, you
completely avoid the data conversion issues that could be a problem with some
libraries.

I've included a few examples below. If you have any questions or comments, I
monitor this group and you're certainly free to email me (cadams@acucorp.com)

**** Here's the code to display a Push Button on the screen borrowed from two
of our sample programs: (Screen Section)  03  push-button, "E&xit Program", 
ok-button,  line 25, cline 23, column 27, size 13.

(Procedure Division)
           display push-button, "E&xit Program",
               ID = 7, ok-button,
               line 23, column 25, size 13,
               handle in handle-7.

Now, here's something more complex. I've borrowed this code from a interactive
report program I wrote awhile back. This creates a grid:

    03  Results-Grid GRID
        LINE 3.25 COL 2,
        LINES Results-Grid-Lines CELLS SIZE Results-Grid-Size CELLS,
        DATA-COLUMNS = (1, 9, 15, 31, 47)
        COLUMN-HEADINGS CENTERED-HEADINGS TILED-HEADINGS,
        HEADING-COLOR = BCKGRND-WHITE, HEADING-DIVIDER-COLOR = BLACK,
        Y = 1
        X = 1 CELL-DATA = "Item ID"     ALIGNMENT="r"
        X = 2 CELL-DATA = "Test ID"     ALIGNMENT="r"
        X = 3 CELL-DATA = "Category"    ALIGNMENT="c"
        X = 4 CELL-DATA = "Time Stamp"  ALIGNMENT="r"
        X = 5 CELL-DATA = "Text"        ALIGNMENT="l"
        ADJUSTABLE-COLUMNS
        VSCROLL,
        3-D,
        USE-TAB,
        EVENT PROCEDURE IS Grid-Event-Proc.

The event procedure allows you to override the runtime's default handling.
Here's what that program used:
Grid-Event-Proc.
    EVALUATE Event-Type
      WHEN MSG-FINISH-ENTRY       CONTINUE
      WHEN MSG-CANCEL-ENTRY       CONTINUE
      WHEN MSG-MENU-INPUT         CONTINUE
      WHEN MSG-END-MENU           CONTINUE
      WHEN MSG-VALIDATE           CONTINUE
      WHEN MSG-GOTO-CELL          CONTINUE
      WHEN MSG-GOTO-CELL-MOUSE    CONTINUE
      WHEN MSG-BITMAP-CLICKED     CONTINUE
      WHEN MSG-HEADING-CLICKED    CONTINUE
      WHEN MSG-HEADING-DBLCLICK   CONTINUE
      WHEN MSG-GOTO-CELL-DRAG     CONTINUE
      WHEN MSG-HEADING-DRAGGED    CONTINUE
      WHEN MSG-BEGIN-DRAG         CONTINUE
      WHEN MSG-END-DRAG           CONTINUE
      WHEN MSG-BEGIN-HEADING-DRAG CONTINUE
      WHEN MSG-END-HEADING-DRAG   CONTINUE
      WHEN MSG-COL-WIDTH-CHANGED  CONTINUE
      WHEN OTHER                  CONTINUE
      WHEN MSG-INIT-MENU
        PERFORM Build-Report-Popup-Menu
      WHEN MSG-BEGIN-ENTRY
        SET EVENT-ACTION TO EVENT-ACTION-FAIL
      WHEN MSG-BITMAP-DBLCLICK
	INQUIRE Results-Grid, CELL-DATA IN Selected-Test-ID
        CALL THREAD "ViewDesc" USING Selected-Test-ID
    END-EVALUATE.

Event-Type is part of a SPECIAL NAMES items which is automatically filled in
by the runtime before the event procedure is called. As you can see, most of
the events were listed only for future programmer's convenience.

The MSG-INIT-MENU event is caused by a right click on a control with a defined
popup menu. In this program menus are dynamic and I have to rebuild the popup
menu before displaying it.

The MSG-BEGIN-ENTRY is triggered when the user double-clicks in a grid cell
or enters a character. Since I was only using the grid to display data, I
used the EVENT-ACTION to effectively make it read-only.

Lastly, in some cells an icon was added. When the user double clicks on it, a
MSG-BITMAP-DBLCLICK event is generated and a utility program is called in a
separate thread.

---
# Chris Adams <cadams@acucorp.com>
Any opinions expressed above may not match those of my employer.

http://www.dejanews.com/rg_mkgrp.xp   Create Your Own Free Member Forum

##### ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL GUI front end

- **From:** redsky@ibm.net (Thane Hubbell)
- **Date:** 1998-09-15T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,alt.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<Jl0PnHJ5PvPd-pn2-C0DpHpGZ8Bss@Dwight_Miller.iix.com>`
- **References:** `<6t84he$fsf$1@eros.clara.net> <6tc5mb$rn4$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>`

```
On Fri, 11 Sep 1998 21:45:47, cadams@acucorp.com wrote:

> Why Acucobol?
> 
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> 

Why not?  You become a slave to the extensions to Display and Accept 
as defined by Accucobol.  Upward and cross system compatibility are 
compromised.  I note, with great interest, that a large portion of the
next COBOL standard deals with cross system source code and 
operational compatibility.  This is very good and very exciting.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL GUI front end

- **From:** cadams@cadams-ntw.acucorp.com (Chris Adams)
- **Date:** 1998-09-15T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,alt.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<slrn6vtin3.iu.cadams@cadams-ntw.acucorp.com>`
- **References:** `<6t84he$fsf$1@eros.clara.net> <6tc5mb$rn4$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com> <Jl0PnHJ5PvPd-pn2-C0DpHpGZ8Bss@Dwight_Miller.iix.com>`

```
On 15 Sep 98 18:56:15 GMT, Thane Hubbell <redsky@ibm.net> wrote:
>> Why Acucobol?
>> 
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>> single-source for GUI and CUI.

>Why not?  You become a slave to the extensions to Display and Accept 
>as defined by Accucobol.  Upward and cross system compatibility are 
>compromised.  I note, with great interest, that a large portion of the

This is definitely a concern when using any vendor's extensions. Of course,
we're no different from the GUI libraries on this regard as there is no
standard way to handle a GUI. Since you're looking at non-standard code anyway,
I feel it's easier to use and maintain well integrated, very portable
proprietary routines than non-integrated proprietary routines. Certainly,
nothing stops anyone from encapsulating their GUI in separate modules for easy
migration.

We're really neutral on the issue: If you want to write ANSI standard COBOL, we
support that. If you want easy, manageable GUIs, we offer that as well. 

----
# Chris Adams <cadams@acucorp.com>
The opinions above are my own and may or may not match those of my employer.
```

>course,
>>> we're no different from the GUI libraries on this regard as there is no

>>That's not correct.  I can take the code written Realia, Fujitsu,
>>MicroFocus, etc and it's always the same, and it compiles and I still
>>get GUI - by making external calls.

>If you make one more level on indirection, you can have it both ways.
>That is: put the external calls or the proprietary extensions in a program
>you call, with your linkage section, then everything is still fine.

This is often a good idea in any case, simply for the added portability. A good
example might involve the current focus on internet applications. Since a web
interface[1] is a fundamentally different means of interaction than most
existing program, it's much easier to add a web interface to a program that has
the backend completely separate from the user interface.

[1] CGI, FastCGI, server module, etc.

----

- **From:** scm@enterprise.net (Shaun C. Murray)
- **Date:** 1998-09-16T00:00:00

- **References:** `<6t84he$fsf$1@eros.clara.net> <6tc5mb$rn4$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com> <Jl0PnHJ5PvPd-pn2-C0DpHpGZ8Bss@Dwight_Miller.iix.com> <slrn6vtin3.iu.cadams@cadams-ntw.acucorp.com> <Jl0PnHJ5PvPd-pn2-GhWFvMdwRmUd@Dwight_Miller.iix.com> <35fee546.0@news1.ibm.net> <slrn6vts0c.iu.cadams@cadams-ntw.acucorp.com>`

```
In article <slrn6vts0c.iu.cadams@cadams-ntw.acucorp.com>, 
cadams@cadams-ntw.acucorp.com says...
>
>This is often a good idea in any case, simply for the added portability. A 
good
>example might involve the current focus on internet applications. Since a web
>interface[1] is a fundamentally different means of interaction than most
>existing program, it's much easier to add a web interface to a program that 
has
>the backend completely separate from the user interface.
>
>[1] CGI, FastCGI, server module, etc.
>

Since front ends change regularly because of changes in OS/platform interfaces 
or because you may want to run the application on multiple platforms, *not* 
seperating the backend logic from the front-end interface is a pretty 
shortsighted method of development.

The same could be said of many areas. eg. File handling which is why I tend to 
use my own file handler. The app just sees a file handler. The file handler 
however could be via SQL, COBOL I/O, on a web server via TCP/IP....
```

run w/o modification in a telnet session to a Unix box as well as on Win31 and
WinNT.</PLUG>

decade old), it's painfully clear that quite a few designs have some rather
unsightly warts. Code that's been hanging around for decades is a lot more
understandable than things that were developed well after some people had
started warning about the problem...

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL GUI front end

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** "COBOL Frog (Huib Klink)" <H.Klink@IMN.nl>
- **Date:** 1998-09-16T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** alt.cobol,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<35FF8467.AAF3554A@IMN.nl>`
- **References:** `<6t84he$fsf$1@eros.clara.net> <6tc5mb$rn4$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com> <Jl0PnHJ5PvPd-pn2-C0DpHpGZ8Bss@Dwight_Miller.iix.com> <slrn6vtin3.iu.cadams@cadams-ntw.acucorp.com> <Jl0PnHJ5PvPd-pn2-GhWFvMdwRmUd@Dwight_Miller.iix.com> <35fee546.0@news1.ibm.net> <slrn6vts0c.iu.cadams@cadams-ntw.acucorp.com>`

```
Chris Adams wrote:

> ... 8< ... a web
> interface[1] is a fundamentally different means of interaction than most
> existing program,

As far as I have seen it, under the assumption that plain html is used without all
those nice embedded java applets or scripts and other "moving" things, .... a
web-interface basically needs just the same type of pseudo-conversationally
structured application programs as conventional transactional programs under f.e.
CICS or IMS. Basically, I repeat. The CGI-type of connection needs some other
approach then traditional call-interface between program and tp-monitor, but each
program stays the same in structure: triggered by the data entry (enter), starting
with the read/in of already transferred data, doing some processing, sending an
answer, doing some way to keep track of the state of the transaction end then stop
run/goback.

> it's much easier to add a web interface to a program that has
> the backend completely separate from the user interface.

Sure and very true. Each conversion is simpler and profits from it when the
original source is structured and modularised.

8< ....

The Frog
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL GUI front end

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** cadams@cadams-ntw.acucorp.com (Chris Adams)
- **Date:** 1998-09-16T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** alt.cobol,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<slrn6vvv1o.iu.cadams@cadams-ntw.acucorp.com>`
- **References:** `<6t84he$fsf$1@eros.clara.net> <6tc5mb$rn4$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com> <Jl0PnHJ5PvPd-pn2-C0DpHpGZ8Bss@Dwight_Miller.iix.com> <slrn6vtin3.iu.cadams@cadams-ntw.acucorp.com> <Jl0PnHJ5PvPd-pn2-GhWFvMdwRmUd@Dwight_Miller.iix.com> <35fee546.0@news1.ibm.net> <slrn6vts0c.iu.cadams@cadams-ntw.acucorp.com> <35FF8467.AAF3554A@IMN.nl>`

```
On Wed, 16 Sep 1998 11:27:03, COBOL Frog (Huib Klink) <H.Klink@IMN.nl> wrote:
>As far as I have seen it, under the assumption that plain html is used without all
>those nice embedded java applets or scripts and other "moving" things, .... a
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>run/goback.

The problem is that there is no state, so your calls need to pass context back
and forth. It looks something like this (and forgive me if you already knew
this):

1. Browser requests page
2. The CGI program is executed.
	a) Initializes
	b) Does processing
	c) Returns HTML
	d) Exits
3. Browser displays page
4. User completes form and submits it
5. Another CGI instance is started
	a) Initializes (e.g. reading context item to see what has been done)
	b) Processes data
	c) Returns HTML
	d) Exits
6. Lather
7. Rinse
8. Repeat

You can use a hidden field with a value to determine what stage of the
transaction your program is in. This makes life extremely difficult for
applications that were structured with DISPLAY/ACCEPT pairs throughout the
code... 




>> it's much easier to add a web interface to a program that has
>> the backend completely separate from the user interface.

>Sure and very true. Each conversion is simpler and profits from it when the
>original source is structured and modularised.

This is one reason why I'd like a sort of "Zen of Programming" course to be
mandatory for a CS degree. A sort of In the Trenches summary...

----
# Chris Adams <cadams@acucorp.com>
The opinions above are my own and may or may not match those of my employer.
```

> >CICS or IMS.

8<

> The problem is that there is no state, so your calls need to pass context back
> and forth. It looks something like this (and forgive me if you already knew
> this):

8< [I knew ;) ]

> You can use a hidden field with a value to determine what stage of the
> transaction your program is in. This makes life extremely difficult for
> applications that were structured with DISPLAY/ACCEPT pairs throughout the
> code...

Yep. Solution (just brainstorming): get the IP address or some other unique id of the
connection and use this id as a key to a db-table or indexed file to record state on
the server per user / connection. gives also the possibility to create an extra
TWA/COMM-like area (I assumed you know CICS, TWA=Transaction Work
Area/COMM=communications).

> >> it's much easier to add a web interface to a program that has
> >> the backend completely separate from the user interface.

8<

> >Each conversion is simpler and profits from it when the
> >original source is structured and modularised.
>
> This is one reason why I'd like a sort of "Zen of Programming" course to be
> mandatory for a CS degree. A sort of In the Trenches summary...

Yes. Always separates code for (1) screen i/o, (2) validation logic, (3) business
logic, (4) data file/base i/o. So easy to switch between all 3 (5 if extremes counted)
C/S types. (thin/medium/thick). And a lot easier to port to another platform too.

> ----
> # Chris Adams <cadams@acucorp.com>
> The opinions above are my own and may or may not match those of my employer.

Your employer need not be ashamed for these !!!

The Frog
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL GUI front end

_(thread depth: 10)_

- **From:** cadams@cadams-ntw.acucorp.com (Chris Adams)
- **Date:** 1998-09-16T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** alt.cobol,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<slrn7005qr.iu.cadams@cadams-ntw.acucorp.com>`
- **References:** `<6t84he$fsf$1@eros.clara.net> <6tc5mb$rn4$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com> <Jl0PnHJ5PvPd-pn2-C0DpHpGZ8Bss@Dwight_Miller.iix.com> <slrn6vtin3.iu.cadams@cadams-ntw.acucorp.com> <Jl0PnHJ5PvPd-pn2-GhWFvMdwRmUd@Dwight_Miller.iix.com> <35fee546.0@news1.ibm.net> <slrn6vts0c.iu.cadams@cadams-ntw.acucorp.com> <35FF8467.AAF3554A@IMN.nl> <slrn6vvv1o.iu.cadams@cadams-ntw.acucorp.com> <36000BA0.C42CDFA8@IMN.nl>`

```
On Wed, 16 Sep 1998, COBOL Frog (Huib Klink) <H.Klink@IMN.nl> wrote:
>> and forth. It looks something like this (and forgive me if you already knew
>> this):
>
>8< [I knew ;) ]

I feared as much... <g>

>> You can use a hidden field with a value to determine what stage of the
>> transaction your program is in. This makes life extremely difficult for
>> applications that were structured with DISPLAY/ACCEPT pairs throughout the
>> code...

>Yep. Solution (just brainstorming): get the IP address or some other unique id of the
>connection and use this id as a key to a db-table or indexed file to record state on
>the server per user / connection. gives also the possibility to create an extra
>TWA/COMM-like area (I assumed you know CICS, TWA=Transaction Work
>Area/COMM=communications).

The IP address won't work because of proxies. A browser cookie is usually the
best choice but there are an astonishing number of otherwise rational
individuals who get phobic about cookies. I think that a hidden value in a form
is probably the most reliable and least likely to provoke complaints.

One other alternative that's a bit buzzword heavy would involve something like
a Java front end, which could dramatically reduce server load. I don't know
about the other COBOL vendors but we've got a product called AcuConnect that
allows you to CALL programs on a remote system. A Java interface to AcuConnect
is in development.  This would allow you to have your Java applet do all of the
annoying validation and interface tasks and call the COBOL backend only for the
heavy work.

>> >Each conversion is simpler and profits from it when the
>> >original source is structured and modularised.
>>
>> This is one reason why I'd like a sort of "Zen of Programming" course to be
>> mandatory for a CS degree. A sort of In the Trenches summary...

>Yes. Always separates code for (1) screen i/o, (2) validation logic, (3) business
>logic, (4) data file/base i/o. So easy to switch between all 3 (5 if extremes counted)
>C/S types. (thin/medium/thick). And a lot easier to port to another platform too.

#4 is exactly the sort of thing the AcuConnect product I mentioned was designed
for. As an example, there's no sense in performing a search through an RDBMS
over a network when you can run the search on the server and pass only the
results back. Unless, of course, you're a gigabit ethernet vendor...

I'd also stress that it's a Good Thing not to be afraid of creating general
subprograms. For example, in one project I created a generic routine to get a
filename from the user. When it returns, you get a filename in the passed item.
Other programmers need not care that non-GUI systems will run the code that
uses an ANSI ACCEPT while under Windows the standard File Open dialog is
displayed. Both of those routines are called from the main module which does
things like sanity-checking passed parameters and verifying filenames.

>> # Chris Adams <cadams@acucorp.com>
>> The opinions above are my own and may or may not match those of my employer.
>
>Your employer need not be ashamed for these !!!

Thanks for the kind words. Unfortunately, I think the lawyers sleep easier with
a disclaimer...

----
# Chris Adams <cadams@acucorp.com>
The opinions above are my own and may or may not match those of my employer.
```

So, let's review: 'I coded in a non-standard manner because of:

1) Ignorance
2) Laziness
3) No attention to elegance.'

Mr Moore, there *are* times when one 'codes backwards', or
'counter-intuitively'... consider the situation of a data-entry screen
into which the operator keys in 25 items.  Good Programming Practise has
it that it is a Good Thing to check these fields in *descending* order,
from item 25 to item 01.

Can you see a reason for this?

DD

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL GUI front end

_(thread depth: 11)_

- **From:** paulr@bix.com (paulr)
- **Date:** 1998-09-17T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<6tr5qg$jnc@lotho.delphi.com>`
- **References:** `<6t84he$fsf$1@eros.clara.net> <35FF8467.AAF3554A@IMN.nl> <slrn6vvv1o.iu.cadams@cadams-ntw.acucorp.com> <36062bef.7060004@nntp.ix.netcom.com> <6tr3ic$4u3$1@callisto.clark.net>`

```
Even I can't see a valid reason for that one Doc. 
If you are checking a whole screen at a time, you
have no idea what the user entered last, so the order
you check the items in will have no signifigance,
as long as you check them all. (And of course, 
with relevence to subsequent items. i.e. an 
entry of "Producta" in field one may dynamically
define fields 2 - 25, but that would probably 
be done in a separate action. Display screen
with 1st field active, check entry in first field,
build screen based upon entry, display screen,
accept screen, validate screen.)

If you are entering stuff a line at a time, then 
most folks would check each item as entered, or
when the smallest possible subset of checking 
can occur, in the order they are entered. 

The *only* time I could see a relevance to this is
if you are displaying a screen of information, yet
processing it field by field, which seems, 
inelegant to say the least. :) 

-Paul

In the case you postulate,  you would drop out of the
evalaute- the "2" would NOT be checked. (Someone 
correct me if I am wrong, but at least in the code
I am looking at right now, a jump table was generated
for the EVALUATE statement. I don't have an LRM 
loaded on this system, but I think that only one branch 
of any evalaute is ever going to process during a 
single execution. 

That is also pretty standard behavior across langauges,
in part because it is a very efficient way to generate the
code. 

If you mean you want to put a GOTO there, then that is
just - eh  - poor design, IMNSHO of course. 

If you are saying you want to evalaute a hunk of 
screen real estate displayed or received from a user,
then why not do it as a separate transaction. :) 

Evaluates are funny in some ways, as are multi-part
if statements. I remember thinking I was pretty
bright once, having tweaked a code generator to 
always check the negative branch of an if first
(i.e. it was sign value check) until I ran into
evaluates cousin! :) 

-Paul


-Paul

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL GUI front end

_(thread depth: 15)_

- **From:** riplin@kcbbs.gen.nz (Richard Plinston)
- **Date:** 1998-09-18T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3298260.72676.11464@kcbbs.gen.nz>`
- **References:** `<6tsh0o$cv7@lotho.delphi.com>`

```
In message <<6tsh0o$cv7@lotho.delphi.com>> paulr@bix.com  writes:
> evalaute- the "2" would NOT be checked. (Someone 
> correct me if I am wrong, but at least in the code
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> code. 

In C and derivitives, the switch statement drops through
all subsequent labels.  This is somewhat different than
EVALUATE.  C provides a 'break' to exit the switch.

The C mechanism is to allow multiple labels to have
the same actions:


     switch ( cahracter_in ) {
     case '0':
     case '1':
                this is done when 0 or 1
     case '2':
                this is done when 2 <<and>> after the action
                for 0 and 1
                break;       /* exits the case */
      ...

In Cobol the EVALUATE can capture multiple values by 
concatenated WHENs so:

      EVALUATE character-in
      WHEN "0"
      WHEN "1"
                  this is done for 0 and 1
      WHEN "2"
                  this is only done for 2
      ...

With C you have to take care to add 'break's to stop dropping
through.  With Cobol you have to be careful of leaving
enpty action blocks that drop through to the next WHEN.
```

docdwarf@clark.net wrote:
> Mr Moore, there *are* times when one 'codes backwards', or
> 'counter-intuitively'... consider the situation of a data-entry screen
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> DD

Dammit, DD, I had to wrack my brains for ten full minutes trying to
remember this old trick.  I never used it, as I recall.  Didn't it have
something to do with moving -1 to an attribute byte?

Of course, I could just be going senile...
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL GUI front end

_(thread depth: 12)_

- **From:** "Leif Svalgaard" <leif@ibm.net>
- **Date:** 1998-09-18T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3601f507.0@news1.ibm.net>`
- **References:** `<6t84he$fsf$1@eros.clara.net> <35FF8467.AAF3554A@IMN.nl> <slrn6vvv1o.iu.cadams@cadams-ntw.acucorp.com> <36062bef.7060004@nntp.ix.netcom.com> <6tr3ic$4u3$1@callisto.clark.net> <3601EB04.64A56F75@worldnet.att.net>`

```

Arnold Trembley wrote in message <3601EB04.64A56F75@worldnet.att.net>...
>docdwarf@clark.net wrote:
>> Mr Moore, there *are* times when one 'codes backwards', or
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
>something to do with moving -1 to an attribute byte?


If there is more than one error, but you only want to show one error
message, you want to show to error in the line with the smallest
line number, because people read from the top on down, so
checking backwards, you simply move the error message to
the error field as you detect them, the last one moved will
be the first error on the screen if you check from the bottom up.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL GUI front end

_(thread depth: 13)_

- **From:** riplin@kcbbs.gen.nz (Richard Plinston)
- **Date:** 1998-09-18T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3298260.73092.11696@kcbbs.gen.nz>`
- **References:** `<3601f507.0@news1.ibm.net>`

```
In message <<3601f507.0@news1.ibm.net>> "Leif Svalgaard" <leif@ibm.net> writes:
> 
> If there is more than one error, but you only want to show one error
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> be the first error on the screen if you check from the bottom up.

That is just silly, why not take the more efficient way and
check from the top until an error is found ?

In many cases the checking can't be done backwards, for
example if an early field is, say, a product code, then
this must be checked first and the product read so that
other fields _can_ be checked.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL GUI front end

_(thread depth: 14)_

- **From:** "Donald Tees" <donald@willmack.com>
- **Date:** 1998-09-18T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<6tv960$gm0$1@news.igs.net>`
- **References:** `<3601f507.0@news1.ibm.net> <3298260.73092.11696@kcbbs.gen.nz>`

```

Richard Plinston wrote in message <3298260.73092.11696@kcbbs.gen.nz>...

>That is just silly, why not take the more efficient way and
>check from the top until an error is found ?
>
And then what, GO TO EXIT-PARAGRAPH?  I have always written
code just as stated ... perform check-for-errors until all-errors-corrected.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL GUI front end

_(thread depth: 15)_

- **From:** riplin@kcbbs.gen.nz (Richard Plinston)
- **Date:** 1998-09-19T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3298261.76684.1599@kcbbs.gen.nz>`
- **References:** `<6tv960$gm0$1@news.igs.net>`

```
In message <<6tv960$gm0$1@news.igs.net>> "Donald Tees" <donald@willmack.com> writes:
> Richard Plinston wrote in message <3298260.73092.11696@kcbbs.gen.nz>...
> 
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> code just as stated ... perform check-for-errors until all-errors-corrected.

Do you think that 'GO TO Exit-Paragraph' is the only way to
stop processing ?

You could, for example, add an IF ( NOT all-errors-corrected )
to each check so that the check is not done if an earlier
one found an error (and thus set the flag to indicate an
error found).

There are many other ways of short-circuiting, including
nesting IF .. ELSEs right accross the page    ;-)

But you didn't answer my real objection to 'doing it from
the bottom' which was that often it is necessary to have
earlier fields correct (such as a valid product or customer)
before other fields can be made sense of.

In fact I dislike the 'ACCEPT all-fields and then validate'
mechanism, I process each field in turn.  This gives much
better feed-back to the user and makes corrections easier.

But then some systems, such as mainframe, can't allow 
this.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL GUI front end

_(thread depth: 16)_

- **From:** "Donald Tees" <donald@willmack.com>
- **Date:** 1998-09-19T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<6u1gdf$nes$1@news.igs.net>`
- **References:** `<6tv960$gm0$1@news.igs.net> <3298261.76684.1599@kcbbs.gen.nz>`

```

Richard Plinston wrote in message <3298261.76684.1599@kcbbs.gen.nz>...

>You could, for example, add an IF ( NOT all-errors-corrected )
>to each check so that the check is not done if an earlier
>one found an error (and thus set the flag to indicate an
>error found).


<wait a minute>*That* is exactly what is being argued in favour of,
and what you are disagreeing with! IF you are going to use an "IF"
in that fashion, THEN the order of the "IF" is important. The construct
can go from top to bottom using an "if error then procedure else"
construct, or from bottom to top using an "if ok then procedure else"
construct.

Sometimes one is most effective, and sometimes the other is.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL GUI front end

_(thread depth: 15)_

- **From:** paulr@bix.com (paulr)
- **Date:** 1998-09-19T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<6u0fjs$974@lotho.delphi.com>`
- **References:** `<3601f507.0@news1.ibm.net> <3298260.73092.11696@kcbbs.gen.nz> <6tv960$gm0$1@news.igs.net>`

```
Well, I'm not at all sure what you guys are talking about
either. Perhpas because I decompose my progams to a point
where error checking, display, and business logic is all
a separate function. I also don't overconcern myself 
with saving cycles; even PC's have cycles to burn these days.
Especially in some so resource light as error checking a 
screen entry. Even on interactive systems with large numbers
of users, the cycles used to handle entry error checking are
not very signifigant. Not signifigant at all in comparison
to eh cycels spent doing the real work, if you get my drift. 

I find that I tend to code screens and input in general with the 
most critical information - to the program - first. 

After a first cut on a screen, I go back and automate / protect
any data entry I can, then reorganize for humans. <grin> Given 
that, can you see why checking from the bottom makes little
or no sense? 

In *screen* based I/O, I flag all the errors anyway; changing the
color of the input screen, flagging it with some other visual cue,
or whatever the output media allows me to do. So order dependancy
is pretty much a non-issue. 

And I would not allow the order I find errors in to dictate the 
order I present them to the user in anyway. :) 

-Paul


Donald Tees (donald@willmack.com) wrote:

: Richard Plinston wrote in message <3298260.73092.11696@kcbbs.gen.nz>...

: >That is just silly, why not take the more efficient way and
: >check from the top until an error is found ?
: >
: And then what, GO TO EXIT-PARAGRAPH?  I have always written
: code just as stated ... perform check-for-errors until all-errors-corrected.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL GUI front end

_(thread depth: 16)_

- **From:** "Donald Tees" <donald@willmack.com>
- **Date:** 1998-09-19T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<6u0i6d$gbu$1@news.igs.net>`
- **References:** `<3601f507.0@news1.ibm.net> <3298260.73092.11696@kcbbs.gen.nz> <6tv960$gm0$1@news.igs.net> <6u0fjs$974@lotho.delphi.com>`

```

paulr wrote in message <6u0fjs$974@lotho.delphi.com>...

>After a first cut on a screen, I go back and automate / protect
>any data entry I can, then reorganize for humans. <grin> Given
>that, can you see why checking from the bottom makes little
>or no sense?


What you are forgetting is that the most efficient way for the program
is also the most efficient way for the programmer.  Doing something
efficiently does not just save machine cycles.  It also saves hours of
writing and debugging code.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL GUI front end

_(thread depth: 17)_

- **From:** paulr@bix.com (paulr)
- **Date:** 1998-09-19T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<6u0tco$dsv@lotho.delphi.com>`

I think it is inefficient to do something in a way that in 
contra-intuitive to begin with. To follow up, I tend to 
depend upon the compiler to optimize code, which is definately
not the way the world used to work, but is the way it always
should have. And today's compilers are good at optimization! :) 

-Paul
