11. Is there a COBOL to C converter ?
Asking  this  question anywhere appears to generate much  general
flaming  and  general  language  wars  and  very  little   useful
information.

No  such beast is listed in the free compilers FAQ, but an ad has
appears  in  the US publication "Programmer's Shop  Catalog"  for
COBOL to C (and PL/I to C) translators. Contact :
Micro-Processor Services,
92 Stone Hurst Lane,
Dix Hills, NY 11746
  Tel: (516) 499 4461
  Fax: (516)- 499-4727
E-Mail: info@mpsinc.com, mpsinc@netusa.net
www: http://www.mpsinc.com
http://www.netusa.net/~mpsinc
A toolset for conversion from COBOL to several other languages is
available.  A  tool  first produces structured  diagrams  (Nassi-
Shneiderman)  from existing source files. Structural  errors  are
identified  and you can edit to correct them. Another tool  takes
those  same  diagrams and produces source code in one of  several
languages  (COBOL,  C,  Ada,  Basic, Clipper,  dBaseIV,  Fortran,
Modula 2, Natural, PL/1, etc.)
The  toolset  is called XperCase by Siemens, and is available  in
the US from:
Boston Technical Distribution Corp.
3 Center Plaza, Suite 440
Boston, MA 02108
  Tel: (617) 248-8989
  Fax: (617) 248-8986
Laurent Sabarthez contributed :
  "Some years ago I was Project Leader on a software project
  termed COBTOC (COBol TO C translation). The company is by
  now out of business, but the rights on this product were
  purchased by NSI (Network Solutions Inc., Herndon, VA, USA -
  Emitt McHenry was Chairman).
  COBTOC is actually a translator generator. It can produce a
  specialized translator for any reasonable COBOL dialect,
  given a dialect description very close to the usual syntax
  notation one can find into any COBOL Reference Manual.
  "semantics" peculiarities are also described in this way.
  Once a translator has been produced in this way, a source
  management module allows automated translation of the COBOL
  source modules. A run-time library is also automatically
  produced as a by-product of the translator.
  The COBTOC user gets a set of C files, each being the
  translation of a corresponding COBOL file. You can get K&R
  C, ANSI C, or common variants like Turbo C. The overall
  structure of the COBOL program is preserved upon
  translation. Identifiers are straightforward transformations
  of COBOL names. Paragraph structure and flow control are
  also preserved, like all name space properties attached to
  I/O and file management.
  The C files are compiled and linked with the run-time
  library, which supports data handling, edition, arithmetic,
  direct I/O, file I/O and transaction management (e.g. CICS).
  Executables are intended to run on any platform supporting
  POSIX C compiling and standard library linkage.
  COBTOC was left by my co-workers and me in an alpha release
  state, mid 1993.
  I don't know the end of the story, but NSI should provide
  more up-to-date information about it.
  There is also a project running to create a COBOL to C
  converter (possibly COBOL to C++ ?) available under the GNU
  license. "
  Another project that can be viewed as either a COBOL to C
  converter or as a compiler, is the "CobCy Project"
For    more    details,    see    the    CobCy    homepage     at
http://www.netsis.it/~asantini/cobcy/.

12. COBOL code generators
12.1 CA-Telon and CA-Telon PWS (Programmable Workstation
CA-Telon and CA-Telon PWS (Programmable Workstation) are complete
solutions for designing, generating and maintaining your mission-
critical  COBOL and PL/1 applications. High-level, non-procedural
design  and prototyping, combined with automatic code generation,
result  in  higher  productivity, consistent standards  and  good
programming  practices. With CA-Telon PWS, you can  offload  your
application  development projects from the mainframe and  provide
your   programmers   with  a  complete  application   development
environment on the workstation.
The CA-Telon Design Facility (TDF) provides a "fill-in-the-blank"
approach  for  designing online and batch programs. An  automated
prototyping facility assists in the analysis/design phases of the
life  cycle. CA-Telon generates structured portable  code  for  a
multitude  of target environments and DBMSs. Available for:  MVS,
DOS, OS/2

12.3 ETK
ETK (Easy ToolKit) was developed by SEMA Group in Belgium. It  is
now marketed by TOSC International, Inc :
TOSC International, Inc.
3900 Essex Lane, suite 250
Houston, TX 77027
  Tel: 713 961 1201
  Fax: 713 961 4989
Contact Ted Langlie. TOSC will also soon be on the Internet.
ETK   works   with  a  repository  where  the  programmer/analyst
describes  the  system to be generated in terms of data  domains,
elements,  tables,  relationships,  business  rules,   and   data
mappings.  From  this description ETK generates  complete  COBOL-
based applications that will run on many platforms.

13. COBOL Tools
This  section  documents  some  of  the  add-on  tools  that  are
available  for use with COBOL compilers. Further submissions  are
welcomed,  but  please try to keep them as  free  from  marketing
"hype" as possible.
Note: This section seems to be entirely devoted to tools that run
in  or  are  targeted  at  the PC and/or  Unix  world.  Mainframe
additions are welcome, but will probably be a while before I  can
add them.

13.1 creating GUI's
13.1.1 VanGui for RM/COBOL.
VanGui  consists  of two major components: a design  tool  and  a
runtime  system.  The design tool is a Windows application  which
provides  COBOL developers with the capability to define windows,
populate  those windows with standard Windows and  VBX  controls,
adjust  the properties of those controls and attach COBOL  event-
handling logic to their events.
The  VanGui  Runtime  is  a  Windows .DLL  that  manages  Windows
messages, provides runtime support for the controls, and provides
a COBOL interface to the Windows API.
For more information on VanGui, see 13.1.1 VanGui for RM/COBOL
http://www.liant.com/products/language/rm/ib.html.
For another GUI tool from Liant, see 5.3.4 RM/Panels

13.1.2 From Acucorp
AcuBench

AcuBench  is an integrated development environment for ACUCOBOL.-
GT. Available for the Windows 95 and
Windows    NT   operating   systems,   AcuBench   combines    the
internationally acclaimed ACUCOBOL-GT
(Graphical Technology) compiler and runtime system with a set  of
graphically-based,  GT-optimized  WYSIWYG  screen  Painter,   and
language sensitive source Code Editor.

ACUCOBOL�-GT

ACUCOBOL�-GT enables COBOL programmers to implement  and  deliver
full-featured GUI COBOL
applications on any of over 600 platforms, with full object  code
portability using a single set of COBOL
source   code.  Programmers  can  create  graphical  applications
including floating windows, graphical
controls  (such  as  entry fields, frames,  radio  buttons,  push
buttons, and labels), menu bars, bitmaps, and toolbars
using COBOL extensions that are consistent with traditional COBOL
syntax.

13.1.3 Flexus COBOL spII
COBOL spII allows the COBOL programmer to create GUI or character
mode  screens  using ANSI standard COBOL CALL  USING  statements.
COBOL  spII screen definitions and source programs are 100% COBOL
compiler independent, 100% operating system independent and  100%
text mode to GUI mode independent.
Automatic   screen   conversion  tools   for   many   proprietary
environments  are also available from Flexus. These automate  the
task  of converting screen definitions from proprietary character
mode screens to GUI screen definitions.

13.1.4 CA-Visual Realia
[From CA's Web site:]
CA-Visual  Realia  is a complete Windows development  environment
that  provides COBOL programmers with an easy-to-use set of tools
for creating mission-critical GUI client/server applications. CA-
Visual  Realia  allows you to create Windows  applications  using
COBOL  for  the  business  logic  and  GUI  tools  for  the  user
interface.  CA-Visual Realia combines tools designed for  Windows
user   interface   development  with   powerful   COBOL   program
development tools. Creating a program's user interface can be  as
easy  as  dragging  controls onto forms, and then  modifying  the
control's properties.

13.1.5 Fujitsu PowerCOBOL
PowerCOBOL   is  a  visual,  object  oriented,  enterprise   wide
client/server Rapid Application Development(RAD) tool  for  COBOL
programmers.   The  PowerCOBOL  development  environment   allows
programmers to use COBOL as the GUI scripting language  to  build
scaleable  and maintainable state-of-the-art COBOL-based  Windows
two-tier  and  three-tier client/server and Internet applications
with data access to local and remote databases.

13.2 Sinc Inc. products
13.2.1 FlexGen 4GL Rapid Application Development Environment
A  repository-based, data dictionary-driven  tool  set  and  code
generator. It supports and generates code for RM/COBOL,  Acucorp,
Micro Focus COBOL, Realia (DOS Only), mbp (DOS Only) and runs and
deploys under DOS, Windows, Windows 95, NT, many flavors of UNIX,
VAX/VMS and Open VMS.

13.2.2 Legacy Liberator COBOL Migration Toolset
For   rehosting  proprietary  COBOLs,  converting  them  to  open
systems,  Acucorp, RM/COBOL or MF COBOL. User interfaces  can  be
converted  to  FlexGen user interfaces including the  ability  to
upgrade them to GUI.

13.2.3 Easy Query
A point-and-shoot report writer and query tool.

13.2.4 ODBC Access Tools
Allows Windows ODBC-enabled applications to access COBOL data.

13.3 Siber Systems
13.3.1 CobolTransformer
CobolTransformer  (SCT) is a next generation Cobol  reengineering
and  parsing toolkit (SDK). By using CobolTransformer components,
developers  are  able  to develop Cobol-transforming  (or  Cobol-
analyzing) product or project faster and the resulting tools  are
of much higher quality.

CobolTransformer is a library with API in C++ that includes:
  �    High quality Cobol Parser that parses 12 most popular Cobol
     dialects and that has tremendous error recovery capability.
  �     C++ library that client uses to browse and transform  the
     tree-based Internal Representation of Cobol programs. Definition-
     Use links attached to the Program Tree effectively make it a
     general case Graph.
  �     PrettyPrinter that transforms our Internal Representation
     back into beautifully indented
     human-readable Cobol program.

13.3.2 Cbl-Beau
CobolBeautifier does the following things:
  a.   Beautifully indents your Cobol program.
b.   Renumbers paragraph-names, section-names, data-names
c.   attach an increasing or decreasing prefix or suffix to all
paragraph-names and/or section-names and/or data-names in your
Cobol program. (Based on CobolTransformer.)
A   free   version  is  available  from  their   Web   site   at:
http://www.siber.com/sct/
The   Commercial   version  will  allow  to  specify   your   own
indentation/pretty-printing rules, Program  you  style  rules  in
CobolTransformer and make all programs to comply automatically.
To be available soon.

13.3.3 Mf2Fsc
Mf2Fsc is a Micro Focus COBOL to Fujitsu COBOL converter.
It   converts  Micro  Focus  Cobol-85  to  Fujitsu  Cobol-85  and
beautifies the program at the same time.
A   free   prototype  is  available  from  their  web  site   at:
http://www.siber.com/sct/
A commercial version will be available soon.

!3.4 What about Year 2000 Tools?

13.4.1 CA-Fix/2000�
CA-Fix/2000,   a   mainframe-based  application-at-a-time   COBOL
remediation  tool,  performs rapid automated  date  analysis  and
correction of your COBOL source code to provide the fastest  path
to  Year  2000  compliance. CA-Fix/2000 eliminates  the  tedious,
error-prone  tasks of finding and correcting the date  fields  in
your programs. Because it can correct an entire application in  a
single step, CA-Fix/2000 helps ensure consistent corrections even
when  date  fields  are  passed from one program  to  another  or
records  are  redefined.  CA-Fix/2000 can  determine  which  date
fields  are  at  risk  for failing to handle the  century  change
correctly  and corrects only these at-risk fields,  limiting  the
impact on programs and reducing testing requirements.

13.5 Misc. tools

13.5.1 Flexus WinPrint
COBOL  WinPrint allows the COBOL programmer to make ANSI standard
COBOL CALL USING statements to completely control and communicate
with   the   Windows  Print  Manager.  Forms  may   be   designed
interactively  with the Forms Editor to include bitmaps,  special
fonts,  colors and many other modern features. These reports  may
be viewed on the screen prior to printing or sent directly to the
Print Manager.

17. What about the Y2K (Millenium) Issue?
  �    Yes, the year 2000 IS a leap year
  �     Yes, the millenium really starts on January 1, 2001 - but
     the software "bug" refers to January 1, 2000 (and several other
     dates)

17.1 Where can I get information about the Y2K problem?
The  Year 2000 Information Center home page on the World Wide Web
is at
     http://www.year2000.com.
The Information Center has a countdown clock, articles on various
aspects  of the problem, vendor information, this FAQ, and  links
to related information.
There is an Internet mailing list operated by Peter de Jager  for
discussions  of year 2000 computer problems. The  list  has  over
1200  members,  and  gets an average of  about  25  messages  per
business day. This is a moderated mailing list managed by a  paid
administrator and fully supported by its members on a "shareware"
basis.  Each member is asked to pay a subscription fee of US  $50
yearly. (You may contribute more if you wish.)
New subscribers get a 30-day free trial.
To  subscribe, select the "Year 2000 Announcement List" link from
the    Year    2000    Information   Center    home    page    at
http://www.year2000.com. On the subscription form, select the Yes
adio  button  for the Discussion List. You can also subscribe  by
sending e-mail to 2k@tor.hookup.net with
     SUBSCRIBE
in  the  subject line of the message. Subscriptions are processed
manually,  so  please  be  patient. The list  can  optionally  be
received  in digest form instead of individual messages. Invoices
and  receipts are available if needed. Details will be sent  when
you subscribe.
The  Year  2000 Forum on CompuServe has discussions of  all  year
2000 issues, and a collection of files, including this FAQ. There
are over 1000 members. To reach the forum, GO YEAR2000.
The  Usenet  newsgroup comp.software.year-2000  is  dedicated  to
discussions of year 2000 computer issues.
The  current  version  of this Year 2000 FAQ  is  available  from
several web sites, an FTP site, and on CompuServe. It is in ASCII
text form at all these sites.
On the Year 2000 Information Center home page at
http://www.year2000.com,
select  the  "Year 2000 Archive" link. The FAQ is the  last  item
listed on the Archive page

18. What can/should I post in the COBOL newsgroups?
Both comp.lang.cobol and alt.cobol are UN-moderated newsgroups.
This means that there is no controlling authority regulating  the
content  of the posts in those groups. Anyone is able  (but  most
assuredly  not welcome) to post anything, relevant  to  COBOL  or
not.   Off-topic  posts  (and  particularly  prolonged  off-topic
threads)are  discouraged, but it is impossible to  prevent  them.
Different  readers of these newsgroups have different  thresholds
for  tolerating off-topic comments. The more that you  post,  the
more  likely  it  is  that  some  readers  will  simply  apply  a
"killfile"  to all of your posts - often after sending  you  (and
possibly the newsgroup) rather pointed comments on your use/abuse
of the newsgroup.
You  should, therefore, apply the following guidelines that  will
help  you  get the most out of both your viewing and postings  at
these sites.
  �     Comp.lang.cobol is the preferred site to use - especially
     for technical issues related to COBOL.
  �    Although threads do stray from the topics, the more targeted
     your inquiry is - and the more closely it relates to COBOL, the
     more likely you are to receive a useful and prompt response. (If
     you  know that you are leaving the current subject, consider
     changing the subject line in your post.)
  �     When asking about a specific structure, error message, or
     situation, it is always best to specify both the compiler that
     you are using and the operating system where you are working.
     (What you may think is a general COBOL question may actually be
     very specific to your current environment.)

Note:  For  those  who  are interested,  the  "charter"  for  the
comp.lang.cobol newsgroup can be found at:
ftp//ftp.uu.net/usenet/news.announce.newgroups/comp/
comp.lang.cobol

18.1 Can I get help with homework via the newsgroups?
Yes,  you  can get help with your homework via these  newsgroups.
HOWEVER, that does mean that you will receive help - you will NOT
find many participants who will be very happy if you ask them  to
do  your  homework FOR YOU. Some hints that you  should  consider
when drafting your post for assistance with homework include:
  �     Make it clear from the beginning that you are asking  for
     homework help. (Most of the newsgroup participants are very good
     at sniffing out those who try and pose homework questions as
     "business need" questions - and they are not very polite  in
     replying to such questions).
  �     Make certain that you specify what compiler and operating
     system  your  homework is targeted at.  Solutions  may  vary
     significantly based on this. (You might also want to include what
     text book you are using.)
  �     The  more information that you can give that demonstrates
     that you really have tried to solve the problem yourself (using
     your text book, class material/presentations, lab assistance,
     etc), the more likely it is that you will get useful and friendly
     responses. If you let us know what you have found on your own and
     why you are still uncertain or confused, you will usually get
     helpful responses; if it looks like you are asking us questions
     without trying to solve it yourself, you are likely to get very
     pointed replies.

NOTE:  if you are looking for a COBOL tutor, (and don't  want  to
see  the  "Do  your own homework" notes that occur in  the  COBOL
newsgroups), you might want to try,
     http://www.tutoraid.org/

18.2 Can I post job openings in the newsgroups and if so what
should I include?
These newsgroups are an excellent place for posting job openings.
Some  participants (those with jobs) often wish that less of  the
bandwidth  was  spent on job postings, but,  for  those  who  are
looking for positions/contracts, these postings are quite useful.
There  are, however, some guidelines that you should follow  when
posting  positions  - unless you like getting  abusive  and  non-
responsive replies to your postings.
  �     Always, ALWAYS, include rates, a range of rates, or  your
     best information on what rates are available. Phrases like "based
     on experience" and "competitive" are bound to receive replies
     questioning your motives. The assumption is that ALL such posts
     are "trolling" for resumes or rates and not serious job searches.
  �     Try  to give the best summary of job location and desired
     expertise in the subject line of your posting. This will assist
     you  in  attracting  readers that are  actually  potentially
     interested in your openings.
  �     The  newsgroups  are international  in  nature,  but  are
     dominated by Americans. If you are posting a position outside the
     US,  please make it clear whether foreigners are or are  not
     welcome to apply - and if so what visas and other paperwork is
     required If you are posting for a job with specific citizenship
     requirements, please make that clear from the start.
  �     In general, the more information that you provide in your
     postings, the better the match will be from those who reply. If,
     however, you are "attaching" a job description, please  make
     certain that you make it clear what type of document-reader is
     required to process it AND make certain that you have  virus
     scanned the document before you post it.

NOTE:  For  those looking for jobs, you might want to  check  out
Dice at
     http://www.dice.com

Appendix A. Changes in recent revisions
This  appendix  documents the major changes  that  were  made  to
create the various (recent) versions of this document.
Appendix A.1 - Changes to create Version 2.0
Huge  numbers of changes! Version numbering jumped from 1.xxx  to
2.0 to reflect this.
FAQ availability details.
Added section on Commercial COBOL Products.
Removed 'what happened to Realia?' section.
Removed references to Wang, as I can find no information  on  any
Wang COBOL Products.
Checked all URLs, and updated/added as appropriate.
Removed reference to IBM 'COBOL Products WWW site'.
Added  entries  in  'GUI Tools' section for  Micro  Focus  Dialog
System and Net Express, Flexus COBOL spII, and CA-Visual Realia.
Removed  ADS/Online from 'Code Generators' section as  it's  not,
apparently.  Also noted that Cullinet have been acquired  by  CA,
who now sell the Telon product.

Appendix A.2 - Changes to create Version 2.05
  �    Significant reformatting
  �    Updated OO COBOL Section
  �    Add URL for IBM COBOL Web site
  �    Added information on "COBOL for Dummies"
  �    Added initial Y2K section
  �    Added information on using the newsgroups
  �    Added information on PCYACC

Appendix A.3 - Changes to create Version 2.08
  �    Started adding Logos
  �    Removed the detailed list of FAQ contributors
  �    Fixed erroneous Standards information
  �    Corrected information about Liant (who acquired RM)
  �    Added information on Amiga freeware compiler
  �    Added information on Siber tools
  �    Corrected CA company and product information
  �     Added information on where to turn for information not in
     the FAQ
  �    Rephrased the information on what can/should be put in the
     newsgroups.
  �    Revised the information on the CobCy project/product
  �    Updated Micro Focus information
  �    Added URL for "Dice" for those looking for COBOL jobs.
  �    Added information on getting a "COBOL Tutor"

Appendix A.4 - Changes to create Version 2.0x
  �    Added and corrected information on Fujitsu
  �    Re-instated the information on obtaining a copy of the draft
     COBOL Standard.
```

<a id="t-049c3075f6"></a>
## Thread: Length of Cobol word 30->31 — 9 msgs

### Length of Cobol word 30->31

- **From:** "Leif Svalgaard" <leif@ibm.net>
- **Date:** 1998-09-12T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<35fac493.0@news1.ibm.net>`

```
Hi all,

I just saw that the next standard has increased the length of a Cobol
word from 30 to 31 characters. Does anybody know the rationale for
this silly thing (except that it makes DEC-Cobol compliant with the
standard)? If the length should be increased, make it substantial
e.g. to 100 characters.
```

#### ↳ Re: Length of Cobol word 30->31

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1998-09-12T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<6tej7c$6t1@dfw-ixnews5.ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<35fac493.0@news1.ibm.net>`

```

Leif Svalgaard wrote in message <35fac493.0@news1.ibm.net>...
>Hi all,
>
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>

I remember that it happened.
   I remember discussion about changing maximum digits from 18 to 31
               (and why this was done)
    but for the life of me, I can't remember why the dataname length was
changed.

If you know either the C or SQL ANSI/ISO Standards, do those allow their
equivalents of data-names to be 31-characters?  That is my only guess for a
"rationale".  If you don't get a good reply here, you might send a query to
    das@microfocus.com
and Don (the Chair of J4) should be able to give you his recollection.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Length of Cobol word 30->31

- **From:** "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@aiservices.com>
- **Date:** 1998-09-13T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<HCQK1.308$pB3.1508111@news1.atlantic.net>`
- **References:** `<35fac493.0@news1.ibm.net> <6tej7c$6t1@dfw-ixnews5.ix.netcom.com>`

```

William M. Klein wrote in message <6tej7c$6t1@dfw-ixnews5.ix.netcom.com>...
>
>Leif Svalgaard wrote in message <35fac493.0@news1.ibm.net>...
…[22 more quoted lines elided]…
>
From the proposed C standard:

Implementation limits

6   The implementation shall treat at least the first 63 characters
      of an internal name (a macro name or an identifier that does
      not have external linkage) as significant. The implementation
      may further restrict the significance of an external name (an
      identifier that has external linkage) to 31 characters. In both
      external and internal names, lower-case and upper-case
      letters are different. The number of significant characters in
      an identifier is implementation-defined.

-------------------------------
Rick Smith
e-mail: < ricksmith@aiservices.com >
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Length of Cobol word 30->31

- **From:** "COBOL Frog (Huib Klink)" <H.Klink@IMN.nl>
- **Date:** 1998-09-15T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<35FE7FAA.C50F64F0@IMN.nl>`
- **References:** `<35fac493.0@news1.ibm.net> <6tej7c$6t1@dfw-ixnews5.ix.netcom.com> <HCQK1.308$pB3.1508111@news1.atlantic.net>`

```
Wait until someone invents and make a success of a computer with 3-state bits.
That will put an end to those silly power-of-two-minus-one limits that are
choosen. And that even in the standard for languages, looks like if the standard
itself is already platform-dependent!

A serious complaint: why such strange limited limits. Take for example that
figure of 64 for datanames (nothing against C, other examples in other languages
possible). That needs a 6 bit binary variable in the compiler-program. Why not
limit it at 32767 so nobody would ever feel short-handed. Now-a-days the limit
you sometimes need to know/remember as a programmer are those weird powers of 2
which just don't stay in my brains.

Sure there are some limits that will count now and then, but why things like
length of datanames ??? Not in modern compilers/languages/standards. This is the
world of 32+ Megabytes, doubling every 18 months ... why do we still think so
small where it is not needed...

The Frog
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ 3-state bits (was Re: Length of Cobol word 30->31)

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** Charles F Hankel <charles@hankel.mersinet.co.uk>
- **Date:** 1998-09-21T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<360589F5.79879405@hankel.mersinet.co.uk>`
- **References:** `<35fac493.0@news1.ibm.net> <6tej7c$6t1@dfw-ixnews5.ix.netcom.com> <HCQK1.308$pB3.1508111@news1.atlantic.net> <35FE7FAA.C50F64F0@IMN.nl>`

```
COBOL Frog (Huib Klink) wrote:
> 
> Wait until someone invents and make a success of a computer with 3-state bits.

I keep hearing things about 3-state bits so, what are the three states? 
No, let me guess, under this hardened exterior that I show the world,
the imagination's bubbling.

On, off and maybe?
On, off and dunno?
On, off and falling?
On, off and you-tell-me?
On, off and i-will-be-your-friend-for-life?
On, off and switch?-what-switch? ?
On, off and bad-acid?
On, off and pass-me-a-beer-while-I-think-about-it?
Sober, drunk and dead?
```

>>I keep hearing things about 3-state bits so, what are the three states?
>No, let me guess, under this hardened exterior that I show the world,

and results in an error, at least in theory.

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Fujitsu V3

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "Fujitsu Software" <cobol@adtools.com>
- **Date:** 1998-09-18T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<6tuif4$eij$1@news.fujitsu.com>`
- **References:** `<6te1cs$44m$1@news02.btx.dtag.de> <6tin4k$rpq$1@news00.btx.dtag.de> <6tr90u$2q$1@news02.btx.dtag.de> <6tsveg$dlm$1@news00.btx.dtag.de>`

```
Paul,

The following call statement provides access to Btrieve from Fujitsu COBOL
32-bit Windows 95/98/NT.  Remember to link with WBTRV32.LIB


*-----------------------------------------------------------------
 CALL-BTRIEVE.

     CALL "BTRCALL" WITH STDCALL USING
                              BY VALUE BTRIEVE-OPERATION-CODE
                          BY REFERENCE BTRIEVE-POSITION-BLOCK
                          BY REFERENCE BTRIEVE-DATA-BUFFER
                          BY REFERENCE BTRIEVE-DATA-BUFFER-LENGTH
                          BY REFERENCE BTRIEVE-FILE-NAME
                                     BY VALUE BTRIEVE-KEY-NUMBER
                          BY REFERENCE BTRIEVE-STATUS-CODE
     END-CALL.

Fujitsu Software Corporation
Developer Tools Group
Phone: (408) 428-0500
FAX:    (408) 428-0600
Email:  cobol@adtools.com
Web:   http://www.adtools.com


Andreas Strzoda wrote in message <6tsveg$dlm$1@news00.btx.dtag.de>...
>Paul Schmitteck schrieb:
>
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

#### ↳ Re: Which is the Cobol for PC most popular?

- **From:** gvwmoore@ix.netcom.com (G Moore)
- **Date:** 1998-09-13T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<35fc1954.10543238@nntp.ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<6tcggc$b97$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>`

```
hmm. well, i started cobol on rm-cobol. then i moved to microfocus.
however, the amount i paid for the rm version included a book with it,
and it was required by the professor. i am sure had i paid full price,
like i did for the microfocus one, it would have been a better
version. oh well, here's my two cents:

its not the compiler which matters. you decide what project u want
to do, and write the code. one thing though. i didn't get the choice
of what OS to run my compiler on. the prof gave me certain projects,
to run under a certain OS (dos). dos allows you to print out text. i
think windows does too. if i was a prof, i'd get a windows compiler,
for 2 reasons:
a> easier to navigate commands (wysiwyg)
b> use of other applications

b comes into play when you want to look at your output, and
your compiler interrupts alt, ctrl or other keys which u use
to task switch.

the drawback is c
c>printing with fonts might be greater/less than 
80 chars/line 25 lines/page

gvwmoore@ix.spam.netcom.com to reply remove the spam
```

## Thread: Web Interface for Cobol Programs — 4 msgs

_Topics: [`Web, XML, modern integration`](topics.md#web)_

### Web Interface for Cobol Programs

- **From:** "Leif Svalgaard" <leif@ibm.net>
- **Date:** 1998-09-12T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<35fac624.0@news1.ibm.net>`

```
Hi All,

I the past few weeks I have been experimenting with
using a standard Web browser as the interface to
interactive Cobol programs. In these days of "thin clients"
this is the ultimate thin client, as NO piece of the
application need be on the client machine.

You can download a demo of this from http://www.etk.com.
The demo allows you to provide a description of one of
your files as a copybook. From this description, the
Web-enabled Cobol program program is generated
in typically 30 seconds.

Try it and let me know how it can be improved.
All ideas are welcome. Thanks.
```

#### ↳ Re: Web Interface for Cobol Programs

- **From:** paulr@bix.com (paulr)
- **Date:** 1998-09-12T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<6tev00$ndc@lotho.delphi.com>`
- **References:** `<35fac624.0@news1.ibm.net>`

```
Oh for Pete's sake.... 
I've been busting my rear end to do just about
exactly this for the last couple weeks. Made a good
deal of progress too. 

What platforms have you tested on, and what compilers? 

Yours,
-Paul

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Web Interface for Cobol Programs

- **From:** "Leif Svalgaard" <leif@ibm.net>
- **Date:** 1998-09-14T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<35fd15e5.0@news1.ibm.net>`
- **References:** `<35fac624.0@news1.ibm.net> <6tev00$ndc@lotho.delphi.com>`

```

paulr wrote in message <6tev00$ndc@lotho.delphi.com>...
>Oh for Pete's sake....
>I've been busting my rear end to do just about
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>


I have only tested it on PC Win95 Fujitsu V3.10, but
the code is completely portable and is guaranteed to
work on any platform and with any (Cobol) compiler.

The main problem is communication between the
WebServer and the Cobol program. Here I use
the LRWP protocol for Xitami from iMatix. (www.imatix.com)

The communication piece is isolated in a
single subroutine and must be changed for
other protocols.

>
>Leif Svalgaard (leif@ibm.net) wrote:
…[17 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

#### ↳ Re: Web Interface for Cobol Programs

- **From:** paulr@bix.com (paulr)
- **Date:** 1998-09-13T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<6tfang$quc@lotho.delphi.com>`
- **References:** `<35fac624.0@news1.ibm.net>`

```

Hi Leif -
  That stuff looks pretty darn good. I'm impressed. 
We have been able to do pretty much exactly what you
have done, and ran into some troubles with different
web servers and compilers. 

  Using COBOL-CGI with Fujitsu drives me slightly nuts;
sometimes it works and sometimes it doesn't. :) It doesn't
like Domnio GO webservers very well, and has trouble
with IIS version 4 (well, we only have Fuji version 3 here,
perhaps versin 4 works better.) 
 
  AcuCOBOL seems to have some trouble linking to STDIN and
STDOUT under Windows NT, and doesn't work at all with the
Xi* webserver. In fact, it doesn't work well at all with
IIS 4, Domnio, or Netscape, probably due to having 
to call the runtime. We loose connectivity to STDIN and
STDOUT. Interstingly enough, with old servers, AcuCOBOL
works flawlessly. 

  We finally settled on IBM Visual AGE COBOL as the development
platform and Domino GO as the server. This was deliberatly 
choosen to allow ports to AS/400 and S/390. It has a few
problems talking to STDIN and STDOUT under Windows NT as well,
but they were fairly easily solved by using a C wrapper in a
cmd script. I chatted with a chap at the Santa Theresa labs
and it those minor troubles may have a relatively easy fix.
Won't know till they have time to check it out. 

  Now given all the above, we found that just putting out
text emulation screens didn't really appeal to users;
besides that would have been TOO easy - we can do that 
from CICS. Without any effort at all.

  But the CICS model is what we are implementing; we 
found that small screens with discrete peices of data
entry on them are what we have to present to the users.
And that means tracking the transaction state between
web pages. Sheebooppa! That isn't EASY. 

  Some of the mechanisms we have explored include sending
hidden fields from page to page, writing partial data,
and writing to a transaction state data file. The last
path is the path we are going to implement. However, 
we haven't been able to sucessfully use LRW yet. On the
other hand, once the IBM runtime loads under NT, we 
get very FAST response from CGI programs. Under simulated
load, a 300 mhz Intel box with 64megs of RAM and plent
of disk space managed to handle 1500 web transactions per
minute with no problems. I didn't push it to the limit,
but I would not be surprised to see that figure 
double with a little optimization. Besides, we can always
cluster the darn things, and CICS will be inserted 
between the web and the database/VSAM files or whatever
data storage is eventually decided upon. It will almost
certianly be a mixture of DB2 and VSAM. 

 The other bugaboos we are battleing with include
"silly" stuff, such as dealing with data validation. 
A small example; on screen or in a GUI I would normally
break up a telephone number into an areacode, an exchange,
and an extension. Three simple autolinked fields on a
screen. On the web, I can't do that because the darn field
sizes tend to change with some whim of browser. One
annoying problem is I often get 15 charcter wide fields
on screen when I want 3 chars. The fields MAXLENGTH
paramter works, and only allows 3 characters in, but
doggon it - it's ugly! And there is no way I know of
in the browser to force numeric entry, so I am 
forced to code login in the COBOL program serving that 
form or part of that form to redisplay it with 
data errors. 

 We also found we had to use templates, if for no
other reason to avoid massive numbers of "display"
statements in the COBOL code. :) Actually, we are 
workign on parameter substitution in the template
files; for example "@username@@@@@@" will be replaced
by the "username" field if embedded in a line, 
and by a series of user information lines if found on
a line by itself. "@HIDDENFIELDS@@@" on a line by itself
inserts all the transactional information we want to
pass along to the next stage. (Usually a key and the transaction
id for the transaction file.) 

 I'm really pleased to see other people workign along these
lines; COBOL is pretty ideally suited to use with the WEB,
and FAR underratted. :) 

Yours,
-Paul


Leif Svalgaard (leif@ibm.net) wrote:
: Hi All,

: I the past few weeks I have been experimenting with
: using a standard Web browser as the interface to
: interactive Cobol programs. In these days of "thin clients"

: You can download a demo of this from http://www.etk.com.
: The demo allows you to provide a description of one of
: your files as a copybook. From this description, the
: Web-enabled Cobol program program is generated
: in typically 30 seconds.

: Try it and let me know how it can be improved.
: All ideas are welcome. Thanks.

Kennet

#### ↳ Re: MF goes windows from OS/2

- **From:** sands@mcmail (David Sands)
- **Date:** 1998-09-12T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<35fa32a6.806780@news.mcmail.com>`
- **References:** `<6tcrd0$34q6$1@news-inn.inet.tele.dk>`

```

It all depends on what your applicaiton is doing.

Straight forward COBOL will migrate between environments with no
problems. If your application uses character based ACCEPT/DISPLAY for
it's user interface then again this will move across to Win95 with no
changes.

But...

If your application used low level API calls to OS/2 then you will
have some work to do implementing the equivalent funcitonality on
Win95. If your application uses a GUI then you may have some problems
migrating that to Windows as some PM user interface features are
different in Win95.

You should also consider moving to Net Express which is the latest
Windows COBOL product from MF rather than the classic workbench.

Hope this helps
David.

<a id="t-558606a6e9"></a>
## Thread: comp3-ANSI-file => MS Access Import — 2 msgs

_Topics: [`Language features and syntax`](topics.md#syntax)_

### comp3-ANSI-file => MS Access Import

- **From:** TIN <soundmachine@writeme.com>
- **Date:** 1998-09-12T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<35FA5044.B1D22C97@writeme.com>`

```
Dear COBOL-users!

I'd like to import a COBOL-file into MS Access2.0. The COBOL-file (which
gets downloaded from the host into an ansi-file) contains 8byte long
comp3-data. The attached Access2.0 function 'BIN2DBL' is supposed to
work with comp-data. How do I have to modify the algorithm to decompress
comp3??

I would appreciate your 'emailed' help very much.
Tin 

Function Bin2Dbl (ByVal b) As Double
    Dim negative As Integer: negative = (CInt(Asc(Mid$(b, 1, 1))) And
128)
    Dim i
    Dim p As Double: p = 1
    Dim x As Double: x = 0
    For i = 8 To 1 Step -1
        x = x + p * IIf(negative, 255 Xor CInt(Asc(Mid$(b, i, 1))),
CInt(Asc(Mid$(b, i, 1))))
        p = p * 256#
    Next i
    Bin2Dbl = IIf(negative, -1#, 1#) * x / 100#
End Function
```

#### ↳ Re: comp3-ANSI-file => MS Access Import

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc123@bellsouth.net>
- **Date:** 1998-09-13T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<fqNK1.2516$p61.3924014@news4.mia.bellsouth.net>`
- **References:** `<35FA5044.B1D22C97@writeme.com>`

```
TIN wrote:
>
>I'd like to import a COBOL-file into MS Access2.0. The COBOL-file (which
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>I would appreciate your 'emailed' help very much.


I have not written one for Access, but here are a couple of BCD to decimal
routines I wrote in QBasic/QuickBasic.  The first converts an arbitrarily
long BCD string to a string of decimal digits, which can be converted to
any numeric type with VAL().  The second converts the string directly to
a LONG.  The logic should be very little, if any, different in Access.

'  **************************************************
'  *                                                *
'  *                 BCD2DEC.BAS                    *
'  *                                                *
'  *         Convert BCD string to Decimal          *
'  *                                                *
'  *              Judson D. McClendon               *
'  *              Sun Valley Systems                *
'  *              329 37th Court N.E.               *
'  *              Birmingham, AL 35215              *
'  *                 205-853-8440                   *
'  *                                                *
'  **************************************************
'
DECLARE FUNCTION Bcd2Dec$ (Bcd AS STRING)
DECLARE FUNCTION Dec2Bcd$ (Dec AS STRING)

DIM Dec AS STRING, Bcd AS STRING, I AS INTEGER
CLS

Dec = "00010203040506070809101112131415161718192021222324"
Dec = Dec + "25262728293031323334353637383940414243444546474849"
Dec = Dec + "50515253545556575859606162636465666768697071727374"
Dec = Dec + "75767778798081828384858687888990919293949596979899"
PRINT Dec

Bcd = Dec2Bcd$(Dec)
FOR I = 1 TO LEN(Bcd)
   PRINT RIGHT$("0" + HEX$(ASC(MID$(Bcd, I, 1))), 2);
NEXT
PRINT

Dec = Bcd2Dec$(Bcd)
PRINT Dec
END

FUNCTION Bcd2Dec$ (Bcd AS STRING)
   CONST ASCII0 = 48     ' ASCII Zero = ASC("0")
   DIM I AS INTEGER, Dec AS STRING, D AS INTEGER

   Dec = ""
   FOR I = 1 TO LEN(Bcd)
      D = ASC(MID$(Bcd, I, 1))
      Dec = Dec + CHR$((D \ 16) + ASCII0) + CHR$((D AND 15) + ASCII0)
   NEXT
   Bcd2Dec$ = Dec
END FUNCTION

FUNCTION Dec2Bcd$ (Dec AS STRING)
   CONST ASCII0 = 48     ' ASCII Zero = ASC("0")
   DIM I AS INTEGER, D1 AS INTEGER, D2 AS INTEGER, Bcd AS STRING

   IF (LEN(Dec) MOD 2 = 1) THEN
      Dec = "0" + Dec
   END IF
   Bcd = ""
   FOR I = 1 TO LEN(Dec) STEP 2
      D1 = ASC(MID$(Dec, I, 1)) - ASCII0
      D2 = ASC(MID$(Dec, I + 1, 1)) - ASCII0
      Bcd = Bcd + CHR$(D1 * 16 + D2)
   NEXT
   Dec2Bcd$ = Bcd
END FUNCTION


'  **************************************************
'  *                                                *
'  *                 BCD2DEC2.BAS                   *
'  *                                                *
'  *         Convert BCD string to Decimal          *
'  *                                                *
'  *              Judson D. McClendon               *
'  *              Sun Valley Systems                *
'  *              329 37th Court N.E.               *
'  *              Birmingham, AL 35215              *
'  *                 205-853-8440                   *
'  *                                                *
'  **************************************************
'
DECLARE FUNCTION BCD2DEC& (BCD AS STRING)
DECLARE FUNCTION DEC2BCD$ (BYVAL DEC AS LONG)

DIM DEC AS LONG, BCD AS STRING
CLS
DEC = 1234567890
PRINT DEC
BCD = DEC2BCD$(DEC)
DEC = BCD2DEC&(BCD)
PRINT DEC
END

FUNCTION BCD2DEC& (BCD AS STRING)
   DIM I AS INTEGER, C AS STRING * 1, D AS INTEGER, DEC AS LONG
   DEC = 0
   FOR I = 1 TO LEN(BCD)
      DEC = DEC * 100
      D = ASC(MID$(BCD, I, 1))
      DEC = DEC + (D AND &HF) + (D \ 16) * 10
   NEXT
   BCD2DEC& = DEC
END FUNCTION

FUNCTION DEC2BCD$ (BYVAL DEC AS LONG)
   DIM C AS STRING * 1, D1 AS INTEGER, D2 AS INTEGER, BCD AS STRING
   BCD = ""
   DO WHILE DEC
      D2 = DEC MOD 10
      DEC = DEC \ 10
      D1 = DEC MOD 10
      DEC = DEC \ 10
      BCD = CHR$(D1 * 16 + D2) + BCD
   LOOP
   DEC2BCD$ = BCD
END FUNCTION
```

<a id="t-332bbf6b19"></a>
## Thread: CICS COBOL PSEUDO / CONVERSATIONAL TASKS — 3 msgs

_Topics: [`Mainframe, z/OS, JCL, CICS`](topics.md#mainframe)_

### CICS COBOL PSEUDO / CONVERSATIONAL TASKS

- **From:** "Graeme Conn" <connman@iafrica.com>
- **Date:** 1998-09-12T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<6tdjcp$nsp$1@news01.iafrica.com>`

```
I am not a CICS person (I am an IDMS, ADSO, some IMS person).   There are a
few questions that are niggling, they are possibly very stupid but here
goes:

- in a pseudo-conversational program, (CICS SEND, RETURN TRANSID) please
confirm Cobol program working storage is not     available and the program
is in INITIAL state each time, one would then have to use other storage
methods

- in a conversational program, after one issues a CICS SEND with no
following return, where is control passed ? does one have to code the SEND/
RECEIVE within and iteration (perform until condition). I assume working
storage for the task is available until the
end of conversation

-I have read somewhere that one can only use a CICS RETURN TRANSID option at
one level below CICS, is that true ? For instance if I had a fairly large
application thread with a few EXEC CICS LINK type commands, could I return
to CICS and end of the preceding thread ???


Sorry if these are dumb questions, I thank you in advance.

Graeme
```

#### ↳ Re: CICS COBOL PSEUDO / CONVERSATIONAL TASKS

- **From:** sbarsky@my-dejanews.com
- **Date:** 1998-09-12T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<6tdqqa$cbd$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>`
- **References:** `<6tdjcp$nsp$1@news01.iafrica.com>`

```
In article <6tdjcp$nsp$1@news01.iafrica.com>,
  "Graeme Conn" <connman@iafrica.com> wrote:
> I am not a CICS person (I am an IDMS, ADSO, some IMS person).   There are a
> few questions that are niggling, they are possibly very stupid but here
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>
The working storage values from the prvious task are not available the next
task.  You have to use other storage such as the COMMAREA or Transient data
or Temporary Storage or can even store a small amount of data on the MAP
(hokey).

> - in a conversational program, after one issues a CICS SEND with no
> following return, where is control passed ? does one have to code the SEND/
> RECEIVE within and iteration (perform until condition). I assume working
> storage for the task is available until the
> end of conversation

I have never writen a Conversational program (I do mainframe only).  Therefore
I am still employed.
>
> -I have read somewhere that one can only use a CICS RETURN TRANSID option at
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
>

-----== Posted via Deja News, The Leader in Internet Discussion ==-----
http://www.dejanews.com/rg_mkgrp.xp   Create Your Own Free Member Forum
```

#### ↳ Re: CICS COBOL PSEUDO / CONVERSATIONAL TASKS

- **From:** vbandke@ibm.net (Volker Bandke)
- **Date:** 1998-09-12T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<35fa60d5.1734203@news2.ibm.net>`
- **References:** `<6tdjcp$nsp$1@news01.iafrica.com>`

```
On Sat, 12 Sep 1998 12:50:19 +0200, "Graeme Conn" <connman@iafrica.com> wrote:

>I am not a CICS person (I am an IDMS, ADSO, some IMS person).   There are a
>few questions that are niggling, they are possibly very stupid but here
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>
In pseudoconversational design the COBOL working storage is only availbale to the current
task (i.e. dialogue step).  After the RETURN TRANSID command the working storage is
released by CICS.  The next transaction0task to run will get a new (initial) working
storage.

To pass data from one task to the next you can
	- use a COMMAREA (up to 32760 bytes) (usually recommended method)
	- use temporary storage (up to 32767 items [don't!!] of up to 32700 bytes ) 
	- use shared getmain (not recommendsd)

>- in a conversational program, after one issues a CICS SEND with no
>following return, where is control passed ? does one have to code the SEND/
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>
Yes, that's exactly what you would need to do - but keep in mind: Everyone that uses
conversational design will get a fair trial - and then will be taken out and shot!  Only
IBM can get away with it.  Using conversational design can effectivly lock out a large
part of the users from CICS.  

>-I have read somewhere that one can only use a CICS RETURN TRANSID option at
>one level below CICS, is that true ? For instance if I had a fairly large
>application thread with a few EXEC CICS LINK type commands, could I return
>to CICS and end of the preceding thread ???

This used to be true in earliere releases of CICS.  With CICS Version 4 though you can
issue the RETURN TRANSID option at any time.  BUT....this command would NOT return you to
CICS!!!  It would only return you to the next higher program in the link hierarchy.  The
TRANSID would just be remembered for the highest LINK level.  It will only take effect
when returning to CICS.  NOTE:  At any time before RETURNing to CICS you can check the
TRANSID via EXEC CICS ASSIGN NEXTTRANSID.

HTH

with kind regards

Volker Bandke
(BSP GmbH)
```

because converting is not a fun process.

The company that I work for sells a serial communications program
called the Software Wedge that is designed to add serial I/O
capability to other applications without any modifications to the
other app. The program works by stuffing incoming serial data into the
keyboard buffer so that serial data looks like keyboard input.
If you are interested please visit http://www.taltech.com

The easiest way is to use the Windows-API. It work's with 16-Bit and 32-Bit
Windows with MF Workbench 3.1, 3.4 and 4.0 (tested). If you have a
DOS-application, it's more difficult. You can access the ports directly, but
that not funny because the buffer of a COM-Port is only 16 Byte and you need a
good synchronisation.

Frank

<a id="t-f6080ecc83"></a>
## Thread: Paragraph Numbering and Program Structure — 56 msgs

_Topics: [`Language features and syntax`](topics.md#syntax)_

### Paragraph Numbering and Program Structure

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc123@bellsouth.net>
- **Date:** 1998-09-13T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** alt.cobol,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<JgPK1.2535$p61.3962448@news4.mia.bellsouth.net>`

```
I was taught as a beginning programmer to use ascending numeric prefixes
on all my paragraph names.  Over the years, I have kept this practice, and
have embellished it in a number of ways.  I like to use the paragraph number
to reflect a relative structure hierarchy.  Over the years, I have also
learned to appreciate ease of copying code from one program into another,
so I have standardized on a six digit prefix.  Below is a stylized example
of how a program might be structured, and how the paragraph numbers would
be used to reflect the hierarchy.  Note the way the paragraph numbering
reflects the actual structural hierarchy of the program.  By reflecting
the structural hierarchy of the code in the paragraph numbering, it makes
it very easy to recognize the program structure.  A paragraph does not
only PERFORM a paragraph of a subordinate level, of course.  The 'global
utility' paragraphs are for routines which are not necessarily subordinate
to the major functions of the program, but can be used by them.  Examples
might be a date edit, or field convert of some kind.  For independent
routines of one paragraph, I use 00nn00, and for routines with several
subordinate paragraphs, I use 00n000 for the top level paragraph.  In
general, paragraph 120000 will have every paragraph 12nnnn subordinate to
it (12300 -> 123nnn, 123400 -> 1234nn, 123450 -> 12345n).  As an aside,
when we used to use sections to segment our code, paragraph 000000 would
start a section, and each different nn0000 paragraph would start another
section.

Many of you may use a similar method.  But I have found this to be a very
flexible and valuable tool over the years, and I like using it very much.
Only rarely do I need to move a group of paragraphs to a different 'level'.
It has contributed a great deal to my productivity, and the effect is
cumulative.  The more code I collect which is similarly structured and
easy to use, the less code I need to write.  The advantages I have reaped
from this are too many to enumerate.  It is easy to find any paragraph in
the program, you do not need to think up unique paragraph names for two
very similar routines in different parts of the program (e.g. doing the
same thing to different tables), it is easy to see the hierarchical program
structure, etc.  That last one is extremely valuable.

If you like this method, then I'm happy to share it with you.  If so, or
if not, please post your comments.  If this example is not clear, let me
know and I will try to be clearer.

      *
      *  ** TOP LEVEL CONTROL STRUCTURE (000000) **
      *
       000000-CONTROL.
           PERFORM 000100-INITIALIZE
           PERFORM 000200-PROCESS
               UNTIL (end of data).
           PERFORM 000300-TERMINATE.
           STOP RUN.

      *
      *  ** SECOND LEVEL CONTROL STRUCTURE (000n00) **
      *
       000100-INITIALIZE.
           Get today's date, open files, read first input record, etc.

       000200-PROCESS.
           PERFORM 010000-major-function-1.
           PERFORM 020000-major-function-2.
           ...
           PERFORM 990000-major-function-99.

       000300-TERMINATE..
           Print ending totals, close files, etc.

      *
      *  ** GLOBAL UTILITY ROUTINES (00nn00) **
      *
       001000-utility-routine.
       001100-utility-routine.
       001200-utility-routine.
           ...

      *
      *  ** MAJOR FUNCTIONS (nn0000) **
      *
       010000-major-function-1.
           PERFORM 011000-intermediate-function-1-1.
           PERFORM 012000-intermediate-function-1-2.
           ...

      *
      *  ** INTERMEDIATE FUNCTIONS (nnx000) **
      *
       011000-intermediate-function-1-1.
           PERFORM 011100-minor-function-1-1-1.
           PERFORM 011200-minor-function-1-1-2.
           ...

      *
      *  ** MINOR FUNCTIONS (nnnx00) **
      *
       011100-minor-function-1-1-1.
           PERFORM 011110-sub-function-1-1-1-1.
           PERFORM 011120-sub-function-1-1-1-2.
           ...

      *
      *  ** SUB FUNCTIONS (nnnnx0) **
      *
       011110-sub-function-1-1-1-1.
           PERFORM 011111-low-function-1-1-1-1-1.
           PERFORM 011112-low-function-1-1-1-1-2.
           ...

      *
      *  ** LOW FUNCTIONS (nnnnnx) **
      *
       011111-low-function-1-1-1-1-1.
           ...

       011111-low-function-1-1-1-1-2.
           ...


       011120-sub-function-1-1-1-2.
           ...


       011200-minor-function-1-1-2.
           ...

       012000-intermediate-function-1-2.
           PERFORM 012100-minor-function-1-2-1.
           PERFORM 012200-minor-function-1-2-2.
           ...

       012100-minor-function-1-2-1.
           ...

       012200-minor-function-1-2-2.
           ...


       020000-major-function-2.
           PERFORM 021000-intermediate-function-2-1.
           PERFORM 022000-intermediate-function-2-2.
           ...

       021000-intermediate-function-2-1.
           PERFORM 022100-minor-function-2-1-1.
           PERFORM 022200-minor-function-2-1-2.
           ...

       021100-minor-function-2-1-1.
           ...

       021200-minor-function-2-1-2.
           ...

       022000-intermediate-function-2-2.
           PERFORM 022100-minor-function-2-2-1.
           PERFORM 022200-minor-function-2-2-2.
           ...

       022100-minor-function-2-2-1.
           ...

       022200-minor-function-2-2-2.
           ...
```

#### ↳ Re: Paragraph Numbering and Program Structure

- **From:** "Leif Svalgaard" <leif@ibm.net>
- **Date:** 1998-09-13T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** alt.cobol,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<35fbd794.0@news1.ibm.net>`
- **References:** `<JgPK1.2535$p61.3962448@news4.mia.bellsouth.net>`

```
Judson McClendon wrote in message ...
>I was taught as a beginning programmer to use ascending numeric prefixes
>on all my paragraph names.  Over the years, I have kept this practice, and
>have embellished it in a number of ways.  I like to use the paragraph
number
>to reflect a relative structure hierarchy

A serious problem with this scheme is that if the structure changes during
maintenance (remember the program may live for decades) you have to
rename the paragraphs as well. This is tedious and errorprone. Also,
numbering paragraphs makes re-use more difficult as the program
into which you import the paragraphs to be re-used may have a different
structure.

In the old days where you would pore over a 4-inch thick listing it did make
some sense to number stuff, but today it does not. A respectable editor
should assist you, by e.g. letting you click or right-click on a paragraph
name and then simply show you that paragraph. Same thing with data-items.
There are also many tools that will show the structure of a program in
various
graphical ways.
```

>
>A serious problem with this scheme is that if the structure changes during
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>structure.

Oh yes. It's one of my least favourite examples of 'style' that I've come 
across. Fine when you're writing code but a pain to maintain and as you say 
re-use.

>
>In the old days where you would pore over a 4-inch thick listing it did make
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>graphical ways.

And even in textual ways. Editors with decent source intelligence have been 
around for years.

The biggest dislike I have with prefixing items is in editors where you can 
type a few letters and it guesses the rest of the name. If you call all your 
data-items ws-data1-... etc and your paragraphs/sections something like 
a20-init, you have to type a load of text before the guessing works.

Shaun
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Paragraph Numbering and Program Structure

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** scm@enterprise.net (Shaun C. Murray)
- **Date:** 1998-09-14T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** alt.cobol,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<6tivgn$7bl$3@news.enterprise.net>`
- **References:** `<JgPK1.2535$p61.3962448@news4.mia.bellsouth.net> <35fbd794.0@news1.ibm.net> <6thl57$4ju$2@news.enterprise.net> <pv4L1.1723$te.2553803@news2.mia.bellsouth.net>`

```
In article <pv4L1.1723$te.2553803@news2.mia.bellsouth.net>, 
judmc123@bellsouth.net says...
>
>Are you are under the impression that I am suggesting the paragraphs actually
>be named "010000-major-function-1" as in my example?

I wasn't singling you out but forgetting the -major-function-1 for a moment, 
the numbers at the front are the problem. It stops editor AI functions from 
working, reduces the portion of the name that is unique AND meaningful apart 
from the issues with reuse and maintenance already given.

>  In an actual program,
>'major-function-1" should be a meaningful name.  I just used representative
>labels in the example to describe the hierarchical structure.

I don't get this arguement. Prefixing with numbers is an odd throwback to older 
coding styles that you simply don't need anymore given the correct tools and 
modern syntax. If your editor doesn't show you structure or the programs 
structure is not obvious there is something wrong there. Sure it might be 
useful if you're stuck using XEDIT but really - get a PC.

After all, you don't see BASIC, C, Java, Pascal or whatever programmers 
prefixing with numbers yet they have no problem understanding structure. Why we 
should still be doing it in COBOL when we have perhaps one of the most 
structured languages is beyond me. Layout your program sensibly and think about 
structure before hand and that's most of the problem solved.
```

- **From:** "Leif Svalgaard" <leif@ibm.net>

- **Newsgroups:** alt.cobol,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<35ff4f4c.0@news1.ibm.net>`
- **References:** `<JgPK1.2535$p61.3962448@news4.mia.bellsouth.net> <35fbd794.0@news1.ibm.net> <6thl57$4ju$2@news.enterprise.net> <pv4L1.1723$te.2553803@news2.mia.bellsouth.net>`

Judson McClendon wrote in message ...
>Are you are under the impression that I am suggesting the paragraphs
actually
>be named "010000-major-function-1" as in my example?  In an actual program,
>'major-function-1" should be a meaningful name.  I just used representative
>labels in the example to describe the hierarchical structure.


No, I was not under that impression, although I've often seen people
use the paragraph (or section) name 000100-mainline or some other
non-representative name.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Paragraph Numbering and Program Structure

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "Leif Svalgaard" <leif@ibm.net>
- **Date:** 1998-09-16T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** alt.cobol,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<35ff5275.0@news1.ibm.net>`
- **References:** `<JgPK1.2535$p61.3962448@news4.mia.bellsouth.net> <35fbd794.0@news1.ibm.net> <6thl57$4ju$2@news.enterprise.net> <pv4L1.1723$te.2553803@news2.mia.bellsouth.net>`

```
Judson McClendon wrote in message ...
>Are you are under the impression that I am suggesting the paragraphs
actually
>be named "010000-major-function-1" as in my example?  In an actual program,
>'major-function-1" should be a meaningful name.  I just used representative
>labels in the example to describe the hierarchical structure.


Someone once told me that most of his programs had the same general
structure, so instead of meaningful names, he would use 'generic names'.
Not GET-EMPLOYEE-RECORD but GET-THE-RECORD. The argument
(which has some validity!) was that to clone the program he only had to
change the innards of some of the paragraphs. So, here you have an
argument against *too* meaningful names.

I must admit that the above argument appeals to me, but that is probably
because most of my programs are not really written by me, but rather
generated automatically from business rules kept in a repository.
I only supply the business rules (Cobol fragments) and the table
descriptions.

To put it differently, the resulting Cobol programs are not for human
consumption. In fact, since code generation is faster than compilation,
we often simply delete the source program after compilation (or rather
the compile procedure does that automatically).

Having admitted that, I may have disqualified myself to speak about
program style and maintainability...
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Paragraph Numbering and Program Stru

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** riplin@kcbbs.gen.nz (Richard Plinston)
- **Date:** 1998-09-16T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3298258.70731.24774@kcbbs.gen.nz>`
- **References:** `<35ff5275.0@news1.ibm.net>`

```
In message <<35ff5275.0@news1.ibm.net>> "Leif Svalgaard" <leif@ibm.net> writes:
> 
> Someone once told me that most of his programs had the same general
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> argument against *too* meaningful names.

The boredom of rewriting the same program over and over
would drive me mad (assuming this state hasn't been reached).
Some years ago I started developing a set of programs that
would replace all the screen-masterfile-maintenance and
print-report programs, or at least all the boring
repetitive ones.

This has paid off handsomely over the years.  For a new
system (such as one I was asked to do a couple of weeks
ago) I can get the basics of masterfile maintenance and
and selection reports just by building dictionaries and
screen layouts that are stored in a system control file.

This does mean that I have several large programs that
are shared accross several systems rather than dozens
or hundreds of small programs.

This also means that the programs has 'get-the-record'
type labels as it neither knows nor cares whether it is
an employee of a product.

> 
> Having admitted that, I may have disqualified myself to speak about
> program style and maintainability...

I am sure that the repository of business rules has the
opportunity ti have a 'style' of some sort, even 
though it is likely to be a table.

Perhaps you could indicate a small part of how the rules
are formed.

For my report printer the report scripts are stored in a 
text file and look like:

*REPORT Name
*FILE CD
*TITLE This appears as the heading on the page
*COMPUTE
[calcualtions can appear here]
*SELECT
SALESYTD >= ?               [ '?' means ask at run time
CUST-GRP IN A*,HA,HB        [ '*' is any, IN means match list
STOPSALE /= Y               [ not=,   up to 20 selection
*INDEX                      [ specifies optional sorting
CUST-GRP P                  [ start new page
SALESYTD -                  [ descending sales within group
*LINE 01                    [ first line
CUSTNAME
SALESYTD
*ENDREPORT


This was designed for ease of processing rather than
readability, a report generator can be used to
maintaine the report scrripts.  Because I have full
control over the programs I can modify them to suit
special needs as required.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Paragraph Numbering and Program Stru

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** "Leif Svalgaard" <leif@ibm.net>
- **Date:** 1998-09-16T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36002c67.0@news1.ibm.net>`
- **References:** `<35ff5275.0@news1.ibm.net> <3298258.70731.24774@kcbbs.gen.nz>`

```
Richard Plinston wrote in message <3298258.70731.24774@kcbbs.gen.nz>...
>In message <<35ff5275.0@news1.ibm.net>> "Leif Svalgaard" <leif@ibm.net>
writes:
>> Someone once told me that most of his programs had the same general
>> structure, so instead of meaningful names, he would use 'generic names'.
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
>repetitive ones.


the ONLY way to go.  One can wonder why the Cobol Vendors
dont' promote this type of development. The problem is of course
that they don't have a standard to code to. Many years ago
IBM was trying to come up with a repository standard:
the ill-fated A/D Cycle. It fell flat on its face for several reasons.
Recently there is the UML movement and (even more hyped)
the XML-description of data and stuff, but as long as everyone
(you, me, etc...) has to invent his own wheel, no progress
really happens.

>This also means that the programs has 'get-the-record'
>type labels as it neither knows nor cares whether it is
>an employee of a product.
>

Indeed.

>>
>> Having admitted that, I may have disqualified myself to speak about
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>Perhaps you could indicate a small part of how the rules

>are formed.


My rules are usually short (1-20 lines) so little structure or
style is needed. The rule is simply Cobol. For some of the
rules one can embed "tokens" that are replaced at code
generation time. A typical one would be:

 IF <SFN> NOT = <NUL>
      MOVE <SFN> TO <FFN>
.
where <FFN> is the "file field-name" and <SFN> is the
"screen field-name" and <NUL> is either SPACES or
ZEROES depending on the type of the field.
```
