##### ↳ ↳ Re: Paragraph Numbering and Program Structure

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc123@bellsouth.net>
- **Date:** 1998-09-14T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** alt.cobol,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<6q4L1.1721$te.2552352@news2.mia.bellsouth.net>`
- **References:** `<JgPK1.2535$p61.3962448@news4.mia.bellsouth.net> <35fbd794.0@news1.ibm.net>`

```
Leif Svalgaard wrote:
>Judson McClendon wrote in message ...
>>I was taught as a beginning programmer to use ascending numeric prefixes
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
>structure.

This is simply not true.  Remember, you are speaking what you 'think' would
be the case, but I am speaking from over 20 years experience using this
style, and maintaining programs written using it.  Here's an example.  Any
programmer's editor these days has regular expression search and replace.
Let's say you want to renumber paragraphs 123nnn to 456nnn.  Using Brief's
syntax, you would search for (omitting quotes) " 123{[0-9][0-9][0-9]-}"
and replace with (again, omitting quotes) " 456\0".  This says "look for a
space, followed by 123, followed by {remembering} three numeric digits and
a hyphen, and replace it by a space, followed by 456, followed by the three
numeric digits and the hyphen which was {remembered} from the input string.
This takes maybe 3-5 seconds, and I have done it countless times.  Even
without regular expressions, it is very easy to search for " 123" and
replace by " 456", though you would want to okay each replacement to avoid
a possible match with a numeric literal.  Hardly a 'serious problem'!  ;-)

You may have missed it in my post, but I specifically pointed out that
encountering the need to restructure such code is rare.  I turn out a lot
of code (~200k lines/year) and I probably don't average needing to do a
restructure such as you suggest, even once a year.  I may have had to do
it a half dozen times in some 20 years.  That's one of the things programming
standards are for, after all.  Use 'em. :-)

>In the old days where you would pore over a 4-inch thick listing it did make
>some sense to number stuff, but today it does not. A respectable editor
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>various graphical ways.


Yes, and you must buy the tools, and run them, and produce the output to
view it.  This is built right into the program, is always there, and costs
nothing.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Paragraph Numbering and Program Stru

- **From:** riplin@kcbbs.gen.nz (Richard Plinston)
- **Date:** 1998-09-14T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3298256.70143.26563@kcbbs.gen.nz>`
- **References:** `<6q4L1.1721$te.2552352@news2.mia.bellsouth.net>`

```
In message <<6q4L1.1721$te.2552352@news2.mia.bellsouth.net>> "Judson McClendon" <judmc123@bellsouth.net> writes:
> >
> >A serious problem with this scheme is that if the structure changes during
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> style, and maintaining programs written using it.  Here's an example.  Any

Exactly what I thought, you are stuck in the past, refining 
decades old mechanisms to its pinnacle.

> programmer's editor these days has regular expression search and replace.
> Let's say you want to renumber paragraphs 123nnn to 456nnn.  Using Brief's
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> a possible match with a numeric literal.  Hardly a 'serious problem'!  ;-)

It is still 'tedious and errorprone' compared to never needing
to do it.  All that this shows is that after 20 years of
practice and finding all the probable errors you have
developed techniques that make it less tedious and errorprone
for you (you may even enjoy it).  Perhaps if everyone did
this for 20 years (or even 5 or 10) they may develop the
particualr skills necessary to avoid all the problems you
have trained yourself to avoid.

Or, then again, they could just write programs not constrained
by a single hierarchical chart imposed by your numbering.

> 
> You may have missed it in my post, but I specifically pointed out that
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> standards are for, after all.  Use 'em. :-)

Are these all batch-merge-and-update or batch-print-report-
from-file ?  Programs which generally _are_ suitable for
hierarchical decomposition ?

> 
> >In the old days where you would pore over a 4-inch thick listing it did make
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
> nothing.

It costs a huge amount.  It has probably cost your employer
thousands of hours of your salary for the time you spent
developing this mechanism.  Now it may well be true that you
are more productive with this than without it, but this
is not a result of the mechanism itself, but simply of the
discipline that you have imposed on yourself in developing
and keeping to the mechanism.

Because of your 20 years of experience, and of fixing up
all the shortfalls, you do this automatically.  Someone
coming to this anew does not have this experience and
training.  It would take some months or years before
they 'visualised' as you undoubtedly do.

What you don't see is the cost of this mechanism (because
to you there is no cost).  It simply would not fit into
any programs I write, it would be entirely an overhead
and would add nothing - I have zero problems with
hierarchy, I have zero problems finding anything, I never
need to 'visualise' any alledged '3D' structure. I never
need to draw structure diagrams, even for large programs
that I am given to work on.

It may be the 'best' mechanism of its type, but it is
still obsolete and only useful for archaic programs.
```

#### ↳ Re: Paragraph Numbering and Program Structure

- **From:** paulr@bix.com (paulr)
- **Date:** 1998-09-13T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** alt.cobol,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<6tgods$ps0@lotho.delphi.com>`
- **References:** `<JgPK1.2535$p61.3962448@news4.mia.bellsouth.net>`

```
Interesting, but, is it really necessary? 
I have a strong background in Ada, Pascal, and C, as well
as several assembly languages, about a dozen other languages,
their dialects, and of course, COBOL. The one thing that
bugs me about COBOL is using those numbers. :) 

Program structure shoudl be obvious from the naming, not 
from a numering scheme. This is where the 30 character limit
intrudes, and is probably why people took to numbering in
COBOL in the first place.

It is also the area where OO technology / methodology can and
proabably will make the most signifigant impact in the COBOL
world. When you can more easily pacakge stuff together, with 
naming haveing a more modern scoping scheme, things will fall
into place. It would also be nice to have functions or procedures,
so programs would read better without the need to use the keyword
"perform." 

Probably goes against the prevailing wisdome, but COBOL can learn 
something from the 20+ years of OO technology out there. :) 

-Paul



Judson McClendon (judmc123@bellsouth.net) wrote:
: I was taught as a beginning programmer to use ascending numeric prefixes
: on all my paragraph names.  Over the years, I have kept this practice, and
: have embellished it in a number of ways.  I like to use the paragraph number
: to reflect a relative structure hierarchy.  Over the years, I have also
: learned to appreciate ease of copying code from one program into another,
: so I have standardized on a six digit prefix.  Below is a stylized example
: of how a program might be structured, and how the paragraph numbers would
: be used to reflect the hierarchy.  Note the way the paragraph numbering
: reflects the actual structural hierarchy of the program.  By reflecting
: the structural hierarchy of the code in the paragraph numbering, it makes
: it very easy to recognize the program structure.  A paragraph does not
: only PERFORM a paragraph of a subordinate level, of course.  The 'global
: utility' paragraphs are for routines which are not necessarily subordinate
: to the major functions of the program, but can be used by them.  Examples
: might be a date edit, or field convert of some kind.  For independent
: routines of one paragraph, I use 00nn00, and for routines with several
: subordinate paragraphs, I use 00n000 for the top level paragraph.  In
: general, paragraph 120000 will have every paragraph 12nnnn subordinate to
: it (12300 -> 123nnn, 123400 -> 1234nn, 123450 -> 12345n).  As an aside,
: when we used to use sections to segment our code, paragraph 000000 would
: start a section, and each different nn0000 paragraph would start another
: section.

: Many of you may use a similar method.  But I have found this to be a very
: flexible and valuable tool over the years, and I like using it very much.
: Only rarely do I need to move a group of paragraphs to a different 'level'.
: It has contributed a great deal to my productivity, and the effect is
: cumulative.  The more code I collect which is similarly structured and
: easy to use, the less code I need to write.  The advantages I have reaped
: from this are too many to enumerate.  It is easy to find any paragraph in
: the program, you do not need to think up unique paragraph names for two
: very similar routines in different parts of the program (e.g. doing the
: same thing to different tables), it is easy to see the hierarchical program
: structure, etc.  That last one is extremely valuable.

: If you like this method, then I'm happy to share it with you.  If so, or
: if not, please post your comments.  If this example is not clear, let me
: know and I will try to be clearer.

:       *
:       *  ** TOP LEVEL CONTROL STRUCTURE (000000) **
:       *
:        000000-CONTROL.
:            PERFORM 000100-INITIALIZE
:            PERFORM 000200-PROCESS
:                UNTIL (end of data).
:            PERFORM 000300-TERMINATE.
:            STOP RUN.

:       *
:       *  ** SECOND LEVEL CONTROL STRUCTURE (000n00) **
:       *
:        000100-INITIALIZE.
:            Get today's date, open files, read first input record, etc.

:        000200-PROCESS.
:            PERFORM 010000-major-function-1.
:            PERFORM 020000-major-function-2.
:            ...
:            PERFORM 990000-major-function-99.

:        000300-TERMINATE..
:            Print ending totals, close files, etc.

:       *
:       *  ** GLOBAL UTILITY ROUTINES (00nn00) **
:       *
:        001000-utility-routine.
:        001100-utility-routine.
:        001200-utility-routine.
:            ...

:       *
:       *  ** MAJOR FUNCTIONS (nn0000) **
:       *
:        010000-major-function-1.
:            PERFORM 011000-intermediate-function-1-1.
:            PERFORM 012000-intermediate-function-1-2.
:            ...

:       *
:       *  ** INTERMEDIATE FUNCTIONS (nnx000) **
:       *
:        011000-intermediate-function-1-1.
:            PERFORM 011100-minor-function-1-1-1.
:            PERFORM 011200-minor-function-1-1-2.
:            ...

:       *
:       *  ** MINOR FUNCTIONS (nnnx00) **
:       *
:        011100-minor-function-1-1-1.
:            PERFORM 011110-sub-function-1-1-1-1.
:            PERFORM 011120-sub-function-1-1-1-2.
:            ...

:       *
:       *  ** SUB FUNCTIONS (nnnnx0) **
:       *
:        011110-sub-function-1-1-1-1.
:            PERFORM 011111-low-function-1-1-1-1-1.
:            PERFORM 011112-low-function-1-1-1-1-2.
:            ...

:       *
:       *  ** LOW FUNCTIONS (nnnnnx) **
:       *
:        011111-low-function-1-1-1-1-1.
:            ...

:        011111-low-function-1-1-1-1-2.
:            ...


:        011120-sub-function-1-1-1-2.
:            ...


:        011200-minor-function-1-1-2.
:            ...

:        012000-intermediate-function-1-2.
:            PERFORM 012100-minor-function-1-2-1.
:            PERFORM 012200-minor-function-1-2-2.
:            ...

:        012100-minor-function-1-2-1.
:            ...

:        012200-minor-function-1-2-2.
:            ...


:        020000-major-function-2.
:            PERFORM 021000-intermediate-function-2-1.
:            PERFORM 022000-intermediate-function-2-2.
:            ...

:        021000-intermediate-function-2-1.
:            PERFORM 022100-minor-function-2-1-1.
:            PERFORM 022200-minor-function-2-1-2.
:            ...

:        021100-minor-function-2-1-1.
:            ...

:        021200-minor-function-2-1-2.
:            ...

:        022000-intermediate-function-2-2.
:            PERFORM 022100-minor-function-2-2-1.
:            PERFORM 022200-minor-function-2-2-2.
:            ...

:        022100-minor-function-2-2-1.
:            ...

:        022200-minor-function-2-2-2.
:            ...
: --
: Judson McClendon      judmc123@bellsouth.net  (remove numbers)
: Sun Valley Systems    http://personal.bhm.bellsouth.net/~judmc
: "For God so loved the world that He gave His only begotten Son, that
: whoever believes in Him should not perish but have everlasting life."
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Paragraph Numbering and Program Structure

- **From:** "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@aiservices.com>
- **Date:** 1998-09-13T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** alt.cobol,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<fWUK1.343$pB3.1596991@news1.atlantic.net>`
- **References:** `<JgPK1.2535$p61.3962448@news4.mia.bellsouth.net> <6tgods$ps0@lotho.delphi.com>`

```

paulr wrote in message <6tgods$ps0@lotho.delphi.com>...
>Interesting, but, is it really necessary?
>[...] The one thing that
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>
To discern structure there must be dimension; but names,
alone, have no dimension. For example, what program
structure exists with a procedure named Get-Employee-ID?
Compare that with 012100-Get-Employee-ID! The numeric
prefix adds dimension and tells me something about the
operational and code structure that the name, alone, cannot.

>It is also the area where OO technology / methodology can and
>proabably will make the most signifigant impact in the COBOL
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>
It is not clear, to me, what you mean by "package" or "scoping."
If you are refering to classes as the package, then modules can
provide the packaging. If by "scoping" you mean "making
references unambiguous" then qualification may be used.

User defined functions are included in the proposed standard.
For example,
        COMPUTE GrossPay = EmployeeGrossPay ( Employee )

[Although, I believe, the, possibly, non-standard
        MOVE EmployeeGrossPay ( Employee ) TO GrossPay
is more COBOL-like.] ;-)

How is
        INVOKE Employee "CalculatePay" RETURNING PayInfo
any clearer than
        PERFORM 012100-CalculatePay or
        CALL "CalculatePay" USING Employee, PayInfo ?
-------------------------------
Rick Smith
e-mail: < ricksmith@aiservices.com >
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Paragraph Numbering and Program Structure

- **From:** paulr@bix.com (paulr)
- **Date:** 1998-09-13T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** alt.cobol,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<6thdo5$2ba@lotho.delphi.com>`
- **References:** `<JgPK1.2535$p61.3962448@news4.mia.bellsouth.net> <6tgods$ps0@lotho.delphi.com> <fWUK1.343$pB3.1596991@news1.atlantic.net>`

```
Well, 
[lots of snipping for brevity -quoting errors are mine]

Rick Smith (ricksmith@aiservices.com) wrote:
: To discern structure there must be dimension; but names,
: alone, have no dimension. For example, what program
: structure exists with a procedure named Get-Employee-ID?
: Compare that with 012100-Get-Employee-ID! The numeric
: prefix adds dimension and tells me something about the
: operational and code structure that the name, alone, cannot.

How many Get-Employ-ID procedures or paragraphs do you have? 
If only one, the numbering doesn't seem to add any structure.

: It is not clear, to me, what you mean by "package" or "scoping."
: If you are refering to classes as the package, then modules can
: provide the packaging. If by "scoping" you mean "making
: references unambiguous" then qualification may be used.

Packaging has to do with how tightly coupled a program's
structure is; in this instance, I was reffering to being
able to group like functins together in a reusable manner.
The only really functional method we have to do this today
is with copybooks, and that lacks some really useful 
features. For example, you canont take something in a
copybooks and dynamically extend its functionality. This 
is where - eh - "snobs" turn up thier noses at COBOL. :) 

An example of that: lets take a base type and call it 
an employee-record. This is very common, and usually 
stored away in a copybook for easy reference among
programs. Now how do I take an employee-record, 
and create a part-time-employee record? Well, in 
today's COBOL, I copy the record definition in the        
copybook or I use a redefine and a variable record file. 
But with OO technology, I coul have done that failry 
easily without modifing a line in the copybook. That doesn't
seem very signifigant to non-OO types at first, but
trust me, it grows on you. :) 

And you are right in that qualified referencing is the 
methodology of choice for that. In this one case, I 
do think COBOL is rather - verbose.  

Employee-Number of Employee-Record of Employee-Master-File
is a bit verbose compared to 
Employee-Master-File.Employee-Record.Employee-Number. 

(only a bit, I grant you, but which one is easier to deal 
with? Hint, English speaking people tend to read 
left to right. :) Of coure, that really is just a bit of a
preference, there is absolutely nothing wrong with the 
current qualification syntax. 

Scoping means that data you define is visible only
within certain packages. In the OO world, you rarely
find someone doing the equivalent of:

  move tr-transaction-id to emp-transaction-id. 

They would call a method to set it:

  set-employee-transaction-id (to: tr-transaction-id);

 
There are dozens of terms for it, data hiding, encapsulation,
etc. It all tends to boil down to what we COBOL types do with
calls. The screen IO packages are good examples of this.
You don't know and you don't care what the package does with
the data you send it, or how it is stored. 

And that is the real key; if you don't allow people to 
have direct access to data you store, then you can change
the implementation all you want. Store it ina file, an 
array, or any of a hundred other ways- as long as you
do NOT change the user interface (API, Method signatures, 
several other names for it here) the users will be none
the wiser. 

Again, this is not stuff that is new to COBOL, heck, probably
half these ideas were invented by some COBOL programmer with
a pin-headed Dilbert-like Boss demanding the impossible. 
But COBOL can use some updating. :) 

That is probably why I am a big Fan of IBM's Object COBOL.
I like the implementation a lot.

-Paul
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Paragraph Numbering and Program Structure

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "Leif Svalgaard" <leif@ibm.net>
- **Date:** 1998-09-13T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** alt.cobol,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<35fc44e8.0@news1.ibm.net>`
- **References:** `<JgPK1.2535$p61.3962448@news4.mia.bellsouth.net> <6tgods$ps0@lotho.delphi.com> <fWUK1.343$pB3.1596991@news1.atlantic.net> <6thdo5$2ba@lotho.delphi.com>`

```
A very efficient way of writing non-trivial programs is to
treat them as Finite State Machines (FSMs). An FMS
operates with a (Finite) set of States. In each state a
number of Events can happen. When an event happens
a list of Actions is carried out, and a New State is selected.

The Actions could simply be paragraphs to be performed.

Now, the FMS is described externally to the program, e.g.
as a simple text file. From this description and from a list
of Actions (in any order), the final Cobol program can be
readily generated. The Actions as written by the programmer
have no structure. So, an FMS is kinda the opposite of
a "structured" program. The structure is supplied by the
FMS description, not the Cobol fragments written by the
programmer.

This way of programming is inherently event-driven and
thus very well suited for interactive programs, but works
equally well for batch programs. In batch, the events are
the arrival (reading) of transactions.

If the program has no structure, paragraph numbering
is superfluous.

One of the many advantages of "structureless" programs
is that there is no structure to be destroyed during
maintenance. Traditional structured program tends to
become less structured with time.

Here is an example of an FMS description:

<Format> is this:
  State
     (..) Event                        -> Next State
           + Action1
           + Action2
</Format>
---------------------------------------------------------------------

After-Init:
    (--) Error                              ->
          + Terminate-The-Program
    (--) Ok                                 -> Showing-Screen
          + Check-Init-Permissions
          + Prepare-The-Screen
          + Get-Action-Bar-Menus
          + Get-Action-Bar-Line
          + Open-The-File
          + Check-If-Get-Wanted

Showing-Screen:
    (14) Exit                               ->
          + Check-If-Data-Changed
          + Check-If-Key-Changed
          + Terminate-The-Program
    (99) Action-Bar                         -> Have-Desired-Action
          + Show-Menu-For-Action
          + Get-Action-Bar-Operation
    (02) Bkwd                               -> Showing-Screen
          + Check-If-Data-Changed
          + Set-For-This-Or-Next
          + Find-The-Record
          + Set-For-Previous
          + Find-The-Record
          + Validate-The-Record
    (01) Fwd                                -> Showing-Screen
          + Check-If-Data-Changed
          + Set-For-Next
          + Find-The-Record
          + Validate-The-Record
    (99) Find-Wanted                        -> Showing-Screen
          + Set-For-This-Or-Next
          + Find-The-Record
          + Validate-The-Record
    (03) Find                               -> Showing-Screen
          + Set-For-This-Or-Next
          + Find-The-Record
          + Validate-The-Record
    (04) More                               -> Showing-Screen
          + Scroll-The-Screen
    (99) Want-Details                       -> Showing-Screen
          + Check-If-Data-Changed
          + Check-If-Key-Changed
          + Determine-Which-Detail
          + Menu--Check-Result
    (99) Select-Details                     -> Showing-Screen
          + Check-If-Data-Changed
          + Check-If-Key-Changed
          + Pop-Up-Select-Details
          + Return-Select-Details
          + Menu--Check-Result
    (07) Detail                             -> Showing-Screen
          + Check-If-Data-Changed
          + Check-If-Key-Changed
          + Show-The-Details
          + Menu--Check-Result
    (05) Add                                -> Showing-Screen
          + Check-Create-Permissions
          + Validate-The-Record
          + Create-The-Record
    (10) Delete                             -> Showing-Screen
          + Check-Delete-Permissions
          + Delete-The-Record
    (09) Modify                             -> Showing-Screen
          + Check-Update-Permissions
          + Validate-The-Record
          + Update-The-Record
          + Re-Position-Cursor
    (06) Show                               -> Showing-Screen
          + Check-If-Show-On-Field
          + Re-Position-Cursor
    (99) Get-Wanted                         -> Showing-Screen
          + Set-Key-Fields-Returned
          + Set-For-This-Or-Next
          + Find-The-Record
          + Validate-The-Record
    (99) Roll-Wanted                        -> Showing-Screen
          + Roll-The-Fields
          + Re-Position-Cursor
    (99) Prompt-Wanted                      -> Showing-Screen
          + Check-If-Prompt-On-Field
          + Return-From-Field-Prompt
          + Re-Position-Cursor
    (11) Prompt                             -> Showing-Screen
          + Check-If-Prompt-On-Field
          + Return-From-Field-Prompt
          + Re-Position-Cursor
    (99) List-Wanted                        -> Showing-Screen
          + Check-If-List-Records
          + Return-From-Record-List
          + Re-Position-Cursor
    (12) List                               -> Showing-Screen
          + Check-If-List-Records
          + Return-From-Record-List
          + Re-Position-Cursor
    (17) Enter                              -> Showing-Screen
          + Check-If-Click-On-Icon
          + Validate-Until-Cursor
          + Re-Position-Cursor
    (13) Menu                               -> Have-Desired-Action
          + Show-Menu-For-Menu
          + Get-Action-Bar-Operation
    (08) Action                             -> Have-Desired-Action
          + Show-Possible-Actions
          + Get-Action-Bar-Operation
    (99) Exception                          -> Confirming-Action
          + Pop-Up-Error-Message
          + Return-Error-Message
    (16) Cancel                             ->
          + Check-If-Data-Changed
          + Check-If-Key-Changed
          + Terminate-The-Program

Confirming-Action:
    (--) Ok                                 -> Showing-Screen
          + Reset-Change-Indicator
          + Get-Function-Key-Event
    (--) Click-Ok                           -> Showing-Screen
          + Reset-Change-Indicator
          + Get-Function-Key-Event
    (--) Click-Yes                          -> Showing-Screen
          + Reset-Change-Indicator
          + Get-Function-Key-Event
    (--) Click-Cancel                       -> Showing-Screen
    (--) Click-No                           -> Showing-Screen

Have-Desired-Action:
    (--) Call-Help                          -> Showing-Screen
          + Call-Dmhlp
          + Return-From-Help
    (--) About-Program                      -> Showing-Screen
          + Pop-Up-Copyright-Notice
          + Return-Error-Message
    (--) Click-Cancel                       -> Showing-Screen
    (--) Click-Exit                         -> Showing-Screen
    (--) Click-Reshow                       -> Have-Desired-Action
          + Reshow-The-Screen
          + Call-Dmact
          + Get-Action-Bar-Operation
    (--) Ok                                 -> Showing-Screen
    (--) Call-Program                       -> Showing-Screen
          + Call-The-Program
          + Return-From-Program

Defaults:
    (99) Exception                          ->
          + Pop-Up-Error-Message
          + Return-Error-Message
    (99) Call-Wanted                        ->
          + Menu--Call-The-Program
          + Menu--Check-Result
    (99) Exit-Wanted                        ->
          + Menu--Check-Exit
          + Terminate-The-Program
    (99) Menu-Wanted                        ->
          + Menu--Call-Menu-Processor
          + Menu--Check-Result
    (99) Ignore                             ->
    (15) Help                               ->
          + Select-Help-For-Context
          + Call-Dmhlp
          + Return-From-Help

Programs-To-Call:
    (--) Ok                                 ->
          + Call-Dmpop
          + Call-Dmact
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Paragraph Numbering and Program Structure

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@aiservices.com>
- **Date:** 1998-09-14T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** alt.cobol,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<hH0L1.347$pB3.1724901@news1.atlantic.net>`
- **References:** `<JgPK1.2535$p61.3962448@news4.mia.bellsouth.net> <6tgods$ps0@lotho.delphi.com> <fWUK1.343$pB3.1596991@news1.atlantic.net> <6thdo5$2ba@lotho.delphi.com>`

```

paulr wrote in message <6thdo5$2ba@lotho.delphi.com>...
>Well,
>[lots of snipping for brevity -quoting errors are mine]
>
[...]
>Packaging has to do with how tightly coupled a program's
>structure is; in this instance, I was reffering to being
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>
Procedure coupling can be reduced by using CALLs. Rather
than copying procedures into a program, CALL that procedure
as a subprogram. If you need to extend the capabilities of
the procedure, you may do so by modifying the subprogram.

>An example of that: lets take a base type and call it
>an employee-record. This is very common, and usually
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
>
It looks like you are talking about inheritance. It is not,
generally, a good idea to inherit from an employee class.
That is because, once an object of a particular type has
been instantiated, it cannot be changed to another type.
It is far better to treat the distinction of part-time as an
attribute, such as HoursClassification. Then inherit the
classes Exempt, FullTime, PartTime, etc. from
HoursClassification.

>And you are right in that qualified referencing is the
>methodology of choice for that. In this one case, I
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>
COBOL only requires two levels of reference. The first is the
elementary or group item that acts as the sending or receiving
operand. The second is any level in the hierarchy which
guarantees uniqueness of reference. In this case
Employee-Number of Employee-Master-File would suffice.
It is not necessary to include any intermediate group names
in the hierarchy.

>(only a bit, I grant you, but which one is easier to deal
>with? Hint, English speaking people tend to read
…[20 more quoted lines elided]…
>
Encapsulation can be implemented through CALLs. For
example, I could have two employee records: EmployeePublic
and EmployeePrivate. When you CALL a subprogram to get
an employee record, I would return only the EmployeePublic
record. You would be required to use the CALL interface to
get access to the fields of the EmployeePrivate record.
You would also use the CALL interface to execute the
methods associated with the Employee.

>And that is the real key; if you don't allow people to
>have direct access to data you store, then you can change
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>
I am not sure there are *that* many changes that may be done
to data without affecting the user programs. Most changes
would almost assuredly appear in the interface.

>Again, this is not stuff that is new to COBOL, heck, probably
>half these ideas were invented by some COBOL programmer with
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>
The deeper I got into OOP, the less impressed I became.
There are benefits, to be sure, but there are also costs and I
have yet to determine that, for most problems, the benefits
will outweigh the costs.
-------------------------------
Rick Smith
e-mail: < ricksmith@aiservices.com >
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Paragraph Numbering and Program Structure

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** paulr@bix.com (paulr)
- **Date:** 1998-09-15T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** alt.cobol,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<6tkctt$deo@lotho.delphi.com>`
- **References:** `<JgPK1.2535$p61.3962448@news4.mia.bellsouth.net> <6tgods$ps0@lotho.delphi.com> <fWUK1.343$pB3.1596991@news1.atlantic.net> <6thdo5$2ba@lotho.delphi.com> <hH0L1.347$pB3.1724901@news1.atlantic.net>`

```
Well, like I said, we do most the OO things already,
we just don't label them OO. I disagree with your
ideas regarding inheritance; it is a very common
thing to create a base type like employee - and 
use it as a virtual type with no instaniations. 

OO technology is here to stay, and despite 
people I have met who are adamantly against
OO techbology (for whatever reason, 
benefit, efficiency, etc.) almost all of them
have changed their minds when exposed to a
shop with and OO methodology. It really 
DOES make sense, though it is for some reason,
hard for people to accept. 

-Paul
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Paragraph Numbering and Program Structure

- **From:** zberger@ldl.net
- **Date:** 1998-09-14T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** alt.cobol,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<6tjhq2$7ba$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>`
- **References:** `<JgPK1.2535$p61.3962448@news4.mia.bellsouth.net> <6tgods$ps0@lotho.delphi.com> <fWUK1.343$pB3.1596991@news1.atlantic.net>`

```
In article <fWUK1.343$pB3.1596991@news1.atlantic.net>,
  "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@aiservices.com> wrote:
>
> paulr wrote in message <6tgods$ps0@lotho.delphi.com>...
…[14 more quoted lines elided]…
> operational and code structure that the name, alone, cannot.

Get-Employee-ID has not structure...it does what it says...gets the employee
id.  And 'perform get-employee-id' should be as close to an atomic (in an hll
sense) instruction as it can be, e.g. it should not get the id, move it n
different places, calculate months on the job or anything else.

With the name 'Get-Employee-ID' we know it just gets the ID.  If you name it
'1234567890-get-employee-ID' it does whatever is needed at place 1234567890
in the structure of that specific program (very hard to just cut and paste)
is unclear to anyone who has ever used another language and adds absolutely
nothing to the understanding of the program unless you are still using punch
cards and a data-entry department.

> >It is also the area where OO technology / methodology can and
> >proabably will make the most signifigant impact in the COBOL
…[23 more quoted lines elided]…
>         CALL "CalculatePay" USING Employee, PayInfo ?

If the entire HR system had an 'Employee' object and one could always invoke
'Get-Employee-ID' and get exactly that, the same identical copy of
get-employee-id for all instances...if the employee ID ever changes (like
adding 2 digits to it :) then one method needs a change, not 6,000 (random
number) programs...of course, if each of the 6,000 programs had things like
(1234-get-employee-id, 1234-load-emp-no, 1234567890-emp-id,
98766462512375767-wrkr-num, ad infinitium) then you will have a huge headache
adding those to digits to a smallish HR system....

If you had "Get-employee-ID" in a copybook (or an object) things
become simple (but you can't custom number it to match the structure of each
using program).

In short, the unnumbered version promotes code reuse and consistency, the
numbered version does not (my observation).

-----== Posted via Deja News, The Leader in Internet Discussion ==-----
http://www.dejanews.com/rg_mkgrp.xp   Create Your Own Free Member Forum
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Paragraph Numbering and Program Structure

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@aiservices.com>
- **Date:** 1998-09-14T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** alt.cobol,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<eHdL1.400$pB3.2006553@news1.atlantic.net>`
- **References:** `<JgPK1.2535$p61.3962448@news4.mia.bellsouth.net> <6tgods$ps0@lotho.delphi.com> <fWUK1.343$pB3.1596991@news1.atlantic.net> <6tjhq2$7ba$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>`

```

zberger@ldl.net wrote in message <6tjhq2$7ba$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>...
>In article <fWUK1.343$pB3.1596991@news1.atlantic.net>,

>
>If the entire HR system had an 'Employee' object and one could always
invoke
>'Get-Employee-ID' and get exactly that, the same identical copy of
>get-employee-id for all instances...if the employee ID ever changes (like
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>98766462512375767-wrkr-num, ad infinitium) then you will have a huge
headache
>adding those to digits to a smallish HR system....
>
Getting any value is never done in isolation. If I invoke a method
to get an employee ID, I still need to know its format; and, that
format must exist in the using program. Changing the format has
the same effect in both OO and structured solutions; wherever
the value is used, the format must be known and, therefore,
modified along with the definition in the class. For example,
if Employee-ID is a key field for accessing data associated with
the employee then the picture of the employee ID must be
changed in the associated files and where those records are
used, as well.

Whether one is using OO or not, it is, probably, better to code all
mutable data definitions with TYPEDEFs. Using Employee-ID as
an example:

        01 Employee-ID-Type is TYPEDEF PIC 9(5).

then, wherever a copy of the Employee-ID is defined, use

        05 Employee-ID is Employee-ID-Type.

If two digits are added to Employee-ID, one need only change the
TYPEDEF picture clause and the code that depends upon the actual
length of the employee ID. The dependency upon length would likely
occur at the boundaries of the system, such as the user interface,
reports, and any import/export routines. These same boundaries
apply to both OO and structured programs.

Of course, any persistent data would need be converted before
putting the changes into effect.

>If you had "Get-employee-ID" in a copybook (or an object) things
>become simple (but you can't custom number it to match the structure of
each
>using program).
>
From a copy book, one could use the REPLACING clause to
replace a generic prefix with the actual prefix.

>In short, the unnumbered version promotes code reuse and consistency, the
>numbered version does not (my observation).
>
-------------------------------
Rick Smith
e-mail: < ricksmith@aiservices.com >
```

```
paulr wrote in message <6tgods$ps0@lotho.delphi.com>...
>It is also the area where OO technology / methodology can and
>proabably will make the most signifigant impact in the COBOL
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>"perform."


The PEFORM keyword is better than having no keyword.  When I'm deep into
scanning a large C program, I always have to look twice to see if something
was a variable or a function.  The () at the end usually doesn't help me
much, as I don't visually tag to the end of a line, but rather the
beginning.  Thus the need to look twice.

If you've always done C, you're obviously not going to run into this
problem.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Paragraph Numbering and Program Structure

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc123@bellsouth.net>
- **Date:** 1998-09-14T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** alt.cobol,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<wP4L1.1727$te.2560303@news2.mia.bellsouth.net>`
- **References:** `<JgPK1.2535$p61.3962448@news4.mia.bellsouth.net> <6tgods$ps0@lotho.delphi.com>`

```
paulr wrote:
>Interesting, but, is it really necessary?
>I have a strong background in Ada, Pascal, and C, as well
>as several assembly languages, about a dozen other languages,
>their dialects, and of course, COBOL. The one thing that
>bugs me about COBOL is using those numbers. :)


Of course, there is nothing about the COBOL language itself which
would demand numbers in paragraph names.  I suspect it has to do
a lot with the kinds of programs written in COBOL.  I frequently
write large online systems.  In these systems, you often have a
large number of routines which are roughly similar.  Probably not
similar enough to use OO classes, but similar enough to warrant
identical names.  If it were not for paragraph numbers, you would
be constantly thinking up similar, but different names.  This can
lead to serious programming errors.  With paragraph names, it is
a relatively simple process to scan a program to see all PERFORMs
withing the 22nnnn section which reference paragraph not in the
22nnnn range.  I have a small BASIC program to do that.  But if
I made the paragraph names unique with no numbers, there would be
no way to automate that.  I think most people not used to this
method don't realize what kind of verification this provides. I
typically bring up a new system, fix maybe 6 or 10 bugs in the
first few weeks, then don't hear from the client again until
they want something new.  I have never brought up a new system
around somebody who did not know me, that they did not express
amazement at how cleanly it came up.  Never.  At the same time, I
maintain a high productivity, around 200k lines/year.  The way I
do this is by employing a number of methods and techniques, each
one contributing some bit toward precision and/or productivity.
Each method or tool has been selected or discarded after careful
study and trial.  This method is one of them.  The proof of the
pudding is in the eating.  It's as simple as that. :-)

>Program structure shoudl be obvious from the naming, not
>from a numering scheme. This is where the 30 character limit
>intrudes, and is probably why people took to numbering in
>COBOL in the first place.

Naming tells you about purpose, not structure.

>It is also the area where OO technology / methodology can and
>proabably will make the most signifigant impact in the COBOL
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>something from the 20+ years of OO technology out there. :)


We can also learn from what proves effective, whether it matches
our theories, or not. ;-)
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Paragraph Numbering and Program Structure

- **From:** zberger@ldl.net
- **Date:** 1998-09-16T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** alt.cobol,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<6tonk3$5ej$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>`
- **References:** `<JgPK1.2535$p61.3962448@news4.mia.bellsouth.net> <6tgods$ps0@lotho.delphi.com> <wP4L1.1727$te.2560303@news2.mia.bellsouth.net>`

```
In article <wP4L1.1727$te.2560303@news2.mia.bellsouth.net>,
  "Judson McClendon" <judmc123@bellsouth.net> wrote:
> paulr wrote:
>[snip]
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
> no way to automate that.  I think most people not used to this

If you have a large number of 'roughly similar' routines, doesn't that scream
loudly to you that you need one 'general routine' with some parms to control
just how 'roughly similar' it needs to be?

An example of what I mean, assume an HR system:
   1000-Calc-profit-share.
      do profit share code for employee < 1 year
      goto 1000-exit
      .
   1100-Calc-profit-share.
      do profit share code for employee < 5 year
      if (whatever) goto 1000-exit
      .
   1200-Calc-profit-share.
      do profit share code for employee < 10 year
      if (whatever) goto 1000-exit
      .
   1300-Calc-profit-share.
      do profit share code for employee < 20 year
      if (whatever) goto 1000-exit
      .
   1400-Calc-profit-share.
      do profit share code for employee > 20 year
      if (whatever) goto 1000-exit
      .
   1000-exit
      exit
      .

This is a real world example (that actually meets a certain nameless shop
standard) that is horrid...

How you ask?

  - the object for this paragraph will roughly 5 times larger than needed.  -
persons that are employeed > 1 year use up extra cycles for no effect  - It
is too wordy to fit on a single screen or single page (when the actual) code
is inserted, thus making it easier to miss something.  - Maintenance to one
paragraph might (or might not ;) require maintenance to one or more of the
other paragraphs.  - Maintenance to one paragraph might (or might not ;)
break one or more of the other paragraphs.  - Excessive use of gotos when
unnecessary detracts from understanding of control flow...also has great
potential to hose up the optimizer, especially when called with 'perform ...
thru ...' as is usually the case in these constructs.

In any other language this would have been written using a construct like:

    pftShr := calcProfitShare(yearsOnJob)

this addresses the problems I cited above, there is:

  - No duplicate code
  - No toasting of control flow
  - Easily optimized
  - Single point of maintenance
  - Changes are isolated from other code

I submit that the correct way to code this (even without functions & objects)
would be something like:

  (copybook PFTSHRWS)
     05  Profit-share-stuff.
       10  PS-hire-date                pic whatever.
       10  PS-profit-share-result      pic whatever.
       10  PS-any work fields needed....

  (copybook PFTSHRCD)
     Calc-profit-share.
          do whatever to calculate results
          exit.

  and in the code, copy the two and:

     move emp-date-hire to ps-hire-date
     perform calc-profit-share
     move ps-profit-share-result to somewhare

Much cleaner, much easier to maintain, much harder to break.....just my humble
$0.02

    Regards,
    Joe Zitzelberger

-----== Posted via Deja News, The Leader in Internet Discussion ==-----
http://www.dejanews.com/rg_mkgrp.xp   Create Your Own Free Member Forum
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Paragraph Numbering and Program Structure

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc123@bellsouth.net>
- **Date:** 1998-09-16T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** alt.cobol,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7xUL1.1521$eM5.1621221@news4.mia.bellsouth.net>`
- **References:** `<JgPK1.2535$p61.3962448@news4.mia.bellsouth.net> <6tgods$ps0@lotho.delphi.com> <wP4L1.1727$te.2560303@news2.mia.bellsouth.net> <6tonk3$5ej$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>`

```
zberger@ldl.net wrote:
>Judson McClendon wrote:
>> Of course, there is nothing about the COBOL language itself which
…[16 more quoted lines elided]…
>some parms to control just how 'roughly similar' it needs to be?

No, that's my point.  The routines tend to be similar in concept, but
very different in detail, enough so to make it counterproductive to
try and use a single common routine, or an OO class.  I realize that
this situation is not common to all COBOL software, but it is very
in the software of my mainframe clients.

Let me speak a little more broadly, not specifically to your post.
What I have seen happen a number of times, is other companies come in
to one of my clients and propose a change in paradigm, to one of the
more 'modern' methods, such as 4GL.  My clients respond by asking for
a benchmark test.  Two teams are selected, one from my client, one
from the other company, and both teams are given a simple system,
cooked up and agreed on right then by both parties, to implement.
In every single case, my client's team, using the coding standards I
taught them, literally *trounced* the other teams.  When I say they
trounced them, I'm not kidding.  Typically they finished in 1/4 the
time of the other guys, with a better end product.  This has happened
three times to one of my clients.

I get a bit frustrated sometimes at the attitude displayed in some of
the posts (though not by Joe's post above) in this thread, that simply
because some technique is newer it *must* be better, or that an older
technique *must* be inferior.  It is a fact that they make paper clips,
not only exactly as they did over 100 years ago, but in many cases with
the very same machines.  Why?  Simply because nobody has come up with
a better machine, and it is very unlikely that anyone will, anytime
soon.  There is such a thing as developing a method which works so well
for a certain specific task, that it cannot be improved upon, or will
only be improved after a long time.  Newer may be better, and often is.
But newer is not *always* better.  Older can be as good as newer, or
even much better.  The thing is to look at the result, not at the hype.
Use the newer method when it is better, but be open to those situations
when older happens to be as good, or better than, the new.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Paragraph Numbering and Program Structure

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "Chris Osborne" <chris_n_osborne@yahoo.com>
- **Date:** 1998-09-16T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** alt.cobol,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<360059f6.0@news2.uswest.net>`

```
How does that go?

Older = Proven.  (one way or another)
New = Unproven.  (one way or another)

The big trouble is getting anyone to agree on what has, and what hasn't,
been proven (one way or another). ;)
```

#### ↳ Re: Paragraph Numbering and Program Structure

- **From:** "COBOL Frog (Huib Klink)" <H.Klink@IMN.nl>
- **Date:** 1998-09-14T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** alt.cobol,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<35FD8396.CBE8B773@IMN.nl>`
- **References:** `<JgPK1.2535$p61.3962448@news4.mia.bellsouth.net>`

```
Judson McClendon wrote:

> I was taught as a beginning programmer to use ascending numeric prefixes
> on all my paragraph names.

8< 8< 8< [need new scissors :) ]
After reading Judsons original post and all those answers I'll put in my own
philosofies.

The usage of external general functions or procedures in a procedural
environment or the usage of methods defined in classes in an OO environment is
completely different from the usage of internal subroutines in a program. The
former routines / methods are intended to be re-used in other (future) contexts
and as such should be clearly defined and named to make them reusable. The
latter ones, internal subroutines, are building blocks of a component and not
meant to be exposed outside, so naming is less important, at least the name will
be seen by less people.

But in a more conventional environment one sees the following kind of programs
lots of times:

Batch programs with multilevel structures, based on processing sequential piles
of data, sorted on one or more key fields which unavoidably shows up in the
program's structure. Of course OO techniques could be applied against such type
of data processing but would IMO not give some extra strength: development will
not be much quicker in time, re-use of classes tends to be low (every separate
data set to proces is a new class). The "standard "approach" using known methods
and techniques, like JSP, IDO, etc (all based on Bï¿½hm/Jacopini concepts,
possibly I am a little benulux oriented here) is reliable, fast and proven.

On-line programs running in transaction oriented environments under/with things
like CICS, IMS-DC, IDMS-DC and other look-alike tp-monitors, have a lot of
similar subroutines, all coming from (and thus unavoidably programmed in
reaction on) the standardized calls/statements/structures that are enforced by
the pseudo-conversational tp-monitor. Lots of the stuff in this world is more of
the same. software products look a lot the same, hardware is a lot the same.
Everybody mimiced IBM in those days ...

In all those situations, the internal program structure could very well be
cleared up (or made some clearer) by using a numbering/naming scheme like
Judson's.

Very different will be the components and their public API's that are build to
compose systems that have to run in environments where modern gadgets rule the
world: OLE, clipboard, ActiveX, ORB's etc. etc. These systems tend to look like
the environment they run in (just like the CICS programs): open-enden API's,
event-driven. Lots of the parts of such systems will be accessible from outside
and as such require a name for usage i.s.o. a number.

In OO (ve it "pure" or some little OO) it is good usage NOT to expose your
internals to the outside world. An object has his well-defined interface to get
from state to state and uses names for that interface, not (containing) numbers
that would show some of the hidden implementation.

After that public face, the body of the object could very well contain some (or
lots) private properties, like internal sub-routines. The builder of that
object/program/function/component could very well number those to reflect some
things, internal structure for example. Just he and perhaps one or two
maintenance programmers are the people that have to understand or change those
numbers. Those 2 or 3 people are a lot less than the audience that will use the
object/prog/func/compo by it's public interface.

Hey: that numbering is a great tool when used at the right time.
A good day to all!!

Frog, Theaching Programming
```

#### ↳ Re: Paragraph Numbering and Program Structure

- **From:** "Ginseng Jones" <chrgeo@inficad.com>
- **Date:** 1998-09-14T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** alt.cobol,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<6tksha$f7c$1@news.inficad.com>`
- **References:** `<JgPK1.2535$p61.3962448@news4.mia.bellsouth.net>`

```
All I can say is from 11+ years of COBOL on IBM MVS systems, I have rarely
ever seen a program without a numeric prefix (9999-) ... on occasion that I
have, that program was soon rewritten to adhere to the paragraph number
prefix "standards" ... not sure what is kosher in the peecee weenie world
... but I have worked for 4 different shops (large companies)

And speaking from a maintenance standpoint, due to the length of most
programs, it makes it a heck of lot easier looking for 2200-GET-EMPLOYEE-SEG
instead of GET-EMPLOYEE-REC, where you have no idea where that code stub is
buried in 10,000+ lines of PROCEDURE DIVISION code ... in the academic world
everything fits into place eloquently but in the real world this is never
the case ... and these are ALREADY sub-divided into a hierarchy of
sub-programs ... yes, the real world is different when 10 million records
have to flow thru the code ...
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Paragraph Numbering and Program Structure

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc123@bellsouth.net>
- **Date:** 1998-09-15T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** alt.cobol,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<NwwL1.970$eM5.845330@news4.mia.bellsouth.net>`

```
Ubiquitous wrote:
>
>Judson McClendon says:
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
>SIX digits? I've rarely needed anything bigger than three.

Some of the online programs I work with are 50,000 to 100,000 lines, and
there's lots of paragraphs in one of those. :)  I use six all the time to
make it easier to copy code from one program to another.  Also, three or
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Paragraph Numbering and Program Structure

- **From:** stevewie@apk.net (SkippyPB)
- **Date:** 1998-09-15T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<35ff975a.8613613@news.apk.net>`
- **References:** `<JgPK1.2535$p61.3962448@news4.mia.bellsouth.net> <905853493.44330@flux>`

```
On 15 Sep 1998 09:57:10 GMT, weberm@polaris.net (Ubiquitous)
enlightened us with:

>In article <JgPK1.2535$p61.3962448@news4.mia.bellsouth.net>, "Judson McClendon" <judmc123@bellsouth.net> says:
>
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>SIX digits? I've rarely needed anything bigger than three.

In the environment that I work in, our paragraph names use a
combination of alpha and numberic prefixes, e.g. A1000-00-INIT.

This format is followed through all of our systems.  Therefore I know
what is being done in any program for any of our systems by the
paragraph names and I know where to find them in the program.  

Regards,


          ////
         (o o)
-oOO--(_)--OOo-
Is there another word for synonym?
 Steve
```

#### ↳ Re: Paragraph Numbering and Program Structure

- **From:** Lookin'@You.2 (WDS)
- **Date:** 1998-09-21T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** alt.cobol,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36041f60.4902056@news1.frb.gov>`
- **References:** `<JgPK1.2535$p61.3962448@news4.mia.bellsouth.net>`

```
On Sun, 13 Sep 1998 12:55:05 GMT, "Judson McClendon"
<judmc123@bellsouth.net> wrote:
>
[snip description and illustration of paragraph numbering and related
program structure]

I use a method similar to what you describe.  While no two metnods are
exactly the same, I have seen several that were related.  Some use
three digits, some use five, some use three preceeded by a letter,
etc., but the improved ability to read (and adhere to) the underlying
program structrue is enhanced, if properly used.  Often, areas of
working storage are keyed into this scheme, as well.

A scheme of one's own choice may often be used for isolated programs
written from scratch.  However, the specifics of the numbering/naming
schemes, etc., are often dictated by published standards within an
organization.  Also, when working on an existing program, the ability
to use such a system is obviously dependent upon what was originally
written and upon who wrote it.
```

#### ↳ Re: Paragraph Numbering and Program Structure

- **From:** Charles F Hankel <charles@hankel.mersinet.co.uk>
- **Date:** 1998-09-21T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** alt.cobol,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36058B5E.49281975@hankel.mersinet.co.uk>`
- **References:** `<JgPK1.2535$p61.3962448@news4.mia.bellsouth.net>`

```
Judson McClendon wrote:
> 
> I was taught as a beginning programmer to use ascending numeric prefixes
> on all my paragraph names.  Over the years, I have kept this practice, and

Hey, what's up Judson?  Trying to wake up a couple of newgroups?

By the way, I prefix.  Even with screens and editors, it still helps me
navigate around the source.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Paragraph Numbering and Program Structure

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc123@bellsouth.net>
- **Date:** 1998-09-21T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** alt.cobol,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<FRsN1.18$UQ1.69156@news3.mia.bellsouth.net>`
- **References:** `<JgPK1.2535$p61.3962448@news4.mia.bellsouth.net> <36058B5E.49281975@hankel.mersinet.co.uk>`

```
Charles F Hankel wrote:
>Judson McClendon wrote:
>>
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>Hey, what's up Judson?  Trying to wake up a couple of newgroups?

With not much success, it seems. ;-)

>By the way, I prefix.  Even with screens and editors, it still helps me
>navigate around the source.

Yes it does.  I love my PC based text editors, and they significantly
improve my productivity.  However, it is quicker to just look and see
what the structure is than it is to have to search for each paragraph
you're interested in, trying to discern what the structure is.  You
write it only once, you may read it many times.  The #1 rule I follow
in formatting source code is this:

  "The program listing serves but a single purpose: to convey information
  about the program through the programmer�s eyes to the programmer�s
  brain as efficiently as possible"

The better it serves this purpose, the more maintainable the program is.
This issue is quite separate from program structure, which is also very
important, of course.  Once the structure is determined, how best should
the code be formatted to achieve the above result?  The answer is tied
closely to the way the eye and brain processes visual information, and
is not nearly as subjective as many think.  One can adapt to any number
of personal preferences, but many of these preferences cannot be defended
from an objective standpoint.  I've spent a good portion of the last 25
years thinking about, studying and experimenting with these issues.  The
result is a series of programming standards which I have made available
and taught to my clients.  The results in improved programming accuracy
and productivity have been so impressive that many MIS shops in this
area have adopted some or all of my standards on their own, distributed
solely through word of mouth.  It is unfortunate that some programmers
exhibit a knee-jerk opposition to methods which do not fit their P.C.
thinking.  The question is, did they come to this P.C. thinking through
objective test and study, or are they merely parroting what they have
been taught by others who are also parroting what they have been taught?
This is a question each person should ask themselves.  Personally, I
always try to remain open to techniques and methods which might improve
my productivity.  I have found this approach to be extremely beneficial.
```

<a id="t-53d52c82f4"></a>
## Thread: Should I learn Cobol before VB — 3 msgs

_Topics: [`Tutorials, books, learning`](topics.md#learning)_

### Re: Should I learn Cobol before VB

- **From:** cwestbury@giant.intranet.com (Chris Westbury)
- **Date:** 1998-09-13T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<1998Sep13.115045.19248@giant>`
- **References:** `<6snjao$q54$3@news-1.news.gte.net> <35fd79eb.10058868@news.online.no>`

```
In article <35fd79eb.10058868@news.online.no>,
itlandm@online.no (Magnus Itland) wrote:
>
> A bit over ten years ago, I spent time and energy tweaking a large
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> Quartz sand is cheap, human brains are expensive.

All well and good, _if_ you have clients who can afford to wait three
years for the state of the art to catch up to their needs.

Your client bought three years from you at a price agreeable to you both. 
If you don't enjoy doing that kind of work, OK, you don't have to do it
again, but I don't see how you come to the conclusion that it isn't worth
it.  That's for the client to decide.

Besides, it takes less time and energy to write an efficient program in
the first place than to tweak it later.
```

#### ↳ Re: Should I learn Cobol before VB

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1998-09-13T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<6th799$lt3@sjx-ixn10.ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<6snjao$q54$3@news-1.news.gte.net> <35fd79eb.10058868@news.online.no> <1998Sep13.115045.19248@giant>`

```

Chris Westbury wrote in message <1998Sep13.115045.19248@giant>...
>In article <35fd79eb.10058868@news.online.no>,
>itlandm@online.no (Magnus Itland) wrote:
…[25 more quoted lines elided]…
>Christopher Westbury, Midtown Associates, 15 Fallon Place, Cambridge, MA
02138

When I worked for Micro Focus, it always struck me as "odd" (and something
of a statement on the LESSENING dominance of COBOL) that no vendor came
along and created an "optimizer" for the various PC COBOL compilers - as
they had originally for the IBM OS/VS COBOL compiler.  I know that Fujitsu
(now) claims to optimize much better than Micro Focus and I know that Micro
Focus *ALWAYS* tries to put additional optimization in each of its releases
and that IBM VisualAge COBOL uses much (but not all) the same optimization
as available on the mainframe. HOWEVER, I also now from working for a PC
vendor, that if someone actually spent the time/effort to create a separate
"optimization" product that read the source code, object code, and
INT/p-code (where available) the same way that Capex (then CA) did with
OS/VS COBOL, that they could come up with REAL optimized COBOL run-time
code.  (Just think what it could do for AcuCOBOL's "portable" code.)  My
assumption of why this never happened are:
  - there are so many DIFFERENT COBOL compilers for the PC (unlike the "olde
days" when OS/VS COBOL really dominated the business market)
  - the assumption is that there is a continually shrinking COBOL market and
that no one thinks such a product (products?) would be profitable
 - OS/VS COBOL was before "object code only" and the information wouldn't be
there for a vendor to "optimize" the proprietary COBOL object code.
 - The people who have the "computer science" background to understand
optimization techniques really can't stand COBOL so they don't even look at
this as a possible product.

Bottom-Line: I think it would be GREAT if the COBOL workstation vendors put
more effort into optimizing their executables, I think it might be even
better if some 3rd party got into this business, but UNFORTUNATELY, I won't
hold my breath until anyone takes this seriously.

P.S. Just try suggesting to Micro Focus that they optimize their code for
applications running in IBM mainframe compatibility mode (CHARSET(EBCDIC)
ARITHMETIC(OSVS) PERFORM-TYPE(OSVS) STICKY-LINKAGE IBMCOMP etc).  I know
that I never could get them to even think about this - and surprisingly few
of our (their) customers actually ever asked for it.  Almost all programs
running in this environment were run as "INT" code and walking was a better
description than "running"!
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Should I learn Cobol before VB

- **From:** cwestbury@giant.intranet.com (Chris Westbury)
- **Date:** 1998-09-20T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<1998Sep20.084425.19261@giant>`
- **References:** `<6snjao$q54$3@news-1.news.gte.net> <6th799$lt3@sjx-ixn10.ix.netcom.com>`

```
In article <6th799$lt3@sjx-ixn10.ix.netcom.com>,
"William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com> wrote:
>
> When I worked for Micro Focus, it always struck me as "odd" (and
> something of a statement on the LESSENING dominance of COBOL) that no
> vendor came along and created an "optimizer" for the various PC COBOL
> compilers - as they had originally for the IBM OS/VS COBOL compiler.

The question is not "can you do X?", but "can you make money doing X?".

IBM's OS/VS CoBOL inadvertently created the evolutionary niche which was
soon occupied by third-party optimizers.

The first factor is that IBM makes money from hardware.  The purpose of
IBM software is to consume as much memory, machine cycles, and EXCPs as
possible so that you will have to buy more hardware.  But it can't just
throw away cycles; people won't stand for that.  IBM's single greatest
achievement over the years is the _plausible_ waste of machine time:
waste that the customer not only doesn't notice, but actually thinks he
wants.

The second factor is that in a large corporation every single run is
charged back to some department in fully-burdened dollars.

The third factor is the peculiar deficiencies of OS/VS CoBOL.  I don't
want to get too deep into this because even on my own compilers I'm never
given enough time to do the code generator right, unless it's ForTran. 
Let's just say there were opportunities there, opportunities that are not
present in today's compilers.

Given unnecessary waste that IBM won't fix for strategic reasons and that
the customer sees in monthly chargeback, if you can show a real reduction
in chargeback, there's room for your fee.


[...]
> My assumption of why this never happened are:
>
>  - there are so many DIFFERENT COBOL compilers for the PC (unlike the
>    "olde days" when OS/VS COBOL really dominated the business market)

Yup.


>  - the assumption is that there is a continually shrinking COBOL market
>    and that no one thinks such a product (products?) would be
>    profitable

I'm not sure the shrinking (or expanding) CoBOL market enters into it. 
The question is, how can you charge?  If you can't charge, you can't make
a profit.  I can get a thousand dollars a month on a mainframe because a)
the savings are demonstrable, and b) they are in real dollar chargebacks. 

PC users have no idea what they are spending.  Published studies range
from $5k to $20k per seat per year.  Since they don't know what they are
spending, they can't see increases or reductions.  The readers of this
newsgroup balk at paying a thousand dollars for a whole compiler, never
mind a thousand dollars a month!  What, then, would they pay for an
optimizer?  Not enough for me to write one, that's for sure.


[...]
>  - the people who have the "computer science" background to understand
>    optimization techniques really can't stand COBOL so they don't even
>    look at this as a possible product.

I have to say that the people who have the "computer science" background
aren't the ones who look at something as a possible product.  Anyone who
wants to make such a product can hire the expertise.


> Bottom-line: I think it would be GREAT if the COBOL workstation vendors
> put more effort into optimizing their executables, I think it might be
> even better if some 3rd party got into this business, but
> UNFORTUNATELY, I won't hold my breath until anyone takes this
> seriously.

It's too bad that the amount of effort spent on CoBOL optimization by all
vendors isn't even one tenth of the amount spent on ForTran optimization
by all vendors, but, unfortunately, as long as there's no money in it for
the vendors, that's the way it's going to be.
```

## Thread: y2k-proofing my programs — 19 msgs

_Topics: [`Y2K and Year-2000 remediation`](topics.md#y2k)_

### y2k-proofing my programs

- **From:** skidmike <skidmike@mindspring.com>
- **Date:** 1998-09-13T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<35FC314B.6C5EE424@mindspring.com>`

```
i tried to post this before and i never saw it, i got some email telling
me that my time zone was wrong.  (damn those black helicopters!)
anyway, i'm an amateur writing a system to run my muffler shop on and
hopefully sell the software.  the only way i see that it would be
subject to y2k is the fiscal calendar, purging old repair estimates
after 15 days, and billing commercial accounts.  my idea was to have in
the system's "control file" a field for yesterday's date yymmdd.  at
each start-of-day routine, the current date is ACCEPTed from the system
and compared to the previous date.  only once every thousand years would
the date in format yymmdd be "less than" the previous day's date.  since
the system will use a 4-digit year the system will check for the
less-than condition and add 20 instead of 19 to the year.  (i checked,
my amd-k6 stores only 2 digits so far as i can tell so it is a perfect
test machine)
ok, what do you think?
```

#### ↳ Re: y2k-proofing my programs

- **From:** "Leif Svalgaard" <leif@ibm.net>
- **Date:** 1998-09-13T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<35fc35fb.0@news1.ibm.net>`
- **References:** `<35FC314B.6C5EE424@mindspring.com>`

```
skidmike wrote in message <35FC314B.6C5EE424@mindspring.com>...
>each start-of-day routine, the current date is ACCEPTed from the system
>and compared to the previous date.  only once every thousand years would
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>ok, what do you think?

It is the Cobol runtime that determines what ACCEPT returns.
The AMD-K6 has nothing to to with this.

You don't really need a "yesterday" field. The standard way of
getting the 4-digit year goes like this:

01  THE-DATE      PIC 9(8).
01  FILLER            REDEFINES THE-DATE.
     02  CC                         PIC 99.
     02  YYMMDD              PIC 9(6).
     02  FILLER                  REDEFINES YYMMDD.
            03  YY                    PIC 99.
            03  MM                  PIC 99.
            03  DD                  PIC 99.

ACCEPT YYMMDD FROM DATE
IF YY < 98
      MOVE 20 TO CC
ELSE
      MOVE 19 TO CC
.

This should work for the next 100 years.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: y2k-proofing my programs

- **From:** skidmike <skidmike@mindspring.com>
- **Date:** 1998-09-13T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<35FC48BA.25E4B6EB@mindspring.com>`
- **References:** `<35FC314B.6C5EE424@mindspring.com> <35fc35fb.0@news1.ibm.net>`

```
i thought about that, but any "if less than <year in this century>"
seemed to be too "patchy" for me.  any way i try to ACCEPT the date from
DATE i get a 6 position field with only the last 2 digits of the year
available.  the way i see it is to let the program find the difference
under a controlled condition  i.e.:
   ACCEPT DATE-IN FROM DATE
       000101
   MOVE date-in to the 8 digit field: 00000101
   OPEN...
   READ...
   IF DATE-STO (which is 98981231) > DATE-IN (which is 00000101) THEN
       string 20 <date-in> into current-date-usage
   the program thereby y2k-proofs itself, with a lot less code.
   oh, and before i forget, CLOSE the control file (which contains more
than the date)
Leif Svalgaard wrote:
> 
> skidmike wrote in message <35FC314B.6C5EE424@mindspring.com>...
…[31 more quoted lines elided]…
> This should work for the next 100 years.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: y2k-proofing my programs

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc123@bellsouth.net>
- **Date:** 1998-09-14T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<xc7L1.6$kN6.13736@news2.mia.bellsouth.net>`
- **References:** `<35FC314B.6C5EE424@mindspring.com> <35fc35fb.0@news1.ibm.net>`

```
A more flexible method is to use a 'moving window'.  In cases where
the date being entered will always be within a certain range of the
then current date, it is easy to resolve the date into the correct
century window in a reliable way.  Here is code which demonstrates a
window of current date -79 years to current date +20 years.  The low
and high numbers can be whatever you choose, but the difference
(high number - low number) must be exactly 99 years.  This code will
work reliably until the year 9999.  One assumes COBOL 85 will be
obsolete by then. ;-)  You can't use this method for birth dates,
unless you will always be dealing with a range of ages no more than
a century wide (e.g. ages 5 to 104: low = -104, high = -5).

           03  CURR-CCYY        PIC  9(04).
           03  CURR-CCYY-R      REDEFINES CURR-CCYY.
               05  CURR-CC          PIC  9(02).
               05  CURR-YY          PIC  9(02).
           03  WORK-CCYY        PIC  9(04).
           03  WORK-CCYY-R      REDEFINES WORK-CCYY.
               05  WORK-CC          PIC  9(02).
               05  WORK-YY          PIC  9(02).

           MOVE <2 digit year> TO WORK-YY.
           PERFORM RESOLVE-CENTURY.
      *    (Resolved 4 digit year is now in WORK-CCYY)

       RESOLVE-CENTURY.
           MOVE FUNCTION CURRENT-DATE (1:4) TO CURR-CCYY.
           MOVE CURR-CC TO WORK-CC.
           IF (WORK-CCYY < CURR-CCYY - 79)
               ADD 100 TO WORK-CCYY
           ELSE IF (WORK-CCYY > CURR-CCYY + 20)
               SUBTRACT 100 FROM WORK-CCYY.
```

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol

```

skidmike wrote:
> 
> i tried to post this before and i never saw it, i got some email telling
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> and compared to the previous date. 
   
  OK, lets just stop here and let me ask a few questions to see if
I understand what you are attempting to accomplish.  I may not 
fully understand what you are doing and why you need to store
the date on a control file.

    1)  I am assuming when you start up the computer first thing 
         in the morning it will be set up to perform some of the 
         house keeping functions, purge files of old estimates ect.

     2) Question, why do you want to store yesterdays date? I 
          would assume that it would be determine when it is the
          first start up of the day so you would perform the
          house keeping routine?  By storing the date you will make sure
          if the computer is restarted during the day that the house
          keeping function is performed only once.
         

> only once every thousand years would the date in format yymmdd 
>be "less than" the previous day's date.  since
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> ok, what do you think?

  I think I more interested in why you are doing this rather than how.
You have a coupe of good ideas posted on how what I am curious 
about is the Why?  
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: y2k-proofing my programs

- **From:** "Donald Tees" <donald@willmack.com>

```
>     2) Question, why do you want to store yesterdays date? I
>          would assume that it would be determine when it is the
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>          keeping function is performed only once.


As an aside, I have a program that goes in every autoexec that does
exactly that.  It reads the last boot date/time off the disk, checks that
the clock has not gone backwards, then updates the disk. If it has
gone backwards, then it pops open a windows, and asks the operator
to check the clock, as the most likely problem is a bad clock/battery.

I wrote it one day after having a system generate an entire day's
transactions with the date set wrong. It created havoc.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: y2k-proofing my programs

- **From:** skidmike <skidmike@mindspring.com>
- **Date:** 1998-09-14T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<35FDBE90.68FCE9EA@mindspring.com>`
- **References:** `<35FC314B.6C5EE424@mindspring.com> <35feb378.3511080@nntp.ix.netcom.com>`

```
I am either an amateur or dense.  probably both.  ok, here's what i did
to see what would happen.  wrote a small cobol pgm to simply ACCEPT and
DISPLAY the date.  all i can get is 6 digits 98mmdd.  so the way i see
it is i have to write the software to put the 19 in front of the year in
my DATE-USAGE PIC 9(8) field and catch the discrepancy when ACCEPT
DATE-IN FROM DATE yields 000101 and is less than dec. 31, 1999.  maybe
when rewriting several thousand lines of someone else's code to keep
armageddon at bay for another 50 years or so is one thing, but when
writing from scratch, might as well head it off at the pass.  my project
uses and 8 digit date field yyyymmdd and there you go.
i am probably doing it the long way or totally unconventional but it's
the best i could think of and seems to be a good preventative measure
for the long run. but i am saving and printing posts with anything to do
with y2k coding.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: y2k-proofing my programs

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1998-09-14T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<6tkhf1$so1@dfw-ixnews8.ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<35FC314B.6C5EE424@mindspring.com> <35feb378.3511080@nntp.ix.netcom.com> <35FDBE90.68FCE9EA@mindspring.com>`

```
To get the 8 digit date in COBOL:
   Assume a Working-Storage item
    05  My-8-Digit-Field  Pic 9(08).

A)  use (this is Standard and works with any CURRENT compiler - but not old
ones)
    Move Function Current-date (1:8) to my-8-digit-field.

B) If you have a compiler that will accept it (Micro Focus and IBM do
already and I think some others are starting to - and it will be in the next
Standard), use
    Accept my-8-digit-field from date YYYYMMDD

C) If you happen to be running on an IBM mainframe and have VS COBOL II but
not LE, then use
   Call "IGZEDT4" using my-8-digit-field

skidmike wrote in message <35FDBE90.68FCE9EA@mindspring.com>...

>to see what would happen.  wrote a small cobol pgm to simply ACCEPT and
…[30 more quoted lines elided]…
```
