## Thread: Any need for PIC A ? — 4 msgs

### Re: Any need for PIC A ?

- **From:** dbryant@netcom.com (David K. Bryant)
- **Date:** 1998-09-13T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<dbryantEz8MH7.9Dz@netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<68n$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com> <C9NK19410@news4.mia.bellsouth.net>`

```
"Judson McClendon" <judmc123@bellsouth.net> writes:

>beelerj@my-dejanews.com wrote:
>>Does anyone know of a circumstance when PIC A would be prefereable to PIC X?
>>My COBOL instructor (a real-world programmer) couldn't think of any, and I
>>was just curious.


>That's a good question.  I've been programming COBOL for 30 years, and have
>never seen a need for it.  I would like to know if anybody else has seen one.


You've probably never seen a PIC A used because you've 
never worked with programmers who have actually opened
the manual to see what nice features Cobol actually has.
Instead they rely on what they learned in two semesters
of academic settings.

Instead of letting the system enforce data integrity thru
the use of PIC 9 or PIC A acceptance fields, they would 
rather code everything as PIC X and handle data integrity 
thru range selections.  If you're lucky.

I spend a part of my week troubleshooting problems that often  
turn out to be related to dirty data.... uninitialized
Working Storage, input into PIC X when the only valid values
are numeric, etc.


Typical:  Need to input all-numeric Social Security Numbers,
telephone numbers, or ZIP (postal) codes.  Sure, we'll just
input into a PIC X and then write a look to scan and check
each digit for 0-9 content....  but they get lazy and leave
out the part following "and then".
```

#### ↳ Re: Any need for PIC A ?

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1998-09-13T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<6th7j8$m13@sjx-ixn10.ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<68n$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com> <C9NK19410@news4.mia.bellsouth.net> <dbryantEz8MH7.9Dz@netcom.com>`

```

David K. Bryant wrote in message ...
>"Judson McClendon" <judmc123@bellsouth.net> writes:
>
>>beelerj@my-dejanews.com wrote:
>>>Does anyone know of a circumstance when PIC A would be prefereable to PIC
X?
>>>My COBOL instructor (a real-world programmer) couldn't think of any, and
I
>>>was just curious.
>
>
>>That's a good question.  I've been programming COBOL for 30 years, and
have
>>never seen a need for it.  I would like to know if anybody else has seen
one.
>
>
…[22 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Do you know of a SINGLE COBOL compiler and run-time that gives any error or
problem if you try and put a numeric into a PIC A field?  I don't!

Furthermore, although there are several types of "terminals" (dumb, smart,
and PC) that will reject an attempt to enter a letter into a PIC 9 "input
field", I don't know of a single one that will "reject" an attempt to put a
number into a PIC A field.  I have tried this on all the IBM 327x terminals,
several PCs (with different operating systems and compilers).  There may be
one out there that actually does the edit for you - but I haven't
experienced it.  (and of course it will become "non-standard" to make such a
distinction in the next Standard).

Just to be clear, there are LOTS of good reasons to use a PIC 9 (numeric or
numeric-edited) field rather than a PIC X field.  However, I have yet to see
a time when using a PIC A field instead of a PIC X field has provided any
advantage.  Such a case may exist - but I haven't found it yet.
```

#### ↳ Re: Any need for PIC A ?

- **From:** docdwarf@clark.net ()
- **Date:** 1998-09-14T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<6tjab8$14q$1@callisto.clark.net>`

```
In article <dbryantEz8MH7.9Dz@netcom.com>,
David K. Bryant <dbryant@netcom.com> wrote:
>"Judson McClendon" <judmc123@bellsouth.net> writes:
>
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
>the manual to see what nice features Cobol actually has.

... or maybe he has worked with programmers who know their codes well
enough to write:

88  VALID-ALPHA VALUES 'A' THRU 'H', 'I' THRU 'R', 'S' THRU 'Z'.

... and rely on a sensible 88 rather than a 2-yr maintenance coder's being
familiar with Pic A?

DD
```

#### ↳ Re: Any need for PIC A ?

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc123@bellsouth.net>
- **Date:** 1998-09-14T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<956L1.1742$te.2584561@news2.mia.bellsouth.net>`
- **References:** `<68n$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com> <C9NK19410@news4.mia.bellsouth.net> <dbryantEz8MH7.9Dz@netcom.com>`

```
David K. Bryant wrote in message ...
>"Judson McClendon" <judmc123@bellsouth.net> writes:
>
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
>of academic settings.

Having only read through various COBOL manuals cover to cover several
dozen times myself, in addition to thousands of hours of study, and
having taught dozens of programmers, that is an interesting statement
to read.  Perhaps you never considered that it is one thing to know
about an option, and how it works, and quite another to actually see
a need for it.

>Instead of letting the system enforce data integrity thru
>the use of PIC 9 or PIC A acceptance fields, they would
>rather code everything as PIC X and handle data integrity
>thru range selections.  If you're lucky.


How many situations can you enumerate where 'dirty data' would cause
a problem, but strictly garbage data, though alphanumeric, would work
correctly?  Non initialized data is, by definition, random, and can be
alphabetic, or numeric by chance.  The answer is to validate all input
data, not just for alpha or numeric, but with appropriate range and
specific value tests as well.  Declaring a field with PIC A is no
substitute for that.  The time to validate the data is when it is being
entered, while the user is looking at it, and has the source document
in front of them.  Not when a batch program is being run at 2AM the next
morning.  And not by bombing a program.  You bomb a program because of
internal data corruption, not invalid input data.

Even if the compiler did test for alphabetic, why would you want the
compiler to generate an alphabetic test for every single move you did
to a PIC A item?  Why would you want to bomb your program, rather than
print an error message?  Because you were too lazy to write code to
validate the data, or just don't know better?  Looks like you never
worked with programmers with enough experience to know how it should
be done, outside of what their professors told them.  Most professors
have zip real world experience.

>I spend a part of my week troubleshooting problems that often
>turn out to be related to dirty data.... uninitialized
>Working Storage, input into PIC X when the only valid values
>are numeric, etc.


Any *you* talk about being around programmers who know what their
doing?

>Typical:  Need to input all-numeric Social Security Numbers,
>telephone numbers, or ZIP (postal) codes.  Sure, we'll just
>input into a PIC X and then write a look to scan and check
>each digit for 0-9 content....  but they get lazy and leave
>out the part following "and then".


You think it is better to input the data directly into PIC 9 fields
and bomb the program, than input it into PIC X fields and validate
it?  Anyway, who said anything about numeric data?  The discussion
is about PIC A.  There are many reasons to use PIC 9.
```

<a id="t-e6ac19779e"></a>
## Thread: CobolTransformer 3.1 Announce — 1 msgs

### CobolTransformer 3.1 Announce

- **From:** Vadim Maslov <vadik@siber.com>
- **Date:** 1998-09-13T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<35FC1238.911D4641@siber.com>`

```
CobolTransformer 3.1 Announce
	-----------------------------

Siber Systems is pleased to announce availability 
of Version 3.1 of CobolTransformer http://www.siber.com/sct/
and Specific Converter Tools based on it.

CobolTransformer is a complete toolkit with well-designed API 
that allows you to focus on particulars of your Cobol 
reengineering projects and not worry about handling 
complexity of quality Cobol lexing, parsing, and code generation.

If you need to automate analysis and/or conversion of your
legacy Cobol code and you want the automated solution
to be delivered fast and have high quality, then 
it is time to get a CobolTransformer license.

CobolTransformer includes:
- Cobol *Parser* that parses 16 most popular Cobol dialects.
This parser could parse everything in the 
Federal Compiler Test Suite CCVS. Unlike many other 
Cobol parsers, it can parse Micro Focus Cobol too.

- Cobol *Program Tree* and library of routines used 
to browse and transform the Program Tree.
Definition-Use links attached to the Program Tree
effectively make it a general case Program Graph.

- *PrettyPrint* and *SourcePrint* code generators that 
transform Program Tree into human-readable Cobol source.

To get more information, please direct your browser
to http://www.siber.com/sct/


See a Demo and Talk To Us
-------------------------

You can see a demo of CobolTransformer and talk to us at 
the Mid-Atlantic Venture Forum '98 that will take place
on September 15-16 at Ritz Carlton Hotel, 
Tysons Corner, Virginia.

Directions: take I-495 (Capital Beltway) to Route 123 in 
No. Virginia. Exit to 123 West, then take next right 
to Tysons Blvd. Address is 1700 Tysons Blvd, McLean, VA.
Hotel phone number is: 703-506-4300.


Major New features in CobolTransformer 3.1
------------------------------------------

** SourcePrint **

The most important feature of 3.1. is revolutionary SourcePrint capability 
that allows you to fully restore the unchanged parts of the Cobol program,
while using the full power of the tree-based program transformation engine.

Details.
Our general sequence of steps needed to perform 
a program transformation is fairly standard:
(1) parse the program making it a Program Tree,
(2) transform the Program Tree, and 
(3) generate the converted Cobol code from the transformed Program Tree.

The only problem with this approach is that 
the Cobol code generated from the Program Tree
usually differs from the original source code.
The differences are minor -- words appear in different columns,
some optional words may be skipped or added.

These differences are result of a process of automatic code generation
which cannot exactly reproduce the original program.  While these
differences may seem insignificant, they do not allow to use *diff* or
any other automatic file comparison utility to independently verify
what lines were changed by the converter.  This is because the little
differences introduced by beautification make every line look
different to these diff utilities.

Enters the SourcePrint.
We store additional data in the tree nodes that allows us to generate
Cobol code that does not differ from the original code -- down to a
single byte -- for the tree fragments that were not changed by
transformations.

Code that is generated from the new tree nodes is generated in the
beautified mode as was the case in the SCT versions prior to 3.1.

What does it mean for you?  It means that Cobol converters that use
SourcePrint are easily and inexpensively verifiable.  
That is, upon finishing the conversion, you can run line diff utility
that compares original directory with the full source of your
application to the directory with the converted source.  The generated
difference logs are guaranteed to be minimal and reflect only changes
made by the converter (and not the changes introduced by
beautification or other parsing/printing side effects).


** Copybook Generation **

The other important feature of the Version 3.1 is
its ability to fully control the copybook structure generation.

As you may already know, a Cobol program consists of 
the main file and copy library (or copy book) files that
are included in the main program by a preprocessor.
Cobol parser needs to inline all the copybooks in order to 
correctly parse the program and build the complete Program Tree.

However, when it comes to code generation, the almost
universal desire of the conversion engineers is to 
have their copybook structure back.
That is, the converted main file must contain 
COPY statements that refer to the converted copybooks.

Since the copybooks also may be affected by a conversion,
this creates a non-trivial problem of un-inlining the copybooks.
By un-inlining we mean process of code generation in which
a main program with COPY statements and series
of copybooks used by these statements is generated from
a single Program Tree.

The correct and fully functioning implementation
of copybook un-inlining is the new feature available 
in CobolTransformer 3.1.

In addition to restoring the original copybook structure 
you can easily change the layout of copybooks:
fully inline the existing copybooks,
separate a fragment of code into a new copybook, or 
generate a totally new hierarchy of copybooks.
All this is made possible by storing the copybook-related 
information on the Program Tree.


** Client-Server **

Another big advance in CobolTransformer is Client-Server Packaging.
This is in addition to the previously standard Library Packaging.

In client-server packaging all non-trivial functions such as parsing
and pretty-printing occur in CobolTransformer server executables that
run in a process/task that is separate from the process/task of your
converter.

The thin CobolTransformer client linked in with your conversion
application contains only the tree transformation functions and proxy
functions for parsing and pretty-printing that call the
CobolTransformer server.

Client-Server Packaging makes CobolTransformer a bit slower, 
because some time is spent on Program Tree externalization and
internalization.  However, it provides numerous advantages 
that are well worth the price:

- It is possible to have CobolTransformer client library in different
languages.  In addition to C++, client libraries in C, Perl and Cobol
are planned.

- Memory allocation problems such as leaks and dangling pointers
cannot possibly propagate from your application to CobolTransformer
and back, because your application and CobolTransformer run in
separate address spaces.

- We are able to provide you with a Free Trial Copy of CobolTransformer.
It was not possible when CobolTransformer was a static library.

The Library Packaging of SCT is still available.


** Free Evaluation Package **

Now, with introduction of Client-Server Packaging
you can try CobolTransformer for free.

You can actually write your own conversion/analysis application that
uses CobolTransformer and see how it works on small (less than 256
lines) programs.

Once you are satisfied with the results, you can purchase a
CobolTransformer license and the application that you wrote will then
work on program of any size.  

This is a new risk free way to appreciate the power and flexibility of
CobolTransformer.


Best regards,
Vadim Maslov
Siber Systems
```

<a id="t-c63c115d84"></a>
## Thread: L S 5 $ W K M C Re: Paragraphs or Sections — 1 msgs

_Topics: [`Language features and syntax`](topics.md#syntax)_

### L S 5 $ W K M C Re: Paragraphs or Sections

- **From:** riplin@kcbbs.gen.nz (Richard Plinston)
- **Date:** 1998-09-13T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3298255.6636.29616@kcbbs.gen.nz>`

```

 
> John points out that in his view, there is no perform
> section strategy.
 
> If section names were for overlay purposes, and section
> numbers were specified (long gone and ignored now I guess),
 
No, I still have some large programs overlayed under DOS,
actually under a Multiuser, multitasking OS that runs MS-DOS
type programs.
 
> then perform a section would include planning to have the
> available memory allocated,
 
The whole point of needing to overlay a program is that there
_isn't_ any memory available from which an allocation can
be made.  When the program is loaded a single overlay buffer or
area is created into which each overlay may be loaded in turn.
 
> and rolling out that area if needed,
 
This is code only, it does not need to be rolled out because
it should not have changed.
 
> then reading the section into ram for execution.
 
Yes, this is needed.  But the section must be loaded into
RAM at some point.  Unoverlayed, every section is loaded
when the program starts.  Overlayed, this is deferred until
the section is needed.  If you get the overlaying _right_
each section is only loaded once.
 
But you are mistaken in that the overlaying of sections is
related to performing of sections.  The section is loaded if a
paragraph within it is performed from outside.  In this case the
section is used as a container for that group of related
paragraphs that should be loaded together.
 
In particular it would be a mistake to have:
 
           PERFORM Process-Transaction
               UNTIL End-Of-File
           ....
 
       Process-Transaction SECTION 77.
       Start-Process.
 
           READ ...
           ......
 
As this would cause the overlay to be reloaded for every read.
In this case it is far better to have:
 
           PERFORM Process-All-Transactions
           ....
 
       Process-Transaction-Overlay SECTION 77.
       Process-All-Transactions.
 
           PERFORM Process-Transaction
               UNTIL End-Of-File
           .
 
       Process-Transaction.
 
           READ ....
 
If the program is carefully structured and assembled then the
overhead of overlaying need not be significant, or even
noticable.  It can be hugely more efficient than, say, dynamic
CALL and CANCEL.
 
But those with a fixed idea about a program should read and
be laid out in the source file may find many inefficiencies.
 
 
> Reducing sections to a method for simplifying perform through,
> etc. in my view has taken the wrong fork in the road. Perform,
> Perform through, and the new perform (immediate) seem easy
> enough to understand even for a slow learner like myself.
 
But why do you think that you need PERFORM .. THRU .. ?
```

```
>Hi, I would like for someone to look at my code and tell me what is
```

```
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…

The FD name should somehow refer to the SELECT, shouldn't it?

Just keep in mind - the instruction RPM (Read Programmers Mind) has not yet been
implemented everywhere
```

#### ↳ Re: Homework help!

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1998-09-14T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<6tjtrd$b14@dfw-ixnews4.ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<35FD5785.1AB0AFFC@dnw.com>`

```
Your example leaves me a little confused.

The
        ASSIGN TO a:\dprog1.dat
   looks like you want to establish the "physical file name" within your
source program and are using PC type names. While the
        ASSIGN TO UT-S-SYSOUT
looks like you want to use a SET statement to establish the physical name
but are using (IBM) mainframe naming standards.

The 1st one is "wrong" because it isn't in quotes and ":" and "\" are not
valid in the middle of COBOL words.

The 2nd one isn't completely wrong but SYSOUT is a *very dangerous* name to
use for your logical file name.  In many (IBM-like) environments, this is
reserved for DISPLAY statements and not WRITE statements.

Finally, (and this is probably your biggest problem), your SELECT statements
use the names "INVENTORY" and  "PRINTED-REPORT" while your FDs use the names
"STUDENT-FILE" and "PRINT-FILE".  You need to change this so that the same
name is used in the SELECT statement and the FD for each file.

David Watson wrote in message <35FD5785.1AB0AFFC@dnw.com>...
>Hi, I would like for someone to look at my code and tell me what is
>wrong with my select and assign part?
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
>---------------------------------------------------------------------------
----------
>     1        IDENTIFICATION DIVISION.
>     2        PROGRAM-ID.     DPROG1.
…[21 more quoted lines elided]…
>    24
    <much snippage>
>   128        PROCEDURE DIVISION.
>   129        PREPARE-INVENTORY-REPORT.
>   130            OPEN INPUT INVENTORY
>   131                 OUTPUT PRINTED-REPORT.
  <much more snippage>
```

#### ↳ Re: Homework help!

- **From:** "Donald Tees" <donald@willmack.com>
- **Date:** 1998-09-14T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<6tkc5n$adm$1@news.igs.net>`
- **References:** `<35FD5785.1AB0AFFC@dnw.com>`

```

David Watson wrote in message <35FD5785.1AB0AFFC@dnw.com>...

Here is a start:

>     8            SELECT INVENTORY  ASSIGN TO "a:\dprog1.dat".

       put quotes in the above like so.
>   132            PROFORM WRITE-HEADING-LINE.
>   133            PROFORM READ-INVENTORY-FILE.
>   134            PROFORM QNT-ON-HAND.
>   135            PROFORM VAL-OF-INVENT.
These should be perform, not proform.

The most common bug is spelling errors, check all errors
for a miss-spelled name first.  Then correct what you can and
recompile.  A lot of the time, one error can cause a dozen
others.
```

```
>The most common bug is spelling errors, check all errors
>for a miss-spelled name first.  Then correct what you can and
>recompile.  A lot of the time, one error can cause a dozen
>others.

<bemused chuckle>
After writing COBOL for 18 years, I still type ASSING instead
of ASSIGN in my select statements once in a while.

Had a friend in Knoxville who always had to go back and
correct HGD-LINE-1 to HDG-LINE-1.  

Typing too fast, letting the compiler become a spell checker
I guess.  Anyone else do this sort of thing?

JC Jones.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Homework help!

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** riplin@kcbbs.gen.nz (Richard Plinston)
- **Date:** 1998-09-17T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3298259.68413.729@kcbbs.gen.nz>`
- **References:** `<35ffd4fd.0@news1.ibm.net>`

```
In message <<35ffd4fd.0@news1.ibm.net>> "Leif Svalgaard" <leif@ibm.net> writes:
> 
> Mine too along with Dispaly and Accetp.
> Luckily, the ETK editor automatically corrects these,
> without even telling me...

Do you really think that this is 'lucky' ?  Typing is a
semi-automatic process that your brain is trained to
do.  By accepting incorrect sequences you are training 
yourself to continue the process of mis-timing. It
starts by hitting the 't' before 'p' in Accept and, if
uncorrected, will lead to chaging gear before putting the
clutch in, putting food in your mouth before opening it,
failing to get the sequence right when using your zip,
and ultimately becoming entirely uncorordinated.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Homework help!

- **From:** casten@usfca.edu (John Casten)
- **Date:** 1998-09-15T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<casten-1509981609090001@138.202.137.50>`
- **References:** `<6tkc5n$adm$1@news.igs.net> <1998091500592100.UAA04031@ladder01.news.aol.com>`

```
In article <1998091500592100.UAA04031@ladder01.news.aol.com>,
jcj0347@aol.com (JCJ0347) wrote:

> After writing COBOL for 18 years, I still type ASSING instead
> of ASSIGN in my select statements once in a while.
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> JC Jones.

When I taught COBOL I would state at the beginning of the course "if there
is one thing that you will learn in this course, it's the difference
between an "O" and a zero.  I'm still learning that every once in awhile. 
Levity aside, this was also to emphasize the new concept of ASCII (or
EBCDIC).  And there were students who just could not get it that a blank
(or space) was a value.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Homework help!

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** riplin@kcbbs.gen.nz (Richard Plinston)
- **Date:** 1998-09-15T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3298257.69665.12224@kcbbs.gen.nz>`
- **References:** `<35fed181.4208971@news.roanoke.infi.net>`

```
In message <<35fed181.4208971@news.roanoke.infi.net>> RaeBC@roanoke.infi.net  writes:
> 
> I invariably type DISPALY for DISPLAY.
> 
If I want 'DISPLAY' I just hit two keys (F9+D) and my editor
puts it in, similarly for PERFORM (F9+P) and many others.
When there are complaints about how verbose Cobol is, I
point out that there are tools which make this no more
difficult than other languages, keystroke macros, context
highlighting, skeletons, and templates can make it as
fast to write as you can plan it.
```

```
=> of ASSIGN in my select statements once in a while.
=>

=>

=> I guess.  Anyone else do this sort of thing?
=> 
=> JC Jones.

em? evner!

rrg
```

```
In article <35FD5785.1AB0AFFC@dnw.com>, David Watson <dnw@dnw.com> wrote:
+Hi, I would like for someone to look at my code and tell me what is
+wrong with my select and assign part?
+
[...]
+
+Here the program and compile errors..

I've taken the liberty of rearranging the error messages to place them
with the errors that caused them. That makes it a little more apparent.

+     1        IDENTIFICATION DIVISION.
+     2        PROGRAM-ID.     DPROG1.
+     3        AUTHOR.         DAVID WATSON.
+     4
+     5        ENVIRONMENT DIVISION.
+     6        INPUT-OUTPUT SECTION.
+     7        FILE-CONTROL.
+     8            SELECT INVENTORY  ASSIGN TO a:\dprog1.dat.

+   8 0474 E FD or SD not defined for SELECT 'INVENTORY'
+  12 0061 E No SELECT clause found for filename 'STUDENT-FILE'

Change INVENTORY to STUDENT-FILE so it matches the FD, or change the FD
to match the SELECT.

+   8 0115 E Unrecognizable word or literal ':'
+   8 0969 W Invalid character ignored in column 42
+   8 0115 E Unrecognizable word or literal 'DPROG1'
+   8 0123 W Space assumed after '.'
+   8 0115 E Unrecognizable word or literal 'DAT'
+   8 0954 W Unexpected period separator found; accepted

Put the file name in quotes.

+     9            SELECT PRINTED-REPORT ASSIGN TO UT-S-SYSOUT.

+  21 0061 E No SELECT clause found for filename 'PRINT-FILE'

Change PRINTED-REPORT to PRINT-FILE to match the FD, or change the FD
to match the SELECT.

+ 111 0115 E Unrecognizable word or literal '-'
+ 111 0115 E Unrecognizable word or literal '-'
+ 111 0115 E Unrecognizable word or literal '-'
+ 111 0115 E Unrecognizable word or literal '-'
+ 111 0115 E Unrecognizable word or literal '-'
+ 111 0115 E Unrecognizable word or literal '-'
+ 111 0115 E Unrecognizable word or literal '-'

Look up the VALUE clause in your textbook.

+   112
+   113        01 TOTAL-LINE2.
+   114            05                  PIC X(8)   VALUE SPACES.
+   115            05                  PIC X(5)   VALUE 'TOTAL'.
+   116            05                  PIC X      VALUE SPACES.
+   117            05                  PIC X(5)   VALUE 'VALUE'.
+   118            05                  PIC X      VALUE SPACES.
+   119            05                  PIC X(2)   VALUE 'OF'.
+   120            05                  PIC X      VALUE SPACES.
+   121            05                  PIC X(3)   VALUE 'ALL'.
+   122            05                  PIC X      VALUE SPACES.
+   123            05                  PIC X(9)   VALUE 'INVENTORY'.
+   124            05                  PIC X(26)  VALUE SPACES.
+   125            05 DET-GTOTALS      PIC 9(8)   VALUE 'ZEROES'.

+ 125 0277 E Non-numeric VALUE 'ZEROES' invalid for numeric item

This should be self-explanatory. If not, again, look up the VALUE
clause in your textbook.

+   126            05                  PIC X(62)  VALUE SPACES.
+   127
+   128        PROCEDURE DIVISION.
+   129        PREPARE-INVENTORY-REPORT.
+   130            OPEN INPUT INVENTORY
+   131                 OUTPUT PRINTED-REPORT.

+ 130 0787 E Undefined symbol 'INVENTORY'
+ 131 0787 E Undefined symbol 'PRINTED-REPORT'

There is no FD having either of those names.

+   132            PROFORM WRITE-HEADING-LINE.
+   133            PROFORM READ-INVENTORY-FILE.
+   134            PROFORM QNT-ON-HAND.
+   135            PROFORM VAL-OF-INVENT.

+ 132 0787 E Undefined symbol 'PROFORM'
+ 132 0115 E Unrecognizable word or literal 'WRITE-HEADING-LINE'
+ 133 0787 E Undefined symbol 'PROFORM'
+ 133 0115 E Unrecognizable word or literal 'READ-INVENTORY-FILE'
+ 134 0787 E Undefined symbol 'PROFORM'
+ 134 0115 E Unrecognizable word or literal 'QNT-ON-HAND'
+ 135 0787 E Undefined symbol 'PROFORM'
+ 135 0115 E Unrecognizable word or literal 'VAL-OF-INVENT'

Check your spelling.

+   136
+   137            STOP RUN.
+   138
+   139        WRITE-HEADING-LINE.
+   140            MOVE HD1 TO PRINT-LINE.
+   141            WRITE PRINT-LINE
+   142                AFTER ADVANCING PAGE.
+   143            MOVE SPACES TO PRINT-LINE.
+   144            MOVE HD2 TO PRINT-LINE.
+   145            WRITE PRINT-LINE.
+   146            MOVE SPACES TO PRINT-LINE.
+   147            MOVE HD3 TO PRINT-LINE.
+   148            WRITE PRINT-LINE.
+   149            MOVE SPACES TO PRINT-LINE.
+   150            MOVE HD4 TO PRINT-LINE.
+   151            WRITE PRINT-LINE.
+   152            MOVE SPACES TO PRINT-LINE.
+   153            MOVE HD5 TO PRINT-LINE.
+   154            WRITE PRINT-LINE.
+   155
+   156        READ-INVENTORY-FILE.
+   157            READ INVENTORY.

+ 157 0575 E Expected file-name; found 'READ'
+ 157 0787 E Undefined symbol 'INVENTORY'

There is no FD with the name INVENTORY.

+   158                AT END MOVE 'NO' TO DATA-REMAINS-SWITCH.

+ 158 0594 E AT END only valid for READ or RETURN or must immediately
+follow
+            SEARCH verb
+ 159 0593 E No corresponding active scope for 'END-READ'

Lose the period on line 157.

+   159            END-READ.
+   160
+   161
+   162        QNT-ON-HAND.
+   163            ADD QTY-ON-HAND TO AMT-RECV.
+   164            SUBTRACT AMT-SHIP FROM AMT-RECV.
+   165            MOVE AMT-RECV TO INV-QTY-ONHAND.

+ 163 0752 E Expected numeric item; found 'QTY-ON-HAND'
+ 163 0753 E Data-name 'AMT-RECV' invalid as numeric destination
+ 164 0752 E Expected numeric item; found 'AMT-SHIP'
+ 164 0753 E Data-name 'AMT-RECV' invalid as numeric destination
+ 165 0622 W Expected numeric data-name; found 'AMT-RECV'; treated as
+USAGE
+            DISPLAY numeric

The compiler is telling you that it can do math only on fields that
have numeric definitions. Check your PICs for these fields.


+   166
+   167        VAL-OF-INVENT.
+   168            MULTIPY UNIT-PRICE BY QTY-ON-HAND.
+ 168 0787 E Undefined symbol 'MULTIPY'
+ 168 0115 E Unrecognizable word or literal 'UNIT-PRICE'
+ 168 0115 E Unrecognizable word or literal 'BY'
+ 168 0115 E Unrecognizable word or literal 'QTY-ON-HAND'

Check your typing on line 168.

+   169            MOVE QTY-ON-HAND TO VALUE-UNIT-PRICE.
+ 169 0622 W Expected numeric data-name; found 'QTY-ON-HAND'; treated as
+USAGE
+            DISPLAY numeric
+ 176 0622 W Expected numeric data-name; found 'QTY-ON-HAND'; treated as
+USAGE
+            DISPLAY numeric
+ 177 0622 W Expected numeric data-name; found 'AMT-RECV'; treated as
+USAGE
+            DISPLAY numeric
+ 178 0622 W Expected numeric data-name; found 'AMT-SHIP'; treated as
+USAGE
+            DISPLAY numeric
+ 180 0622 W Expected numeric data-name; found 'UNIT-PRICE'; treated as
+USAGE 
+            DISPLAY numeric

Same problem as immediately above.


+   170
+   171        TOTALS.
+   172            ADD VALUE-UNIT-PRICE TO GTOTALS.
+   173
+   174        WRITE-DETAIL-LINE.
+   175            MOVE PART-NAME TO DET-PART-NAME.
+   176            MOVE QTY-ON-HAND TO DET-BEG-ON-HAND.
+   177            MOVE AMT-RECV TO DET-RECV.
+   178            MOVE AMT-SHIP TO DET-SHIP.
+   179            MOVE INV-QTY-ONHAND TO DET-END-ON-HAND.
+   180            MOVE UNIT-PRICE TO DET-UNIT-PRICE.
+   181            MOVE VALUE-UNIT-PRICE TO DET-TOTAL-VAL.
+   182            MOVE DETIAL1 TO PRINT-LINE.

+ 182 0787 E Undefined symbol 'DETIAL1'

Typo: 'DETIAL' for 'DETAIL'

+   183            WRITE PRINT-LINE
+   184                AFTER ADVANCING 2 LINES.
+   185
+   186
+CA-Realia Classroom COBOL Version 4.200         DPROG1.COB
+
```

#### ↳ Re: Homework help!

- **From:** Kitty Carr <kcarr1@bellsouth.net>
- **Date:** 1998-09-15T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<35FDEECA.63CE@bellsouth.net>`
- **References:** `<35FD5785.1AB0AFFC@dnw.com>`

```
David Watson wrote:
> 
> Hi, I would like for someone to look at my code and tell me what is
…[283 more quoted lines elided]…
> 


After you match your SELECT statements with your FD's, remove the
periods after the FD statements.  Five error messages occurred for each
FD because of the period.

You need to study your error messages just a tad better if you think all
those errors resulted from two SELECT statements.  Look at msgs for
'Undefined symbol  'PROFORM.'  For each PROFORM you got 2 errors. 
(Spelling?)

Look at your READ-INVENTORY-FILE routine.  Even if the file INVENTORY
existed, which it doesn't, you'd still get errors.  Compare your READ
statement to any READ statement in your textbook.  

Error line 163 - 'Expected NUMERIC item; found 'QTY-ON-HAND.'  (Did you
look in your program where you defined ('QTY-ON-HAND'?)

The same for "Data-name 'AMT-RECV' invalid as NUMERIC destination."  Did
you look at AMT-RECV in your program?

Error line 168 - "Unidentfied symbol 'MULTIPY.'"  Your spelling error
caused three other errors.  Even if you spell it right, you'll still get
errors unless you fix error lines 163 - 165.

(Just my opinion - put an asterisk in position 7 on your blank lines)
```

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "Donald Tees" <donald@willmack.com>
- **Date:** 1998-09-15T00:00:00

```
Kitty Carr wrote in message <35FE3168.314A@bellsouth.net>...
>Bill Lynch wrote:
>> Kitty Carr wrote:
>And, Bill . . . I really MUST insist on the asterisk in col. 7 because
>that's the way I do it, AND IT MAKES ME MAD WHEN PEOPLE DON'T DO THINGS
>THE WAY I DO THEM!!  (How was that?)


NEVER!  NEVER!  NEVER!  Make them SECTIONS!.  That way if
the blank lines load, they will all be together!  And number them
while you are at it.  You have to be able to find them later.
```

#### ↳ Re: Homework help!

- **From:** Kitty Carr <kcarr1@bellsouth.net>
- **Date:** 1998-09-15T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<35FDFB82.28B2@bellsouth.net>`
- **References:** `<35FD5785.1AB0AFFC@dnw.com>`

```
David Watson wrote:
> 
> Hi, I would like for someone to look at my code and tell me what is
…[282 more quoted lines elided]…
> 


Even after you match your SELECT statements with your FDs, remove the
periods after the FD statement.  Each period caused 5 errors.

You need to study your error messages a lot better if you think your
SELECT statements are causing all those errors.

Err. line 132 - Undefined symbol 'PROFORM.'  (Spelling?)  Each
misspelling caused two errors.

Look at your READ-INVENTORY-FILE paragraph.  Even if the file INVENTORY
existed, which it doesn't, you'd still get errors.  Compare your READ
statement with any READ statement in your textbook.  

Err. line 163 - Expected NUMERIC item; found 'QTY-ON-HAND.'  Did you
look in your program where you defined QTY-ON-HAND?  The same for the
next four lines.  Doesn't the word NUMERIC kind of jump out at you? 
Look at your program where you've defined each of these fields.

Misspelling MULTIPY caused three error lines.  When you spell it
correctly, you'll still get error msgs, just different ones, unless you
correct the errors in lines 163 - 165.

(This is my personal preference, maybe they don't teach it anymore; put
an asterisk in pos. 7 on your blank lines.)
```

#### ↳ Re: Homework help!

- **From:** Kitty Carr <kcarr1@bellsouth.net>
- **Date:** 1998-09-15T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<35FE089E.21D2@bellsouth.net>`
- **References:** `<35FD5785.1AB0AFFC@dnw.com>`

```
David Watson wrote:
> 
> Hi, I would like for someone to look at my code and tell me what is
…[282 more quoted lines elided]…
> 

Even when you match your SELECT statements and FDs, you'll still get
errors if you don't remove the periods after the FD statements.  Each
period caused 5 errors.

(You need to study your error messages better if you think all these
errors were caused by your SELECT statements!)  Look at err. line 132 -
Undefined symbol 'PROFORM.'  (Spelling?)  Each misspelling caused two
errors.  Your READ-INVENTORY-FILE caused 4 errors.  (Not just because
the file INVENTORY doesn't exist.)  Compare your READ statement with any
READ statement in your textbook.

Err. line 163 - Expected NUMERIC item; found 'QTY-ON-HAND.'  (Did you
look back at your program where you defined QTY-ON-HAND to see if you
could determine what caused the error?)  The same for lines 164 - 165.

Err. line 168 - Undefined symbol 'MULTIPY.'  (Spelling, again?)  Even
when you correct the spelling, you'll still get errors unless you
correct the above err. lines 163 - 165.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Homework help!

- **From:** casten@usfca.edu (John Casten)
- **Date:** 1998-09-15T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<casten-1509981617110001@138.202.137.50>`
- **References:** `<35FD5785.1AB0AFFC@dnw.com> <35FE089E.21D2@bellsouth.net>`

```
In article <35FE089E.21D2@bellsouth.net>, kcarr1@bellsouth.net wrote:

> >      8            SELECT INVENTORY  ASSIGN TO a:\dprog1.dat.

I was trying to explain the concept of an internal ASSIGN to one of the
operators, and sat with him and wrote a SELECT statement with a physical
path name.  He got it.  However, the sys mgr was walking by and had to
point out that never, ever, ever hard code a physical path name in a
program.  Well, duh.  Didn't matter, I was toasted cheese as far as he was
concerned.  Just shows you gotta be careful...
```

#### ↳ Re: Homework help!

- **From:** "COBOL Frog (Huib Klink)" <H.Klink@IMN.nl>
- **Date:** 1998-09-16T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<35FF895E.34CEA639@IMN.nl>`
- **References:** `<35FD5785.1AB0AFFC@dnw.com>`

```
David Watson wrote:

> Hi, I would like for someone to look at my code and tell me what is
> wrong with my select and assign part?
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
> -------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

8< [that's a p.o.scissors]

>      8            SELECT INVENTORY  ASSIGN TO a:\dprog1.dat.
>      9            SELECT PRINTED-REPORT ASSIGN TO UT-S-SYSOUT.

0^^^^ [that's a saw]

After using all the help in other posts, you may very well end up with something like:
SELECT ... ASSIGN TO ...SYSIN... and
SELECT ... ASSIGN TO ...SYSOUT... .
The SET statements will then still cause you errors. You should use:
set sysin=a:\dprog1.dat
set sysout=a:\dprog1.rpt
That is: no spaces around the =. Use SET VAR=VALUE and not SET VAR = VALUE.

Changing the files is then simply changing the SET statements without recompiling the
program.

Good luck
The Frog
```

---

#### ↳ Re: where can I purchase books or manuals of COBOL LE for VSE ?

- **From:** Bal Oberoi <oberoi@ibm.net>
- **Date:** 1998-09-21T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3606588D.7A7C@ibm.net>`
- **References:** `<6tkm5p$98c$1@supernews.com>`

```
ohara garcia wrote:
 
> Iam interested in learn COBOL LE for VSE.
> I need information about web sites for the purchase books for this 
> COBOL.

The COBOL Center has a page that lists COBOL books in various 
categories. The URL of the page:
     http://www.infogoal.com/cbd/cbdbks.htm

For AIX and various mainframe operating systems, IBM has an online 
"library" of their COBOL manuals at: 

     http://www.software.ibm.com/ad/cobol/library.htm

Some of the manuals are in PDF format and can be downloaded in their 
entirety.

Good luck, and the best of regards,

Bal
----- 
oberoi@ibm.net
http://www.geocities.com/~oberoi/mainfrme.html
-----
"The universe is full of magical things, 
patiently waiting for our wits to grow sharper." - Eden Phillpotts
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: where can I purchase books or manuals of COBOL LE for VSE ?

- **From:** rkrayhawk@aol.com (RKRayhawk)
- **Date:** 1998-09-23T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<19980923014508.09343.00000589@ng109.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<3606588D.7A7C@ibm.net>`

```

For those who like the old fashion kind of book printed on paper, you can buy
almost any of the IBM manuals you find at their websites.

Call 1 800 879-2755 for IBM sales of printed material.

It is a god idea to know exactly what you want before calling, because on any
given subject there are many manuals and many versions.
They do have search capability to help you.
The staff I have encountered are bright and definitely commited, but the task
of narrowing it down can be very difficult if you yourself are not clear from
the start.

They can send you faxes of list that match your inquiry if not immediately
resolvable.

The manuals are generally surprisingly inexpensive.
```

#### ↳ Re: where can I purchase books or manuals of COBOL LE for VSE ?

- **From:** mark0young@aol.com (Mark0Young)
- **Date:** 1998-09-21T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<19980921040000.AAA21665@ladder03.news.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<6tkm5p$98c$1@supernews.com>`

```
In article <6tkm5p$98c$1@supernews.com>, "ohara garcia" <oharag@tld.net>
writes:

>Iam interested in learn COBOL LE for VSE.
>
>I need information about web sites for the purchase books for this COBOL.

Unfortunately, the only references I am aware of are the COBOL/VES and LE/VSE
manuals from IBM.

IBM has a number of manuals available over the web, but I don't know the web
address for them. IBM's home page is at http://www.ibm.com, but they also have
a "VSE home page" (which doesn't have the COBOL manuals there).

If you are interested, I could see if I could find what textbooks are used for
our COBOL classes, but I suspect they are not VSE-specific.

As far as COBOL syntax goes, generally you are looking at the COBOL '85
standard _with_ intrinsic functions. The Language Environment adds a number of
subroutines for various things, including date manipulation.

Oh yes, I am _not_ an expert, just someone who installed COBOL/VSE & LE/VSE on
our administrative guest last December. 8) (Our educational guest is still
using COBOL II.)


Mark A. Young
```

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36070364.464E@sprynet.com>`
- **References:** `<6tkm5p$98c$1@supernews.com> <19980921040000.AAA21665@ladder03.news.aol.com>`

```
> 
> Unfortunately, the only references I am aware of are the COBOL/VES and LE/VSE
…[15 more quoted lines elided]…
> using COBOL II.)

Hey Mr. Expert [:-}], got a question for you.  Our systems people are attempting
to install LE/VSE (and COBOL/VSE).  The first task is to get both DOS/VS COBOL
and VS COBOL II programs working.  From what I recall, the DOS/VS COBOL programs
are all running, but the COBOL II with DL/I calls are not.  (I believe the non-DL/I
COBOL II programs are working.)  Any ideas?
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: where can I purchase books or manuals of COBOL LE for VSE ?

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1998-09-21T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<6u73d1$o76@sjx-ixn6.ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<6tkm5p$98c$1@supernews.com> <19980921040000.AAA21665@ladder03.news.aol.com> <36070364.464E@sprynet.com>`

```

Frank Swarbrick wrote in message <36070364.464E@sprynet.com>...
>Mark0Young wrote:

  <some snippage>
The first task is to get both DOS/VS COBOL
>and VS COBOL II programs working.  From what I recall, the DOS/VS COBOL
programs
>are all running, but the COBOL II with DL/I calls are not.  (I believe the
non-DL/I
>COBOL II programs are working.)  Any ideas?
>--
>Frank Swarbrick
>home: infocat@sprynet.com
>work: frank.swarbrick@1stbank.com

From everyone's favorite busy-body (not the VSE expert).

The usual DL/I with VS COBOL II things to check are:

1) What are your compiler options for RENT and DATA? How about LE "AMODEALL"
options?

2) Are you overriding any of the IBM "produced" linkage editor options?

3) Do you have the correct "SYSLIB" concatenation for the LE-enabled DL/I
module in your link-edit step.  (These are MVS terms, but I think that VSE
has comparable steps - just called different things.)

All of this is assuming that you are getting the "usual" S0C4 type problem
at run-time.  If you are getting some other problem with your VS COBOL II -
DL/I stuff, tell us what the symptoms are.

P.S. Have you (or "your people") looked at

http://ppdbooks.pok.ibm.com/cgi-bin/bookmgr/bookmgr.cmd/BOOKS/FL2PGE00/24.2

(The "24.2 Using the Interface between LE/VSE and DL/I" section in the
"LE/VSE V1R4 Programming Guide" Document Number: SC33-6684-00)

I don't know for certain that V1R4 is the latest version of LE/VSE - but
this should get your started.
```

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol

```
> since i didn't get much of an answer, does anyone know any
> translation products that go from lisp / autolisp/cad files to cobol?
> 

I doubt that anyone knew what you were asking, let alone 
what an answer may be.  Lisp ?, CAD ?  why would a 
Cobol group know those (or vice versa).  Granted there
are generalists who do have a wide array of interests.

But what are you asking ?  Convert Lisp programs to
Cobol source code ?  Allow Cobol to read a CAD design
file ?  Export CAD data to a form that Cobol can read ?

If the CAD package can output in any text format as a
flat file or CSV then your Cobol program can read it.
Whether you can understand it or do anything with it
is a different issue.
```

#### ↳ Re: lisp to cobol

- **From:** AS-DATA@t-online.de (Andreas Strzoda)
- **Date:** 1998-09-16T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<6tns4n$t82$2@news02.btx.dtag.de>`
- **References:** `<35fdb091.2767670@nntp.ix.netcom.com>`

```
G Moore schrieb:

> ok, i wrote a question a week ago about CAD and cobol.
> since i didn't get much of an answer, does anyone know any
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> gvwmoore@ix.spam.netcom.com to reply remove the spam

   I don't believe, that a KI-language, a self-modifying, interpretative

language with a nice implementation of the lambda-construct can ever
be converted in a cobol-structur. There is nothing like
a List-constructor nor an atom in Cobol.
You can write a LISP-interpreter in cobol
(hardly, but i've done it on a sinclair spectrum with basic long ago )
but you can't convert
LISP to cobol. You can write Programms in
COBOL that does the same as your little LISP-Progs, but
not automaticly!

regards

AS
```

```
Nope.  The damned thing is also case sensitive with no way of turning
case sensitivity off.  I'll try the quotes and get back on that.  I would
switch

attributes
by highlighting a component and right clicking ... beats the hell out of
trying

language
and scrap the manuals altogether - just have the editor indirect into a
hyperlink
table, and then cut and paste back.  Maybe Version 5.0.  BTW, If Fujitsu is
reading this (and I know they do), *everything* in a manual should have an
example that can be cut and pasted into a first time program for that
feature.

The other thing that would be lost is the jump from error message to
line in program.  That is the biggest thing that is wrong with the
integrated
environment ... it gives you some wonderful features, but then loads you
down with basically weak development tools.
```

```
<justparts@tbcnet.com> wrote:

> compiled using RM/COBOL.  We have run this system under WFW3.11 just

Troubleshooting tab of the File System properties, of the Performance

```

#### ↳ Re: RM/COBOL problems under Win95

- **From:** "R. Heady" <NoSpamTodayheady@liant.com>
- **Date:** 1998-09-15T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<35FE8349.D876E818@liant.com>`
- **References:** `<35FD1F75.272B0895@tbcnet.com>`

```


Just Parts, Inc. wrote:

> I am running an accounting/inventory/order entry cobol system which is
> compiled using RM/COBOL.  We have run this system under WFW3.11 just
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>

It would be helpful to know what kind of 30 errors you are getting. A call
to C$RERR using either a four or eleven character parameter will return the
extended error code.  Knowing what kind of I/O statement is returning the
error would be helpful also.

What version of RM/COBOL are you using?  Version 6.51.00 is the current
version for Window 95.

If you are using Novell's Netware Client32 for Windows 95 instead of
Microsoft's client for Netware make sure that you are using the most current
version.   RM/COBOL version 6.5 for Windows 95 requires version 2.11 of
NetWare Client32 for Windows 95 (dated 8/21/96, file size 461,359 bytes).
Record locking does not work properly and file corruption may occur with
earlier versions of Client32.  Additionally there may be open errors (94,xx)
or other permanent errors (30,xx) with older versions of Client32.
Receiving error 30, MS Windows error 1 (30,12,00001 from C$RERR) may
indicate
 a broken version of Client32.

If even one client on the network is using an older version of Novell's
Netware Client32 then you may be seeing these problems.  A simple test to
ensure record locking is working properly is to run the verification program
pacetest.cob from two clients accessing the files on the server.

Hope this helps.

Robert Heady
```

---

## Thread: Cobol Forever! — 2 msgs

_Topics: [`COBOL's future, legacy, and obsolescence`](topics.md#future)_

### Re: Cobol Forever!

- **From:** redsky@ibm.net (Thane Hubbell)
- **Date:** 1998-09-14T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<Jl0PnHJ5PvPd-pn2-OBEGyiTnEpTY@Dwight_Miller.iix.com>`
- **References:** `<1998083106515800.CAA29316@ladder01.news.aol.com> <01bdd4c6$91349f40$2c050380@s70> <Jl0PnHJ5PvPd-pn2-y1gNddpvWJDl@Dwight_Miller.iix.com> <GlUH1.3678$gM3.2741911@news4.mia.bellsouth.net> <35f05882.30976823@news.enter.net> <Jl0PnHJ5PvPd-pn2-R2dd3dQgdexQ@Dwight_Miller.iix.com> <O8KClEh29GA.188@upnetnews03>`

```
On Mon, 7 Sep 1998 03:38:09, "Dennis J. Minette" 
<dennis_minette@email.msn.com> wrote:

> 
> Thane Hubbell wrote in message ...
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> 

I'm a bit flexible.  If the standard requires it, I have no argument 
with it.

The only problem with your period terminator is the fact that mixed 
with the method I endorse, it leads to early termination of if 
statements and the like.  The two just don't mix.  

You should either terminate EVERY statement with a period, or only use
one per paragraph.  Mixing methods just doesn't work well.
```

#### ↳ Re: Cobol Forever!

- **From:** riplin@kcbbs.gen.nz (Richard Plinston)
- **Date:** 1998-09-14T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3298256.82564.11836@kcbbs.gen.nz>`
- **References:** `<Jl0PnHJ5PvPd-pn2-OBEGyiTnEpTY@Dwight_Miller.iix.com>`

```
In message <<Jl0PnHJ5PvPd-pn2-OBEGyiTnEpTY@Dwight_Miller.iix.com>> redsky@ibm.net  writes:
> 
> The only problem with your period terminator is the fact that mixed 
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> You should either terminate EVERY statement with a period, or only use

Which obvious doesn't work and needs to be changed to:

      "terminate EVERY statement unless it is part of
       a conditional block or an in-line perform block
       or anywhere else where it may affect the 
       intended logic."

> one per paragraph.  Mixing methods just doesn't work well.
> 

I especially hate the "." on every line where is is possible
style, mainly because they all have to be removed where
they are put under a conditional and then re-added
when taken back out.  A _complete_ waste of time as well
as being error-prone.

I also dislike 'shortcutting' the correct number of END-IFs
by putting in a ".".  Get the number of them right as a
check on the IF-ELSE structure.  There may be a compiler
warning if you get them wrong, or your IFs are not the
way you hoped.

It may even be useful to have a compiler option to enforce
the correct END- be present in all cases.
```

## Thread: Finding Variable-Length Record Length — 1 msgs

### Re: Finding Variable-Length Record Length

- **From:** Clark Morris <morrisc@nbnet.nb.ca>
- **Date:** 1998-09-14T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<6tk60v$4m1$1@garnet.nbnet.nb.ca>`
- **References:** `<Jl0PnHJ5PvPd-pn2-ExXi45TkedGY@Dwight_Miller.iix.com> <35f56717.5477962@news-s01.ny.us.ibm.net>`

```
Assuming both input and output files have variable length records, the files   
should be described in the FD's as RECORD VARYING DEPENDING ON INPUT-SIZE and   
RECORD VARYING DEPENDING ON OUTPUT-SIZE where INPUT-SIZE and OUTPUT-SIZE are   
PIC 9(9) BINARY working storage fields.  When a record is selected move   
INPUT-SIZE to OUTPUT-SIZE and then WRITE OUTPUT-RECORD FROM INPUT-RECORD (1 :   
INPUT-SIZE)  
 
>  
> On Sat, 5 Sep 1998 16:34:27, Paul_Knudsen@ibm.net (Paul Knudsen)  
…[16 more quoted lines elided]…
> 

Clark F. Morris, Jr. 
CFM Technical Programming Services 
Bridgetown, Nova Scotia, Canada 
cmorris@fox.nstn.ca 
on assignment at morrisc@nbnet.nb.ca
```

## Thread: muffler shop update — 12 msgs

### muffler shop update

- **From:** skidmike <skidmike@mindspring.com>
- **Date:** 1998-09-15T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol

```
well, i have enough of the system written to put it all together into a
nice little demo to show my boss and other shop owners.  gonna put it on
my website next month.  looking over all of my code (10 source files
each with 200-300 lines plus copy files for FD, working-storage, and
screen sections, i see that i ended up with some messy stuff (all the
quick rewrites and patches when the compiler spewed it's indignation)
so while the demo circulates and i get some guidance from my shop's
owner on the bookkeeping requirements i'm going to rewrite the code. 
here is my idea:
main program, calls lots and lots of dll's to do the actual jobs.  most
will have no go to's except the big screen i-o (like for customer info)
dll's.  each function will have it's own dll like "create an estimate"
or "print an invoice"
any advice on structure would be appreciated.  am i doing this in such a
way as to keep it simple? i.e. lots of small source files, well
documented
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: muffler shop update

- **From:** "Leif Svalgaard" <leif@ibm.net>
- **Date:** 1998-09-17T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<360139ec.0@news1.ibm.net>`
- **References:** `<35ff0af8.0@news1.ibm.net> <3298258.9686.19879@kcbbs.gen.nz>`

```
Richard Plinston wrote in message <3298258.9686.19879@kcbbs.gen.nz>...
>In message <<35ff0af8.0@news1.ibm.net>> "Leif Svalgaard" <leif@ibm.net>
writes:
>>
>> this is a good way of doing it.
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>What would count as 'huge' ?  and why not ?


Not huge in terms of bytes. A pic x(10000) is OK.
But huge in the sense that it contains many unrelated
items. In the limit I've seen programs where the
main program passed every 01 level in its working-storage
section to all of its subprograms which in turn didn't have
working-storage of their own. The programmer's excuse:
"I used to work with a system, that supported GLOBAL
storage (Fortran style) and this was a way of carrying on
with that habit)".

Maybe I should have said something like the above
in my post, cause this is what I meant.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: muffler shop update

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** riplin@kcbbs.gen.nz (Richard Plinston)
- **Date:** 1998-09-17T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3298259.86353.24526@kcbbs.gen.nz>`
- **References:** `<360139ec.0@news1.ibm.net>`

```
In message <<360139ec.0@news1.ibm.net>> "Leif Svalgaard" <leif@ibm.net> writes:
> >
> >What would count as 'huge' ?  and why not ?
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
> in my post, cause this is what I meant.

I do have many systems which has a very small core program
that CALLs/CANCELs the rest of the application by 
alternating between menu and program (though it can go
program -> program bypassing the menu).

This has a 'global area' that passes system information to each
in turn, these tend to be unrelated in many cases:  Current
colouration, keyboard settings, operator name, system
name, various state information, ....

One wouldn't want each application in turn having to extract
this from conrol files each time.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: muffler shop update

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** Ken Foskey <waratah@zip.com.au>
- **Date:** 1998-09-21T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<360658A7.367DC77B@zip.com.au>`
- **References:** `<360139ec.0@news1.ibm.net> <3298259.86353.24526@kcbbs.gen.nz>`

```
This is called information hiding.  If you need some common information
across many different modules you create a set of modules that will
return the information to you without rereading the information every
time.  This is fairly simple:

hidden large routine:

reads all the control data and stores it in working storage on the first
call.  All subsequent calls just return the same data.  (Maybe all
windows setup criteria from an ini file)

other modules:

a set of support modules are written that call 'hidden large routine'
and extract the small section of data that the calling program is
interested in (maybe background color)

Calling programs can then call these minor routines many times with only
a extremely small performance hit but greatly improved maintainability.

Sound like a lot of work.  Implement you monolithic call and then move
it from an ini file to a SQL server.  Then change that to a dynamic
change.

The purpose is to isolate the nitty gritty of the program from the basic
business functions.  This is difficult to do especially for 'basic'
cobol programmers who have not worked out how to call instead of using a
paragraph.

Hope this helps
Ken

Richard Plinston wrote:
> 
> In message <<360139ec.0@news1.ibm.net>> "Leif Svalgaard" <leif@ibm.net> writes:
…[28 more quoted lines elided]…
> this from conrol files each time.
```

#### ↳ Re: muffler shop update

- **From:** "Donald Tees" <donald@willmack.com>
- **Date:** 1998-09-15T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<6tn9cc$d0s$1@news.igs.net>`
- **References:** `<35FEF04F.6BF508D6@mindspring.com>`

```

skidmike wrote in message <35FEF04F.6BF508D6@mindspring.com>...
>
>any advice on structure would be appreciated.  am i doing this in such a
>way as to keep it simple? i.e. lots of small source files, well
>documented

One file I/O subroutine for each file.  Never do the file stuff outside
the file read/write subroutine.
```

### Best book to learn/refresh Cobol

- **From:** bettykat@mindspring.com (Betty Katzenelson)
- **Date:** 1998-09-15T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<35fe9913.6111565@news.mindspring.com>`

```
I am looking for a recommendation as to what book would be the best to
read to refresh (after 15+ years, learn might be a better word)  my
Cobol knowledge.  I looked at the FAQ, but I can't afford to buy all
the books listed, much less read them all.  My goal is to become
marketable as a contract programmer.  I have some experience
in other languages, most recently I took some C++ classes at a local
university.  Thanks for any input  
```

#### ↳ Re: Best book to learn/refresh Cobol

- **From:** Bal Oberoi <oberoi@ibm.net>
- **Date:** 1998-09-15T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<35FEAAB9.7CD8@ibm.net>`
- **References:** `<35fe9913.6111565@news.mindspring.com>`

```
Mitch wrote:
 
> I am looking for a recommendation as to what book would be the best to
> read to refresh (after 15+ years, learn might be a better word)  my
> Cobol knowledge.

You'll doubtless want to buy at least one introductory book to refresh 
your old know how of COBOL. The COBOL Center has a page that lists 
COBOL books in various categories. The URL of the page:
     http://www.infogoal.com/cbd/cbdbks.htm

For mainframe and AIX programmers, IBM has an online "library" of their 
COBOL manuals at: 
     http://www.software.ibm.com/ad/cobol/library.htm
One cannot recommend these manuals for *learning* COBOL, but they are 
invaluable as works of reference. 

Good luck, and the best of regards,
Bal
----- 
oberoi@ibm.net
http://www.geocities.com/~oberoi/mainfrme.html
-----
"The universe is full of magical things, 
patiently waiting for our wits to grow sharper." - Eden Phillpotts
```

```
In article <35FF3679.5251E2FF@iamerica.net>, ron kozak <beready@iamerica.net>
writes:

>
>Does anyone know anything about King Computer Services Inc., located in
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>

The COBOL, Just in Time course is written around the best selling OOBOL Text
book, Teach Yourself COBOL in 21 Days.  It has a study guide that takes you
through the book lesson by lesson giving additional data and tips where
necessary.  Particularly in the beginning stages this is a great help in
getting the student off to a qucik start with lessons on how to install and use
the compiler and editor, so you don't have to wrestle with a 1" thick manual to
get going. The course comes with a compiler, basic book on computers, a great
computer glossary, printer spacing charts, flowcharting templates and more. 
There are electronic study aids to help a student located hard to find coding
errors- vital if the student doesn't have someone helping him.  And there'a a
more advanced book of professional COBOL programmers tips and tricks.  The
Course is designed to teach the basics of  COBOL to an entry to intermediate
level, and is in use in government agencies, corporations, and educational
institutions.
If you have any questions. feel free to contact me.  


Helen
COBOL, Just in Time Course Administrator
King Computer Services, Inc.
```

### Help! Is there any useful tools to develop COBOL

- **From:** "Alan@IMS" <alanpo@netvigator.com>
- **Date:** 1998-09-15T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<35FDD9CF.EE02B5BB@netvigator.com>`

```
Dear sir,

I am a student of a University in Hong Kong and I need to write a
project in COBOL. Since this is not my favourite language and I hope
that COBOL like the other langauge, have some tools to develop the
language such as Application Generator in dBase or Foxpro. Please give
me some advices or tell me that you are hopeless :-(.

Thanks for any advice.

Alan Po (alanpo@bee-net.com)
```

#### ↳ Re: Help! Is there any useful tools to devel

- **From:** riplin@kcbbs.gen.nz (Richard Plinston)
- **Date:** 1998-09-15T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3298257.70034.12442@kcbbs.gen.nz>`
- **References:** `<35FDD9CF.EE02B5BB@netvigator.com>`

```
> 
> I am a student of a University in Hong Kong and I need to write a
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> me some advices or tell me that you are hopeless :-(.

Yes of course there are many code generators for Cobol, some
will write complete systems.  These have been available for
decades, since long before Vulcan was reanamed as dBase II.

For example: LINK, SourceWriter, Forms2, Screens, ...

Some as as modern as any other Windows development system,
such as SP2 and CaseGen.
```

```
from. It's available in Chinese too!
```

<a id="t-9f0df385a8"></a>
## Thread: Looking for Revolve/2000 and other y2k tools — 2 msgs

_Topics: [`Y2K and Year-2000 remediation`](topics.md#y2k)_

### Looking for Revolve/2000 and other y2k tools

- **From:** smayer@t-online.de (Sebastian Mayer)
- **Date:** 1998-09-15T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<6tmqr7$p6u$1@news02.btx.dtag.de>`

```
Hi,
I´m looking for tools to automaticly convert Cobol sources for y2k. It
seems that revolve/2000 is the most advanced tool for this. Now here are
my questions:
1)  Does any one know an other tool with equivalent power?
2)  Is it possible to buy an used revolve/2000 from somebody who doesn´t
need it any more?
thanx in advance
Sebastian
```

#### ↳ Re: Looking for Revolve/2000 and other y2k tools

- **From:** Vadim Maslov <vadik@siber.com>
- **Date:** 1998-09-19T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36035562.F2532257@siber.com>`
- **References:** `<6tmqr7$p6u$1@news02.btx.dtag.de>`

```
Sebastian Mayer wrote:
> 
> Hi,
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> 1)  Does any one know an other tool with equivalent power?

We have a tool called y2k-fix.
This tool applies windowing to the user-specified 
data items in the Cobol code.

It can fix both main files and copybooks.
It does not reformat the unhcnaged parts of the program,
thanks to SourcePrint capability.

You can download free evaluation package for this tool
from http://www.siber.com/sct/tools/y2k-fix.html.

This tool has several ways of selecting the 
data items that are dates: by explicit list, by picture and by name.

Our tool certainly is a way cheaper than Revolve/2000,
but it does not have a nice visual shell, it's a command line tool,
which is usually OK for big conversions that run in batches.

The big thing about this tool is that if you are willing
to program in C++, we can give you source for this tool
and you can enhance it in any way you like.

Usually end users do not want to do this,
but power users in conversion houses may need
a capability to implement additional transformations 
to their liking.

> 2)  Is it possible to buy an used revolve/2000 from somebody who doesnï¿½t
> need it any more?

As far as I heard, MF charges usage fee based on number of lines
that you convert -- so in a sense "used" Revolve/2000 may not exist at all.

Also I remember that Revolve/2000 actually does only analysis for Y2K,
and to do transformations you need to get SmartFix/2000,
but I am not really sure.

> thanx in advance
> Sebastian

Vadim Maslov
```

<a id="t-94da4c6124"></a>
## Thread: CICS, DISPLAY, and LE — 7 msgs

_Topics: [`Mainframe, z/OS, JCL, CICS`](topics.md#mainframe)_

### CICS, DISPLAY, and LE

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1998-09-15T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<6tn1mq$n3m@dfw-ixnews9.ix.netcom.com>`

```
I thought that I should post a reply from a "usually reliable source" (at
IBM's mainframe COBOL development sight at STL) about the reports of being
able to use the DISPLAY verb in a CICS application (MVS or OS/390) when
using the LE run-time library,

"Bill,

   DISPLAY has never been supported under CICS by IBM.  It may 'work' in
some cases, but if it ever has a problem IBM will point to all of the
different manuals that explicitly say that DISPLAY is not supported under
CICS.  COBOL APG, CICS App Prog Guide, etc."

If anyone can find ANY "LE - or other" documentation indicating that it is
supported (whether it writes to the CEEMSG DDname or a TSQueue), please let
me know.
```

#### ↳ Re: CICS, DISPLAY, and LE

- **From:** daniel.jacot@winterthur.ch
- **Date:** 1998-09-17T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<6tqg12$8cj$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>`
- **References:** `<6tn1mq$n3m@dfw-ixnews9.ix.netcom.com>`

```
In article <6tn1mq$n3m@dfw-ixnews9.ix.netcom.com>,
  "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com> wrote:
> I thought that I should post a reply from a "usually reliable source" (at
> IBM's mainframe COBOL development sight at STL) about the reports of being
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
>

In chapter '4.8.1.6 Coding Restrictions' of 'COBOL for OS/390 & VM Programming
Guide', Document Number: SC26-9049-01, DISPLAY is still restricted ('should be
followed')...

But anyway, it works fine. I would <never> use DISPLAY in a production
environment, but it's <very> usefull for debugging purpose. If something
works wrong with my program, I have to fix it and I use <everything> that
helps me to find the reason, even it is not 'IBM supported'. By the way,
XPEDITER isn't IBM-supported too!?!

Cheers

Daniel

-----== Posted via Deja News, The Leader in Internet Discussion ==-----
http://www.dejanews.com/rg_mkgrp.xp   Create Your Own Free Member Forum
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: CICS, DISPLAY, and LE

- **From:** Frank Swarbrick <infocat@sprynet.com>
- **Date:** 1998-09-17T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36015D0E.242A@sprynet.com>`
- **References:** `<6tn1mq$n3m@dfw-ixnews9.ix.netcom.com> <6tqg12$8cj$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>`

```
daniel.jacot@winterthur.ch wrote:
> 
> In chapter '4.8.1.6 Coding Restrictions' of 'COBOL for OS/390 & VM Programming
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> XPEDITER isn't IBM-supported too!?!

Just wondering, where does it display *to*?
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: CICS, DISPLAY, and LE

- **From:** daniel.jacot@winterthur.ch
- **Date:** 1998-09-18T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<6tt9ap$8ro$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>`
- **References:** `<6tn1mq$n3m@dfw-ixnews9.ix.netcom.com> <6tqg12$8cj$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com> <36015D0E.242A@sprynet.com>`

```
In article <36015D0E.242A@sprynet.com>,
  Frank Swarbrick <infocat@sprynet.com> wrote:
> daniel.jacot@winterthur.ch wrote:
> >
> > In chapter '4.8.1.6 Coding Restrictions' of 'COBOL for OS/390 & VM
Programming
> > Guide', Document Number: SC26-9049-01, DISPLAY is still restricted ('should
be
> > followed')...
> >
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
>

The COBOL DISPLAY allways writes to the LE/370 MSGFILE. Under CICS, this is
the transient data queue named 'CESE'.

All RPTSTG, RPTOPTS and LE-Messages are written to CESE, as well as the
CEEDUMP.

This is documented, for example in chapter '3.1.2.2  For CICS Applications' of
the 'COBOL for OS/390 & VM Version 2 Release 1 Compiler and Run-Time Migration
Guide', Document Number GC26-4764-04.

And - not IBM-supported - the DISPLAY output appears at the same location.

Cheers

Daniel

-----== Posted via Deja News, The Leader in Internet Discussion ==-----
http://www.dejanews.com/rg_mkgrp.xp   Create Your Own Free Member Forum
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: CICS, DISPLAY, and LE

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1998-09-18T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<6tu3uv$htj@dfw-ixnews6.ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<6tn1mq$n3m@dfw-ixnews9.ix.netcom.com> <6tqg12$8cj$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com> <36015D0E.242A@sprynet.com> <6tt9ap$8ro$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>`

```

daniel.jacot@winterthur.ch wrote in message

  <snip>
>
>The COBOL DISPLAY allways writes to the LE/370 MSGFILE. Under CICS, this is
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>This is documented, for example in chapter '3.1.2.2  For CICS Applications'
of
>the 'COBOL for OS/390 & VM Version 2 Release 1 Compiler and Run-Time
Migration
>Guide', Document Number GC26-4764-04.
>
>And - not IBM-supported - the DISPLAY output appears at the same location.
>


Let's be 100% clear, what 3.1.2.2 states is

"Under CICS, Language Environment output goes to a transient data queue
named CESE. Each record written to the file has a header that includes the
terminal id, the transaction id, date, and time. Figure 22 lists the types
of Language Environment output and location. "

So you are certainly correct that IBM documents the RPTxxx stuff and other
LE messages going to this location.  HOWEVER, this says NOTHING about
DISPLAY or other "application" information going anywhere (or being even
slighly supported).

I really, REALLY, hope that people are not using a documented as UNSUPPORTED
feature under CICS.

FYI - if you want to limit your application programmers to SUPPORTED
language, I suggest that you compile all CICS application programs with the

    WORD(CICS)

compiler option.  This will avoid any future problems with unsupported
interfaces and will help you insure that future IBM modifications don't
break your existing applications.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: CICS, DISPLAY, and LE

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1998-09-17T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<6trnkq$1v0@sjx-ixn9.ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<6tn1mq$n3m@dfw-ixnews9.ix.netcom.com> <6tqg12$8cj$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>`

```

daniel.jacot@winterthur.ch wrote in message <6tqg12
  <much snippage>
>
>But anyway, it works fine. I would <never> use DISPLAY in a production
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Interesting that you should say that.  In fact, there is a real difference
between "add-on 3rd party products" and using "documented as unsupported
features".  (Anyone remember people who used to use the entire high-order
byte instead of bit - and what happened when XA came around?)

The really interesting thing about you mentioning Xpediter is that it *IS*
on the "LE-enabled" list of products in the *IBM produced" 3rd party list.
In my opinion, this means that it IS supported as using "valid interfaces"
to the IBM products.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: CICS, DISPLAY, and LE

- **From:** daniel.jacot@winterthur.ch
- **Date:** 1998-09-18T00:00:00

<a id="t-1ba9b0c69f"></a>
## Thread: Micro Focus NetExpress questions — 12 msgs

_Topics: [`Compilers and vendors`](topics.md#compilers)_

### Micro Focus NetExpress questions

- **From:** kfloyd <kfloyd@shawsystems.com>
- **Date:** 1998-09-15T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<35FE3FF1.FF1AA088@shawsystems.com>`

```
I've used Micro Focus 16 bit for several years and the 32 bit version 4
for several months.  Now I'm in the process of evaluating NetExpress to
see if it will be our development environment of choice for a new
project.  Time is short, and I'm having difficulty figuring out the
NetExpress project environment.  So I'm asking for help before I give up
on NetExpress.

Here's what I want to do.  I want to have an OLE Automation Server that
can dynamically call .DLL's.  Some of the .DLLs will be based on source
that has DB2 embedded SQL.  I don't want to use the NetExpress ODBC SQL,
but instead run the source through the DB2 translator.

My first problem is that I don't see a way to put the un-translated
source in the project so that I could run the DB2 translator as part of
the build.  Does anybody know a way to accomplish that ?  My work-around
would be to run the translator outside the project.  NetExpress seems to
notice that the source has changed and will re-compile the resulting new
.CBL file.

The second problem for me is that the whole project thing seems
cumbersome for a large development effort.  I'd really rather manage
everything myself and run the compiles from the command line.  I realize
that I'm 'speaking' from ignorance, since I haven't really learned my
way around NetExpress.  But it looks like every file has to be added to
the project individually and that it would be difficult to handle
'merging' one developer's work with another.  Does anybody have
experience with large project management - several hundred .DLL's plus
hundreds more copybooks worked on by a dozen developers ?  I'm ready to
be convinced that projects are wonderful -- I just can't see it from
here.

I saw in the doc that OLE Servers have to be in a project, so my next
thought was to do that, but keep the bulk of the other code elsewhere
and do command line compiles.  But that leads to the Animator.  This is
a new development effort, so Animating is vital.  I can't figure out how
to set up the OLE Server project so that it will find the .DLL's I've
kept out of the project and go into animation on them, too.  So far I
keep getting 'called program not found'.  I've tried setting the COBDIR,
PATH, COBIDY, etc. both as project parameters and as System level
environment variables.  Does anybody know if this should work, and if
so, how to set it up ?

Thanks in advance for any help and advice

Ken Floyd
Shaw Systems
```

#### ↳ Re: Micro Focus NetExpress questions

- **From:** sands@mcmail (David Sands)
- **Date:** 1998-09-15T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<35fedaee.5211904@news.mcmail.com>`
- **References:** `<35FE3FF1.FF1AA088@shawsystems.com>`

```
On Tue, 15 Sep 1998 16:20:19 GMT, kfloyd <kfloyd@shawsystems.com>
wrote:


>My first problem is that I don't see a way to put the un-translated
>source in the project so that I could run the DB2 translator as part of
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>.CBL file.

Net Express has support for the DB/2 precompiler built in. You do not
need to Precompile outside the IDE but do need to set the relevant
directives. There is a documentation download on WebSync which details
all the directives. I think it also still accepts the directive that
could be using in Workbench for DB2 support. You will need to IBM DB2
SDK installed.
>
>.  But it looks like every file has to be added to
>the project individually and that it would be difficult to handle
>'merging' one developer's work with another. 

Check out the import of LBT files. It is an easy way to automate the
loading of elements to a project even if you never used them. You can
easily set up a LBT for import using redirection :- 

"dir /b *.int > myapp.lbt"

You can then highlight the INTs of the programs you want in the DLL
and right click. Select package as a DLL.

If you have multiple developers working together you should consider
investing in a good source control package (MS Source Safe or PVCS
come to mind) which could help manage the situation.

>
>I saw in the doc that OLE Servers have to be in a project, so my next
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
>so, how to set it up ?

If the DLL name if different to the entry point you are trying to call
then you need to load the DLL first by using procedure pointers:-

01 ws-point    usage is procedure-pointer.

	set ws-point to entry "mydllname"

Hope this helps
David.
```

- **From:** kfloyd <kfloyd@shawsystems.com>
- **Date:** 1998-09-16T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<35FFB07D.D9692BF6@shawsystems.com>`
- **References:** `<35FE3FF1.FF1AA088@shawsystems.com> <35fedaee.5211904@news.mcmail.com>`

```
> Net Express has support for the DB/2 precompiler built in. You do not
```
