…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> SDK installed.

Thanks for the pointer to WebSync.  I couldn't find any references to the DB2
pre-processor still being built in.  I'll check out Websync.
```

#### ↳ Re: Micro Focus NetExpress questions

- **From:** "Gene Webb" <gene.webb@newsguy.com>
- **Date:** 1998-09-15T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<6tnbuo$sr9@enews2.newsguy.com>`
- **References:** `<35FE3FF1.FF1AA088@shawsystems.com>`

```
I agree that the IDE/Project Environment is cumberson and a lot of times
leaves questions in my mind as to what is being compiled and linked into my
DLL/EXE.  I don't like the fact that I can't change the default compiler
directives when it comes up (you have to use a directives file to override
this).  I personally do not use the IDE at ALL!  I compile and link from the
command line. If I have to debug I use the CBL_BREAK compiler directive and
still run from the command line using the IDE only enough to animate.

We have three other developers that use Netexpress right now.  They all use
the IDE. The biggest reason they use the IDE (99%) is that they all come
from a mainframe environment where someone else usually set up the compile
and link JCL and do not understand how to compile and link from the command
line.

Of all of the problems that they have experienced 99.5% are directly related
to the IDE or not understanding how to use the IDE.  This has caused all
kinds of problems.

I STRONGLY recommend that you set up make files and compile from the command
line.  I create one directory for each program and I have a standard make
file that I replace the program name with a variable in the make file that
works for every DLL and one for every EXE file.  I can rebuild my entire
application (50 or more programs) in a couple of minutes with a simple batch
file.

I have had very few problems with the Netexpress compiler but tons of
problems with the IDE.  I remember someone once wrote in this newgroup that
an IDE should make you at least 50% more productive and I agree.  The
Netexpress IDE takes 3 to 5 times longer (I personally found the Fujitsu IDE
worse) to use, IF YOU UNDERSTAND compiling and linking.  I would contend if
you don't (and don't take the time to learn) you are going to have more
problems in the long run whether you use the IDE or not.

Okay thats my 2 cents worth.

kfloyd wrote in message <35FE3FF1.FF1AA088@shawsystems.com>...
>I've used Micro Focus 16 bit for several years and the 32 bit version 4
>for several months.  Now I'm in the process of evaluating NetExpress to
…[44 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

#### ↳ Re: Micro Focus NetExpress questions

- **From:** smayer@t-online.de (Sebastian Mayer)
- **Date:** 1998-09-15T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<6tmrrh$pgp$1@news02.btx.dtag.de>`
- **References:** `<35FE3FF1.FF1AA088@shawsystems.com>`

```
kfloyd wrote:
> 
> 
…[29 more quoted lines elided]…
> 
I don´t have any experience with dll´s because I´m working with int,gnt
and 
lbr´s. But perhaps you could adapt the behaviour.
1) With the above files you don´t need to take them into a project. You
can
   edit/compile one single file (like in 16bit Workbench). If another
file
   calls it, it must be only accesible via cobdir. If you use a lbr, you
must
   call it if you want to call a file within, e.g. to use file
"subprog.gnt"
   in "lib1.lbr" you must do the following:
   call "lib1.lbr"    (do it at the beginning of the program)
   ...
   call "subprog" using ...
2) If you don´t have them in the project, NetExpress doesn´t 
   notice that the source has changed and will NOT re-compile the file
3) If you have problems, animating dll´s writen in COBOL try to replace
the
   programms with their gnt/int equivalent.
```

I have not found one I like for even the smaller stuff ... my systems
typically
run about 50-80 programs, maybe 10 databases.  Not one of the systems
allows me to set up a project the way that I feel it should be set up. The
more
complex the system, the less versatile.

Funnily enough, they would all be fairly decent if they simply (and not one
I have used does)  allowed nesting. The concept of a large project being
composed of small projects never seems to have occurred to one of them.
There has also not been one that had a hook.  I want to backup by versions,
for example, as part of my make.  A nice simple make allows copies.  I have
yet to see one that scans Cobol programs and *BIULDS* dependency lists.

I see that Fujitsu actually uses a second party(MS) make, and the GUI
project
manager simply builds the make files.

#### ↳ Re: Micro Focus NetExpress questions

- **From:** smb@mfltdz.co.ukz (Steve Biggs)
- **Date:** 1998-09-16T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<6tnrsd$kv9@hyperion.mfltd.co.uk>`
- **References:** `<35FE3FF1.FF1AA088@shawsystems.com>`

```
kfloyd <kfloyd@shawsystems.com> wrote:
>I saw in the doc that OLE Servers have to be in a project

Hi Ken,
Small correction: COBOL OLE servers can be compiled outside of a project, 
it's only the class wizard that requires a project to be present in order 
to automatically create one.

After compiling, you can use a command line to link, such as:
cbllink -D -Re <nameofclassfile.obj> dllserver.obj

>, so my next
>thought was to do that, but keep the bulk of the other code elsewhere
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>so, how to set it up ?

If David Sands' tip about calling the DLL first doesn't work, try linking 
it as "Dynamic" (-R? using cbllink) which means that the DLL will use the 
registry for finding the COBOL run-time, rather than looking for COBDIR 
etc.

Steve.
Micro Focus Development.
```

- **From:** "Donald Tees" <donald@willmack.com>

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol

kfloyd wrote in message <35FFB1C9.4E01C555@shawsystems.com>...
>> kfloyd <kfloyd@shawsystems.com> wrote:

>> >thought was to do that, but keep the bulk of the other code elsewhere
>> >and do command line compiles.  But that leads to the Animator.  This is
>> >a new development effort, so Animating is vital.  I can't figure out how
>> >to set up the OLE Server project so that it will find the .DLL's I've


Don't know if this will work or not, but I had the same problem with
Fujitsu,
then I discovered that when a DLL is created, a parallel LIB is created. The
LIB contains pointers to the DLL, and is included in the link of the second
program that uses the DLL. That results in the calling program knowing the
correct name of the DLL, as well as the entry point.  Once I used that to
link,
windows was smart enough to follow the path statements.
```

```
KJALGER wrote:
>What is, and where does one find, a good Cobol compiler for an Intel based
>desktop computer?  I am interested in learned Cobol, but cannot find a
>compiler.  Any help would be great.


Thorough answer: http://pw2.netcom.com/~wmklein/cobolfaq.htm

Quick answer: Go to www.adtools.com and download Fujitsu COBOL version 3,
a full commercial production compiler, plus documentation, for *free*.

### HER NAME is CHARLA, SHE is A COKE WHORE

- **From:** dm_telvis5440orfight@my-dejanews.com
- **Date:** 1998-09-15T00:00:00

## Thread: Configuration Question Micro Focus — 4 msgs

_Topics: [`Compilers and vendors`](topics.md#compilers)_

### Configuration Question Micro Focus

- **From:** Frank Pohlmann <Frank.Pohlmann@baeurer.de>
- **Date:** 1998-09-16T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<35FFDF80.A9CB92DB@baeurer.de>`

```
I have a programm that calls and cancels many other programms.
When using MF Workbench 3.4 or 4.0 I get several errors.
In 4.0 I get "routine table overflow". So I configure a COBOPT.CFG with
routine_table_size=1024 and it works.

But in 3.4 I get the error 157 Object file too large. I think, the
COBOPT.CFG has no function in 16 bit and I had some attempts with the
stack size (/S16000 up and down), but I still have the same error. Does
anybody has some ideas which other switches or configurations may help?
If I put the programs in a LBR, it works, but there are situations when
I don't want to have a LBR.

Frank Pohlmann
```

#### ↳ Re: Configuration Question Micro Focus

- **From:** "Gael Wilson" <gw@mfltd.co.uk>
- **Date:** 1998-09-17T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<6tt53t$94c@hyperion.mfltd.co.uk>`
- **References:** `<35FFDF80.A9CB92DB@baeurer.de>`

```
You're quite correct that COBOPT.CFG is not used in 3.4. If you set the P3
switch it will increase the number of programs that can be loaded.


Frank Pohlmann wrote in message <35FFDF80.A9CB92DB@baeurer.de>...
>I have a programm that calls and cancels many other programms.
>When using MF Workbench 3.4 or 4.0 I get several errors.
…[21 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

#### ↳ Re: Configuration Question Micro Focus

- **From:** "Gael Wilson" <gw@mfltd.co.uk>
- **Date:** 1998-09-17T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<6tt53v$94c@hyperion.mfltd.co.uk>`
- **References:** `<35FFDF80.A9CB92DB@baeurer.de>`

```
Frank,

You are quite correct that COBOPT.CFG is not used in V3.4. However, if you
use the P3 switch it will increase the number of programs you can have
loaded.


Frank Pohlmann wrote in message <35FFDF80.A9CB92DB@baeurer.de>...

<a id="t-2fae4098a7"></a>
## Thread: IBM mainframe COBOL - how to close an output file and reopen it with a different DSN — 8 msgs

_Topics: [`Mainframe, z/OS, JCL, CICS`](topics.md#mainframe) · [`Help requests and how-to`](topics.md#help)_

### Re: IBM mainframe COBOL - how to close an output file and reopen it with a different DSN

- **From:** Binyamin_Dissen@theoffice.net (Binyamin Dissen)
- **Date:** 1998-09-16T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,alt.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<35ffb208.101793553@news.netvision.net.il>`
- **References:** `<6s0p5r$4v0$1@holly.prod.itd.earthlink.net>`

```
On Wed, 26 Aug 1998 06:45:36 -0400 "Jim Gallagher" <jimgall@sprintmail.com>
wrote:

:>Hello,
:>I am new to the IBM mainframe environment (not new to COBOL)  - I am writing
:>batch programs using COBOL II and DB2 in an OS390 environment.  One program
:>must create a file for each of multiple institutions. The files would be
:>named BK.PROD.001INST, BK.PROD.002INST etc. Institution number is a 3
:>position field.  Is there a way in IBM COBOL (other than allocating and
:>declaring FDs for 999 files in my program) to handle this ? i.e. create
:>BK.PROD.001INST, when institution # changes close it and reopen it as
:>BK.PROD.002INST.

:>Any pointers would be most appreciated.  BTW this is not homework !!

Jim, 

I am just back from a US trip and it appears that no one has suggested the
following solution.

Assuming that you will always be producing all the files, the following JCL
will do the job.

//MYDD   DD  DSN=BK.PROD.001INST,......,FREE=CLOSE
//MYDD   DD  DSN=BK.PROD.002INST,......,FREE=CLOSE
//MYDD   DD  DSN=BK.PROD.003INST,......,FREE=CLOSE
//MYDD   DD  DSN=BK.PROD.004INST,......,FREE=CLOSE
//MYDD   DD  DSN=BK.PROD.005INST,......,FREE=CLOSE

Each time you close the file assigned to MYDD the current allocation will be
released and when the file is reopened the next MYDD will be used. 

Of course, with this approach, if you for example wish to skip 003INST you
would still have to open and close it.
```

#### ↳ Re: IBM mainframe COBOL - how to close an output file and reopen it with a different DSN

- **From:** "COBOL Frog (Huib Klink)" <H.Klink@IMN.nl>
- **Date:** 1998-09-16T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,alt.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36000E60.3415D546@IMN.nl>`
- **References:** `<6s0p5r$4v0$1@holly.prod.itd.earthlink.net> <35ffb208.101793553@news.netvision.net.il>`

```
Binyamin Dissen wrote:

> //MYDD   DD  DSN=BK.PROD.001INST,......,FREE=CLOSE
> //MYDD   DD  DSN=BK.PROD.002INST,......,FREE=CLOSE
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> released and when the file is reopened the next MYDD will be used.

8<

Very interesting. You mean that the DD statements with the same label (MYDD) are
kind of stacked (LIFO) and at run time popped and activated one-by-one at each
CLOSE? I never knew this. Is this unwanted behavior (a bug), undesigned behavior
(a feature) or designed by intend (a function) of JCL?

The Frog
```

#### ↳ Re: IBM mainframe COBOL - how to close an output file and reopen it with a different DSN

- **From:** "Chris Osborne" <chris_n_osborne@yahoo.com>
- **Date:** 1998-09-16T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,alt.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36005f7b.0@news2.uswest.net>`
- **References:** `<6s0p5r$4v0$1@holly.prod.itd.earthlink.net> <35ffb208.101793553@news.netvision.net.il>`

```

Binyamin Dissen wrote in message
<35ffb208.101793553@news.netvision.net.il>...
>On Wed, 26 Aug 1998 06:45:36 -0400 "Jim Gallagher" <jimgall@sprintmail.com>
>wrote:
>
>:>Hello,
>:>I am new to the IBM mainframe environment (not new to COBOL)  - I am
writing
>:>batch programs using COBOL II and DB2 in an OS390 environment.  One
program
>:>must create a file for each of multiple institutions. The files would be
>:>named BK.PROD.001INST, BK.PROD.002INST etc. Institution number is a 3
…[21 more quoted lines elided]…
>Each time you close the file assigned to MYDD the current allocation will
be
>released and when the file is reopened the next MYDD will be used.
>
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>Director, Dissen Software, Bar & Grill - Israel



I've know about FREE=CLOSE for a long time, and it's utility in getting
report files out to the JES2 spool queue long before a job ends is
enormously useful.
Before your post, I would have expected your JCL to a) attempt to reallocate
the first MYDD statement after the program closed the file, or b) take a JCL
error of some kind.  But after looking at it for a minute, I realize that
it's very interesting.

I don't have a reason to use this right now, but I'll remember it for the
future.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: IBM mainframe COBOL - how to close an output file and reopen it with a diff

- **From:** sff5ky@aol.com (Sff5ky)
- **Date:** 1998-09-20T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<19980920221800.SAA23830@ladder01.news.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<360556D2.3EC83434@hankel.mersinet.co.uk>`

```
Some time back I saw some code posted that implied the use of Assembler inline
with the COBOL source.  I would appreciate someone expanding on that brief code
snippet and explain how this can be accomplished under COBOL for MVS/LE/OS390. 
I can get everything to compile upto the point of the ASM code.

I've been hammering at this piece for a week and no progress.  I suspect that
this is system dependent or COBOL68. I do not remember the ability to handle
inline alternate languages after COBOL74.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: IBM mainframe COBOL - how to close an output file and reopen it with a diff

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "Joel C. Ewing" <jcewing@acm.org>
- **Date:** 1998-09-24T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<360B20B4.4E12249F@acm.org>`
- **References:** `<360556D2.3EC83434@hankel.mersinet.co.uk> <19980920221800.SAA23830@ladder01.news.aol.com>`

```
Sff5ky wrote:
> Some time back I saw some code posted that implied the use of Assembler inline
> with the COBOL source.  I would appreciate someone expanding on that brief code
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> inline alternate languages after COBOL74.

IBM has spent considerable effort over the years in standardizing
calling conventions and run-time environments to allow linking together
in a single load module code produced by Assembler and a multitude of
compilers.  It wouldn't make sense (unless the standard language
definition required it) to imbed/replicate assembler-like capabilites
into other compilers, when this can be done with greater clarity by a
separate assembly, using the linkage editor to merge.

The original problem posed (allocating a large number of datasets with a
"data-related" dataset name with a minimal number of FD's) would
probably be done at our shop using a CALL to a DYNAM subroutine
(implemented in assembler to supply functionality not available in
COBOL).  This subroutine, originally obtained from the MVS MODS tape
(available through SHARE, NaSPA, and possibly other sources), provides
an interface for dynamically allocating and deallocating a data set to a
DDNAME (in other words, performs at program execution time using runtime
data the equivalent effect of including a DD statement in the JCL), but
allows re-use of the same DDNAME and all the allocation parameters can
be variables under program control.  The general sequence of actions is
DYNAM ALLOC CALL, OPEN, process, CLOSE, DYNAM DEALLOC call; and this
sequence may be repeated many times for a single DD NAME (FD name in
program).
```

#### ↳ Re: IBM mainframe COBOL - how to close an output file and reopen it with a different DSN

- **From:** kleidner@earthlink.net (Ken Leidner)
- **Date:** 1998-09-17T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,alt.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<6tpnjd$ahf$1@holly.prod.itd.earthlink.net>`
- **References:** `<6s0p5r$4v0$1@holly.prod.itd.earthlink.net> <35ffb208.101793553@news.netvision.net.il>`

```

Binyamin_Dissen@theoffice.net says...
>
>On Wed, 26 Aug 1998 06:45:36 -0400 "Jim Gallagher" <jimgall@sprintmail.com>
>wrote:
>

how JCL / Programs will work.  Remember some of the problems that SMS caused 
because people assumes it was OK to code JCL that had the same DSN with the 
"first" one DISP=(OLD,DELETE) and the "second" one DSIP=(NEW,CATLG)

I would stear clear of this solution. 

>Assuming that you will always be producing all the files, the following JCL
>will do the job.
…[17 more quoted lines elided]…
>Director, Dissen Software, Bar & Grill - Israel
```

#### ↳ Re: IBM mainframe COBOL - how to close an output file and reopen it with a different DSN

- **From:** Bill Lynch <wblynch@att.net>
- **Date:** 1998-09-17T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,alt.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<360082A0.18B99A8C@att.net>`
- **References:** `<6s0p5r$4v0$1@holly.prod.itd.earthlink.net> <35ffb208.101793553@news.netvision.net.il>`

```
Binyamin Dissen wrote:
> 
(snip)
> Assuming that you will always be producing all the files, the following JCL
> will do the job.
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> //MYDD   DD  DSN=BK.PROD.005INST,......,FREE=CLOSE

Unless the spec has changed recently, these are invalid DSNs. The 3rd
node begins with a numeric character, a no no.

Bill Lynch

#### ↳ Re: Merge Algorithms

- **From:** paulr@bix.com (paulr)
- **Date:** 1998-09-16T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<6tpbdp$e2j@lotho.delphi.com>`
- **References:** `<6tnvfs$296$1@news.latrobe.edu.au>`

```
Sure- didn't everyone in their sophmore year? :) 
(And who hasn't written a Pascal compiler? :) 

Check out Knuth's tomes for some pretty decent 
algorithms for doing merges and sorts.

They are probably available in your library, public
or school. Volume 3 I think, but I am not sure of that
at all.

-Paul



Jason Coates (jasonc@lucifer.its.latrobe.edu.au) wrote:
: Hey all,

: Other than using the Merge Facility within COBOL, has anyone ever devised
: there own Merge Algorithm. I would be very interested to know if anyone 
: else other than myself is beating himself up.

: Jason

#### ↳ Re: Merge Algorithms

- **From:** pitchl@tdbank.ca (Lew Pitcher)
- **Date:** 1998-09-16T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<35ffaa43.64654304@news21.bellglobal.com>`
- **References:** `<6tnvfs$296$1@news.latrobe.edu.au>`

```
On 16 Sep 1998 09:13:32 GMT, jasonc@lucifer.its.latrobe.edu.au (Jason Coates) wrote:

>Hey all,
>
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>Jason

One of the first algorithms I was taught is the "Balanced Line Merge". I'm not sure, but
it is possible that Knuth's "The Art of Computer Programming" volume 3 ("Sorting and
Searching") has it. I'm sure that TACPv3 has a number of merge algorithms.

System Consultant, Delivery Systems Architecture

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Merge Algorithms

Lew Pitcher wrote in message <35ffaa43.64654304@news21.bellglobal.com>...
>On 16 Sep 1998 09:13:32 GMT, jasonc@lucifer.its.latrobe.edu.au (Jason
Coates) wrote:
>
>>Hey all,
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>>else other than myself is beating himself up.


You really don't want to mess with that. But, if you must, the Polyphase
Merge
is one of the best. To give you a feel for what you are in for, I'm  showing
below
the FSORT program from ETK. It makes this post rather longish, so I
apologize
for that, but, one can't really show it five words or less.

Here goes:

000100 IDENTIFICATION DIVISION.
000200 PROGRAM-ID.    FSORT.
000300
000400 AUTHOR.        LEIF SVALGAARD.
000500 DATE-WRITTEN.  89/09/16
000600     -REVISED:  97/07/18.
000700
000800 ENVIRONMENT DIVISION.
000900
001000 CONFIGURATION SECTION.
001100 SOURCE-COMPUTER. PORTABLE.
001200 OBJECT-COMPUTER. PORTABLE.
001300
001400 DATA DIVISION.
001500
001600 WORKING-STORAGE SECTION.
001700
001800 01  CONSTANT-SECTION.
001900     02  GREATER-THAN            PIC X      VALUE "G".
002000     02  LESS-THAN               PIC X      VALUE "L".
002100
002200 01  BEGIN-DYNAMIC-STORAGE       PIC X(4)   VALUE "<ETK".
002300
002400 01  SORT-FILE-HANDLING.
002500     02  NBR-OF-FILES            PIC S9(3)  COMP  VALUE +6.
002600     02  TOP-FILE-NBR            PIC S9(3)  COMP  VALUE +7.
002700     02  LAST-MERGE-FILE         PIC S9(3)  COMP.
002800     02  INPUT-FILE              PIC S9(3)  COMP.
002900     02  OUTPUT-FILE             PIC S9(3)  COMP.
003000     02  SELECTED-FILE           PIC S9(3)  COMP.
003100     02  FROM-FILE               PIC S9(3)  COMP.
003200     02  TO-FILE                 PIC S9(3)  COMP.
003300     02  NEXT-FILE               PIC S9(3)  COMP.
003400     02  PREV-FILE               PIC S9(3)  COMP.
003500     02  THIS-FILE               PIC S9(3)  COMP.
003600     02  FILE-NBR                PIC S9(3)  COMP.
003700     02  SMALLEST-SO-FAR         PIC S9(3)  COMP.
003800     02  NBR-OF-ACTIVE-FILES     PIC S9(3)  COMP.
003900
004000     02  WORKFILE-NAME.
004100         03  FILLER              PIC X(5)   VALUE "SORTW".
004200         03  WORKFILE-NBR        PIC 9(1).
004300
004400     02  THE-FILES                          OCCURS 6 TIMES.
004500         03  THE-USAGE           PIC X(01).
004600             88  USING-SORTFILE             VALUE IS "S".
004700         03  THE-STATUS          PIC X(01).
004800             88  FILE-NOT-EXHAUSTED         VALUE IS " ".
004900             88  END-OF-THE-FILE            VALUE IS "E".
005000         03  THE-FILENAME        PIC X(08).
005100         03  THE-RECORD          PIC X(89).
005200         03  THE-SORTKEY         PIC X(89).
005300
005400     02  FILLER                             OCCURS 7 TIMES.
005500         03  WHICH-FILE          PIC S9(3)  COMP.
005600         03  ACTIVE-FILE         PIC S9(3)  COMP.
005700         03  IDEAL-RUNS          PIC 9(9).
005800         03  DUMMY-RUNS          PIC 9(9).
005900
006000 01  POLYPHASE-VARIABLES.
006100     02  MERGE-LEVEL             PIC S9(3)  COMP.
006200     02  SMALLEST-ONE            PIC S9(3)  COMP.
006300     02  NBR-OF-RUNS             PIC 9(9).
006400     02  RECORD-NBR              PIC 9(9).
006500     02  MAX-RECORD-NBR          PIC S9(9)  VALUE +999999999.
006600     02  RECORD-INCREMENT        PIC S9(9)  VALUE -1.
006700     02  RUN-INDICATOR           PIC X.
006800         88  END-OF-RUN                     VALUE IS "E".
006900
007000 01  COMPARISON-HANDLING.
007100     02  COMPARE-RESULT          PIC X.
007200         88  COMPARE-GREATER-THAN                 VALUES "G".
007300         88  COMPARE-LESS-THAN                    VALUES "L".
007400
007500 01  CHAR-HANDLING.
007600     02  CHAR-NBR                PIC S9(3)  COMP.
007700     02  COPY-NBR                PIC S9(3)  COMP.
007800     02  FROM-POSN               PIC S9(3)  COMP.
007900     02  TO-POSN                 PIC S9(3)  COMP.
008000     02  MAX-POSN                PIC S9(3)  COMP  VALUE +80.
008100
008200 01  LINE-HANDLING.
008300     02  COMPARE-NBR             PIC S9(3)  COMP.
008400     02  PIVOT-NBR               PIC S9(3)  COMP.
008500     02  LINE-NBR                PIC S9(3)  COMP.
008600
008700     02  CUR-LOWER-LIMIT         PIC S9(3)  COMP.
008800     02  CUR-UPPER-LIMIT         PIC S9(3)  COMP.
008900     02  PARTITION-SIZE          PIC S9(3)  COMP.
009000
009100     02  LOWER-PTR               PIC S9(3)  COMP.
009200     02  MIDDLE-PTR              PIC S9(3)  COMP.
009300     02  UPPER-PTR               PIC S9(3)  COMP.
009400     02  LINE-PTR                PIC S9(3)  COMP.
009500
009600     02  STACK-PTR               PIC S9(3)  COMP.
009700     02  STACK-NXT               PIC S9(3)  COMP.
009800     02  QUICKSORT-STACK                          OCCURS 32 TIMES.
009900         03  LOWER-LIMIT         PIC S9(3)  COMP.
010000         03  UPPER-LIMIT         PIC S9(3)  COMP.
010100
010200 01  CONVPK-CONTROL.
010300     02  CONVPK-TASK-ID          PIC X(4).
010400     02  CONVPK-OPERATION        PIC X.
010500     02  CONVPK-FEEDBACK         PIC X.
010600
010700 01  CONVPK-TEXT.
010800     02  CONVPK-CHAR             PIC X            OCCURS 81 TIMES.
010900
011000 01  CONVPK-VALUES.
011100     02  CONVPK-VALUE            PIC S9(3)  COMP  OCCURS 81 TIMES.
011200
011300 01  SORTIO-CONTROL.
011400     02  SORTIO-TASK-ID          PIC X(4).
011500     02  SORTIO-OPERATION        PIC X.
011600     02  SORTIO-FEEDBACK         PIC X.
011700         88  SORTIO-END-OF-FILE             VALUE IS ".".
011800     02  SORTIO-FILENAME         PIC X(8)   VALUE SPACES.
011900
012000 01  SORTIO-RECORD.
012100     02  SORTIO-TEXT             PIC X(80).
012200     02  SORTIO-RECNBR           PIC 9(09).
012300
012400 01  LINE-POINTERS.
012500     02  LAST-LINE-PTR           PIC S9(3)  COMP.
012600     02  MAX-LINE-PTR            PIC S9(3)  COMP  VALUE +368.
012700     02  LINE-NUMBER             PIC S9(3)  COMP  OCCURS 368.
012800
012900 01  LINE-STORAGE.
013000     02  LINE-STORED             PIC X(89)  OCCURS 368 TIMES.
013100
013200 01  LINE-SORTKEYS.
013300     02  LINE-SORTKEY            PIC X(89)  OCCURS 368 TIMES.
013400
013500*    THE CONSTANT 368 SATISFIES: 368 * (80 + 9) = 32752 < 32K
013600
013700 01  STRNPK-CONTROL.
013800     02  STRNPK-TASK-ID          PIC X(4).
013900     02  STRNPK-OPERATION        PIC X.
014000     02  STRNPK-FEEDBACK         PIC X.
014100     02  STRNPK-SIZE             PIC S9(3)  COMP.
014200
014300     02  STRNPK-TEXT             PIC X(80).
014400     02  STRNPK-FILLCHAR         PIC X(01).
014500     02  FILLER                  PIC X(79).
014600
014700 01  PARAMETER-HANDLING.
014800     02  PARAMETER-NBR           PIC S9(3)  COMP.
014900
015000 01  PARAMETER-CONTROL.
015100     02  PARAMETER-TASK-ID       PIC X(4).
015200     02  PARAMETER-OPERATION     PIC X.
015300     02  PARAMETER-FEEDBACK      PIC X.
015400     02  PARAMETER-FILENAME      PIC X(8)   VALUE "FSORT".
015500
015600 01  PARAMETER-LIST.
015700     02  PARAMETER-COUNT         PIC S9(3)  COMP.
015800
015900     02  PARAMETER-VALUES.
016000         03  UNSORTED-FILE       PIC X(8)   VALUE "UNSORTED".
016100         03  SORTED-FILE         PIC X(8)   VALUE  "SORTED".
016200         03  FILLER              PIC X(8)   OCCURS 62 TIMES.
016300
016400     02  FILLER                  REDEFINES  PARAMETER-VALUES.
016500         03  PARAMETER-VALUE     PIC X(8)   OCCURS 64 TIMES.
016600
016700     02  PARAMETER-SWITCHES.
016800         03  CHARS-SWITCH        PIC X(8)   VALUE  "CHARS".
016900         03  SORT-ORDER-SWITCH   PIC X(8)   VALUE  "SORT".
017000         03  SORT-WORD-SWITCH    PIC X(8)   VALUE  "WORD".
017100         03  FROM-POSN-SWITCH    PIC X(8)   VALUE  "FROM".
017200         03  TO-POSN-SWITCH      PIC X(8)   VALUE   "TO".
017300         03  FILLER              PIC X(24)  VALUE  SPACES.
017400
017500         03  CHARS-SWITCH-CONTROL PIC X(1)  VALUE    "N".
017600             88  CHARS-SWITCH-PRESENT       VALUE IS "Y".
017700         03  SORT-SWITCH-CONTROL PIC X(1)   VALUE    "N".
017800             88  SORT-SWITCH-PRESENT        VALUE IS "Y".
017900         03  WORD-SWITCH-CONTROL PIC X(1)   VALUE    "N".
018000             88  WORD-SWITCH-PRESENT        VALUE IS "Y".
018100         03  FROM-SWITCH-CONTROL PIC X(1)   VALUE    "N".
018200             88  FROM-SWITCH-PRESENT        VALUE IS "Y".
018300         03  TO-SWITCH-CONTROL   PIC X(1)   VALUE    "N".
018400             88  TO-SWITCH-PRESENT          VALUE IS "Y".
018500         03  FILLER              PIC X(3)   VALUE   SPACE.
018600
018700         03  SWITCH-VALUES.
018800             04  CHARS-SWITCH-VALUE
018900                                 PIC X(8).
019000                 88  RANKED-CHARS-WANTED    VALUE IS "RANKED".
019100             04  SORT-SWITCH-VALUE
019200                                 PIC X(8).
019300                 88  DESCENDING-SORT-WANTED VALUES "DESC" "BACK".
019400                 88  BACKWARDS-SORT-WANTED  VALUE IS "BACK".
019500             04  WORD-SWITCH-VALUE
019600                                 PIC X(8).
019700                 88  SORT-ON-WORD-WANTED    VALUE IS "WORD".
019800             04  FROM-SWITCH-VALUE
019900                                 PIC X(8).
020000             04  TO-SWITCH-VALUE PIC X(8).
020100             04  FILLER          PIC X(24)  VALUE    SPACES.
020200
020300         03  FILLER              REDEFINES  SWITCH-VALUES.
020400             04  SWITCH-VALUE    PIC X(8)   OCCURS 8 TIMES.
020500
020600 01  SWITCH-HANDLING.
020700     02  ACCUMULATED-NBR         PIC S9(9)  COMP.
020800
020900     02  NUMBER-INDICATOR        PIC X.
021000         88  NUMBER-WAS-FOUND               VALUE IS "Y".
021100
021200     02  OVERRIDE-LIST.
021300         03  FILLER              PIC X(10)  VALUE "1 RANKED".
021400         03  FILLER              PIC X(10)  VALUE "1 ASIS".
021500         03  FILLER              PIC X(10)  VALUE "2 DESC".
021600         03  FILLER              PIC X(10)  VALUE "2 BACK".
021700         03  FILLER              PIC X(10)  VALUE "2 ASC".
021800         03  FILLER              PIC X(10)  VALUE "3 WORD".
021900     02  FILLER                  REDEFINES  OVERRIDE-LIST.
022000         03  OVERRIDE-VALUE      PIC X(10)  OCCURS 6 TIMES.
022100
022200     02  MAX-OVERRIDE-NBR        PIC S9(3)  COMP  VALUE +6.
022300     02  OVERRIDE-NBR            PIC S9(3)  COMP.
022400
022500     02  SWITCH-CONTROL.
022600         03  SWITCH-NBR          PIC 9.
022700         03  FILLER              PIC X.
022800         03  SWITCH-OVERRIDE.
022900             04  SWITCH-CHAR     PIC X      OCCURS 8 TIMES.
023000         03  SWITCH-DIGITS       REDEFINES  SWITCH-OVERRIDE.
023100             04  SWITCH-DIGIT    PIC 9      OCCURS 8 TIMES.
023200
023300*DIALOG MANAGER.
023400 COPY BSKELD.
060100
060200******************    INITIALIZE THE PROGRAM    ******************
060300
060400 INITIALIZE-THE-PROGRAM.
060500     MOVE SESSION-TASK-ID
060600       TO PARAMETER-TASK-ID
060700          CONVPK-TASK-ID
060800          SORTIO-TASK-ID
060900          STRNPK-TASK-ID
061000
061100     MOVE SPACES TO SORTIO-RECORD
061200     PERFORM SET-OK
061300     .
061400
061500 SET-OK.
061600     MOVE OK-EVENT TO THE-NEXT-EVENT
061700     .
061800
061900*******************    EXECUTE THE PROGRAM    ********************
062000
062100 EXECUTE-THE-PROGRAM.
062200     PERFORM GET-PARAMETERS
062300     PERFORM OPEN-FILES
062400     PERFORM DISTRIBUTION-PHASE
062500     PERFORM MERGE-PHASE
062600     PERFORM CLOSE-FILES
062700     .
062800
062900 GET-PARAMETERS.
063000     MOVE "GET" TO PARAMETER-OPERATION
063100     PERFORM CALL-PARAMETER-PACKAGE
063200
063300     IF UNSORTED-FILE = SPACES
063400         MOVE "UNSORTED" TO UNSORTED-FILE
063500     .
063600     IF SORTED-FILE = SPACES
063700         MOVE "SORTED" TO SORTED-FILE
063800     .
063900     IF NOT SORT-SWITCH-PRESENT
064000     OR NOT DESCENDING-SORT-WANTED
064100         MOVE "ASC"  TO SORT-SWITCH-VALUE
064200     .
064300     IF NOT WORD-SWITCH-PRESENT
064400     OR NOT SORT-ON-WORD-WANTED
064500         MOVE SPACES TO WORD-SWITCH-VALUE
064600     .
064700     IF NOT CHARS-SWITCH-PRESENT
064800     OR NOT RANKED-CHARS-WANTED
064900         MOVE "ASIS" TO CHARS-SWITCH-VALUE
065000     .
065100     PERFORM SET-SWITCH-IF-FOUND
065200       VARYING OVERRIDE-NBR FROM 1 BY 1
065300         UNTIL OVERRIDE-NBR > MAX-OVERRIDE-NBR
065400
065500     MOVE 2 TO PARAMETER-NBR
065600     PERFORM ACCUMULATE-NBR-IN-PARAMS
065700     IF NUMBER-WAS-FOUND
065800         MOVE ACCUMULATED-NBR TO FROM-POSN
065900     ELSE
066000         MOVE FROM-SWITCH-VALUE TO SWITCH-DIGITS
066100         PERFORM ACCUMULATE-NBR-IN-SWITCH
066200         IF NUMBER-WAS-FOUND
066300             MOVE ACCUMULATED-NBR TO FROM-POSN
066400         ELSE
066500             MOVE 1 TO FROM-POSN
066600     .
066700     PERFORM ACCUMULATE-NBR-IN-PARAMS
066800     IF NUMBER-WAS-FOUND
066900         MOVE ACCUMULATED-NBR TO TO-POSN
067000     ELSE
067100         MOVE TO-SWITCH-VALUE TO SWITCH-DIGITS
067200         PERFORM ACCUMULATE-NBR-IN-SWITCH
067300         IF NUMBER-WAS-FOUND
067400             MOVE ACCUMULATED-NBR TO TO-POSN
067500         ELSE
067600             MOVE MAX-POSN TO TO-POSN
067700     .
067800     IF TO-POSN < FROM-POSN
067900         MOVE    1     TO FROM-POSN
068000         MOVE MAX-POSN TO TO-POSN
068100     .
068200
068300 CALL-PARAMETER-PACKAGE.
068400     CALL "PARMPK"
068500         USING PARAMETER-CONTROL
068600               PARAMETER-LIST
068700     .
068800
068900 SET-SWITCH-IF-FOUND.
069000     MOVE OVERRIDE-VALUE (OVERRIDE-NBR) TO SWITCH-CONTROL
069100     PERFORM CHECK-AND-INSTALL-SWITCH
069200       VARYING PARAMETER-NBR FROM 2 BY 1
069300         UNTIL PARAMETER-NBR > PARAMETER-COUNT
069400     .
069500
069600 CHECK-AND-INSTALL-SWITCH.
069700     IF PARAMETER-VALUE (PARAMETER-NBR) = SWITCH-OVERRIDE
069800         MOVE SWITCH-OVERRIDE TO SWITCH-VALUE (SWITCH-NBR)
069900         PERFORM USE-DEFAULT-FILE-IF-2ND-PARM
070000     .
070100
070200 USE-DEFAULT-FILE-IF-2ND-PARM.
070300     IF PARAMETER-NBR = 2
070400         MOVE "SORTED" TO SORTED-FILE
070500     .
070600
070700 ACCUMULATE-NBR-IN-PARAMS.
070800     MOVE ZERO TO ACCUMULATED-NBR
070900     MOVE "NO" TO NUMBER-INDICATOR
071000     PERFORM ACCUMULATE-PARAM-DIGITS
071100       VARYING PARAMETER-NBR FROM PARAMETER-NBR BY 1
071200         UNTIL PARAMETER-NBR > PARAMETER-COUNT
071300            OR NUMBER-WAS-FOUND
071400
071500     IF NUMBER-WAS-FOUND
071600         PERFORM CHECK-IF-VALID-POSN
071700     .
071800
071900 ACCUMULATE-PARAM-DIGITS.
072000     MOVE PARAMETER-VALUE (PARAMETER-NBR) TO SWITCH-DIGITS
072100     IF SWITCH-CHAR (1) IS NUMERIC
072200         PERFORM USE-DEFAULT-FILE-IF-2ND-PARM
072300         PERFORM GET-THE-NUMBER
072400     .
072500
072600 GET-THE-NUMBER.
072700     MOVE "YES" TO NUMBER-INDICATOR
072800     PERFORM ADD-IN-DIGIT
072900       VARYING CHAR-NBR FROM 1 BY 1
073000         UNTIL CHAR-NBR > 8
073100     .
073200
073300 ADD-IN-DIGIT.
073400     IF SWITCH-CHAR (CHAR-NBR) IS NUMERIC
073500         COMPUTE ACCUMULATED-NBR = ACCUMULATED-NBR * 10
073600                                 + SWITCH-DIGIT (CHAR-NBR)
073700     .
073800
073900 CHECK-IF-VALID-POSN.
074000     IF ACCUMULATED-NBR <  1
074100         MOVE    1     TO ACCUMULATED-NBR
074200     ELSE
074300     IF ACCUMULATED-NBR > MAX-POSN
074400         MOVE MAX-POSN TO ACCUMULATED-NBR
074500     .
074600
074700 ACCUMULATE-NBR-IN-SWITCH.
074800     IF SWITCH-CHAR (1) IS NUMERIC
074900         PERFORM GET-THE-NUMBER
075000     ELSE
075100         MOVE "NO" TO NUMBER-INDICATOR
075200     .
075300     IF NUMBER-WAS-FOUND
075400         PERFORM CHECK-IF-VALID-POSN
075500     .
075600
075700 OPEN-FILES.
075800     COMPUTE LAST-MERGE-FILE = NBR-OF-FILES - 1
075900     PERFORM INIT-WORKFILE-FOR-OUTPUT
076000       VARYING FILE-NBR FROM 1 BY 1
076100         UNTIL FILE-NBR > LAST-MERGE-FILE
076200
076300     MOVE NBR-OF-FILES  TO INPUT-FILE,
076400                           OUTPUT-FILE, FILE-NBR
076500
076600     MOVE   "INPUT"     TO SORTIO-OPERATION
076700     MOVE UNSORTED-FILE TO THE-FILENAME (FILE-NBR)
076800     MOVE  "SORTFILE"   TO THE-USAGE    (FILE-NBR)
076900
077000     PERFORM CALL-IO-ON-FILE-NBR
077100
077200     IF BACKWARDS-SORT-WANTED
077300         MOVE MAX-RECORD-NBR TO RECORD-NBR
077400         MOVE       -1       TO RECORD-INCREMENT
077500     ELSE
077600         MOVE      ZERO      TO RECORD-NBR
077700         MOVE       +1       TO RECORD-INCREMENT
077800     .
077900
078000 INIT-WORKFILE-FOR-OUTPUT.
078100     PERFORM PREPARE-WORKFILE
078200     PERFORM REWRITE-FILE
078300     .
078400
078500 PREPARE-WORKFILE.
078600     MOVE   FILE-NBR    TO WORKFILE-NBR
078700     MOVE WORKFILE-NAME TO THE-FILENAME (FILE-NBR)
078800     MOVE  "WORKFILE"   TO THE-USAGE    (FILE-NBR)
078900     .
079000
079100 REWRITE-FILE.
079200     MOVE THE-FILENAME (FILE-NBR) TO SORTIO-FILENAME
079300     MOVE "OUTPUT" TO SORTIO-OPERATION
079400     PERFORM CALL-IO-ON-FILE-NBR
079500
079600     MOVE HIGH-VALUES TO THE-SORTKEY (FILE-NBR)
079700     .
079800
079900 CALL-IO-ON-FILE-NBR.
080000     MOVE THE-FILENAME (FILE-NBR) TO SORTIO-FILENAME
080100     IF USING-SORTFILE (FILE-NBR)
080200         CALL "SORTIO"
080300             USING SORTIO-CONTROL
080400                   SORTIO-RECORD
080500         MOVE  RECORD-NBR     TO SORTIO-RECNBR
080600         ADD RECORD-INCREMENT TO RECORD-NBR
080700     ELSE
080800     IF FILE-NBR = 1
080900         CALL "SORTW1"
081000             USING SORTIO-CONTROL
081100                   SORTIO-RECORD
081200     ELSE
081300     IF FILE-NBR = 2
081400         CALL "SORTW2"
081500             USING SORTIO-CONTROL
081600                   SORTIO-RECORD
081700     ELSE
081800     IF FILE-NBR = 3
081900         CALL "SORTW3"
082000             USING SORTIO-CONTROL
082100                   SORTIO-RECORD
082200     ELSE
082300     IF FILE-NBR = 4
082400         CALL "SORTW4"
082500             USING SORTIO-CONTROL
082600                   SORTIO-RECORD
082700     ELSE
082800     IF FILE-NBR = 5
082900         CALL "SORTW5"
083000             USING SORTIO-CONTROL
083100                   SORTIO-RECORD
083200     ELSE
083300     IF FILE-NBR = 6
083400         CALL "SORTW6"
083500             USING SORTIO-CONTROL
083600                   SORTIO-RECORD
083700     .
083800     IF SORTIO-FEEDBACK = SPACE
083900         MOVE SPACE TO THE-STATUS (FILE-NBR)
084000     ELSE
084100         MOVE "END" TO THE-STATUS (FILE-NBR)
084200     .
084300
084400 DISTRIBUTION-PHASE.
084500     PERFORM INIT-FILE-RUNS
084600       VARYING FILE-NBR FROM 1 BY 1
084700         UNTIL FILE-NBR > NBR-OF-FILES
084800
084900     MOVE 1 TO MERGE-LEVEL,
085000               SELECTED-FILE
085100
085200     MOVE INPUT-FILE TO FILE-NBR
085300     PERFORM READ-NEXT-RECORD
085400     PERFORM DISTRIBUTE-RUNS
085500       UNTIL END-OF-THE-FILE (INPUT-FILE)
085600     .
085700
085800 INIT-FILE-RUNS.
085900     IF FILE-NBR = INPUT-FILE
086000         MOVE 0 TO NBR-OF-RUNS
086100     ELSE
086200         MOVE 1 TO NBR-OF-RUNS
086300     .
086400     MOVE NBR-OF-RUNS TO IDEAL-RUNS (FILE-NBR),
086500                         DUMMY-RUNS (FILE-NBR)
086600     .
086700
086800 READ-NEXT-RECORD.
086900     MOVE "GET" TO SORTIO-OPERATION
087000     PERFORM CALL-IO-ON-FILE-NBR
087100     IF SORTIO-FEEDBACK = SPACE
087200         MOVE SORTIO-RECORD TO THE-RECORD (FILE-NBR)
087300         PERFORM BUILD-THE-SORTKEY
087400     ELSE
087500         MOVE LOW-VALUES TO SORTIO-RECORD
087600     .
087700     MOVE SORTIO-RECORD TO THE-SORTKEY (FILE-NBR)
087800     .
087900
088000 BUILD-THE-SORTKEY.
088100     IF 1 < FROM-POSN OR TO-POSN < MAX-POSN
088200         MOVE SORTIO-TEXT TO STRNPK-TEXT
088300         PERFORM DROP-LEADING-POSITIONS
088400         PERFORM TRUNCATE-AND-TEST-FOR-WORD
088500         MOVE STRNPK-TEXT TO SORTIO-TEXT
088600     ELSE
088700     IF SORT-ON-WORD-WANTED
088800         MOVE SORTIO-TEXT TO STRNPK-TEXT
088900         PERFORM GET-FIRST-WORD-OF-TEXT
089000         MOVE STRNPK-TEXT TO SORTIO-TEXT
089100     .
089200     IF RANKED-CHARS-WANTED
089300         MOVE SORTIO-TEXT TO CONVPK-TEXT
089400         PERFORM RANK-TEXT-IN-ORDER
089500         MOVE CONVPK-TEXT TO SORTIO-TEXT
089600     ELSE
089700     IF DESCENDING-SORT-WANTED
089800         MOVE SORTIO-TEXT TO CONVPK-TEXT
089900         PERFORM INVERT-THE-TEXT
090000         MOVE CONVPK-TEXT TO SORTIO-TEXT
090100     .
090200
090300 RANK-TEXT-IN-ORDER.
090400     MOVE "RANKED" TO CONVPK-OPERATION
090500     PERFORM CALL-CONVPK
090600     IF DESCENDING-SORT-WANTED
090700         PERFORM INVERT-THE-TEXT
090800     .
090900
091000 INVERT-THE-TEXT.
091100     MOVE "INVERT" TO CONVPK-OPERATION
091200     PERFORM CALL-CONVPK
091300     .
091400
091500 CALL-CONVPK.
091600     CALL "CONVPK"
091700         USING CONVPK-CONTROL
091800               CONVPK-TEXT
091900               CONVPK-VALUES
092000     .
092100
092200 DROP-LEADING-POSITIONS.
092300     COMPUTE STRNPK-SIZE = FROM-POSN - 1
092400     IF STRNPK-SIZE > 1
092500         MOVE "DROP" TO STRNPK-OPERATION
092600         PERFORM CALL-STRING-PACKAGE
092700     .
092800
092900 TRUNCATE-AND-TEST-FOR-WORD.
093000     COMPUTE STRNPK-SIZE = TO-POSN - FROM-POSN + 1
093100     MOVE "TRUNCATE" TO STRNPK-OPERATION
093200     PERFORM CALL-STRING-PACKAGE
093300
093400     IF SORT-ON-WORD-WANTED
093500         PERFORM GET-FIRST-WORD-OF-TEXT
093600     .
093700
093800 GET-FIRST-WORD-OF-TEXT.
093900     MOVE SPACE  TO STRNPK-FILLCHAR
094000     MOVE "WORD" TO STRNPK-OPERATION
094100     PERFORM CALL-STRING-PACKAGE
094200     .
094300
094400 CALL-STRING-PACKAGE.
094500     CALL "STRNPK"
094600         USING STRNPK-CONTROL
094700     .
094800
094900 DISTRIBUTE-RUNS.
095000     PERFORM SELECT-FILE
095100     IF THE-SORTKEY (SELECTED-FILE) NOT >
095200        THE-SORTKEY  (INPUT-FILE)
095300         PERFORM COPY-RUN-TO-SELECTED-FILE
095400     .
095500     IF FILE-NOT-EXHAUSTED (INPUT-FILE)
095600         SUBTRACT 1 FROM DUMMY-RUNS (SELECTED-FILE)
095700         PERFORM COPY-RUN-TO-SELECTED-FILE
095800     .
095900
096000 SELECT-FILE.
096100     COMPUTE NEXT-FILE = SELECTED-FILE + 1
096200     IF DUMMY-RUNS (SELECTED-FILE) < DUMMY-RUNS (NEXT-FILE)
096300         MOVE NEXT-FILE TO SELECTED-FILE
096400     ELSE
096500         PERFORM NEXT-LEVEL-IF-NO-DUMMY-RUNS
096600         MOVE 1 TO SELECTED-FILE
096700     .
096800
096900 NEXT-LEVEL-IF-NO-DUMMY-RUNS.
097000     IF DUMMY-RUNS (SELECTED-FILE) = ZERO
097100         ADD 1 TO MERGE-LEVEL
097200         MOVE IDEAL-RUNS (1) TO NBR-OF-RUNS
097300         PERFORM ASSIGN-NEW-RUNS
097400           VARYING THIS-FILE FROM 1 BY 1
097500             UNTIL THIS-FILE > LAST-MERGE-FILE
097600     .
097700
097800 ASSIGN-NEW-RUNS.
097900     COMPUTE NEXT-FILE = THIS-FILE + 1
098000     COMPUTE DUMMY-RUNS (THIS-FILE) = NBR-OF-RUNS +
098100             IDEAL-RUNS (NEXT-FILE) - IDEAL-RUNS (THIS-FILE)
098200
098300     ADD DUMMY-RUNS (THIS-FILE) TO IDEAL-RUNS (THIS-FILE)
098400     .
098500
098600 COPY-RUN-TO-SELECTED-FILE.
098700     MOVE INPUT-FILE TO FILE-NBR
098800
098900     MOVE ZERO TO LAST-LINE-PTR
099000     PERFORM STORE-LINE-AND-GET-NEXT
099100       UNTIL SORTIO-FEEDBACK > SPACE
099200          OR LAST-LINE-PTR = MAX-LINE-PTR
099300
099400     PERFORM SORT-LINES-IN-MEMORY
099500     PERFORM WRITE-LINES-TO-SELECTED-FILE
099600
099700     IF THE-SORTKEY (INPUT-FILE) NOT < THE-SORTKEY (SELECTED-FILE)
099800         MOVE "CONTINUE" TO RUN-INDICATOR
099900         MOVE SELECTED-FILE TO TO-FILE
100000         MOVE  INPUT-FILE   TO FROM-FILE
100100         PERFORM COPY-SORT-RECORD
100200           UNTIL END-OF-RUN
100300     .
100400
100500 STORE-LINE-AND-GET-NEXT.
100600     ADD 1 TO LAST-LINE-PTR
100700     MOVE LAST-LINE-PTR TO LINE-NUMBER  (LAST-LINE-PTR)
100800
100900     MOVE THE-RECORD  (FILE-NBR) TO LINE-STORED  (LAST-LINE-PTR)
101000     MOVE THE-SORTKEY (FILE-NBR) TO LINE-SORTKEY (LAST-LINE-PTR)
101100
101200     PERFORM READ-NEXT-RECORD
101300     .
101400
101500 SORT-LINES-IN-MEMORY.
101600     MOVE 1 TO STACK-PTR,  LOWER-LIMIT (STACK-PTR)
101700     MOVE LAST-LINE-PTR TO UPPER-LIMIT (STACK-PTR)
101800
101900     PERFORM QUICKSORT-THE-LINES
102000       UNTIL STACK-PTR = ZERO
102100     .
102200
102300 QUICKSORT-THE-LINES.
102400     MOVE LOWER-LIMIT (STACK-PTR) TO CUR-LOWER-LIMIT
102500     MOVE UPPER-LIMIT (STACK-PTR) TO CUR-UPPER-LIMIT
102600     IF CUR-LOWER-LIMIT < CUR-UPPER-LIMIT
102700         MOVE CUR-LOWER-LIMIT TO LOWER-PTR
102800         MOVE CUR-UPPER-LIMIT TO UPPER-PTR
102900
103000         COMPUTE PARTITION-SIZE = UPPER-PTR - LOWER-PTR + 1
103100         IF PARTITION-SIZE > 3
103200             MOVE CUR-LOWER-LIMIT TO LOWER-PTR
103300             MOVE CUR-UPPER-LIMIT TO UPPER-PTR
103400
103500             PERFORM FIND-A-PIVOT-LINE
103600             PERFORM PARTITION-LINES
103700               UNTIL UPPER-PTR < LOWER-PTR
103800
103900             PERFORM QUICKSORT-THE-PARTITIONS
104000             ADD 1 TO STACK-PTR
104100         ELSE
104200             PERFORM VERY-SIMPLE-SORT
104300             SUBTRACT 1 FROM STACK-PTR
104400     ELSE
104500         SUBTRACT 1 FROM STACK-PTR
104600     .
104700
104800 FIND-A-PIVOT-LINE.
104900     COMPUTE MIDDLE-PTR = (LOWER-PTR + UPPER-PTR) / 2
105000     MOVE LINE-NUMBER (MIDDLE-PTR) TO PIVOT-NBR
105100     .
105200
105300 PARTITION-LINES.
105400     MOVE LESS-THAN TO COMPARE-RESULT
105500     PERFORM INCREASE-LOWER
105600       UNTIL COMPARE-GREATER-THAN
105700
105800     MOVE GREATER-THAN TO COMPARE-RESULT
105900     PERFORM DECREASE-UPPER
106000       UNTIL COMPARE-LESS-THAN
106100
106200     IF LOWER-PTR NOT > UPPER-PTR
106300         PERFORM EXCHANGE-UPPER-AND-LOWER
106400         ADD      1  TO  LOWER-PTR
106500         SUBTRACT 1 FROM UPPER-PTR
106600     .
106700
106800 INCREASE-LOWER.
106900     MOVE LINE-NUMBER (LOWER-PTR) TO COMPARE-NBR
107000     IF LINE-SORTKEY (COMPARE-NBR) < LINE-SORTKEY (PIVOT-NBR)
107100         ADD 1 TO LOWER-PTR
107200     ELSE
107300         MOVE GREATER-THAN TO COMPARE-RESULT
107400     .
107500
107600 DECREASE-UPPER.
107700     MOVE LINE-NUMBER (UPPER-PTR) TO COMPARE-NBR
107800     IF LINE-SORTKEY (COMPARE-NBR) > LINE-SORTKEY (PIVOT-NBR)
107900         SUBTRACT 1 FROM UPPER-PTR
108000     ELSE
108100         MOVE LESS-THAN    TO COMPARE-RESULT
108200     .
108300
108400 COMPARE-LINES.
108500     IF LINE-SORTKEY (COMPARE-NBR) > LINE-SORTKEY (PIVOT-NBR)
108600         MOVE GREATER-THAN TO COMPARE-RESULT
108700     ELSE
108800         MOVE LESS-THAN    TO COMPARE-RESULT
108900     .
109000
109100 EXCHANGE-UPPER-AND-LOWER.
109200     MOVE LINE-NUMBER (LOWER-PTR) TO LINE-NBR
109300     MOVE LINE-NUMBER (UPPER-PTR) TO LINE-NUMBER (LOWER-PTR)
109400     MOVE LINE-NBR                TO LINE-NUMBER (UPPER-PTR)
109500     .
109600
109700 QUICKSORT-THE-PARTITIONS.
109800     COMPUTE STACK-NXT = STACK-PTR + 1
109900     IF UPPER-PTR - CUR-LOWER-LIMIT < CUR-UPPER-LIMIT - LOWER-PTR
110000         MOVE CUR-LOWER-LIMIT TO LOWER-LIMIT (STACK-NXT)
110100         MOVE     UPPER-PTR   TO UPPER-LIMIT (STACK-NXT)
110200
110300         MOVE     LOWER-PTR   TO LOWER-LIMIT (STACK-PTR)
110400         MOVE CUR-UPPER-LIMIT TO UPPER-LIMIT (STACK-PTR)
110500     ELSE
110600         MOVE     LOWER-PTR   TO LOWER-LIMIT (STACK-NXT)
110700         MOVE CUR-UPPER-LIMIT TO UPPER-LIMIT (STACK-NXT)
110800
110900         MOVE CUR-LOWER-LIMIT TO LOWER-LIMIT (STACK-PTR)
111000         MOVE     UPPER-PTR   TO UPPER-LIMIT (STACK-PTR)
111100     .
111200
111300 VERY-SIMPLE-SORT.
111400     IF PARTITION-SIZE = 2
111500*        DECREASES SORT BY 12%
111600         PERFORM SORT-THE-TWO-ITEMS
111700     ELSE
111800     IF PARTITION-SIZE = 3
111900*        DECREASES SORT BY 6%
112000         PERFORM SORT-THE-THREE-ITEMS
112100     .
112200
112300 SORT-THE-TWO-ITEMS.
112400     MOVE LINE-NUMBER (LOWER-PTR) TO COMPARE-NBR
112500     MOVE LINE-NUMBER (UPPER-PTR) TO PIVOT-NBR
112600     IF LINE-SORTKEY (COMPARE-NBR) > LINE-SORTKEY (PIVOT-NBR)
112700         PERFORM EXCHANGE-UPPER-AND-LOWER
112800     .
112900
113000 SORT-THE-THREE-ITEMS.
113100     COMPUTE MIDDLE-PTR = LOWER-PTR + 1
113200     MOVE LINE-NUMBER (LOWER-PTR)  TO COMPARE-NBR
113300     MOVE LINE-NUMBER (MIDDLE-PTR) TO PIVOT-NBR
113400     PERFORM COMPARE-LINES
113500
113600     MOVE LINE-NUMBER (UPPER-PTR) TO COMPARE-NBR
113700     IF COMPARE-GREATER-THAN
113800         PERFORM COMPARE-LINES
113900         IF COMPARE-GREATER-THAN
114000             MOVE LINE-NUMBER (LOWER-PTR) TO PIVOT-NBR
114100             PERFORM COMPARE-LINES
114200             PERFORM EXCHANGE-LOWER-AND-MIDDLE
114300             IF COMPARE-LESS-THAN
114400                 PERFORM EXCHANGE-UPPER-AND-MIDDLE
114500             ELSE
114600                 NEXT SENTENCE
114700         ELSE
114800             PERFORM EXCHANGE-UPPER-AND-LOWER
114900     ELSE
115000         PERFORM COMPARE-LINES
115100         IF COMPARE-LESS-THAN
115200             MOVE LINE-NUMBER (LOWER-PTR) TO PIVOT-NBR
115300             PERFORM COMPARE-LINES
115400             PERFORM EXCHANGE-UPPER-AND-MIDDLE
115500             IF COMPARE-LESS-THAN
115600                 PERFORM EXCHANGE-LOWER-AND-MIDDLE
115700     .
115800
115900 EXCHANGE-UPPER-AND-MIDDLE.
116000     MOVE LINE-NUMBER (MIDDLE-PTR) TO LINE-NBR
116100     MOVE LINE-NUMBER (UPPER-PTR)  TO LINE-NUMBER (MIDDLE-PTR)
116200     MOVE LINE-NBR                 TO LINE-NUMBER (UPPER-PTR)
116300     .
116400
116500 EXCHANGE-LOWER-AND-MIDDLE.
116600     MOVE LINE-NUMBER (MIDDLE-PTR) TO LINE-NBR
116700     MOVE LINE-NUMBER (LOWER-PTR)  TO LINE-NUMBER (MIDDLE-PTR)
116800     MOVE LINE-NBR                 TO LINE-NUMBER (LOWER-PTR)
116900     .
117000
117100 WRITE-LINES-TO-SELECTED-FILE.
117200     MOVE SELECTED-FILE TO FILE-NBR
117300     PERFORM GET-AND-WRITE-LINE
117400       VARYING LINE-PTR FROM 1 BY 1
117500         UNTIL LINE-PTR > LAST-LINE-PTR
117600
117700     MOVE LINE-SORTKEY (LINE-NBR) TO THE-SORTKEY (FILE-NBR)
117800     .
117900
118000 GET-AND-WRITE-LINE.
118100     MOVE LINE-NUMBER (LINE-PTR) TO LINE-NBR
118200     MOVE LINE-STORED (LINE-NBR) TO SORTIO-RECORD
118300
118400     MOVE "PUT" TO SORTIO-OPERATION
118500     PERFORM CALL-IO-ON-FILE-NBR
118600     .
118700
118800 COPY-SORT-RECORD.
118900     MOVE TO-FILE TO FILE-NBR
119000     MOVE THE-RECORD  (FROM-FILE) TO SORTIO-RECORD
119100     MOVE THE-SORTKEY (FROM-FILE) TO THE-SORTKEY (TO-FILE)
119200
119300     MOVE "PUT" TO SORTIO-OPERATION
119400     PERFORM CALL-IO-ON-FILE-NBR
119500
119600     MOVE FROM-FILE TO FILE-NBR
119700     PERFORM READ-NEXT-RECORD
119800     IF THE-SORTKEY (FROM-FILE) < THE-SORTKEY (TO-FILE)
119900         MOVE "END" TO RUN-INDICATOR
120000     ELSE
120100         MOVE SPACE TO RUN-INDICATOR
120200     .
120300
120400 MERGE-PHASE.
120500     PERFORM PREPARE-OUTPUT-FILE
120600     PERFORM INIT-WORKFILE-FOR-INPUT
120700       VARYING FILE-NBR FROM 1 BY 1
120800         UNTIL FILE-NBR > LAST-MERGE-FILE
120900
121000     PERFORM MERGE-WORKFILES
121100       VARYING MERGE-LEVEL FROM MERGE-LEVEL BY -1
121200         UNTIL MERGE-LEVEL = 0
121300     .
121400
121500 PREPARE-OUTPUT-FILE.
121600     MOVE OUTPUT-FILE TO FILE-NBR
121700     MOVE OUTPUT-FILE TO WHICH-FILE (FILE-NBR)
121800     PERFORM PREPARE-WORKFILE
121900     PERFORM REWRITE-FILE
122000     .
122100
122200 INIT-WORKFILE-FOR-INPUT.
122300     MOVE FILE-NBR TO WHICH-FILE (FILE-NBR)
122400     PERFORM REREAD-FILE
122500     .
122600
122700 REREAD-FILE.
122800     MOVE THE-FILENAME (FILE-NBR) TO SORTIO-FILENAME
122900
123000     MOVE "CLOSE" TO SORTIO-OPERATION
123100     PERFORM CALL-IO-ON-FILE-NBR
123200
123300     MOVE "INPUT" TO SORTIO-OPERATION
123400     PERFORM CALL-IO-ON-FILE-NBR
123500
123600     PERFORM READ-NEXT-RECORD
123700     .
123800
123900 MERGE-WORKFILES.
124000     MOVE ZERO TO DUMMY-RUNS (OUTPUT-FILE)
124100     PERFORM MAP-OUTPUT-FILE
124200     PERFORM REWRITE-FILE
124300
124400     PERFORM MERGE-THE-RUNS
124500       VARYING NBR-OF-RUNS FROM IDEAL-RUNS (LAST-MERGE-FILE) BY -1
124600         UNTIL NBR-OF-RUNS = 0
124700
124800     IF MERGE-LEVEL > 1
124900         PERFORM MAP-OUTPUT-FILE
125000         PERFORM REREAD-FILE
125100
125200         MOVE IDEAL-RUNS (LAST-MERGE-FILE) TO NBR-OF-RUNS
125300         PERFORM ROTATE-FILES
125400           VARYING THIS-FILE FROM TOP-FILE-NBR BY -1
125500             UNTIL THIS-FILE = 0
125600
125700         MOVE NBR-OF-RUNS TO IDEAL-RUNS (1)
125800     .
125900
126000 MAP-OUTPUT-FILE.
126100     MOVE WHICH-FILE (OUTPUT-FILE) TO FILE-NBR
126200     IF MERGE-LEVEL = 1
126300         MOVE SORTED-FILE TO THE-FILENAME (FILE-NBR)
126400         MOVE "SORTFILE"  TO THE-USAGE    (FILE-NBR)
126500     .
126600
126700 MERGE-THE-RUNS.
126800     MOVE ZERO TO NBR-OF-ACTIVE-FILES
126900     PERFORM SELECT-ACTIVE-FILE
127000       VARYING THIS-FILE FROM 1 BY 1
127100         UNTIL THIS-FILE > LAST-MERGE-FILE
127200
127300     IF NBR-OF-ACTIVE-FILES = ZERO
127400         ADD 1 TO DUMMY-RUNS (OUTPUT-FILE)
127500     ELSE
127600         PERFORM MERGE-A-RUN
127700           UNTIL NBR-OF-ACTIVE-FILES = ZERO
127800     .
127900
128000 SELECT-ACTIVE-FILE.
128100     IF DUMMY-RUNS (THIS-FILE) > ZERO
128200         SUBTRACT 1 FROM DUMMY-RUNS (THIS-FILE)
128300     ELSE
128400         ADD 1 TO NBR-OF-ACTIVE-FILES
128500         MOVE WHICH-FILE  (THIS-FILE)
128600           TO ACTIVE-FILE (NBR-OF-ACTIVE-FILES)
128700     .
128800
128900 ROTATE-FILES.
129000     IF THIS-FILE > 1
129100         COMPUTE PREV-FILE = THIS-FILE - 1
129200     ELSE
129300         COMPUTE PREV-FILE = TOP-FILE-NBR
129400     .
129500     MOVE WHICH-FILE (PREV-FILE) TO WHICH-FILE (THIS-FILE)
129600     MOVE DUMMY-RUNS (PREV-FILE) TO DUMMY-RUNS (THIS-FILE)
129700
129800     COMPUTE IDEAL-RUNS (THIS-FILE) = IDEAL-RUNS (PREV-FILE)
129900                                    - NBR-OF-RUNS
130000     .
130100
130200 MERGE-A-RUN.
130300     MOVE 1 TO SMALLEST-ONE
130400     PERFORM FIND-SMALLEST
130500       VARYING THIS-FILE FROM 2 BY 1
130600         UNTIL THIS-FILE > NBR-OF-ACTIVE-FILES
130700
130800     MOVE WHICH-FILE  (OUTPUT-FILE)  TO TO-FILE
130900     MOVE ACTIVE-FILE (SMALLEST-ONE) TO FROM-FILE
131000     PERFORM COPY-SORT-RECORD
131100
131200     IF END-OF-RUN
131300         MOVE ACTIVE-FILE (NBR-OF-ACTIVE-FILES)
131400           TO ACTIVE-FILE (SMALLEST-ONE)
131500         SUBTRACT 1 FROM NBR-OF-ACTIVE-FILES
131600     .
131700
131800 FIND-SMALLEST.
131900     MOVE ACTIVE-FILE (THIS-FILE)    TO FILE-NBR
132000     MOVE ACTIVE-FILE (SMALLEST-ONE) TO SMALLEST-SO-FAR
132100     IF THE-SORTKEY (FILE-NBR) < THE-SORTKEY (SMALLEST-SO-FAR)
132200         MOVE THIS-FILE TO SMALLEST-ONE
132300     .
132400
132500 CLOSE-FILES.
132600     PERFORM CLOSE-AND-ERASE-WORKFILE
132700       VARYING FILE-NBR FROM 1 BY 1
132800         UNTIL FILE-NBR > NBR-OF-FILES
132900
133000     MOVE OUTPUT-FILE TO FILE-NBR
133100     MOVE "CLOSE" TO SORTIO-OPERATION
133200     PERFORM CALL-IO-ON-FILE-NBR
133300     .
133400
133500 CLOSE-AND-ERASE-WORKFILE.
133600     PERFORM PREPARE-WORKFILE
133700
133800     MOVE "CLOSE" TO SORTIO-OPERATION
133900     PERFORM CALL-IO-ON-FILE-NBR
134000
134100     MOVE "ERASE" TO SORTIO-OPERATION
134200     PERFORM CALL-IO-ON-FILE-NBR
134300     .
134400
134500*****************    DIALOG PROGRAM NOT FOUND    *****************
134600
134700 DIALOG-PROGRAM-NOT-FOUND.
134800     EXIT
134900     .
135000
135100*******************    TERMINATE THE PROGRAM    ******************
135200
135300 TERMINATE-THE-PROGRAM.
135400     MOVE TERMINATE-EVENT TO THE-NEXT-EVENT
135500     .
135600
135700******************    BEFORE USING RESOURCES    ******************
135800
135900 BEFORE-USING-RESOURCES.
136000     EXIT
136100     .
136200
136300******************    AFTER USING RESOURCES    *******************
136400
136500 AFTER-USING-RESOURCES.
136600     EXIT
136700     .
136800
136900*******************    PREMATURE DIALOG END    *******************
137000
137100 PREMATURE-DIALOG-END.
137200     EXIT
137300     .
```

> >>

…[11 more quoted lines elided]…

Weel, if that was a 'do my homework' post then that certainly

they won't be able to get any clues from it at all    ;-)

#### ↳ Re: Merge Algorithms

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc123@bellsouth.net>
- **Date:** 1998-09-16T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<umOL1.1045$kN6.1425612@news2.mia.bellsouth.net>`
- **References:** `<6tnvfs$296$1@news.latrobe.edu.au>`

```
Jason Coates wrote:
>
>Other than using the Merge Facility within COBOL, has anyone ever devised
>there own Merge Algorithm. I would be very interested to know if anyone
>else other than myself is beating himself up.


An n-way merge is very simple to do.  Below is a template which can be used
to do an n-way merge on as many files as you like.

If you have a varying number of files to merge, then proceed like this:

   1. Merge two files into one
   2. Merge the output file above with another input file
   3. Repeat step 2 until all files merged

This process can be improved by merging up to n files in steps 1 and 2.

       01  LOW-FILE                PIC  9(02).
       01  LOW-KEY.
           03  F1K-END-FLAG        PIC  X(01)  VALUE SPACE.
           03  F1K-key-segment-1   PIC  ...
           03  F1K-key-segment-2   PIC  ...
           ...
       01  FILE-1-KEY.
           03  F1K-END-FLAG        PIC  X(01)  VALUE SPACE.
           03  F1K-key-segment-1   PIC  ...
           03  F1K-key-segment-2   PIC  ...
           ...
       01  FILE-2-KEY.
           03  F2K-END-FLAG        PIC  X(01)  VALUE SPACE.
           03  F2K-key-segment-1   PIC  ...
           03  F2K-key-segment-2   PIC  ...
           ...
       01  FILE-3-KEY.
           03  F3K-END-FLAG        PIC  X(01)  VALUE SPACE.
           03  F3K-key-segment-1   PIC  ...
           03  F3K-key-segment-2   PIC  ...
           ...


       TOP-LEVEL.
           PERFORM INITIALIZE.
           PERFORM PROCESS
               UNTIL (F1K-END-FLAG = "X")
                      AND
                     (F2K-END-FLAG = "X")
                      AND
                     (F3K-END-FLAG = "X")
                      AND
                     ...
           PERFORM TERMINATE.
           STOP RUN

       INITIALIZE.
           Open input files and output file.
           PERFORM READ-FILE-1.
           PERFORM READ-FILE-2.
           PERFORM READ-FILE-3.
           ...
           PERFORM


       PROCESS.
           IF (FILE-1-KEY < FILE-2-KEY)
               MOVE FILE-1-KEY TO LOW-KEY
               MOVE 1          TO LOW-FILE
           ELSE
               MOVE FILE-2-KEY TO LOW-KEY
               MOVE 2          TO LOW-FILE
           END-IF.

           IF (FILE-3-KEY < LOW-KEY)
               MOVE FILE-3-KEY TO LOW-KEY
               MOVE 3          TO LOW-FILE
           END-IF.

           IF (FILE-n-KEY < LOW-KEY)
               MOVE FILE-n-KEY TO LOW-KEY
               MOVE n          TO LOW-FILE
           END-IF.

           ...

           EVALUATE LOW-FILE
               WHEN 1
                   move FILE-1 data to output record
                   write output record
                   PERFORM READ-FILE-1
               WHEN 2
                   move FILE-2 data to output record
                   write output record
                   PERFORM READ-FILE-2
               WHEN 3
                   move FILE-3 data to output record
                   write output record
                   PERFORM READ-FILE-3
               WHEN n
                   move FILE-n data to output record
                   write output record
                   PERFORM READ-FILE-n
               ...
           END-EVALUATE.


       TERMINATE.
           Close files.


       READ-FILE-1.
           READ FILE-1
               AT END
                   INITIALIZE FILE-1-KEY
                   MOVE "X" TO F1K-END-FLAG
               NOT AT END
                   Move key segments from record
           END-READ.

       READ-FILE-2.
           READ FILE-2
               AT END
                   INITIALIZE FILE-2-KEY
                   MOVE "X" TO F2K-END-FLAG
               NOT AT END
                   Move key segments from record
           END-READ.

       READ-FILE-3.
           READ FILE-3
               AT END
                   INITIALIZE FILE-3-KEY
                   MOVE "X" TO F3K-END-FLAG
               NOT AT END
                   Move key segments from record
           END-READ.
      ...
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Merge Algorithms

- **From:** "COBOL Frog (Huib Klink)" <H.Klink@IMN.nl>
- **Date:** 1998-09-16T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3600219F.717E4E7C@IMN.nl>`
- **References:** `<6tnvfs$296$1@news.latrobe.edu.au> <umOL1.1045$kN6.1425612@news2.mia.bellsouth.net>`

```
Newbies, attention please.

Judson wrote a fine, educationally very appreciated solution to the much
re-occurring real world problem of "more-than-one-file-at-the-time".

Judson, your solution is nice, but I do have some ... questions (*and*
solutions):

I don't like your LOW-FILE indicator. It prevents 2 or more files containing
the same key value to be processed in one cycle. This would be no problem in a
bare merge, but suppose you are updating a master file with changes recorded in
two or more update-files. If one master record with key value X has updates in
both files, a better (better? extended may be better said!) solution i.s.o

>            EVALUATE LOW-FILE
>                WHEN 1
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
>                    move FILE-3 data to output record

would be:
*was: EVALUATE
      IF FILE-1-KEY = LOW-KEY
      THEN
          move&write/whatever process file-1
          PERFORM read-file-1
      END-IF
      IF FILE-2-KEY = LOW-KEY
      THEN
          move&write/whatever process file-1\2
          PERFORM read-file-2
      END-IF
      IF FILE-n-KEY = LOW-KEY
      THEN
          move&write/whatever process file-n
          PERFORM read-file-n
      END-IF
When some of these (update) files are allowed to have even duplicates this
could be further enhanced (suppose file 2 may have dups):
      IF FILE-1-KEY = LOW-KEY
      THEN
          move&write/whatever process file-1
          PERFORM read-file-1
      END-IF
      IF FILE-2-KEY = LOW-KEY
      THEN
          PERFORM whatever sub-init-code or just CONTINUE
          PERFORM WITH TEST BEFORE
                  UNTIL FILE-2-KEY NOT = LOW-KEY
              move&write/whatever process file-1\2
              PERFORM read-file-2
          END-PERFORM
          PERFORM whatever sub-end-code or just CONTINUE
      END-IF
      IF FILE-n-KEY = LOW-KEY
      THEN
          move&write/whatever process file-n
          PERFORM read-file-n
      END-IF
Etc. etc.
This is a provesn method I teach my students for years now. Here in Holland we
are used to take 0 / 1 i.s.o. space / X. (Of course what realy matters is that
the value for EOF is greater than the other one, 0 and 1 do show this slightly
clearer IMO).

Not really for efficiency but more to test my students' understanding of the
logic, I require them to code
PERFORM PROCESS UNTIL (LK-END-FLAG = "X")
i.s.o. your (undoubtedly more clear/readable) code:
PERFORM PROCESS UNTIL (F1K-END-FLAG = "X")
                       AND
                      (F2K-END-FLAG = "X")
                       AND
                      (F3K-END-FLAG = "X")
                       AND
                      ...
Besides, I let them always use 88-levels (condition names) for these tests.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Merge Algorithms

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc123@bellsouth.net>
- **Date:** 1998-09-17T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<wyZL1.1966$eM5.1767416@news4.mia.bellsouth.net>`
- **References:** `<6tnvfs$296$1@news.latrobe.edu.au> <umOL1.1045$kN6.1425612@news2.mia.bellsouth.net> <3600219F.717E4E7C@IMN.nl>`

```
I don't disagree with your method, but to me, a 'merge' and an 'update'
are two separate things.  A merge simply combines the contents of two
or more files in key sequence.  If two records have the same key value,
they are considered equivalent, for merge purposes.  An update, on the
other hand, would consider one file as the 'master', then apply one or
more files against it as 'transactions'.  In this case, sequence becomes
very important, because you need to know if two or more transactions in
different files have the same key value, in which order should they be
processed.  This is a very different situation from a simple 'merge',
and I agree that the logic would need to be different.  I would likely
approach this situation by merging all the transaction files into one
file (if necessary), using a sequence indicator (time?) to determine
processing order.  Then bump the one transaction file against the master
file, applying each transaction in proper sequence.

Yes, your method of testing each file for equality on each pass could
be more CPU efficient, as long as there were enough duplicate records
to more than compensate for the fact that you evaluate the compare for
every key every time, where my code only evaluates the compare until
an equal is found, averaging 50%.  If there are no, or few, duplicate
keys in the several files, your method would be less efficient. :-)

LK-END-FLAG will never be = "X", unless you force another pass through
the loop, when no more records are present.  You would need to code an
additional test to skip the write code when LK-END-FLAG = "X".

I generally prefer 0/1 myself over space/X, and started to use it. :-)

I tend to avoid 88 levels, because you cannot now use them in SET.  If
you could say "SET data-name TO 88-name, or "SET 88-name", then it would
be clear in the code when you also said "IF 88-level-name".  But when
you look at code which says "MOVE 123 TO data-name" then later "IF
88-name", there is no reason to necessarily connect them mentally, unless
you think to go look at what the 88 level value is, and what data-name it
is associated with.  However, if you see "MOVE 123 TO data-name" then
later "IF data-name = 123", that is instantly clear.  You can even search
for "123" and find both of them.  When I use a data-name to store codes,
I always put the possible code values and meanings as comments right
under the data-name definition.  I also like to comment their use in the
procedure division.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Merge Algorithms

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<6tr2n8$1pn$1@news.ses.cio.eds.com>`
- **References:** `<6tnvfs$296$1@news.latrobe.edu.au> <umOL1.1045$kN6.1425612@news2.mia.bellsouth.net> <3600219F.717E4E7C@IMN.nl> <wyZL1.1966$eM5.1767416@news4.mia.bellsouth.net>`

```
Does your comment below regarding 88 levels apply to this merge
functionality only or in general?  I find 88 levels very helpful.  And also,
our conventions/standards prohibit literals in the procedure division unless
the meaning is obvious/implicit.
```

Followup on my previous message

"The Art of Computer Programming, Volume 3; Sorting and Searching"
by Donald E. Knuth 
Addison-Wesley Publishing Company, Reading Massachusetts
ISBN 0-201-03803-X

My copy ( (c) 1973) has chapters entitled "Sorting by Merging",
"Minimum-Comparison Merging", "Multiway Merging and Replacement
Selection", "The Polyphase Merge", "The Cascade Merge", and "Practical
Considerations for Tape Merging"

Admittedly, my copy is out of date; there's a new edition on the
bookstands these days. P'haps the original poster would be interested
in reading a copy.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Merge Algorithms

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "COBOL Frog (Huib Klink)" <H.Klink@IMN.nl>
- **Date:** 1998-09-17T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36017719.8DA4A0BC@IMN.nl>`
- **References:** `<6tnvfs$296$1@news.latrobe.edu.au> <umOL1.1045$kN6.1425612@news2.mia.bellsouth.net> <3600219F.717E4E7C@IMN.nl> <wyZL1.1966$eM5.1767416@news4.mia.bellsouth.net>`

```
Comments inserted in between ...

Judson McClendon wrote:

> I don't disagree with your method, but to me, a 'merge' and an 'update'
> are two separate things.  A merge simply combines the contents of two
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> and I agree that the logic would need to be different.

I agree there is difference. What I also would say and hope we agree upon is that
this difference is of the kind general/special. I meant to say that the
more-than-one-file-at-the-time method, allowing duplicates is the general form.
MERGE is just a, let me call it stripped or simplified, sub-type of these general
class of processing structure.

>  I would likely
> approach this situation by merging all the transaction files into one
> file (if necessary), using a sequence indicator (time?) to determine
> processing order.  Then bump the one transaction file against the master
> file, applying each transaction in proper sequence.

That would triple the amount of I/O operations: each record tobe read, written to
the combined file and then read again in the second program.

> Yes, your method of testing each file for equality on each pass could
> be more CPU efficient, as long as there were enough duplicate records
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> keys in the several files, your method would be less efficient. :-)

Agreed. Side-effect of the genral-method-that-does-it-all. :))

> LK-END-FLAG will never be = "X", unless you force another pass through
> the loop, when no more records are present.  You would need to code an
> additional test to skip the write code when LK-END-FLAG = "X".

You're right. My fault. I automatically assumed you had a separate routine, say
"determine-lowest" containing the following code (which is a stripped version of
yours):

DETERMINE-LOWEST.
          IF (FILE-1-KEY < FILE-2-KEY)
               MOVE FILE-1-KEY TO LOW-KEY
           ELSE
               MOVE FILE-2-KEY TO LOW-KEY
           END-IF.

           IF (FILE-3-KEY < LOW-KEY)
               MOVE FILE-3-KEY TO LOW-KEY
           END-IF.

           IF (FILE-n-KEY < LOW-KEY)
               MOVE FILE-n-KEY TO LOW-KEY
           END-IF.
I further assumed that this routine was PERFORMed once in the first initialising
routine and then at the foot of each main processing cycle, just like the priming
read construct. (Note: when you reaaly first meerged the files and then processed
them as one, this would turn the determine to a real read-subroutine).
Anyway, under these assumptions, the last cycle would end in EOF for ALL files and
hence LK-END-FLAG would become 'X'.

I would have looked further than my nose length if it weren't that long!

> I generally prefer 0/1 myself over space/X, and started to use it. :-)
>
> I tend to avoid 88 levels, because you cannot now use them in SET.  If
> you could say "SET data-name TO 88-name, or "SET 88-name", then it would
> be clear in the code when you also said "IF 88-level-name".

Yep. COBOL '85: SET conditioname TO TRUE. Isn't that what you look for or do I just
not understand your point here?

>  But when
> you look at code which says "MOVE 123 TO data-name" then later "IF
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
> whoever believes in Him should not perish but have everlasting life."
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Merge Algorithms

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** redsky@ibm.net (Thane Hubbell)
- **Date:** 1998-09-17T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<Jl0PnHJ5PvPd-pn2-0lprGOG94Yub@Dwight_Miller.iix.com>`
- **References:** `<6tnvfs$296$1@news.latrobe.edu.au> <umOL1.1045$kN6.1425612@news2.mia.bellsouth.net> <3600219F.717E4E7C@IMN.nl> <wyZL1.1966$eM5.1767416@news4.mia.bellsouth.net>`

```
On Thu, 17 Sep 1998 01:25:48, "Judson McClendon" 
<judmc123@bellsouth.net> wrote:

> I tend to avoid 88 levels, because you cannot now use them in SET.  If
> you could say "SET data-name TO 88-name, or "SET 88-name", then it would
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> is associated with. 



I'm missing something.

01  Test-Data    Pic XXX Value Spaces.
       88  Test-123   Value "123".
       88  Test-456   Value "456".

You can use:

SET Test-123 TO TRUE

and it will move "123" to Test-Data.

You cannot say:  Set Test-456 to "456" but you can set them to True.

That's in the '85 standard.
```

On Wed, 16 Sep 1998 12:42:02, "Judson McClendon" 
<judmc123@bellsouth.net> wrote:

> An n-way merge is very simple to do.  Below is a template which can be used
> to do an n-way merge on as many files as you like.
> 

Not picking on you Judson - your post was just convenient .

Is there a general aversion to the Merge statement?  If so, why?  
Merge with an output procedure is very powerful.  However, I have only
used it once in the real world.  What is usually called a merge, I 
call a master file update.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Merge Algorithms

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc123@bellsouth.net>
- **Date:** 1998-09-16T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<thRL1.1079$kN6.1532199@news2.mia.bellsouth.net>`
- **References:** `<6tnvfs$296$1@news.latrobe.edu.au> <umOL1.1045$kN6.1425612@news2.mia.bellsouth.net> <Jl0PnHJ5PvPd-pn2-8KnR6bdted9J@Dwight_Miller.iix.com>`

```
Thane Hubbell wrote:
>
>Is there a general aversion to the Merge statement?  If so, why?
>Merge with an output procedure is very powerful.  However, I have only
>used it once in the real world.  What is usually called a merge, I
>call a master file update.

I've only used it a very few times myself.  The original poster seemed
merely curious about how it might be done.  The difference in my merge
code and what I would call a master file update, is that I would expect
a master file update to support transactions (add, modify, delete) to be
applied from one or more transaction files against the master file.
```

- **References:** `<6tnvfs$296$1@news.latrobe.edu.au> <umOL1.1045$kN6.1425612@news2.mia.bellsouth.net> <Jl0PnHJ5PvPd-pn2-8KnR6bdted9J@Dwight_Miller.iix.com>`

```
redsky@ibm.net (Thane Hubbell) wrote:

>On Wed, 16 Sep 1998 12:42:02, "Judson McClendon" 
><judmc123@bellsouth.net> wrote:
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
>

If it's just a straight merge (no record usage decisions) and the
layout of the two files are exactly the same, then I just concatenate
the two files in the JCL and just SORT.  Works for me.

However, if I'm updating one file from another, then I load the
"master file" into a VSAM file and then update it from the
"transaction file".  Easier in coding and maintaining.  I haven't done
a sequential file merge/update program in years. 

Boyce G. Williams, Jr.

 | detached concern with life itself, in the face of death itself."   |
 |                                                     Norman F. Dixon|
 '--------------------------------------------------------------------'
```

## Thread: Linkage section — 22 msgs

### Linkage section

- **From:** "Garry Dell" <Garry_dell@hotmail.com>
- **Date:** 1998-09-16T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<u6Q7T2e49GA.235@upnetnews05>`

```
Does anyone write code that utalizes the linkage section.  I am trying to
figure out how it would work, but the books I have seen don't really touch
on the subject.  any code snippets would be apriciated as then I could look
at real-life situations and not simulations.   That you for your support and
help.
```

#### ↳ Re: Linkage section

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1998-09-16T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<6tq2ff$req@dfw-ixnews8.ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<u6Q7T2e49GA.235@upnetnews05>`

```

Garry Dell wrote in message ...
>Does anyone write code that utalizes the linkage section.  I am trying to
>figure out how it would work, but the books I have seen don't really touch
>on the subject.  any code snippets would be apriciated as then I could look
>at real-life situations and not simulations.   That you for your support
and
>help.
>


Linkage section is used for two types of programs (mostly?)

1) (and this is the only STANDARD way) is to get data from a calling program
into called subprogram. Check your books under USING and passing data from
program to program and they should discuss this.

2) For getting addressability to a field for which you already have the
address and the lay-out - but do not currently have addressability to.  If
your compiler supports the
     SET ADDRESS OF ...
           or
     USAGE POINTER
          or
      SET xxxx to ADDRESS OF ...
statements, then it probably supports this EXTENSION. This may well not be
in your books - but should be in your "programming guide" with your compiler
IF it supports it.
```

- **From:** gvwmoore@ix.netcom.com (G Moore)

```
On Wed, 16 Sep 1998 20:47:29 -0700, "Garry Dell"
<Garry_dell@hotmail.com> wrote:

>Does anyone write code that utalizes the linkage section.  I am trying to
>figure out how it would work, but the books I have seen don't really touch
>on the subject.  any code snippets would be apriciated as then I could look
>at real-life situations and not simulations.   That you for your support and
>help.

*program1
working-storage  section.
05 modified-data		pic x	value 0.
05 unmodified-data	pic x	value 0.
procedure division.
call program2-filename	using	by reference modified-data
				by content     unmodified-data

display unmodified-data
*should still be 0
display modified-data
*1 is added

stop run
..


*program2
linkage section.
05 modified-data		pic x.
05 unmodified-data	pic x.
procedure division.
add 1 to modified-data
add 1 to unmodified-data
exit program
..



gvwmoore@ix.spam.netcom.com to reply remove the spam
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Linkage section

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1998-09-18T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<6tuncd$om0@sjx-ixn10.ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<6tsdq8$3fs@dfw-ixnews10.ix.netcom.com> <3298260.71785.9803@kcbbs.gen.nz>`

```


>
>A CALL must have its [by reference] parameters somewhere other
>than Linkage.  Maybe function parameters are the same.
>

Nope,
   There is no problem with any Standard COBOL compiler with passing
parameters from a main program (pgmA) to a sub-program (pgmB) which will get
the parameters in the Linkage Section.  PgmB can then quite happily call
another sub-sub-program (pgmC) passing parameters from its Linkage Section
to the sub-sub-program (which will also get it in its linkage section).

Also true for Functions!
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Linkage section

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** Karl Wagner <kwagner@sympatico.ca>
- **Date:** 1998-09-18T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3602E1A9.E90A15C8@sympatico.ca>`
- **References:** `<6tsdq8$3fs@dfw-ixnews10.ix.netcom.com> <3298260.71785.9803@kcbbs.gen.nz>`

```
Richard Plinston wrote:
> 
<<SNIP>>
> 
> A CALL must have its [by reference] parameters somewhere other
> than Linkage.  Maybe function parameters are the same.

That's not technically true. You can use a linkage item in a call by
reference as long as can address the memory area defined by the linkage
item. Two examples:

1) Your program is a subroutine of another program which passed a
parameter which will, in turn, be passed to a subroutine of your
program.

2) You're using dynamic memory allocation.
```
