###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Linkage section

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "Donald Tees" <donald@willmack.com>
- **Date:** 1998-09-18T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<6ttk99$otl$1@news.igs.net>`
- **References:** `<u6Q7T2e49GA.235@upnetnews05> <3601946A.1EB1D46@mindspring.com> <3601a584.0@news1.ibm.net> <3601B139.9E0AA73F@mindspring.com>`

```

skidmike wrote in message <3601B139.9E0AA73F@mindspring.com>...
>the fields are divided and defined the same, when i try to use the LS
>field in the called program, the compile error is "<field name> must be
>a field defined in the working storage section"
>
aha, the famous this function does not work with indirection dilemma. You
have to remember that linkage items may or may not be really "there". In
most cases, what actually exists in the subroutine is a pointer to the real
data which is still back in the calling program.  There are various methods
used by the compilers, and you can change methods (the call by statements),
but in fact any operation done on linkage items is different than stuff done
to regular working storage data.

For example, DISPLAY linkage-section-data-name will often not work
with a given compiler.  The solution is to move the data to a working
storage data name before using it.  Another common ones that causes
problems is filenames, and records from files.  ( call "abc" using
this-is-an-fd-01-record)
```

#### ↳ Re: Linkage section

- **From:** "Donald Tees" <donald@willmack.com>
- **Date:** 1998-09-17T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<6tqv0a$8kp$1@news.igs.net>`
- **References:** `<u6Q7T2e49GA.235@upnetnews05>`

```

Garry Dell wrote in message ...
>Does anyone write code that utalizes the linkage section.  I am trying to


Calling program:
    call "test" using abc, def.  <-- these in working storage.
Subroutine:
Linkage Section.
01 xxx    picture 999.
01 yyy    picture 999.
procedure divison using xxx yyy.
here, xxx is abc, yyy is def.  Pictures must match (if
you want it to work).
```

```
when using linkage to accept PARMs from JCL.
```

#### ↳ Re: Linkage section

- **From:** casten@usfca.edu (John Casten)
- **Date:** 1998-09-18T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<casten-1809981544200001@138.202.137.33>`
- **References:** `<u6Q7T2e49GA.235@upnetnews05>`

```
In article <u6Q7T2e49GA.235@upnetnews05>, "Garry Dell"
<Garry_dell@hotmail.com> wrote:

> Does anyone write code that utalizes the linkage section.  I am trying to
> figure out how it would work

easiest way to understand it (for me at least) is to think of it as shared
memory.  Calling program puts something into a field or fields (or not),
calls a program which (supposedly) looks at them and maybe does something,
passes control back to the calling program, which now has (possibly)
something new to play with.  Be careful with your fields; things can get
very interesting if they do not match.  Always practice safe computing.

calling program:
WORKING-STORAGE SECTION.
01  SWITCHES-N-SHIT.
...
01    TRN-CALL-AREA.
    03  TRN-WORK-FIELD         PIC X(64).
    03  TRN-LENGTH             PIC 9(09) COMP SYNC.
...
PROCEDURE DIVISION.                       
...
    MOVE MY-CITY               TO TRN-WORK-FIELD
    MOVE 20                    TO TRN-LENGTH
    CALL 'XXXTRN'           USING TRN-WORK-FIELD
                                  TRN-LENGTH
    MOVE TRN-WORK-FIELD (1:20) TO MY-CITY


called program:
*
**linkage section
*
LINKAGE SECTION.
01  CALLED-FIELD.
    03  CONVERT-BYTE         OCCURS 64
                            INDEXED CONV-INX
                                PIC X(01).

01  CALLED-LENGTH               PIC 9(09)  COMP SYNC.
/
*
** procedure division
*
PROCEDURE DIVISION USING CALLED-FIELD
                         CALLED-LENGTH.
MAIN-PROC
    .
*
** guard against overflow; if 0 we'll pretend we didn't notice
*
    IF CALLED-LENGTH > 64
       PERFORM YOURE_A_WANKER
       EXIT PROGRAM
    END-IF
*
** compress out multiple spaces, and left justify
*
    PERFORM COMPRESS-OUT-SPACES
etc.

Study the manual and experiment; there are alot of things you can do with
this feature.  You wouldn't want to write a game or figure out the
trajectory of a missile using COBOL - but you could if you have a good
run-time library.
```

<a id="t-3f1c96e211"></a>
## Thread: Is it possible to trap long running subprogram CALL ? — 4 msgs

### Is it possible to trap long running subprogram CALL ?

- **From:** "Steve Baldwin" <Steven.Baldwin@hancorp.com.au>
- **Date:** 1998-09-16T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<6tngcr$qin$1@news.mel.aone.net.au>`

```
Greetings,

We have a situation where we have a "listener" program that has been written
in COBOL (actually Microfocus on HP-UX).  When this program receives a
message, it dynamically calls a COBOL subprogram to process it.  Does anyone
know of a way we can trap that subprogram taking an extraordinarily long
time ?  In C it would be easy by setting a timer, and then trapping the
Alarm clock signal, and I thought about interfacing a C routine with the
COBOL.  However I'm not sure if this would solve my problem.

Your thoughts would be very much appreciated.

Steve Baldwin
```

#### ↳ Re: Is it possible to trap long running subprogram CALL ?

- **From:** redsky@ibm.net (Thane Hubbell)
- **Date:** 1998-09-16T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<Jl0PnHJ5PvPd-pn2-5QfhFCJQRjMw@Dwight_Miller.iix.com>`
- **References:** `<6tngcr$qin$1@news.mel.aone.net.au>`

```
On Wed, 16 Sep 1998 04:56:04, "Steve Baldwin" 
<Steven.Baldwin@hancorp.com.au> wrote:

> Greetings,
> 
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
> 

You can accept the current time either by using Accept from Time, or 
Function Current-Date, just before the call and then again immediately
after returning from the call.  Then figure the duration.
```

#### ↳ Re: Is it possible to trap long running subprogram CALL ?

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1998-09-16T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<6tpem8$23q@dfw-ixnews10.ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<6tngcr$qin$1@news.mel.aone.net.au>`

```

Steve Baldwin wrote in message <6tngcr$qin$1@news.mel.aone.net.au>...
>Greetings,
>
>We have a situation where we have a "listener" program that has been
written
>in COBOL (actually Microfocus on HP-UX).  When this program receives a
>message, it dynamically calls a COBOL subprogram to process it.  Does
anyone
>know of a way we can trap that subprogram taking an extraordinarily long
>time ?  In C it would be easy by setting a timer, and then trapping the
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>
Assuming that you want to catch this situation BEFORE you get back from the
CALLed subprogram *and* assuming that you only want your solution to work
with Micro Focus COBOL, then contact your MF rep and ask them about the
"CALLMAP" compiler directive.  This is a VERY LITTLE USED (and almost
undocumented) interface that lets you "capture" each CALL statement before
it gets to the CALLed subprogram.

Note: I am not positive this got ported from the 16-bit to the 32-bit
products - so you may want to ask MF about that too.
```

#### ↳ Re: Is it possible to trap long running subprogram CALL ?

- **From:** "grant mc" <grantmc@voyager.co.nz>
- **Date:** 1998-09-19T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<6tu8o3$6f9$1@reader1.reader.news.ozemail.net>`
- **References:** `<6tngcr$qin$1@news.mel.aone.net.au>`

```
An alternative option is to avoid the use of a call altogether. This would
avoid control being parsed to the called module, and thus there is no
dependency on how long the called module takes. Your main app can then carry
on and the subrountine catches up eventually. And yes you have to be very
careful in real time situations.
Essentially the parameters are parsed as records in a file. You declare the
file as type PORT. Physically it is a stack file locating in memory, thus
volatile. But it means that there is never an AT END condition and you're
not forced to test for it. The main program just does the equivalent of the
CALL by writing a record to the stack (with all parameters as fields). The
subroutnine periodically reads the file and if there is a record it performs
its process on the record, checks again if there's a record and if so
repeats until such time as there are no rercords. At which time it goes to
sleep using a WAIT of some sort you feel appropriate.
In order to get the subroutine to actually end, you have to parse a special
record to it through the port that it understands as time to STOP RUN.

Hope this might give you an idea or two,
Grant McNamara

Steve Baldwin wrote in message <6tngcr$qin$1@news.mel.aone.net.au>...
>Greetings,
>
>We have a situation where we have a "listener" program that has been
written
>in COBOL (actually Microfocus on HP-UX).  When this program receives a
>message, it dynamically calls a COBOL subprogram to process it.  Does
anyone
>know of a way we can trap that subprogram taking an extraordinarily long
>time ?  In C it would be easy by setting a timer, and then trapping the
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

## Thread: os/vs cobol to cobol II for mvs — 5 msgs

_Topics: [`Compilers and vendors`](topics.md#compilers) · [`Mainframe, z/OS, JCL, CICS`](topics.md#mainframe)_

### os/vs cobol to cobol II for mvs

- **From:** "Larry Vervynckt" <lawrence.vervynckt@eds.com>
- **Date:** 1998-09-16T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<01bde195$be772d20$b95efa90@kopc1157.delcoelect.com>`

```
I am interested in information regarding the conversion to cobol II. 
Hints, available guides, resources of any type.  We are in the beginnings
of a mass conversion and need some help from all of you gurus out there. 
Any help would be appreciated.
```

#### ↳ Re: os/vs cobol to cobol II for mvs

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1998-09-16T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<6tpem9$23q@dfw-ixnews10.ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<01bde195$be772d20$b95efa90@kopc1157.delcoelect.com>`

```
Hint number 1
   Do not even THINK about converting to "COBOL II".  The product VS COBOL
II is quite old and has been superceded.  Although IBM will support it thru
2000, it is not the way to solve Y2K problems.  The compiler that you want
to look at (assuming your MVS or OS/390 - and not VSE) is
    IBM COBOL for OS/390 & VM
    (you could also use COBOL for MVS & VM - but that too is semi-old. If
you hear about COBOL/370 - support for that has already been dropped!)

Once you have figured out the compiler to go to, the first book that you
should read is IBM's "Migration Guide".  There are separate versions for VS
COBOL II, COBOL for MVS & VM, and COBOL for OS/390 & VM.  Get the one for
the compiler that you are moving to.  (If you are going to one of the COBOL
for this-and-that compilers, you will also want to get a copy of the LE
(Language Environment) "Migration Guides".

There are courses that you can take on utilizing the new products and/or
migrating from the old ones.  There are also courses on analyzing your own
shop's situation and how difficult the conversion will be.  Some of these
have already been discussed in this newsgroup.  I suggest you look at
DejaNews for some of the old threads.  If you need more hints, please let us
know.

Larry Vervynckt wrote in message
<01bde195$be772d20$b95efa90@kopc1157.delcoelect.com>...
>I am interested in information regarding the conversion to cobol II.
>Hints, available guides, resources of any type.  We are in the beginnings
>of a mass conversion and need some help from all of you gurus out there.
>Any help would be appreciated.
>
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: os/vs cobol to cobol II for mvs

- **From:** rkrayhawk@aol.com (RKRayhawk)
- **Date:** 1998-09-17T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<19980916201328.29931.00000323@ng86.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<6tpem9$23q@dfw-ixnews10.ix.netcom.com>`

```

The recommended Migration Guide is  indispensable.

Your conversion will take a lot of resources if  you have many programs. In the
vendor's documentation the least emphasized aspect of the switch-over is the
ending of support for non-standard IBM extensions to COBOL.

Beware of ODOs (occurs depending on clauses) in your time estimates, there are
surprises there. The need to change programs from EXAMINE to INSPECT can
require much work too, especially in testing.

As a previous responder has pointed out, you eventually need to be hussling
your puppies into COBOL 390 (again assuming this is not VSE).  But you could
face the problem that many shops do, you may not be ready to go to OS/390 until
later.

I am not sure that COBOL 390 will fly in MVS/ESA etc.

Beware that the LE/390 world is slightly different that the LE/370 world. 
LE/390 is required with COBOL 390.  LE/370 is optional.  I have been in
environments where features of LE/370 are turned on and off in MVS contexts
just to see how much code is still not ready for OS 390.

Your main concern now is to get to COBOL-85 syntax.  COBOL II, and other
compilers  prior to the COBOL 390 can get you there. The surprising list of
recent names and name changes for the mainframe COBOL compiler is confusing. 
Most of the names have the name of an operation system in it. 

Do not be misled by the product names.  You are NOT primarily involved in an
operating system change. Instead, you are involved in a major source code
syntax change (from the COBOL 74 standard to the COBOL 85 standard), and the
dropping of certain vendor extensions to the language.

Your issue is the source code.  Much of it needs attention.

It is best to start discussion early on about rebuilding entire systems, (as an
option), rather than tweaking parts and relinking.
IBM, an extremely talented group of people, has established ways to combine old
code and new code (code compiled under prior compilers, and code compiled under
newer compilers). 

This defintiely can allow delay in source code conversion.   But the new code /
old code mix links become rather more abstract than application programmers
might be used to.
Also note that certain strategies offered (like MIXRES) in intermediate COBOL
85 products have disappered in the later COBOL (for OS/390).

IBM definitely should be admired for what they have accomplished with the COBOL
technologies.  But the mix approach runs a real risk of generating a Tower of
Babel effect. It involves concepts and terminology alien to the application
technicians and managers.

This is an opinion, but you may be in the  best  position later on if you regen
entire applications from the source code out.

For some organizations, the conversion to the new syntax can be truely major.
Obviously, a divide and concur approach becomes necessary.  It is, perhaps,
best to divide way up at the application level, and do not mix old code and new
code in a single run unit.

Any divide strategy that stages portions of code into production over time does
run into the dificulty of having programs on either side of an interface (file
or online I/O area) compiled with different language standards.

Here you must distinguish syntax (COBOL 74/ versus COBOL 85) from semantics,
which is the menaing of code.

The BINary data items are one focal point here, and an enumeration of such
items in interfaces may be a portion of the estimate process.  Generally, you
are looking at TRUNC(BIN) for the new world.  The places  where this could
cause problems between old code and new code are usually rare, (this is because
the old code is already operating with a TRUNC(STD) domain of data.

None the less, program conversion may bring about changes that increase length,
the new code may be able to see it, the old code may not.

The list goes on.  It is a major undertaking if you have lots of programs. 
Many of the language changes involve details which will necessitate substantial
testing. Resources of a large order may be required from user groups.

Also note that the migration guide also describes hardware resource
implications.
The new world is much fatter than the old. But the resource hog is the
transition period, where many application components exist in duplicate.

Less emphasized in the COB(OL migration guide is the hardware resources needed
to support major system testing during the transition.  Data review can be
lengthy, and the need for reruns can slow things also.  The amount of DASD and
printer time to support the users during system verification, will require
major commitments.













Robert Rayhawk
RKRayhawk@aol.com
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: os/vs cobol to cobol II for mvs

- **From:** scomstock@aol.com (S Comstock)

- **Message-ID:** `<19980919103614.11915.00001824@ng123.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<19980916201328.29931.00000323@ng86.aol.com>`

```

You haven't mentioned which compiler you are using, but if it is a Micro
Focus product you can program the action of the various function keys using
the ADISCF utility. This will allow you to set the enter key to move to the
next field as required.
```

#### ↳ Re: screen section ACCEPTing

- **From:** redsky@ibm.net (Thane Hubbell)
- **Date:** 1998-09-17T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<Jl0PnHJ5PvPd-pn2-tIH63KmldevS@Dwight_Miller.iix.com>`
- **References:** `<36003E40.EF052863@mindspring.com>`

```
On Wed, 16 Sep 1998 22:40:00, skidmike <skidmike@mindspring.com> 
wrote:

> going thru the code i have so far, i see where i really need to
> eliminate some go to's.  for instance, in my customer-info i-o screen i
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> individual field?  how do you guys do it?

Let's look at how you used cursor-position.  Because that works.

Heres one that works.  Every time you press enter it goes to the next 
field.  It's not pretty though.  (Like hard coded field positions).

000010 IDENTIFICATION DIVISION.                              
000020 PROGRAM-ID DEMO1.                                     
000030 ENVIRONMENT DIVISION.                                 
000040 CONFIGURATION SECTION.                                
000070 SPECIAL-NAMES.                                        
000071      CRT Status Is CRT-STATUS                         
000072      CURSOR IS CURSOR-POS.                            
000120 DATA DIVISION.                                        
000140 WORKING-STORAGE SECTION.                              
000150 01  Crt-Status      Value Spaces.                     
000160     03  Type-Accept Pic X.                            
000170     03  Key-Press   Pic X.                            
000171     03  Unused      Pic X.                            
000172 01  Cursor-Pos      Value "0109".                     
000174     03  Cursor-Row  Pic 99.                           
000175     03  Cursor-Column Pic 99.                         
000176 01  Field-1 Pic X(20) Value Spaces.                   
000177 01  Field-2 Pic X(20) Value Spaces.                   
000178 01  Field-3 Pic X(20) Value Spaces.                   
000179 01  Field-4 Pic X(20) Value Spaces.                   
000180 Screen Section.                                       
000181 01  The-Screen Blank Screen.                          
000182     03  Line 01 Column 01 Value "Field 1".            
000183     03  Line 01 Column 40 Value "Field 2".            
000184     03  Line 02 Column 01 Value "Field 3".            
000185     03  Line 02 Column 40 Value "Field 4".            
000186     03  Line 10 Column 20 Value "Press F3 to Exit".   
000187     03  Pic X(20) Line 01 Column 9  Using Field-1.    
000188     03  Pic X(20) Line 01 Column 49 Using Field-2.    
000189     03  Pic X(20) Line 02 Column 9  Using Field-3.    
000190     03  Pic X(20) Line 02 Column 49 Using Field-4.    
000191 PROCEDURE DIVISION.                                   
000200     Perform Until Key-Press = X"03"                   
000201        Display The-Screen                             
000202        Accept The-Screen                              
000203        Evaluate True                                  
000204           When Cursor-Pos < "0149"                    
000205                Move "0149" To Cursor-Pos              
000206           When Cursor-Pos < "0209"                    
000207                Move "0209" To Cursor-Pos              
000208           When Cursor-Pos < "0249"                    
000209                Move "0249" To Cursor-Pos              
000210           When Other                                  
000211                Move "0109" To Cursor-Pos              
000212        End-Evaluate                                   
000213     End-Perform                                       
000214     Stop Run                                          
000220     .
```

```
you present in this example.  CURSOR-ROW and
CURSOR-COLUMN contain zeros in my system after
an accept.   What am I missing?

I am successful in setting the cursor position to an exact
location before the display, however.  It's just that with my
particular application, it won't do me any good unless I know
where the cursor was after the ACCEPT.

Regards,
JC Jones.
```

<a id="t-cdab91be39"></a>
## Thread: "API" problem — 4 msgs

### "API" problem

- **From:** "Tony Tynan" <ttynan@iol.ie>
- **Date:** 1998-09-16T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<01bde1ba$79b65100$3a307dc2@default>`

```

Help would be great.......

Got "Microsoft COBOL For DOS and OS/2 Systems"  Version 4, on floppies. 
Installed it with SETUP from the FDs and took the defaults.

With sleeves rolled up ready to banish 30-yr old COBOL cobwebs.............

Problem:  On entering the COBOL verb (followed by a demo filename from the
set)   I get the message
'The Application Program Interface (API) entered will only work in
Microsoft Operating System/2  mode'

A .BAT proggie in the DEMO dir. will, however appear to run OK

I won't bore the readership (yet!) with the various investigations I've
done like like  RTFM'ing and checking that the right .DLLs  & not the DLE's
(or vice versa) or otherwise that are supposed to be there are there,   but
if anybody can say " Aha! you have to add this line to your CONFIG etc or
change that line...   I'd be most grateful and would save much further
head-banging off wall.

This problem is the same on my-

a)  Olde DX2-66 486 PC and  (DOS 6.2 and WFW 3.z)
b)  PII 233 m/c running Win 95 booted in MS-DOS ( 7.z  I expect)

Many thanks
```

#### ↳ Re: "API" problem

- **From:** "Paddy Coleman" <pc@microfocus.com>
- **Date:** 1998-09-17T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<6tr8ma$kth@hyperion.mfltd.co.uk>`
- **References:** `<01bde1ba$79b65100$3a307dc2@default>`

```
Tony,

The EXEs that you are trying to run are bound i.e. they run on both DOS and
OS/2.
They determine which by looking at the type of memory manager that is
present
(if memory serves).  In your case you have loaded the DOS EMM386 program
which fools the EXEs in to thinking it is OS/2.  As it is not you get the
error
message you described.

As far as I am aware the solution is to:

1)  Remove EMM386 from your environment whilst running COBOL.
2)  Upgrade to a later version of COBOL which was released after EMM386 and
can cope.

Hope this helps.

Paddy Coleman
Team Leader, Distributed Computing Support (WinTel)
Micro Focus International.




Tony Tynan wrote in message <01bde1ba$79b65100$3a307dc2@default>...
>
>Help would be great.......
…[25 more quoted lines elided]…
>Many thanks
```

- **From:** "Tony Tynan" <ttynan@iol.ie>
- **Date:** 1998-09-17T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<01bde268$c3b5f540$1a307dc2@default>`
- **References:** `<01bde1ba$79b65100$3a307dc2@default> <6tr8ma$kth@hyperion.mfltd.co.uk>`

```
Paddy

Very many thanks for the tip.   I'll look into it straight away and revert
asap.  

Rgds

PS I've had some trouble in the past arising from  EMM386 Vs.  other memory
managers loaded - but can't remember the details.

 
Paddy Coleman <pc@microfocus.com> wrote in article
<6tr8ma$kth@hyperion.mfltd.co.uk>...
> Tony,
> 
> The EXEs that you are trying to run are bound i.e. they run on both DOS
and
> OS/2.
etc
```

- **Date:** 1998-09-17T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol

- **References:** `<01bde1ba$79b65100$3a307dc2@default> <6tr8ma$kth@hyperion.mfltd.co.uk>`

```

EMM386 in my AUTO or CONFIG files, but it was snuck in there in the
\WINDOWS dir, so,  I temporarily renamed it and rebooted without any ill
effects. (Mustn't forget to restore it though)  Now I just plough ahead
'til I meet the (inevitable) next challenge!  Out with pessimism, say I.

Regards again
```

## Thread: COBOL and Perl — 2 msgs

### Re: COBOL and Perl

- **From:** philjach@usa.net (Phil Jach)
- **Date:** 1998-09-16T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.perl.misc,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<35ffd7ef.0@news1.ibm.net>`
- **References:** `<6r7fi0$9a7$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com> <6rldhj$na5@lotho.delphi.com> <tglnnz8roi.fsf@noise.bsd.uchicago.edu> <6t0dk0$ep5$4@news.enterprise.net>`

```
In article <6t0dk0$ep5$4@news.enterprise.net>
scm@enterprise.net (Shaun C. Murray) wrote:

[snip]

> 
> as I said, shorthand.
> 
What you call shorthand, IMHO, I call symbolism. 

Perl uses cryptic/shorthand characters/symbols to bridge the gap between
culturally diverse speaking/writing languages.  It makes it easier for a 
person that doesn't know a lick of English to read my Perl program.

Can the same be said about COBOL?

Larry Wall, the creator of Perl and the person who is mainly responsible 
for the future direction of Perl, discusses Perl as a global language in the 
following speech.  Please read it.

http://www.perl.com/pace/pub/perldocs/1998/08/show/onion.html

[snip]

----------------------
Remember being young and getting your first toy that required assembly, 
wanting it together so bad but not knowing how to do it, not knowing 
what instructions were or where to find them?  I'm glad my Dad didn't 
FLAME me for not knowing.  He showed me, with kindness.
```

#### ↳ Re: COBOL and Perl

- **From:** riplin@kcbbs.gen.nz (Richard Plinston)
- **Date:** 1998-09-17T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3298259.69355.1239@kcbbs.gen.nz>`
- **References:** `<35ffd7ef.0@news1.ibm.net>`

```
In message <<35ffd7ef.0@news1.ibm.net>> philjach@usa.net  writes:
> scm@enterprise.net (Shaun C. Murray) wrote:
> 
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
> person that doesn't know a lick of English to read my Perl program.

Unfortunately you take a very American view of the rest of
the world and make an assumption that all cultures use
the same symbols and conventions.  Thus you feel that this
symbolism is 'universal' in a way that English is not.

Even in such simple things as representation of numbers
there is no 'universal' representation:

        1.025,65      1,025.65

With Cobol, though the words are English, they can be easily
understood with any standard dictionary giving equivalent
words.  Now where can I fnd a 'standard text' that will
describe '~' as meaning 'logical NOT' in any language, and
this assumes that the character '~' is not only part of
the langage but is also on their keyboards and screens.
Many European languages, let alone Asian ones, substitute
their own character sets and this may replace symbols.

Using a restricted character set (such as using words
rather than hen scratchings) ensures that as wide as
possible numbers of computers and people use a common
representation.  Your assertion may be true of non-English
speakers in America, but, as usual, the rest of the 
world remains a mystery to you.
```

<a id="t-389652bba1"></a>
## Thread: OS390/VM Cobol vs VS Cobol? — 5 msgs

_Topics: [`Compilers and vendors`](topics.md#compilers) · [`Mainframe, z/OS, JCL, CICS`](topics.md#mainframe)_

### OS390/VM Cobol vs VS Cobol?

- **From:** paulr@bix.com (paulr)
- **Date:** 1998-09-16T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<6tpbkt$e2j@lotho.delphi.com>`

```
Is there a quick reference somewhere that compares 
these two dialects? Is there any particular reason,
other than it being old, to NOT port some fairly
generic code to VS Cobol? 

Looking for advice. :) 
Thanks
-Paul
```

#### ↳ Re: OS390/VM Cobol vs VS Cobol?

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1998-09-16T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<6tpfqb$2qh@dfw-ixnews10.ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<6tpbkt$e2j@lotho.delphi.com>`

```

paulr wrote in message <6tpbkt$e2j@lotho.delphi.com>...
>Is there a quick reference somewhere that compares
>these two dialects? Is there any particular reason,
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>


By "VS COBOL" I assume that you mean either the OS/VS COBOL compiler or the
DOS/VS COBOL compiler.  The former has been out of support for several years
and the other one is dying next year.

If by "porting" you mean to Fujitsu, Micro Focus, or some other PC compiler
that provides compatibility with the IBM (dead) compiler language, then you
could do this, but is seems pretty short-sited to me.

I should say, however, that if you have mainframe code that you are "porting
to the PC" for production work and the current version is written in OS/VS
COBOL or DOS/VS COBOL, then, YES it makes sense to do the porting first and
then do the language conversion 2nd.  In other words, do one change at a
time.  Get your mainframe programming working completely on the PC (server
or workstation) using as much of the existing language (even if it is OS/VS
COBOL) and THEN convert it to a more modern dialect.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: OS390/VM Cobol vs VS Cobol?

- **From:** paulr@bix.com (paulr)
- **Date:** 1998-09-17T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<6tpvgq$anr@lotho.delphi.com>`
- **References:** `<6tpbkt$e2j@lotho.delphi.com> <6tpfqb$2qh@dfw-ixnews10.ix.netcom.com>`

```
Sorry- I wasn't clear William. This is porting TO 
VM/ESA, from VisualAge and/or AcuCOBOL. VisualAge is
spot on compatible with:

   "COBOL for OS/390 & VM"

   "COBOL Set for AIX"

 & "VisualAge COBOL"

AcuCOBOL is pretty generic in most cases.

The compiler on the VM system is currently VS COBOL,
or perhaps VS II (?). I was wondering if there was
any reason to not go ahead and port up the code to 
VS COBOL now, or if I should wait until they upgrade
the compiler a bit. 

Yours,
-Paul

```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: OS390/VM Cobol vs VS Cobol?

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1998-09-16T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<6tq2uu$rpj@dfw-ixnews8.ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<6tpbkt$e2j@lotho.delphi.com> <6tpfqb$2qh@dfw-ixnews10.ix.netcom.com> <6tpvgq$anr@lotho.delphi.com>`

```
Porting from VisuagAge COBOL would be easy to COBOL for OS/390 & VM but
would be a real nightmare to OS/VS COBOL (or DOS/VS COBOL if you VM is
actually running VSE).  It would be somewhat difficult to VS COBOL II - but
how difficult would depend on how many new features you were using
(Intrinsic Functions? OO? procedure-pointers? etc).

As far as going from AcuCOBOL to OS/VS COBOL, my guess is that this too
would be difficult IF you are using any of the built-in AcuCOBOL extensions.
However, as I have never thought about much less been involved in that, I am
not certain.
```

```
Very heavy use of intrinsic functions, adn being a 1992 compiler
it won't have those will it? Thanks for pointing that out
and saving me some trouble. :) 

They are going to put a new up to date compiler on the machine,
probably in the next few weeks, so I think I will just hold
off on doing the serious porting. 

Thanks
-Paul
```

<a id="t-2524d69f4c"></a>
## Thread: Micro Focus Cobol to AS/400 — 2 msgs

_Topics: [`Compilers and vendors`](topics.md#compilers) · [`AS/400, iSeries, RPG`](topics.md#as400)_

### Micro Focus Cobol to AS/400

- **From:** H���kon Olsen <hakon.olsen@narvesen.no>
- **Date:** 1998-09-16T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<360020BE.6048@narvesen.no>`

```
We have an application written in Micro Focus Cobol and running on
PC/MS-DOS that, for a variety of reasons, we would like to move to our
AS/400 machine.

Any comments on the feasibility of such a project?

Regards
Haakon Olsen
Oslo
```

#### ↳ Re: Micro Focus Cobol to AS/400

- **From:** rtwolfe@flexus.com (Bob Wolfe)
- **Date:** 1998-09-16T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<35ffb2c1.3079725@news.enter.net>`
- **References:** `<360020BE.6048@narvesen.no>`

```
H�kon Olsen <hakon.olsen@narvesen.no> wrote:

>We have an application written in Micro Focus Cobol and running on
>PC/MS-DOS that, for a variety of reasons, we would like to move to our
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>Oslo


H�kon:

I'm sure that it is very feasible.  You need to address the impact of
possible changes to your source program.

I believe that Micro Focus does not support the OS/400 operating
system, but you should first check with them to see if they have a
compiler which does indeed support that environment.  That would be
the "path of least resistance" so to speak.  In other words, using the
same compiler on the new environment should result in minimal (if any)
source code changes.

If Micro Focus does not have a compiler which runs on an AS/400
machine, then you may wish to contact IBM and Ryan McFarland (Liant).

I'm quite sure that both IBM and Ryan McFarland have COBOL compilers
supporting this platform.

Here are the web sites:

Micro Focus COBOL

http://www.mfltd.co.uk

IBM COBOL

http://www.software.ibm.com/ad/cobol/cobol.htm

Ryan McFarland COBOL

http://www.liant.com/products/language/rm/rmcobol.html

You may have quite a task ahead of you in converting your source code
to run with a different compiler.  It depends on such issues as:

- use of proprietary or platform-specific compiler extensions used in
your existing source program

- file handling differences between the various compilers

- block mode screen handling on an AS/400 (5250 terminal emulation
issues) versus character mode screen handling on the PC

...and probably many more things that I haven't even thought about.
I'm not trying to discourage you, just alert you to the possibility
that there will most likely need to be source code changes when you
make the move.

Good luck!


Bob Wolfe, flexus
Check out The Flexus COBOL Page at http://www.flexus.com
```

---

## Thread: IDLE LOOP (win95). — 5 msgs

### IDLE LOOP (win95).

- **From:** "Donald Tees" <donald@willmack.com>
- **Date:** 1998-09-17T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<6trmbm$84l$1@news.igs.net>`

```
Does anybody have a subroutine for Windows95 that calls
the idle API?  I need to put it into a wait for file lock loop.

I am planning to use it with Fujitsu, but if anyone has one for
another compiler, I will convert it and post it back. There
should not be much of a difference.
```

#### ↳ Re: IDLE LOOP (win95).

- **From:** "Leif Svalgaard" <leif@ibm.net>
- **Date:** 1998-09-17T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<360163b7.0@news1.ibm.net>`
- **References:** `<6trmbm$84l$1@news.igs.net>`

```
Donald Tees wrote in message <6trmbm$84l$1@news.igs.net>...
>Does anybody have a subroutine for Windows95 that calls
>the idle API?  I need to put it into a wait for file lock loop.
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>should not be much of a difference.


Donald,
The etk2syst.dll from http://www.etk.com/products/etkf32a has a sleep
routine that sleeps a number of milliseconds.
Alternatively you can call Sleep using milliseconds in the WIN32 API.
```

#### ↳ Re: IDLE LOOP (win95).

- **From:** Karl Wagner <kwagner@sympatico.ca>
- **Date:** 1998-09-17T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3601723C.6A1B7F9C@sympatico.ca>`
- **References:** `<6trmbm$84l$1@news.igs.net>`

```
Donald Tees wrote:
> 
> Does anybody have a subroutine for Windows95 that calls
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> should not be much of a difference.

Here's how to call the API directly from MF COBOL.

SPECIAL-NAMES.
    CALL-CONVENTION 74 IS APIENTRY.

* Wait 100 microseconds
    CALL APIENTRY "Sleep" USING
       BY VALUE 100 SIZE 4 
    END-CALL

I think it looks something like this in Fujitsu but I haven't had much
chance to look at their implementation yet so I could be messed up about
byte ordering and the syntax for a STDCALL.
      
        01  SLEEP-MICROSECONDS       PIC 9(9) BINARY.

	CALL "_Sleep" USING BY CONTENT SLEEP-MICROSECONDS 

All that aside, you should probably use a Mutex or Semaphore and
WaitForSingleObject() instead of coding a polling loop.
```

#### ↳ Re: IDLE LOOP (win95).

- **From:** "Fujitsu Software" <cobol@adtools.com>
- **Date:** 1998-09-18T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<6tuj6c$elr$1@news.fujitsu.com>`
- **References:** `<6trmbm$84l$1@news.igs.net>`

```
Donald,

In Fujitsu COBOL you would call the WIN32 Sleep function as follows:

              CALL "SleepEx" WITH STDCALL USING
                                  BY VALUE SLEEP-MICROSECONDS
                                  BY VALUE 1
              END-CALL
```

### migration from mainframe to client server

- **From:** gvwmoore@ix.netcom.com (G Moore)
- **Date:** 1998-09-17T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36007e6c.2534294@nntp.ix.netcom.com>`

```
hey, earlier i was reading threads about migration to c/s from
mainframe being hard. does any1 know about thins? is it because
there is alot of source on mainframe that can't easily be duplicated
to c/s, or is it because of the time involved in the migration of all
the custom code?


gvwmoore@ix.spam.netcom.com to reply remove the spam
```

#### ↳ Re: migration from mainframe to client server

- **From:** "COBOL Frog (Huib Klink)" <H.Klink@IMN.nl>
- **Date:** 1998-09-17T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36017A9F.6FE6F86B@IMN.nl>`
- **References:** `<36007e6c.2534294@nntp.ix.netcom.com>`

```
G Moore wrote:

> hey, earlier i was reading threads about migration to c/s from
> mainframe being hard. does any1 know about thins? is it because
> there is alot of source on mainframe that can't easily be duplicated
> to c/s, or is it because of the time involved in the migration of all
> the custom code?

Perhaps you could call a complete porting of all COBOL code from a
mainframe to a wintel environment with client PC's and one/more servers a
migration to C/S. In that case I have experience: am just doing that (not
alone happily). We try to convert as much 1-to-1 resulting in thick
client in de C/S paradigma. (Remember the famous Gardner-scheme for
2-tier C/S:1 all on client / very very thick client
2 db on server, rest client / thick client
3 db&business logic on server, rest client / normal
4 server now has also validation, client is just interface (net-PC, html)
/ thin client
5 all on server, dumb terminal on client / traditional mf
)
Now most problems come from intertwining business logic, validation,
screenhandling all in one cobol program cluster. Code separation
(interface // validation // bus.logic // i/o-handling) was never thought
about in relation to concepts as "client" and "server" those days when
the app. was originally built. So porting to a C/S like platform is quit
a job ...

The Frog
```

```
On Thu, 17 Sep 1998 03:16:11 GMT, gvwmoore@ix.netcom.com (G Moore) wrote:

>hey, earlier i was reading threads about migration to c/s from
>mainframe being hard. does any1 know about thins? is it because

Sorry, aren't you mixing a few things up?  Migrating from mainframe to C/S?  Who says that
these two are mutually exclusive?  I have been using Client/Server techniques on
mainframes for about 20 years now, beginning somewhere with CICS 1.4.  Okay, we didn't
CALL it C/S then, just Function Request Shipping, but that's jus a name.

Could you perhaps rephrase your question to make it more clear to me WHAT it is you really
want to know?


with kind regards

Volker Bandke
(BSP GmbH)
```

#### ↳ Re: migration from mainframe to client server

- **From:** Vadim Maslov <vadik@siber.com>
- **Date:** 1998-09-18T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3601DE70.B8786B6A@siber.com>`
- **References:** `<36007e6c.2534294@nntp.ix.netcom.com>`

```
G Moore wrote:
> 
> hey, earlier i was reading threads about migration to c/s from
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> gvwmoore@ix.spam.netcom.com to reply remove the spam


Usually the problem lies not in generic mainframe vs c/s
antipathy, and not in perceived bad performance of c/s
(c/s is now on a par with mainframe) but in concrete 
subsystems that tend to be entirely different:

1. User Interface. CICS or IMS DC on mainframe.
Screen Section, or httpd on c/s. 

2. Database. IDMS, IMS, Datacom, or DB/2 on mainframe.
Oracle, Sybase, DB/2, SQL Server on c/s.

Just one of those can make migration very difficult.
Two of them make it even more difficult, and what ytou are
fighting in this case is not the changing syntax of calls
but moving to a different paradigm: from network model DB
to relational DB, from CICS to HTTP daemon, and so on.

We are now studying a conversion, where in addition to the
two standard UI/DB migartions there is Y2K remed.

Still conversions like this need to happen and 
we at Siber Systems believe that they are even automatable.

Regards,
Vadim Maslov
```

#### ↳ Re: migration from mainframe to client server

- **From:** Charles F Hankel <charles@hankel.mersinet.co.uk>
- **Date:** 1998-09-21T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3605928C.18076F64@hankel.mersinet.co.uk>`
- **References:** `<36007e6c.2534294@nntp.ix.netcom.com>`

```
G Moore wrote:
> 
> hey, earlier i was reading threads about migration to c/s from
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> the custom code?

The usual problems that I have witnessed have much to do with
environment and less to do with c/s itself.

1.  The mainframe programmers take one look at the c/s environment.
1a. They wonder if the bosses are serious.
1b. They realise they always knew their bosses are serious.
1c. They wonder if their bosses are idiots.
1d. They realise that they know their bosses are idiots.
1e. The rearely get asked about the system being replaced.

2.  The UNIX guys walk into the building.
2a. They know the application on their first day, it's easy.
2b. They discuss strategy, methodology, design features, etc.
2c. The discuss the editor to use and what hilight colours to use.
2d. They then all disappear onto Oracle/Sybase/you-name-it courses.
2e. They discuss strategy, methodology in light of the courses in 2d.

3.  The UNIX guys have coded their project and it works.
3a. The c/s team get some live data from the mainframe guys.
3b. They don't understand the data.
3c. They ask about delimiters, mainframe guy shrugs.
3d. They ask about data structures, mainframe guy shrugs.
3e. They ask about VSAM believing it to be ISAM.

Struth, I wish I had the energy for this but pretty soon I'm going to be
watching live ML baseball so I'll stop here.  The main problem is lack
of communication, a lack of respect for the old farts on the mainframe,
the evangelical belief that any UNIX system can outperform any S/390
box, etc.

I have not yet witnessed a successful c/s conversion though I have seen
a few fail and a few get cancelled before failure becomes evident.  This
may all be a bit unfair to the c/s community but IMHO a conversion of a
working mainframe application to c/s is a waste of time and money.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: migration from mainframe to client server

```
>3e. They ask about VSAM believing it to be ISAM.

ok, is or is not vsam an indexing engine?


gvwmoore@ix.spam.netcom.com to reply remove the spam
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: migration from mainframe to client server

- **From:** Charles F Hankel <charles@hankel.mersinet.co.uk>
- **Date:** 1998-09-21T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3606BD61.E41EA57A@hankel.mersinet.co.uk>`
- **References:** `<36007e6c.2534294@nntp.ix.netcom.com> <3605928C.18076F64@hankel.mersinet.co.uk> <3606dbfc.1832957@nntp.ix.netcom.com>`

```
G Moore wrote:
> 
> On Mon, 21 Sep 1998 00:41:00 +0100, Charles F Hankel
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> ok, is or is not vsam an indexing engine?

Actually, I don't care.  It is not ISAM though, nor is it QSAM, QISAM,
BSAM, BPAM, BDAM, QDAM, etc.

The point is that in the exercise that I started to outline, the UNIX
guys arrive with a lot of preconceptions about how a system works and
what suers do.  Often these preconceptions fail muster.  Often they
don't understand data that has no delimiters, they have trouble with
simple stuff like packed decimal, etc., etc.

In short they decry the old system without either understanding that it
might actually work, and they feel that they have to not only reinvent
the wheel but ignore that a wheel aready exists that couuld be used for
reference.  The users detect this, a lot of users are not stupid.

Fact.  At one site, I was expressly forbidden to talk to users without
project management in attendance.  I should point out that this was a
c/s implementation using a well-known financials package and the users
ahd been told from Day 1 that it was the best thing since rat's fur. 
One lunchtime, I was backed into a corner (literally) by some users and
asked one simple question "We realise that this new system is going to
happen; but they can't honestly be serious that we are going to get a
better system from this, can they?"  My answer was that I had that day
depreciated a thousand assets on the production machine which was
otherwise empty at the time, and it had taken forty-five minutes.  The
users knew that they had a hundred & twenty thousand assets that were
depreciated every month.  We parted on the basis that I had told them
nothing and they went off to ponder this problem.  I understand that the
project was cancelled about six month later but I had moved on by that
time.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: migration from mainframe to client server

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** paulr@bix.com (paulr)
- **Date:** 1998-09-21T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<6u6lbc$q4q@lotho.delphi.com>`
- **References:** `<36007e6c.2534294@nntp.ix.netcom.com> <3605928C.18076F64@hankel.mersinet.co.uk> <3606dbfc.1832957@nntp.ix.netcom.com> <3606BD61.E41EA57A@hankel.mersinet.co.uk>`

```
Well, to be fair, the mainframe guys come in with a lot of 
preconceptions too, and those are just as false. For example,
they know little or nothing about streams, client/server
communication to distributed or remote notes, TCP/IP, 
RPC, or other things a UNIX person takes for granted.

Some folks know some of this, of course, but then again,
some UNIX people know things about mainframes too. :) 

My question is why should a mainframe have to *batch* 
a compile? Could it be that UNIX might just know 
something about process management and process priority
management that some mainframes don't? 

:) 
-Paul




Charles F Hankel (charles@hankel.mersinet.co.uk) wrote:
```

```
G Moore (gvwmoore@ix.netcom.com) wrote:
: On 21 Sep 1998 22:52:28 GMT, paulr@bix.com (paulr) wrote:

: >Well, to be fair, the mainframe guys come in with a lot of 
: >preconceptions too, and those are just as false. For example,
: >they know little or nothing about streams, client/server
: >communication to distributed or remote notes, TCP/IP, 
: >RPC, or other things a UNIX person takes for granted.

: i think rpc is the best model of c/s, compared to tcpip or streams or
: notes. make the client very fat, and have it update and recompile on
: its own machine. takes more cpu cycles, but them machines aren't doing
: anything. and technicians cost the same whether u repair a dumb
: terminal or a PC.

Depends upon what you are doing. RPC can be expensive if you are just
doing display work, or perfect if you are doing distributed processing.
But in the last case, the remotes are far from dumb and are actually
sharing some of the work load. I think mainframe to PC Client with 
distributed processing is really a good model to move to for 
very mainframe-centric shops. 

I noted in a previous message that you mentioned hooking up "dumb
terminals" - that is pretty much out of style these days, though
we still do it for a lot of reasons. ANd I am including text
based emulators in that. 

The best example of client server remote display I can think of
that mainframers will recognize is the CICS EPI interface. Very nice.



: >Some folks know some of this, of course, but then again,
: >some UNIX people know things about mainframes too. :) 

: >My question is why should a mainframe have to *batch* 
: >a compile? Could it be that UNIX might just know 
: >something about process management and process priority
: >management that some mainframes don't? 

: i have never heard of batching a compile.

Well, that is what they are talking about with an MVS system. 
You dispatch it interactively, but it really goes into a compiler
queue. Strange! 

-Paul


: gvwmoore@ix.spam.netcom.com to reply remove the spam
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: migration from mainframe to client server

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** Charles F Hankel <charles@hankel.mersinet.co.uk>
- **Date:** 1998-09-22T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36082921.B11C31FD@hankel.mersinet.co.uk>`
- **References:** `<36007e6c.2534294@nntp.ix.netcom.com> <3605928C.18076F64@hankel.mersinet.co.uk> <3606dbfc.1832957@nntp.ix.netcom.com> <3606BD61.E41EA57A@hankel.mersinet.co.uk> <6u6lbc$q4q@lotho.delphi.com> <360915ec.2983965@nntp.ix.netcom.com> <6u7aen$2j4@lotho.delphi.com>`

```
paulr wrote:
> 
> : >My question is why should a mainframe have to *batch*
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> queue. Strange!

It's not really that strange.  However, first a note to point out that
the compile job does not go into a compile queue.  It goes into a queue
for an initiator which runs the compile or whatever other sort of job
that is being run.  A compile may join a queue that is dealt with by one
or several initiators, depending on the system usage at the time, and
the compile may happen instantaneously or not.

The reason that things are done this way is for efficient use of
available system resources, so that a compile, for instance, does not
chew up resources that are needed by the live online systems that are
also probably running in the mainframe virtual environment.  This way, a
quart is squeezed into a pint pot.  Compared to what typically goes on
in a mainframe environment, a UNIX system does remarkably few things.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: migration from mainframe to client server

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** paulr@bix.com (paulr)
- **Date:** 1998-09-23T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<6u9jbg$j1b@lotho.delphi.com>`
- **References:** `<36007e6c.2534294@nntp.ix.netcom.com> <3605928C.18076F64@hankel.mersinet.co.uk> <3606dbfc.1832957@nntp.ix.netcom.com> <3606BD61.E41EA57A@hankel.mersinet.co.uk> <6u6lbc$q4q@lotho.delphi.com> <360915ec.2983965@nntp.ix.netcom.com> <6u7aen$2j4@lotho.delphi.com> <36082921.B11C31FD@hankel.mersinet.co.uk>`

```
How many things typically go on in a Mainframe system? On a 
"dead" UNIX system, with no users, and no database applications
or client server stuff running, about 40 processing are in use,
most of which are inactive. ON a modest server system running
with 50 or 60 users, there are - 3071 processes running, not
counting all the threads, and each user is getting real-time
response. 

I'm thinking that initators are sort of like process management
daemons in UNIX - which make sure the processors are used
efficiently and with some measure of fairness. 

To be fair, I've never seen a UNIX system supporting 
thousands of online users at one time, though it isn't
uncommon to see them supporting thousands of clients 
from database or other applications. And I think UNIX
systems these days probably have at least as much
RAM in them as mainframes typcially do (the one I just
checked with 3071 processes running has a half GIG of
RAM, and 60 GIGS of disk storage local to it. Another 
few hundred gigs are mounted via NFS from a raid
array.) 

Yours,
-Paul


Charles F Hankel (charles@hankel.mersinet.co.uk) wrote:

: > 
: > : i have never heard of batching a compile.
: > 
: > Well, that is what they are talking about with an MVS system.
: > You dispatch it interactively, but it really goes into a compiler
: > queue. Strange!

: It's not really that strange.  However, first a note to point out that
: the compile job does not go into a compile queue.  It goes into a queue
: for an initiator which runs the compile or whatever other sort of job
: that is being run.  A compile may join a queue that is dealt with by one
: or several initiators, depending on the system usage at the time, and
: the compile may happen instantaneously or not.

: The reason that things are done this way is for efficient use of
: available system resources, so that a compile, for instance, does not
: chew up resources that are needed by the live online systems that are
: also probably running in the mainframe virtual environment.  This way, a
: quart is squeezed into a pint pot.  Compared to what typically goes on
: in a mainframe environment, a UNIX system does remarkably few things.

: -- 
: Charles F Hankel, Wirral, UK
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: migration from mainframe to client server

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** Ken Foskey <waratah@zip.com.au>
- **Date:** 1998-09-23T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3607BAB6.10342D0D@zip.com.au>`
- **References:** `<36007e6c.2534294@nntp.ix.netcom.com> <3605928C.18076F64@hankel.mersinet.co.uk> <3606dbfc.1832957@nntp.ix.netcom.com> <3606BD61.E41EA57A@hankel.mersinet.co.uk> <6u6lbc$q4q@lotho.delphi.com> <360915ec.2983965@nntp.ix.netcom.com>`

```
G Moore wrote:
> 
> >My question is why should a mainframe have to *batch*
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> gvwmoore@ix.spam.netcom.com to reply remove the spam

On unix try 'man batch'.  There are tools that allow the Unices to
schedule processing not take it on Willie nilly and hope to god the CPU
has enough of everything not to die.  The mainframe has an orderly
processing ability that is a set of traffic lights that allow only a few
process to have access to more resources and with co-operation the whole
thing goes a lot faster.

I am still trying to weed out the bugs of Unix where we start a process
and whoops you do not have enough resources and Unix rejects it.  That
could never happen on the big iron.

There is a new 'at' command on NT that is similar to the batching
concept of unix.  If you are talking huge projects then this might be
worth looking at

It is very frustrating knowing how you would structure a process on a
mainframe and on Unix this just does not work.  The code might port but
it just works very differently.  Mainframe reads files and writes files
and has just received a kind of piping tool, unix seems to like doing
this as a matter of course and PC pretty well require a monolithic
program (even if it uses DLL's to achieve it).

Each are different animals and each require different designs.

Ken
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: migration from mainframe to client server

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "Donald Tees" <donald@willmack.com>
- **Date:** 1998-09-22T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<6u9c77$34b$1@news.igs.net>`
- **References:** `<36007e6c.2534294@nntp.ix.netcom.com> <3605928C.18076F64@hankel.mersinet.co.uk> <3606dbfc.1832957@nntp.ix.netcom.com> <3606BD61.E41EA57A@hankel.mersinet.co.uk> <_GON1.7$OX1.26237@news4.mia.bellsouth.net>`

```
Judson McClendon wrote in message
<_GON1.7$OX1.26237@news4.mia.bellsouth.net>...

>Don't you just love those guys who say "I never consider efficiency, it's
>just not important?" ;-)


I stayed out of that last argument, but I am going to take issue
on this one<g>. There is a major difference between "not considering
the efficiency" when relating it back to machine level code, and
not benchmarking at the evaluation stage when you have a time
critical application.

With regard to the first, I would seldom make an array counter
a comp-1,2,3,4,5 etc. data type, even though I know it would
be more efficient.  I make it picture 9(n) because I then I know
it will work the same on every machine and under every Cobol,
and that the inefficiency is rather trivial(in 99.9% of the cases
for the nitpickers).

With regard to the second, I have a customer that wants a flashy
GUI at the senior management level, and they are going to convert
to the brand new product on the block, assisted by a team of twenty
engineers, seventeen management consultants, and ten C++
experts.  I was told the other day that since they knew speed was
important, they had been tuning, and had increased the system
throughput to 6 transactions per minute (from 3).

Now this happens to be a toll booth, for real trucks driven by real
ornery truck drivers.  I did not have the heart to tell him that a truck
arrives every four seconds for at least three hours per day, and
that waiting time costs them each about $50 per hour out of pocket.
I figure they have to learn the basics of the business the hard way,
and I am going to stand to one side and watch that "systems
engineer" try to keep the eighteen wheelers from being aimed
directly at him and his computer on his first day.

There are efficiencies, and there are efficiencies.  It is important
to note the differences.
;<)
P.S. -- One of the reasons I am going to let it happen is that I am
slightly miffed about the whole thing.  That system has run for 18
years now, 24 hours per day, with a total of 5 1/2 hours of down time.
That includes four major conversions, three platforms, and four
Cobol compilers. The last time it was down (3 hours, hit by a truck)
the line-up was six and one half miles long by the time I got the
spare machine running. I am still thinking about what rate I will
charge to get things working again after the conversion.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: migration from mainframe to client server

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc123@bellsouth.net>
- **Date:** 1998-09-22T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<zNWN1.671$OX1.319531@news4.mia.bellsouth.net>`
- **References:** `<36007e6c.2534294@nntp.ix.netcom.com> <3605928C.18076F64@hankel.mersinet.co.uk> <3606dbfc.1832957@nntp.ix.netcom.com> <3606BD61.E41EA57A@hankel.mersinet.co.uk> <_GON1.7$OX1.26237@news4.mia.bellsouth.net> <6u9c77$34b$1@news.igs.net>`

```
Donald Tees wrote:
>Judson McClendon wrote:
>>Don't you just love those guys who say "I never consider efficiency, it's
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>critical application.

I agree.  My point has been that if you design with efficiency in mind
all along, you likely won't have to back up and punt when the thing is
too slow, because you have already taken care of it.  And that includes
I/O efficiency as well as CPU efficiency, of course.  I/O efficiency is
normally more significant when looking at elapsed time, but CPU efficiency
is sometimes more significant when looking at overall system load on a
server or mainframe.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: migration from mainframe to client server

- **From:** redsky@ibm.net (Thane Hubbell)
- **Date:** 1998-09-21T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<Jl0PnHJ5PvPd-pn2-yDf3htQxUwWj@Dwight_Miller.iix.com>`
- **References:** `<36007e6c.2534294@nntp.ix.netcom.com> <3605928C.18076F64@hankel.mersinet.co.uk>`

```
On Sun, 20 Sep 1998 23:41:00, Charles F Hankel 
<charles@hankel.mersinet.co.uk> wrote:
> I have not yet witnessed a successful c/s conversion though I have seen
> a few fail and a few get cancelled before failure becomes evident.  This
> may all be a bit unfair to the c/s community but IMHO a conversion of a
> working mainframe application to c/s is a waste of time and money.

The company I used to work for is a Service company for the retail 
industry.  They have a very good, in house product they use to provide
their service.  They decided to license this system to larger 
retailers.  Well, everyone told them (this was in 92-93) that it had 
to be Client/Server - the mainframe is dead.  So they decide, they 
will kill three birds with one stone.  Y2K will be fixed, the 
mainframe eliminated, and they will have a C/S system to sell.  So 
they hire Microsoft.  No kidding.  Well, they bring in a bunch of 
programmer from India who click up some visual basic screens and try 
to develop a system.   It dies a horrible death.  Not to be quelled, 
however, the project is resurrected, after firing Microsoft, with C++ 
GURU's.  Some of who have DOCTORATE degrees is - Object Oriented 
Programming Design methods.  So, since 1994 this has been an ongoing 
project.  It's now ready.  I hear they sold a copy of it.  But - They 
have decided that it will not perform adequetly, nor will it account 
properly, nor can it reproduce the output of the mainframe product, so
it can't be used to replace the mainframe.  They will stick with their
mainframe product for the forseeable future.

Oh yeah - one other thing.  They approached the prospects who said it 
had to be C/S in 1992.  They want a system now that will run on OS/390
- C/S is dead.
```

```
is, are any of the Compiler venders that do not allready have them,
```

```
>Hi to all!
>
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
>

I have clients running the SCO port of RM/Cobol under Linux with no

needs this.

Also, AcuCobol has a specific port of their products for Linux,

MIke.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Cobol for Lunix

- **From:** cadams@cadams-ntw.acucorp.com (Chris Adams)
- **Date:** 1998-09-21T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<slrn70dcej.c0.cadams@cadams-ntw.acucorp.com>`
- **References:** `<3600F8EE.735A118@infonet.com.py> <6ttdq1$o2f$1@news01.iafrica.com>`

```
On Fri, 18 Sep 1998 12:52:54 +0200, Mike Rochford <miker@easirun.co.za> wrote:
>>I know this has been asked before, and I know about the FAQ. My question
>>is, are any of the Compiler venders that do not allready have them,
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>>this.

>I have clients running the SCO port of RM/Cobol under Linux with no
>problems. Apparently their is a SCO compatibility option under Linux and RM
>needs this.

If you're installing linux, the word to look for is IBCS (IIRC, Intel Binary
Compatibility Spec). I've heard of people who managed to get things like Oracle
running under it.

>Also, AcuCobol has a specific port of their products for Linux,

Yes, it's one of the fully supported platforms.

----
"Civilization won't *die* from Y2k. It'll be more like Civilization goes out
drinking and the next morning realizes the benefits of drinking gin from
smaller containers."
The opinions above are my own and may or may not match those of my employer.
```

## Thread: Help... VB -> COBOL -> Access ??? — 10 msgs

_Topics: [`Migration and conversion`](topics.md#migration) · [`Help requests and how-to`](topics.md#help)_

### Help... VB -> COBOL -> Access ???

- **From:** "AM" <AM69107@glaxowellcome.com>
- **Date:** 1998-09-17T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<01bde267$69c8fb50$c58c3398@us0071826>`

```
hi fellow COBOL professionals,

well i guess you can call this homework... but it will not be graded...

I have been programming for the IBM mainframe for about 9+ years now.
I have a background of programming in Clipper and DBAseIII+ and Foxbase
and building a knowledge in Visual basic and Access.

i am reading this newgroup for a while now and just got interested in
doing some experimenting and brush up with my PC skills.
This is what i want to do. Create a small program with Visual Basic 
front end, COBOL to do all the business processing, and Access as a
database. How do i do the interfaces? Just point me to the right
directions,
I may not need too much detail (but it'll be appreciated..) i.e, how to
call/set up VB to call COBOL, COBOL to Access... etc. any documentation
I can get my hands on?

This is what I have: CA-Realia Workbench, VB 4, Access 97.

Can I access Access SQL, just like I do in DB2???

I'm a mainframer, so please be understanding of my bit of ignorance in PC
programming, its been a while (9+ years). I can maneuver myself quite
professionally in Windows/OS2/DOS PC environments - just rusty in
the "new" terminology and programming. But programming to a programmer
is the same in all platforms, right???

Aedehl
```

#### ↳ Re: Help... VB -> COBOL -> Access ???

- **From:** "Art Perry" <arthur.perry@eds88.com>
- **Date:** 1998-09-17T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<6trtai$hdl$1@news.ses.cio.eds.com>`
- **References:** `<01bde267$69c8fb50$c58c3398@us0071826>`

```
AM wrote in message <01bde267$69c8fb50$c58c3398@us0071826>...
>This is what i want to do. Create a small program with Visual Basic
>front end, COBOL to do all the business processing, and Access as a
>database. How do i do the interfaces

I'm sorry, I don't know much about COBOL for the PC.  I would assume there
might be an ODBC driver for access that could be accessed by COBOL.

However, the Access (Jet) Database engine and format are native to Visual
Basic, and the Data Access Objects are pretty simple to use.  I think you
would save time and the processing would be more efficient to access the
database directly from VB.

But that wouldn't be as fun, now would it?
```

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol

```
how about creating a VB screen just to do initial validation on the PC
and then pass the business processing to a COBOL program on the mainframe.
data will be saved on the mainframe. how will I do that? What things should
I be 
looking at, in terms of documentation and directions...

Art Perry <arthur.perry@eds88.com> wrote in article
<6trtai$hdl$1@news.ses.cio.eds.com>...

```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Help... VB -> COBOL -> Access ???

- **From:** paulr@bix.com (paulr)
- **Date:** 1998-09-18T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<6tshmc$cv7@lotho.delphi.com>`
- **References:** `<01bde267$69c8fb50$c58c3398@us0071826> <6trtai$hdl$1@news.ses.cio.eds.com>`

```
I'm not sure this is a wise thing to do right now. :) 

First, you *can* do it. You use the ACCESS ODBC driver
that comes with VB, and a COBOL that knows how to
talk ODBC. VisualAge does, and I think that AcuCOBOL and
MicroFocus do as well. I'm not absolutely positive
about them though. 

Should you do it that way is another animal, and
just free advice, I would say "no."

If you really want to use Access as your database,
then just use VB and Access. This will be much eaiser
and reliable for you than inserting COBOL in the middle.

Or better yet, use one of the COBOLS that can do windoze
front ends (VisualAge, AcuCOBOLE, MicroFocus, Fujitsu)
and just do it all with COBOL files.

If you *must* separate out the database part, you r
will be well advised to use SQL Server or another
similar database (DB2, Oracle, etc.) as your database,
and Cobol *or* VisualBasic as your front end. 

Trust me, what you are wanting to do doesn't make
a lot of sense. 

Free advice, which I hesitated to give, but
which I will stand by. :) 

-Paul


Art Perry (arthur.perry@eds88.com) wrote:
: AM wrote in message <01bde267$69c8fb50$c58c3398@us0071826>...
: >This is what i want to do. Create a small program with Visual Basic
: >front end, COBOL to do all the business processing, and Access as a
: >database. How do i do the interfaces

: I'm sorry, I don't know much about COBOL for the PC.  I would assume there
: might be an ODBC driver for access that could be accessed by COBOL.

: However, the Access (Jet) Database engine and format are native to Visual
: Basic, and the Data Access Objects are pretty simple to use.  I think you
: would save time and the processing would be more efficient to access the
: database directly from VB.

: But that wouldn't be as fun, now would it?
: --

: Arthur Perry
```

- **From:** cadams@cadams-ntw.acucorp.com (Chris Adams)

```
On 18 Sep 1998 02:48:44 GMT, paulr <paulr@bix.com> wrote:
>First, you *can* do it. You use the ACCESS ODBC driver
>that comes with VB, and a COBOL that knows how to
>talk ODBC. VisualAge does, and I think that AcuCOBOL and
>MicroFocus do as well. I'm not absolutely positive
>about them though. 

Just for confirmation, Acucobol has an Acu4GL for ODBC. The Acu4GLs
automatically generate the SQL statements from COBOL verbs, so you don't even
need to recompile.
```

#### ↳ Re: Help... VB -> COBOL -> Access ???

- **From:** gvwmoore@ix.netcom.com (G Moore)
- **Date:** 1998-09-18T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<360cb309.5129995@nntp.ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<01bde267$69c8fb50$c58c3398@us0071826>`

```
On 17 Sep 1998 18:15:47 GMT, "AM" <AM69107@glaxowellcome.com> wrote:

>i am reading this newgroup for a while now and just got interested in
>doing some experimenting and brush up with my PC skills.
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>directions,

umm, i would not do it this way. instead, i would go with a pc cobol
compiler with odbc connectivity and windows support.

>Can I access Access SQL, just like I do in DB2???

visual basic allows SQL statements, and probably a cobol compiler does
also. however, i would not use any DB2 specific SQL extensions
(assuming there are any).

the main extensions i would look for SQL on PCs are 1 level commits,
cached databases, and defragmentation.

C Adams wrote

>Just for confirmation, Acucobol has an Acu4GL for ODBC. The Acu4GLs
>automatically generate the SQL statements from COBOL verbs, so you don't even
>need to recompile.

hmm. this might be a way to even skip the SQL part.
remember, having multiple languages in any product is a
bad idea.

of course, there might be some vendor specs on the 4gl's FDs
do to use of .mdb files, but that can be fixed with an extra level of
abstraction.


gvwmoore@ix.spam.netcom.com to reply remove the spam
```

## Thread: HELP! What's wrong with this program? — 23 msgs

_Topics: [`Help requests and how-to`](topics.md#help)_

### HELP! What's wrong with this program?

- **From:** casten@usfca.edu (John Casten)
- **Date:** 1998-09-17T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<casten-1709981348460001@138.202.137.35>`

```
4IDENTIFICATION-DIVISION.
PROGRAM ID. ERRORS-R-US.

ENVIRONMENT-DIVISION.
CONFIGURATION SECTION.
S0URCE-COMPUTER  VAX-13.
OBJECT-COMPUTER  VAX-13.

INPUT OUTPUT SECTION.
FILE-CONTROL.

    SELECT INPUT FILE
       ASSIGN       INPUT

    SELECT OUTPUT-FILE
       ASSIGN       OUTPUT.

DATA DIVISION.
FILE SECTION.

01  INPUT-FILE.
    03  IN-SSN             PIC X(09).
    03  IN-NAME            PIC X(30).
    05  IN-BALANCE-A       PIC X(05).
    05  IN-BALANCE-B       PIC X(05).

FD  OUTPUT-FILE.
01  OUTPUT-RECORD.
    03  OUT-SSN            PIC X(09)  VALUE SPACES.
    03  OUT-AMOUNT         PIC 9(07)  VALUE 0.

WORKING STORAGE SECTION.
01  EOF                    PIC X(01).
01  CALC-FIELD             PIC 9(07).
01  I-O COUNTERS.
    03  RECORDS-READ       PIC 9(05).
    03  RECORDS-WRITTEN    PIC 9(05).

PROCEDURE DIVISION.
0000-MAIN.

    PERFORM 1000-INIT.
    PREFORM 2000-READ.
    PERFORM 3000-PROCESS.
      UNTIL EOF = "YES".
    PERFORM 4OOO-TOTALS.
    PERFUME 5OOO-CLOSE.

1000-INITIALIZE

    OPEN INPUT-FILE
    OPEN OUTPUT-FILE.

2000-READ

    READ INPUT-FILE
      AT END
         MOVE Y    TO END-OF-FILE.
    ADD 1 TO RECS-READ.

3000-PROCESS

    ADD IN-BALANCE-A
        IN-BALANCE-B
    USING CALC-FIELD.

    MOVE IN-SSN      TO OUT-SSN
    MOVE CALC-FIELD  TO OUT-AMOUNT

    WRITE OUTPUT-RECORD.
    ADD 1 TO RECS-WRITTEN

4000-TOTALS.

    DISPLAY "NUMBER OF READS:  " RECS-WRITTEN.
    DISPLAY "NUMBER OF WRITES: " RECS-READ.

5000-CLOSE.

    CLOSE INPUT-FILE
          OUTPUT-FILE

* gave this as a quiz once
```

#### ↳ Re: HELP! What's wrong with this program?

- **From:** Karl Wagner <kwagner@sympatico.ca>
- **Date:** 1998-09-17T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36018DFE.696BC7E9@sympatico.ca>`
- **References:** `<casten-1709981348460001@138.202.137.35>`

```
Other than various missing or extra periods, quotes, hyphens, an
infinite loop condition, setting one flage and testing another,
mismatched picture clauses for numerics and some literals, a procedural
problem where you write forever after having done a single read, some
incorrectly ordered keywords, use of reserved words here and there,
missing verbs, use of obsolete constructs, and the sadistic teacher who
inflicted this upon his students... nothing a complete rewrite wouldn't
fix.

John Casten wrote:
> 
>
<<<SNIP>>>
 
> * gave this as a quiz once
> 
> --
```

```

…[15 more quoted lines elided]…

I work in The Land Before Time, where dinosaurs rule the earth.  What
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: HELP! What's wrong with this program?

- **From:** Karl Wagner <kwagner@sympatico.ca>
- **Date:** 1998-09-18T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<360256DE.ACEF345E@sympatico.ca>`
- **References:** `<casten-1709981348460001@138.202.137.35> <36018DFE.696BC7E9@sympatico.ca> <3601CFED.306A@bellsouth.net>`

```
Kitty Carr wrote:
> 
> Karl Wagner wrote:
…[23 more quoted lines elided]…
> REXX LEXX

I suppose 'obsolete' is a poor choice of words. Any talk of coding
techniques is likely to start another holy war but at the risk of
inciting a riot here goes...

1) The program (and I use the term loosely) does not take advantage of
any ANSI-85 'END-' conditions. If it did, most of the missing period
problems would go away, and the logic problem with doing a single read
and multiple writes would stick out. 

2) A few level 88 variables would go a long way to avoiding that mess
with the EOF/END-OF-FILE "Y"/"YES" problem.

3) Testing an AT END condition instead of a file status code means that
the program will not handle any of the other errors that could occur.
What happens if the input file is missing? Of course, this **ahem**
program doesn't have any exception processing so the point is moot.

4) Putting sequence numbers in the labels made a lot of sense when the
only way to analyze the code was by reading the listing. Editors do a
pretty good job of providing a 'FIND' function. There are tools to do
structure analysis and give you pretty pictures about how the paragraphs
which do a better job than paragraph naming conventions.
```

```
<much snippage>
John Casten wrote in message ...

When I worked for Micro Focus, I used to joke (when shown code like this),

   "Gee, I always wanted to be a compiler when I grow up!"

P.S. Except in this case, I would need to become a compiler AND a run-time
system to find all the errors.
```

#### ↳ Re: HELP! What's wrong with this program?

- **From:** "Chris Osborne" <chris_n_osborne@yahoo.com>
- **Date:** 1998-09-17T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36019c07.0@news2.uswest.net>`
- **References:** `<casten-1709981348460001@138.202.137.35>`

```
Uh, when you post a question like this (not counting the blasting you'll
get), it would be just a little more polite if you actually could tell us
what went wrong.

If you has posted the compiler listing . . . it would have the error
messages, and if they were obscure, someone could explain what they meant in
general terms, so you could learn about COBOL diagnostics.

But on zero information, just what are we supposed to do?  Go over the
entire program?  Did you copy&paste this?  If you typed it in by hand, what
do I assume are real errors, and what are typos caused by retyping?

Just to be polite on my own behalf, I did scan through your program.  I
spotted 6 major errors, and others amounting to 14 total errors.

What am I supposed to assume from this?  That you spent a long time working
hard trying to figure out what the diagnostic messages meant, and then
trying to eliminate them?

Would you post the total number of times you compiled this program before
you decided you couldn't solve this problem?  Maybe you could describe the
steps you took in trying to solve each error message?

Really, I'm not being mean about this.


John Casten wrote in message ...
>4IDENTIFICATION-DIVISION.
>PROGRAM ID. ERRORS-R-US.
…[83 more quoted lines elided]…
>Like everyone who is not in love, he imagined that one chooses the person
one loves after endless deliberation and on the strength of diverse
qualities and advantages.
>- Proust
```

```
^missing period
>

>    03  IN-NAME            PIC X(30).

^ values not allowed in FD
>

^infinite loop (eof is 1 byte)

The compiler exists to do this task,
I do not know just why you ask ...
```

```
In article <36026a5e.0@news2.uswest.net>, "Chris Osborne"
<chris_n_osborne@yahoo.com> wrote:

> The errors in this program are _too_ much.  The author hasn't posted back.

> Anyone else suspect this is another Troll?

Well, I'm the trolling author, back out from under the bridge.  I'm having
great fun reading the thread.  Kudos to Karl Wagner.  He sent me an email
with the following (and I'm cutting and pasting you Karl):

If you haven't read it, you might want to look at "Code Complete" by
Steve McConnell (Microsoft Press). It doesn't cover COBOL but it's got
some good ideas about practical coding techniques that everybody should
use.

One of the guys in our shop sent a postcard from NYC, and I quote:

   03 NEW-YORK-FUNDS       PIC 9(04).
      88 GOT-MONEY            VALUE 1000.
      88 OUT-OF-MONEY        VALUE 0.

   IF GOT-MONEY
      PERFORM 2000-SEE-BIG-APPLE VARYING NEW-YORK-FUNDS
         BY 100 UNTIL OUT-OF-MONEY
   END-IF

funny guy
```

```
*i've only taken 1 course in cobol, and the rest i learned on my own
*take these as comments, not necessarily certainties
*the locations of the periods i am sure are pretty off...


On 17 Sep 1998 19:51:28 GMT, casten@usfca.edu (John Casten) wrote:

>4IDENTIFICATION-DIVISION.
number
>PROGRAM ID. ERRORS-R-US.

>ENVIRONMENT-DIVISION.

number
>OBJECT-COMPUTER  VAX-13.

>INPUT OUTPUT SECTION.
>FILE-CONTROL.

>    SELECT INPUT FILE
>       ASSIGN       INPUT
no period no file name no organization

>    SELECT OUTPUT-FILE
>       ASSIGN       OUTPUT.

same

>DATA DIVISION.
>FILE SECTION.

no fd
>01  INPUT-FILE.
>    03  IN-SSN             PIC X(09).
should just be 9
>    03  IN-NAME            PIC X(30).
>    05  IN-BALANCE-A       PIC X(05).
5
>    05  IN-BALANCE-B       PIC X(05).
5

>FD  OUTPUT-FILE.
>01  OUTPUT-RECORD.
>    03  OUT-SSN            PIC X(09)  VALUE SPACES.
9, set value spaces?
>    03  OUT-AMOUNT         PIC 9(07)  VALUE 0.
7
can u have a set value?


>WORKING STORAGE SECTION.
>01  EOF                    PIC X(01).
1
>01  CALC-FIELD             PIC 9(07).
7
>01  I-O COUNTERS.
>    03  RECORDS-READ       PIC 9(05).
>    03  RECORDS-WRITTEN    PIC 9(05).
5,5

>PROCEDURE DIVISION.
>0000-MAIN.

>    PERFORM 1000-INIT.
bad name

>    PREFORM 2000-READ.
>    PERFORM 3000-PROCESS.
>      UNTIL EOF = "YES".
>    PERFORM 4OOO-TOTALS.
>    PERFUME 5OOO-CLOSE.
no stop run.



>1000-INITIALIZE

>    OPEN INPUT-FILE
>    OPEN OUTPUT-FILE.
period on next line instead


>2000-READ

>    READ INPUT-FILE
>      AT END
>         MOVE Y    TO END-OF-FILE.

no Y vairable (use quotes)

>    ADD 1 TO RECS-READ.
bad name
period on next line


>3000-PROCESS

>    ADD IN-BALANCE-A
>        IN-BALANCE-B
>    USING CALC-FIELD.

bad statement altogether

>    MOVE IN-SSN      TO OUT-SSN
>    MOVE CALC-FIELD  TO OUT-AMOUNT
>    WRITE OUTPUT-RECORD.

>    ADD 1 TO RECS-WRITTEN
bad var name

..


>4000-TOTALS.
>    DISPLAY "NUMBER OF READS:  " RECS-WRITTEN.
>    DISPLAY "NUMBER OF WRITES: " RECS-READ.

need extra period?


>5000-CLOSE.

>          OUTPUT-FILE

need end period
```
