```
>These are just some thoughts, I hope they help.
>
Cheers,
Kevin
```

#### ↳ Re: Cobol/370 abend w/native DLI calls

- **From:** rkrayhawk@aol.com (RKRayhawk)
- **Date:** 1998-09-19T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<19980919135548.03465.00001824@ng-fa1.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<9C23731407A4B4B5.79C56BBBEDD23675.FDEA1DAD552F939D@library-proxy.airnews.net>`

```


On  Fri, 18 Sep 1998 23:36:46 GMTFrom: kthompson@netexas.net (Kevin) stated
>> 
We haven't changed the calls but are getting a
S0C4 in the call and the psb address being returned is a negative
number.
<<

If memory serves me well the schedule PSB call will have two parameters, the
function and the PSBname.

I am not certain you actually get a PSB address per se. Instead, you need to be
trafficking in one or morePCB addresses.  Generally, those addresses would have
been resolved upon entry (ENTRY 'DLITCBL', or procedure division USING clause.

The ancient tradition of program interfaces in the mainframe environment was to
pass a pointer in register 1 to a list of parm addresses.  So for an IMS
application program at entry, register one pointed to a list of PCB addresses. 


The tradition further held that such an address list could be variable in
length, so we needed a way to terminate the list. The high order bit of the
address is turned on to so designate the last (or only) PCB on the list as the
end of the list.  In a 24-bit address achitecture, the entire upper eight bits
of addresses used to be zero, so a end of list address would look like it had
x'80' in it.

This is not a negative number.  The bit is a semaphore.

This style of address management causes problems in 31 bit architectures. 
There  are a lot of scrub-up mechanism to get rid of the x'80' where necessary.
 But you have to inform the compiler and linker of the kind of combinations you
intend so that the right service module bindings will be brought in.

The other responders are right-on in guiding you to the link parms, load libs
and LE settings (if any).  But you may have to resolve whether or not you have
a viable plan.

To use the native DLI calls (if they introduce DATA 24 link requirements), may
not be compatible with what you really want to be doing with CICS. Which would
seem to raise the gruesome prospect of a conversion to EXEC DLI, which I would
bet increases the project size intolerably.

Do you have assurance from the vendor that native DLI calls are viable in the
CICS and OS environment that you plan to deploy?












Robert Rayhawk
RKRayhawk@aol.com
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Cobol/370 abend w/native DLI calls

- **From:** kthompson@netexas.net (Kevin)
- **Date:** 1998-09-20T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9C3F1E95ECABA9A5.C553576950B70BD9.126092967BE89D09@library-proxy.airnews.net>`
- **References:** `<9C23731407A4B4B5.79C56BBBEDD23675.FDEA1DAD552F939D@library-proxy.airnews.net> <19980919135548.03465.00001824@ng-fa1.aol.com>`

```
Thanks for the help.  Seems with Cob2 they should have deleted the BLL
cells in LS and performed a CICS call to set the LS pointer addresses
after scheduling the psb.  Seems Cob2 still allowed the prior but with
Cob3 you now get a S0C4.  Modified the program Saturday morning and it
works like a charm. 

Being from an IMS shop this was handed off to me (native IMS calls)
though it was a CICS "feature" - having to schedule the psb and
retrieve the address.

Cheers,
Kevin 

On 19 Sep 1998 17:55:48 GMT, rkrayhawk@aol.com (RKRayhawk) wrote:

>
>
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

<a id="t-10b3693fc1"></a>
## Thread: Mainframe Cobol job with Unix MF Cobol/Fileshare experience; Difficult? — 4 msgs

_Topics: [`Jobs, careers, recruiting, salary`](topics.md#jobs) · [`Compilers and vendors`](topics.md#compilers) · [`Mainframe, z/OS, JCL, CICS`](topics.md#mainframe)_

### Mainframe Cobol job with Unix MF Cobol/Fileshare experience; Difficult?

- **From:** toldark_no_spam@toldark.com.au
- **Date:** 1998-09-18T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3601dd16.941252239@news.spirit.com.au>`

```
Hi all,

I'm an IT professional (contractor)  with 20 years of experience in a
variety of languages/platforms. My last major project (~500K lines of
MF Cobol on a Unix platform using MF's FileShare as the datastore)
has just finished and I'm looking for work. Unfortunately, the only
Cobol-related jobs going in this city are mainframe-based - the most
common requirement is CICS/COBOL.

How difficult would it be to learn the CICS/Cobol development
environment? Any suggestions as to how I could go about convincing
someone to hire me given my background? Would a short-term contract as
a Y2K tester give me any useful experience?

Thanks muchly for any comments / suggestions!

Janna
```

#### ↳ Re: Mainframe Cobol job with Unix MF Cobol/Fileshare experience; Difficult?

- **From:** redsky@ibm.net (Thane Hubbell)
- **Date:** 1998-09-18T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<Jl0PnHJ5PvPd-pn2-wG991KdLcF3T@Dwight_Miller.iix.com>`
- **References:** `<3601dd16.941252239@news.spirit.com.au>`

```
On Fri, 18 Sep 1998 04:10:09, toldark_no_spam@toldark.com.au wrote:

> How difficult would it be to learn the CICS/Cobol development
> environment? Any suggestions as to how I could go about convincing
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> Thanks muchly for any comments / suggestions!


I'm a smart guy.  I tried to learn CICS programming from several text 
books (years and years ago).  I didn't "Get it".  I finally took a 
couple courses from IBM  at their "Guided Learning Center".  The 
courses are not cheap, but not too expensive.  It might be worth your 
while to see if they are still offered (I did mine in 1989).  

There is a good reason they don't hire people with no  CICS.  It's not
"hard" but it is "special".   

If you are good at taking a task, doing it right now, then forgetting 
you ever did it, you can do CICS. <G>
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Mainframe Cobol job with Unix MF Cobol/Fileshare experience; Difficult?

- **From:** "AM" <AM69107@glaxowellcome.com>
- **Date:** 1998-09-18T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<01bde32f$10ad8fe0$c58c3398@us0071826>`
- **References:** `<3601dd16.941252239@news.spirit.com.au> <Jl0PnHJ5PvPd-pn2-wG991KdLcF3T@Dwight_Miller.iix.com>`

```
I don't think it would be difficult. I learned CICS from a co-worker back
in 1990,
we used realia-CICS as a training tool so we won't mess up the mainframe,
especially
the training was not sponsored by the company. We had 3 sessions in a week.

And I believe I did very well. As a programmer of PC background (Clipper,
FoxBase, DBaseIII+, back then)
and a year in mainframe DB2/COBOL/CSP - the only thing that was a little
hard for me 
to understand then was the Pseudo-conversational thingie of CICS
programming - it's
like how in the ^%@*#%* did my working storage go away, kind of a thing -
something
that I don't think about when doing IBM CSP or DbaseIII+.   ;o))

<Jl0PnHJ5PvPd-pn2-wG991KdLcF3T@Dwight_Miller.iix.com>...
| On Fri, 18 Sep 1998 04:10:09, toldark_no_spam@toldark.com.au wrote:
| 
…[20 more quoted lines elided]…
|
```

#### ↳ Re: Mainframe Cobol job with Unix MF Cobol/Fileshare experience; Difficult?

- **From:** "rmch" <rmch@cadvision.com>
- **Date:** 1998-09-19T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3603b986.0@news.cadvision.com>`
- **References:** `<3601dd16.941252239@news.spirit.com.au>`

```
I learned CICS on the job as well.  I wouldn't say it's "easy", because it's
not like learning another procedural language.  However, if you grasp the
pseudoconversational concept, then that's a great start.  I would suggest
looking at IBM's redpages on the web.  I'm pretty sure they have a CICS
manual online kicking around somewhere.  Other than that, a course from IBM
might be the way to go... as a contractor can't you write off the expense?
```

<a id="t-26bd03ab40"></a>
## Thread: Need help with "user logic error" (file-related?) — 3 msgs

_Topics: [`Help requests and how-to`](topics.md#help)_

### Need help with "user logic error" (file-related?)

- **From:** "Irene Jacobsen" <irene.jacobsen@lillehammer.fou.telenor.no>
- **Date:** 1998-09-18T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<6tu992$n1f@info.telenor.no>`

```
Hey.

I get the message:
+IGZ0201  A user logic error occurred. Neither FILE STATUS nor a declarativs
was specified for file 'qpr001' in program 'p031form' at relative location
X'18c8'. The i-o status code was '44'.
EA995I Symptom dump output
User completion code=1020
.....
IEF450I T031007U s030 p010 - ABEND=s000 U1020 REASON=00000000


and i am troubling with what the real error is..????...

I am working on a big program, producing a "raw-data-file", which then i
processed through a "formatting-program" (p031form)to at last produce my
"report" (qpr001).

The raw-data-file is defined as:
DSN=&&worka,disp=(new,pass),unit=Work,space=(cyl,(10,10),,,round) in the jcl
, and as:
label record standard
recording mode is f
record contains 2856 characters
block contains 0 records.
01 tab-a-post pic x(2856).   in the program to produce the raw-data-file.

Because i use the "formatting program" on more than one "raw-data-file" i
have:
DSN=&&worka,disp=(old,delete)  in the jcl.  for the input file to the
"formatting program", and
DSN=&bib.031p.sr007.s020.taba,disp=new,catlg),
DCB=(recfm=vb,lrecl=4004,blksize=8008), space=(8008,(2000,200),rlse)  in the
jcl for the output file from the "formatting program".

and
label record is standard
recording mode is F
record contains 000 characters
data record is data-posten.
01 data-posten.
   02 data-symbol                   pic x.
   02 data-post.
         03  data-t     occurs 32759 times pic x.

in the program for my input-file.

and

label record standard
recording mode is v
record varying from 1 to 4000 depending on ut-lengde
block contains 0 records.
01 ut-post.
     03  ut-t  occurs 1 to 4000 times depending on ut-lengde pic x.

in my program for my "report-file"  (and of course i have defined my
ut-lengde in working-storage)

The size of the "raw-data-file" depends on which parameters i send into my
program. When i produce a "small" "raw-data-file" everything works perfect,
and i get the result. So, when just testing on smaller things there is no
problem.

My customers (whom i work for) though wants to use parameters producing
"large raw-data-files", and hence i must have that working also.
When trying to produce a larger raw-data-file i get the error i wrote in the
beginning.

I am not very good at jcl-programming, so i think my problem may be there.
My formatting program don't even reach the "open" statement for my
input-file. The output file may end up in about equal size of the
"raw-data-file" or normally about half the size, but i don't think it will
become larger.

Could someone out there please help me?
```

#### ↳ Re: Need help with "user logic error" (file-related?)

- **From:** "Jeff" <a@a.com>
- **Date:** 1998-09-18T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<2sBM1.702$4E6.5337004@storm.twcol.com>`
- **References:** `<6tu992$n1f@info.telenor.no>`

```

>X'18c8'. The i-o status code was '44'.


This means that the record size in your COBOL does not match the size of the
actual file. Not sure what happens if you specify a different record length
in the JCL than what is the actual size of the file, but I am sure it causes
an error. Error code 44 is what would be in the status code variable in
COBOL had you defined one.


>The raw-data-file is defined as:
>DSN=&&worka,disp=(new,pass),unit=Work,space=(cyl,(10,10),,,round) in the
jcl
>, and as:
>label record standard
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>01 tab-a-post pic x(2856).   in the program to produce the raw-data-file.


Don't use RECORD CONTAINS  clause, it is just a pain in the ass, although
sometimes it is a shop standard.


>DSN=&&worka,disp=(old,delete)  in the jcl.  for the input file to the
>"formatting program", and
>DSN=&bib.031p.sr007.s020.taba,disp=new,catlg),
>DCB=(recfm=vb,lrecl=4004,blksize=8008), space=(8008,(2000,200),rlse)  in
the
>jcl for the output file from the "formatting program".

>
>and
>label record is standard
>recording mode is F
>record contains 000 characters

Normally this would tell JCL to pick the best block size, but since you
specified it it must match with the JCL, thus it should be BLOCK CONTAINS 2
RECORDS. Remove it to eliminate this type of error.

>data record is data-posten.
>01 data-posten.
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>

I believe the maximum record length for a file is 32759 as you specified,
but it is a variable length record, thus the maximum record length is 32755.

If you specify a block size in JCL it must match the BLOCK contains clause
in COBOL. So your JCL says a block contains 2 records, so your COBOL should
say BLOCK CONTAINS 2 RECORDS. Remember 4 bytes for record length, so this
does not need to be accounted for in COBOL.

I find it better to specify as few record properties as possible in COBOL.
And to always use BLKSIZE=0, in JCL if specified as all.

>in the program for my input-file.
>
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>recording mode is v

**************************************************************
>record varying from 1 to 4000 depending on ut-lengde
>block contains 0 records.
**************************************************************
Not sure if this is needed, eliminate it is if does not cause a compile
error. COBOL looks at the physical storage size in your 01 groupings to
determine the max and min of the record length.

>01 ut-post.
>     03  ut-t  occurs 1 to 4000 times depending on ut-lengde pic x.
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
>When trying to produce a larger raw-data-file i get the error i wrote in
the
>beginning.
>
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
>

One thing I have found helpful is to specify as few JCL parameters as
possible. With exception of specifying LRECL & RECFM. I always specify
BLKSIZE=0 in JCL unless the files are designated otherwise. The only
required options when creating a file are DISP and SPACE.

Some of this may seem like symantics, but I have changed thousands of JCL
streams and following some standard proactices really helps. Your shop may
require you to specify certain parameters in certain ways, htis is usually
for a good reason.

If I sound insulting I apologize, but you needed help and here it is.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Need help with "user logic error" (file-related?)

- **From:** "Irene Jacobsen" <irene.jacobsen@lillehammer.fou.telenor.no>
- **Date:** 1998-09-19T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<6u0e62$c06@info.telenor.no>`
- **References:** `<6tu992$n1f@info.telenor.no> <2sBM1.702$4E6.5337004@storm.twcol.com>`

```
Thank's Jeff.

I don't think you are insulting. I am thankful that people like you take the
time reading and helping others with their problems. I now think i have
found the error. It is good for the "not-so-big-experts" to get help from
other who have reached longer in the way of understanding this stuff.

Thanks. :-)

Irene Jacobsen.


Jeff wrote in message <2sBM1.702$4E6.5337004@storm.twcol.com>...
>
>>X'18c8'. The i-o status code was '44'.
>
>
>This means that the record size in your COBOL does not match the size of
the
>actual file. Not sure what happens if you specify a different record length
>in the JCL than what is the actual size of the file, but I am sure it
causes
>an error. Error code 44 is what would be in the status code variable in
>COBOL had you defined one.
...................................................
>Some of this may seem like symantics, but I have changed thousands of JCL
>streams and following some standard proactices really helps. Your shop may
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

```
Charlie Goodman
```

```
> 
> Have you checked out the Fujitsu COBOL compiler.  Their full blown
```

#### ↳ Re: MF Cobol prices

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1998-09-18T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<6tu4tg$igs@dfw-ixnews6.ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<3601CD25.B896FA8E@mbnet.mb.ca>`

```

Charles Goodman wrote in message <3601CD25.B896FA8E@mbnet.mb.ca>...
>I am about to purchase a compiler but am a bit astonished when quoted
>prices by MicroFocus Canada:
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
>Charlie Goodman

Yes those prices are (roughly) comparable to other countries.  What isn't
true is that you are "buying a compiler".  What you are buying is a complete
development environment/kit (which includes a compiler).  Micro Focus (for a
year or so) sold its "Object COBOL" compiler as a separate product (in the
5-6 hundred dollar range) but for whatever reasons (never publicly stated -
but most likely having to do with lack of demand) have gone back to selling
only "complete environments" and fairly high prices (but prices that are
comparable to both the IBM and CA "full environment" prices).

P.S. (In response to another of your posts) as stated earlier in this
newsgroup, Fujitsu now has many (most?) of the cbl_ routines in a compatible
form to the Micro Focus ones.
```

## Thread: Student needs help!!! — 1 msgs

_Topics: [`Tutorials, books, learning`](topics.md#learning)_

### Re: Student needs help!!!

- **From:** Binyamin_Dissen@theoffice.net (Binyamin Dissen)
- **Date:** 1998-09-18T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36053842.267267427@news.netvision.net.il>`
- **References:** `<01bde2ac$25085fc0$416532d1@honnster>`

```
On 17 Sep 1998 22:28:40 -0500 "Scott M. Honn" <scotthonn@iname.com> wrote:

:>I'm a student taking my first COBOL course and have run into a run-time
:>error that I can't figure out.  

:>Here's the error msg I'm getting:
:>RCL0002: File status 48 on C:pgm1smh.rpt
:>	   Error detected at offset 01c0 in segment 00 of pgm1smh

:>I've opened and closed the two files I'm using in, seemingly, the right
:>places.  I know that it's difficult to pinpoint a problem you can't see but
:>I'm only looking for things to look for that may be the cause.  Anybody
:>have any ideas???  I've attached the pgm1smh.lst just in case someone wants
:>to take a look.

As half of the listing is compiler errors do to a missing period, I would
suggest correcting the compiler errors before trying to execute the program.

You must clear up all E or higher errors. I personally like to clear up W
errors as well (except for the ^$%$^$ truncation warning).
```

<a id="t-e96e7c1d50"></a>
## Thread: Y2K Humor — 1 msgs

_Topics: [`Y2K and Year-2000 remediation`](topics.md#y2k)_

### Y2K Humor

- **From:** redsky@ibm.net (Thane Hubbell)
- **Date:** 1998-09-18T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,comp.software.year-2000
- **Message-ID:** `<Jl0PnHJ5PvPd-pn2-Qd9FpcJSoMIV@Dwight_Miller.iix.com>`

```
Came across this one, have not seen it here before.  We all knew COBOL
is the butt of many a joke.




The Top 15 Unforeseen Consequences of The "Millennium Bug"
----------------------------------------------------------------------
 

15. IRS demands a hundred years of interest from stunned
      taxpayers.

14. "99 Bottles of Beer on the Wall" song gets stuck in infinite
      loop.

13. At the stroke of midnight, Windows 99 turns back into DOS
     1.0, the Pentium V turns back into an 8088, and the Handsome User
     is left holding a beautiful glass mouse.

12. Internet Movie Database now lists "1901: A Space Odyssey"

11. Residents of Indiana have to figure out if they're off by
     999 years, 364 days and 23 hours, or 1000 years and one
     hour.

10. Bob Dole's age erroneously listed with only 2 digits.

9. Mel Brooks's "2000 year old man" skit stops being funny....
    Oops, too late.

8. Sales of Coca Cola jumps drastically after original cocaine-
    laden formula becomes legal again.

7. Software engineers point out that since computers think it's
    almost 1900, we technically have to "Party like it's 1899,"
    which, frankly, doesn't seem like much fun.

6. Microsoft declares the year 1900 to be the new standard of
    the "Gatesian" calendar.

5. Jesus shows up late for His second coming, blames it on
    COBOL programmers.

4. Computers temporarily fooled into thinking Strom Thurmond is
    only 103.

3. First Top 5 List of the year? "Reasons No One Would Ever
    Assassinate President McKinley"

2. Using a computerized adoption service, Michael Jackson
    mistakenly takes home some octogenarians.


..and the Number 1 Unforeseen Consequence of the "Millennium
Bug"...


1. Unexpected demand for COBOL programmers results in severe
    understaffing of fast-food restaurants.
```

---

```
recall that a fellow at work dumped the Mac OS and runs Linux on the
```

```
(LinuxPPC and MkLinux). I don't think any vendors support these platforms,
but it certainly wouldn't be much work to do the port...

out drinking and the next morning discovers the importance of drinking
```

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<6u5c61$gbq$2@news.enterprise.net>`
- **References:** `<nospam-1909981355100001@pm05-35.sac.verio.net> <nqnzfptvtnagrzybet.ezjto52.pminews@news> <Pine.OSF.4.03.9809192330370.31456-100000@octane.dasb.fhda.edu>`

```
In article <Pine.OSF.4.03.9809192330370.31456-100000@octane.dasb.fhda.edu>, 
rrg10300@dasb.fhda.edu says...

>
>IF it is possible to dual boot the Mac so that you can select either Mac
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>Apple box.)

I don't know of any LinuxPPC, MkLinux or for that matter Linux68K port of a 
COBOL compiler so I'd guess the original poster is out of luck. The only 
MacCOBOL I know of is a very old port of Micro Focus Level II COBOL from about 
1985 and I haven't seen that around for years. It's probably not 32bit clean 
for a start.

About the only way you'll get something working is through either a x86 PC 
emulation and the many PC compilers or through UAE and run the Amiga's NRCOBOL.
```

## Thread: Seeking: Stories/Poems in Cobol code? — 2 msgs

### Seeking: Stories/Poems in Cobol code?

- **From:** dswan@acs5.acs.ucalgary.ca (Daniel Swan)
- **Date:** 1998-09-19T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<6u0r5u$gbg@ds2.acs.ucalgary.ca>`

```
I have heard stories of people writing functional stories or
poems as Cobol code.. can anyone point me to some examples of this?
```

#### ↳ Re: Seeking: Stories/Poems in Cobol code?

- **From:** skidmike <skidmike@mindspring.com>
- **Date:** 1998-09-19T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36042572.4609C08E@mindspring.com>`
- **References:** `<6u0r5u$gbg@ds2.acs.ucalgary.ca>`

```
identification division.
program id.  response.
environment division.
configuration section.
special names.
    skidmike is mike.
data division
procedure division.
    IF this-is-what-we-do-for-recreation = TRUE THEN
        PERFORM GET-A-LIFE
    END-IF.

GET-A-LIFE.
    ACCEPT adult-beverages AND adult-entertainment AT local-strip-bar

lol.  don't rhyme but i didn't have time.  
prose in cobol would be amusing
but hard in the tongue we're using
i worry all day
'bout y2k
and how it's 2000
to say
to entertain
in cobol i must train
lest my coding is in vain
and i have to call thane
i surely don't expect to see
in cobol penned the oddesey
or shakespeare the as/400 upon
but please, no TRON!	

Daniel Swan wrote:
> 
> I have heard stories of people writing functional stories or
> poems as Cobol code.. can anyone point me to some examples of this?
```

<a id="t-967dce46bf"></a>
## Thread: thanks, thane — 1 msgs

### thanks, thane

- **From:** skidmike <skidmike@mindspring.com>
- **Date:** 1998-09-19T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36047450.647F927E@mindspring.com>`

```
i see what i was doing wrong now.  i did it close to your way, but after
re-setting the cursor position, i ACCEPTED the field again.  by the way,
quick tip.  when i was writing the daily sales report program, i didn't
think about the amounts it would be handling.  so like the "create
estimate" and "create invoice" programs, all the dollar amounts were pic
999v99.  therefore for a 1292.21 dollars in sales day, my daily report
printed 292.21.  that was a 4 to 5 hour headache.
```

### salary?

- **From:** "Krisstian" <embracor@bellsouth.net>

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<Md5N1.2846$Wv6.3651093@news3.mia.bellsouth.net>`

```
what salary should one expect working for a mid-size company with the
following Cobol education:

Self-Educated?
Certificate by a Technical/vocational institute?
Degree by a Technical/vocational Institute?

thanks
```

#### ↳ Re: salary?

- **From:** Mickey <mickeyb@nospam.home.com>
- **Date:** 1998-09-20T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36057BD4.375CE859@nospam.home.com>`
- **References:** `<Md5N1.2846$Wv6.3651093@news3.mia.bellsouth.net>`

```
Krisstian wrote:
> 
> what salary should one expect working for a mid-size company with the
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> Degree by a Technical/vocational Institute?

Nonw of the above is relevant. Experience is relevant, all else is
fluff. I have a few self-taught people working for me who I wouldn't
trade for 10 coders with PhDs. I began my career pulling coax through
holes in the floor in 1971. It has been at least 25 years since anybody
even asked what my educational background is.

mickey

> 
> thanks

You are welcome.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: salary?

- **From:** cadams@cadams-ntw.acucorp.com (Chris Adams)
- **Date:** 1998-09-21T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol

```
Well, I'm biased in sharing the same sort of background but I've always felt
that a someone who learned something because they wanted to has an advantage
over the guy who saw dollar signs looking at the course catalog...


----
"Civilization won't *die* from Y2k. It'll be more like Civilization goes out
drinking and the next morning realizes the benefits of drinking gin from
smaller containers."
The opinions above are my own and may or may not match those of my employer.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: salary?

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc123@bellsouth.net>
- **Date:** 1998-09-22T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<VYON1.11$OX1.35548@news4.mia.bellsouth.net>`
- **References:** `<Md5N1.2846$Wv6.3651093@news3.mia.bellsouth.net> <36057BD4.375CE859@nospam.home.com> <slrn70ddl1.c0.cadams@cadams-ntw.acucorp.com> <3606E086.A985C072@nospam.home.com>`

```
Mickey wrote:
>Chris Adams wrote:
>> Mickey wrote:
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
>me".


I agree with the above posts, but I think the real important thing is a bit
more fundamental.  What I appreciate is the right attitude.  If they lack
knowledge you can teach them, if they lack experience they can gain it.  But
if they don't have a good attitude, it doesn't matter what they know, they
aren't worth the gunpowder to blow them up, as my granddad used to say.  When
a programmer has the basic talent and the right attitude, the rest is just
a matter of time.  There is an attitude in academia which elevates academic
knowledge above real-world experience, and this attitude seeps into many of
the students.  This is a very bone-headed attitude which often severely
limits the graduate's willingness to learn from experience and those with
experience.  I suspect that, like myself, this is what the posters above
really object to.  Not the education itself, which is a good thing in itself,
but the bone-headed attitude that education alone is sufficient, and even
superior.  As Mickey pointed out above, which would you trust, a pilot with
one year's experience, or a person who had never flown, but read about it
for four years?  Only a moron (or a bone-head) would choose the latter.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: salary?

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** skyraven@home.com (Sue L. )
- **Date:** 1998-09-24T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<360aaec0.12640035@news>`
- **References:** `<Md5N1.2846$Wv6.3651093@news3.mia.bellsouth.net> <3606E086.A985C072@nospam.home.com> <VYON1.11$OX1.35548@news4.mia.bellsouth.net> <36083620.6FB74288@att.net> <6ub9dg$cv$1@clarknet.clark.net> <3609705D.6D5C@netbox.com>`

```
A good team player yes... a surly SOB no but someone who gets the work
done yes.. if you can put the good team player together with someone
who gets the work done then you're talking about a person who would be
perfect for the job in my book if I was in the shoes of the
interviewer. 

I'm not sure about what everyone else's thoughts are here but I
realize that programmer's have attitudes and yea they've got huge
egos, well some of them do. Least a few of the ones that I've worked
with in the past on the job have and that's caused problems big time
and it's costed a lot of wasted time which has costed the company a
lot of business and money. If you look at it from a Management
perspective when it comes to putting the right people in the right
areas, they want to make sure they all get along and that they'll all
work as a team to get the job done, on time, under demand and get it
done right the first time.. WITHOUT any mistakes.

just speaking my own thoughts..

Sue
```

```
>I agree with the above posts, but I think the real important thing is a bit

I agree Judson, however I would like to expand on one point. You mentioned
"basic talent", let's not minimize that. I think that apptitude is *very*
important. It is amazing how many people get a 4yr C/S degree then can't code
their way out of a paper bag.

Anyone with above average intelegence and the desire can learn the syntax to a
language but if they don't really understand how to put the pieces together to
solve the business problem at hand they don't do me or my business any good.
Part of it is ability, part is imagination, the rest is magic, craft or art?

Attitude is great, education is fine but experience is the only sure fire way
that I can tell that someone has the ability to sling code!

Mike Myers
```

```
Oh, that. Well, all *that* takes is guts, determination, sweat,
and a couple hundred or so hours of missed sleep.

Pick up the Fujitsu Compiler, and write an application. Sit down and
do it from scrap, from the very first line to the finish. Pick something
relatively hard too, like an accounting package or a warehouse 
management system, or a system for a book store. *Anything* will
do, because you will most likely sell it sooner or later. 

And you will have some real code and at least a little experience to 
show your prospective employers, not to mention you will have demonostrated
that you can do the work.

-Paul
```

#### ↳ Re: salary?

- **From:** jcj0347@aol.com (JCJ0347)
- **Date:** 1998-09-20T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<19980920112020.04775.00002004@ng75.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<Md5N1.2846$Wv6.3651093@news3.mia.bellsouth.net>`

```

>what salary should one expect working for a mid-size company with the
>following Cobol education:
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>thanks

The question is too open-ended.  With those particular education
backgrounds, a major factor in salary would be how much experience
the programmer has, and how good they were at their job.

A good programmer, self-starter and knows what they are doing,
doesn't screw up more code than they fix, with 2 years experience
should make $25,000 - $30,000 on W2 with a VoTech degree.  Maybe
slightly less for a certificate or self-educated.  Slightly more if the 
programmer can sling some CICS code and is familiar with JCL.

Regards,
JC Jones.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: salary?

- **From:** Bill Lynch <wblynch@att.net>
- **Date:** 1998-09-21T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36061C60.4E8A3C17@att.net>`
- **References:** `<Md5N1.2846$Wv6.3651093@news3.mia.bellsouth.net> <19980920112020.04775.00002004@ng75.aol.com>`

```
JCJ0347 wrote:
> 
> >what salary should one expect working for a mid-size company with the
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> the programmer has, and how good they were at their job.

I think two other big factors would be the geographic area, e.g., salary
scales are higher in NYC than St. Louis (not a putdown of St. Louis);
and the type of business, meaning brokerage pays more than manufacturing
(speaking in generalities, of course). If the salary surveys I read are
accurate, I make more as a senior technical person working for a bank in
NYC than lots of CIOs make (I'm at the project manager level in our
salary structure).

This is an area with *lots* of variation. I think the best approach here
it so ask around locally to get a feel for the market. Nose around the
'Net, too.

Bill Lynch
```

```
In message <<6u4fcn$me3@world6.bellatlantic.net>> "Aggiepa" <aggiepa@bellatlantic.net> writes:
> PCL language that can be easily saved in a file readable by a COBOL program?
> I am currently using Wordperfect 5.0, but the output generated requires a
> complex program to convert.

Any file can be readable by Cobol, but that doesn't that the
program can make sense of it.  

I use a Forms merge program to write PCL forms: output the
merge control data as a text file and the execute the
merger program which puts the fields into the form and prints.
```

<a id="t-67943038e1"></a>
## Thread: Eztrieve and comp-3 data? — 6 this month / 7 total

_Topics: [`Language features and syntax`](topics.md#syntax)_

### Eztrieve and comp-3 data?

- **From:** Steve Young <syoung@bcpl.net>
- **Date:** 1998-09-20T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<360496AB.ECF293C4@bcpl.net>`

```
I am trying to run an Eztrieve report program on a file that contains
comp-3 dates. The copybook lists it as an 8 byte comp-3 field. The date
contains no slashes so shouldn't it be listed as s (5) comp-3?   My
Eztrieve program defines the field as follows:
				eff-date     34    8   P    0
This gives me a data exception error on the report statement.
Should it be:   eff-date    34   5   N
or:             eff-date    34   8   N
or something else?
I've worked with conp-3 data in the past but the solution has eluded
this old brain.
```

#### ↳ Re: Eztrieve and comp-3 data?

- **From:** WOB@my-dejanews.com
- **Date:** 1998-09-20T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<6u2tfa$uc5$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>`
- **References:** `<360496AB.ECF293C4@bcpl.net>`

```
In article <360496AB.ECF293C4@bcpl.net>,
  Steve Young <syoung@bcpl.net> wrote:
> I am trying to run an Eztrieve report program on a file that contains
> comp-3 dates. The copybook lists it as an 8 byte comp-3 field. The date
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
>
Steve,

Eight (8) bytes packed is represented in COBOL as 9(15) COMP-3. Five (5)
bytes packed is 9(09) COMP-3. What is the format of the date, CCYYMMDD?
Remember, all valid packed-decimal data contains a sign, located in the
low-order nibble of the last byte. Also, all packed-decimal data is
right-justified with high-order zeros. I'm not familiar with the nuances of
EZTRIEVE, but the actual data must match the copybook definition of 8-Bytes
Packed. If it doesn't, then unpredictable results may occur (plagiarized IBM
statement). The other definition you included for data-type 'N' is probably
for DISPLAY-NUMERIC data, which is NOT packed.

HTH....

WOB

-----== Posted via Deja News, The Leader in Internet Discussion ==-----
http://www.dejanews.com/rg_mkgrp.xp   Create Your Own Free Member Forum
```

#### ↳ Re: Eztrieve and comp-3 data?

- **From:** hcarter@cluster.mcs.net (Harry Carter)
- **Date:** 1998-09-20T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3606137e.50076988@news.mcs.net>`
- **References:** `<360496AB.ECF293C4@bcpl.net>`

```
Steve Young <syoung@bcpl.net> wrote:

>I am trying to run an Eztrieve report program on a file that contains
>comp-3 dates. The copybook lists it as an 8 byte comp-3 field. The date
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
>this old brain.


By eight byte to you pic PIC 9(15) COMP-3 OR
PIC 9(8) COMP-3?

Anyway

PAK-VAR   PIC S9(15) COMP-3
equals  PAK-VAR 1  8 P 0 in easytrieve speak. 

1 means it starts at position 1 in the file, goes for a length of 8
((15 + 1) / 2) and has no decimal places (if you want Easytrieve to
handle the value as signed you must give the number of decimal places
even if it's 0 - Easytrieve assumes if you don't specify decimal
places that the value is unsigned)

If it was PAK-VAR PIC S9(13)V99 COMP-3 it would be
PAK-VAR 1 8 P 2 (the length always includes the number of decimal
places). 

Harry Carter
hcarter@cluster.mcs.net - finger for PGP key
Home Page: http://www.mcs.net/~hcarter

"Make no little plans; they have no magic to stir men's blood."
Daniel Burnham
```

#### ↳ Re: Eztrieve and comp-3 data?

- **From:** bruce.chris@hyder.com
- **Date:** 1998-09-22T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<6u7l2v$2ce$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>`
- **References:** `<360496AB.ECF293C4@bcpl.net>`

```
In article <360496AB.ECF293C4@bcpl.net>,
  Steve Young <syoung@bcpl.net> wrote:
> I am trying to run an Eztrieve report program on a file that contains
> comp-3 dates. The copybook lists it as an 8 byte comp-3 field. The date
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
>
hI,

If it is 8 bytes and not 8 digits then the definition should be

EFF-DATE    34    8    P

This will give you 15 digits in the 8 byte comp-3 field. Probably not a date
field?

The data type N that you used is generally known as external decimal, zoned
decimal, display decimal but not packed decimal/comp-3.

If its actually 8 digits then the definition should be

EFF-DATE    34    5    P

It's 5 bytes as packed decimal digit within a variable each take half a byte
plus half a byte for the sign, round (up) to whole number of bytes.

-----== Posted via Deja News, The Leader in Internet Discussion ==-----
http://www.dejanews.com/rg_mkgrp.xp   Create Your Own Free Member Forum
```

#### ↳ Re: Eztrieve and comp-3 data?

- **From:** Gilbert Berry <redbranch@earthlink.net>
- **Date:** 1998-09-25T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<360C00AD.2A588075@earthlink.net>`
- **References:** `<360496AB.ECF293C4@bcpl.net>`

```
Are you sure its a date, looks more like a timestamp as 8 P 0 will unpack
to 15 N 0.  Try moving it to a field that is 15 N 0 with mask
'999999999999' and display it to see what is actually there and how it is
formated.

Steve Young wrote:

> I am trying to run an Eztrieve report program on a file that contains
> comp-3 dates. The copybook lists it as an 8 byte comp-3 field. The date
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> this old brain.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Eztrieve and comp-3 data?

- **From:** "Grant Engelbrecht" <Grant_Englebrecht@compuware.com>
- **Date:** 1998-09-27T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<01bdea6f$673a8600$53011bac@g-englebrecht>`
- **References:** `<360496AB.ECF293C4@bcpl.net> <360C00AD.2A588075@earthlink.net>`

```
"The copybook lists it as an 8 byte comp-3 field"

your definition "eff-date     34    8   P    0" is for a field which has a
max length of 16 (bit big for a date).  Try "eff-date     34    4  P    0" 
which has a max Length of 8 (bit closer to what you want.

G

Gilbert Berry <redbranch@earthlink.net> wrote in article
<360C00AD.2A588075@earthlink.net>...
> Are you sure its a date, looks more like a timestamp as 8 P 0 will unpack
> to 15 N 0.  Try moving it to a field that is 15 N 0 with mask
…[17 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

---

<a id="t-be978d4c79"></a>
## Thread: A Y2K 'Fix' Story — 34 msgs

_Topics: [`Y2K and Year-2000 remediation`](topics.md#y2k)_

### Re: A Y2K 'Fix' Story

- **From:** The Goobers <docdwarf@erols.com>
- **Date:** 1998-09-20T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.software.year-2000,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3605B596.7678@erols.com>`
- **References:** `<3604FCED.4515@erols.com> <6u40p3$kjj@news-central.tiac.net>`

```
ddgsk@tic.net wrote:
> 
> x-no-archive: yes
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> the way it was in the first go-round?

Because the Charter of my Project was Y2K remediation; it was my
assigned job to make sure that the code functioned the same way *after*
31 Dec 1999 as it does now.  The newbie, in fact, *violated* said
Charter when he made the mod.  Truth be known I didn't even notice the
original error as it was part of a statement of equality (IF WS-YRMO =);
we had been instruced to leave such statements alone.

Now you may indeed ask 'which is better, to do what you have contracted
for and been specifically instructed to do or to do That Which Is
Right?'... and lo, to dwell upon such matters at great length is indeed
praisworthy.  Doing Y2K remediation is allotted x amount of time; doing
Y2K remediation *and* fixing broken programs is x + y amount of time. 
One can say 'But it was only one little line in one little program'...
but every program contains many lines; to start fixing things then
brings in what is called by some 'scope creep'... and alowing the scope
of a project to increase is a surefire way to miss a deadline... in this
case the deadline is 31 Dec 1999.

DD
```

#### ↳ Re: A Y2K 'Fix' Story

- **From:** Arnold Trembley <arnold.trembley@worldnet.att.net>
- **Date:** 1998-09-21T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.software.year-2000,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3605EFF7.5822EA11@worldnet.att.net>`
- **References:** `<3604FCED.4515@erols.com> <6u40p3$kjj@news-central.tiac.net> <3605B596.7678@erols.com>`

```
The Goobers wrote:
> 
> ddgsk@tic.net wrote:
…[25 more quoted lines elided]…
> DD

Okay, so the corrected code should be:

WS-YRMO  PIC 9(04) USAGE IS DISPLAY.
...
IF WS-YRMO(3:2) = '04' OR '06' OR '09' OR '11'
    MOVE 30 TO WS-DD
ELSE
IF WS-YRMO(3:2) = '02'
    MOVE 28 TO WS-DD
ELSE
    MOVE 31 TO WS-DD.

That wasn't so hard, was it?  Well, apparently it was.  A better fix
would include elimination of the reference modification and correction
of the February problem for leap years.

DD has a good point.  No question about it, "requirements creep" has
ruined many a project, and delayed countless others.  As long as you're
already working on it, could we have additional reports by region?  I
know implementation is only two months away, but could you add support
for foreign currencies as well?  Can you fix it so the column of
percentages totals to exactly 100.0%?  For some reason that never seems
to come out right.

I tend to disagree (but just a little bit).  There's a certain amount of
overhead to breaking open a program to change it.  If a simple fix to an
obvious defect won't add appreciable time to the overall project then it
ought to be considered.  If it's not going to delay the project, then I
would argue that it should be fixed.  I don't know if DD's specific
example would have created a significant amount of additional testing.
```

```
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…

Bill Lynch
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: A Y2K 'Fix' Story

- **From:** Mickey <mickeyb@nospam.home.com>
- **Date:** 1998-09-21T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.software.year-2000,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36066873.12744EA1@nospam.home.com>`
- **References:** `<3604FCED.4515@erols.com> <6u40p3$kjj@news-central.tiac.net> <3605B596.7678@erols.com> <3605EFF7.5822EA11@worldnet.att.net> <36061DF7.A89E6ACD@att.net>`

```
Bill Lynch wrote:
> 
> Arnold Trembley wrote:
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
> 

Mickey's first law of migrations/conversions. NEVER change anything not
germain to the conversion.

Mickey's second law of migrations/conversions. NEVER change any logic.
The moment you do, you buy all the 	previously existing bugs.

Mickey's third law of migrations/conversions. ALWAYS lock down
(protected datasets and such) ANY common module 	or copybook. The moment
you don't, 73 coders will modify it so that it works with THEIR program.

Mickey's fourth law of migrations/conversions. KNOW thy scope: or, be
prepared to paint the restrooms.

Mickey's fifth law of migrations/conversions. The customer provided
inventory represents 80% of the total code 	to be migrated/converted.

Mickey's sixth law of migrations/conversions. It is the already
compliant system that will present the real 	challenge.

Mickey's seventh law of migrations/conversions. 90% of the code will
take 10% of the effgot, and the remaining 	10%  of the code will take
150% of the effort.


mickey (conversions are still worse than migrations)
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: A Y2K 'Fix' Story

- **From:** The Goobers <docdwarf@erols.com>
- **Date:** 1998-09-21T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.software.year-2000,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36062786.6A9B@erols.com>`
- **References:** `<3604FCED.4515@erols.com> <6u40p3$kjj@news-central.tiac.net> <3605B596.7678@erols.com> <3605EFF7.5822EA11@worldnet.att.net>`

```
Arnold Trembley wrote:

[snippage]

> I tend to disagree (but just a little bit).  There's a certain amount of
> overhead to breaking open a program to change it.  If a simple fix to an
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> example would have created a significant amount of additional testing.

Ahhhh, the old 'Camel's Nose' argument, sure... I do not know what is
meant by 'significant' overhead but in this case it added a few
(unplanned) days to testing the jobstream... y'see, it started with an
IEBGENER to create an &TEMP file... which was sorted and processed into
another &TEMP file... and processed and sorted... each step
insignificant to test, of course...

... and then camel's head comes into the tent... and the neck... and the
shoulders...

... requirements creep, as you mentioned.  'Say, while you're at it
could you re-sort this sales report by descending barometric pressure? 
I know it'll throw the control breaks off but all ya gotta do is... '

NO REQUIREMENTS CREEP!  *Especially* when, in this case you have a crew
of outside consultants/contractors working on code which nobody
understands... it takes time to figure out what the stuff does (versus
what 'it is supposed to do', of course), as well.

DD
```

```
The Goobers  <docdwarf@erols.com> wrote:
}Arnold Trembley wrote:
}> I tend to disagree (but just a little bit).  There's a certain amount of
}> overhead to breaking open a program to change it.  If a simple fix to an
}> obvious defect won't add appreciable time to the overall project then it
}> ought to be considered.  If it's not going to delay the project, then I
}> would argue that it should be fixed.  I don't know if DD's specific
}> example would have created a significant amount of additional testing.

}Ahhhh, the old 'Camel's Nose' argument, sure... 
}... and then camel's head comes into the tent... and the neck... and the
}shoulders...

}NO REQUIREMENTS CREEP!  *Especially* when, in this case you have a crew
}of outside consultants/contractors working on code which nobody
}understands... it takes time to figure out what the stuff does (versus
}what 'it is supposed to do', of course), as well.

   Absolutely.
   You are going to find all sorts of abominations
   -- if you start spending just a few minutes here
   and there on even a fraction of them you are
   doomed -- leave 'em.  If they want it, you
   could just keep a logbook[1] of them for them
   to look at later:

      Framitz System
          frobozz.cob
             procedure mung
                 line 317
                     + suspicious compare of WS-YRMO
                     + ignores leap year?

   Unless you've been tasked/hired for more than y2k
   remediation [in which case, run away], your job is:
     a) after you get done it should work the same in pre-00 time
     b) after you get done it should work sanely in post-00 time

John
[1] I sometime wonder if it wouldn't be money-ahead to
    supply someone to do the clerical crap for top
    dollar talent -- silly to have $200 people doing
    $10 clerking -- esp. when they are in short supply
    and the deadline is real fixed.  Maybe places are
    doing this, but I haven't seen it.  Of course, this
    whole deal is a giant pennywise-poundfoolish case
    study...
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: A Y2K 'Fix' Story

- **From:** Arnold Trembley <arnold.trembley@worldnet.att.net>
- **Date:** 1998-09-21T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.software.year-2000,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36062CF0.1F9BDB5B@worldnet.att.net>`
- **References:** `<3604FCED.4515@erols.com> <6u40p3$kjj@news-central.tiac.net> <3605B596.7678@erols.com> <3605EFF7.5822EA11@worldnet.att.net> <36062786.6A9B@erols.com>`

```
The Goobers wrote:
>  
> ... and then camel's head comes into the tent... and the neck... and the
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
> DD

Okay, if I'm working this project with you and Bill Lynch and Lan-Lord
then I'm outvoted.  No additional changes.

What we do in situations like this is make a note of it and add it to
the production support list for a future release or an out-of-release
fix (so as not to jeopardize the target date).  But I've also seen
things like this go the other way, where the Quality Assurance group
actually finds a this kind of defect in testing and holds up your
implementation until it's resolved.  The resolution might still be
"pre-existing defect - fix in a future release".

If you can't find time to do it right, when are you going to find time
to do it over?

If the users are already living with dates of April 31 and February 31,
who am I to complain?
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: A Y2K 'Fix' Story

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** docdwarf@clark.net ()
- **Date:** 1998-09-21T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.software.year-2000,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<6u5ng7$nvo$1@callisto.clark.net>`
- **References:** `<3604FCED.4515@erols.com> <3605EFF7.5822EA11@worldnet.att.net> <36062786.6A9B@erols.com> <36062CF0.1F9BDB5B@worldnet.att.net>`

```
In article <36062CF0.1F9BDB5B@worldnet.att.net>,
Arnold Trembley  <arnold.trembley@worldnet.att.net> wrote:
>The Goobers wrote:
>>  
…[15 more quoted lines elided]…
>then I'm outvoted.  No additional changes.

Weeelllll... at least none you get *caught* at.  All statements of
absolute certainty are worthless... including this one.

>
>What we do in situations like this is make a note of it and add it to
>the production support list for a future release or an out-of-release
>fix (so as not to jeopardize the target date).

'We'?  Plural majestatus est, as is said in Spanish... the system I am
working on is the Orphan System; not only does *nobody* want anything to
do with it but those of us who *do* work on it receive, in response to our
queries, a mixture of sympathy and scorn: 'Oh, you're working on the XYZ
system?  *Nobody* knows *nothin'* about that one, they all quit or died...
you poor (common term for one of illegitimate birth)... but that's why
*you* get paid the Big Bucks, right?  Hyuk hyuk hyuk!'

> But I've also seen
>things like this go the other way, where the Quality Assurance group
>actually finds a this kind of defect in testing and holds up your
>implementation until it's resolved.

As I know *nothing* about QA on this site I cannot say, one way or the
other... but hey, the code made it through back in '88, no?

> The resolution might still be
>"pre-existing defect - fix in a future release".
>
>If you can't find time to do it right, when are you going to find time
>to do it over?

When someone else, with appropriate authority, allocates time and budget
to fix it; 'finding time' is not only Not My Job but it is a Job Which I
have Steadfastly Refused... remember:

'The allocation, co-ordination and motivation of personnel and resources
towards the accomplishment of a stated Executive Goal is the
responsibility of (all together now, gang!) MANAGEMENT.'

>
>If the users are already living with dates of April 31 and February 31,
>who am I to complain?

Back to the Law of Well-Functioning Systems again, aye... chilling, ain't
it?

DD
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: A Y2K 'Fix' Story

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "Jeff Kennedy, Senior System Programmer" <jakcert@ibm.net>
- **Date:** 1998-09-21T00:00:00

- **References:** `<3604FCED.4515@erols.com> <6u40p3$kjj@news-central.tiac.net> <3605B596.7678@erols.com> <3605EFF7.5822EA11@worldnet.att.net> <36062786.6A9B@erols.com> <36062CF0.1F9BDB5B@worldnet.att.net> <Jl0PnHJ5PvPd-pn2-LhcUMwEXkHpe@Dwight_Miller.iix.com>`

```
Thane Hubbell wrote:
> 
> On Mon, 21 Sep 1998 10:39:51, Arnold Trembley
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
> to have each month with 31 days.


I think I posed this question last year or before.  I had asked if
anyone was tempted to do a little tweaking while they were in doing
remediation.I got some interesting email replies but the general feeling
I got was to leave the code alone except for date fixes.  Getting
sidetracked fixing bad/poorly written code is a trap that will prove
fatal in the long run.  I think you have to leave the program alone
except for remediation.  Some of these errors should be caught in
testing.  
  I think it was Arnold T. who said he had run into the same temptation
and warned me against trying to do too much when my nose was burried in
code. 
  I think you were right on to leave the code alone whether you noticed
the problem or not.

Pat Hall
```

```
One of the things that I am seeing happening with Y2K projects, which I 
have NOT seen before, is that the milestones and deadlines are being 
identified much more aggressively and being dealt with much moire 
honestly. In my project, we moved heaven and earth to keep to the time 
frames and have done just that. This project will end on time and under 
budget. I am seeing more and more of that also.

It is the oldest cliche to talk about projects that never finish but I 
see real differences in the Y2K work, which is why I think we will be 
better off than some other folks around here.

Present company excluded, of course.<smile>
```

```
I was working next to a guy recently (we shared a cubicle) who was
fired for various reasons, one of which was he kept arguing with the
project lead that we should fix the code when we saw potential
problems. The project lead kept saying 'It is our mandate to fix Y2K
problems. Anything else is for the support people'.

I have seen MANY instances where I could improve/fix/repair/tweak a
program. My job was to remediate. If I saw something that would cause
an obvious problem, I would let the support people know.

Bob Tyler...
```

#### ↳ Re: A Y2K 'Fix' Story

- **From:** "Art Perry" <arthur.perry@eds88.com>
- **Date:** 1998-09-21T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.software.year-2000,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<6u5qll$ml$1@news.ses.cio.eds.com>`
- **References:** `<3604FCED.4515@erols.com> <6u40p3$kjj@news-central.tiac.net> <3605B596.7678@erols.com>`

```
The Goobers wrote in message <3605B596.7678@erols.com>...
>Because the Charter of my Project was Y2K remediation; it was my
>assigned job to make sure that the code functioned the same way *after*
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>we had been instruced to leave such statements alone.

Mister DD,

Please help me to understand something because I am confused.  The check for
equality compares a 4 digit YRMO against a single digit numeric literal.
Your example data for YRMO (for the year 2000) was 0004, 0006, etc.

I assume that data for September, 1998 would be 9809, which would *fail* the
equality check, as all months would do for all the years that this code has
been running, and yield the value 31.  But in the year 2000, the values
0004, 0006 would *pass* the equality check, yielding different values for
different months.  This code would *not* function the same way after 31 Dec
1999 as it has been all these years.

In this respect, wouldn't it now be part of the scope of your remediation?
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: A Y2K 'Fix' Story

- **From:** docdwarf@clark.net ()
- **Date:** 1998-09-21T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.software.year-2000,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<6u5tkq$mlj$1@clarknet.clark.net>`
- **References:** `<3604FCED.4515@erols.com> <6u40p3$kjj@news-central.tiac.net> <3605B596.7678@erols.com> <6u5qll$ml$1@news.ses.cio.eds.com>`

```
In article <6u5qll$ml$1@news.ses.cio.eds.com>,
Art Perry <arthur.perry@eds88.com> wrote:
>The Goobers wrote in message <3605B596.7678@erols.com>...
>>Because the Charter of my Project was Y2K remediation; it was my
…[19 more quoted lines elided]…
>In this respect, wouldn't it now be part of the scope of your remediation?

In a word... nope!  As noted above (and in previous postings) our
instructions were to leave comparisons of equality alone; this translates
into 'don't touch statements containing only '=''.  Since the statement in
question was one of equality, *even though it was incorrectly coded a
decade ago*, it was a statement of equality and therefor outside the scope
of the remediations we had been chartered to perform.

DD
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: A Y2K 'Fix' Story

- **From:** "Art Perry" <arthur.perry@eds88.com>
- **Date:** 1998-09-21T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.software.year-2000,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<6u6a7j$9q3$1@news.ses.cio.eds.com>`
- **References:** `<3604FCED.4515@erols.com> <6u40p3$kjj@news-central.tiac.net> <3605B596.7678@erols.com> <6u5qll$ml$1@news.ses.cio.eds.com> <6u5tkq$mlj$1@clarknet.clark.net>`

```
docdwarf@clark.net wrote in message <6u5tkq$mlj$1@clarknet.clark.net>...
>In article <6u5qll$ml$1@news.ses.cio.eds.com>,
>Art Perry <arthur.perry@eds88.com> wrote:
>>The Goobers wrote in message <3605B596.7678@erols.com>...
[snip
>>>we had been instruced to leave such statements alone.
[snip]
>>In this respect, wouldn't it now be part of the scope of your remediation?
>
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>of the remediations we had been chartered to perform.

Sorry, I didn't realized what you meant by "instruced"  (just kidding
)   )

I see your point, but is there a specific reason why you ignore statements
of equality?  I assume that you do not ignore comparisons of greater
than/less than and the rest.  Why is this?  As I mentioned before, this
program will work differently after 31 Dec 1999 than before, the kicker
being that it  will actually work correctly rather than incorrectly.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: A Y2K 'Fix' Story

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** The Goobers <docdwarf@erols.com>
- **Date:** 1998-09-21T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.software.year-2000,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36070336.711D@erols.com>`
- **References:** `<3604FCED.4515@erols.com> <6u40p3$kjj@news-central.tiac.net> <3605B596.7678@erols.com> <6u5qll$ml$1@news.ses.cio.eds.com> <6u5tkq$mlj$1@clarknet.clark.net> <6u6a7j$9q3$1@news.ses.cio.eds.com>`

```
Art Perry wrote:
> 
> docdwarf@clark.net wrote in message <6u5tkq$mlj$1@clarknet.clark.net>...
…[20 more quoted lines elided]…
> than/less than and the rest.  Why is this?

First and foremost... because He Who Signs My Timesheet says so. 
Secondly... the problem with > and < statements is that they show false
under two digits:

IF 99 > 98
    PERFORM something.

IF 00 > 99
    PERFORM something.

... in the first case 'something' will be PERFORMed (executed), in the
second, not... compare to:

IF 1999 > 1998
    PERFORM something.

IF 2000 > 1999
    PERFORM something.

... in which case 'something' gets PERFORMed in *both* instances.

This is not a problem with statements of equality:

IF 98 = 98

IF 99 = 99

IF 00 = 00

... all the same.
    
> As I mentioned before, this
> program will work differently after 31 Dec 1999 than before, the kicker
> being that it  will actually work correctly rather than incorrectly.

Ahhhh... but the incorrect performance that it manifests in February,
April, June etc. of 2000 (0002, 0004, 0006) are *not* the result of an
inability to recognise and deal with dates past 1 Jan 2000 and as a
result such incorrect performance is not within the scope of
remediation.

DD
```

```
mickey
```

```
The Goobers  <docdwarf@erols.com> wrote:
}> I see your point, but is there a specific reason why you ignore statements
}> of equality?  I assume that you do not ignore comparisons of greater
}> than/less than and the rest.  Why is this?
}
}First and foremost... because He Who Signs My Timesheet says so. 
}Secondly... the problem with > and < statements is that they show false
}under two digits:
}This is not a problem with statements of equality:
}IF 98 = 98
}IF 99 = 99
}IF 00 = 00
}... all the same.

  It seems to me that it is possible to contrive
  an equality test that breaks in 2000:

     foo = year - 1900;
     bar = year % 100;                       /* '%' is modulo */
     if (foo == bar) ...        /* breaks outside of 1900-1999 */

  [Sorry, I'd do it in Cobol if I could rub enough synapses
   together at this hour to remember how to do modulus in it]

John
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: A Y2K 'Fix' Story

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** john@iastate.edu (John Hascall)
- **Date:** 1998-09-23T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.software.year-2000,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<6ubbfb$11g$1@news.iastate.edu>`
- **References:** `<3604FCED.4515@erols.com> <36070336.711D@erols.com> <6u71a3$6et$1@news.iastate.edu> <36077CAA.2@erols.com>`

```
The Goobers  <docdwarf@erols.com> wrote:
}John Hascall wrote:
}> The Goobers  <docdwarf@erols.com> wrote:
}> }First and foremost... because He Who Signs My Timesheet says so.
}> }Secondly... the problem with > and < statements is that they show false
}> }under two digits:
}> }This is not a problem with statements of equality:
}> }IF 98 = 98
}> }IF 99 = 99
}> }IF 00 = 00
}> }... all the same.
}> 
}>   It seems to me that it is possible to contrive
}>   an equality test that breaks in 2000:
}>      foo = year - 1900;
}>      bar = year % 100;                       /* '%' is modulo */
}>      if (foo == bar) ...        /* breaks outside of 1900-1999 */

}Mr Hascall, the key word in this example is 'contrive'...

    Perhaps, in my years I've seen stuff a lot worse.

    At least under Unix, since tm.tm_year is (lamely, IMO) defined as
    years since 1900, there is plenty of reason for doing stuff like:

        tm.tm_year = year - 1900;

    and people certainly do use just that last 2 digits of the
    year (that should be self-evident), so seeing something like:

        yy = year % 100;

    should come as no surprise (whether using modulo or some
    other extraction method).

    So perhaps:

       if (tm.tm_year == yy) ...

    or its ilk is not so impossible.


    In my experience, when someone tells you "don't bother looking
    at X, there can't be any problems there", they are almost
    always wrong.  Of course, when they are paying, being wrong
    is their perogative.

John
```

## Thread: crtest.cob — 3 msgs

### crtest.cob

- **From:** skidmike <skidmike@mindspring.com>
- **Date:** 1998-09-20T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36059452.B75A5C2B@mindspring.com>`

```
thanks to mr hubbell, i chopped a lot of go to's from my original source	
thought i'd post the new source for some feedback
```

#### ↳ Re: crtest.cob

- **From:** riplin@kcbbs.gen.nz (Richard Plinston)
- **Date:** 1998-09-21T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3298263.67956.14978@kcbbs.gen.nz>`
- **References:** `<36059452.B75A5C2B@mindspring.com>`

```
In message <<36059452.B75A5C2B@mindspring.com>> skidmike <skidmike@mindspring.com> writes:
> 
> thanks to mr hubbell, i chopped a lot of go to's from my original source 
> thought i'd post the new source for some feedback

Changing a 'GO TO F4-TERM' which does an EXIT PROGRAM into
a 'PERFORM F4-TERM' which does an EXIT PROGRAM doesn't make
the program 'more structured', it simply disguises the
GO TO as a PERFORM-that-will-never-return.  This is a _bad_
thing because the next programmer along will expect
PERFORMs to return.
```

#### ↳ Re: crtest.cob

- **From:** riplin@kcbbs.gen.nz (Richard Plinston)
- **Date:** 1998-09-21T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3298263.68298.15194@kcbbs.gen.nz>`
- **References:** `<36059452.B75A5C2B@mindspring.com>`

```

> 000630 
> 000640 PROCEDURE DIVISION USING EST-INFO-LINK.
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
> 000673     MOVE 5 TO LINE-VALUE.
              PERFORM A000000
                  UNTIL Key-Code = 4

              EXIT PROGRAM
              .
> 000674
> 000675 A00000.    
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> 000702                 PERFORM F12-TERM-ROUTINE
                          ^change to MOVE 4 TO Key-Code perhaps

plus remove the END-PERFORM and PERFORM F4- and the end of 
A000000.
```

## Thread: Setting path to runtime files in Fujitsu Cobol 4 — 3 msgs

_Topics: [`Compilers and vendors`](topics.md#compilers)_

### Setting path to runtime files in Fujitsu Cobol 4

- **From:** sweathe1@nycap.rr.com (Steven Weatherwax)
- **Date:** 1998-09-20T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<360584ee.20053234@news>`

```

I just received Fujitsu Cobol 4 and am trying to run through the
sample programs. I can build the samples correctly, however when I try
to run the .EXE I get an error starting the program that tells me
Sample1 is linked to a missing export (F3BIPRCT.DLL). 

If the .EXE file is located in the same folder as the runtime files
there is no problem.  How do I tell the compiler where to look for the
runtime files?

Thanks.
```

#### ↳ Re: Setting path to runtime files in Fujitsu Cobol 4

- **From:** "Leif Svalgaard" <leif@ibm.net>
- **Date:** 1998-09-20T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36059908.0@news1.ibm.net>`
- **References:** `<360584ee.20053234@news>`

```

Steven Weatherwax wrote in message <360584ee.20053234@news>...
>
>I just received Fujitsu Cobol 4 and am trying to run through the
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>runtime files?


set lib=fujitsu dir;your dir1;your dir2    etc.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Setting path to runtime files in Fujitsu Cobol 4

- **From:** sweathe1@nycap.rr.com (Steven Weatherwax)
- **Date:** 1998-09-21T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3606dda6.6081604@news>`
- **References:** `<360584ee.20053234@news> <36059908.0@news1.ibm.net>`

```
Thanks for the advice, however I get the error message prior to being
able to enter environment variables.  

I was able to fix the problem by uninstalling the compiler from my D:
drive and re-installing it on the C: drive.  Evidently the compiler
will look only in this drive for the run time files.

wrote:
```

## Thread: accept/display with NetExpress — 4 msgs

_Topics: [`Language features and syntax`](topics.md#syntax)_

### accept/display with NetExpress

- **From:** Charles Goodman <cgoodman@mbnet.mb.ca>
- **Date:** 1998-09-20T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<360514D1.4EE17E0A@mbnet.mb.ca>`

```
I have a bunch of application programs that I use accept and display.  I
understand that I can compile with NetExpress to run natively as 32 bit
code under Win95 or NT.  They will appear in a square box on the screen,
similar to a MS-DOS box but native under windows.

Please correct me if I am misinformed.

These programs currently run under MS-DOS compiled with old MF compiler
3.??.  They use large screens, by doing MS-DOS MODE command to set the
display with LINES= and COLS=.  Most simply use COLS=80 LINES=43.  Some
use wide screend by using some old SVGA utility that works like the MODE
statement.  Of course the COBSW is set to allow large screens.

My question...

Are such large text displays supported under the NetExpress native 32
bit environment?

Thanks in advance

Charlie Goodman

PS  Yes I know I could convert to GUI but would rather just recomplie.
```

```
DESCRIPTION
To gather and analyze information for developing and modifying data processing
systems and programs, prepare system and program specifications and record
layouts. To design and code the most complex programs.

Principal Responsibilities:
1. Define requirements for improving or replacing systems.
2. Perform interviews and other data gathering.
3. Document current systems operations.
4. Prepare specifications for systems improvements.
5. Develop systems testing and conversion plans.
6. Develop accurate and efficient computer programs.
7. Modify existing programs.
8. Test Programs thoroughly.
9. Provide guidance to and assist in training less
   experienced Information Systems personnel.

Bachelors degree Computer Science or related.
Minimum 5 years experience programming in COBOL, CICS, JCL, and DB2.

Training in systems analysis with special emphasis in user relations, data
gathering techniques, written and oral communications, and management
information systems.

Ability to work effectively under deadlines and extreme workloads. Competence
in fulfilling any additional responsibilities which may be assigned.

CONTACT:  Ed Billion
          Digital Careers, Inc.
          edbillion@digcareers.com
          (714) 992-6145 Fax
          (714) 992-6144 Phone
```

## Thread: Entry-Level Advice — 5 msgs

### Entry-Level Advice

- **From:** housej5@gate.net
- **Date:** 1998-09-20T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3604AB91.6E1EB845@gate.net>`

```
Hello All,
    I was wondering if any of you veterans could give some advice to a
begginner to this cobol language. I am trying to get an Entry-Level
position in cobol programming. I thought I might have a little better
chance since we did all of our work at USF on their IBM Mainframe. We
were also introduced to a little VSAM, DB2 and JCL. I would really like
t o start a job in what I went  to class for. A couple consultants have
told me about a help desk position. Should I go for that until I can get
a programming position ?
  Thanks,
             J.House Jr.

-- You can view my resume here at;
http://www.gate.net/~housej5/resume.html
```

#### ↳ Re: Entry-Level Advice

- **From:** rkrayhawk@aol.com (RKRayhawk)
- **Date:** 1998-09-20T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<19980920113752.06733.00002562@ng-fa2.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<3604AB91.6E1EB845@gate.net>`

```


If you have exposure to the IBM mainframes and any training at all on "...
VSAM, DB2 and JCL....", then you are much more valuable in the market place
than you may currently imagine.  Do not go for a helpdesk job. Go for a coders
job.

If one may be so bold as to interpret 'USF' as the Uuniversity of San
Francisco, and assume that that is you current location, then you are in luck
for sure.  Demand for your skill set is high.  Look to the banks or the large
consulting firms advertising in your paper.

Be aware that jobs relating to Y2K may have a short-term duration (perhaps 24
more months considering repairs and support early in year 2000), and that may
not be what you want, although the pay is good.

If you are starting your career, or making a career change, you may actually
want to select the job that has better long term prospects.  Y2K work is
drudgery, and unthinking maintenance.  For long term security you may do best
if you get involved as a junior programmer now in either a DEVELOPMENT project
or MAJOR ENHANCEMENT project, that will engage all of your skills in creating
and perfecting full programs.

Your should prioritize getting DB2 experience! Combining that with COBOL will
make you more valuable when you remarket your self later.  Knowledge of SQL
will also permit you to broaden your skill base to other language disciplines
easily.

Do not avoid COBOL+SQL opportunities on other platforms. In other words, do not
sell yourself short. You are right to think that the IBM mainframe exposure
gives you a leg up in that sector of the market, but desktops are hot and will
be hot.  There are many variations on this, IBM AIX COBOL DB2, for example.

Surely you should try to get top dollar now.
But bare in mind there is a possibility that many shops will disgorge a lot of
experienced programmers in 18 to 24 months as the Y2K thing settles down. The
market will then be seeking folks to do development work, and that is the thing
you will want experience with.

Companies also like people with application knowledge. Try to go where your
strenghts are. Go towards companies that are involved in areas where you have
prior experience with the substance of what they do, or where you have strong
personal interests. 

Try to find a company which will put you in a position to learn about there
business, not just programming, and commit your self to that business activity.
Such an arrangement will make you a bit more secure on the down slope of the
current employment demand bulge.










Robert Rayhawk
RKRayhawk@aol.com
```

<a id="t-ef4e078330"></a>
## Thread: A Y2K 'Fix' Story — 8 msgs

_Topics: [`Y2K and Year-2000 remediation`](topics.md#y2k)_

### A Y2K 'Fix' Story

- **From:** The Goobers <docdwarf@erols.com>
- **Date:** 1998-09-20T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.software.year-2000,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3604FCED.4515@erols.com>`

```
Well, been a while since I posted anything technical... so here goes:

As some of you might have suspected I'm a COBOL jockey, working on Y2K
fixes for a utility... ugly, smell, dull, dull, DULL work, sifting
through code old enough to vote and applying unimaginative fixes to it. 
Pays the bills, though.

Anyhow... the working routine is such that when a programmer starts
remediating a chunk o' code the first thing that's done is to rund said
code through a scanning utility, looking for certain combinations of
characters... DATE, YEAR, MONTH, YY and the like.  If hits show up (yes,
this does *not* work for 'cutesie-poo programmer names' like LUIGI,
ELIZABETH or EVE-OF-DESTRUCTION) the code is examined to see what *kind*
of manipulations these fields are subject to; if an unequal comparison
is made (greater or less than) then Y2K mods must be made but equality
is ignored... 00 = 00, usually.

Well, a bunch o' months back I checked through a program (let's call it
PATTY) and the only hit I got was on a field called WS-YRMO being
checked for equality... IF WS-YRMO = quack quack.  I ignored it, checked
the rest of the programs in the jobstream, made my mods, tested the
jobstream against Production data and outputs and all was good.

SIDE NOTE:  Testing consisted of remediating all programs in a
jobstream, running the job against Prod data (as mentioned above) and
then running file-compares of the output of the remediated jobstream
against the present Prod output, 'ageing' the files with a utility I had
written which adds a few years to the dates in the files, re-running the
remediated code agains the aged data and comparing the output with the
dates masked out... in other words, if I know a record of 80 characters
contains a six-digit date in positions 10 - 15 I use parameter input to
the file-compare utility (SUPERCE) to check positions 1 - 9, skip 10 -
15 and check 16 - 80.  The reasons for this should be obvious.

This was months and months ago... last week another programmer doing
testing came to me and said 'You know program PATTY you modified a while
back?  It isn't working, fix it.'  I checked the output she was getting
and sure enough, PATTY was throwing bad data... how was this happening,
I hadn't modified the code?  I checked more deeply and found...

... that in the intervening months *another* programmer, a rather junior
lad (but *very* enthusiastic and earnest!), had been given a different
jobstream to remediate, one of the steps of which invoked PATTY.  He did
a source-scan on PATTY and latched on to the statement I had skipped. 
(Now things get technical)  He found that WS-YRMO was a four-digit field
defined as a numeric:

05    WS-YRMO  PIC 9(4).

... and that the comparison being done, while being an equivalency test,
was... inaccurate.  The code was looking to force the date to month-end;
with that in mind the programmer had written, during the last mods done
in 1988:

IF WS-YRMO = 4 OR 6 OR 9 OR 11
    MOVE 30 TO WS-DD
ELSE
IF WS-YRMO = 2
    MOVE 28 TO WS-DD
ELSE
    MOVE 31 TO WS-DD.

Now, ignoring the utter lack of leap-year checking for the value of 2...
WS-YRMO is a *four*-digit field.  The first comparison would be true
only for the months of April, June, September and November in the year
2000 (0004, 0006, etc.), the second one only for February, 2000... so
the program had been defaulting to the last value...

... and ever since 1988... in other words, for nigh a decade... this
program had been saying that *every* month has 31 days.  For nigh a
decade it had been spewing out Bad Data.

This young programmer, full of urine and acetic acid, then coded a
change... it has to be *right*, don't you know!  He made use of a
feature of COBOL II called 'reference modification'; since he knew that
the month began in position 3 of WS-YRMO and had a length of 2
characters he changed the code to read:

IF WS-YRMO(3:2) = 4 OR 6 OR 9 OR 11
    MOVE 30 TO WS-DD
ELSE
IF WS-YRMO(3:2) = 2
    MOVE 28 TO WS-DD
ELSE
    MOVE 31 TO WS-DD.

There, isn't that nice?

NO!  Reference modification treats fields as *alphabetic*, not
numeric... so that on one side of the equation you have an alpha of two
characters (WS-YRMO(3:2)) being compared for equality (=) to, for the
most part, *single-digit* numerics (4, 6, etc.)... and the months were
*still* being forced to 31...

... *EXCEPT* for the month of November, because the 11 in the comparison
*also* has two digits... and *that* was where the tester found the
errors... because when she tested the output and found discrepancies she
saw that *all* months had 31 days *except* for November, which had 30.

After finding out this lovely state of affairs the tester and I went to
our Group Leader and explained the matters... and he said 'Oh yeah, I
remember a couple'a months back ol' Junior came to me and said that he
had found a mistake which had slipped past you (pointing to *me*)... but
that he'd corrected it and I didn't think much of it after that.'

DD
```

#### ↳ Re: A Y2K 'Fix' Story

- **From:** "kennet" <kennet@post11.tele.dk>
- **Date:** 1998-09-20T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.software.year-2000,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<6u3a8m$5k5a$1@news-inn.inet.tele.dk>`
- **References:** `<3604FCED.4515@erols.com>`

```
But, in fact, the program *was* wrong all along defaulting to 31 days pr.
month. Did noone notice this, or were they perhaps even using this?

By the way, I didn't even know that you could use notation such as
WS-YRMO(3:2)  when WS-YRMO isn't explicitly declared as pic x. Just never
tried it...

Kennet

The Goobers skrev i meddelelsen <3604FCED.4515@erols.com>...

>
…[103 more quoted lines elided]…
>DD
```
