##### ↳ ↳ Re: how to determine string length ?

- **From:** bgwillia@vcu.edu (Boyce G. Williams, Jr.)
- **Date:** 1998-09-25T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<360b936f.160138985@usenet.acw.vcu.edu>`
- **References:** `<36075d20.4540031@news.iaehv.nl> <6u8a2n$bql$1@news.igs.net>`

```
"Donald Tees" <donald@willmack.com> wrote:

>
>Jarl Wijman wrote in message <36075d20.4540031@news.iaehv.nl>...
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>
That's refering to the field's maximium length.  What the original
poster wanted to know is the length of a string that is short of the
fieldlength.

From Gary DeWard Brown "Advanced ANSI COBOL with Structured
Programming", page 381:

01 SB-DEF.
	05  S-B-L  PIC 9(9) BINARY.
	05  S-B    PIC X(30).

77  T-NUM      PIC 9(9) BINARY.

MOVE 14 TO S-B-L.
MOVE "  124THE7890  " TO S-B.
PERFORM WITH TEST BEFORE
	VARYING T-NUM FROM S-B-L BY -1
	UNTIL S-B(T-NUM:1) IS NOT EQUAL TO SPACES
	   OR T-NUM IS EQUAL TO ZEROS
END-PERFORM.

T-NUM should equal to 12.

Hope this helps,

Boyce G. Williams, Jr.

 .--------------------------------------------------------------------.
 | "People should have two virtues:  purpose- the courage to envisage |
 | and pursue valued goals uninhibited by the defeat of infantile     |
 | fantasies, by guilt and the failing fear punishment;  and wisdom- a|
 | detached concern with life itself, in the face of death itself."   |
 |                                                     Norman F. Dixon|
 '--------------------------------------------------------------------'
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: how to determine string length ?

- **From:** riplin@kcbbs.gen.nz (Richard Plinston)
- **Date:** 1998-09-26T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3298268.68512.1178@kcbbs.gen.nz>`
- **References:** `<360b936f.160138985@usenet.acw.vcu.edu>`

```
In message <<360b936f.160138985@usenet.acw.vcu.edu>> bgwillia@vcu.edu  writes:
> 
> From Gary DeWard Brown "Advanced ANSI COBOL with Structured
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>     OR T-NUM IS EQUAL TO ZEROS

Even the 'experts' get it wrong then.  The two tests should
be reversed because Cobol will test the condition from
left to right.  When T-Num is zero it will give a bound
error in S-B before checking for zero.

It _will_ work OK with:

     UNTIL T-Num = ZERO
        OR S-B(T-Num:1) NOT = SPACES

because it only needs to find that T-Num is true to know
that it does not need to continue evaluation:
true OR anything -> true
```

#### ↳ Re: how to determine string length ?

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc123@bellsouth.net>
- **Date:** 1998-09-22T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<LwON1.5$OX1.20289@news4.mia.bellsouth.net>`
- **References:** `<36075d20.4540031@news.iaehv.nl>`

```
Jarl Wijman wrote:
>
>how can i determine the string length of a variable in Cobol ?
>The variable i'm using contains either a 9 or 20 digit code,
>how can i determine this ?


In general, to determine the length of any string, you can do this:

       IDENTIFICATION DIVISION.
       ENVIRONMENT DIVISION.
       CONFIGURATION SECTION.
       DATA DIVISION.
       WORKING-STORAGE SECTION.
       01  STRING-WORK-AREA.
           03  SW-SIZE                     PIC  9(02) COMP.
           03  SW-STRING                   PIC  X(20).
       PROCEDURE DIVISION.
       DO-IT-TO-IT.
           DISPLAY "ENTER STRING".
           ACCEPT SW-STRING.

           IF (SW-STRING = SPACES)
               MOVE 0 TO SW-SIZE
           ELSE
               MOVE FUNCTION LENGTH(SW-STRING) TO SW-SIZE
               PERFORM UNTIL SW-STRING(SW-SIZE:1) NOT = SPACE
                   SUBTRACT 1 FROM SW-SIZE
               END-PERFORM
           END-IF.

           DISPLAY SW-SIZE " CHARACTERS".


More specifically, if you *know* that your code is always 9 or 20
characters long, you can do this:

       01  STRING-WORK-AREA.
           03  SW-SIZE                     PIC  9(02) COMP.
           03  SW-STRING.
               05      FILLER              PIC  X(09).
               05  SW-STRING-20            PIC  X(11).

           IF (SW-STRING = SPACES)
               MOVE  0 TO SW-SIZE
           ELSE IF (SW-STRING-20 = SPACES)
               MOVE  9 TO SW-SIZE
           ELSE
               MOVE 20 TO SW-SIZE.
```

#### ↳ Re: how to determine string length ?

- **From:** scm@enterprise.net (Shaun C. Murray)
- **Date:** 1998-09-22T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<6u8do1$93$1@news.enterprise.net>`
- **References:** `<36075d20.4540031@news.iaehv.nl>`

```
In article <36075d20.4540031@news.iaehv.nl>, merlyn@iaehv.nl says...
>
>Hi,
>
>how can i determine the string length of a variable in Cobol ?

Many ways!

Left or right justified? ;-)

look up 'length of', 'Inspect ... tallying', 'function reverse' or use a 
perform loop counting characters from the start or end depending on 
justification rules.

eg. 
    >* left justified code
    move 0 to count-var
    perform until digit-code(count-var:1) = space or count-var > 10
        add 1 to count-var
    end-perform

>The variable i'm using contains either a 9 or 20 digit code,
>how can i determine this ?

You could also just try looking at the 10th digit using reference modification. 
If it's a space, it's 9 digits.

eg. if digit-code(10:1) = space
        display "9 digits"
    else
        display "20 digits"
    end-if

of course I didn't cater for it all being spaces but you can add that.
```

> 
> how can i determine the string length of a variable in Cobol ?
> The variable i'm using contains either a 9 or 20 digit code,
> how can i determine this ?

           IF ( Code-Field(10:) = SPACES )
        or
            MOVE ZERO    TO Code-Lenth
            INSPECT Code-Field
                TALLYING Code-Length FOR CHARACTERS
                    BEFORE INITIAL SPACE
         or
             IF ( Code-Field(1:20) NUMERIC )
                 ..
             ELSE
             IF ( Code-Field(1:9) NUMERIC )
                 ..

Jarl Wijman wrote:
> 
> Hi,
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…

This smells like homework... but what the hell.  Assuming:

05  WS-VAR-STRING  PIC X(20).

... code

05  WS-TST-CHAR PIC X VALUE SPACES.
    88 VALID-CHAR VALUES 'A' THRU 'H', 'I' THRU 'R', 'S' THRU 'Z', 'a'
THRU 'h',
                         'i' THRU 'r', 's' THRU 'z', '0' THRU '9'. <==
etc etc etc.

... and ...

MOVE WS-VAR-STRING(20:1) TO WS-TST-CHAR.

IF VALID-CHAR
    PERFORM LEN-20-RITUAL THRU L2R-EX
ELSE
MOVE WS-VAR-STRING(09:1) TO WS-TST-CHAR
IF VALID-CHAR
    PERFORM LEN-9-RITUAL THRU L9R-EX
ELSE
    PERFORM INVALID-STRING-RITUAL THRU ISR-EX.

Let's see... without my handy mainframe to check that's a series of MVIs
and CLIs... getting more efficiency would be tedious.

DD
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: how to determine string length ?

- **From:** Alex Romaniuk <ales.romaniuk@zag.si>
- **Date:** 1998-09-23T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3608DEB5.4CA9@zag.si>`
- **References:** `<36075d20.4540031@news.iaehv.nl> <36077F88.47D6@erols.com> <3607b355.26612630@news.iaehv.nl>`

```
Jarl Wijman wrote:
> 
> Hi,
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> 

01 Max20 PIC X(20).
01 Ptr	 PIC 9(4) COMP.

PERFORM VARYING Ptr FROM 20 BY -1 UNTIL Max20(Ptr:1) NOT= SPACE OR 
					Ptr = 0
   CONTINUE
END-PERFORM

Now, Ptr holds position of last character. 
(I wrote this from my head, but I thing it should work ok)
If Max20(Ptr:1) produces an error than you should
do something like this:
01 Max20 PIC X(20).
01 One   REDEFINES Max20 OCCURS 20 TIMES PIC X.
01 Ptr	 PIC 9(4) COMP.

PERFORM VARYING Ptr FROM 20 BY -1 UNTIL One(Ptr) NOT= SPACE OR 
					Ptr = 0
   CONTINUE
END-PERFORM

If you're working on VAX/AXP computer, then it is simply:
CALL "STR$TRIM" USING BY DESCRIPTOR Max20 Max20 BY REFERENCE Ptr

Below is my version of TRIM function:

Usage:
   CALL "TRIM" USING Max20 "020" Ptr
   MOVE Max20(1:Ptr) TO ....

Hope, to help you. 

	Alex


+=====================================+
! Ales Romaniuk, Slovenia(Europe)     !
! Software Developement (VMS,AXP,DOS) !

! E-mail: mailto:ales.romaniuk@zag.si !
+=====================================!
! HomePg: http://www.zag.si/~romaniuk !
+=====================================+
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: how to determine string length ?

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc123@bellsouth.net>
- **Date:** 1998-09-23T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<dtbO1.1206$_G3.972796@news2.mia.bellsouth.net>`
- **References:** `<36075d20.4540031@news.iaehv.nl> <36077F88.47D6@erols.com> <3607b355.26612630@news.iaehv.nl> <3608DEB5.4CA9@zag.si>`

```
Alex Romaniuk wrote:
>
>01 Max20 PIC X(20).
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>END-PERFORM

Alex, that code is broken.  You are testing Max20(Ptr:1) NOT = SPACE
at the same time as testing for Ptr = 0.  This is a no-no.  If the
test for space is done before the test for 0, the code will blow.
Even if you get it to run, another compiler, or another version of
the same compiler, might generate the code in a different order and
blow.  You *must* insure that your index has not exceeded boundaries
before you use it.  In this example, a simple way is to test for all
spaces before doing the PERFORM. :-)
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: how to determine string length ?

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** riplin@kcbbs.gen.nz (Richard Plinston)
- **Date:** 1998-09-24T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3298266.22117.14557@kcbbs.gen.nz>`
- **References:** `<dtbO1.1206$_G3.972796@news2.mia.bellsouth.net>`

```
In message <<dtbO1.1206$_G3.972796@news2.mia.bellsouth.net>> "Judson McClendon" <judmc123@bellsouth.net> writes:
> >
> >PERFORM VARYING Ptr FROM 20 BY -1 UNTIL Max20(Ptr:1) NOT= SPACE OR
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> at the same time as testing for Ptr = 0.  This is a no-no.  If the

While he does have the order wrong it is not a 'no no'
to do these 'at the same time'.

I is OK (with ANSI85) to have:

          PERFORM
               VARYING Ptr FROM 20 BY -1
                 UNTIL Ptr = ZERO
                    OR Max20(Ptr:1) NOT = SPACE


> test for space is done before the test for 0, the code will blow.
> Even if you get it to run, another compiler, or another version of
> the same compiler, might generate the code in a different order and

Actually the current standard guarantees the evaluation 
sequence and will cease as soon as the value can be determined.
No different standard (85) compiler will generate the 
code in a different order.

> blow.  You *must* insure that your index has not exceeded boundaries
> before you use it.  In this example, a simple way is to test for all
> spaces before doing the PERFORM. :-)

Testing for spaces prior to the perform would ensure that
it would not reach ptr = ZERO and may be a substitute
for that particular item in the condition, but as a 
general rule you can rely on (in this example) there
not being a bound exception as long as it is checked
first in the evaluation sequence (ie is to the left of
its use).
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: how to determine string length ?

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc123@bellsouth.net>

…[9 more quoted lines elided]…

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: how to determine string length ?

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** riplin@kcbbs.gen.nz (Richard Plinston)
- **Date:** 1998-09-25T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3298267.71530.10300@kcbbs.gen.nz>`
- **References:** `<SjrO1.1515$OX1.1521087@news4.mia.bellsouth.net>`

```
In message <<SjrO1.1515$OX1.1521087@news4.mia.bellsouth.net>> "Judson McClendon" <judmc123@bellsouth.net> writes:
> >
> >While he does have the order wrong it is not a 'no no'
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> necessarily implement a strict evaluation order?  It is difficult

I write Cobol programs in Cobol and other languages in their
norms.  All languages are to a lesser or greater extent
idiomatic.  It is important to adopt an appropriate idiom.

In Cobol (85) it _is_ appropriate to range check an array
index in the same condition as its use as long as it is
checked to the left of its use (unlike the original post).
Anyone who knows Cobol should understand that.


> enough to get it right, without intentionally dangling over the edge.
> I cannot believe you would actually justify such code!  All I can say
> is, I hope *you* are the one called at 2AM when it blows. :-)

As you were the one insistant on knowing the output from
a compiler I would have thought you could verify whether
your compiler gets it 'right', I know mine does.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: how to determine string length ?

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc123@bellsouth.net>
- **Date:** 1998-09-26T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<%c4P1.3294$R_4.3030022@news3.mia.bellsouth.net>`
- **References:** `<SjrO1.1515$OX1.1521087@news4.mia.bellsouth.net> <3298267.71530.10300@kcbbs.gen.nz>`

```
Richard Plinston wrote:
>Judson McClendon writes:
>> >
…[14 more quoted lines elided]…
>Anyone who knows Cobol should understand that.

You view this is a completely technical question, which demonstrates
a misunderstanding of human nature.  It does not matter what the
language manual says, in the final analysis it is a human being who
will write the code, and you seem unaware of the significance of this.
It is an absolute fact that when doing two tasks which are very
similar, humans are prone to confuse the two, often without being
consciously aware of it.  Discipline can reduce, but *cannot* eliminate
this, because it is an integral part of the way humans form habits.
There isn't a programmer alive who has written a lot of code in several
languages who hasn't at one time or another written a statement using
the syntax of a wrong language.  You may catch it immediately, but if
you have programmed very long in multiple languages, you have done it.
Most computer languages conditional statements are structured similarly.
A practice such as you suggest is absolutely guaranteed to increase the
probability of writing broken code, and there is nothing in this world
you can do to eliminate that, except avoid the practice altogether.
This is a fact, not an opinion.  Like I said, you can dangle over the
edge all you want, but my intent is to write as rock solid code as I can
manage.  And that necessitates considering the human dimension.  Just
because a construct is permissible, does not mean it is wise to use it.
After all, do you make a habit of using GO TO and ALTER?  Why not?  You
can write absolutely correct code using both of them.  But when you do
use them, you are reducing the *probability* of doing so.  The same is
true here, when programming with multiple languages, though not to the
same degree, of course.

>> enough to get it right, without intentionally dangling over the edge.
>> I cannot believe you would actually justify such code!  All I can say
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>your compiler gets it 'right', I know mine does.

You misrepresent my position.  You can search my posts and you will not
find one single time I have recommended writing code based on the
specific output of a specific compiler.  What I have stated repeatedly
is that one should be aware of the *kind* of assembly code that would
need to be generated for the HLL code you are writing.  It doesn't even
need to be for your own CPU.  A move is a move, a multiply is a multiply,
a branch is a branch, a loop is a loop.  All assembly languages must have
these fundamental operations, and you can assume that the CPU can do them
efficiently.  Assembly languages tend to be more similar than HLLs
because, at bottom, the tasks they do are very similar.  But if you
program in a HLL and don't have a clue what it takes for the CPU to
execute the various commands, then you are programming blind, as far as
efficiency goes.  And looking at the software being turned out these days,
that happens quite a lot.  You only write code once, but it may execute
billions of times.  CPU power is cheap, and you don't have to hoard it.
But there is no reason to throw it away, either, because time is precious,
and millions of people waste away their lives 1, 2, 3 seconds at a time,
waiting on some program when they shouldn't have to, because programmers
didn't have a clue, and couldn't care less, about efficiency.  Who cares
about .1 second?  We all should care, because those .1 second delays are
multiplied untold billions of times every day.  The cumulative effect is
mind boggling.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: how to determine string length ?

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** cwestbury@giant.intranet.com (Chris Westbury)
- **Date:** 1998-09-26T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<1998Sep26.081110.19286@giant>`
- **References:** `<36075d20.4540031@news.iaehv.nl> <36077F88.47D6@erols.com> <3608DEB5.4CA9@zag.si>`

```
In article <3608DEB5.4CA9@zag.si>,
Alex Romaniuk <ales.romaniuk@zag.si> wrote:
>
> PERFORM VARYING Ptr FROM 20 BY -1 UNTIL Max20(Ptr:1) NOT= SPACE OR
>                                         Ptr = 0
>    CONTINUE
> END-PERFORM

Technically, MAX20(0:1) is not legal, which I think you already know. 
The relation condition PTR = 0 (which should be the sign condition PTR IS
ZERO) is called a "guard", because it is used to guard against the
evaluation of an illegal expression.  However, in CoBOL, conditionals are
evaluated from left to right, so in a guarded loop the guard must come
before the expression:

    UNTIL  Ptr IS ZERO  OR  Max20(Ptr:1) NOT= SPACE

When the sign condition PTR IS ZERO is satisfied, the other half of the
OR is not evaluated.  This is called "short-circuit" evaluation: the
compiler bails out of the evaluation as soon as the conditional is either
true or false.

This simple problem can be used to illustrate a more general technique,
the sentinel loop.  Consider:

    01 FILLER.
       02 FILLER PIC X(01) VALUE LOW-VALUE.
       02 Max20  PIC X(20).

This guarantees the existence of a nonblank subscript zero, index zero,
or reference mod zero:

    PERFORM
    VARYING Ptr FROM 20 BY -1
      UNTIL Max20(Ptr:1) NOT= SPACE
            CONTINUE
    END-PERFORM

It is superficially similar to what you wrote, but notice that a guarded
loop must evaluate two conditionals per iteration at run time, while a
sentinel loop evaluates only one.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: how to determine string length ?

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc123@bellsouth.net>
- **Date:** 1998-09-26T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<Z16P1.3334$R_4.3079358@news3.mia.bellsouth.net>`
- **References:** `<36075d20.4540031@news.iaehv.nl> <36077F88.47D6@erols.com> <3608DEB5.4CA9@zag.si> <1998Sep26.081110.19286@giant>`

```
Chris Westbury wrote in message <1998Sep26.081110.19286@giant>...
>
>This simple problem can be used to illustrate a more general technique,
…[17 more quoted lines elided]…
>sentinel loop evaluates only one.

Chris, that won't help you if the compiler tests the value of Ptr
to be out of bounds.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: how to determine string length ?

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** cwestbury@giant.intranet.com (Chris Westbury)
- **Date:** 1998-09-28T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<1998Sep28.074201.19290@giant>`
- **References:** `<36075d20.4540031@news.iaehv.nl> <36077F88.47D6@erols.com> <Z16P1.3334$R_4.3079358@news3.mia.bellsouth.net>`

```
In article <Z16P1.3334$R_4.3079358@news3.mia.bellsouth.net>,
"Judson McClendon" <judmc123@bellsouth.net> wrote:
>
> In article <1998Sep26.081110.19286@giant>,
> "Chris Westbury" <cwestbury@intranet.com> wrote:
> >
[...]
> that won't help you if the compiler tests the value of Ptr to be out of
> bounds.

I strive to illustrate techniques with as little change as possible to
the original poster's code.  If I showed what I would actually write,
with parameterized length and so on, the concept I am trying to convey
would be lost in all the differences.

Once you get the idea of a sentinel, though, it is trivial to transform
the zero-loop to a one-loop if you must (still trying to follow the
original poster's style):

    01 WorkArea.
       02 FILLER PIC X(01) VALUE LOW-VALUE.
       02 Max20  PIC X(20).

    PERFORM
    VARYING Ptr FROM 20 BY -1
      UNTIL WorkArea(1 + Ptr:1) NOT= SPACE 
            CONTINUE
    END-PERFORM

There are of course other equivalent one-loops such as:

    PERFORM
    VARYING Ptr FROM 21 BY -1
      UNTIL WorkArea(Ptr:1) NOT= SPACE 
            CONTINUE
    END-PERFORM

depending on what you want to do next with PTR.  Surprisingly, perhaps,
the second loop is no more efficient on a base/displacement machine than
the first, because on a base/displacement machine the preaddition of a
constant is free.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: how to determine string length ?

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** riplin@kcbbs.gen.nz (Richard Plinston)
- **Date:** 1998-09-27T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3298269.5996.21832@kcbbs.gen.nz>`
- **References:** `<1998Sep26.081110.19286@giant>`

```
In message <<1998Sep26.081110.19286@giant>> cwestbury@giant.intranet.com  writes:
> 
> This simple problem can be used to illustrate a more general technique,
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> or reference mod zero:

I always leave bound checking on, I would rather have the 
program tell me that something is going wrong rather
than the possibility of corrupt data.

> 
>     PERFORM
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> sentinel loop evaluates only one.

As Judson has already pointed out, for this specific case it
is better to do a:

       IF Max20 = SPACES
           MOVE ZERO      TO Ptr
       ELSE
           PERFORM .... UNTIL Max20(Ptr:1) NOT = SPACE

 no fiddles for zero index, no bound check errors.

However many cases are not so simple.
```

#### ↳ Re: how to determine string length ?

- **From:** brixford@my-dejanews.com
- **Date:** 1998-09-23T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<6ubs9k$d6t$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>`
- **References:** `<36075d20.4540031@news.iaehv.nl>`

```
In article <36075d20.4540031@news.iaehv.nl>,
  merlyn@iaehv.nl (Jarl Wijman) wrote:
> merlyn@iaehv.nl  /  j.wijman@face.

 A different solution:
000191 WORKING-STORAGE SECTION.
000192 01  A-STRING    PIC X(20) VALUE 'ABCEDFGHI'.
000194 01  I           PIC 9(4).
004800 PROCEDURE DIVISION.
004900     COMPUTE I = 1
004910     STRING A-STRING DELIMITED BY ' '
004920      INTO A-STRING POINTER I.
004921     COMPUTE I = I - 1.
Variable I now contains the space delimited leng of the text in A-STRING.
(Does not handle embedded spaces)

>

-----== Posted via Deja News, The Leader in Internet Discussion ==-----
http://www.dejanews.com/rg_mkgrp.xp   Create Your Own Free Member Forum
```

#### ↳ Re: how to determine string length ?

- **From:** "Scott Nelson" <sbnelson@i1.net>
- **Date:** 1998-09-24T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7vzO1.132$1c3.1875187@news1.i1.net>`
- **References:** `<36075d20.4540031@news.iaehv.nl>`

```
Hi Jarl.

Pro*COBOL is a pre-processor that goes in front of your real compiler.

I have two ways to determine the length of a string, assuming that the
string may have imbedded blanks.

Method 1:

77 the-string pic x(20).
77 the-size pic 9(2).

perform varying the-size from 20 by -1
until the-size = zero
or the-string(the-size:1) not = spaces
* just looping
end-perform

Be sure to test for "the-size = zero" before testing to see if
"the-string(the-size:1) = spaces" because the string could be empty.

Method 2: This is an extension (i.e., not portable) that acuCobol compilers
have:

77 the-string pic x(20).
77 the-size pic 9(2).

move zero to the-size
inspect the-string tallying the-size for TRAILING spaces.

Jarl Wijman wrote in message <36075d20.4540031@news.iaehv.nl>...
>Hi,
>
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
>merlyn@iaehv.nl  /  j.wijman@face.nl
```

<a id="t-4af316f9cb"></a>
## Thread: VS COBOL II and Y2K (was Re: COBOL II Support Dates..was also C-A ....) — 4 msgs

_Topics: [`Y2K and Year-2000 remediation`](topics.md#y2k) · [`Compilers and vendors`](topics.md#compilers)_

### VS COBOL II and Y2K (was Re: COBOL II Support Dates..was also C-A ....)

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1998-09-22T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** bit.listserv.ibm-main,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<6u9k6r$nmv@dfw-ixnews4.ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<19980922003229.TZLE4499@39.chicago-05-10rs.il.dial-access.att.net> <3607E628.5B11@netvision.net.il>`

```
It was an earlier post of mine that made ERRONEOUS statements about VS COBOL
II and being "year 2000 compliant."  Since I made that post, I have gained a
little more "official" knowledge which this note hopes to impart.

Note: The following is true for VS COBOL II under MVS (or VM); for the
status under VSE, it is important to note that support has already ended
there.  This also applies only to Release 4.0 of VS COBOL II; all earlier
releases are already "dropped" as far as both marketing and support goes.

1) IBM does NOT talk (for any product) about "Year 2000 compliant" - they
only talk about "year 2000 ready" - and this has slightly different meanings
for hardware, software (products), and software (applications).  The
following is the definition for software products (which is where any COBOL
compiler fits),

"IBM uses the term "Year 2000 ready" to refer to products that meet the
following definition.  Specifically, "Year 2000 ready" means that "a
product, when used in accordance with its associated documentation, is
capable of correctly processing, providing and/or receiving date data within
and between the 20th and 21st centuries, provided that all products (for
example, hardware, software and firmware) used with the product properly
exchange accurate date data with it."

"IBM considers products not ready for Year 2000 if they don't meet the
definition set out above, or if they have not been tested.  Products not
affected by the Year 2000, such as hardware frames, keyboards, power
supplies, and hardware or software product publications are considered
ready."

  NOTE WELL: the previous definition includes "not tested means not ready".

2) When you use the IBM tool for determining VS COBOL II's (product
5668-958) readiness - you will receive the response
    NOT READY

(See www.ibm.com/2000 for accessing their Y2K evaluation database)

3) The only MVS or OS/390 COBOL products that get a "READY" status (and a
currently supported status) are
   IBM COBOL for MVS & VM - (5688-197)
   IBM COBOL for OS/390 & VM - (5648-A25)
   VA COBOL MLE FOR OS/390&VM - (5648-MLE)

(The last of these "sort-of" uses one of the previous 2)

4) Support for VS COBOL II is provided thru March 31, 2001.  This means that
it will continue to "function correctly" including handling of dates
(including leap day) as defined in the ANSI/ISO Standards for COBOL and in
the IBM LRM for this product.

5) Although IBM does NOT call VS COBOL II "Year 2000 ready", there are two
APARs out there that make it seem as close as you can get (for an "untested
and unsupported-as-Y2K-ready" product).

If you are running the VS COBOL II compiler with the VS COBOL II run-time
libraries, then

   APAR PN76666 provides a new software facility that allows you (the
application program) to call an IBM provided subroutine
    IGZEDT4
which provides the current date WITH a 4 digit year.

Note; APARs PN84080 and PN85139 provide ongoing run-time support for this
routine when you upgrade to the LE run-time library.

If you are running with the VS COBOL II compiler but with the LE run-time
library, then

  APAR PN79703 documents that it is legal (supported) to use the COBOL
dynamic CALL facility to obtain any of the date/time services provided by
the LE callable subroutines.  This means that such VS COBOL II programs can,
not only get 4-digit years, but can also get the full range of IBM supplied
"windowing" routines.

  NOTE: The IBM provided "millenium language extensions" which provide COBOL
native language support for "date windowing" are NOT available for VS COBOL
II.  All this APAR provides is access to the callable "windowing services"
from VS COBOL II.

   **********

Bottom-Line:

A) VS COBOL II is not (and will not) be labeled "Year 2000 ready"

B) VS COBOL II will be supported until the end of 1st quarter of 2001
(including the correct handling of dates - as defined in its language
reference manual)

C) Although the VS COBOL II product is not "year 2000 ready", via APARs you
can get the 4-digit year date under any run-time library and can get date
windowing when running VS COBOL II compiled programs with the LE run-time
library.

D) As stated in my original post, you really REALLY should be starting your
migration to one of the newer IBM COBOLs (probably IBM COBOL for OS/390 &
VM - and ideally with  MLE) now - and should not continue relying on VS
COBOL II.  VS COBOL II does NOT include support for

   - Millenium Language Extensions
   - The "CURRENT-DATE" intrinsic function (providing a 4-digit year)
   - ACCEPT from DAY or DATE getting a 4-digit year
```

#### ↳ Re: VS COBOL II and Y2K (was Re: COBOL II Support Dates..was also C-A ....)

- **From:** Frank Swarbrick <infocat@sprynet.com>
- **Date:** 1998-09-23T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3609AE5D.4C7D@sprynet.com>`
- **References:** `<19980922003229.TZLE4499@39.chicago-05-10rs.il.dial-access.att.net> <3607E628.5B11@netvision.net.il> <6u9k6r$nmv@dfw-ixnews4.ix.netcom.com>`

```
Here is my question regardling Y2K "compliance."  (Well, some statements, and
maybe a questions.)  We have a lot of DOS/VS COBOL programs.  Specifically, an
ATM system that we purchased from an outside company.  This company has supplied
us with "patches" in order to become Y2K compliant.  Basically, most of the dates
they use are in Julian with a two digit year (ie 98123 is the 123rd day of 1998).
There are gazillions of date comparisions, not to mention many date fields
(six per record in one particular file).  What they've done is, when we read
in this record it now gets run through a subroutine to where the output (a
separate field) is the number of days since 1900.  This subroutine originally,
of course, concidered the year "10" to be 1910, etc., but it has been modified
so that the number 36525 is added to it if the year is less than 90.  So,
obviously it will only work for dates from 1990 to 2089.  Anyway, other dates
in the program are also converted to this "DAYS" format in order for the 
comparisons to work.

This is all done, of course, on a "non-Y2K-compiant" compiler.  My question I
guess is, why do we *need* to go to COBOL for VSE (we're a VSE shop) in order
to make our programs Y2K compliant?

Before you answer, realize that I personally hate using old-COBOL.  I intend to
convert all of these programs to COBOL II, and to then "convert" them to COBOL
for VSE whenever our systems programmers can get it working.  The problem is,
it *isn't* working yet, and we don't have time to wait for them to *get* it
working.  Even if they got it working today, we need to get the Y2K patches 
going ASAP in order to get testing going.  Don't say we still have a year for
that, because our internal deadline (or goal, I should say), is the end of this
year.  In a perfect world these programs would have been converted to COBOL II
years ago (most of our inhouse programs are--well, the CICS ones anyway, and many
of the batch ones).  But they aren't; so there you are.  As long as the code works
with the patches, do I have to beat myself over the head to convert them?  Even
if I do convert them, I don't know that we're going to start using the date
functions that handle four-digit years.  The program is *so* based on the 
two-digit years that it just doesn't seem worth it.

Still, I feel sorry for anyone who has to deal with the Y2.1K problem!  :-)
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: VS COBOL II and Y2K (was Re: COBOL II Support Dates..was also C-A ....)

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1998-09-23T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<6uc8rq$hme@dfw-ixnews8.ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<19980922003229.TZLE4499@39.chicago-05-10rs.il.dial-access.att.net> <3607E628.5B11@netvision.net.il> <6u9k6r$nmv@dfw-ixnews4.ix.netcom.com> <3609AE5D.4C7D@sprynet.com>`

```

Frank Swarbrick wrote in message <3609AE5D.4C7D@sprynet.com>...

  <lots of snippage>
>   But they aren't; so there you are.  As long as the code works
>with the patches, do I have to beat myself over the head to convert them?
Even
>if I do convert them, I don't know that we're going to start using the date
>functions that handle four-digit years.  The program is *so* based on the
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>work: frank.swarbrick@1stbank.com

The problem with using DOS/VS COBOL (or VS COBOL II under VSE) for your Y2K
compliant program logic has to do with all the other systems that they
interface with.

FOR EXAMPLE,
  As you are a VSE shop, I am going to guess that you probably have some
CICS in your shop.  Are you aware that the "old" CICS won't even come up
after Jan 1, 2000?  Once you go to the new CICS (and I'll admit that I know
MVS better than VSE), the chances are that your "old" COBOL will have
problems with your "new" CICS. And when you are running DOS/VS COBOL or VS
COBOL II compiled programs, you are "unsupported".  (Well actually there are
some rules about supporting "existing" object code - IF you are using the
new LE run-time, but I'll bet you aren't doing that with your DOS/VS COBOL
programs).

This means that everything is fine - until it isn't!

In other words, running DOS/VS COBOL, VS COBOL II (under VSE), or any other
"unsupported" software will work just fine, until some day some other piece
of software or hardware "breaks" your existing applications - and you have
no one to turn to.  IBM is simply going to tell you to get on a "supported"
product and THEN they will help you resolve the problem with your existing
application working with the new hardware/software.

Does this help explain your problem?

P.S.  In case it got lost in the middle of this, IBM fully uspports
continued use of DOS/VS COBOL or VS COBOL II compiled programs when run with
LE/VSE.  However, such programs will NOT be "Y2K-compliant" - as far as IBM
is concerned.

P.P.S.  IBM had a known error in calculating that Feb 29, 2000 was a valid
leap day - when running with VS COBOL II under VSE.  Make certain that you
have the APAR applied to your products and that you test that out with your
"patched" application.
```

## Thread: program structure — 9 msgs

### program structure

- **From:** skidmike <skidmike@mindspring.com>
- **Date:** 1998-09-22T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36084E7D.A4128F4E@mindspring.com>`

```
advice from old hands, please.  i can write without a go to.  only use
performs and perform untils.  how much should i break down my code into
smaller subroutines for future maintenance by other programmers?
right now, in a print program, i use a lot of evaluate's and the when's
specify move... or add... etc. instead of the when's performing a
subroutine to do the action.  are there guidelines to go by?
```

#### ↳ Re: program structure

- **From:** redsky@ibm.net (Thane Hubbell)
- **Date:** 1998-09-23T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<Jl0PnHJ5PvPd-pn2-pVndyvlVt6VV@Dwight_Miller.iix.com>`
- **References:** `<36084E7D.A4128F4E@mindspring.com>`

```
On Wed, 23 Sep 1998 01:27:25, skidmike <skidmike@mindspring.com> 
wrote:

> advice from old hands, please.  i can write without a go to.  only use
> performs and perform untils.  how much should i break down my code into
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> subroutine to do the action.  are there guidelines to go by?

Here's what I do.

I start with a program that's all comments.

* Open the Files
* Print the Report
* Close the Files

Then I break it down further. (In comment)

Untill all that is left is the code.

Try for one function per paragaph.   You CAN go too far, but once it's
distilled down it should be pretty obvious.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: program structure

- **From:** "Donald Tees" <donald@willmack.com>

```

skidmike wrote in message <360975A9.81CF5B6D@mindspring.com>...
>i am aiming for one function per subroutine, but from looking at the
>code supplied in one of the books i have "Learn COBOL in 21 Days" and
>from what you guys say it can go too far.  all of the code in the book


Well, I'll tell you how I do it.  I sort of write the thing.  Then I think
about it,
and analyse what tools I would have liked to have.  I write a few, and add
them to my tools for the next system.  Then I sort of write the next thing.
Then I build a few more tools.  I repeat that, maybe once more, then I
retrofit the first system as its about 3-4 years old by that time.  I think
about the whole cycle then, and maybe do a bit of upgrading.  Then you
start over.  Maybe new languages, a new OS.  You start over, knowing
a bit more, and building new stuff.  top down for a while, bottom up for a
while.  Then you start to decide what to write with it.
```

#### ↳ Re: program structure

- **From:** "G. van Vlimmeren" <gvlimmer@hoogenbosch.nl>
- **Date:** 1998-09-23T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<01bde6d2$10401e00$2c050380@s70>`
- **References:** `<36084E7D.A4128F4E@mindspring.com>`

```
Hi,

I learned a trick once, that works quite well:
Whenever you have to think for more than 10 seconds to write a line of
code, use a subroutine.

So, when you're writing on the go, you get the basic structure of the
program first, afterwards you can fill in the blanks by working out the
subroutines. 

On the other hand. You could try documenting the structure first, then
write the program. :)

'Gentlemen, grap your templates and start drawing....'

Gerben van Vlimmeren

skidmike <skidmike@mindspring.com> schreef in artikel
<36084E7D.A4128F4E@mindspring.com>...
> specify move... or add... etc. instead of the when's performing a
> subroutine to do the action.  are there guidelines to go by?
>
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: program structure

- **From:** jcj0347@aol.com (JCJ0347)
- **Date:** 1998-09-23T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<19980923194657.08532.00000810@ng140.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<01bde6d2$10401e00$2c050380@s70>`

```

>'Gentlemen, grap your templates and start drawing....'
>
>
I once got into real trouble by grapping my template.

Seriously, I use a similar method for anything stronger
than read-a-record print-a-line.  I just write out the
functions I want the program to perform, then
keep that on my desk in front of the keyboard while
I'm coding.  Each function becomes a subroutine.

After the program is in production, that basic structure
goes into a file folder as documentation and it really
helps if (no... make that when) I have to go back to make
a mod.  Along with a source listing, of course :)

That's also helpful if the program is going to write a temporary
file or two.  Jot down the data names for filename, record name,
etc. and it sure saves a lot of flipping back up to the data divison.

JC Jones.
```

>performs and perform untils.  how much should i break down my code into
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>subroutine to do the action.  are there guidelines to go by?

A guideline which is frequently quoted is "subroutines should not exceed
a page of code."  However, I have found that artificial and not overly
helpful.  First and foremost, a subroutine should "embody one clear and
separate purpose".  This is important for logical abstraction.  Secondly,
you don't want to put too much code in a subroutine, because it becomes
more difficult to mentally assimilate.  This is where the 'one page'
idea makes some sense, though probably too restrictive.  For in some
programs, such as many online programs, you may have long series of field
validations, each a simple sentence.  There is often no logical place
to break that code up, so you can end up with one very long routine.
But such code is usually easy to recognize and understand.  On the other
hand, breaking up your code into a multitude of subroutines, many of
which have no "clear and separate purpose", also makes the code difficult
to understand.  Good code strikes a balance between these priorities.  In
the final analysis, it becomes more of a skill and an art, rather than an
exact science.
```

#### ↳ Re: program structure

- **From:** gvwmoore@ix.netcom.com (G Moore)
- **Date:** 1998-09-24T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<360d68a4.7454970@nntp.ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<36084E7D.A4128F4E@mindspring.com>`

```
On Tue, 22 Sep 1998 21:27:25 -0400, skidmike <skidmike@mindspring.com>
wrote:

>advice from old hands, please.  i can write without a go to.  only use
>performs and perform untils.  how much should i break down my code into
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>subroutine to do the action.  are there guidelines to go by?

hmm. this sounds like a troll, but i usually break down my subroutines
by their name. thus, write-header, report-content-loop,
format-a-record (implicitly reads record), write-footer.

well, u get the idea. and then, i break them down even further. but
generally, i don't get the subroutines smaller than 10 lines.

abstracting the record read/write section is a generally good thing.

another thing i like to do is have copy files for code and data, and
the code section pointing to the data section used.

maintainenece isnt as much about how small the code is as compared to
how easy it is to edit. the only reason i use the multiple copy file
method is because PC-based dos editors, in general, suck.



gvwmoore@ix.spam.netcom.com to reply remove the spam
```

On Tue, 22 Sep 1998 23:44:00, Charles F Hankel 
<charles@hankel.mersinet.co.uk> wrote:

> There is no difference in calling PL/1 from COBOL than for any other
> called program - the source language of the called program is not
> relevant.
>

Not true on all platforms.  It is true where PL/1 runs however.

On the PC, there is the Pascal calling convention, the C calling 
convention, COBOL and ASM and some others.

##### ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL calls to PL/I

- **From:** scottp4.removethis@ibm.net (Scott Peterson)
- **Date:** 1998-09-24T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<360ec76d.20458108@news3.ibm.net>`
- **References:** `<36077B75.52651FA3@hotmail.com> <36083640.C25986E2@hankel.mersinet.co.uk>`

```
In comp.lang.cobol, Charles F Hankel <charles@hankel.mersinet.co.uk>
wrote:

>There is no difference in calling PL/1 from COBOL than for any other
>called program - the source language of the called program is not
>relevant.

I can't speak to the current versions, but in the past this statement
would not be correct for PL/1 subroutines.. In order for a PL/1
program to be called you had to call a PL/1  service module
(PLISTART?) to establish and maintain the environment before the first
call was made to a PL/1 subroutine. There were also a number of
gotchas because of differing data types, array handling and unique
paramete list issues.

			Scott Peterson
```

```

>would not be correct for PL/1 subroutines.. In order for a PL/1
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Scott:
Like you I can't speak to the current versions, but I do remember that the
overhead in establishing the PL/1 environment from a COBOL call  was staggering
from a system resources perspective. So much so that we (NYTel) programmers
were prohibited from doing so. 
Frank J. Boll
Computer Solutions

## Thread: Ethical Question — 6 msgs

### Re: Ethical Question

- **From:** Lookin'@You.2 (WDS)
- **Date:** 1998-09-22T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3606e9b9.361131@news1.frb.gov>`
- **References:** `<6spk4b$ome$1@news-2.news.gte.net> <3298247.81807.423@kcbbs.gen.nz>`

```
On 5 Sep 98, riplin@kcbbs.gen.nz (Richard Plinston) wrote:
[...]
>However, my real view is that there is nothing dangerous
>about a GO TO.  It is a very clear statement, has no
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
>that point.
[snip]

If I remember correctly, _The_Devil's_DP_Dictionary_ described a
solution to the above.  Instead of using GO TO, the compiler could
implement a COME FROM statement.

On the other hand, I guess that someone could come up with a theory
that COME FROM could cause problems similar to those of GO TO, but in
the opposite direction.  Seeing a label would give no clue that
control could be yanked elsewhere.  Makes debugging fun. :)
```

>
<g>
Not knowing where I am going is bad enough.  Now you want

#### ↳ Re: Ethical Question

- **From:** Bill Lynch <wblynch@att.net>
- **Date:** 1998-09-22T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36083532.ECB7896E@att.net>`
- **References:** `<6spk4b$ome$1@news-2.news.gte.net> <3298247.81807.423@kcbbs.gen.nz> <3606e9b9.361131@news1.frb.gov>`

```
WDS wrote:
> 
(snip)
> 
> If I remember correctly, _The_Devil's_DP_Dictionary_ described a
> solution to the above.  Instead of using GO TO, the compiler could
> implement a COME FROM statement.

This is how the 1400 series did subroutine linkage in Autocoder. They
had A & B registers. After a branch was taken the B register pointed to
the next sequential instruction after the branch, so the first
instruction in the subroutine was:

	SBR   EXIT+3               save B register
	.
	.
EXIT  	B     *

No thoughts of being reentrant, of course. I gues it's true, "Everything
old is new again".

Bill Lynch
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Ethical Question

- **From:** "Donald Tees" <donald@willmack.com>
- **Date:** 1998-09-23T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<6uas0u$slp$1@news.igs.net>`
- **References:** `<6spk4b$ome$1@news-2.news.gte.net> <3298247.81807.423@kcbbs.gen.nz> <3606e9b9.361131@news1.frb.gov> <36083532.ECB7896E@att.net>`

```

Bill Lynch wrote in message <36083532.ECB7896E@att.net>...

>This is how the 1400 series did subroutine linkage in Autocoder. They
>had A & B registers. After a branch was taken the B register pointed to
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> SBR   EXIT+3               save B register


Thinking back, the PDP-8 had a similar mechanism. The location
called was changed to the return address, and the call jumped to the
first location after the one named.  To do a return, you indirected
through the called location, so you could not even a nested call.

sub:    0
            do something
           jmp indirect sub        ;type of thing.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Ethical Question

- **From:** trblshtr@my-dejanews.com
- **Date:** 1998-09-24T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<6uc89d$pnh$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>`
- **References:** `<6spk4b$ome$1@news-2.news.gte.net> <3298247.81807.423@kcbbs.gen.nz> <3606e9b9.361131@news1.frb.gov> <36083532.ECB7896E@att.net> <6uas0u$slp$1@news.igs.net>`

```
In article <6uas0u$slp$1@news.igs.net>,
  "Donald Tees" <donald@willmack.com> wrote:
>
> Bill Lynch wrote in message <36083532.ECB7896E@att.net>...
…[17 more quoted lines elided]…
>
I think it was, essentially, a 'pre-stack' architecture in both cases.
It did not prevent recursive or reentrant or nested constructs.  You
simply had to program for that yourself (i.e. pick up the addr called
from and save it in an appropriately allocated buffer and restore it
to the exit point before exiting.  (Actually was nice in the days when
you, as the programmer, had a bit more control over what was
actually occuring). :-)
```

<a id="t-8fbba65d5d"></a>
## Thread: CICS EPI? — 3 msgs

_Topics: [`Mainframe, z/OS, JCL, CICS`](topics.md#mainframe)_

### CICS EPI?

- **From:** "rmch" <rmch@cadvision.com>
- **Date:** 1998-09-22T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36083265.0@news.cadvision.com>`

```
Someone in this newsgroup mentioned CICS EPI...

Can anyone elaborate on what this is ?  (I am familiar with CICS
programming).

Thanks.
```

#### ↳ Re: CICS EPI?

- **From:** paulr@bix.com (paulr)
- **Date:** 1998-09-23T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<6u9knp$j1b@lotho.delphi.com>`
- **References:** `<36083265.0@news.cadvision.com>`

```

EPI is the External Presentation Interface.  It allows
CICS 3270 applications to be invoked by a non 3270
application, as in workstation GUI programes. :) 

It provides a suite of calls that allow a client 
application to pass 3270 data streams to and from
a CICS application. 

There are a couple of examples programs distributed 
with CICS these days to demonstrate EPI.

Yours,
-Paul



rmch (rmch@cadvision.com) wrote:
: Someone in this newsgroup mentioned CICS EPI...

: Can anyone elaborate on what this is ?  (I am familiar with CICS
: programming).

: Thanks.
```

#### ↳ Re: CICS EPI?

- **From:** vbandke@ibm.net (Volker Bandke)
- **Date:** 1998-09-23T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3608ef23.4527403@news2.ibm.net>`
- **References:** `<36083265.0@news.cadvision.com>`

```
On Tue, 22 Sep 1998 17:25:26 -0600, "rmch" <rmch@cadvision.com> wrote:

>Someone in this newsgroup mentioned CICS EPI...
>
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>


EPI, as in External Presentation Interface.  Allows the native GUI type application to
emulate a 3270 terminal to CICS.  In Mainframe, this is called FEPI.  Not really a
client0server approach, but more of a screen scraper


HTH
with kind regards

Volker Bandke
(BSP GmbH)
```

From:     State of Kansas

Re:       Applications Programmer Analyst III
          $17.41 per hour/40hrs a week

The Division of Project Management & System Development is accepting
applications for an Applications Programmer Analyst III, Topeka, Kansas. 
This position will be responsible for development of major online and batch
mainframe systems utilizing COBOL, CICS, Natural 2 and ADABAS.	Incumbent
will participate as a team member on various projects and require the
following:

�	Knowledge of operating systems, control languages, data base management,
direct access techniques, remote processing and virtual systems.
�	Knowledge of one or more computer programming languages.
�	Designing/analyzing, coding, testing, debugging and/or documenting
application programs and/or database programs.  Education in computer
programming and/or systems analysis.  (Minimum of 3 years experience)

The State of Kansas has a competitive benefit package and is an equal
opportunity employer, women and minorities are encouraged to apply.  Please
contact Libby Tidwell  at 785/296-4767, or email me at
eag@srsprsnl.wpo.state.ks.us  for information about this position. 
Application deadline is October 2, 1998.

<a id="t-7e1ddf5f7d"></a>
## Thread: SuperC Or Not So SuperC? That Is The Question...Calling All SuperC Gurus — 1 msgs

### SuperC Or Not So SuperC? That Is The Question...Calling All SuperC Gurus

- **From:** theodp@aol.com (Theo DP)
- **Date:** 1998-09-22T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<19980922004736.26464.00002943@ng-fd2.aol.com>`

```

Does anyone know how (or if) SuperC can be used to compare OLD and NEW files
that contain multiple record types, each of which may contain a variable number
of pre- (YYMMDD) and post- (YYYYMMDD) at various locations. Using SECTions and
CMPCOL* commands result in error messages - SuperC appears to require ALL
commands to be in column order, but the multiple record types make this
impossible. Specific examples or references to examples would be appreciated -
please, no generalities (the Y2K devil's in the details) or unsolicited
workarounds (e.g., split off record types to separate files, etc.). Thanks!
```

<a id="t-871fce1b5a"></a>
## Thread: UNISYS: assembler translation assistance — 12 msgs

### UNISYS: assembler translation assistance

- **From:** sff5ky@aol.com (Sff5ky)
- **Date:** 1998-09-23T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<19980923005600.UAA11369@ladder01.news.aol.com>`

```
I am attempting to convert some old COBOL68 programs with inline ASSEMBLER code
to fit ANSI85.  Would also like to find assistance for mapping PIC 9 COMP
fields to an equivalent ANSI85 data type and what does 'INTRINSIC TRANSLATE'
using source-field, trans-table, giving result-field
. 
.
    ENTER SYMBOLIC.
       MVN 1 NCRVAR:1:+100
       MVN 0000  NCRVAR:4:+200
    ENTER COBOL.
       READ NCRVAR
.
.
        ADD 1 TO NCR-REC-NO.
 012-NCR-COPY.
     ENTER SYMBOLIC.
        BOT 01 80 NCRVAR:+200
        NEQ 012-NCR-COPY
        SUB NCRVAR:6:+210 NCRVAR:6:+234 NCR-REC-SIZE.
     ENTER COBOL.
        MOVE SPACES TO NCR-STARCOM-PRINT.
.
.
     IF NCR-RECD-LNGTH NOT > ZERO
         MOVE 1 TO END-TRLR-FLAG
         GO TO 030-EXIT.
     ENTER SYMBOLIC.
       MVN TRLR-ADDR IX1.
       INC TRLR-ADDR IX1.
       MVALUE NCR-TRLR-AREA:DSP:IX1:2 TRLR-LNGTH-X.
     ENTER COBOL.
     MOVE TRLR-LNGTH TO WORK-4.
.
.
     MOVE DEC-VALUE TO TRLR-LNGTH
                       CNT-LNGTH.
     ENTER SYMBOLIC.
       MVN TRLR-ADDR IX1.
       INC TRLR-ADDR IX1.
       MVN 0         IX2.
 030-MV-LOOP.
       MVALUE NCR-TRLR-AREA:DSP:IX1:00 NCR-STARCOM-SVTRLR:DSP:IX2:
       INC 200 IX1.
       INC 200 IX2.
       DEC 100 CNT-LNGTH.
       GTR 030-MV-LOOP.
     ENTER COBOL.
     SUBTRACT TRLR-LNGTH FROM NCR-RECD-LNGTH.
     ADD TRLR-LNGTH TO TRLR-ADDR.
.
.
     MOVE ZERO TO NA-TRLR-LNGTH.
     ENTER SYMBOLIC.
       MVN NA-TRLR-ADDR IX1.
       INC NA-TRLR-ADDR IX1.
       MVALUE NCR-NA-SUB-TRLRS:DSP:IX1:1 NA-TRLR-LNGTH-X:DSP:+1:1.
     ENTER COBOL.
     MOVE NA-TRLR-LNGTH TO WORK-4.
.
.
     MOVE DEC-VALUE TO NA-TRLR-LNGTH
                       CNT-LNGTH.
     ENTER SYMBOLIC.
       MVN NA-TRLR-ADDR IX1.
       INC NA-TRLR-ADDR IX1.
       MVN 0            IX2.
*
 056-MV-LOOP.
       MVALUE NCR-NA-SUB-TRLRS:DSP:IX1:00
           NCR-NA-SUB-TRLR:DSP:IX2:00.
       INC 200 IX1.
       INC 200 IX2.
       DEC 100 CNT-LNGTH.
       GTR 056-MV-LOOP.
     ENTER COBOL.
     SUBTRACT NA-TRLR-LNGTH FROM TRLR-LNGTH.
     ADD NA-TRLR-LNGTH TO NA-TRLR-ADDR.
.
.
     MOVE NCR-NAME-LINE(SUB1) TO WORK-BYTES.
     ENTER SYMBOLIC.
       TRN 35 WORK-BYTES ASC-TO-EBC WORK-BYTES.
     ENTER COBOL.
     IF WORK-BYTES NOT = SPACES
```

#### ↳ Re: UNISYS: assembler translation assistance

- **From:** "grant mc" <grantmc@voyager.co.nz>
- **Date:** 1998-09-23T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<6ua7fi$j47$2@reader1.reader.news.ozemail.net>`
- **References:** `<19980923005600.UAA11369@ladder01.news.aol.com>`

```
Can you provide a little information. Specifically what model machine is
this code from? The A-Series system since 1984 hasn't offered an assembler
and from the look of the code you've provided it looks more like some old
intrinsic functions from a software product, probably from a B-Series
machine. I've got a heap of very old manuals so might be able to help you.

PIC 9 COMP are simply hex code without the F character and are stored in a
half byte. You can store them like this because we know the item is a
number. COMP items can be held together and thus don't waste any space.
Where as if we declare items mixed between display and comp then half bytes
are lost.

Grant Mc
Sff5ky wrote in message <19980923005600.UAA11369@ladder01.news.aol.com>...
>I am attempting to convert some old COBOL68 programs with inline ASSEMBLER
code
>to fit ANSI85.  Would also like to find assistance for mapping PIC 9 COMP
>fields to an equivalent ANSI85 data type and what does 'INTRINSIC
TRANSLATE'
>using source-field, trans-table, giving result-field
>.
…[79 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

#### ↳ Re: UNISYS: assembler translation assistance

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc123@bellsouth.net>
- **Date:** 1998-09-23T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<Wb5O1.327$R_4.752508@news3.mia.bellsouth.net>`
- **References:** `<19980923005600.UAA11369@ladder01.news.aol.com>`

```
What you see below appears to be from the Unisys V Series (the old Burroughs
Medium Systems).  I have written a lot of Medium Systems assembly, and a fair
amount of ENTER SYMBOLIC code, though it's been a while.  I still have a copy
of the Medium Systems Assembler Reference, but I no longer have the COBOL 68
manual, and I don't recall the construct 'MVALUE'.  But I will try to give you
a bit of help.  Note that this system addresses digits (half bytes), so the
index register values will be twice the number of bytes.  Lengths and offsets
will be in bytes for DISPLAY fields and digits for COMP fields.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: UNISYS: assembler translation assistance

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc123@bellsouth.net>
- **Date:** 1998-09-23T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<CJbO1.1211$_G3.985767@news2.mia.bellsouth.net>`
- **References:** `<19980923005600.UAA11369@ladder01.news.aol.com> <Wb5O1.327$R_4.752508@news3.mia.bellsouth.net>`

```
Judson McClendon wrote:
>Sff5ky wrote:
>>
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>equivalent to COBOL 85: MOVE 1 TO NCRVAR(51:1).

Sorry, that offset could be in bytes or digits, depending on NCRVAR
being display or comp.  If NCRVAR is display, read this:

MoVe Numeric 1 to field NCRVAR:length 1:offset +100 (+100 bytes)
equivalent to COBOL 85: MOVE 1 TO NCRVAR(101:1).
```

 FILE-CONTROL.
     SELECT NCRVAR               ASSIGN TO TAPE-PE
                                 NO TRANSLATION.
*
 FILE SECTION.
 FD  NCRVAR.
*
 01  NCR-STARCOM-REC-LITTLE         PIC X(3000).
*
 01  NCR-STARCOM-REC-BIG            PIC X(30000).
*
 01  NCR-INPUT-DATA.
     03  FILLER                     PIC X(3).
     03  NCR-REC-ID                 PIC 9(16)   COMP.
     03  FILLER                     PIC X(2989).


Hope that this helps in explaining what the assembler code is doing.

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: UNISYS: assembler translation assistance

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc123@bellsouth.net>
- **Date:** 1998-09-24T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8KrO1.1536$OX1.1532639@news4.mia.bellsouth.net>`
- **References:** `<CJbO1.1211$_G3.985767@news2.mia.bellsouth.net> <19980923223636.00295.00000216@ngol05.aol.com>`

```
Sff5ky wrote:
>
>Judson McClendon writes:
…[26 more quoted lines elided]…
>Hope that this helps in explaining what the assembler code is doing.

Wow, that is Not Good.  A reference to the file name itself refers to
the FIB (File Information Block), constructed by the compiler in the
executable, and maintained by the MCP and compiler generated code
during file I/O.  One good thing is it tells us the reference is COMP,
so the offsets and lengths are in digits.  I have done a lot of 'FIB
fiddling', and used to know the FIB like the back of my hand.  But it
has been so long (>20 years), I don't remember the details.  The COBOL
68 compiler generated code to do record blocking/de-blocking, which
increased the speed of blocked sequential files significantly.  I once
disassembled the code and used a similar technique in a file conversion
utility program.

I pretty much ignored the COBOL code before, but looking at the code
now, it appears that the BOT in 012-NCR-COPY is checking something
like if the next record is in the buffer.  That's a guess, of course,
and I don't know why the test would be done after the READ.  The SUB
following possibly computes the physical block size from the read.
Sorry, but that's the best I can do without a FIB layout.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: UNISYS: assembler translation assistance

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "DENDEN" <DENDEN@prodigy.net>
- **Date:** 1998-09-25T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<01bde88c$b808eca0$LocalHost@denny-s-pc>`
- **References:** `<CJbO1.1211$_G3.985767@news2.mia.bellsouth.net> <19980923223636.00295.00000216@ngol05.aol.com> <8KrO1.1536$OX1.1532639@news4.mia.bellsouth.net>`

```
If it would be of any help, I have an old Burroughs yellowed parchment
named "The Truth About FIBS". This is from my B-2500 days and I didn't do
much Assembler programming then. According to this, a "1" at FIB position
100 indicates a "read forward". As I recall, some of these tricks were used
to handle "off-brand" tapes, such as IBM and NCR :). And this particular
file smells of variable length, to boot.  This type of code was used to
handle record marks, record lengths, etc. I'm wondering if the input file
could be dumped and checked for such things. If it is in fact such a file,
it might be better to forget about the Assembler code entirely, and come up
with a COBOL routine that redefines the buffer area into the possible
record sizes. I.E., 3000, 6000, 9000, etc. up to 30,000. The Unisys DMPALL
can help in determining what's what. Ain't this stuff fun nowadays? <G>.

Denny
```

I am reasonably certain that you are correct in your assumptions.  I know that

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: UNISYS: assembler translation assistance

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc123@bellsouth.net>
- **Date:** 1998-09-26T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<tq4P1.3302$R_4.3034890@news3.mia.bellsouth.net>`
- **References:** `<01bde88c$b808eca0$LocalHost@denny-s-pc> <19980925165625.00294.00000408@ngol05.aol.com>`

```
Sff5ky wrote:
>
>If all this assembler stuff was for dealing with Variable length records, why
>couldn't the program have been developed with standard COBOL syntax to begin
>with?  (Pardon my ignorance  here,  just asking dumb questions.)

The problem comes when you have a tape from another system which is formatted
in a way that your operating system and HLL compiler won't handle properly.
Sometimes there are even hardware considerations.  For example, the Burroughs
Medium Systems tape hardware could not write an odd number of bytes to a block,
and the operating system could not handle odd byte record lengths.  There was
a good efficiency reason for this, but it made reading an odd record length a
problem.  Sometimes you could handle it in COBOL, sometimes you needed assembly.
I used to do a fair amount of media conversion, and sometimes using assembly is
the only, or perhaps the quickest or most efficient, way to get things done.
On the other hand, sometimes programmers just want to bit-fiddle a little. :-)
There may be good reasons why ENTER SYMBOLIC was used here, but perhaps not.
You would have had to been there at the time to know for sure, I suspect.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: UNISYS: assembler translation assistance

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** "DENNIS A BROUSE" <DENDEN@prodigy.net>
- **Date:** 1998-09-28T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<6unrlk$212e$1@newssvr04-int.news.prodigy.com>`
- **References:** `<01bde88c$b808eca0$LocalHost@denny-s-pc> <19980925165625.00294.00000408@ngol05.aol.com>`

```
Judson hit the nail on the head with his reply to your question on "Why use
assembler?".  For whatever reason, old  Burroughs systems did not handle odd
length records, for example 133 character print lines. But with print lines
it didn't matter much.  You could fgure it out from the report.  With media
conversion, one way was to use assembler calls.  But since your FD shows
3000 to 30000 characters, that may or may not be the situation here.  If the
site cannot provide you with a dump of the tape, it's going to be real tough
to figure it out for sure. If the old program is still running, they could
also dump the program while it is executing, and you might be able to use
that. Good luck!

Denny
```

It strikes me that if you look at the program that now creates the 
tape being read with the assistance of Assembler, you will discover 
that it goes to enormous lengths to accurately mimic an archaic NCR 
format. You should look at, and repair/upgrade/simplify, both the 
writing and reading programs, if possible. You might be lucky and 
find that they are both running (or going to run) on the same 
platform.
```

>>how can i determine this ?

*** COBOL Solutions Follow ***

>
>
…[50 more quoted lines elided]…
></PRE></HTML>
```

#### ↳ Re: Part II - This Is Your Brain On SAS...This Is Your Brain On COBOL...Any Questions?

- **From:** "Paul M. Dorfman" <pdorfma@ucs.att.com>
- **Date:** 1998-09-23T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36093AB1.B2B26A3A@ucs.att.com>`
- **References:** `<19980922233436.09267.00000504@ng148.aol.com>`

```
Theo DP, in part, wrote:

> >Jarl Wijman wrote:
> >>
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> FIELDLEN=LENGTH(TRIM(FIELD));

Theo,

Actually, your SAS code is too wordy... Using TRIM function is redundant since
LENGTH function returns the length of a string without trailing blanks, anyway.
Hence,

FIELDLEN = LENGTH(FIELD);

will suffice. Here is some extra couple of items that would make the lives of
fellow COBOL guys a lot easier if they were implemented in COBOL:

1. Length of a string including LEADING and TRAILING blanks:

FIELDLEN = LENGTH(RIGHT(FIELD));
  
2. Length of a string without LEADING blanks:

FIELDLEN = LENGTH(LEFT(FIELD));

3. Length of a string with all blanks eliminated:

FIELDLEN = LENGTH(COMPRESS(FIELD));

4. Length of a string with multiple blanks squeezed into a single blank:

FIELDLEN = LENGTH(COMPBL(FIELD)); 

5. Number of embedded blanks in a string:

NUMBER = LENGTH(LEFT(FIELD)) - LENGTH(COMPRESS(FIELD));

6. Number of times the character '*' appears in a string:

NUMBER = LENGTH(COMPRESS(FIELD)) - LENGTH(COMPRESS(FIELD,"*,' '"));

7. Number of words in a string delimited by blank(s):

NWORDS = (COMPRESS(FIELD) NE ' ') * 
         (LENGTH(LEFT(COMPBL(FIELD)))-LENGTH(COMPRESS(FIELD))+1);

8. A string with leading blanks removed:

FIELD = REVERSE(TRIM(REVERSE(FIELD)));

9. A string shifted 1 character to the right:

FIELD = ' ' || FIELD;

10. A string shifted N characters to the right:

FIELD = REPEAT(' ', N - 1) || FIELD;

11. A string shifted N characters to the left:

FIELD = SUBSTR(FIELD, N + 1);

12. The FIRST nonblank character of a string:

FIRST = SUBSTR(LEFT(FIELD),1,1);

13. The LAST nonblank character of a string:

LAST = SUBSTR(LEFT(REVERSE(FIELD)),1,1);

or

LAST = SUBSTR(REVERSE(RIGHT(STRING)),1,1);

14. The digits of a phone number with slashes, dashes, parentheses removed:

PHONE = COMPRESS(PHONE, '/-()');

15. A phone nnumber with letters converted to digits:

PHONE = TRANSLATE(PHONE,'22233344455566677778889990',
                        'ABCDEFGHIJKLMNOPQRSTUVWXYZ');

16. A left-aligned string:

FIELD = LEFT(FIELD);

17. A right-aligned string:

FIELD = RIGHT(FIELD);

18. A centered string:

FIELD = SUBSTR(RIGHT(FIELD), CEIL(.5 * (LENGTH(RIGHT(FIELD))
                            - LENGTH(LEFT(FIELD)))) + 1;

And so on... Of course, it is just a tiny tip of the iceberg. 

Regards, Paul.   

  
14.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Part II - This Is Your Brain On SAS...This Is Your Brain On COBOL...Any Questions?

- **From:** cadams@cadams-ntw.acucorp.com (Chris Adams)
- **Date:** 1998-09-23T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<slrn70ijlr.bu.cadams@cadams-ntw.acucorp.com>`
- **References:** `<19980922233436.09267.00000504@ng148.aol.com> <36093AB1.B2B26A3A@ucs.att.com>`

```
On Wed, 23 Sep 1998 14:15:13 -0400, Paul M. Dorfman <pdorfma@ucs.att.com> wrote:

I think you might want to consult an ANSI-85 manual, as a number of these are
already implemented. I don't have a function list handy but I think several of
the remaining ones have been implemented in the 1989 addendum.

>5. Number of embedded blanks in a string:
>
>NUMBER = LENGTH(LEFT(FIELD)) - LENGTH(COMPRESS(FIELD));

INSPECT Field TALLYING counter FOR ALL SPACE

>6. Number of times the character '*' appears in a string:
>
>NUMBER = LENGTH(COMPRESS(FIELD)) - LENGTH(COMPRESS(FIELD,"*,' '"));

INSPECT field TALLYING counter FOR ALL "*"

Note that this would also allow things like

INSPECT field TALLYING counter FOR ALL "*" "&" "/" AFTER "q" BEFORE "X".

>7. Number of words in a string delimited by blank(s):
>
>NWORDS = (COMPRESS(FIELD) NE ' ') * 
>         (LENGTH(LEFT(COMPBL(FIELD)))-LENGTH(COMPRESS(FIELD))+1);

UNSTRING Field DELIMITED BY SPACE INTO items
  TALLYING Word-Count

>8. A string with leading blanks removed:
>
>FIELD = REVERSE(TRIM(REVERSE(FIELD)));

INSPECT field TALLYING offset FOR LEADING SPACE
DISPLAY field(offset:)

>9. A string shifted 1 character to the right:
>10. A string shifted N characters to the right:

MOVE source(1:size_of_source - N) TO dest

or

MOVE source(1:size_of_source - N) TO dest(N:)

(I'm not sure exactly which one you wanted)

>11. A string shifted N characters to the left:

MOVE source(N:) TO dest? 


>12. The FIRST nonblank character of a string:
>
>FIRST = SUBSTR(LEFT(FIELD),1,1);

INSPECT field TALLYING offset FOR LEADING SPACE
DISPLAY field(offset:1)

>13. The LAST nonblank character of a string:

A standard INSPECT doesn't provide an easy way to do this; Acucobol does
support an extended INSPECT ... TALLYING FOR TRAILING.  

>15. A phone nnumber with letters converted to digits:
>
>PHONE = TRANSLATE(PHONE,'22233344455566677778889990',
>                        'ABCDEFGHIJKLMNOPQRSTUVWXYZ');

INSPECT phone CONVERTING "22233344455566677778889990" TO
"ABCDEFGHIJKLMNOPQRSTUVWXYZ"

----
The opinions above are my own and may or may not match those of my employer.
```

#### ↳ Re: Part II - This Is Your Brain On SAS...This Is Your Brain On COBOL...Any Questions?

- **From:** Brian Annis <bannis@wnec.edu>
- **Date:** 1998-09-23T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<360962DB.9F615A70@wnec.edu>`
- **References:** `<19980922233436.09267.00000504@ng148.aol.com>`

```
Theo DP wrote:
> COBOL Standards champions, please take note...
> 
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> *** COBOL Solutions Follow ***
<<<CLIPPED>>> 

Since you seem to be more impressed with how concise the code is
than its efficiency, I would suggest as a COBOL version:

    UNSTRING FIELD DELIMITED BY SPACE INTO TRASH COUNT IN FIELDLEN.

where TRASH is any convenient scratch variable. Note that this is
not an equivalent to the SAS version, but solves the original
problem just as well.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Part II - This Is Your Brain On SAS...This Is Your Brain On COBOL...Any Questions?

- **From:** "Paul M. Dorfman" <pdorfma@ucs.att.com>
- **Date:** 1998-09-23T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<360972D2.431C9790@ucs.att.com>`
- **References:** `<19980922233436.09267.00000504@ng148.aol.com> <360962DB.9F615A70@wnec.edu>`

```
Brian Annis wrote:
> 
> Theo DP wrote:
…[16 more quoted lines elided]…
> problem just as well.

Brian,

First, it is I/O handling and not string manipulations where COBOL's performance
superiority versus SAS actually resides. Second, it seems to me that you have
missed Theo's point. It was simply asserted that it would be a lot more
convenient and self-explanatory to be able to write something like

MOVE LENGTH(FIELD) TO FIELDLEN

rather than having the FIELDLEN as a byproduct of UNSTRING statement with the
extra expense of a junk variable, to say nothing of the more verbose COBOL
example posted by Theo. Another big benefit of having functions instead of
corresponding statements is the ability of the functions to be nested. That
allows to avoid using auxiliary variables performing no duty but holding
intermediate results. Whilst it is silly to replace a self-documenting statement
by a cryptic function for the sole purpose of being concise, it is hardly
possible to invent anything more self-explanatory, efficient and syntactically
simple than 

FIELDLEN = LENGTH(FIELD)

(Theo's usage of TRIM function is redundant), regardless of whether it is 'MOVE'
from left to right or '=' from right to left.


Kindest regards,

Paul.
```

Cobol stands for 'Common Business Oriented Language'.

business is wordprocessing....

text lenght. In those days, people used coding forms to input data anyway.
And those nice & lovely punch typists would type exactly what you told them
to. Ah, the good old days. 

Power to the people, but not PC's.

(please don't take this too seriously. I know I don't :)

Gerben 'punch this' van Vlimmeren

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Part II - This Is Your Brain On SAS...This Is Your Brain On COBOL...Any Questions?

- **From:** scottp4.removethis@ibm.net (Scott Peterson)
- **Date:** 1998-09-25T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<360aa142.7907310@news3.ibm.net>`
- **References:** `<19980922233436.09267.00000504@ng148.aol.com> <01bde6d1$614c8500$2c050380@s70>`

```

>> *** SAS Solution Follows ***
>> 
>> FIELDLEN=LENGTH(TRIM(FIELD));
>> 
>> 

Why post B.S. comments like this?  You are comparing use of a function
in one language to a source code implementation in another.  If you
want to be honest, either post a version done is SAS with no function
calls or post the HONEST COBOL equivalent.  Which would be:

	CALL VARLEN using FIELD.

We could probably even be more elegant in versions of COBOL that
support functions.   

So now we've proved COBOL is just as source code simple as SAS.  What
have you accomplished by starting this?
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Part II - This Is Your Brain On SAS...This Is Your Brain On COBOL...Any Questions?

- **From:** theodp@aol.com (Theo DP)
- **Date:** 1998-09-27T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<19980927150554.28784.00003048@ng95.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<360aa142.7907310@news3.ibm.net>`

```

><HTML><PRE>Subject: Re: Part II - This Is Your Brain On SAS...This Is Your
>Brain On COBOL...Any Questions?
…[25 more quoted lines elided]…
></PRE></HTML>

Still scratching my head over your examples...

I too could be more elegant with my SAS examples if I'm allowed to use
imaginary subroutines and features, but I was focusing on using STANDARD
features of a language that have been AVAILABLE FOR YEARS. 

While you and others speculate about the "coulda, woulda, shoulda" of COBOL,
the fact is that other languages have included useful features for years that
COBOL programmers still long for. This leads to unnecessary extra logic and
bugs, which has been illustrated quite well in a number of threads, including
one you participated in (see below)!

Just trying to point out a few areas where the standards bearers could improve
things...

BTW, SAS code to strip leading zeros (correctly) is as simple as:

FIELD2=LEFT(FIELD1);

-------------------------------------------------

mark0young@aol.com (Mark0Young) wrote:

>In article <6gmguj$n1f$2@newsfep3.sprintmail.com>, ramdan@mailexcite.com
(Tony)
>writes:
>
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>


I'm a bit rusty, but couldn't you do the following?  This should be a
lot simpler than using inspect. 

01   TEST-FIELDS.
	05  FIELD1   PIC  s9(04) comp. 
	05  FIELD2   PIC ZZZZZZ9. 
	05  FIELD4   PIC X(08) JUST LEFT. 

Then just 

MOVE FIELD1 TO FIELD2.
MOVE FIELD2 TO FIELD4.

I think this will work.  If it doesn't, you could might have to add: 

	05 FIELD3    PIC X(08) REDEFINES FIELD2. 

then

MOVE FIELD1 TO FIELD2.
MOVE FIELD3 TO FIELD4. 

One of these should work. 

				Scott Peterson

>> 
>Don't work for me.  
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>sender and receiver.  It dosen't modify any bytes.  

That's what I get for trying this from memory. Either that or I'm
mixing languages. <g> You're correct that MF COBOL definitely doesn't
support this..  

		Scott Peterson
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Part II - This Is Your Brain On SAS...This Is Your Brain On COBOL...Any Questions?

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1998-09-27T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<6um87o$fe3@dfw-ixnews9.ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<360aa142.7907310@news3.ibm.net> <19980927150554.28784.00003048@ng95.aol.com>`

```


>
>I too could be more elegant with my SAS examples if I'm allowed to use
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>While you and others speculate about the "coulda, woulda, shoulda" of
COBOL,
>the fact is that other languages have included useful features for years
that
>COBOL programmers still long for. This leads to unnecessary extra logic and
>bugs, which has been illustrated quite well in a number of threads,
including
>one you participated in (see below)!
>
>Just trying to point out a few areas where the standards bearers could
improve
>things...
>


I don't want to "defend or attack" any parts of this conversation so far,
but did want to make a couple of points.

One of the examples of how things could be done in COBOL used the CALL verb.
This was simply trying to demonstrate that a STANDARD program could call a
simple (user-defined routine) to do whatever you want with a string.  With
the draft of the next Standard (and by the way - you will note that there is
no such thing as "Standard SAS" - because SAS is a proprietary language),
you will also be able to have user-defined functions as well as subroutines.
This means that in the next Standard (and those implementations that have it
already as an extension), the CALL statement could be changed to a function
reference - just like the SAS example.

As far as adding things to the Standard, COBOL for various reasons has had
pretty poor string handling facilities.  (STRING, UNSTRING, and INSPECT -
along with some '85 Standard intrinsic functions can do most things - but
are pretty awkward and/or inefficient in many cases).  When the Standards
committee began planning the scope of the current revision two topics that
were discussed but were rejected (and I cannot tell you why) were:

  - improved string handling functions
  - enhancements to report writer to make it compete with "word processing"
programs

In both cases, it would seem to me that the features fit well in the arena
of a "business oriented language" but in both cases, little work has been
done in the current draft Standard.  My guess is that they think that, for
these facilities, there ARE better products than COBOL to do some tasks.  If
I were you and I were interested in COBOL improving its facilities in these
areas, I would send a note to Don Schricker (the chair of J4) at
das@microfocus.com and suggest that the items be added to the (already
existing) list of possible enhancements for a future Standard.

Just FYI,  in my opinion intrinsic functions could be provided for
  - stripping leading/trailing characters
  - finding the location of one string within another string
  - padding (leading/trailing) a string
  - several items existing in languages like SAS, REXX, etc

Note: Most of these CAN be done using INSPECT, STRING, UNSTRING, etc - but
NOT in what I would call an "intuitively obvious" syntax.
```

## Thread: Windows 95 and DLLs (new) — 4 msgs

### Windows 95 and DLLs (new)

- **From:** "Donald Tees" <donald@willmack.com>
- **Date:** 1998-09-23T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<6ub341$auk$1@news.igs.net>`

```
A new DLL topic.

I understand from what I read that a DLL can be
a repository for data.  Is that read only, or can I write
into it?  i.e.-could I use a DLL for a block of global
data that an entire system could use -- set-up data
type of thing?

If so, how do I do I allocate it, etc.?
```

#### ↳ Re: Windows 95 and DLLs (new)

- **From:** "Fujitsu Software" <cobol@adtools.com>
- **Date:** 1998-09-23T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<6ub6go$non$1@nntp2.ba.best.com>`
- **References:** `<6ub341$auk$1@news.igs.net>`

```
Donald,

Yes, you can create a DLL which shares global data for an entire system by
using the EXTERNAL keyword on data item.  This is the way in which
PowerCOBOL shares data between sheets.  For example:

01  MyApplicationState    IS EXTERNAL
                                            PIC 9(4) COMP-5 .

For more information see the DIALOG sample at
http://www.adtools.com/support/samplesv3.htm

Fujitsu Software Corporation
Developer Tools Group

>
