```

Fujitsu Software wrote in message <6ub6go$non$1@nntp2.ba.best.com>...
>Donald,
>Yes, you can create a DLL which shares global data for an entire system by
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…

Well, I downloaded the example you mentioned, and cannot even
open the project.  I get a message saying that it is either not a project
file, or I am out of memory(I have 64 megs). I thought that it could
be a version 3 program, so tried for several hours to convert it, but
could find only references to the fact that there is supposed to be
a conversion utility mentioned in a readme.  I read the 23 readme's
scattered across the CD, but could not find anything but pointers
telling me to look somewhere else.

So I tried it on my own, and could not get it to work at all.  Each program
that calls a DLL seems to have it's own data, no matter what I do.  The
calling program and the DLL seem to be able to share data with an
external declaration, but if a second program running in a second window
calls the same DLL, it gets a new data area, regardless of "EXTERNAL"
and "GLOBAL" declarations.

To repeat the question.  Can a DLL have a block of data that any program
on the computer can set, look at, etc.  If so, how?   "EXTERNAL GLOBAL"
data has to reside somewhere, where is it? ... in the first program that
calls the DLL? Is the DLL itself changed?
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Windows 95 and DLLs (new)

- **From:** "Fujitsu Software" <cobol@adtools.com>
- **Date:** 1998-09-26T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<6uitct$8n0$1@nntp2.ba.best.com>`
- **References:** `<6ub341$auk$1@news.igs.net> <6ub6go$non$1@nntp2.ba.best.com> <6uee2j$p90$1@news.igs.net>`

```
Donald,

A GLOBAL declaration provides access to the data item from within ANSI
COBOL85 Nested Program modules.  An ANSI COBOL85 EXTERNAL declaration is a
shared data item between two or more programs in the same run unit.

In Fujitsu PowerCOBOL if you want to share a data item between separate GUI
events, then you want to designate it as GLOBAL and if you want to share a
data item between separate sheets, then you want to designate it as
EXTERNAL.  You can combine GLOBAL and EXTERNAL so that a data item is shared
between events and sheets.

If you want to share data between two separate COBOL run units then you need
to use Fujitsu Common Communication Interface which is fully demonstrated in
SAMPLE09.

Fujitsu Software Corporation
Developer Tools Group
Phone: (408) 428-0500
FAX:    (408) 428-0600
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: good COBOL Compilers?

- **From:** "Fujitsu Software" <cobol@adtools.com>

```
Fujitsu COBOL assumes that the program is a sub-program unless it is
compiled with the MAIN compiler directive.  Recompile the program with the
MAIN compiler directive and relink.

For a detail step-by-step instructions see the Quick Start Tutorial at
http://www.adtools.com/support/tutorialv3.htm
```

<a id="t-76ccfb8388"></a>
## Thread: DOS disk space allocation for indexed files — 7 msgs

_Topics: [`VSAM, files, sorting`](topics.md#files)_

### DOS disk space allocation for indexed files

- **From:** jcj0347@aol.com (JCJ0347)
- **Date:** 1998-09-23T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<19980923193921.08532.00000808@ng140.aol.com>`

```


In the mini/midrange world we had lots of useful utilities to help manage
the disk space used by data files, especially those highly active transaction
and history files that grow and shrink on a routine basis.  Now I'm in a client
server environment and I'm a little confused.

How DOS does it remains a mystery to me.  Assume I have a history file with
100,000 records and it occupies X bytes.

I purge a set of records from the file, say... 15,000 records, and the file
still
occupies X bytes.  So I run Micro Focus' REBUILD utility, and guess what,
the file still occupies X bytes, less a few in the .IDX where the keys have
been removed.

As an experiement I wrote a quick program to write out a new file using the old
file as input.  Now the new file occupies much less space.

Is this the only solution to recovering disk space when a file has shrunk?

I have a transaction file that may contain up to 3,500 records before the end
of the day, then gets purged, and DOS reports this sucker eats up almost 4Mb 
of disk space.  I need to get this resolved, what are the rest of you in the
PeeCee
world doing?

Thanks,
JC Jones.
```

#### ↳ Re: DOS disk space allocation for indexed files

- **From:** "Donald Tees" <donald@willmack.com>
- **Date:** 1998-09-23T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<6uc629$878$1@news.igs.net>`
- **References:** `<19980923193921.08532.00000808@ng140.aol.com>`

```
JCJ0347 wrote in message <19980923193921.08532.00000808@ng140.aol.com>...

>occupies X bytes.  So I run Micro Focus' REBUILD utility, and guess what,
>the file still occupies X bytes, less a few in the .IDX where the keys have
>been removed.
>
Rebuild has two modes -- "rebuild filename" is the first, and basically
does a completely non-destructive rebuild. That is what you are doing.
That type of rebuild is done to re-org and balance the index tree for
speed, as well as a recovery method for power failures and glitches.
You should set up a batch file to do that on all Isam files, and put it
aside
as an emergency recovery tool.  For ninety-five percent of all user calls
re weird things happening, telling the user to submit that batch file and
call back with the results will take care of it.

(BTW-I redirect the output to the printer in the batch file. When there
are problems still there, you tell the user to fax you the printer output,
and you will be able to take care of another 4 1/2 % of the problems
on the next call)

Now, that type of rebuild always leaves your data file unaffected, and so
can be done over and over again. If you have to patch around a bad block
or something, you can null out the bad data and recover what is left, etc.
Problem is, as you discovered, it does not remove empty space in the data
file.  For that, you run the rebuild like so: Rebuild infile, outfile. That
produces
a brand new output, all nicely cleaned up, but it is a brand new file.  The
cycle
can be ["backup", "rebuild backup to newfile", "start again"] or ["rename",
"rebuild oldfile to newfile", "startagain"].  Eithor way, you are in trouble
if
it fails and the user trys again. That is, run it twice, and you
self-destruct.

That is because the only safe sequence is really "do it", if it fails, you
have
to know exactly why to avoid disaster. You may want to do an extra backup
and put it aside while you think about it.  So I seldom give such procedures
to any but the very safest users. (twice in the last fifteen years, and the
one
guy destroyed himself.)

Over the years, I have found the safest way is as follows.  You set up
maintenance
such that purges are regular and often (daily or weekly).  The idea is to
keep
your data file allocated but empty after purges.  In other words, the data
file sort of hits a constant size that is comfortable. When you do a defrag,
you end up with a block of continginous data on the disk that is "MY SPACE".
Nice and efficient.  Then on a daily basis, you run that recovery batch
file.
That keeps your index's nice and tight.

Have the "compress data" batch file ready and tested for a rainy day,
but put it aside in an area for the pro running the place (or self). I
usually
run it only when I am on site fixing a problem, or am tidying up at year
ends.

Last, but not least, rebuild will tell you how to do the above by going
into DOS and typing "rebuild help" or "rebuild/?" or something like that.
I always have to do it when I put the batch files together.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: DOS disk space allocation for indexed files

- **From:** "Sven Brueggemann" <Sven.Brueggemann@brem.ftn.de>
- **Date:** 1998-09-24T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<0000bf6c@ftn.de>`
- **References:** `<6uc629$878$1@news.igs.net>`

```
Dear Donald,

DT> your data file allocated but empty after purges.  In other words, the
DT> data file sort of hits a constant size that is comfortable. When you
DT> do a defrag, you end up with a block of continginous data on the disk
DT> that is "MY SPACE". Nice and efficient.  Then on a daily basis, you
DT> run that recovery batch file. That keeps your index's nice and tight.

Well - nice thought but it does not do what you want to achieve. By  
running your recovery batch file (rebuild file) the index is rebuild and  
it's free space list is deleted so that "MY SPACE" is never reused and the  
file keeps on growing when adding records.

Best regards

Sven

Send a mail with subject "SEND PGPKEY" to receive my public key
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: DOS disk space allocation for indexed files

- **From:** jcj0347@aol.com (JCJ0347)
- **Date:** 1998-09-24T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<19980924013440.28474.00001049@ng121.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<6uc629$878$1@news.igs.net>`

```

The REBUILD INFILE, OUTFILE  is just like COPY WITH REORG on some other
platforms I've worked on.  You're right, it can cause major
problems in the hands of an inexperienced user.

BTW just typing REBUILD will list the parms.  I always like to see a
running count but can never remember whether it's /v or /e.

Thanks again,
JC Jones
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: DOS disk space allocation for indexed files

- **From:** gvwmoore@ix.netcom.com (G Moore)
- **Date:** 1998-09-26T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3611ed01.3362453@nntp.ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<19980923193921.08532.00000808@ng140.aol.com> <6uc629$878$1@news.igs.net>`

```
On Wed, 23 Sep 1998 21:13:03 -0400, "Donald Tees"
<donald@willmack.com> wrote:

>For that, you run the rebuild like so: Rebuild infile, outfile. That
>produces
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
>to know exactly why to avoid disaster. 

speaking of which, i was writing a online database defrag spec... it's
on comp.databases from a few days ago.

something like a binary tree, BUT...

the data file has a pointer back to the binary tree's pointer,
and a size, and a deleted boolean variable.

gvwmoore@ix.spam.netcom.com to reply remove the spam
```

#### ↳ Re: DOS disk space allocation for indexed files

- **From:** "Sven Brueggemann" <Sven.Brueggemann@brem.ftn.de>
- **Date:** 1998-09-24T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<0000bf6b@ftn.de>`
- **References:** `<19980923193921.08532.00000808@ng140.aol.com>`

```
Dear JC,

j> How DOS does it remains a mystery to me.  Assume I have a history file
j> with 100,000 records and it occupies X bytes.
j> I purge a set of records from the file, say... 15,000 records, and the
j> file still occupies X bytes.  So I run Micro Focus' REBUILD utility,
j> and guess what, the file still occupies X bytes, less a few in the .IDX
j> where the keys have been removed.

This behavior is not (only) related to DOS file handling. If you delete a  
record from your file, the record is marked as deleted and the space it  
consumes is entered into the free space list (FSL) in the IDX file. If you  
add a record to your file, you will see that the file does not grow as  
long as there are enough deleted records in the file.

That REBUILD had no effect is simply because you did not use it correctly.  
If you use the syntax "REBUILD file" the index (and only the index) is  
rebuild. By doing this you even delete the FSL so that unused space in the  
data file cannot be reused.

Try reorganizing the file using the syntax "REBUILD infile,outfile".

Best regards

Sven

Send a mail with subject "SEND PGPKEY" to receive my public key
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: DOS disk space allocation for indexed files

- **From:** jcj0347@aol.com (JCJ0347)
- **Date:** 1998-09-25T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<19980925151549.22573.00001741@ng76.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<0000bf6b@ftn.de>`

```

>Try reorganizing the file using the syntax "REBUILD
>infile,outfile".

Yeppers, that works just fine.  Since I'm a W2 here I don't
have to worry about a ham-fisted user renaming and deleting
the wrong files.

I only have to worry about my own ham-fisted renaming and
deleting the wrong files :-)

Thanks to all,
JC Jones.
```

<a id="t-bfc13cc8ae"></a>
## Thread: COBOL for MVS - using LIBEXIT — 3 msgs

_Topics: [`Mainframe, z/OS, JCL, CICS`](topics.md#mainframe)_

### COBOL for MVS - using LIBEXIT

- **From:** brixford@my-dejanews.com
- **Date:** 1998-09-23T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<6ub7jd$oj7$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>`

```
I am trying to use the LIBEXIT in COBOL for MVS (or COBOL II for that matter)
to filter the copybooks before compilation.  I am getting a very fatal Cobol
error when I pass the record back thru the linkage.  I am passing the length,
returncode and am 80 byte buffer but the compile burps and terminates.  I can
call the exit and not pass back a buffer and the base program will compile.
The IBM example shows a SYSIN exit and I copied that code BUT it does not work
on the LIBEXIT.

Clues???

-----== Posted via Deja News, The Leader in Internet Discussion ==-----
http://www.dejanews.com/rg_mkgrp.xp   Create Your Own Free Member Forum
```

#### ↳ Re: COBOL for MVS - using LIBEXIT

- **From:** skyraven@home.com (Sue L. )
- **Date:** 1998-09-24T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<360bb620.14528365@news>`
- **References:** `<6ub7jd$oj7$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>`

```
>The IBM example shows a SYSIN exit and I copied that code BUT it does not work
>on the LIBEXIT.

You have a SYSIN... do you have a SYSOUT on there as well?? 

I know it's a dumb question but I have to ask....if not that could
very well be your problem...
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL for MVS - using LIBEXIT

- **From:** brixford@my-dejanews.com
- **Date:** 1998-09-26T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<6uhr5k$nlq$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>`
- **References:** `<6ub7jd$oj7$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com> <360bb620.14528365@news>`

```
I could  have been a little vague.  The SYSIN I was referring to was the
replacement code for the SYSIN reader that is supplied with the complier. 
The LIBEXIT is an EXITstub that will replace the code that the compiler uses
to read copy books (expanding COPY CPYBOOK staements by reading the PDS) with
your own reader.  The sample code from IBM shows only the code to replace the
SYSIN reader and not the Copybook reader.

On MVS, THE exit can be invoked via:
//GOSTEP EXEC PGM=IGYCRCTL,REGION=2M,COND=(5,LT),
//       PARM='EXIT(LIBEXIT(''SRT2'',BPAM))'

where BPAM is the program to exec to read the copybooks.  I was going to use
the exit to filter, on the fly, copybook source. But it will take a superior
programmer than I.

PS. Librarian systems(PANVALET, LIBRARIAN, CHANGEMAN) use this exit.


-----== Posted via Deja News, The Leader in Internet Discussion ==-----
http://www.dejanews.com/rg_mkgrp.xp   Create Your Own Free Member Forum
```

- **Date:** 1998-09-23T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol

```
I don't know if all the comp.lang.cobol people who might be interested in
this, already know about it.  The following message is from the IBM-MAIN
group.

Jim Mulder wrote in message <5010300021667893000002L032*@MHS>...
>>   The machine/OS synching requirements will not be as stringent as those
for
> >MVS/XA and MVS/ESA.  The 64 bit OS/390 will not require a 64 bit
addressing
> >machine - it will run on older machines.
>
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>VxRy will provide 64 bit functions only on machines which have 64 bit
addressing
>capability.  For ease of migration, you will also be able to run OS/390
VxRy
>in non-64 bit mode (i.e. it will function the same as it would on a non-64
bit
>machine, and support expanded storage) on a 64 bit capable machine.  And of
>
>course, older releases of OS/390 will run (but provide no 64 bit functions)
on
>64 bit capable machines.
>
>Jim Mulder   OS/390 System Test   IBM Corp.  S/390 Division  Poughkeepsie,
NY
```

<a id="t-fc8d8cb9b2"></a>
## Thread: IBM COBOL - CALLING OS COMMANDS — 2 msgs

_Topics: [`Compilers and vendors`](topics.md#compilers)_

### IBM COBOL - CALLING OS COMMANDS

- **From:** "Tom McCormick" <tmccormick@amp.com>
- **Date:** 1998-09-23T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<01bde6fc$ae1e2480$02d011cc@tmccormick.gb.amp.com>`

```
Simple question,

We have recently "upgraded" from MF COBOL to IBM COB2, we have about 6
COBOL programs, key to these programs is the ability to CALL an OS function
in our case an AIX 4.0 UNIX shell script. Using MF COBOL we called SYSTEM
passing the shell script name as a parameter, the syntax is incorrect for
IBM COBOL I only have postscript manual which I can't search.

My question is how can I run (Call) an OS command from the COBOL programs


TIA

rcooper@amp.com
rcooper@psesoft.com
rcooper@netcomuk.co.uk
```

#### ↳ Re: IBM COBOL - CALLING OS COMMANDS

- **From:** paulr@bix.com (paulr)
- **Date:** 1998-09-24T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<6uc56q$ugb@lotho.delphi.com>`
- **References:** `<01bde6fc$ae1e2480$02d011cc@tmccormick.gb.amp.com>`

```
Off the top of my head, you could call the "_system" call in
the CLIB library directly of course, but that may not be very 
portable. Why not try something like this? 

Create a C object file with a function like this in it:

> $ cat systemcall.c          
> void systemcall( char *cmd) 
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> $                           

Then create a COBOL program something like this:

> $ cat c.cbl                                             
>       **********************                            
…[17 more quoted lines elided]…
> $                                                         

Then it is very simple to link them together, and you simply
change your null terminated string to whatever command or
set of commands you want to execute. 


> $ cc -c systemcall.c
> $ cob2 -q"pgmname(mixed) callint(optlink) adata" -o c c.cbl systemcall.o
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> End of compilation 1,  program C,  highest severity: Informational.  

Running the "c" program created fro the COBOL compile works just
as expected. 

You can find more examples of this, and probably better ones 
on pp. 376-385 of the Programming Guide.  

I tested this under AIX 4.2.1 by the way. I assume it will work
just fine under 4.3. 

Yours,
-Paul





















: We have recently "upgraded" from MF COBOL to IBM COB2, we have about 6
: COBOL programs, key to these programs is the ability to CALL an OS function
: in our case an AIX 4.0 UNIX shell script. Using MF COBOL we called SYSTEM
: passing the shell script name as a parameter, the syntax is incorrect for
: IBM COBOL I only have postscript manual which I can't search.

: My question is how can I run (Call) an OS command from the COBOL programs


: TIA

: rcooper@amp.com
: rcooper@psesoft.com
: rcooper@netcomuk.co.uk
```

```
PY wrote:
> 
> Hello, I'm looking for a *good* shareware compiler or interpreter, can

> Thanks ;)

Can't say how good it is, but try surfing over to:
http://www.shareware.com/

Then do a search for DOS programs using the keyword COBOL.  There's a
free COBOL interpreter there.  I believe the same COBOL interpreter is
also included on the "COBOL for Dummies" CD-ROM, and they have versions
of it for windows and Unix.

If you can find "COBOL for Dummies" at your local bookstore, the CD
included with it contains a version of the Fujitsu COBOL compiler and a
couple of other compilers (NetExpress?, AcuCOBOL?).  The CD alone is
worth the price of the book.

Regards,
```

### COBOL on AS/400...HELP!!!!

- **From:** "William R. Fink" <bfink@apci.net>
- **Date:** 1998-09-24T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<6udi25$nsf@mhstl1.monsanto.com>`

```
I know next to nothing about the AS/400.  I need to know the proper way to
set up a library, and file, and a queue for compiling a COBOL program.
Additionally, I need to know how to compile and run COBOL.

I have had some luck setting up everything, and even compiling; but can not
find my compiler listings.


Are there any AS/400 people out there that could help?  Are there any web
sites for AS/400 programmers?

Bill Fink
bfink@apci.net
```

#### ↳ Re: COBOL on AS/400...HELP!!!!

- **From:** terrywin.nospam.@spectra.net (Terry Winchester)
- **Date:** 1998-09-24T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<360aabf2.2862311@supernews.spectra.net>`
- **References:** `<6udi25$nsf@mhstl1.monsanto.com>`

```
On Thu, 24 Sep 1998 08:32:18 -0500, "William R. Fink" <bfink@apci.net>
wrote:

>I know next to nothing about the AS/400.  I need to know the proper way to
>set up a library, and file, and a queue for compiling a COBOL program.
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
>
Bill,

Your compiler output generally will go to the default queue set up
under your user profile.  Use the WRKSPLF command to see _all_
of your printed output that is currently linked to your present
user-id.  Then you will see what output queue is assigned to
your compile listing.

Terry
```

```
-Paul
```

```
To find your spoolfile, use the WRKSPLF command. It will show all your spool
files.

Also, when you compile, make sure you prompt the command or option with F4,
and make sure you have specified the listing be printed.

Also, Try www.as400/ibm/com - Almost every IBM AS/400 manual is online there.
The site is being remodeled, but the manuals are out there.

Bob Larkin
```

<a id="t-47c82b1354"></a>
## Thread: Record area availability after EOF - J4 Response. — 5 msgs

_Topics: [`COBOL standards (ANS, ISO, 74/85/2002/2014)`](topics.md#standards)_

### Record area availability after EOF - J4 Response.

- **From:** redsky@ibm.net (Thane Hubbell)
- **Date:** 1998-09-24T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<Jl0PnHJ5PvPd-pn2-qs6v7nuT9X6v@Dwight_Miller.iix.com>`

```
Well, It's not an OFFICIAL response, but it's as far as I care to take
it at this point - it agrees with my understanding and that of others 
posted here.  You CAN move something to the record area after EOF - 
the "undefined" portion is the CONTENTS and not the AREA itself.  Or 
so it seems.... The response from the J4 Chair, with my original query
at the bottom:
----------------------------------------------------------------------
-----
Thane,

This is my personal opinion, not an official interpretation of J4.
If you want to submit an official interpretation request to J4,
let me know.  However, J4 is giving priority to preparation of the
next COBOL standard, so a swift reply should not be expected.

The content of the record area after a READ statement
that results in the AT END condition is analogous to the content of
the record area after a WRITE statement. Both are undefined.  

As you stated, the record area is available after the OPEN statement 
and is no longer available after the CLOSE statement.  There are no
words about the record being unavailable after the AT END condition, 
only words that say "the content of the associated record area is
undefined".

Sincerely,
Don Schricker

Thane Hubbell wrote:
> 
> There is a difference of opinion among past and present J4 committee
…[27 more quoted lines elided]…
> Thank You.
```

#### ↳ Re: Record area availability after EOF - J4 Response.

- **From:** "Warren G. Simmons" <warrens@inficad.com>
- **Date:** 1998-09-24T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<360AC27A.A644FEA2@inficad.com>`
- **References:** `<Jl0PnHJ5PvPd-pn2-qs6v7nuT9X6v@Dwight_Miller.iix.com>`

```


Thane Hubbell wrote:

> Well, It's not an OFFICIAL response, but it's as far as I care to take
> it at this point - it agrees with my understanding and that of others
> posted here.  You CAN move something to the record area after EOF -

--------rest snipped for bandwidth-----------

Hopefully, these concerned do NOT include pseudo records created for
internal
control purposes.  At one time, some files that required multi-volume media
had end-of-media records written by the file creation program.  This record
might also contain some "management information".  It even might contain an
intermediate recovery routine.  The last media of this file would contain a
final version of this record and possible other information.  First and
subsequent media probably had start of file, or continue file records at the
front of the file, too.

As such such records would be within the bounds of the OS read/write
responsibility AND could be accessed by the program.  This illustrates the
only case I ever saw that involved accessing file buffers before/after
records were available/no longer available, and then the sentinel records
were also defined as logical records that everyone understood.

Warren Simmons
```

#### ↳ Re: Record area availability after EOF - J4 Response.

- **From:** "Joel C. Ewing" <jcewing@acm.org>
- **Date:** 1998-09-25T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<360B2EA5.17A19A04@acm.org>`
- **References:** `<Jl0PnHJ5PvPd-pn2-qs6v7nuT9X6v@Dwight_Miller.iix.com>`

```
Thane Hubbell wrote:
> Well, It's not an OFFICIAL response, but it's as far as I care to take
> it at this point - it agrees with my understanding and that of others
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> Thane,
...
> 
> The content of the record area after a READ statement
…[32 more quoted lines elided]…
> > the AREA still exists and may be manipulated in the COBOL program.
...
> --
> Don Schricker
> doncobol@mediaone.net   978-887-6692 (Massachusetts, USA)

Assuming that record storage can be referenced after an EOF sounds like
a risky proposition to me.  The fact that readers of the standard are
uncertain whether the wording "undefined" in this context might also
mean "undefineable" suggests that compiler designers might also feel
justified in assuming the most convenient (for them) interpretation.  I
would argue that it is certainly unclear what should be the proper
semantic interpretation associated with changing "record storage" when
no record exists.

Purely from the standpoint of one familiar with the Assembler QSAM I/O
macros on MVS (used by COBOL under the covers), COBOL is doing the
equivalent of a LOCATE Mode GET to return a pointer to (the address of)
the next record in the actual I/O buffer pool.  When an EOF is detected,
"GET" branches to an EOF address and NO address is returned.  What value
does the COBOL runtime assume for its record pointer in this case?  It
may by default be the last valid record pointer for the previous record,
but this sounds like an implementation dependent happenstance that could
change in the future.  If it pointed instead just after the last record
in the last block read, references to this storage might work most of
the time but occasionally do "strange" things when this happens to hit
on the last block in the buffer pool and changes affect storage outside
the buffer pool.  The program could appear to function OK in testing,
yet exhibit "nondeterministic" behavior in production.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Record area availability after EOF - J4 Response.

- **From:** trblshtr@my-dejanews.com
- **Date:** 1998-09-25T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<6ug530$t6l$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>`
- **References:** `<Jl0PnHJ5PvPd-pn2-qs6v7nuT9X6v@Dwight_Miller.iix.com> <360B2EA5.17A19A04@acm.org>`

```

> Thane Hubbell wrote:
> > Well, It's not an OFFICIAL response, but it's as far as I care to take
> > it at this point - it agrees with my understanding and that of others
> > posted here.  You CAN move something to the record area after EOF -
> > the "undefined" portion is the CONTENTS and not the AREA itself.  Or
<snip>
> > As you stated, the record area is available after the OPEN statement
> > and is no longer available after the CLOSE statement.  There are no
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> > Don Schricker
  "Joel C. Ewing" <jcewing@acm.org> wrote:
> Assuming that record storage can be referenced after an EOF sounds like
> a risky proposition to me.  The fact that readers of the standard are
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
> "GET" branches to an EOF address and NO address is returned.
<snip>
This is, I believe, essentially correct.  However, I think you will find that
the "runtime" either does, or does not, MOV data from the low-level
I/O buffer/record area to the storage area defined for the "record" by
the "program" itself OR in those cases where the low-level buffer
itself is used does, or does not, update the pointer in the "program"
itself.  In either case, the buffer/record area itself would still be
available (with CONTENT undefined) until the issuance of the
CLOSE and subsequent release of resources associated with the
"program" itself.  In the latter "pointer" case if the "runtime" did,
in fact, release the buffer/set the pointer to what would be
essentially be an "illegal" address either a) it would be intelligent
enough to generate an error if the "program" attempts to use
that illegal address or b) it would be a very poor compiler/runtime.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Record area availability after EOF -

- **From:** riplin@kcbbs.gen.nz (Richard Plinston)
- **Date:** 1998-09-26T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3298268.72537.5634@kcbbs.gen.nz>`
- **References:** `<360B2EA5.17A19A04@acm.org>`

```
In message <<360B2EA5.17A19A04@acm.org>> "Joel C. Ewing" <jcewing@acm.org> writes:
> 
> Assuming that record storage can be referenced after an EOF sounds like
> a risky proposition to me.  The fact that readers of the standard are
> uncertain whether the wording "undefined" in this context might also
> mean "undefineable" suggests that compiler designers might also feel

Their 'uncertainty' (actually they _were_ certain, just wrong)
is based entirely on misreading.  They had read the word 
'undefined' and assumed it referred to the record area,
whereas it actually quite clearly stated 'the content of the
record area'.

> justified in assuming the most convenient (for them) interpretation.  I
> would argue that it is certainly unclear what should be the proper
> semantic interpretation associated with changing "record storage" when
> no record exists.

They would actually read the standard instead of using some
vauge idea about what it said.

> 
> Purely from the standpoint of one familiar with the Assembler QSAM I/O
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
> yet exhibit "nondeterministic" behavior in production.

You are assumming that Cobol is very simplistic in its use
of this underlying mechanism.  It is usually inappropriate
to simply have the record area point to a position in the
file buffer.  It is (in many cases) valid for the program
to set the record area to any arbitrary values for, say,
doing a START or random READ, or even to REWRITE the
record.  You certainly wouldn't want this to happen in
the file buffer as it may constitute corruption.

In fact with a variable length record it is quite valid
to change it and REWRITE (in general) and if this is
done in the buffer it may overwrite the next record(s)
or cause the system to lose track of where the next record 
is.

If Cobol did use the mechanism you described that it
certainly would have already exhibited non-determinsitic
behaviour.

Perhaps 40 years ago Cobol did use that mechanism and
there were problems.  'Tribal memory' in the form of
site standards were developed and, though Cobol 
specified exactly what should happen, many rely
on ancient taboos and folk medicines rather than
understand the language.
```

## Thread: building MF DLLs in 32 bit — 1 msgs

### building MF DLLs in 32 bit

- **From:** shrmtech@myna.com
- **Date:** 1998-09-24T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3609c3df.2270935@news.myna.com>`

```
Greetings,

I have two questions:

1) I am looking for sample source or some pointers for building a 32
bit DLL that includes other statically linked *.CBL files using MF WB
95/NTver 4.0 . We have been able to call a single source cobol routine
in a DLL by using "call-convention 74 is staticNTStdCall" for the
routine to be exported. However, we are having problems calling other
routines from within the cobol DLL that we thought were statically
linked into the DLL and are intended as external functions. Whenever,
the routines that reside in other *.CBL files are invoked, the parent
program that calls the DLL terminates without any messages. The
statically linked programs in cobol are not called with the convention
above since they are internal to the DLL. Is there anything special
declaration required ? All Microfocus examples I have seen via fax or
otherwise are several years old and do not address 32 bit development.


2) When running animator, there are programs that are in DLLs that we
would like to step over. But what happens is we see a message to the
effect that "the file or directory is not found". What we do next is
set a break point after the function call and run but we still see
this message. In the past, we would recompile the called source with
debug turned on but this seems silly to have to do so. We should be
able to "step over" and allow function to be called but not debugged.

Any information on these questions is greatly appreciated.
Thank you,

Nawien
```

```
UNIX. I want to know what is the cause behind the same and how it can be

-----== Posted via Deja News, The Leader in Internet Discussion ==-----
http://www.dejanews.com/rg_mkgrp.xp   Create Your Own Free Member Forum
```

#### ↳ Re: About character "=="

- **From:** scm@enterprise.net (Shaun C. Murray)
- **Date:** 1998-09-24T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<6ud8a8$guj$1@news.enterprise.net>`
- **References:** `<6ucsm3$fqs$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>`

```
In article <6ucsm3$fqs$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>, manoj2671@my-dejanews.com says...
>
>The character ï¿½==ï¿½ developed under SCREENS utility of MF COBOL undr DOS
>returns some junk values on the Video when compiled and executed under SCO
>UNIX. I want to know what is the cause behind the same and how it can be
>rectified ?

I don't know what you mean by the character "==" but the reason a character on 
a DOS machine doesn't show under UNIX is usually because of the terminal type 
or code page of the DOS machine. Not knowing either I can only guess that they 
don't match.

If your DOS is American, it'll have codepage 437. Europe is 850. Certain 
characters are different. Under UNIX, you have ANSI, VT100, ISO8859 etc. I 
can't tell from your domain so I'd guess you need to set codepage 437 on DOS 
and TERM=ANSI under SCO. Their may even be a TERM=IBM PC under SCO.
```

<a id="t-f46559181d"></a>
## Thread: Determining string length & right justifying — 1 msgs

_Topics: [`Language features and syntax`](topics.md#syntax)_

### Determining string length & right justifying

- **From:** Bill Lynch <wblynch@att.net>
- **Date:** 1998-09-24T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<360A106F.F260DD0E@att.net>`

```
This is not very timely, but I just discovered the following routine
which extracts the numeric portion of a BMS input field and moves the
number, right justified, to a second field:

      *--------------------------------------------------------------*
      *    First step is to extract the numeric part of XCTLIDI      *
      *    (1-15 decimal digits, S-1 contains the byte count WHEN    *
      *    we're done)                                               *
      *--------------------------------------------------------------*
           PERFORM VARYING  S-2 FROM  +1  BY  +1
                     UNTIL  S-2        >   LENGTH OF XCTLIDI
              IF  XCTLIDI (S-2:1)     NUMERIC
                   MOVE  S-2           TO  S-1
               ELSE
                   MOVE  +999          TO  S-2
               END-IF
           END-PERFORM

      *--------------------------------------------------------------*
      *    and right justify it in our work area                     *
      *--------------------------------------------------------------*
           MOVE  LENGTH OF WS-ABSTIME-WORKA    TO  S-2
           INITIALIZE                  WS-ABSTIME-WORKA
           SUBTRACT  S-1               FROM  S-2
           ADD   +1                    TO  S-2

      *--------------------------------------------------------------*
      *    Move just the numeric part of XCTLIDI into the ABSTIME    *
      *    work area, right justified                                *
      *--------------------------------------------------------------*
           MOVE  XCTLIDI (1:S-1)       TO  WS-ABSTIME-WORKA (S-2:S-1)
           MOVE  WS-ABSTIME-WORKA      TO  WS-MONGO-DATE
           PERFORM  G300-DISPLAY-ABSTIME

The fields are defined as:

	05	XCTLIDI			PIC  X(44).
	05	WS-MONGO-DATE		PIC  S9(15).
	05	WS-ABSTIME-WORKA	PIC  X(15).    (not certain)
	05	S-1			PIC  S9999  BINARY.
	05	S-2			PIC  S9999  BINARY.

Hope someone finds this useful,
Bill Lynch
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Environment variables

- **From:** as999@torfree.net (Adrian Boldan)
- **Date:** 1998-09-28T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<F008CJ.Eo0.0.bloor@torfree.net>`
- **References:** `<01bde8d9$8f2425a0$62973586@mis8mk> <6uldl5$8av$1@news.megsinet.net>`

```
Darius Cooper (Darius_cooper@Bigfoot.com) wrote:
: I don't have my manual handy, but I think MF-COBOL has functions that can be
: CALLed to do what you want.

: CFC wrote in message <01bde8d9$8f2425a0$62973586@mis8mk>...
: >What I am trying to do is reset an environment variable within a cobol
: >program, for use later in another cobol program in my job-stream.

Are you sure that you remain in the same environment after changing the 
variable? If, for instance, you are in Windows, each window inherits the 
environment variables from the operating system, but doesn't modify the 
original. If the next program in your stream is clonning a new 
environment... that will be your problem.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Environment variables

- **From:** AS-DATA@t-online.de (Andreas Strzoda)
- **Date:** 1998-09-28T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<6un9cd$pha$2@news00.btx.dtag.de>`
- **References:** `<01bde8d9$8f2425a0$62973586@mis8mk> <6uldl5$8av$1@news.megsinet.net>`

```
Darius Cooper schrieb:

> I don't have my manual handy, but I think MF-COBOL has functions that
> can be
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
> >Does anyone know of another method to do this.

   You can't change the environment-variable, becaouse every task
start's
with a copy of the environment-variables. You have to backtrace the
call-structure in memory to
find out the root-environment (..not very easy in DOS/WIN..) and then
you may
change the value (depending on size etc.)

No programm directly support these way, so you have to write your own
assembler-routine
to change the value permanently.

AS
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Environment variables

- **From:** Haluk Okur <spbis@sbs.de>
- **Date:** 1998-09-28T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<360FBCD9.2E97@sbs.de>`
- **References:** `<01bde8d9$8f2425a0$62973586@mis8mk> <6uldl5$8av$1@news.megsinet.net> <6un9cd$pha$2@news00.btx.dtag.de>`

```
Andreas Strzoda wrote:
> 
>    You can't change the environment-variable, becaouse every task
> start's
> with a copy of the environment-variables.
....
> AS


My Unix-days are long gone but I seem to remember the "export"
statement which passed the new value of an environment variable
to the parent shell. Can't this can be done from within COBOL
also?
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Environment variables

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** Kevin Digweed <ked@mfltd.co.uk>
- **Date:** 1998-09-28T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<360F954D.19E521C0@mfltd.co.uk>`
- **References:** `<01bde8d9$8f2425a0$62973586@mis8mk> <6uldl5$8av$1@news.megsinet.net> <6un9cd$pha$2@news00.btx.dtag.de> <360FBCD9.2E97@sbs.de>`

```
Haluk Okur wrote:
> 
> Andreas Strzoda wrote:
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
> also?

No. You're thinking of Bourne shell (and it's derivatives) which use
the same syntax for working with local variables and "environment"
variables (ie, X=1; print $X). "export" simply tags a variable name
as "external" (inherited by sub-processes) rather than local to the
shell.

Obviously, this doesn't apply to COBOL programs, whose local variables
are in the data division and are different beasts altogether from
environment variables, so the equivalent of "export" is implied.

Back to the original post - it states that the DISPLAY syntax is used
to modify the environment variable, but the order it gives is
DISPLAY ... UPON ENVIRONMENT-VALUE, DISPLAY ... UPON ENVIRONMENT-NAME.
This is the wrong order - you must set the NAME before you set (or
read) the VALUE.

Cheers,
Kev.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Help...

- **From:** riplin@kcbbs.gen.nz (Richard Plinston)
- **Date:** 1998-09-26T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3298268.70634.2111@kcbbs.gen.nz>`
- **References:** `<19980925221123.29328.00001652@ng124.aol.com>`

```
In message <<19980925221123.29328.00001652@ng124.aol.com>> jcj0347@aol.com  writes:
> +
> +1/1/99
> Why should this be any more special than 1/1/98 ?

Because a system may have had built into it that year '99'
(or indeed any specific year) is to be used for 'unknown'
dates and dealt with differently from other numbers.

There is a widely used accounting system here (not mine,
not Cobol) that used year 99 in this way.  On April 1
this year the new 'year end' date was input as end March
1999 and various problems appeared in the system.  For
example forward-dated invoices came back out of the 
system immediately.

It was easy enough to change year-end to 31/12/98
until a patch arrived, but date related problems are
not restricted just to 2000.

> +9/9/99
> Why would there be a problem with this? 9 Sept 1999 would *not* be
> stored as 9999, as many people seem to think, but as 990909. I see
> no reason to believe that anything will break on that day.

Because 9/9/99 may be treated in the system as an 'unknown'
date.  The program may require a valid date be entered but
in cases where the date is simply not known a special
date (or year) is used as a 'flag' to indicate that it
is, for example, not to be processed.

These only apply to systems where this trick was used. The
problem is knowing whether the system in use at _your_
site uses this trick, or did use it years ago in the
source code that you cannot find.
```

#### ↳ Re: Help...

- **From:** docdwarf@clark.net ()
- **Date:** 1998-09-28T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<6uo9nq$98e$1@clarknet.clark.net>`

## Thread: Fujitsu Cobol Vaporware! — 2 msgs

_Topics: [`Compilers and vendors`](topics.md#compilers)_

### Re : Fujitsu Cobol Vaporware!

- **From:** dmacary@cima-inc.com
- **Date:** 1998-09-25T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<6ugp9s$ir9$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>`

```
Fujitsu Cobol is definitely not vaporware.  I was attracted to its
promise of being able to use ActiveX components, and generate COM
compliant objects.  These features either don't work very well or are
not sufficiently documented to figure them out.  I am still waiting for
specific example programs that show how to use MTS.  Several ActiveX
controls I tried generated protection faults, although I got some to
work too.  I never really got the PowerCobol debugger to work consistently
and I'm not sure why.  Emails to Fujitsu took many weeks to be answered.
I like the way it  handles ODBC and SQL but the requirement to have all
host variables as 01 Levels is a killer for me.   Also a killer is the
SQL whenever only allows a "GO TO" and not a "PERFORM".
I couldn't justify its use for deployment of our complex enterprise
application because of these factors.  For small scale stuff, it probably
is Ok.


-----== Posted via Deja News, The Leader in Internet Discussion ==-----
http://www.dejanews.com/rg_mkgrp.xp   Create Your Own Free Member Forum
```

## Thread: Help on Winapi — 1 msgs

_Topics: [`Help requests and how-to`](topics.md#help)_

### RE: RE: Help on Winapi

- **From:** "Information Services" <h&p@tic.ab.ca>
- **Date:** 1998-09-25T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<01bde8ca$f1635a60$e5dca1c6@hp>`

```
How do I connect the printer object from a COBOL program?  I cannot find
anything on connecting except a second monitor.

Patti Sommer


> Does any one know how to use the MFcobol 'WINAPI' command to write a
string
> of data to the com-port?  I need to send a string of data to the Thermal
> Printer which is connected to com1 or com2.   I am using "CreateFileA" 
to
> open the port, "GetCommState" to get the status and "SetCommState" to
> ensure the status is set accordingly for the printer.  I have to reset
the
> com-port each time I print since the same port can be used for the modem
> via a switch box.  I do not get any errors until I "WriteFile" and I
cannot
> "CloseHandle" successfully.  

You are doing WAY too much work.  Just use the MF WIN PRINTER calls, 
and CONNECT the printer object  (Generic Text Printer) to the COM 
port.
```

<a id="t-1988d9cf4d"></a>
## Thread: Parts of a program that break in Y2K? — 12 msgs

_Topics: [`Y2K and Year-2000 remediation`](topics.md#y2k)_

### Re: Parts of a program that break in Y2K?

- **From:** dxmixxer@netdirect.net (Doug Miller)
- **Date:** 1998-09-26T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,comp.software.year2000,alt.folklore.computers
- **Message-ID:** `<6ujdqp$r9c_005@p19-term2-and.netdirect.net>`
- **References:** `<6uhk17$u14_002@p17-term1-and.netdirect.net> <6uidcl$klu@axalotl.demon.co.uk>`

```
In article <6uidcl$klu@axalotl.demon.co.uk>,
   huge@nospam.demon.co.uk (Hugh Davies) wrote:
+In article <6uhk17$u14_002@p17-term1-and.netdirect.net>, 
dxmixxer@netdirect.net (Doug Miller) writes:
+
+>+1/1/99
+>Why should this be any more special than 1/1/98 ?
+
+Because many programmers overloaded the meaning of year 99 to mean
+"invalid data" or somesuch.

I agree that many programmers overloaded the meaning of the 6-digit
date 999999 to mean invalid data or an infinite date. But year 99?
Personally, I've never seen that. Maybe you program like that. I don't.


+
+>+9/9/99
+>Why would there be a problem with this?
+
+Ditto.

Wrong. In 6-digit form, this would be 990909, not 9999 as many
people seem to think.

+
+>+2/29/04
+>Here, too, I wonder if the test is really necessary. Code that correctly
+>recognizes 1996 and 2000 as leap years is highly unlikely to break in 2004.
+
+You're happy to predict the behaviour of broken code?
+
I guess you didn't read too carefully. "Code that correctly recognizes
1996 and 2000 as leap years" is not by any definition "broken code." 
So what is the basis for your assumption that I am attempting to predict
the behavior of broken code? My statement made it very clear that I am
predicting that correctly functioning code will continue to function 
correctly. If you disagree, please provide a realistic example of code 
that correctly recognizes 1996 and 2000 as leap years and fails to recognize 
2004 as a leap year.
```

#### ↳ Re: Parts of a program that break in Y2K?

- **From:** seebs@plethora.net (Peter Seebach)
- **Date:** 1998-09-27T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,comp.software.year2000,alt.folklore.computers
- **Message-ID:** `<mzfP1.2371$Ge.7791423@ptah.visi.com>`
- **References:** `<6uhk17$u14_002@p17-term1-and.netdirect.net> <6uidcl$klu@axalotl.demon.co.uk> <6ujdqp$r9c_005@p19-term2-and.netdirect.net>`

```
In article <6ujdqp$r9c_005@p19-term2-and.netdirect.net>,
Doug Miller <dxmixxer@netdirect.net> wrote:
>+>+9/9/99
>+>Why would there be a problem with this?

>Wrong. In 6-digit form, this would be 990909, not 9999 as many
>people seem to think.

True.  However, there is a *known* Y2K problem with some in-home dialysis
machines which go into self-cleaning mode.

My assumption is that the people doing the "enter a date" routine were
too lazy to allow for an exception for the "magic dates", so they just said
"oh, just use 9/9/99".

>I guess you didn't read too carefully. "Code that correctly recognizes
>1996 and 2000 as leap years" is not by any definition "broken code." 

Yes, but it is quite possible that code which is broken might be broken
in a way where it gets 1996 and 2000 right, but 2004 wrong.

>So what is the basis for your assumption that I am attempting to predict
>the behavior of broken code? My statement made it very clear that I am
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>2004 as a leap year.

When people propose things like

	if (y % 4) {
		return 0;
	}
	if (y < 2000) {
		return 1;
	}
	if (y == 2000) {
		return 1;
	}
	return 0;

don't be too hasty to say "that's unrealistic", real code is often *at least*
that stupid.

-s
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Parts of a program that break in Y2K?

- **From:** dxmixxer@netdirect.net (Doug Miller)
- **Date:** 1998-09-27T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,comp.software.year2000,alt.folklore.computers
- **Message-ID:** `<6uk8ha$l7s_001@p19-term2-and.netdirect.net>`
- **References:** `<6uhk17$u14_002@p17-term1-and.netdirect.net> <6uidcl$klu@axalotl.demon.co.uk> <6ujdqp$r9c_005@p19-term2-and.netdirect.net> <mzfP1.2371$Ge.7791423@ptah.visi.com>`

```
In article <mzfP1.2371$Ge.7791423@ptah.visi.com>,
   seebs@plethora.net (Peter Seebach) wrote:
+In article <6ujdqp$r9c_005@p19-term2-and.netdirect.net>,
+Doug Miller <dxmixxer@netdirect.net> wrote:
[...]
+>So what is the basis for your assumption that I am attempting to predict
+>the behavior of broken code? My statement made it very clear that I am
+>predicting that correctly functioning code will continue to function 
+>correctly. If you disagree, please provide a realistic example of code 
+>that correctly recognizes 1996 and 2000 as leap years and fails to recognize 
+>2004 as a leap year.
+
+When people propose things like
+
+	if (y % 4) {
+		return 0;
+	}
+	if (y < 2000) {
+		return 1;
+	}
+	if (y == 2000) {
+		return 1;
+	}
+	return 0;
+
+don't be too hasty to say "that's unrealistic", real code is often *at least*
+that stupid.
+
I don't think it's hasty at all to say that this example is clearly contrived,
and obviously completely unrealistic. A naive implementation of leap year,
testing only for divisibility by 4 and ignoring century, is much more likely
to look something like
	if (y % 4) {
		return 1;
	}
	else {
		return 0;
	}
This produces correct results for all years from 1901 to 2099 inclusive.

So once again, I ask: can anyone provide a *realistic* example of code which
recognizes 1996 and 2000 as leap years, and fails to recognize 2004?
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Parts of a program that break in Y2K?

- **From:** seebs@plethora.net (Peter Seebach)
- **Date:** 1998-09-27T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,comp.software.year2000,alt.folklore.computers
- **Message-ID:** `<jLjP1.2442$Ge.7916374@ptah.visi.com>`
- **References:** `<6uhk17$u14_002@p17-term1-and.netdirect.net> <6ujdqp$r9c_005@p19-term2-and.netdirect.net> <mzfP1.2371$Ge.7791423@ptah.visi.com> <6uk8ha$l7s_001@p19-term2-and.netdirect.net>`

```
In article <6uk8ha$l7s_001@p19-term2-and.netdirect.net>,
Doug Miller <dxmixxer@netdirect.net> wrote:
[re:]
>+	if (y % 4) {
>+		return 0;
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
>+that stupid.

>I don't think it's hasty at all to say that this example is clearly contrived,
>and obviously completely unrealistic.

Nonsense!

We are talking about *OLD CODE*, often code written by *more than one person*,
with *poor maintenance*.

REVISION 1:
	"make a function check for a leap year"
	return !(y%4);

REVISION 2:
	"someone says the leap year code is too tricky"
	/* leap years are divisible by 4, so a remainder is not a leap year */
	if (y % 4)
		return 0;
	return 1;

REVISION 3:
	"one of our customers said 2000 isn't a leap year, because it's
	 a century"
	/* leap years are divisible by 4, so a remainder is not a leap year */
	if (y % 4)
		return 0;
	/* because centuries aren't leap years */
	if (y < 2000)
		return 1;
	return 0;

REVISION 4:
	"one of our customers says our leap year code is wrong, 2000 is a
	 leap year".
	if (y % 4)
		return 0;
	/* because centuries aren't leap years */
	if (y < 2000)
		return 1;
	/* but 2000 is */
	if (y == 2000)
		return 1;
	return 0;

Sure, it's stupid.

REAL PROGRAMMERS MAKE STUPID MISTAKES.

>A naive implementation of leap year,
>testing only for divisibility by 4 and ignoring century, is much more likely
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>This produces correct results for all years from 1901 to 2099 inclusive.

Yes, it does.  But we're *assuming* that we know that, somewhere in the
last 20 years, at least one developer did something wrong.  I think checking
for 2004 is a good idea.

>So once again, I ask: can anyone provide a *realistic* example of code which
>recognizes 1996 and 2000 as leap years, and fails to recognize 2004?

I put it to you that if you don't think that code is realistic, you've never
worked with a wide cross section of the sorts of morons who produce code
in this reality.  Maybe you've just been lucky?

-s
```

```
> So once again, I ask: can anyone provide a *realistic* example of code
> which
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…

What about this code I've found in a working program?

      *--- FEBRUAR ----------------------------------
       DT-20.
           IF WD-TEST-JJ = 80 OR
              WD-TEST-JJ = 84 OR
              WD-TEST-JJ = 88 OR
              WD-TEST-JJ = 92 OR
```

```
   AS-DATA@t-online.de (Andreas Strzoda) wrote:

+
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Parts of a program that break in Y2K?

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** gnohmon8715@my-dejanews.com
- **Date:** 1998-09-30T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,comp.software.year2000,alt.folklore.computers
- **Message-ID:** `<6utr81$uk2$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>`
- **References:** `<6uhk17$u14_002@p17-term1-and.netdirect.net> <6uidcl$klu@axalotl.demon.co.uk> <6ujdqp$r9c_005@p19-term2-and.netdirect.net> <mzfP1.2371$Ge.7791423@ptah.visi.com> <6uk8ha$l7s_001@p19-term2-and.netdirect.net> <6un9cc$pha$1@news00.btx.dtag.de> <6up4vf$ovg_003@p4-term3-and.netdirect.net> <361114f7.4356701@news.vip.net> <6urdbf$j2u$1@winter.news.erols.com>`

```
In article <6urdbf$j2u$1@winter.news.erols.com>,
  "Michael F. Coyle" <mjcoyle@erols.com> wrote:
> Gene Wirchenko wrote in message <361114f7.4356701@news.vip.net>...
> >dxmixxer@netdirect.net (Doug Miller) wrote:
…[14 more quoted lines elided]…
> >>+              GO DT-40.


Barfacious? Well, you see, instead of an expensive divide-and-get-remainder
operation, it does a nice simple (and cheap) compare. If it fails, it does
another one. By the time you start to get dates like 96, the code is running
on much faster hardware, so it doesn't matter that it fails 4 compares before
passing one.

(If you cannot see the invisible smiley, go ahead and flame me. If you
believe I don't realize what happens when date equals 77, please point it
out.))

"You shouldn't use loops because it's slower." Somebody told me that once,
with no smiley.

Catastrophic-looking cobol code could be caused by credence in such creeds.

-----------== Posted via Deja News, The Discussion Network ==----------
http://www.dejanews.com/       Search, Read, Discuss, or Start Your Own
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Parts of a program that break in Y2K?

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** "Foobar T. Clown" <foobar@gazonk.del>
- **Date:** 1998-09-30T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,comp.software.year2000,alt.folklore.computers
- **Message-ID:** `<36128316.43ED@gazonk.del>`
- **References:** `<6uhk17$u14_002@p17-term1-and.netdirect.net> <6uidcl$klu@axalotl.demon.co.uk> <6ujdqp$r9c_005@p19-term2-and.netdirect.net> <mzfP1.2371$Ge.7791423@ptah.visi.com> <6uk8ha$l7s_001@p19-term2-and.netdirect.net> <6un9cc$pha$1@news00.btx.dtag.de> <6up4vf$ovg_003@p4-term3-and.netdirect.net> <361114f7.4356701@news.vip.net> <6urdbf$j2u$1@winter.news.erols.com> <6utr81$uk2$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>`

```
gnohmon8715@my-dejanews.com wrote:
> 
> "You shouldn't use loops because it's slower." Somebody told me that
> once, with no smiley.

I once worked for a manager who held that belief.  He didn't actually
order me to unroll any loops, but I *DID* have to present a pretty
strong argument before he would let me write a subroutine.

I guess his reason was O.K.:  Our FORTRAN II system stored the
executable code for each subroutine in a separate disk file, and our
disk OS allowed only five characters for a file name, and no
subdirectories.
```

#### ↳ Re: Parts of a program that break in Y2K?

- **From:** tph@longhorn.uucp (Tom Harrington)
- **Date:** 1998-09-28T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,comp.software.year2000,alt.folklore.computers
- **Message-ID:** `<6uofpp$14b4@eccws1.dearborn.ford.com>`
- **References:** `<6uhk17$u14_002@p17-term1-and.netdirect.net> <6uidcl$klu@axalotl.demon.co.uk> <6ujdqp$r9c_005@p19-term2-and.netdirect.net>`

```
Doug Miller (dxmixxer@netdirect.net) wrote:
: +>+1/1/99
: +>Why should this be any more special than 1/1/98 ?
: +
: +Because many programmers overloaded the meaning of year 99 to mean
: +"invalid data" or somesuch.

: I agree that many programmers overloaded the meaning of the 6-digit
: date 999999 to mean invalid data or an infinite date. But year 99?
: Personally, I've never seen that. Maybe you program like that. I don't.

I don't either.  But that's not really the point.  The idea is to catch
any limitations in code written 20-30 years ago by any of an innumerable
population of programmers with currently-unknown amounts of skill,
experience, and common sense.  How much are you willing to bet that
in the history of computing, nobody has ever done anything which might
have a problem on this date?  In retrospect it may seem silly, but you're
dealing with code written in a different world than the one in which
we live, one with different concerns.  Hindsight is, of course, 20-20, but
that doesn't mean that compromises weren't made, or that these compromises
didn't make perfect sense at the time.
```

---

<a id="t-a0c3264b40"></a>
## Thread: Testing for End of File condition of indexed files? — 22 this month / 25 total

_Topics: [`VSAM, files, sorting`](topics.md#files)_

### Testing for End of File condition of indexed files?

- **From:** Don Tarshes <dtarshes@popd.ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1998-09-26T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<360D6B72.7CF2@popd.ix.netcom.com>`

```
Does anyone know the correct way to test for the End of File condition
in indexed files? My compiler (MFCOBOL) objects to this way:

	PERFORM UNTIL END-OF-FILE
		READ MY-FILE
			AT END MOVE "E" TO FILE-FLAG
			NOT AT END PERFORM DISPLAY-FILE
		END-READ
	END-PERFORM.

FILE-FLAG is defined in the w-s section like this:

01  FILE-FLAG	PIC X.
    88 END-OF-FILE VALUE "E".

This code is acceptable for sequential files but not indexed files.  For
indexed files, it generates the following errors:

After the "AT END" line: Exception phrase inappropriate
After the "NOT AT END" line: A "NOT" phrase did not have a matching verb
and was discarded
After the "END-READ" line: A scope-delimiter did not have a matching
verb and was discarded

The nature of the errors would lead one to look for a syntax error, but
like I said, the compiler accepts this exact same code for sequential
files.

I would greatly appreciate any suggestions.

Thanks.

Don
```

```

skidmike wrote in message <360D7574.36E452B1@mindspring.com>...
>I'm not sure of the others, but for the end-read, try taking that out
>>         PERFORM UNTIL END-OF-FILE
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>>
You know, I have never trusted "end-of" procedures, and got used to
coding before there was anything after that at end. I always pre-read,
then make the next read the last thing in the loop. I have never had a
problem in either of the two languages that you mention. IE;

    working-storage.
    01 end-flag            picture  value "n".
    in-the-startup.
        open filename.
        read filename at end move "y" to end-flag.
        perform main-loop until end-flag is equal to "y".
        close filename.
        stop run.
    main-loop.
***********deal with all record-data here
        read filename at end move "y" to end-flag.
****thats it.
```

#### ↳ Re: Testing for End of File condition of indexed files?

- **From:** "Sven Brueggemann" <Sven.Brueggemann@brem.ftn.de>
- **Date:** 1998-09-27T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<00010826@ftn.de>`
- **References:** `<360D6B72.7CF2@popd.ix.netcom.com>`

```
Dear Don,

DT> Does anyone know the correct way to test for the End of File condition
DT> in indexed files? My compiler (MFCOBOL) objects to this way:
DT>
DT> 	PERFORM UNTIL END-OF-FILE
DT> 		READ MY-FILE
DT> 			AT END MOVE "E" TO FILE-FLAG
DT> 			NOT AT END PERFORM DISPLAY-FILE
DT> 		END-READ
DT> 	END-PERFORM.

There are two different types of READ for indexed files with ACCESS MODE  
DYNAMIC: Random Read as *coded* above and sequential READ as *ment* above.  
Code "READ MY FILE NEXT RECORD" or change the ACCESS MODE to SEQUENTIAL.

Best regards
```

#### ↳ Re: Testing for End of File condition of indexed files?

- **From:** sff5ky@aol.com (Sff5ky)
- **Date:** 1998-09-27T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<19980926203808.04331.00000865@ngol01.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<360D6B72.7CF2@popd.ix.netcom.com>`

```

In article <360D6B72.7CF2@popd.ix.netcom.com>, Don Tarshes
<dtarshes@popd.ix.netcom.com> writes:

>
>Does anyone know the correct way to test for the End of File condition
…[32 more quoted lines elided]…
>

I would like to know for sure what access method is defined 'random',
'dynamic', or 'sequential'.  But, I believe that the syntax being used is the
root of the problem.

For sequential processing of an indexed file, you can use the READ ... AT END
sequence as follows.

READ MY-FILE NEXT RECORD
        AT END
               { DO SOME END OF FILE STUFF}
       NOT AT END
               {DO SOME RECORD PROCESSING STUFF}
       END-READ

NEXT RECORD is probably the key piece that you are missing.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Testing for End of File condition of indexed files?

- **From:** traveler <Hbp1@cris.com>

```
hI,

    iF A FILE IS OPENED TO READ SEQUENTIALLY, THEN THE AT END CLAUSE IS
VALID.  iF A FILE  IS  OPENED TO READ RANDOMLY, THEN THE AT END CLAUSE
WOULD HAVE NO   MEANING.
   rEMEMBER THAT AN iNDEXED OR kEYED FILE CAN BE OPENED WITH EITHER
RANDOM OR SEQUENTIAL ACCESS.  aN iNVALID kEY CLAUSE WOULD NOT MEAN MUCH IN
A FILE BEING READ SEQUENTIALLY.  aN at end CLAUSE WOULD NOT MEAN MUCH  IN
A FILE BEING  READ  RANDOMLY.

    iT IS ALWAYS A GOOD IDEA,  ESPECIALLY FOR iNDEXED OR KEYED FILES TO
SET UP A FILE STATUS FIELD WHICH YOU CAN INTERROGATE AFTER ANY FILE
ACTIONS.

     gOOD lUCK!

    HARLOW PEASE
```

#### ↳ Re: Testing for End of File condition of ind

- **From:** riplin@kcbbs.gen.nz (Richard Plinston)
- **Date:** 1998-09-27T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3298269.7714.25438@kcbbs.gen.nz>`
- **References:** `<360D6B72.7CF2@popd.ix.netcom.com>`

```
In message <<360D6B72.7CF2@popd.ix.netcom.com>> Don Tarshes <dtarshes@popd.ix.netcom.com> writes:
> 
> This code is acceptable for sequential files but not indexed files.  For
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
> files.

Your sequential file is specified as ACCESS SEQUENTIAL while
your Indexed file is specified to be ACCESS RANDOM or
ACCESS DYNAMIC.  In these access modes a READ is deemed to
be a random read so AT END is inappropriate and INVALID
KEY is appropriate.

Change ACCESS MODE to SEQUENTIAL or to DYNAMIC and add NEXT
RECORD to the read to indicate sequential reading.
```

#### ↳ Re: Testing for End of File condition of indexed files?

- **From:** vbandke@ibm.net (Volker Bandke)
- **Date:** 1998-09-27T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<360df98a.2907400@news2.ibm.net>`
- **References:** `<360D6B72.7CF2@popd.ix.netcom.com>`

```
On Sat, 26 Sep 1998 15:32:18 -0700, Don Tarshes <dtarshes@popd.ix.netcom.com> wrote:

>Does anyone know the correct way to test for the End of File condition
>in indexed files? My compiler (MFCOBOL) objects to this way:
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
>


In your ASSIGN statement you (should) have an ACCESS IS SEQUENTIAL (or ACCESS IS DYNAMIC),
which would allow SEQUENTIAL PROCESSING (START, READ NEXT).

If you have ACCESS IS RANDOM, and END OF FILE condition does not exist at all, because
there is no END to the file when records are read directly.  In this case, you would have
to use the INVALID KEY and NOT INVALID KEY clauses of the READ verb

with kind regards

Volker Bandke
(BSP GmbH)
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Testing for End of File condition of indexed files?

- **From:** paulr@bix.com (paulr)
- **Date:** 1998-09-27T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<6ulnuk$fri@lotho.delphi.com>`
- **References:** `<360D6B72.7CF2@popd.ix.netcom.com> <360df98a.2907400@news2.ibm.net>`

```
More likely, you would have ACCESS IS DYNAMIC, which allows
access either via indexed key or sequentially. In which case,
make you code look like:

     Perform until END-OF-FILE
       Read My-File next-record
         At End
           move  "E" to File-Flag
         Not At End
           perform Display-File
         end-read 
       End Perform.


[ I didn't compile that to check it, so please correct any 
  typos! ]
-Paul

: >			NOT AT END PERFORM DISPLAY-FILE
: >		END-READ
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Testing for End of File condition of indexed files?

- **From:** paulr@bix.com (paulr)

```
You have to use "next record" with the read though, when
you are in Dynamic access.

code on multiple compilers with no problems. :)
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Testing for End of File condition of indexed files?

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** jcj0347@aol.com (JCJ0347)
- **Date:** 1998-09-28T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<19980927224210.05885.00002874@ng-fd1.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<6umh2k$gg5@lotho.delphi.com>`

```


> I think to get it to work with DYNAMIC access,
>: one would have to eliminate the AT END and NOT AT END clause completely
>: and just use the INVALID KEY to test for end-of-file, like you said,
>: which I was not doing.

Viele, if the access mode is SEQUENTIAL, you can check for an
AT END condition after a READ filename.

If the access mode is DYNAMIC, you can check for an AT END
condition after a READ filename NEXT [RECORD].  The current
record pointer is sitting on the first record (in primary key sequence)
of the file when opened in this mode.  A READ filename NEXT will
retrieve the first record, and subsuquent READ filename NEXT 
I-O will sequentially process the file until the AT END condition is found.

The INVALID KEY clause will not work with either example above.

INVALID KEY would only work with access mode DYNAMIC and
a READ filename.  INVALID KEY would be invoked if the record
matching the key field in the FD area was not present in the file.
(Invalid key also applies to the WRITE and REWRITE verbs, too.)

Most compiler manuals have a nice little matrix showing ACCESS MODES,
OPEN MODES, and ALLOWABLE FILE I-O depending on the access
and open modes that were going to be used in processing.

JC Jones,
---------------
who though it might cloud the issue to mention RANDOM access.
```

#### ↳ Re: Testing for End of File condition of indexed files?

- **From:** redsky@ibm.net (Thane Hubbell)
- **Date:** 1998-09-28T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<Jl0PnHJ5PvPd-pn2-emzwlvQYJNNo@Dwight_Miller.iix.com>`
- **References:** `<360D6B72.7CF2@popd.ix.netcom.com>`

```
On Sat, 26 Sep 1998 22:32:18, Don Tarshes 
<dtarshes@popd.ix.netcom.com> wrote:

> Does anyone know the correct way to test for the End of File condition
> in indexed files? My compiler (MFCOBOL) objects to this way:
…[27 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Don, if the access mode on your file is RANDOM then the code posted 
will not work - you can't have an end of file condition resulting from
a random read.  Check the access mode and change it to sequential or 
dynamic.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Testing for End of File condition of indexed files?

- **From:** "Jay Moseley" <jay^_^moseley@ibm-dot-net>
- **Date:** 1998-09-29T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<wnlzbfryrlvozarg.f024im0.pminews@news1.ibm.net>`
- **References:** `<360D6B72.7CF2@popd.ix.netcom.com> <Jl0PnHJ5PvPd-pn2-emzwlvQYJNNo@Dwight_Miller.iix.com> <36102863.A861F912@mindspring.com>`

```
On Mon, 28 Sep 1998 20:22:59 -0400, skidmike wrote:

:>speaking of which, can i have an indexed file used by more than one
:>program and depending on how each respective program uses it, define
:>access as sequential in one program and dynamic in another?

Yes. The method of access is not determined by the physical file but the
program. That is why you must specify this clause in each program's SELECT
statement. However, you can access an indexed file both sequentially and
randomly under a single SELECT statement by using DYNAMIC access. You just
have to use a START prior to the READ if DYNAMIC access is specified.

Regards,
Jay Moseley, CCP
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Testing for End of File condition of indexed files?

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** jcj0347@aol.com (JCJ0347)
- **Date:** 1998-09-29T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<19980929160938.25715.00003654@ng122.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<wnlzbfryrlvozarg.f024im0.pminews@news1.ibm.net>`

```

>You just have to use a START prior to the READ if 
> DYNAMIC access is specified.

If I may clarify this just a tad:

If you're just going to process the file sequentially,
a READ file-name NEXT RECORD as the first I-O
will get you the first record (in primary key sequence).
Subsequent READ file-name NEXT RECORD I-Os will
get the rest of the records in primary key sequence.

A START is only required if you need to go to a specific record
before doing further READ file-name NEXT.  The word RECORD
is optional but I usually put it in my code for readability.

The above requires the file be access DYNAMIC.

Regards,
JC Jones.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Testing for End of File condition of indexed files?

- **From:** "Donald Tees" <donald@willmack.com>
- **Date:** 1998-09-29T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<6uq6af$9fh$1@news.igs.net>`
- **References:** `<360D6B72.7CF2@popd.ix.netcom.com> <Jl0PnHJ5PvPd-pn2-emzwlvQYJNNo@Dwight_Miller.iix.com> <36102863.A861F912@mindspring.com>`

```

skidmike wrote in message <36102863.A861F912@mindspring.com>...
>speaking of which, can i have an indexed file used by more than one
>program and depending on how each respective program uses it, define
>access as sequential in one program and dynamic in another?

You DEFINE it as indexed in both, but you PROCESS it either
randomly or sequentially.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Testing for End of File condition of indexed files?

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc123@bellsouth.net>
- **Date:** 1998-09-29T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<UE2Q1.404$2P.729935@news2.mia.bellsouth.net>`
- **References:** `<360D6B72.7CF2@popd.ix.netcom.com> <Jl0PnHJ5PvPd-pn2-emzwlvQYJNNo@Dwight_Miller.iix.com> <36102863.A861F912@mindspring.com>`

```
skidmike wrote:
>speaking of which, can i have an indexed file used by more than one
>program and depending on how each respective program uses it, define
>access as sequential in one program and dynamic in another?

Yes.  You can even have two or more FD's in one program open to the same
file, with different ACCESS modes.  But the idea of DYNAMIC is that you
can use it either way.  You don't really need SEQUENTIAL or RANDOM with
indexed files, because DYNAMIC can do either.  Just use the appropriate
syntax when you process it.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Testing for End of File condition of indexed files?

- **From:** "Sven Brueggemann" <Sven.Brueggemann@brem.ftn.de>
- **Date:** 1998-09-30T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<00015bcc@ftn.de>`
- **References:** `<36102863.A861F912@mindspring.com>`

```
Dear "skidmike",

s> speaking of which, can i have an indexed file used by more than one
s> program and depending on how each respective program uses it, define
s> access as sequential in one program and dynamic in another?

of course. Or you can define access as dynamic and use the appropriate  
READ statements (READ file NEXT RECORD AT END, READ file INVALID KEY).

Best regards

Sven
```

### HELP! College Student --binary bit manipulation

- **From:** "Jason Bogin" <jason@jax-inc.com>
- **Date:** 1998-09-26T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<6uob21$54q@news.southeast.net>`

```
I have a project that requires us to change certain bits in a single byte.
For example: Prompt the user for what bit to change (1-8), prompt for 1 or 0
and then we must come up with the mask and change the original and finally
write it to disk.

Help!?  I have no idea even where to begin.  I understand bit manipulation
and masks on paper but I have no clue as to how to program it!

Thanks,
Jason Bogin
jason@jax-inc.com
```

#### ↳ Re: HELP! College Student --binary bit manipulation

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc123@bellsouth.net>
- **Date:** 1998-09-28T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<jtQP1.94$3a6.226243@news3.mia.bellsouth.net>`
- **References:** `<6uob21$54q@news.southeast.net>`

```
Jason Bogin wrote:
>I have a project that requires us to change certain bits in a single byte.
>For example: Prompt the user for what bit to change (1-8), prompt for 1 or 0
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>and masks on paper but I have no clue as to how to program it!

COBOL is not well suited for this kind of thing.  But you can usually get
the job done, although the solutions tend to be system/compiler specific.
Micro Focus COBOL provides bit fiddling functions for this sort of thing.
Here is a general approach which will usually work, though you will need
to adapt it for your specific compiler.  Basically, you define a PIC X
field and redefine it as a binary numeric type, or vice-versa, then you
reference the binary numeric type as a numeric value, using add, subtract,
etc. to do the work.  The following Micro Focus program contains a routine
which takes a byte and splits each bit into a separate PIC 9 field, and
another routine which merges the separate fields into one byte.  Once the
bits are separated, you can easily test them individually.

       IDENTIFICATION DIVISION.
       ENVIRONMENT DIVISION.
       DATA DIVISION.
       WORKING-STORAGE SECTION.
       77  WS-CHAR             PIC  X(01).
       77  WS-VALUE            PIC  9(03).
       01  BINARY-WORK.
           03  BW-BINARY       PIC S9(04)  BINARY  VALUE 0.
           03  BW-ALPHA        REDEFINES BW-BINARY.
               05  FILLER          PIC  X(01).
               05  BW-CHAR         PIC  X(01).
           03  BW-BITS.
               05  BW-BIT128   PIC  9(01).
               05  BW-BIT64    PIC  9(01).
               05  BW-BIT32    PIC  9(01).
               05  BW-BIT16    PIC  9(01).
               05  BW-BIT8     PIC  9(01).
               05  BW-BIT4     PIC  9(01).
               05  BW-BIT2     PIC  9(01).
               05  BW-BIT1     PIC  9(01).
           03  BW-BIT          REDEFINES BW-BITS
                               OCCURS 8 TIMES
                               INDEXED BY BW-X
                                   PIC  9(01).
       PROCEDURE DIVISION.
       TEST-IT.
           MOVE "I" TO BW-CHAR.
           MOVE BW-CHAR   TO WS-CHAR.
           MOVE BW-BINARY TO WS-VALUE.
           PERFORM SPLIT-BYTE.
           DISPLAY WS-CHAR " "
                   BW-BIT128 BW-BIT64 BW-BIT32 BW-BIT16
                   BW-BIT8   BW-BIT4  BW-BIT2  BW-BIT1
                   " " WS-VALUE.

           MOVE 1 TO BW-BIT16.
           MOVE 0 TO BW-BIT1.

           PERFORM MERGE-BYTE.
           MOVE BW-CHAR   TO WS-CHAR.
           MOVE BW-BINARY TO WS-VALUE.
           DISPLAY WS-CHAR " "
                   BW-BIT128 BW-BIT64 BW-BIT32 BW-BIT16
                   BW-BIT8   BW-BIT4  BW-BIT2  BW-BIT1
                   " " WS-VALUE.
           STOP RUN.

       SPLIT-BYTE.
           PERFORM VARYING BW-X FROM 8 BY -1
                   UNTIL BW-X < 1
               DIVIDE 2 INTO BW-BINARY
                   GIVING BW-BINARY
                   REMAINDER BW-BIT(BW-X)
           END-PERFORM.

       MERGE-BYTE.
           MOVE 0 TO BW-BINARY.
           PERFORM VARYING BW-X FROM 1 BY 1
                   UNTIL BW-X > 8
               COMPUTE BW-BINARY = BW-BINARY * 2 + BW-BIT(BW-X)
           END-PERFORM.
```

#### ↳ Re: HELP! College Student --binary bit manip

- **From:** riplin@kcbbs.gen.nz (Richard Plinston)
- **Date:** 1998-09-30T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3298272.71560.13518@kcbbs.gen.nz>`
- **References:** `<6uob21$54q@news.southeast.net>`

```
In message <<6uob21$54q@news.southeast.net>> "Jason Bogin" <jason@jax-inc.com> writes:
> For example: Prompt the user for what bit to change (1-8), prompt for 1 or 0
> and then we must come up with the mask and change the original and finally
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> and masks on paper but I have no clue as to how to program it!

There are only 256 different values that can be held in a byte.
A table of all the values could be used then it is only
necessary to use the input values and the original value
to select the appropriate result.  This way the change
can be done without changing any bit values at all.

It is also possible to do it aritmetically, but adding or
subtracting must only be done if the bit is not already
in the desired setting.
```
