```
11. Is there a COBOL to C converter ?
Asking  this  question anywhere appears to generate much  general
flaming  and  general  language  wars  and  very  little   useful
information.

No  such beast is listed in the free compilers FAQ, but an ad has
appears  in  the US publication "Programmer's Shop  Catalog"  for
COBOL to C (and PL/I to C) translators. Contact :
Micro-Processor Services,
92 Stone Hurst Lane,
Dix Hills, NY 11746
  Tel: (516) 499 4461
  Fax: (516)- 499-4727
E-Mail: info@mpsinc.com, mpsinc@netusa.net
www: http://www.mpsinc.com
http://www.netusa.net/~mpsinc
A toolset for conversion from COBOL to several other languages is
available.  A  tool  first produces structured  diagrams  (Nassi-
Shneiderman)  from existing source files. Structural  errors  are
identified  and you can edit to correct them. Another tool  takes
those  same  diagrams and produces source code in one of  several
languages  (COBOL,  C,  Ada,  Basic, Clipper,  dBaseIV,  Fortran,
Modula 2, Natural, PL/1, etc.)
The  toolset  is called XperCase by Siemens, and is available  in
the US from:
Boston Technical Distribution Corp.
3 Center Plaza, Suite 440
Boston, MA 02108
  Tel: (617) 248-8989
  Fax: (617) 248-8986
Laurent Sabarthez contributed :
  "Some years ago I was Project Leader on a software project
  termed COBTOC (COBol TO C translation). The company is by
  now out of business, but the rights on this product were
  purchased by NSI (Network Solutions Inc., Herndon, VA, USA -
  Emitt McHenry was Chairman).
  COBTOC is actually a translator generator. It can produce a
  specialized translator for any reasonable COBOL dialect,
  given a dialect description very close to the usual syntax
  notation one can find into any COBOL Reference Manual.
  "semantics" peculiarities are also described in this way.
  Once a translator has been produced in this way, a source
  management module allows automated translation of the COBOL
  source modules. A run-time library is also automatically
  produced as a by-product of the translator.
  The COBTOC user gets a set of C files, each being the
  translation of a corresponding COBOL file. You can get K&R
  C, ANSI C, or common variants like Turbo C. The overall
  structure of the COBOL program is preserved upon
  translation. Identifiers are straightforward transformations
  of COBOL names. Paragraph structure and flow control are
  also preserved, like all name space properties attached to
  I/O and file management.
  The C files are compiled and linked with the run-time
  library, which supports data handling, edition, arithmetic,
  direct I/O, file I/O and transaction management (e.g. CICS).
  Executables are intended to run on any platform supporting
  POSIX C compiling and standard library linkage.
  COBTOC was left by my co-workers and me in an alpha release
  state, mid 1993.
  I don't know the end of the story, but NSI should provide
  more up-to-date information about it.
  There is also a project running to create a COBOL to C
  converter (possibly COBOL to C++ ?) available under the GNU
  license. "
  Another project that can be viewed as either a COBOL to C
  converter or as a compiler, is the "CobCy Project"
For    more    details,    see    the    CobCy    homepage     at
http://www.netsis.it/~asantini/cobcy/.

12. COBOL code generators
12.1 CA-Telon and CA-Telon PWS (Programmable Workstation
CA-Telon and CA-Telon PWS (Programmable Workstation) are complete
solutions for designing, generating and maintaining your mission-
critical  COBOL and PL/1 applications. High-level, non-procedural
design  and prototyping, combined with automatic code generation,
result  in  higher  productivity, consistent standards  and  good
programming  practices. With CA-Telon PWS, you can  offload  your
application  development projects from the mainframe and  provide
your   programmers   with  a  complete  application   development
environment on the workstation.
The CA-Telon Design Facility (TDF) provides a "fill-in-the-blank"
approach  for  designing online and batch programs. An  automated
prototyping facility assists in the analysis/design phases of the
life  cycle. CA-Telon generates structured portable  code  for  a
multitude  of target environments and DBMSs. Available for:  MVS,
DOS, OS/2

12.3 ETK
ETK (Easy ToolKit) was developed by SEMA Group in Belgium. It  is
now marketed by TOSC International, Inc :
TOSC International, Inc.
3900 Essex Lane, suite 250
Houston, TX 77027
  Tel: 713 961 1201
  Fax: 713 961 4989
Contact Ted Langlie. TOSC will also soon be on the Internet.
ETK   works   with  a  repository  where  the  programmer/analyst
describes  the  system to be generated in terms of data  domains,
elements,  tables,  relationships,  business  rules,   and   data
mappings.  From  this description ETK generates  complete  COBOL-
based applications that will run on many platforms.

13. COBOL Tools
This  section  documents  some  of  the  add-on  tools  that  are
available  for use with COBOL compilers. Further submissions  are
welcomed,  but  please try to keep them as  free  from  marketing
"hype" as possible.
Note: This section seems to be entirely devoted to tools that run
in  or  are  targeted  at  the PC and/or  Unix  world.  Mainframe
additions are welcome, but will probably be a while before I  can
add them.

13.1 creating GUI's
13.1.1 VanGui for RM/COBOL.
VanGui  consists  of two major components: a design  tool  and  a
runtime  system.  The design tool is a Windows application  which
provides  COBOL developers with the capability to define windows,
populate  those windows with standard Windows and  VBX  controls,
adjust  the properties of those controls and attach COBOL  event-
handling logic to their events.
The  VanGui  Runtime  is  a  Windows .DLL  that  manages  Windows
messages, provides runtime support for the controls, and provides
a COBOL interface to the Windows API.
For more information on VanGui, see 13.1.1 VanGui for RM/COBOL
http://www.liant.com/products/language/rm/ib.html.
For another GUI tool from Liant, see 5.3.4 RM/Panels

13.1.2 From Acucorp
AcuBench

AcuBench  is an integrated development environment for ACUCOBOL.-
GT. Available for the Windows 95 and
Windows    NT   operating   systems,   AcuBench   combines    the
internationally acclaimed ACUCOBOL-GT
(Graphical Technology) compiler and runtime system with a set  of
graphically-based,  GT-optimized  WYSIWYG  screen  Painter,   and
language sensitive source Code Editor.

ACUCOBOL�-GT

ACUCOBOL�-GT enables COBOL programmers to implement  and  deliver
full-featured GUI COBOL
applications on any of over 600 platforms, with full object  code
portability using a single set of COBOL
source   code.  Programmers  can  create  graphical  applications
including floating windows, graphical
controls  (such  as  entry fields, frames,  radio  buttons,  push
buttons, and labels), menu bars, bitmaps, and toolbars
using COBOL extensions that are consistent with traditional COBOL
syntax.

13.1.3 Flexus COBOL spII
COBOL spII allows the COBOL programmer to create GUI or character
mode  screens  using ANSI standard COBOL CALL  USING  statements.
COBOL  spII screen definitions and source programs are 100% COBOL
compiler independent, 100% operating system independent and  100%
text mode to GUI mode independent.
Automatic   screen   conversion  tools   for   many   proprietary
environments  are also available from Flexus. These automate  the
task  of converting screen definitions from proprietary character
mode screens to GUI screen definitions.

13.1.4 CA-Visual Realia
[From CA's Web site:]
CA-Visual  Realia  is a complete Windows development  environment
that  provides COBOL programmers with an easy-to-use set of tools
for creating mission-critical GUI client/server applications. CA-
Visual  Realia  allows you to create Windows  applications  using
COBOL  for  the  business  logic  and  GUI  tools  for  the  user
interface.  CA-Visual Realia combines tools designed for  Windows
user   interface   development  with   powerful   COBOL   program
development tools. Creating a program's user interface can be  as
easy  as  dragging  controls onto forms, and then  modifying  the
control's properties.

13.1.5 Fujitsu PowerCOBOL
PowerCOBOL   is  a  visual,  object  oriented,  enterprise   wide
client/server Rapid Application Development(RAD) tool  for  COBOL
programmers.   The  PowerCOBOL  development  environment   allows
programmers to use COBOL as the GUI scripting language  to  build
scaleable  and maintainable state-of-the-art COBOL-based  Windows
two-tier  and  three-tier client/server and Internet applications
with data access to local and remote databases.

13.2 Sinc Inc. products
13.2.1 FlexGen 4GL Rapid Application Development Environment
A  repository-based, data dictionary-driven  tool  set  and  code
generator. It supports and generates code for RM/COBOL,  Acucorp,
Micro Focus COBOL, Realia (DOS Only), mbp (DOS Only) and runs and
deploys under DOS, Windows, Windows 95, NT, many flavors of UNIX,
VAX/VMS and Open VMS.

13.2.2 Legacy Liberator COBOL Migration Toolset
For   rehosting  proprietary  COBOLs,  converting  them  to  open
systems,  Acucorp, RM/COBOL or MF COBOL. User interfaces  can  be
converted  to  FlexGen user interfaces including the  ability  to
upgrade them to GUI.

13.2.3 Easy Query
A point-and-shoot report writer and query tool.

13.2.4 ODBC Access Tools
Allows Windows ODBC-enabled applications to access COBOL data.

13.3 Siber Systems
13.3.1 CobolTransformer
CobolTransformer  (SCT) is a next generation Cobol  reengineering
and  parsing toolkit (SDK). By using CobolTransformer components,
developers  are  able  to develop Cobol-transforming  (or  Cobol-
analyzing) product or project faster and the resulting tools  are
of much higher quality.

CobolTransformer is a library with API in C++ that includes:
  �    High quality Cobol Parser that parses 12 most popular Cobol
     dialects and that has tremendous error recovery capability.
  �     C++ library that client uses to browse and transform  the
     tree-based Internal Representation of Cobol programs. Definition-
     Use links attached to the Program Tree effectively make it a
     general case Graph.
  �     PrettyPrinter that transforms our Internal Representation
     back into beautifully indented
     human-readable Cobol program.

13.3.2 Cbl-Beau
CobolBeautifier does the following things:
  a.   Beautifully indents your Cobol program.
b.   Renumbers paragraph-names, section-names, data-names
c.   attach an increasing or decreasing prefix or suffix to all
paragraph-names and/or section-names and/or data-names in your
Cobol program. (Based on CobolTransformer.)
A   free   version  is  available  from  their   Web   site   at:
http://www.siber.com/sct/
The   Commercial   version  will  allow  to  specify   your   own
indentation/pretty-printing rules, Program  you  style  rules  in
CobolTransformer and make all programs to comply automatically.
To be available soon.

13.3.3 Mf2Fsc
Mf2Fsc is a Micro Focus COBOL to Fujitsu COBOL converter.
It   converts  Micro  Focus  Cobol-85  to  Fujitsu  Cobol-85  and
beautifies the program at the same time.
A   free   prototype  is  available  from  their  web  site   at:
http://www.siber.com/sct/
A commercial version will be available soon.

!3.4 What about Year 2000 Tools?

13.4.1 CA-Fix/2000�
CA-Fix/2000,   a   mainframe-based  application-at-a-time   COBOL
remediation  tool,  performs rapid automated  date  analysis  and
correction of your COBOL source code to provide the fastest  path
to  Year  2000  compliance. CA-Fix/2000 eliminates  the  tedious,
error-prone  tasks of finding and correcting the date  fields  in
your programs. Because it can correct an entire application in  a
single step, CA-Fix/2000 helps ensure consistent corrections even
when  date  fields  are  passed from one program  to  another  or
records  are  redefined.  CA-Fix/2000 can  determine  which  date
fields  are  at  risk  for failing to handle the  century  change
correctly  and corrects only these at-risk fields,  limiting  the
impact on programs and reducing testing requirements.

13.5 Misc. tools

13.5.1 Flexus WinPrint
COBOL  WinPrint allows the COBOL programmer to make ANSI standard
COBOL CALL USING statements to completely control and communicate
with   the   Windows  Print  Manager.  Forms  may   be   designed
interactively  with the Forms Editor to include bitmaps,  special
fonts,  colors and many other modern features. These reports  may
be viewed on the screen prior to printing or sent directly to the
Print Manager.

14. Other sources of information.
14.1 CompuServe
CA has a Forum. To get to this, use
     GO CAIDEV
Liant has a Forum too, to support the RM/COBOL and Relativity(TM)
family of products. To access it, use
     GO LIANT
14.2 Bix
There is a COBOL forum on Bix. Don Nelson is the moderator.

14.3 Micro Focus Faxback
In  Palo  Alto,  Micro Focus runs a system for obtaining  certain
technical information by fax. You need a tone-dial 'phone to  use
it.
  The number is (415) 496 7170.

14.4 Micro Focus WWW server
Micro Focus has a WWW server covering many COBOL issues. The
URL is http://www.microfocus.com/

14.5 CA WWW server
CA also has a WWW server. It's URL is http://www.cai.com/
14.6 Liant and Ryan McFarland WWW server
Liant has a WWW server at http://lsc.liant.com/. This server also
hosts the RM WWW site at http://lsc.liant.com/rm/.

14.7 The Acucorp WWW server
This is at http://www.Acucorp.com/.

14.8 The COBOL Foundation
Dave  McFarland,  formerly  of  Ryan  McFarland,  has  begun   an
organization  aimed at promoting COBOL and providing  information
to  and about the COBOL community. Members (including RM, MF, and
IBM)  pay  yearly  dues  and  in  return  are  included  in   the
Foundation's promotion efforts, literature, directories, etc. and
have  their  company  and  product  information  posted  on   the
Foundation's web server. The URL for the server is
http://www.cobol.org/

14.9 The IBM COBOL products WWW server
IBM  has  enhanced  the  COBOL you currently  use  with  powerful
features  to  increase  development  productivity,  simplify  the
maintenance of your legacy code, and provide seamless portability
from your host to your workstation. Whether you're maintaining or
reengineering   legacy  code  or  creating  new   object-oriented
client/server  applications, IBM's COBOL family  offers  you  the
year  2000 ready application development environment designed  to
do the job right.

IBM  COBOL  provides  a  complete offering of  compatible,  cross
platform,  cross product compilers which support OS/2�,  OS/390�,
MVS�,  VM,  VSE/ESA�, AS/400�, AIX�, and Windows�. IBM gives  you
the tools you need to tackle your COBOL year 2000 challenge while
leveraging  your existing applications. IBM COBOL  also  provides
the  tools you need to amplify your program development, enabling
you  to  position your enterprise to take advantage of tomorrow's
technologies.
See http://www.software.ibm.com/ad/cobol/

14.10 The Flexus WWW server
Contains all sorts of information, including a copy of the  COBOL
650 compiler. It's at http://www.flexus.com/

14.11 COBOL User Groups
There are a large number of Micro Focus User Groups. Rather  than
reproduce  the list here and have it constantly out of  date,  it
can be found at
http://pita.microfocus.com/UserGroup_PC/
The  list  and the web pages are maintained by Debbie  Vincent  <
tommygigio@aol.com>.

15. Information required for the FAQ.
Corrections  and  additions  to  existing  material  are   always
welcome. I'd like to add a section of reviews of different  COBOL
books. If I am sent any reviews I will collate them and add these
to the FAQ. More information on mainframe COBOL products would be
useful.
A section covering COBOL programming could be worthwhile.
Note:  I still have a "folder full" of comments sent to the  last
owner of this FAQ that have yet to be applied. It is my intent to
get them verified and applied as soon as possible.

16. Contributors to the FAQ.
Many  people  have  contributed  to  this  FAQ  in  each  of  its
iterations. In the past an ongoing list of these people have been
included  within  the  FAQ.  At this time,  i  am  stopping  this
practice, although I ,in no way, want to deprecate all  the  work
and  input  that these people have provided. I have continued  to
identify people whom I am quoting (directly or indirectly) and do
still  hope  that everyone who has input for this  document  will
continue to provide it.

17. What about the Y2K (Millenium) Issue?
  �    Yes, the year 2000 IS a leap year
  �     Yes, the millenium really starts on January 1, 2001 - but
     the software "bug" refers to January 1, 2000 (and several other
     dates)

17.1 Where can I get information about the Y2K problem?
The  Year 2000 Information Center home page on the World Wide Web
is at
     http://www.year2000.com.
The Information Center has a countdown clock, articles on various
aspects  of the problem, vendor information, this FAQ, and  links
to related information.
There is an Internet mailing list operated by Peter de Jager  for
discussions  of year 2000 computer problems. The  list  has  over
1200  members,  and  gets an average of  about  25  messages  per
business day. This is a moderated mailing list managed by a  paid
administrator and fully supported by its members on a "shareware"
basis.  Each member is asked to pay a subscription fee of US  $50
yearly. (You may contribute more if you wish.)
New subscribers get a 30-day free trial.
To  subscribe, select the "Year 2000 Announcement List" link from
the    Year    2000    Information   Center    home    page    at
http://www.year2000.com. On the subscription form, select the Yes
adio  button  for the Discussion List. You can also subscribe  by
sending e-mail to 2k@tor.hookup.net with
     SUBSCRIBE
in  the  subject line of the message. Subscriptions are processed
manually,  so  please  be  patient. The list  can  optionally  be
received  in digest form instead of individual messages. Invoices
and  receipts are available if needed. Details will be sent  when
you subscribe.
The  Year  2000 Forum on CompuServe has discussions of  all  year
2000 issues, and a collection of files, including this FAQ. There
are over 1000 members. To reach the forum, GO YEAR2000.
The  Usenet  newsgroup comp.software.year-2000  is  dedicated  to
discussions of year 2000 computer issues.
The  current  version  of this Year 2000 FAQ  is  available  from
several web sites, an FTP site, and on CompuServe. It is in ASCII
text form at all these sites.
On the Year 2000 Information Center home page at
http://www.year2000.com,
select  the  "Year 2000 Archive" link. The FAQ is the  last  item
listed on the Archive page

18. What can/should I post in the COBOL newsgroups?
Both comp.lang.cobol and alt.cobol are UN-moderated newsgroups.
This means that there is no controlling authority regulating  the
content  of the posts in those groups. Anyone is able  (but  most
assuredly  not welcome) to post anything, relevant  to  COBOL  or
not.   Off-topic  posts  (and  particularly  prolonged  off-topic
threads)are  discouraged, but it is impossible to  prevent  them.
Different  readers of these newsgroups have different  thresholds
for  tolerating off-topic comments. The more that you  post,  the
more  likely  it  is  that  some  readers  will  simply  apply  a
"killfile"  to all of your posts - often after sending  you  (and
possibly the newsgroup) rather pointed comments on your use/abuse
of the newsgroup.
You  should, therefore, apply the following guidelines that  will
help  you  get the most out of both your viewing and postings  at
these sites.
  �     Comp.lang.cobol is the preferred site to use - especially
     for technical issues related to COBOL.
  �    Although threads do stray from the topics, the more targeted
     your inquiry is - and the more closely it relates to COBOL, the
     more likely you are to receive a useful and prompt response. (If
     you  know that you are leaving the current subject, consider
     changing the subject line in your post.)
  �     When asking about a specific structure, error message, or
     situation, it is always best to specify both the compiler that
     you are using and the operating system where you are working.
     (What you may think is a general COBOL question may actually be
     very specific to your current environment.)

Note:  For  those  who  are interested,  the  "charter"  for  the
comp.lang.cobol newsgroup can be found at:
ftp//ftp.uu.net/usenet/news.announce.newgroups/comp/
comp.lang.cobol

18.1 Can I get help with homework via the newsgroups?
Yes,  you  can get help with your homework via these  newsgroups.
HOWEVER, that does mean that you will receive help - you will NOT
find many participants who will be very happy if you ask them  to
do  your  homework FOR YOU. Some hints that you  should  consider
when drafting your post for assistance with homework include:
  �     Make it clear from the beginning that you are asking  for
     homework help. (Most of the newsgroup participants are very good
     at sniffing out those who try and pose homework questions as
     "business need" questions - and they are not very polite  in
     replying to such questions).
  �     Make certain that you specify what compiler and operating
     system  your  homework is targeted at.  Solutions  may  vary
     significantly based on this. (You might also want to include what
     text book you are using.)
  �     The  more information that you can give that demonstrates
     that you really have tried to solve the problem yourself (using
     your text book, class material/presentations, lab assistance,
     etc), the more likely it is that you will get useful and friendly
     responses. If you let us know what you have found on your own and
     why you are still uncertain or confused, you will usually get
     helpful responses; if it looks like you are asking us questions
     without trying to solve it yourself, you are likely to get very
     pointed replies.

NOTE:  if you are looking for a COBOL tutor, (and don't  want  to
see  the  "Do  your own homework" notes that occur in  the  COBOL
newsgroups), you might want to try,
     http://www.tutoraid.org/

18.2 Can I post job openings in the newsgroups and if so what
should I include?
These newsgroups are an excellent place for posting job openings.
Some  participants (those with jobs) often wish that less of  the
bandwidth  was  spent on job postings, but,  for  those  who  are
looking for positions/contracts, these postings are quite useful.
There  are, however, some guidelines that you should follow  when
posting  positions  - unless you like getting  abusive  and  non-
responsive replies to your postings.
  �     Always, ALWAYS, include rates, a range of rates, or  your
     best information on what rates are available. Phrases like "based
     on experience" and "competitive" are bound to receive replies
     questioning your motives. The assumption is that ALL such posts
     are "trolling" for resumes or rates and not serious job searches.
  �     Try  to give the best summary of job location and desired
     expertise in the subject line of your posting. This will assist
     you  in  attracting  readers that are  actually  potentially
     interested in your openings.
  �     The  newsgroups  are international  in  nature,  but  are
     dominated by Americans. If you are posting a position outside the
     US,  please make it clear whether foreigners are or are  not
     welcome to apply - and if so what visas and other paperwork is
     required If you are posting for a job with specific citizenship
     requirements, please make that clear from the start.
  �     In general, the more information that you provide in your
     postings, the better the match will be from those who reply. If,
     however, you are "attaching" a job description, please  make
     certain that you make it clear what type of document-reader is
     required to process it AND make certain that you have  virus
     scanned the document before you post it.

NOTE:  For  those looking for jobs, you might want to  check  out
Dice at
     http://www.dice.com

Appendix A. Changes in recent revisions
This  appendix  documents the major changes  that  were  made  to
create the various (recent) versions of this document.
Appendix A.1 - Changes to create Version 2.0
Huge  numbers of changes! Version numbering jumped from 1.xxx  to
2.0 to reflect this.
FAQ availability details.
Added section on Commercial COBOL Products.
Removed 'what happened to Realia?' section.
Removed references to Wang, as I can find no information  on  any
Wang COBOL Products.
Checked all URLs, and updated/added as appropriate.
Removed reference to IBM 'COBOL Products WWW site'.
Added  entries  in  'GUI Tools' section for  Micro  Focus  Dialog
System and Net Express, Flexus COBOL spII, and CA-Visual Realia.
Removed  ADS/Online from 'Code Generators' section as  it's  not,
apparently.  Also noted that Cullinet have been acquired  by  CA,
who now sell the Telon product.

Appendix A.2 - Changes to create Version 2.05
  �    Significant reformatting
  �    Updated OO COBOL Section
  �    Add URL for IBM COBOL Web site
  �    Added information on "COBOL for Dummies"
  �    Added initial Y2K section

  �    Added information on PCYACC

Appendix A.3 - Changes to create Version 2.08
  �    Started adding Logos

  �    Corrected information about Liant (who acquired RM)
  �    Added information on Amiga freeware compiler

     the FAQ

     newsgroups.

Appendix A.4 - Changes to create Version 2.0x

  �    Re-instated the information on obtaining a copy of the draft
     COBOL Standard.
```

<a id="t-f3b7338ce8"></a>
## Thread: L S 6 jyW K M C Re: Efficiency and Cobol coding — 1 msgs

### L S 6 jyW K M C Re: Efficiency and Cobol coding

- **From:** riplin@kcbbs.gen.nz (Richard Plinston)
- **Date:** 1998-09-26T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3298268.80889.12870@kcbbs.gen.nz>`

```
 
Judson McClendon writes:
 
> What I have stated repeatedly is that one should be aware of
> the *kind* of assembly code that would need to be generated for
> the HLL code you are writing.
 
Yes, you have said many things repeatedly.  I simply don't agree
with you.  It is important to make the _Cobol_ efficient. It is
unnecessary to wory about the grubby details of whatever CPU
underlies the code.  In many cases there is _no_ assembly code
generated.  Many compilers output byte codes for a virtual
machine that is not specified in any detail.  This may run
unchanged on anything from an 8080 to a 64bit Alpha.
 
Any particular source may run directly to the CPU or via a
called general purpose routine.
 
> It doesn't even need to be for your own CPU.  A move is a move,
> a multiply is a multiply,
 
Wrong.  A MOVE in Cobol may generate a simple sequence of
instructions, _or_ it may generate a complex sequence, _or_
it may generate a call to a routine to do some far more
complex manipulation.  The generated sequence may depend on
the target machine and the type of the variables.  For
example some processors can generate simple moves for
particular allignments while others cannot deal with those
alignments directly and need to generate far more code.
 
 
> and you can assume that the CPU can do them efficiently.
 
If you think that then I suggest that your code may be
horribly inefficient on some machines.
 
> Assembly languages tend to be more similar than HLLs
 
Wrong.  You should get out more.
 
Some assemblers look like high-level language.  I have seen
assembler where every instruction specified the next instruction
address (the processor did not update the PC to the next address
for some very good reason, it was already too late to do that one
next), assembler where a branch had to be coded two instructions
before it was required (due to run-on), assembler where every
instruction is conditional.
 
 
> But if you program in a HLL and don't have a clue what it takes
> for the CPU to execute the various commands, then you are
> programming blind, as far as efficiency goes.
 
I doubt that you would have a clue how modern CPUs execute
code with parallel sequences, speculative execution,
run on, loop unrolling, result elimination, ...
 
I suspect that you may know very well a 30 year old design
and base everything on this.
 
> And looking at the software being turned out these days,
> that happens quite a lot.
 
It is entirely possible to write efficient _Cobol_ code without
worrying too much about the underlying CPU.
 
For example, worrying about whether to use USAGE INDEX or
subscripts with COMP, COMP-3, or COMP-5 is _pointless_ if you are
coding a bubble sort of a table when a better algorithm is more
appropriate, or when the sort could be avoided altogether with a
better program.
 
Of course, on your other 'point' that efficicy in results means
efficiency in programmer time is also, in general, completely
wrong.
 
For example, most programmers should be able to get a bubble sort
working first time up.  They should be able to get the boundry
conditions right very quickly and make it solid code in a
reasonable time with little need to use a debugger.
 
However, if, say, a quicksort were to be more efficient, then
I doubt they would get it working correctly in every case in
anywhere near the same time.
 
In the other direction a SORT on a table is currently an MF
extension (will it be a Cobol9x feature?) but any programmer
can make this work in a few minutes.
 
Given SORT table is available would you suggest (based on your
ideas of 'efficiency') that programmer use?:
 
       a) SORT table
       b) Bubble sorting and argue about COMP-? or INDEX
       c) Quick Sort        "        "      "       "
 
and which do you think would use least programmer's time
and be least liable to a call at 2am ?
 
Myself, I would probably design the system in the first place so
the data for the table can be read already in order (with eg
an alternate key) as this removes all code and programmer
ineffiencies.  It would also, most likely, eliminate
problems of data exceeding table size.
 
That is the way that _I_ achieve efficiency.
 
 
> You only write code once, but it may execute billions of times.
> CPU power is cheap, and you don't have to hoard it. But there
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> mind boggling.
 
Feeling better now ?  or would you like a little lie down ?
```

```
>Because many programmers overloaded the meaning of year 99 to mean

99/12/31 for about a billion years to disable a data check. For example,
name of contract(in data file)"this contract never expires", expiry date(in
data file:99/12/31).  Result, 10,000,000 policies become due. "High values"
have a special meaning in data files.
```

## Thread: Audio-CD ID — 1 msgs

### Audio-CD ID

- **From:** "Sven Brueggemann" <Sven.Brueggemann@brem.ftn.de>
- **Date:** 1998-09-26T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<0000f245@ftn.de>`

```
Hello,

nearly all CD player programs provide features to store plain text  
information for tracks, artists and sometimes even lyrics, bitmaps of the  
CD cover,...

To do this, the program has to be able to identify the inserted CD. Most  
do this with an 8-digit hex number (in window's CDPLAYER.EXE stored in  
CDPLAYER.INI).

I wonder, what kind of number this is, where it is stored on the CD and if  
it is simply an ID number or if it contains any information (manufacturer,  
label, maybe even artist?).

Any information is appreciated.

Best regards

Sven

Send a mail with Subject "SEND PGPKEY" to retrieve my PGP public key
```

<a id="t-429904fdf7"></a>
## Thread: Help : Calling Btireve Functions from FujitsuCobol 4.0 — 1 msgs

_Topics: [`Compilers and vendors`](topics.md#compilers) · [`Help requests and how-to`](topics.md#help)_

### Help : Calling Btireve Functions from FujitsuCobol 4.0

- **From:** TKS-Schmitteck@t-online.de (Paul Schmitteck)
- **Date:** 1998-09-26T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<6ui94t$msn$1@news00.btx.dtag.de>`

```
Hello Everybody,
    I have got a sample program from Fujitsu  Corporation to access the
btrieve native requester to gain an access to the btrieve files. The call to
the btrieve requester is made as follows


     CALL "BTRCALL" WITH STDCALL USING
                          BY VALUE                BTRIEVE-OPERATION-CODE
                          BY REFERENCE    BTRIEVE-POSITION-BLOCK
                          BY REFERENCE    BTRIEVE-DATA-BUFFER
                          BY REFERENCE    BTRIEVE-DATA-BUFFER-LENGTH
                          BY REFERENCE    BTRIEVE-FILE-NAME
                          BY VALUE                BTRIEVE-KEY-NUMBER
                          BY REFERENCE    BTRIEVE-STATUS-CODE.

We tried  to compile  the program and link it with the library file
W3BTRV7.LIB. When we run the program it does not give the desired results.
The program shows Open successful for the btrieve file which is not
existing.

Can anybody give us an Idea how to access the btrieve files with the help of
the native btrieve requester "BTRCALL". We do not want to declare the files
in the environment division. The library file WBTRV32.LIB is not the right
file to be linked to access the btrieve requester.

TIA
Paul Schmitteck.
```

```
I'm very curious about this..... Here's the situation. Going to
college and at the campus they're running MF but don't have a problem
with me running Fujitsu and doing all of my work at home, which is a
god send in its own right so I don't have to work my life around
campus schedule and can actually do all my work from my home computer.
Works out real well. 

My question is, with the program it gives you the option to do the
conversion over to Micro Focus. Has any one attempted that and if so,
how difficult is it? I attempted it and it kept throwing back Line
feed errors at me however the program itself was running fine and
didn't have anything wrong with it at all. If anyone has any ideas
about this or thoughts, let me know... Thanks.

Sue
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Fujitsu COBOL 3.. Has anyone attempted conversion yet?

- **From:** "Fujitsu Software" <cobol@adtools.com>
- **Date:** 1998-09-30T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<6utm93$okp$1@nntp2.ba.best.com>`
- **References:** `<360e972e.9457054@news> <36121ad3.655732@news>`

```
Sue,

If you want to insure that your Fujitsu COBOL program will compile under
Micro Focus COBOL, then add the following compiler directives:

LANGLVL(85)
FLAGSW(STDH)

Fujitsu Software Corporation
Developer Tools Group
Phone: (408) 428-0500
FAX:    (408) 428-0600
Email:  cobol@adtools.com
Web:   http://www.adtools.com
```

```
tori0208 wrote:

Unix/Solaris: Yes, e.g. interpreter comes on CD with book COBOL for Dummies,
```

## Thread: ISO a supplier of 132 col Printer Design forms — 4 msgs

### ISO a supplier of 132 col Printer Design forms

- **From:** wade1701@aol.com (Wade1701)
- **Date:** 1998-09-27T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<19980927153910.08533.00002865@ng140.aol.com>`

```

Hi,

I was wondering if someone could help me here.  I am in search of a supplier
that produces the 132 column Printer spacing design charts in any Color other
than a White/Gray combination. Is there a subsitute method out there for
designing a report layout while retaining the spacing information needed for
the code in your program ?
 IBM at one time did have nice White/Green colored forms but decided to change
to a lousy Gray/White color.  The Gray/White forms are horrible to look at when
designing a report layout using a pencil (tried using different colored pencils
but they don't erase as well).

Thanks,
Wade

Wade1701@aol.com
```

#### ↳ Re: ISO a supplier of 132 col Printer Design forms

- **From:** "tmj" <tmj@spam.com>
- **Date:** 1998-09-28T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<6uojdg$k13@musky.state.wi.us>`
- **References:** `<19980927153910.08533.00002865@ng140.aol.com>`

```
Too bad you didn't ask last week.  I tossed one of those old IBM pads in the
recycling bin last friday.

Instead of paper I use a text editor (or a word processor with a fixed
font).  At the top of the screen I put:
         1         2         3 ...
123456789012345678901234567890 ...

Then I type, cut & paste untill the report template looks good.  This way I
don't kill nearly as many trees as I used to.

Some text editors will display the line/column number somewhere on the
screen.  I know the MicroFocus Edit/Animator does.

when
```


#### ↳ Re: ISO a supplier of 132 col Printer Design forms

- **From:** jcj0347@aol.com (JCJ0347)
- **Date:** 1998-09-28T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<19980927222433.05885.00002858@ng-fd1.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<19980927153910.08533.00002865@ng140.aol.com>`

```

You could probably do one in Excel on legal paper if
8ï¿½ inches is enough veritcal area.  Or Lotus 1-2-3 or
just about any other spreadsheet.  Experimenting around
with the column width to get the right spacing shouldn't be
too hard.  You were looking for 10cpi, right?  Output to a
color ink jet & you're all set.

JC Jones.
```

<a id="t-f1d54e9680"></a>
## Thread: MF COBOL PROBLEM — 3 msgs

_Topics: [`Compilers and vendors`](topics.md#compilers)_

### MF COBOL PROBLEM

- **From:** Rizwan Abbasi <abbasi2@ibm.net>
- **Date:** 1998-09-27T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<360DC336.4F5C2BF4@ibm.net>`

```
Hi,
I am running MF cobol under AIX 4.2.1on IBM F50 box, today all of a
sudden error comes during DATA-entry and all DATA entry is stop because
of this error
The error says
Execution Error : file error code : 114, Pc=0, call=-1. seg=0
114 Attempt to access items beyond bounds of memory. (sgnal 11)

After investigating the manuals of MF COBOL error 114 means "Memory
access violation has been detected by you Operating system."

However I can execute the same data-entry program. Also after doing
furhter efforts I do find out that the same data entry program can be
executed
without any errors with  another userid,both the userids are alsmost
same with
same attributes. I have also check the no of processes in OS envoirment
as
well as .profile of both the users. Any suggestions or tips will be of
great
help. Thanks in advance

Rizwan Abbasi
```

#### ↳ Re: MF COBOL PROBLEM

- **From:** cbailey@galstar.com
- **Date:** 1998-09-27T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<6ulj3f$nld$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>`
- **References:** `<360DC336.4F5C2BF4@ibm.net>`

```
In article <360DC336.4F5C2BF4@ibm.net>,
  Rizwan Abbasi <abbasi2@ibm.net> wrote:
> Hi,
> I am running MF cobol under AIX 4.2.1on IBM F50 box, today all of a
…[21 more quoted lines elided]…
>
-------------------------------------------------------
Does your program have an array (occurs) in it?
Just a thought.  Trying to access an array element
that is way (way) outside the bounds of the array
would cause such a problem.  Is the user id used as
an index into an array?

Cliff
>

-----== Posted via Deja News, The Leader in Internet Discussion ==-----
http://www.dejanews.com/rg_mkgrp.xp   Create Your Own Free Member Forum
```

### debugging 101 <watch out for that "dot"!>

- **From:** skidmike <skidmike@mindspring.com>
- **Date:** 1998-09-28T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<361042D4.ABB86E96@mindspring.com>`

```
here's today's "headache report" before i go to bed.  i loaded my
computer up and took it to the shop (meineke for those who don't know
me) to proudly show off the system i am writing.  the main screen for
the system is the "waiting list" a list of customers either waiting for
inspections or for service.  guess what???  the waiting list doesn't
display!  so my pc patiently sits in my bay while i work on car after
car and in between i go thru the code i have written so far.  can't find
the bug.  well, after loading the mother@#$%er up and going home to a
cold beer and installation of fujitsu cobol 4.0 i FOUND THE GOD&^%$
BUG.  a period
a single solitary dot on my monitor
something a fraction of an eighth of an inch
that unfortunately made UnderCarPro 1.0 NOT add a customer to the
waiting list!
watch out for those dots mistakenly placed in the middle [as opposed to
the end] of your subroutines.
before you laugh, i know you guys have done the same thing!
```

#### ↳ Re: debugging 101 <watch out for that "dot"!>

- **From:** "Leif Svalgaard" <leif@ibm.net>
- **Date:** 1998-09-29T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3610833b.0@news1.ibm.net>`
- **References:** `<361042D4.ABB86E96@mindspring.com>`

```
Mike,
You have just learned the badness of dots.
It is better never to use a dot unless strictly
necessary, i.e. at the END of the paragraph.
(and at the end of an IF clause, if you don't
use END-IF). Then put the dot on line by itself
in col. 12.


skidmike wrote in message <361042D4.ABB86E96@mindspring.com>...
>here's today's "headache report" before i go to bed.  i loaded my
>computer up and took it to the shop (meineke for those who don't know
…[14 more quoted lines elided]…
>before you laugh, i know you guys have done the same thing!
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: debugging 101 <watch out for that "dot"!>

- **From:** Bill Lynch <wblynch@att.net>
- **Date:** 1998-09-30T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3611CB03.4067063F@att.net>`
- **References:** `<361042D4.ABB86E96@mindspring.com> <3610833b.0@news1.ibm.net>`

```
Leif Svalgaard wrote:
> 
> Mike,
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> in col. 12.

Assuming your compiler supports it, I recommend coding the END-IF as
religiously as you code END-EXEC to terminate CICS commands (assuming
you have an inkling of CICS commands) and not putting a dot here. If you
can, configure your compiler to require it.

Bill Lynch
```

#### ↳ Re: debugging 101 <watch out for that "dot"!>

- **From:** Haluk Okur <spbis@sbs.de>
- **Date:** 1998-09-29T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36115DD6.2C73@sbs.de>`
- **References:** `<361042D4.ABB86E96@mindspring.com>`

```
skidmike wrote:
<snip>
> watch out for those dots mistakenly placed in the middle [as opposed to
> the end] of your subroutines.
> before you laugh, i know you guys have done the same thing!

Sure, and how about this:

    IF BLAH-BLAH-BLAH
       DO-THIS-AND-THAT .---->  This dot is on the 73. column!
    PROGRAM-GOES-ON-AND-ON.

You can't see the exact location of the characters on the screen
of vi easily. It took me half a day to find out the error: The dot
was shifted right one character when I inserted something, so the
compiler was seeing the next dot -wherever it was- as the terminator
of the IF clause...

BTW, this was before COBOL85. There wasn't any scope terminators
other than the "period space" then.

Later I wrote a small C program which read the COBOL sources and
reported lines longer than 72 chars. It proved very useful over
the years...

You know:
"...progress is not made by early risers,
 progress is made by lazy people
 looking for easier ways to do something..."
```

```
On Tue, 29 Sep 1998 15:23:18 -0700, Haluk Okur <spbis@sbs.de> wrote:
>You can't see the exact location of the characters on the screen
>of vi easily. It took me half a day to find out the error: The dot

This is one reason why I'm using vim. I've enabled the ruler feature, so I have
a line/column indicator at the bottom of the screen. With the COBOL syntax
module, lines which go past column 72 are highlighted, as are a number of other
common mistakes...

----
The opinions above are my own and may or may not match those of my employer.
```

```
Chris Adams wrote in message ...
>On Tue, 29 Sep 1998 15:23:18 -0700, Haluk Okur <spbis@sbs.de> wrote:
>>You can't see the exact location of the characters on the screen
>>of vi easily. It took me half a day to find out the error: The dot
>
>This is one reason why I'm using vim. I've enabled the ruler feature, so I
have
>a line/column indicator at the bottom of the screen. With the COBOL syntax
>module, lines which go past column 72 are highlighted, as are a number of
other
>common mistakes...

Chris

Why don't you use the editor in AcuBench ???

In programming mode it will not let you type past column 72 and is
intelligent enough to wrap the line itself and place the continuation in the
correct columns.

If you are typing a literal, it will even place the "-" in column 7 and
automatically re-openthe literal with an open quote.

Surely you could get it for a good price !!!!!!!

MIke
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: debugging 101 <watch out for that "dot"!>

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** cadams@cadams-ntw.acucorp.com (Chris Adams)
- **Date:** 1998-09-30T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<slrn714nut.66o.cadams@cadams-ntw.acucorp.com>`
- **References:** `<361042D4.ABB86E96@mindspring.com> <36115DD6.2C73@sbs.de> <slrn71266h.66o.cadams@cadams-ntw.acucorp.com> <6usva2$mr7$1@news01.iafrica.com>`

```
On Wed, 30 Sep 1998 12:01:55 +0200, Mike Rochford <miker@easirun.co.za> wrote:
>>>You can't see the exact location of the characters on the screen
>>>of vi easily. It took me half a day to find out the error: The dot
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>>common mistakes...

>Why don't you use the editor in AcuBench ???

>In programming mode it will not let you type past column 72 and is
[Kind things snipped]
>Surely you could get it for a good price !!!!!!!

I do use (and like) the code editor when I'm running under Win95/NT. I spent
the better part of the last year working on the AcuBench project, so I wouldn't
have much of an excuse if I didn't like it. I've done a great deal of internal
development using Acucobol-GT and have been using AcuBench since the early
betas by choice.  

However, the last few weeks I've been working under Linux doing some genuinely
unusual things (Anyone else using Perl-generated COBOL?). There's also this
little matter that unlike NT Workstation, Linux doesn't become progressively
unstable and slow over the course of a day or two. I also must admit that I
prefer 12 132x50 text-mode virtual consoles[1] to almost any GUI I've tried.
I've created quite a few GUI apps and always make sure that they're responsive,
flexible and have keyboard shortcuts. I can only wish all programmers were so
considerate...

[1] Linux switches between them very quickly, too - ALT+F1-12. It's as close to
instantaneous as I can measure.

----
The opinions above are my own and may or may not match those of my employer.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: debugging 101 <watch out for that "dot"!>

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** tnguru@termnetinc.com (Ben Coleman)
- **Date:** 1998-09-30T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<361283c0.580227142@news.mindspring.com>`
- **References:** `<361042D4.ABB86E96@mindspring.com> <36115DD6.2C73@sbs.de> <slrn71266h.66o.cadams@cadams-ntw.acucorp.com> <6usva2$mr7$1@news01.iafrica.com> <slrn714nut.66o.cadams@cadams-ntw.acucorp.com>`

```
On 30 Sep 1998 16:45:47 GMT, cadams@cadams-ntw.acucorp.com (Chris Adams)
wrote:

>Anyone else using Perl-generated COBOL?

No, but I used Perl to go the other direction.  I had a variable-length
record file generated on a IBM mailframe which I needed to read from a
Clarion for Windows program.  I also had a COBOL copybook with the layout
of the file.  I ended up writing 3 perl programs which read the copybook,
generated the equivalent Clarion layout, generated code to do EBCDIC-ASCII
translations and byte-swaps for fields which needed it, documented fields
which might need manual EBCDIC-ASCII translation(because they were part of
a REDEFINES & thus couldn't just be automatically translated), all wrapped
up in a Clarion template, which made it easy to use from the Clarion
development environment.

Oh, the joys of computer multi-lingualism!

Ben
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: debugging 101 <watch out for that "dot"!>

```
On Wed, 30 Sep 1998 19:32:45 GMT, Ben Coleman <tnguru@termnetinc.com> wrote:

>No, but I used Perl to go the other direction.  I had a variable-length
>record file generated on a IBM mailframe which I needed to read from a

I definitely have to agree with the guy who compared Perl to Unix's Swiss Army
Chainsaw. I don't know that I'd want to maintain production systems written in
Perl (Too many programmers seem to learn Perl by reading the Obfuscated Perl
entries...) but it's sure handy for hacking something into functionality. Sort
of like duct tape...

>Oh, the joys of computer multi-lingualism!

My question here is about how many languages you have to know before you can
really claim to be a programming polyglot?

----
"Civilization won't *die* from Y2k. It'll be more like Civilization goes out
drinking and the next morning realizes the benefits of drinking gin from
smaller containers."
The opinions above are my own and may or may not match those of my employer.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: debugging 101 <watch out for that "dot"!>

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** tnguru@termnetinc.com (Ben Coleman)
- **Date:** 1998-09-30T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<361294e0.584611016@news.mindspring.com>`
- **References:** `<361042D4.ABB86E96@mindspring.com> <36115DD6.2C73@sbs.de> <slrn71266h.66o.cadams@cadams-ntw.acucorp.com> <6usva2$mr7$1@news01.iafrica.com> <slrn714nut.66o.cadams@cadams-ntw.acucorp.com> <361283c0.580227142@news.mindspring.com> <slrn7153fk.66o.cadams@cadams-ntw.acucorp.com>`

```
On 30 Sep 1998 20:02:23 GMT, cadams@cadams-ntw.acucorp.com (Chris Adams)
wrote:

>I definitely have to agree with the guy who compared Perl to Unix's Swiss Army
>Chainsaw. I don't know that I'd want to maintain production systems written in
>Perl (Too many programmers seem to learn Perl by reading the Obfuscated Perl
>entries...) but it's sure handy for hacking something into functionality. Sort
>of like duct tape...

Depends on how they're written.  It's possible to write readable Perl.  The
perl programs I mentioned earlier aren't just a quick one-time hack - I've
kept them around because when our vendor changes the file format, just
about all I have to do is run the new copybook through the scripts, and I'm
ready to deal with the new format.

>My question here is about how many languages you have to know before you can
>really claim to be a programming polyglot?

One answer is "enough so that picking up yet another language isn't too
hard".

A better answer is "enough so that you can readily distinguish between
programming concepts and their implementations in different languages"(and
as a result, find it easier to pick up new languages - it's just a matter
of figuring out how the new language implements the programming concepts
you already know, and then picking up any new ones it introduces).  Alas,
not everyone who learns several languages gets that far.  Hence the old
jokes about people who can "write Fortan in any language".

Ben
```

```
I quite agree with you - I was complaining about the culture, not the language.
I think the average Perl programmer is less aware of the importance of
maintainability compared to, say, COBOL programmers. Of course, when Perl is as
old as COBOL, I have no doubt that most programmers will have learned that
lesson. It's really just time and education...

>>My question here is about how many languages you have to know before you can
>>really claim to be a programming polyglot?

>One answer is "enough so that picking up yet another language isn't too
>hard".
[SNIP]
>not everyone who learns several languages gets that far.  Hence the old
>jokes about people who can "write Fortan in any language".

Yeah - I'm down to about a week on average for a new language. It disturbs me
to meet people who use only one language and have no interest in expanding
their knowledge (When all you have is a hammer...).
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: debugging 101 <watch out for that "dot"!>

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc123@bellsouth.net>
- **Date:** 1998-09-29T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<bj6Q1.596$T5.819525@news4.mia.bellsouth.net>`
- **References:** `<361042D4.ABB86E96@mindspring.com> <Jl0PnHJ5PvPd-pn2-mFb7i4lsow8b@Dwight_Miller.iix.com>`

```
Thane Hubbell wrote:
>
>skidmike wrote:
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
>This is why I only use one period  - at the end of each paragraph.  <G>

Though Thane's approach works, and it seems a number of programmers use
it, being an old-timer, I still follow the traditional style.  The way
I address this issue is to use a fairly simple code scanner which checks
for missing/extra periods.  As long as you follow a consistent pattern
of indenting, you can easily check for missing/extra periods against your
indenting.  A large number of logic errors have been found this way.
```

---

<a id="t-72f977f3b4"></a>
## Thread: New to COBOL — 9 msgs

### New to COBOL

- **From:** "Kenneth Morrison" <kennethmorrison@sprintmail.com>
- **Date:** 1998-09-28T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<6unlg3$3ol$1@ash.prod.itd.earthlink.net>`

```
Hello

    I'm interested in learning COBOL until y2k is history. I'm studying C++
right now, and asked my professor what he thought was the best thing to do
to get ready. He doesn't think I have enough time.
    I'm getting a certificate in computer informations systems, trying to
learn a few languages, and I figure COBOL would be a good thing to learn
simply because of the y2k problem.
    Is my prof right? Am I too late to get into this?

Be well
Ken
```

#### ↳ Re: New to COBOL

- **From:** Jeff Farkas <Jeffrey.Farkas@gte.net>
- **Date:** 1998-09-28T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<6uo0eg$lln$3@news-1.news.gte.net>`
- **References:** `<6unlg3$3ol$1@ash.prod.itd.earthlink.net>`

```
Hi Ken,

Kenneth Morrison wrote:

> Hello
>
>     I'm interested in learning COBOL until y2k is history. I'm studying C++
> right now, and asked my professor what he thought was the best thing to do
> to get ready. He doesn't think I have enough time.

I think your professor needs to get out more and see what's really
happening. He seems to feel that everything will be done by
Jan 1 2000, which is far from true. There should be plenty of
work after Jan 1 2000 fixing the programs that were not mission
critical and also fixing new bugs created by the Y2K fix.

>
>     I'm getting a certificate in computer informations systems, trying to
> learn a few languages, and I figure COBOL would be a good thing to learn
> simply because of the y2k problem.
>     Is my prof right? Am I too late to get into this?

Don't learn Cobol just because of the Y2K problem, learn Cobol because
it is used by a large number of companies and it will continue to be used
by a lot of places after Jan. 1, 2000.

Your professor is far from correct, Cobol will be used well after
the Year 2000 problem is "fixed".


>
>
> Be well
> Ken
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: New to COBOL

- **From:** skidmike <skidmike@mindspring.com>
- **Date:** 1998-09-28T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<361027DC.5E00BFB7@mindspring.com>`
- **References:** `<6unlg3$3ol$1@ash.prod.itd.earthlink.net> <6uo0eg$lln$3@news-1.news.gte.net>`

```
Don't let your prof scare you away from COBOL.  It's easy to learn.  I
picked the simple basics up right away.  (still a LONG way to go, ask
anyone here).  It will be a long long long long time before the last
line of cobol has been lost.  COBOL will probably live to see the Y2100
crisis.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: New to COBOL

- **From:** "Kenneth Morrison" <kennethmorrison@sprintmail.com>
- **Date:** 1998-09-28T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<6upaem$db2$1@birch.prod.itd.earthlink.net>`
- **References:** `<6unlg3$3ol$1@ash.prod.itd.earthlink.net> <6uo0eg$lln$3@news-1.news.gte.net> <361027DC.5E00BFB7@mindspring.com>`

```
Hello and thank you to all replyers

    I knew I would never learn COBOL in time for y2k, and I think mabye my
prof misunderstood and thought that was what I wanted. However, I didn't
know it would continue to be used so much after 2000. Your answers have
prompted me to think about replacing code for a little while, especially
after hearing how much COBOL programmers are getting paid.
    I'm guessing COBOL is easier to learn than C++ (what I'm learning now)
because its older. Am I right? I'm not finding C++ hard at all.
    My school stopped teaching COBOL classes because of its age (argh!). Is
it possible to learn it on my own or should I try to find a class elsewhere?
I don't think I would have a problem--I taught myself BASIC on a TRS-80 when
I was a kid.

Be well
Ken
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: New to COBOL

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc123@bellsouth.net>
- **Date:** 1998-09-29T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7x2Q1.402$2P.727506@news2.mia.bellsouth.net>`
- **References:** `<6unlg3$3ol$1@ash.prod.itd.earthlink.net> <6uo0eg$lln$3@news-1.news.gte.net> <361027DC.5E00BFB7@mindspring.com> <6upaem$db2$1@birch.prod.itd.earthlink.net>`

```
Kenneth Morrison wrote:
>I'm guessing COBOL is easier to learn than C++ (what I'm learning now)
>because its older. Am I right? I'm not finding C++ hard at all.

Learning COBOL is comparable in difficulty to learning QBasic.  Learning
C++ is, by comparison, like learning Sanskrit.  Looks pretty much the same,
too. ;-)  The reason you're finding C++ 'not hard at all' is, I suspect,
because you haven't really gotten into the subtleties (and the difficulties
which arise from that) of it as yet.  COBOL is very simple and has very few
subtleties, by comparison.  With the addition of OO and pointers to COBOL,
that is changing some.
```

```
 "COBOL will probably live to see the Y2100 crisis"

     > There will be problems long before the year 2100.
        Many companies are using windowing as a stop-gap measure,
        particularly to resolve Electronic Data Interchange (EDI) issues,
        where they receive non-compliant input from other companies.
        Let's say Company A adopted 20 as their window.
        They will write some windowing code, for example:
                 MOVE EDI-DT-INPUT-YYMMDD TO WS-EDI-DT-INPUT-YYMMDD.
                 IF EDI-DT-INPUT-YY < 20
                     MOVE  20 TO WS-EDI-DT-INPUT-CC
                 ELSE
                     MOVE  19 TO WS-EDI-DT-INPUT-CC.

         However, Company A books Sales Orders up to 2 years ahead of
delivery.
         That means in the year 2018, the windowing routine will be
processing
         input dates for the year 2020.  However, the logic as written will
set the
         Century to 19.  Which means the window will have to be moved.
Again and again.

    >  COBOL is not going away soon, despite  any actual or perceived
deficiencies
       in the language. The major problem is the databases, and the data in
them.
       Companies face a massive task to convert the databases from IMS DB/DC
       or CICS, to SAP or J D Edwards, for example.  Then they have to
convert the
       programs and JCL and Ops documentation.  And retrain the Users.
       And keep a global business humming WITHOUT MISSING A BEAT.
       Been there, more than once.
Bottom line.  Don't sell COBOL or mainframes short.  There are thousands of
                       companies with miillions of mission-critical
databases and programs
                       which will require development, maintenance and
support for decades.
                       Unless of course Microsoft decides it doesn't want to
be JUST a
                       PC software company, and goes after IBM's mainframe
business.
                       Which they will not accomplish and cannot do without
a viable
                       conversion support task force.  And a much beefier
WIN NT.

skidmike wrote in message <361027DC.5E00BFB7@mindspring.com>...

>crisis.
```

## Thread: Newbie needs advice — 3 this month / 4 total

_Topics: [`Tutorials, books, learning`](topics.md#learning)_

### Newbie needs advice

- **From:** "Mark Adams" <adams@angry.prestel.co.uk>
- **Date:** 1998-09-28T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<01bdeb18$24b005e0$LocalHost@default>`

```
Hi Gang,

I would like to introduce myself to Cobol prior to attending a college
course or I may have a bash at home.
I need advice on how / where to buy or otherwise obtain a version of Cobol
and what version do I buy - if that is applicable.
What beginners books are available ?

Any advice would be appreciated.

Mark Adams
```

#### ↳ Re: Newbie needs advice

- **From:** Jeff Farkas <Jeffrey.Farkas@gte.net>
- **Date:** 1998-09-28T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<6uou15$mtb$1@news-1.news.gte.net>`
- **References:** `<01bdeb18$24b005e0$LocalHost@default>`

```
Hi Mark,

Mark Adams wrote:

> Hi Gang,
>
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> and what version do I buy - if that is applicable.

A real nice Cobol compiler is available from Fujistu at:
http://www.adtools.com. Its a free download and it works just
fine. I think it is a good way to go if you want to learn Cobol.

>
> What beginners books are available ?

As for books I think this is mostly a personal choice. Presently
I am using Advanced ANSI Cobol with Structured Programming
by Gary DeWard Brown. It is not a bad book and it covers the topic
quite well. Another good book is Teach Yourself Cobol in 21
Days. This seems to be popular with a large group of people and
I would check it out if I were starting out.

>
>
> Any advice would be appreciated.
>
> Mark Adams
```

#### ↳ Re: Newbie needs advice

- **From:** coboljit@aol.com (COBOLJIT)
- **Date:** 1998-09-30T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<19980930164225.04702.00001401@ngol08.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<01bdeb18$24b005e0$LocalHost@default>`

```

<< I would like to introduce myself to Cobol prior to attending a college
course or I may have a bash at home.
I need advice on how / where to buy or otherwise obtain a version of Cobol
and what version do I buy - if that is applicable.
What beginners books are available ?

Any advice would be appreciated. >>
The very least you need is a good book and a compiler.  Teach Yourself COBOL in
21 Days is the best selling COBOL book, and it's used in colleges and training
programs across the world, because its VERY easy to understand.  It assumes you
know nothing about programming and teaches it very simply giving lots of
analogies, and defining new terms and concepts at they arise.  It's very good
for teaching the basics of COBOL, which is what you want right now.

The next thing you need is a compiler.  We recommend the MicroFocus COBOL
Compiler for DOS, as thats what TYC21D was written to go with originally.  The
student version is $50.00.

Some people have trouble locating errors in their code and do not have anyone
to help them, so for these people we recommend the disk of code for Teach
Yourself COBOL in 21 Days, and the EZ-Compare Utility.  Then, if you write the
practice lesson and there's an error you just can't find, you can compare the
code you wrote to the code that's on the disk, and it will show you where the
error are.

For those that need a more structured approach or even more help with the
compiler and editor, we recomend the COBOL, Just in Time course, which has
explicit lessons on how to install and use the compiler and editor. If you
don't want to bother with trying to find these locally, give me a call.

Helen
COBOL, Just in Time Course Administrator
King Computer Services, Inc.
818-951-5240
Helen

------------------------------------------------------------------------------
King Computer Services, Inc.
COBOL Training for the Year 2000
"COBOL, Just in Time" Crash Course
"COBOL, Dates and the Year 2000" Technical Reference.
818-951-5240
```

## Thread: Console Colors in MF Personal Cobol — 9 this month / 14 total

_Topics: [`Compilers and vendors`](topics.md#compilers)_

### Console Colors in MF Personal Cobol

- **From:** heidegger@my-dejanews.com
- **Date:** 1998-09-28T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<6uo747$ct5$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>`

```
A thousand pardons if this is off-topic and/or a FAQ, but MF Personal Cobol
running on my Win95 box displays barely legible colors in the output console
window. I can't seem to find any way to configure this.

TIA for any light shed on this subject!

-----== Posted via Deja News, The Leader in Internet Discussion ==-----
http://www.dejanews.com/rg_mkgrp.xp   Create Your Own Free Member Forum
```

#### ↳ Re: Console Colors in MF Personal Cobol

- **From:** "Jay Moseley" <jay^_^moseley@ibm-dot-net>
- **Date:** 1998-09-28T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<wnlzbfryrlvozarg.f007pv0.pminews@news1.ibm.net>`
- **References:** `<6uo747$ct5$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>`

```
I use Microsoft COBOL 5.0 (which is really repackaged MicroFocus COBOL) and I
have experienced what you describe. Often I write quick&dirty programs to
test code fragments and I finally got annoyed enough with the barely legible
dark red on black information DISPLAYed that I looked into the problem. I
discovered that when I was linking the programs I was not including the 2
libraries LCOBOL and COBAPI. The programs linked and ran fine without them,
but when I put these 2 libraries into the link command the text DISPLAYed
became white on black, which is much more readible.

Hope this helps.

Jay Moseley, CCP


On Mon, 28 Sep 1998 14:40:07 GMT, heidegger@my-dejanews.com wrote:

:>A thousand pardons if this is off-topic and/or a FAQ, but MF Personal Cobol
:>running on my Win95 box displays barely legible colors in the output console
:>window. I can't seem to find any way to configure this.
:>
:>TIA for any light shed on this subject!
:>
:>-----== Posted via Deja News, The Leader in Internet Discussion ==-----
:>http://www.dejanews.com/rg_mkgrp.xp   Create Your Own Free Member Forum
```

```
for hapless beginning CS students such as myself. Although there is an option
to add compiler directives, a) I don't know what they would look like, and b)
I don't see anything resembling the libs you linked in to improve your
console display. Since Personal Cobol is crippled in that it cannot produce
executable files, I wonder how much flexibility is possible when it comes to
link arguments.

Sorry if this sounds fairly brainless but I'm about three weeks into a first
Cobol course and am in fact brainless at this stage of the game where Cobol is
concerned.
```

```
screwing about.
```

```
heidegger@my-dejanews.com wrote in message
<6ur64c$l59$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>...
>AYE, there's the rub: I'm using the Windoze version! So the console in
>question is a Win95 text console window.
>NOW, call me paranoid, but I suspect MF set up this academic product with
>an almost unreadable DISPLAY output so that the chance would be almost
>zero that it might be put to actual use. Yes, no, punt?

I'd call you paranoid <g>. In fact, it is common to give away the
last version cheap to students.  Problem is, there are no *old*
Windows95 versions,  so the final of the last version happens
to be a DOS version, written and sold before the Windows95
"console" even existed.  That is when I bought it, and as I recall
I paid in the $600 dollars Canadian range for it.  That is about
a grand in U.S.A. dollars.  The current upgrade is about $6000
in our money, so you got a good deal.  I would not complain ...
it is good enough to learn on.  Like I said, you can get a fairly
good picture by going DOS mode fully, or playing with the
Windows95 palettes.

As I recall, that is a pretty solid compiler.  While I have switched
companies (I find the new product grossly overpriced), I earned
in the one million range using it for five or six years.
```

```
>being that i hate the "softcopy" style manuals, is $190 a good price for
>the full set of manuals in book form?

I have just stuck it in my budget.  I ***HATE*** the online manuals. They
are not worth a fiddler's fuck.  First of all, you cannot find anything,
then when you do, you cannot read it, then when you manage to get
it to a size that it is readable, you cannot cut and paste the examples.

A help system, then eight separate online manuals is stupid.  If they
are going to be online, then why not part of the help?  I want one of
those old-fashioned charts (standard Cobol) with every term and the
arguments in various levels of brackets.  Then every word should be
a hypertext link to the next level of detail, right down to an example
I can paste into my code. *That* would be worth as much as the product.
```

## Thread: pctran — 2 msgs

### pctran

- **From:** wouter wijker <wouterwijker@unn.unisys.com>
- **Date:** 1998-09-28T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<360FC73A.10F@unn.unisys.com>`

```
PCTRAN is still widely in use on the Unisys S/80 models.
A description can be found in UP-8842 REv.4 at page F-4.

The batch-mode works with the UTS STEP-emulation and a CMDFILE.
In case of problems, check the patch-level of your machine to see if it
is adequate. ( Contact your Unisys-rep)

I know of a problem with the newer versions of Attachmates PEP-gate in
respect to the batch-mode. 
Revert to an older version of PEP/STEP or again contact your Unisys-rep.
```

- **From:** "rmch" <rmch@cadvision.com>

## Thread: help reqd in reference modification — 3 msgs

_Topics: [`Tutorials, books, learning`](topics.md#learning) · [`Help requests and how-to`](topics.md#help)_

### help reqd in reference modification

- **From:** "D.Bhaskar Reddy" <Bhaskar.Reddy@pcm.bosch.de>
- **Date:** 1998-09-28T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<360F5775.52F7BA92@pcm.bosch.de>`

```
Is there any one to clear my doubt in reference modification ? Pls tell
me if the following code is valid.

With reference modification we can move a part of string into another
string like

MOVE STRING1 (1:5) TO STRING2.

This will move 5 charecters from string1 starting from charecter 1. Now
look at this

MOVE STRING1(1:LENGTH) TO STRING2.

LENGTH is a Working storage variable and holds a value 5. Is this 2nd
statement valid ? Will this work same as the previous move statement ? I
am using the VS-COBOL II compiler.

TIA & Regds
```

#### ↳ Re: help reqd in reference modification

- **From:** "Peter Ruzicka" <RuzickaP@Wien.Spardat.at>
- **Date:** 1998-09-28T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<01bdeade$ddb04d00$394212ac@W0188000064.f000.d0188.sd.spardat.at>`
- **References:** `<360F5775.52F7BA92@pcm.bosch.de>`

```
> 
> MOVE STRING1(1:LENGTH) TO STRING2.
> 

I think it will not work, because LENGTH is a reserved COBOL word,
but it would work, if you use another name for the variable
(and the daclaration of the variable is correct numeric).
```

### Help needed in reference modification

- **From:** "D.Bhaskar Reddy" <Bhaskar.Reddy@pcm.bosch.de>
- **Date:** 1998-09-28T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<360F5349.844055B6@pcm.bosch.de>`

```
Is there anyone to clear my doubt regarding reference modification ?
Tell me if the following code works.

    working-storage section
    01    start      pic    9(01) value 6.
    01    string1    pic    x(09) value 'microsoft'.
    01    string2    pic    x(05).
    procedure division
    move string1 (6:4) to string2
*  this above statement will work and the value of string2 will be SOFT.

    move string1 (start:4) to string2.
* Is this statement valid ?


TIA & Regds
xxxyyyzzz
```

<a id="t-66e0dd8baf"></a>
## Thread: Fujitsu Stop Run? — 3 msgs

_Topics: [`Compilers and vendors`](topics.md#compilers)_

### Fujitsu Stop Run?

- **From:** "Donald Tees" <donald@willmack.com>
- **Date:** 1998-09-28T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<6uof4c$hou$1@news.igs.net>`

```
This is probably a really stupid question, but how do
you stop a Version 4 PowerCobol program?  The
standard "stop run" gives an error message,
that it is illegal, though it does stop.  I have tried
invoke the "terminate" method of the main form,
and that does not seem to work.  I have tried to
call "CLOSESHEET" of the main form, no luck. I have
scanned the documentation for "stop" "end" "close"
"run", etc, and cannot seem to find a method that
terminates a program.  I just want to abort the damned
thing after an error.
```

#### ↳ Re: Fujitsu Stop Run?

- **From:** skidmike <skidmike@mindspring.com>
- **Date:** 1998-09-28T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<361028A8.E5968591@mindspring.com>`
- **References:** `<6uof4c$hou$1@news.igs.net>`

```
EXIT PROGRAM?  it's what i use.  STOP RUN made fsc 3.0 hurl on me too
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Fujitsu Stop Run?

- **From:** "Donald Tees" <donald@willmack.com>
- **Date:** 1998-09-29T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<6uq5ih$9c9$1@news.igs.net>`
- **References:** `<6uof4c$hou$1@news.igs.net> <361028A8.E5968591@mindspring.com>`

```

skidmike wrote in message <361028A8.E5968591@mindspring.com>...
>EXIT PROGRAM?  it's what i use.  STOP RUN made fsc 3.0 hurl on me too

No, exit program is a subroutine exit.  In a code fragment (a
GUI event), it only exits from the event.  It does not abort the
entire program.  I did eventually discover the problem -- the
correct command was what I tried first:
INVOKE "Terminate" OF MAINFORM.

However, the manual shows it as
INVOKE "Terminate" USING form RETURNING ERROR.

Now, the interesting thing is that if you call it with an error
return, it fails and returns no error. (IE-it returns, and it should
not, as it is supposed to end the program).  However, if you
omit the RETURNING clause, it never returns, which is
correct.  Go figure.
```

- **From:** alphaman@hsv.sungardtrust.com

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<6uoj8v$qdc$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>`

```
All,

I've been able to get MicroFocus to the point of investigating whether they
should port NetExpress to Windows NT running on the Alpha platform.

IMO, this is an excellent combination, in that it allows people coming from a
VMS or UNIX environment running on Alphas the ability to re-engineer their
legacy application's interface to a Web-based GUI with little effort while
staying on the same hardware platform.  This makes an excellent investment
protection story for your customers while raising customer satisfaction!

The fact that it's also the fastest NT platform around is a very nice side
benefit too!

If you are interested in seeing this package ported to Alpha NT, please
contact the following Robert Collins at Micro Focus to let him know your
needs:

 nospam-rdc@microfocus.com-nospam

(be sure to remove the nospam- from the above when replying.)

If you have any questions, feel free to contact me regarding this issue.

Regards and thanks for your consideration,
Aaron
```

```
Can I advise the readers of this news group about the Special Offer now
available at www.casegen.co.uk.

For $50 US, we will send you the latest "Casegen COBOL for Windows CD" which
makes it easy to write 32-bit Windows applications in Cobol.  The tool
includes a GUI screen painter, a really-fast Cobol syntax-highlighting
editor with many features to simplify writing Casegen event code or for use
on any Cobol files, together with the Fujitsu V3 compiler which you could
later upgrade to the new highly-acclaimed V4 compiler.  There are no
limitations - this is the full product.

Casegen lets you use the full power of Cobol in your Windows programs, with
a simplified and easy-to-use Windows interface, although you can still issue
Windows calls yourself in Cobol if you really want to!  Think how great it
would be to add a GUI front end to your existing Cobol programs or access
your Cobol files through a user-friendly interface!

Unlike other tools - watch your Windows programs execute at speed!  Casegen
generates 99% Cobol source code with Windows calls in Cobol.  Simple, well
structured, fast programs with small Cobol executables.  The Cobol run time
library is required, but there are no runtime charges.

Casegen also supports Fujitsu V4, and will support MicroFocus compilers
shortly and for those of you already familiar with Casegen, we are
constantly upgrading and improving it, often based on your feedback.

Soon, the "Casegen COBOL for Windows Professional" product will be available
at an introductory price of $100 US.  This product adds full SQL/ODBC
capability to the standard product making client/server programming against
databases such as SQL Server, DB2, Access and SQL Anywhere simpler than ever
before.  Customers purchasing the standard ($50 US) version now, will be
able to upgrade to the Professional product when it is available for an
additional $50 US.

This special price is for a limited time only and is limited to one copy per
customer, small company, or department in a larger company.  Additional
copies cost $250 US.  Electronic documentation is provided together with
many sample programs to get you started very quickly.

Write Windows programs today with just your Cobol experience - our customers
tell us its definitely easier than VB.

If you are not completely satisfied, simply return the CD and we will refund
your money (less postage) up to 28 days after Casegen arrives.  We will also
provide the first 90 days free email support.

Please email me at simonc@casegen.co.uk for more information.

Simon Cordingley
Technical Support Manager
Casegen Systems Ltd.
```
