<a id="t-329c242563"></a>
## Thread: Unix->MVS file transfer; MF COBOL — 1 this month / 5 total

_Topics: [`Compilers and vendors`](topics.md#compilers) · [`Mainframe, z/OS, JCL, CICS`](topics.md#mainframe)_

### Unix->MVS file transfer; MF COBOL

- **From:** "Michael C. Kasten" <mck9@swbell.net>
- **Date:** 1998-09-28T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36104E32.A0E@swbell.net>`

```
Is there a way to transfer a MF COBOL file, with binary data and
variable length records, to MVS?

Our shop is migrating an MVS application to Unix (specifically HPUX),
using among other things MicroFocus COBOL.  One of our clients is now
getting a file from the MVS version.  It is a variable-blocked file
(recfm=vb) with both character and binary fields (i.e. COMP and COMP-3 
as well as PIC 9 and PIC X).

This client insists on receiving exactly the same file format from the
Unix-based system.  He refuses to make any changes to his programs, and
upper management has promised (without asking us grunts, of course)
that he won't have to.

We can handle the ASCII-EBCDIC issues, the use of COMP-3, and so 
forth.  However, we haven't found a way to ship such a file from Unix 
to MVS.

I've looked into both FTP and NDM.  So far as I can tell, either can 
produce variable-blocked files on the MVS end, but only if the Unix 
file is a text file, with records delimited by line feeds.  Since
our file must contain binary data, we can't rely on line feeds to
mark the record boundaries.

MicroFocus offers their own variable-length format where each record
is preceded by something like a record descriptor word to encode the
length.  However, this format is not identical to the corresponding
MVS format.

I wrote some C routines to emulate the MVS style of record blocking.
However, when I FTP the file to MVS in binary mode, the records are
are simply concatenated into big long records, with record descriptor 
words treated as data.  The intended file structure is lost.  I
haven't tried NDM yet, but the manual leads me to expect similar
results.

Of course, the sensible approach would be to send the file in an 
all-character format, and then convert it on the other end.  For 
political reasons, however, this approach is not an option unless we 
can show that it is absolutely unavoidable.

Our Plan B is to send an all-character version to our mainframe, 
reformat it, and then send the reformatted version to the client's 
mainframe.

Can anyone suggest a less ugly approach?

Michael C. Kasten	mck9@swbell.net
http://home.swbell.net/mck9/cobol/cobol.html
```

---

<a id="t-69aa7f2830"></a>
## Thread: Fujitsu 4.0 vs MF NetExpress & MF Resolve — 3 this month / 5 total

_Topics: [`Compilers and vendors`](topics.md#compilers)_

### Re: Fujitsu 4.0 vs MF NetExpress & MF Resolve

- **From:** "Gene Webb" <gene.webb@newsguy.com>
- **Date:** 1998-09-29T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<6urdq1$188@enews2.newsguy.com>`
- **References:** `<6ur3gb$hp1$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>`

```
You didn't mention one large piece of criteria, stability.  Go back and
search for problems with the Fujitsu product and problems with the
NetExpress product (they both have them).  Also search for other notes on
these two products in the NG.  Some of the Fujitsu problems I have read seem
to be fundamental things that would have been worked out in a mature
product.

Also look into support.  While I can't commend the great telephone support
for either (I have experience with Microfocus but not Fujitsu).  I am a big
fan of the MicroFocus AnswerLab and have gotten a lot of answers from other
users just a fast as from technical support (which I think is great!).  I
don't know if Fujitsu has something similar.  Plus there is a much large
installed base for MF which I think is a plus for any product.  I think it
tends to mean problems get report and fixed faster.

I also like the WebSync forum for getting updates to my NetExpress product a
lot better than the old way updates were distributed. I don't know if
Fujitsu has this type of thing or not.

I can tell you hands down if you plan on writting OO Cobol, Fujitsu is not
an option for a mission critical application. It lacks fundamental support
for collections and call backs, is NOT REENTRANT (and this is not likely to
change in the near future according to my source as it would require a
complete overall of the product).

I think in a couple of years Fujitsu will be a super product, however, if
you have to deliver a mission critical application NOW I would recommend
NetExpress.  There used to be an old saying "You Never Get Fired for Buying
IBM".  I think this holds true with the NetExpress product.  I am not going
to blow smoke at you NetExpress has/had problems but I have been using MF
products for the last decade and have always been able to deliver production
applications.

There were also some recent discussions on delivering statically linked
applications (which I only followed on the edge) you might look into this.

As far as integration with VB I have VB frontends to my NetExpress
application and had no trouble with this.  Version 3.0 which is due out
later this year (I think) has a lot more support for VB integration I am
told.  It really doesnt matter making calls to NetExpress DLLs is really no
different than calling a C or WINAPI DLL not rocket science and if you are
the one writing the DLLs it becomes a no brainer once you know the
integration issues (NO ARRAYS in your parameter list if you don't want
headaches, which has nothing to do with NE or Fujitsu).

I guess my bottom line is unless you are an individual developer the cost
should not be an issue, you get what you pay for in terms of maturity and
functionality.  If I have to err I always err on the side of maturity in a
product.

Thats my 2 cents
```

#### ↳ Re: Fujitsu 4.0 vs MF NetExpress & MF Resolve

- **From:** redsky@ibm.net (Thane Hubbell)
- **Date:** 1998-09-29T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<Jl0PnHJ5PvPd-pn2-bsZRHviefQXk@Dwight_Miller.iix.com>`

```
On Tue, 29 Sep 1998 19:51:14, "Gene Webb" <gene.webb@newsguy.com> 
wrote:

> I can tell you hands down if you plan on writting OO Cobol, Fujitsu is not
> an option for a mission critical application. It lacks fundamental support
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> 

Not sure this is true.  I've been told that you CAN use recursion with
Fujitsu but I did not ask "How" (It may not be the same as Reentrant -
I know).

As far as OO goes, I think it is unfair to critisize Fujitsu - or ANY 
OTHER implementation of OO in COBOL - The standard has been in flux - 
ANY coding of a production OO COBOL system may find itself 
incompatible with the new standard.

 
> As far as integration with VB I have VB frontends to my NetExpress
> application and had no trouble with this.  Version 3.0 which is due out
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>

What have you heard about the future of Dialog system?  A friend has 
the 3.0 beta and can't get it to run (Incident open at MicroFocus).  
Have you used it?  Opinion?  Do you think they are moving away from it
and toward a VB or Internet based GUI?
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Fujitsu 4.0 vs MF NetExpress & MF Resolve

- **From:** "Gene Webb" <gene.webb@newsguy.com>
- **Date:** 1998-09-30T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<6utrep$9lp@enews4.newsguy.com>`
- **References:** `<6ur3gb$hp1$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com> <6urdq1$188@enews2.newsguy.com> <Jl0PnHJ5PvPd-pn2-bsZRHviefQXk@Dwight_Miller.iix.com>`

```
Thane Hubbell wrote in message ...

>Not sure this is true.  I've been told that you CAN use recursion with
>Fujitsu but I did not ask "How" (It may not be the same as Reentrant -
>I know).

Try implementing your own call back routine in a class library and see if
you don't get a different answer.  I got this from a Fujitsu employee who
used to be a MicroFocus employee.

>As far as OO goes, I think it is unfair to critisize Fujitsu - or ANY
>OTHER implementation of OO in COBOL - The standard has been in flux -
>ANY coding of a production OO COBOL system may find itself
>incompatible with the new standard.

Not true, there have been versions approved before the last was in 95.  If a
compiler company waits until a standard is "approved" they would be way
behind the times when it got approved.  In the case of a production system
using NetExpress class libaries I have been told that the next version will
comply with the latest standard but backward compatibility will be provided.
Even if it was not after converting code from NetExpress to Fujitsu (which
is suppose to be more compliant with the latest standard) it meant only
changing the class-control section to the repository section and using
factory instead of class-object.  This however was not my big complaint it
was the lack of support for collections and call backs.  If you ever use OO
Cobol for any heavy work you need these.  Other languages such as C++ and
SmallTalk all come with support for these things with the compiler that you
buy.

>What have you heard about the future of Dialog system?  A friend has
>the 3.0 beta and can't get it to run (Incident open at MicroFocus).
>Have you used it?  Opinion?  Do you think they are moving away from it
>and toward a VB or Internet based GUI?

I have thought long and hard about this question in the past.  My bottom
line answer is that I make all my applications (except the very smallest) 3
tiered so I could replace my user interface with anything from VB to Java to
Dialog Systems without a hugh effort.  This allows me not to care what
MicroFocus or Fujitsu or any other vendor does with their User Interface
support.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Loadlib member dates in TSO/ISPF?

- **From:** Gilbert Saint-flour <gsf@ibm.net>
- **Date:** 1998-09-29T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3611223b$5$tfs$mr2ice@news>`
- **References:** `<6uquqj$c0b$2@nnrp1.dejanews.com> <6ur580$icv$1@callisto.clark.net>`

```
In <6ur580$icv$1@callisto.clark.net>, on 29 Sep 1998 at 17:26,
docdwarf@clark.net () said:

>In article <6uquqj$c0b$2@nnrp1.dejanews.com>,  <gcooper@imf.org> wrote:
>>Does anyone have a way to list members of a loadlib and
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>>I'm all ears.

>IEHLIST, I believe.

The LISTIDR function of IEHLIST displays the compilation and link-edit
dates (julian).

Check if you havd the public-domain PDS command on your system (it may be
called PDS, or PDS83, PDS84 or PDS85).  PDS displays link-edit dates in
directory listings; its HISTORY command displays compiler codes, levels
and dates.

Gilbert Saint-flour <gsf@ibm.net>
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Loadlib member dates in TSO/ISPF?

- **From:** rkrayhawk@aol.com (RKRayhawk)
- **Date:** 1998-09-30T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<19980930170236.05667.00004344@ng112.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<3611692B.3AA1@netbox.com>`

```

gcooper@imf.org wrote:
>> Does anyone have a way to list members of a loadlib and their creation
dates?  This is a question specific to a TSO mainframe environment with OS 390,
VS COBOL & TSO/ISPF.
> >

It is possible that you need look no further than ISPF itself. The Utilities
Menu (which is option 3 in most shops), has a submenu option that allows you to
list the members of a PDS (usually function X), and you can send it to your
current .LIST dataset. When you do that hit PF1 to get an exact DSN.

It may be best to make this the only activity during the ISPF session you use
to go for the list So as to not get other distracting material in the .LIST
dataset.


After invoking the Utility procedure, exit ISPF with an option to save the
.LIST dataset. I would not necessarily print it the first time through this
exercise (that is, until you get accustom to the format, and decide if you like
it).

Instructing ISPF, upon exit, to save the dataset will induce the ISPF to also
leave the dataset alone the next time you enter, i.e. the next .LIST dataset
will have the next higher number. And the old .LIST dataset will be left around
indefinitely.

Re-enter ISPF.  For novices the quickest way may be to logoff and logon again.

You can now browse (or even edit) the older ISPF .LIST dataset.  You will find
that it has a formatted member listing of the PDS you asked  the Utility menu
to list. In some shops you will find that the .LIST dataset may have other
material in it that you were entirely unaware you were recording. So it is
good, in some cases, that you can indeed edit that prior .LIST dataset, to trim
it to just the report on the PDS you want.

After you have edited the dataset, you can print it, ... it already has
carriage control.  Generally that report is going to come out in alphabetical
order. There is probably a way to get ISPF to generate it in Date order
Ascending or Descending.  The report is fixed format so you can do alot of
interesting things to it with the SORT command from within ISPF edit.
(although your headings and carriage control will get disrupted if you sort it
in Edit).

Incidentally, STATS have to be on for the PDS dataset for this to work (or for
IEHLIST to work). I have seen ENDEVOR (mentioned by another poster)
environments, and other unique source code maintenance software, that defeats
STATS.  So it is useful to just inquire the PDS first before you pursue this.
If a Browse member list shows STATS you are okay. (Yes you can Browse a loadlib
PDS, It is very interesting).  

You will also notice sometimes that specific members are stat-less. That can
result from non-IBM software moving the members around, or from an 'advanced'
user of editors (such as ISPF itself) who has knowingly or unknowingly turned
STATS off for the most recent session with that member, that dataset, or for
all of their edit sessions.

It is a fact that you can not list the statistics if they are not there.
Recently, I saw a discussion of STATs apparently dissappearing when a shop
converted to OS/390.  But not really. They just genned the new OS with the less
favorable options, and as they converted individual datasets, the outbound
datasets had no stats, and the attribute was shut off for each PDS so created. 
They had to back track and reconvert.  All IBM OSes will faithfully generate
stats if the utility writing the dataset requests it (unless it is turned of at
the dataset level).

Best Wishes,
Robert Rayhawk
RKRayhawk@aol.com
```

```
>Can anybody tell me the keyboard shortcuts. First year student any tips.


They are at the top, labelled F1 through F12,
though that may vary by system.  They generally
shortcut to various things, depending on the program,
though that may vary by system, and program. They
can generally be reassigned to do other things, though
that will vary by system, program, and user.

The only variable you have mentioned is your name. With
that single variable set, the answer is that there are no
shortcuts, you will have to do some homework first, then
come back and ask for more.
```

## Thread: Fujitsu 4.0 vs MF NetExpress & MF Resolve — 1 msgs

_Topics: [`Compilers and vendors`](topics.md#compilers)_

### Re: Fujitsu 4.0 vs MF NetExpress & MF Resolve

- **From:** redsky@ibm.net (Thane Hubbell)
- **Date:** 1998-09-29T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<Jl0PnHJ5PvPd-pn2-2wzkdpPVI7Pc@Dwight_Miller.iix.com>`
- **References:** `<6ur3gb$hp1$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>`

```
On Tue, 29 Sep 1998 16:56:44, Clive.Allwork@gems8.gov.bc.ca wrote:

> Fujitsu 4.0 Enterprise 189.00 (CDN)
> MF NetExpress 5,220.00 (CDN)
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> 

MicroFocus is all things to all people.  Fujitsu is a little more 
narrow.  However, once you step into the world of NetExpress - they 
are pretty equal.

Micofocus has a tool in NetExpress to create HTML forms and interact 
with them as your user interface.  Reviews are still mixed.  Fujitsu 
has a similar product - NetCOBOL which I have not tried and cannot 
comment on.

> Some of our requirements:
> 1) EASE OF USE
> 2) INTEGRATION WITH VISUAL BASIC
> 3) SUPPORT FOR ORACLE DATABASES
> 

Ease of use - Give MicroFocus a SLIGHT edge with their WorkBench - 
although Fujitsu 4.0 is lightyears better than 3.0.

Integration with VB - Fujitsu hands down.

Support for Oracle Databases - Equal footing, but I think Fujitsu is 
more stable.
```

<a id="t-6de5b7ee6c"></a>
## Thread: Fujitsu 4.0 vs MF NetExpress & MF Resolve — 1 msgs

_Topics: [`Compilers and vendors`](topics.md#compilers)_

### Re: Fujitsu 4.0 vs MF NetExpress & MF Resolve

- **From:** "Donald Tees" <donald@willmack.com>
- **Date:** 1998-09-29T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<6urfl6$nt0$1@news.igs.net>`
- **References:** `<6ur3gb$hp1$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>`

```

Clive.Allwork@gems8.gov.bc.ca wrote in message
<6ur3gb$hp1$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>...
>Fujitsu 4.0 Enterprise 189.00 (CDN)
>MF NetExpress 5,220.00 (CDN)
>MF Revolve 4,5670.00 (CDN)
>
I have found Fujitsu to be more solid, and probably a bit more
versatile.  On the other hand, it is a much more immature
product, with a thousand little quirks that are a pain in the
ass.  The editor, for example, is one of the worst that I have
ever worked with (the full screen equivalent of EDLIN), but
so integrated that you almost have to use it.  I have decided
to go with Fujitsu because of the price, and the fact that they
seem *much* more responsive to their customers.  I certainly
hope to see a Version 4.1, 4.2, etc. in the near future, though.
They badly need to stand back and do some cleanup/tuning.
```

```
AFAIK sources weren't issued to the field engineers.

Brian
```

```
I'm using the VisualAge for COBOL version 2.2 in Windows95. Is there an
"easy" way to couple a Java program to COBOL, such as	

* Write AND link a COBOL DLL so that it acts as a Native Interface for a
JAVA applet ?
   I was able to do it with JDK1.1 and the shareware Java Coroutine Class
Library for            Win32 which is different from the Java Native
Interface (JNI).
A 1996 IBM COBOL Newsletter explained the procedure for AIX and promised an
analogous example for Windows later, but I've never seen it...?

* Use IBM's CORBA (DSOM) to generate to IDL and SOM-repository and then
integrate with JAVA-IDL interface in JDK1.2 ? It is not clear how the
JDK1.2 ORB works together with DSOM.

By the way, I refuse to accept that the answer would be VisualAge for C++
as an intermediate...

Any examples or suggestions are warmly welcomed !

Guido Dedene

P.S. I know some other products have native Java translation, which is
another circumvent...
```

<a id="t-b2e5d4918d"></a>
## Thread: Win32 Linking — 2 msgs

### Win32 Linking

- **From:** "Dick McMullen" <dmcmulle@exicorp.com>
- **Date:** 1998-09-30T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<361275f4.0@207.226.241.101>`

```
All,

Anyone know how to LINK a MicroFocus program that calls a Win32 function?  I
am trying to call a simple "Sleep" routine under NT and can't resolve the
SLEEP@4 external.  Can't find instructions in the MicroFocus doc on LINK
instructions for this.

Thanks,

Dick McMullen
```

#### ↳ Re: Win32 Linking

- **From:** Karl Wagner <kwagner@sympatico.ca>
- **Date:** 1998-09-30T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36127ACF.FB07E6F6@sympatico.ca>`
- **References:** `<361275f4.0@207.226.241.101>`

```
Assuming you've got a SPECIAL-NAMES paragraph with a CALL-CONVENTION 74 IS
WINAPI statement then the would look like this.

	CALL WINAPI "Sleep" USING BY VALUE 10 size 4 

You must use the CASE compiler directive to allow case-sensitive entrypoints.
The entrypoint in the API is Sleep@4, not SLEEP@4.

Dick McMullen wrote:
> 
> All,
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> Dick McMullen
```

```
Sorry about that....an MDI allows the user to open multiple files
simultaneously for editing or viewing, etc... much like MS Word and other
word processor's or spreadsheet's.....this is a guess on my part as I'm not
familiar with Fujitsu as yet....still delving the mysteries of Main
Framing.....but I do have some experience with VB and VC and that's what MDI
is for those platforms as opposed to SDI or Single Document Interface where
only on file at a time can be opened.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: MDI Forms -- Fujitsu

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** cadams@cadams-ntw.acucorp.com (Chris Adams)
- **Date:** 1998-09-30T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<slrn71574v.66o.cadams@cadams-ntw.acucorp.com>`
- **References:** `<6usj4v$i77$1@medousa.forthnet.gr> <Jl0PnHJ5PvPd-pn2-zhnzK1HpVhLY@Dwight_Miller.iix.com> <6uts8j$q6u$1@news.ses.cio.eds.com> <Jl0PnHJ5PvPd-pn2-0Cd7KUqzHwmn@Dwight_Miller.iix.com>`

```
On 30 Sep 98 20:26:37 GMT, Thane Hubbell <redsky@ibm.net> wrote:
>> Could it be Multiple Document Interface as is found in VB?

>Could be - but remember - I said I was TERMINALLY ignorant - what is 
>"Mutliple Document Interface"?

When a program has multiple subwindows that are all contained within a single
main window, it's using MDI. The alternative is creating multiple independent
windows.  Netscape does not use MDI as every new browser you open creates a
top-level window independent of the others. Programs like Excel or Word, on the
other hand, do use MDI as each document window is open inside of the main
application window.

The idea is that each application has a window and every document you open has
a subwindow inside that main window. If you have Win95, only the main windows
show up on the taskbar. Personally, I don't like MDI as it prevents usage of
ALT+TAB in the proper fashion.It's also worth noting that IBM's usability
testing showed MDI to be a bad idea and has basically depracated it on OS/2.


----
"Civilization won't *die* from Y2k. It'll be more like Civilization goes out
drinking and the next morning realizes the benefits of drinking gin from
smaller containers."
The opinions above are my own and may or may not match those of my employer.
```

## Thread: Test your COBOL II knowledge at new website! — 2 this month / 15 total

_Topics: [`Compilers and vendors`](topics.md#compilers) · [`Meta: FAQs, group policy, charter`](topics.md#meta)_

### Test your COBOL II knowledge at new website!

- **From:** Michael Littman <mikelittman@erols.com>
- **Date:** 1998-09-30T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** alt.cobol,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<361289EF.EE36355D@erols.com>`

```
Dear COBOL II programmer,

We are currently beta testing our web-based COBOL II knowledge test.
The format is
multiple choice.  To take the test, simply go to www.123apply.com and
select the link,
"Take a beta test".  The rest should be self-explanatory.  Before taking
the test, check out the 'top ten' list to see the best scores to date!

Upon completion of the test, you will be scored 1-5 (1-Novice,
2-Beginner, 3-Intermediate, 4-Advanced and 5-Master).  The test is
actually comprised of two tests
of 20 questions each (that's two chances to make the top ten!).  Any
feedback you can
provide us on the quality of the questions and the test itself would be
greatly appreciated.

You will be given the opportunity to provide feedback on each question
as it is
administered as well as at the end of the test.

The test is delivered in our production environment, so things might
seem a little 'strange'
prior to taking the test.  However, any information you provide will be
kept anonymous
(unless you make the top ten - we figured the bragging rights would make
it worth it!).

If you have any problems getting to the site, or have any questions in
general, please
feel free to contact me.  We hope that you enjoy the test and look
forward to your
feedback!

Good luck!

Mike
```

### what is the difference btw interactive and batch in cobol?

- **From:** chua_ennie <chua_ennie@pacific.net.sg>
- **Date:** 1998-09-30T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36121ECA.45CCFCE@pacific.net.sg>`

```
Dear all newsgroup users,

I am a student in cobol programming, just started recently. Wonder if
you can help me out about indexed, squential file, access mode. I felt
extremely confused about the single array and double array...when can we
implement it in a cobol program

And though I read up the Perform Varying in a cobol textbook, i still
cannot know how to use it, especially with a subscript.

I'll appreciate it if you can help me out regarding this.
```

#### ↳ Re: what is the difference btw interactive and batch in cobol?

- **From:** "Donald Tees" <donald@willmack.com>
- **Date:** 1998-09-30T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<6uu3vq$6pc$1@news.igs.net>`
- **References:** `<36121ECA.45CCFCE@pacific.net.sg>`

```
chua_ennie wrote in message <36121ECA.45CCFCE@pacific.net.sg>...
<snip>
You asked a whole bunch, but lets start out with
the perform varying.  But the following into your
code, compile it, and execute it.  By that time
you should be able to figure out what to ask.

In the working storage.
01 this-is-a-number        picture 9(3).

In the procedure division.
Main-line.
    perform this-is-a-paragraph
        varying this-is-a-number from 1 by 1
            until this-is-a-number is greater than 10.
    stop run.

this-is-a-paragraph.
    display this-is-a-number.
```

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3612B941.F1E94A93@mindspring.com>`

```
ok, i moved all of my source over to fujitsu 4 and compiled the
project.  the main program when [f2] is pressed calls the "create
estimate" program.  create estimate gets all the items on the estimate,
totals and saves them, then gets the customer's info.  2 screen i-o
routines that PERFORM UNTIL KEY-CODE = 4 ([f4] key pressed).  the first
time i execute the program, the estimating works fine.  the second time
the f2 key is pressed at the main screen, the called create estimate pgm
skips the 2 screen io routines.  i finally added a line right at the
beginning of the called pgm's source to move zero to key-code.  in
version 3, i never had this problem.  anyone run into something like
this?
```

#### ↳ Re: strange action of CALLed programs

- **From:** "Donald Tees" <donald@willmack.com>
- **Date:** 1998-09-30T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<6uuq7i$m03$1@news.igs.net>`
- **References:** `<3612B941.F1E94A93@mindspring.com>`

```

skidmike wrote in message <3612B941.F1E94A93@mindspring.com>...
<SNIP>

*Any* unititialized  variables can be anything at the start of a Cobol
program. I am presuming that "key-code" is in your working storage.
It should be initialized with a VALUE statement.  You are probably
starting with high values, and it is buggering up a logical
or on the shift key as a result.
```

## Thread: Editor PLEASE — 1 this month / 14 total

### Editor PLEASE

- **From:** "Donald Tees" <donald@willmack.com>
- **Date:** 1998-09-30T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<6uur63$ngo$1@news.igs.net>`

```
The editor on the Fujitsu Cobol Version 4 is driving me nuts.
It is completely unworkable on any program over two pages
long (the search does not work) and I am attempting to work
on a 4000 line program. It is completely impossible, to say
the least.  To complicate the problem, the compiler is as fussy
as hell about tabs Vs blanks in columns 1-7. You cannot tell
from most editors whether you are looking at legal code or
not, as <tab7><space> will not compile, while <space*8>
will.  <At least that is what I *think* is happening, as I cannot
really tell without editing my source in fucking hex. You get
these mysterious errors that disappear when you go in
and make sure there are 8 or 12 leading spaces>.

BTW, Thane, they corrected that problem in version 3 where
you could trick it by using quotes.  Now, a quote is illegal. You
apparently cannot search for anything but a string of letters. No
dashes, no tabs, no asterisks, no spaces nothing but strings
of pure unadulterated letters.  Whoever figured that out had
to be brain dead.  I don't know why they even bothered to write
it.

Now that I have gotten THAT off my chest, can anybody give me
a pointer to a downloadable editor?  I have heard several
mentioned in the last few weeks.  I am quite desperate to get
something NOW, even if it only works for thirty days while
I get a cheque cut and mailed.
```

<a id="t-0d957696c2"></a>
## Thread: Multi Threading within MicroFocus Cobol — 2 this month / 4 total

_Topics: [`Compilers and vendors`](topics.md#compilers)_

### Multi Threading within MicroFocus Cobol

- **From:** "Graeme Conn" <connman@iafrica.com>
- **Date:** 1998-09-30T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<6uso69$jh7$1@news01.iafrica.com>`

```
I require a method to start another thread while the current thread is still
executing. I need to simulate a CICS START TRANSACTION in MicroFocus Cobol
environment running under NT .

Any Ideas would be greatl\y appreciated.

Thanking you in advance
Graeme Conn
```

#### ↳ Re: Multi Threading within MicroFocus Cobol

- **From:** Kevin Digweed <ked@mfltd.co.uk>
- **Date:** 1998-09-30T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3611F35E.5AE8C28C@mfltd.co.uk>`
- **References:** `<6uso69$jh7$1@news01.iafrica.com>`

```
Graeme Conn wrote:
> 
> I require a method to start another thread while the current thread is still
> executing. I need to simulate a CICS START TRANSACTION in MicroFocus Cobol
> environment running under NT .

You don't say what COBOL version you are using, but under NetExpress
there is a whole host of CBL_THREAD_... API's to achieve threading
as well as extended syntax.

Note that older versions of the COBOL product were thread SAFE (ie,
the RTS ensures that only one thread may enter COBOL at a time), but
were not thread AWARE (ie, you cannot write a true multi-threaded COBOL
application).

Cheers,
Kev.
```

```
Patrick Lykins wrote in message <361211f9.0@news.mountain.net>...
```

#### ↳ Re: sound

- **From:** cadams@cadams-ntw.acucorp.com (Chris Adams)
- **Date:** 1998-09-30T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<slrn714oia.66o.cadams@cadams-ntw.acucorp.com>`
- **References:** `<361211f9.0@news.mountain.net>`

```
On Wed, 30 Sep 1998 07:08:41 -0400, Patrick Lykins
<lynkins@access.mountain.net> wrote:
>Can anyone tell me if their is a way to put sound into a CoBOL program?
>
>I really don't care how crude it is, if it's just a series of signal tone
>beeps, it is better than nothing.

What compiler are you using and what platform is it running on? 

If it's any flavor of text-mode, you're looking at a console bell (check your
manual to see if DISPLAY/ACCEPT have a bell or beep option). On the other hand
if you're running on Windows, your vendor may have provided a library routine
to play wave files (WIN$PLAYSOUND on Acucobol-GT, see I-144) and you can always
call the Windows API.

----
The opinions above are my own and may or may not match those of my employer.
```

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<6utiv6$8dn$1@nclient3-gui.server.virgin.net>`

```
Please help me, I am studying computers at college and can not seem to link
a .obj file into an .exe.
It comes up with "No entry point defined"!!
How do I fix this?


Regards

Luke Medland
```

#### ↳ Re: Help with linking on Fujitsu Cobol V3

- **From:** Jeff Farkas <Jeffrey.Farkas@gte.net>
- **Date:** 1998-09-30T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<6utm7o$pnj$1@news-1.news.gte.net>`
- **References:** `<6utiv6$8dn$1@nclient3-gui.server.virgin.net>`

```


Luke Medland wrote:

> Please help me, I am studying computers at college and can not seem to link
> a .obj file into an .exe.
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> Luke Medland

Hi Luke,

When you compile your program make sure you have the option
MAIN selected. Doing this will clear up your problem and you should
be able to link you program.
```

#### ↳ Re: Help with linking on Fujitsu Cobol V3

- **From:** "Fujitsu Software" <cobol@adtools.com>
- **Date:** 1998-09-30T00:00:00

- **References:** `<6utiv6$8dn$1@nclient3-gui.server.virgin.net>`

```
Luke,

Tutorial see: http://www.adtools.com/support/tutorialv3/exercise3.htm
```

- **Message-ID:** `<Jl0PnHJ5PvPd-pn2-YVSIivvHvU5S@Dwight_Miller.iix.com>`
- **References:** `<6utjog$iv7$1@nclient5-gui.server.virgin.net>`

```
On Wed, 30 Sep 1998 15:31:47, "Luke Medland" <luke.medland@virgin.net>
wrote:

> Please help me, I am studying computers at college and can not seem to link
> a .obj file into an .exe.
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> Luke Medland

The comp.lang.cobol FAQ has the answer.  But the short answer is, you 
need to set a compile option - to MAIN, or even easier:

Insert one line of source code at the begining of the program.  Start 
in Column 8 - and it MUST be all uppercase:

@OPTIONS MAIN

Then it will compile and link.
```
